column1: Patagonian species have been differentially impacted by past climatic dynamics. Different demographic processes and trajectories (e.g.
column2: demographic or range expansion or stability
column3: fragmentation
column4: secondary contact) have been recorded in co-distributed species (; ). A pattern of north-to-south postglacial colonization was commonly observed in Patagonian rodents (e.g.
column5: Eligmodontia morgani and Reithrodon auritus)
column6: although more complex processes were also reported
column7: such as within-region differentiation and population persistence at putative refugia (; ). Given this rather species-specific response to climatic oscillation in southern Chile and Argentina
column8: it is of interest to explore phylogeographical patterns in additional lineages before any generalization be advanced about the effect of glaciations on the local fauna.

column1: In this study
column2: we focus on the recently described sigmodontine species Abrothrix manni (). Mann’s soft-haired mouse mainly distributes in the humid forests of north-western Patagonia
column3: in the Chilean Regions of Los Ríos and Los Lagos
column4: including northern Chiloe Island. From west to east
column5: this species is present in the Pacific coast and reaches the Andes; it is also known from a single locality in Neuquén
column6: Argentina (; ). Because A. manni was only recently described
column7: information on its natural history is scarce as no ecological study has focused on it. Much of the available information on the life history of the species has been reported in the literature under A. sanborni or of purported hybrids between that species and A. hirta (referred earlier as A. longipilis; see ). Mann’s soft-haired mouse is active both day and night (). It feeds on fungi
column8: moderate amounts of mature arthropods and larvae
column9: plant foliage
column10: seeds
column11: and fruits (). A. manni breeds through the spring and summer (). Recording localities of A. manni
column12: all laying in the temperate Valdivian rainforest
column13: suggest that the species is a forest specialist.

column1: Compared with most Patagonian mouse species
column2: the geographic range of A. manni is noticeably smaller (e.g.
column3: ; ) and lies in an area that was highly impacted during the Quaternary period. Here
column4: historical environment diversity ranges from more or less continuous ice cap to putative refugial areas for local biota. A lowland glacial refugium has been proposed along the coast north of the latitude 42°S (; ; ). Other co-distributed (at least partially) sigmodontine species in this area exhibit contrasting genetic footprints. For instance
column5: within the area of sympatry with Abrothrix manni
column6: populations of the co-generic A. olivacea belong to two distinct intraspecific lineages ()
column7: while populations of Oligoryzomys longicaudatus are genetically homogeneous
column8: without distinct lineages (; ). This is also the case of continental populations of the pudu deer Pudu puda (). Therefore
column9: given that A. manni occurs in a geographic area that was directly affected by Pleistocene glaciations
column10: and considering that the impact of glaciations was differential over species
column11: we address here the phylogeographic pattern of A. manni. We aim to assess the species genetic structure
column12: testing for the existence of intraspecific lineages
column13: and inferring past demographic signals.

column1: Analyses were based on the first 801 bp of the mitochondrial gene Cytochrome-b (Cytb) from 49 individuals of Abrothrix manni collected at 10 localities along most part of its known distributional range (see  and ). Fieldwork and procedures with animals were were approved by the Servicio Agricola Ganadero (Permits number 1231/2017
column4: 5165) and the Comité de Bioética of the Universidad Austral de Chile (Nb. 311/2018). Nineteen sequences were downloaded from GenBank
column5: while 30 were newly generated from specimens housed at the Colección de Mamíferos de la Universidad Austral de Chile (UACH). Unpublished sequences were generated following the protocol outlined by ; external DNA sequencing service was provided by Macrogen (Seoul
column6: South Korea). All new DNA sequences were deposited at GenBank (see accession numbers in ).

column1: Locality numbers are those depicted in .

Sequences were aligned using Clustal W () implemented in Mega 7 () to establish character primary homology. Haplotype and nucleotide diversity indexes were calculated using DNAsp (). Genetic distances (p-distances) were calculated using Mega 7. Genealogical analyses were based on a matrix containing one representative of each allelic class to speed up searches. Haplotype selection was conducted using DNAsp following a visual corroboration of the absence of segregating sites within haplotype classes. This was conducted observing branch length in a neighbor-joining tree constructed with Mega 7. Sequences of Cytb from Abrothrix andina (GenBank accession: AF108671)

column4: A. longipilis (HM167785)
column5: A. sanborni (KP666004)
column6: and A. olivacea (HM167800) were used to conform the outgroup. Best-fit model of nucleotide substitution for this dataset was determined based on maximum likelihood estimates of model parameters and Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) using jModeltest2 (). The selected model was set in genealogical reconstruction using two approaches
column7: maximum likelihood (ML) and Bayesian inference (BI). The ML tree was inferred using IQ-TREE () implemented in the IQ-TREE web server (); branch supports were calculated with the ultrafast bootstrap (). The BI analysis was conducted using MrBayes (); four independent runs were conducted
column8: each consisting of 20 × 106 MCMC iterations
column9: where chains were sampled every 1
column10: 000 generations. Convergence among chains was corroborated by inspecting values (<0.01) of the average standard deviation of split frequencies. The first 25% of the samples were discarded as burn-in; remaining trees
column11: sampled well after stationarity was reached
column12: were used to compute a consensus tree with posterior probability (PP) estimates for each clade.

column1: Historical demography was assessed on the basis of the distribution of pair-wise differences and related tests using Arlequin 3.5. A multimodal mismatch distribution indicates that samples were drawn from a population in demographic equilibrium
column2: while a unimodal curve is expected for populations that experienced a recent demographic expansion (). The smoothness of the mismatch distribution was quantified using the raggedness index (hr); a significant p-value indicates that the sample is drawn from a recently expanded population (). The fit of observed data to a null model of stationary population size was tested via the sum of squares deviation (SSD) test. Also

Patagonian sigmodontine species have differentially responded to historical climatic events. Many of these broadly distributed species lack significant phylogeographic breaks (). Fewer are the cases where allopatric clades were reported; among these are the Patagonian chinchilla mouse Euneomys chinchilloides ()
column2: the long-haired mouse Abrothrix hirta () and the olive mouse Abrothrix olivacea (; ). Notice
column3: however
column4: that these species show phylogeographic structure over their large distributional ranges covering most of the open (the former) or open and forested (the last two) areas of Patagonia. Meanwhile the distributional range of Abrothrix manni is considerably smaller than that of the species mentioned above
column5: including the co-generic and co-distributed A. hirta and A. olivacea (). Even so
column6: A. manni exhibits a clear differentiation in two main clades that are essentially allopatric (). Interestingly
column7: in our study area
column8: which traditionally has been considered as large refugial area (; ) no phylogeographic structure is found in other mammals
column9: such as the olive mouse A. olivacea ()
column10: the long-tailed mouse O. longicaudatus (; )
column11: the marsupial Dromiciops gliroides (sensu ; clade C in )
column12: and the pudu Pudu puda (). Therefore
column13: setting aside the limitations of the data analyzed in terms of loci and geographic coverage
column14: the phylogeographic pattern found in A. manni is unique among the mammals studied to date in the Patagonian Valdivian forest.

The distributional area of A. manni has been highly impacted by the Quaternary climate oscillations. Environmental dynamics in this area include changes in the spatial configuration of forests (e.g.
column2: reduction
column3: fragmentation
column4: and expansion) and the extension of a glacial ice cap in pre-Andean and Andean areas during the LGM (; ; ; ). Having in mind that A. manni is a forest-dwelling species with a restricted geographic range
column5: the uncovered phylogeographic pattern suggests a process of local differentiation in at least two areas that are latitudinally segregated
column6: one in the north and another in the south of the species distribution. Determining the absolute time for the formation of the two main intraspecific lineages of A. manni remains a challenging task. However
column7: considering the estimation of 0.6 MY as the crown age of the species (see  in ; but note the error bar ranging from more than 1 MY to a few thousand years ago)
column8: it is possible that the two main lineages of A. manni may have diverged in the context of Pleistocene forest fragmentation occurring in the area (; ; ). If
column9: as hypothesized
column10: both main mitochondrial lineages of A. manni differentiated in allopatry
column11: it is necessary to propose an event of secondary contact after the differentiation of the northern and southern lineages of A. manni. Our sampling design detected a single intermediate locality of sympatry of these lineages (locality 6
column13: ). Since there are not noteworthy discontinuities in the non-sampled area between the known areas of occupancy of both clades
column14: we anticipate that more sites of contact might be found with a denser geographic coverage.

column1: The existence of Pleistocene refugia in the Valdivian forest of North-Western Patagonia has been proposed in an increasing number of studies
column2: including reptiles (; ; )
column3: amphibians ()
column4: fishes (; ) and insects (). An initial model of a single coastal refugium has been rapidly replaced by a more complex scenario of multiple refugia (; ; ; ; ). In any case
column5: palynological
column6: palaeoclimatic and genetic data suggest that non-glaciated coastal areas from northern Chiloe up northwards were mainly forested during the LGM (; ; ). Accordingly
column7: several tree species
column8: such as Nothofagus pumilio
column9: N. oblicua
column10: Podocarpus nubigena
column11: and Euchryphia cordifolia
column12: would have persisted not only in the coastal zone
column13: but also near and at Los Andes (; ; ; )
column14: some of them probably moving down and uphill during the Pleistocene and the Holocene (; ).  identified two types of refugial areas in southern Chile
column15: a peripheral glacial refugium along the western slopes of the Andes
column16: and a lowland glacial refugium located in the Western Chilean Pacific coast. The first one is supported by genetic evidence of—among other plants and animal species—the sigmodontine mouse Loxodontomys micropus (; ). On the other hand
column17: another sigmodontine rodent
column18: the olive mouse Abrothrix olivacea
column19: supports the lowland coastal refugium (). In addition
column20: both coastal and Andean refugia has been also proposed for same species (; e.g.
column21: ; ; ; ). Whether the suggested refugia for A. manni were coastal and/or pre-Andean is uncertain. Both scenarios are plausible for either northern or southern refugium. Irrespective the precise location of refugial areas for northern and southern lineages
column22: our results support the local persistence of A. manni during the LGM in its current general distributional area at the Valdivian forest. In line with this idea is the fact that neither demographic indexes nor mismatch distribution test for the six geographical groups of localities showed clear signals of recent population expansion; the eastern range () that considers localities that would have been covered by the ice sheet during the LGM (). In addition
column23: the six groups analyzed (–) show a general pattern of population reduction towards the present. Noteworthily
column24: the eastern range group shows a trend of gradual increment in the effective population size between ca. 100 KYA to 25 KYA with a minor reduction towards the present (). In this regard
column25: it is of interest in future studies to include more samples of A. manni from the eastern range
column26: particularly from Argentina (i.e.
column27: east side of the Andes) to assess if the uncovered pattern changes.

column1: In addition to the mainland area of Central-Southern Chile
column2: the north-west half of the large Chiloe Island has been also proposed as a refugial area (). Due to sampling limitations we could not test here for demographic stability of populations of A. manni from Chiloe
column3: but we observed that island genetic variants are closely related to those recovered in the mainland. This was also observed in the marine otter Lontra felina ()
column4: the pudu deer Pudu puda ()
column5: the marsupial Dromicios gliroides ()
column6: and in the long-tailed mouse Oligoryzomys longicaudatus (). These results are in line with well-known evidence showing that during Pleistocene glacial oscillations
column7: the Chiloe Island remained connected with the mainland due to a drop in sea level ().

column1: In north-western Patagonia
column2: different mammals show signals of demographic expansion within our study area. This is the case of Dromiciops gliroides (referred as clade C in )
column3: Abrothrix olivacea (; but see ) and Oligoryzomys longicaudatus (). Contrary to the mentioned species
column4: A. manni fails to show signals of recent demographic expansion in both northern and southern portions of its distributional range (). On the contrary
column5: Bayesian skyline plots showed a temporal line with an increase in the effective population size followed by a reduction towards the present (). This population decline was estimated as occurring about 15
column7: 000 YA. This is the approximate timing of the end of the last glaciation. Distinct processes could explain the inferred recent reduction in population sizes of Mann’s soft-haired mouse. One of this is a scenario where populations of A. manni have been negatively affected by interspecific competition with other species
column8: likely sigmodontine rodents
column9: which would have expanded in the area after glacial conditions retreated. Also
column10: it is possible that the demographic reduction experienced by A. manni may have been caused by the wildfire that increased in Northern Patagonian forests ca. 11
column12: 500 YA (; ) and/or eruptions such as that of the regional volcanoes Mocho-Choshuenco and Puyehue-Cordón Caulle (). For the moment
column13: the understanding of the natural history of A. manni is much limited (see ); as such
column14: the just posed suggestions needs to be further evaluated with additional genetic studies (allowing testing the reduction in population sizes) as well as with field studies.

column1: The genetic variation of the mitochondrial Cytb gene of Abrothrix manni supports a history of persistence during Quaternary oscillations in its restricted distributional area at the Valdivian forest of north-western Patagonia. This
column2: however
column3: does not imply that this mouse was not impacted by climatic changes; on the contrary
column4: forest fragmentation would have caused a differentiation process that led to two divergent intraspecific lineages and the posterior secondary contact between them. These conclusions are noteworthy because of the reduced geographic scale inhabited by the species. They also imply that this small area may have harbored multiple Pleistocene refugia.

column1: Finally
column2: with this study
column3: which constitutes the first description of the spatial distribution of the genetic variation and historical demography of the recently described Mann’s soft-haired mouse A. manni
column4: we hope to contribute to the understanding of evolutionary history of the local fauna
column5: particularly of the sigmodontine rodents.

column1: 1|10.7717/peerj.16574|Acute heat priming promotes short-term climate resilience of early life stages in a model sea anemone| Glass  Benjamin H. |2023-12-5|"Anthropogenic ocean warming threatens the survival of marine invertebrates
column2: and early life stages (e.g.
column3: larvae and juveniles) are particularly susceptible to heat stress (; ; ; ; ). Given the widespread negative effects experienced by marine invertebrates during and after marine heatwaves
column4: in some cases including mass mortality (; ; ; ; )
column5: it is unclear whether these organisms possess the capacity to persist in the face of unprecedented
column6: rapid climate change (; ; ; ). Within-generation acclimatization may promote resilience to ocean warming
column7: and has gained increased attention in marine invertebrates including corals and sea anemones in recent years (; ; ; ; ). While a unified terminology has yet to emerge for discourse surrounding within-generation acclimatization
column8: modified responses to repeat exposure to abiotic stress are often termed “environmental memory” (; ; ) or “legacy effects” (; )
column9: which can imply negative or positive responses to repeated stress. These phenomena may promote the persistence of marine invertebrates in a warming ocean
column10: and are worthy of further investigation.

column1: Beneficial acclimatization can develop via hormetic priming
column2: in which exposure to low or intermediate doses of abiotic stress elicits physiological benefits
column3: while higher doses lead to increasing net harm (; ). Hormesis gives rise to characteristic “U-” or “J-shaped” dose-response curves
column4: in which a phenotype of interest (e.g.
column5: a physiological metric) peaks at intermediate values when quantified across a range of an abiotic stressor
column6: giving the resulting curve the appearance of an inverted letter “U” or “J” (; ; ). For example
column7: sublethal exposure to abiotic stressors (e.g.
column8: temperature or toxins) is known to have beneficial effects such as increased growth and improved stress tolerance upon repeat exposure in a broad diversity of taxa
column9: from plants to animals (; ). In addition
column10: hormetic priming in response to hyperthermal stress (i.e.
column11: heat or thermal priming) has been observed in plants ()
column12: insects ()
column13: cnidarians ()
column14: birds ()
column15: mammals ()
column16: and other taxa. In marine invertebrates
column17: thermal priming has been best characterized in adult scleractinian corals
column18: and typically involves a reduction in the bleaching response of symbiotic species to a subsequent exposure following an initial sublethal heat exposure (; ). Beneficial priming in adult cnidarians has been linked to mechanisms including changes in gene expression (; )
column19: cellular function (; ; )
column20: and metabolism (). Despite growing knowledge surrounding heat priming in adult corals
column21: we still lack an understanding of how priming effects vary across multiple priming temperatures (i.e.
column22: hormesis) and their role in other marine invertebrate taxa
column23: particularly those that do not have algal endosymbionts. This gap in knowledge limits our understanding of how marine invertebrates and their particularly vulnerable early life stages may fare in future seas.

column1: Current knowledge regarding the mechanisms and ecological implications of heat priming in marine invertebrates has largely arisen from studies on adult organisms
column2: yet there is evidence that thermal priming also occurs during marine invertebrate early life stages (; ; ). For example
column3: heat priming of gametes from the reef-building corals Acropora cytherea and A. pulchra partially ameliorates heat-induced fertilization failure (
column4: ). Additionally
column5: in the coral Pocillopora damicornis
column6: conditioning of brooded larvae to simulated ocean warming and acidification while still inside the adult colony increases settlement and survival following planulation ()
column7: as well as bleaching resistance and photosynthetic performance of algal endosymbionts under elevated temperatures (). However
column8: in the corals A. tenuis and Montipora capitata
column9: exposure to heat stress at the larval stage does not alter performance during a secondary heat exposure during the juvenile stage (; )
column10: demonstrating that the effects of heat priming on early life stages are taxonomically variable and need to be characterized in a broader diversity of species and temperature regimes. As marine invertebrate early life stages are particularly sensitive to abiotic stressors (; ; ; )
column11: heat priming may be an important mechanism for the reproductive resilience of these taxa. However
column12: key questions remain surrounding heat priming in early life stages. For example
column13: it is necessary to further investigate what temperatures lead to beneficial priming as opposed to neutral or negative effects (i.e.
column14: hormesis)
column15: as most previous studies focus solely on comparisons between ambient and a single elevated (priming) temperature. Furthermore
column16: molecular and cellular mechanisms underpinning temperature hormesis in marine invertebrate early life stages are unknown and in need of further investigation. These topics are of vital importance in a rapidly changing climate
column17: particularly since spawning for many marine invertebrates occurs during the warmest months of the year ()
column18: meaning that larvae are being exposed to marine heatwaves with increasing frequency.

column1: To address existing gaps in knowledge surrounding mechanisms of temperature hormesis in marine invertebrate early life stages
column2: we used the widely studied sea anemone Nematostella vectensis as a model organism. N. vectensis inhabits temperate estuarine environments characterized by high diel and seasonal variability in abiotic conditions (; )
column3: and has evolved plastic mechanisms to support rapid acclimatization (; ; ; ). For example
column4: N. vectensis inhabits salt marshes along the Atlantic coast of New Jersey
column5: USA
column6: where temperatures can range from less than 0 °C to greater than 42 °C (annual median ~16–18 °C) and vary up to 20 °C within a single day
column7: indicating that this species is a eurytherm (; ). Additionally
column8: while annual patterns of wild spawning in N. vectensis are currently uncharacterized
column9: gametogenesis requires only ~2 weeks in this species and has been observed at culture temperatures between 16–24 °C (; )
column10: suggesting that N. vectensis may be able to spawn throughout the year in the wild. Given that N. vectensis displays wide temperature tolerance ()
column11: plasticity in thermal tolerance in response to both abiotic and biotic environmental factors (; ; )
column12: and can easily be induced to spawn (; ; )
column13: this species is an important and tractable model in which to investigate environmental memory and hormesis in early life stages (; ; ). Additionally
column14: given its wide geospatial distribution and similar stress responses to other invertebrate taxa
column15: including ctenophores and echinoderms (; ; ; ; )
column16: N. vectensis can provide insight into the capacity for environmental memory in the early life stages of marine invertebrates more broadly. Finally
column17: N. vectensis is a common model species for the study of early development
column18: so characterizing priming responses in this species will provide important knowledge regarding the effects of environmental factors on development
column19: while also laying the foundation for future work on this topic. Here
column20: larvae from N. vectensis were exposed to control (18 °C) or elevated (24 °C
column23: or 39 °C) temperatures for 1 h at 3 days post-fertilization (DPF). Larvae were then returned to culture at 18 °C and assessed for growth
column24: development
column25: and various physiological metrics before
column26: during
column27: and after settlement (4
column30: respectively). To investigate a possible mechanism of modified heat tolerance following priming
column31: the expression of heat shock protein 70 (HSP70) in primed larvae was quantified at 11 DPF. Additionally
column32: to gain insight into the temporal extent of priming effects
column33: a second larval cohort was also primed and followed through 6 weeks post-fertilization. Overall
column34: this study constitutes a novel examination of heat priming in a model sea anemone
column35: and suggests that dose-dependent priming may augment marine invertebrate resilience to anthropogenic ocean warming via hormesis by the modulation of developmental and physiological phenotypes during early life stages.

column1: Adult Nematostella vectensis sea anemones were collected from a salt marsh in Brigantine
column2: New Jersey in the fall of 2020. Following transport to the laboratory
column3: anemones were kept in 12 parts per thousand (ppt) artificial seawater (1/3-ASW; Spectrum Brands
column4: Madison
column5: WI
column6: USA) at 18 °C in a dark incubator (Boekel Scientific
column7: Feasterville Trevose
column8: PA
column9: USA). Animals were fed twice per week with Artemia nauplii (Brine Shrimp Direct
column10: Ogden
column11: UT
column12: USA) with water changes occurring every 2 weeks for approximately 2.5 years. Spawning was induced using a standard method for N. vectensis (; ; )
column13: which entailed exposing anemones (N = 200 adults across four containers) to light and elevated temperatures (24 °C) for 14 h followed by transfer to room temperature (∼18–19 °C)
column14: after which spawning occurred within 1–2 h. As culture containers housed both male and female anemones
column15: eggs were left to fertilize during spawning and then transferred to a plastic dish with ~25 mL new 1/3-ASW. Fertilized embryos were then held at 18 °C in the dark for 3 days.

column1: At 3 days post-fertilization (DPF)
column2: 200 swimming planula larvae were pipetted into each of 15 conical tubes (15 mL)
column3: for a total of three replicate tubes of 200 larvae (N = 600 larvae priming temperature−1) for each of the five priming temperature treatments: 18 °C (control)
column7: and 39 °C (). At a duration of 1 h
column8: these temperatures are sublethal for N. vectensis larvae (; )
column9: and representative of the range of temperatures and diel variability that can occur in this species’ natural habitat (). Tubes were placed in water baths (Thermo Fisher Scientific
column10: Waltham
column11: MA
column12: USA) set to each treatment temperature for 1 h. Following the treatment period
column13: tubes containing larvae were poured into petri dishes (15 mL capacity) and a partial water change was performed by aspirating and replacing ~7.5 mL (~50%) 1/3-ASW; dishes were then held at 18 °C in the dark for the remainder of the experiment. The day after the priming treatment
column14: one dish of larvae primed at 39 °C displayed 100% mortality along with dense overgrowth of an unidentified microbe
column15: and was therefore removed from the experiment. All other larvae were left unfed and in the same water for the remainder of the short-term experiment (11 days in total). For the long-term experiment
column16: this entire procedure was repeated for a second cohort of larvae
column17: which were held at 18 °C for 6 weeks following priming at 3 DPF. In both experiments
column18: control animals were kept at 18 °C for the entire experiment duration
column19: having gone through the priming treatment but without a change in temperature.

column1: Larval heat tolerance was determined at 4
column3: and 11 DPF using previously established methods (; ). Specifically
column4: six larvae from each culture dish were exposed to each of the following peak temperatures: 39 °C
column8: or 43 °C
column9: yielding 2–3 dose-response curves (N = 30 larvae curve−1) per priming temperature. Specifically
column10: individual larvae were pipetted into wells of a 96-well PCR plate with 100 μL of 1/3-ASW
column11: and plates were sealed with an adhesive plate cover to prevent evaporation during the assay. Thermocyclers (Thermo Fisher Scientific
column12: Waltham
column13: MA
column14: USA) were used to generate the heat ramps
column15: and were programmed as follows: (i) 1 min at 25 °C; (ii) 4 min at 30 °C; (iii) 4 min at 38 °C; (iv) 1 h at peak temperature (39–43 °C); (v) 4 min at 38 °C; (vi) 4 min at 30 °C; and (vii) infinite hold at 22 °C. Following completion of the heat ramp
column16: the plates were uncovered and placed at 18 °C for 48 h
column17: after which larvae were scored as dead (visible tissue lysis) or alive (no visual abnormalities; often swimming). For each dose-response curve
column18: the proportion of larvae surviving at each peak temperature was calculated by dividing the number of larvae surviving by the total number of larvae treated. Data pertaining to proportion survival were used to calculate lethal temperature 50s (LT50s) for each dose-response curve as detailed below.

column1: In order to characterize the persistence of heat priming effects on growth and heat tolerance
column2: a second cohort of larvae was produced 2 weeks after the initial spawning by the same adult population and exposed to the same priming treatment at 3 DPF as described above. Following priming
column3: larvae were kept in culture through 6 weeks post-fertilization (WPF) at 18 °C in the dark
column4: where they progressed to the juvenile stage and began to grow tentacles (). Animals were fed twice per week (i.e.
column5: every 3–4 days) after settlement (~7 DPF) with homogenized Artemia nauplii. Specifically
column6: 3 mL of live Artemia nauplii were homogenized in a 15 mL conical using a rotostator at 20
column7: 000 rpm for 10 s
column8: and 200 μL of the resulting slurry was added to each culture dish. Within 2–3 h after feeding
column9: a partial water change was performed for each dish by aspirating and replacing 5–7 mL (33–50%) of 1/3-ASW. Images were collected weekly as described above to characterize long-term effects of priming on growth
column10: and the number of tentacles possessed by each juvenile in the images was also quantified. After 6 weeks
column11: juvenile heat tolerance was determined via a single-temperature heat challenge at 42 °C due to fewer juveniles in the long-term priming experiment vs the short-term experiment. Specifically

column14: so the assay was concluded at that time. Finally
column15: the number of surviving juveniles was divided by the number treated to obtain the proportion surviving.

column2: proportion survival data from larval heat tolerance assays were used to create dose-response curves represented by two-parameter log-logistic functions using the package drc ()
column3: and an LT50 was determined for each curve using the package chemCal (). Data pertaining to growth (body column length and juvenile tentacle number)

column1: Here
column2: we found that sublethal heat stress led to hormesis during early life stages in the model sea anemone Nematostella vectensis. Growth
column3: development
column4: and heat tolerance all displayed hormetic responses
column5: being augmented in larvae primed at intermediately (24 °C
column7: and 35 °C) but not extremely (39 °C) elevated temperatures compared to controls (18 °C). Additionally
column8: expression of heat shock protein 70 (HSP70) was significantly higher in larvae primed at elevated temperatures even 8 days after priming
column9: yet was negatively correlated with heat tolerance
column10: suggesting that HSP70 protein expression alone cannot explain the modulation of larval hyperthermal performance following priming. Furthermore
column11: the effects of priming on growth and heat tolerance persisted beyond the larval stage
column12: up to at least 6 weeks post-fertilization (WPF). These findings represent a novel contribution to a growing body of knowledge surrounding hormesis in marine invertebrates
column13: and suggest that dose-dependent hormetic priming can have long-term
column14: beneficial effects on marine invertebrate early life stages
column15: which may augment the resilience of these imperiled organisms to anthropogenic ocean warming.

column1: Priming at intermediately elevated temperatures (24 °C
column3: and 35 °C) led to increased body column lengths in animals at 7 and 11 days post-fertilization (DPF). This response may be beneficial for fitness
column4: as body size is positively correlated with survival and reproductive output in a broad diversity of organisms (). However
column5: it is also possible that increased growth after priming at intermediately elevated temperatures might incur a trade off with other traits. For example
column6: the use of metabolic resources for increased growth could result in decreases in important homeostatic mechanisms
column7: such as morphological regulation (; ). However
column8: we did not observe tradeoffs between growth and any of the other traits measured in this study
column9: and suggest this topic for future study. The observation of a parabolic relationship between temperature and growth is common for biological rate processes ()
column10: and is congruent with previous findings that larval growth in N. vectensis larvae peaks following sustained exposure to intermediately elevated temperatures (). However
column11: it was surprising that hyperthermal priming for just 1 h at 3 DPF was sufficient to produce augmented growth
column12: as it was previously believed that only sustained exposure to elevated temperatures would produce this response (). Additionally
column13: it was intriguing that hormetic effects of heat priming on short-term growth were largely absent in fed larvae at 1 and 2 WPF. Regardless of priming temperature
column14: fed larvae displayed body column lengths similar to those of starved larvae primed at intermediately elevated temperatures (24 °C
column16: and 35 °C) at 7 and 11 DPF. This trend suggests that
column17: in fed larvae
column18: no additional growth benefit was gained following heat priming
column19: possibly because nutritional resources had already allowed these larvae to grow to a stage-specific size optimum. Strikingly
column20: despite the absence of hormetic responses in growth at 1 and 2 WPF in fed larvae
column21: juvenile body column lengths and tentacle numbers (i.e.
column22: developmental rate) later displayed classical hormetic U-curves—being increased at intermediately elevated priming temperatures—that persisted through 6 WPF. These findings demonstrate that a single hour of exposure to hyperthermal stress at 3 DPF can have long-term consequences for growth and development
column23: which may be dampened early in ontogeny when external nutritional resources are abundant. It was also striking that larvae primed at 39 °C displayed no growth after 2 WPF
column24: particularly given that N. vectensis can transiently experience temperatures in the range of 39–43 °C and diel variability in the range of 20 °C day−1 in its natural estuarine habitats (). This result suggests that N. vectensis larvae are likely to be threatened by anthropogenic global warming

column1: Mosquitoes oviposit eggs in aquatic habitats where the larvae and pupae typically develop within several weeks and then emerge into terrestrial adults (). Mosquito larvae primarily feed on microbial organisms that colonize plant and animal detritus (; ). Larval physiology and ecology affect the distribution and abundance of adults by moderating survival and adult fitness parameters
column2: including such as body size
column3: which can affect adult survival
column4: biting rate
column5: and ultimately the ability to vector and transmit pathogens (). There is little information on the effect of UV-B radiation on the metabolic rates and survival of mosquitoes or on the microbial food resources on which they feed. To our knowledge only one study has rigorously assessed the effects of UVR on mosquitoes ()
column6: which demonstrated clear negative effects of increasing UVR on larvae and pupae of Ae. aegypti (L.) and Cx. pipiens. However
column7: a significant limitation of the study is that the UVR levels used in the study were not comparable to field conditions.

column1: Ultraviolet radiation (UVR)
column2: especially UV-B radiation
column3: could have important effects on the distribution and abundance of pathogen-transmitting species. This is the first study that has rigorously tested the effects of UV-B radiation on the metabolic rates and fitness of medically important mosquitoes and the activity of their microbial food resources. Our results showed that UV-B radiation increased the metabolic rates of both Ae. albopictus and Cx. pipiens larvae
column4: with significantly higher rates in full-sun conditions compared to shade conditions and a no-UV control after 8 and 15 days of exposure. In field conditions
column5: metabolic rate increased in mosquito larvae between emergence and day 4–5 (). Microbial metabolic rates were also lower in full-sun conditions after 8 days of exposure compared to shade and no-UV conditions. These results suggest that UV-B radiation at levels typically found in open spaces in the field is likely have strong and important impacts on the ecologies of Ae. albopictus and Cx. pipiens mosquitoes and potential disease transmission.

column1: Ecological theory and empirical evidence suggest that trade-offs occur among life-history traits when limited energy is partitioned among growth
column2: maintenance
column3: and reproduction (). Environmental factors at the larval stage have been shown to affect a range of traits in adult mosquitoes
column4: mostly via changes in body size
column5: including susceptibility to viral infection ()
column6: reproduction ()
column7: and longevity (; )
column8: all of which can mediate changes in vectorial capacity (; ; ). In addition to affecting larval survival
column9: it is also possible that the effects of UV-B radiation at the larval life-stage might also manifest in adult traits ().

column1: In addition to affecting mosquito populations through changes in microbial communities
column2: we cannot disregard direct negative effects of elevated UV-B radiation on larvae. The only prior study to rigorously study the effects of UVR on mosquito larvae observed severe negative impacts on larval mobility (e.g.
column3: loss of movement coordination) and eclosion rates after exposure to UVR for 48 h (). Histological analyses showed damage in the larvae cuticle
column4: disintegration of the abdominal segments
column5: and partial paralysis (). However
column6: examined the effects of UV-C radiation (180–254 nm)
column7: which does not naturally reach the earth’s surface and is only commonly found from artificial sources unlikely to affect mosquitoes (e.g.
column8: mercury lamps
column9: welding torches). To our knowledge
column10: our study is the first to test field-relevant UVR on the ecology of mosquitoes and the activity of their microbial food resources.

column1: The specific effects of UV-B radiation on the cells of living organisms is still unclear (). Prior studies have demonstrated that UV-B radiation damages the DNA
column2: proteins
column3: membranes
column4: and photochemical efficiency of photosynthetic prokaryote organisms
column5: affecting their photosynthesis and biomass production (e.g.
column6: ; ; ). For example
column7: the spiral structure of cyanobacteria
column8: Arthrospira platensis
column9: can be broken and its photosynthetic activity disrupted with exposure to UV-B radiation within the temperature range of 18–20 °C
column10: resulting in lower biomass ().  reported that exposure to 6 h of UV-B radiation broke the spiral filaments of A. platensis into small pieces
column11: affecting its photosynthesis activity
column12: as well as important photosynthetic electron transport and pigment-protein complexes.

column1: Deforestation and the conversion of natural environments (e.g.
column2: grassland
column3: agriculture) to build has increased over the last 100 years (; ; ). These land use changes are most likely to increase open spaces with full-sun conditions
column4: although large building structures may increase shade in some areas ().  predicts that by 2030 urban land cover will increase by 1.2 million km2 if current population trends continue. Urbanization also contributes to increasing pollutants from industrialization
column5: vehicles
column6: and electrical power generation (; )
column7: many of which (e.g.
column8: CO2
column9: smog
column10: particulate matter) may absorb and reflect UVR away from the earth’s surface (). The combination of more open spaces and higher pollution could have complex impacts on the amount of UVR reaching ground surfaces in both space and time ().

column1: Ae. albopictus and Cx. pipiens
column2: two of the most broadly distributed mosquito species worldwide
column3: are medically important because they are vectors for a number of arboviruses and they inhabit peridomestic areas (; ). West Nile virus (WNV) is the most important mosquito-borne human disease in the United States
column4: with over 48
column5: 183 reported cases since being first detected in North America in 1999 (). In the northeastern part of the United States
column6: WNV is primarily circulated by Cx. pipiens among bird populations that amplify the virus (). Ae. albopictus is capable of acting as a vector of WNV in the laboratory
column7: but only rarely bites birds in the field and instead feeds on a range of hosts
column8: including humans ().

column1: Anthropogenic environmental changes and the resultant increases in the exposure to UV-B radiation to container habitats could alter the distributions and abundances of these species. But their effects are difficult to predict and tease apart from associated changes to physio-chemical and biological conditions
column2: including temperature
column3: hydroperiod
column4: and nutrients. Nevertheless
column5: numerous studies have shown that Cx. pipiens is more likely to utilize containers that are more exposed to sunlight than Ae. albopictus and other mosquito species (; ); thus
column6: we might expect Cx. pipiens to be especially prone to changes in UV-B exposure. On the other hand
column7: Ae. Albopictus
column8: which shows a strong preference to oviposit in shaded areas and at ground level (; )
column9: could experience changes in its behavior
column10: abundance
column11: and distribution as responses to changes in UV-B exposure
column12: changes that are likely to have important implications for the transmission of vector-borne diseases. It has been found that Ae. albopictus can occasionally lay eggs at greater heights depending on environmental factors like temperature () and habitat availability (). For example
column13: Ae. albopictus abundance could increase in dense urban areas (high density housing with high buildings) compared to peri urban/suburban areas (low density housing with low buildings)
column14: because of more abundant shaded environments to lay eggs
column15: which could increase the risk of viral diseases spread by these mosquitoes.  found higher populations of Ae. albopictus in dense urban areas compared to peri urban areas in Guangzhou
column16: China. Ae. albopictus also showed faster larval development
column17: higher adult emergence rate
column18: and longer survival time compared to peri urban and rural areas ()
column19: which could increase vector capacity. Ae. albopictus had showed high plasticity to adapt to new environments (; ).  showed that Ae. albopictus could vertically move as high as 60 m to oviposit in apartment buildings when there is not suitable habitat at ground level.

column1: Better understanding of impacts of UV-B in the ecology of Ae. albopictus and Cx. pipiens is necessary to optimally assess human risk. By manipulating only UV-B radiation in a controlled laboratory experiment
column2: our study was able to pinpoint the effects of UV-B radiation and demonstrated important impacts of altered exposure that we might expect from changes to shade.

column1: In summary
column2: this study shows that UV-B radiation can have strong negative effects on the larval survival of both Ae. albopictus and Cx. pipiens. These effects were likely indirect
column3: through decreases in the availability of microbial food
column4: but could have also been direct
column5: through adverse impacts on the cells of larvae. This is among the first studies to rigorously test the effects of UV-B radiation on the survival and fitness of mosquito larvae using levels typically observed in open and shaded habitats in the field. The impacts of UV-B radiation on larval survival are likely to affect the distribution and abundances of Ae. albopictus and Cx. pipiens but in ways that may be difficult to detect in the field since most landscapes are a patchwork of open and shaded areas that adult individuals can move between. Future research should examine the effects of UV-B radiation on the ecology
column6: physiology
column7: and behavior of other mosquito species and life-stages (eggs
column8: adults)
column9: as well as on other community processes
column10: such as predation
column11: parasitism
column12: and on vector competence across other disease systems such as chikungunya and Zika virus to gather a greater understanding of the potentially important impacts of UV-B radiation on vector ecology.

column1: 3|10.7717/peerj.5801|Agrichemicals and antibiotics in combination increase antibiotic resistance evolution| Kurenbach  Brigitta |2018-10-12|"Neither reducing the use of antibiotics nor discovery of new ones may be sufficient strategies to avoid the post-antibiotic era. This is because bacteria may be exposed to other non-antibiotic chemicals that predispose them to evolve resistance to antibiotics more quickly. Herbicides are examples of some of the most common non-antibiotic chemicals in frequent global use. We previously showed that in some combinations the herbicides we tested made bacteria phenotypically resistant to higher concentrations of antibiotics
column2: while in other combinations bacteria became susceptible at lower antibiotic concentrations. Here we demonstrate that in both cases the herbicides worked with antibiotics to accelerate genotypic resistance evolution. Unfortunately
column3: antibiotic resistance may increase even if total antibiotic use is reduced
column4: and new ones are invented
column5: unless other environmental exposures are also controlled.

column1: As fundamental tools for infection control
column2: antibiotics underpin diverse human systems ranging from hospital care to concentrated animal feeding operations through to crop and pollinator disease management. The loss of this tool due to antibiotic resistance will result in higher mortality and morbidity
column3: but also deny access to many routine medical procedures for risk of subsequently untreatable infections (; ). Antibiotic resistances also threaten agricultural productivity (; ). Despite over a half century of warning
column4: neither science nor public and private innovation strategies have managed to avert the threat of a post-antibiotic era.

column1: One stewardship strategy is reduction of use which might help increase longevity (; ). If bacteria almost never encounter antibiotics at concentrations high enough to harm them
column2: there would be little opportunity for resistant variants to emerge and establish. Based on this
column3: it has been suggested that judicious and low use of antibiotics that keeps most antibiotic exposures to less than the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) should preserve antibiotic susceptibility in bacteria (). In practice
column4: the lowest concentration of antibiotic leading to the evolution of resistance in a given environment
column5: the so called minimum selective concentration (MSC)
column6: can be much lower than the MIC () (; ; ). Keeping the use of antibiotics to below MSC concentrations is more challenging still.

column1: Herbicides can change responses to antibiotics. Solid curves indicate survival as a function of only antibiotic concentration
column2: intersecting the x-axes at the MIC. Dashed curves show herbicides (A) increasing the MIC
column3: (B) decreasing the MIC
column4: and (C) having no effect on MIC but altering population survival at antibiotic concentrations below-MIC. The hypothetical MSC is the point of divergence of solid and dashed curves and the area in gray illustrates all antibiotic concentrations where the herbicide changes the response to antibiotics.

column1: Variation in antibiotic responses can be caused by either genetic or physiological differences between individual bacteria. The toxic effect of an antibiotic may occur at different concentrations for different individuals because some have acquired genes or alleles through mutation or horizontal gene transfer (i.e.
column2: change in genotype). Also
column3: organisms can have innate differences between them
column4: e.g.
column5: due to differences in permeability.

column1: Innate resistance can also be dependent upon genes expressed or repressed conditionally
column2: resulting in increased efflux or decreased influx of antibiotics and overall lower intracellular antibiotic concentrations (). Such genes or expression induction thresholds may differ between species
column3: and individuals within a species may phenotypically differ depending on whether or not they expressed those genes before being inhibited by the antibiotic. This resistance through changes in gene expression is also known as an adaptive (change in phenotype) response () which acclimates bacteria to the environment. It can be triggered by antibiotics and other chemical toxins or environmental cues (; ; ).

column1: We have previously shown that concurrent exposure of Escherichia coli or Salmonella enterica sv. Typhimurium to various commercial herbicides and antibiotics from different classes can lead to adaptive increases () or decreases () in their susceptibility to the antibiotics. In some cases
column2: herbicide exposures changed the survival at below-MIC antibiotic concentrations of antibiotic
column3: while MIC itself was not affected (). Adaptive responses were specific for the combination of bacteria
column4: antibiotic and herbicide used. Effects were shown for commercial formulations ()
column5: for label listed active ingredients
column6: and for common adjuvants ().

column1: Populations of adaptively resistant bacteria can in time produce variants with acquired resistance to even higher concentrations of antibiotic (; ; ). This raises the possibility that environmental stimuli that cause phenotypic antibiotic resistance variation between individuals could be hotspots for evolution of acquired antibiotic resistance.

Here we exploit our previous observations to test the novel hypothesis that both adaptive increases and decreases in antibiotic susceptibility caused by exposure to herbicide formulations can lead to an increase in the rate of acquired resistance evolution in populations of E. coli and S. typhimurium. For this purpose we conducted short term evolution experiments using either the natural variability within a monoculture of E. coli or S. typhimurium
column2: or isogenic strains only differing in their level of antibiotic resistance
column3: which represents natural variability.

column1: Natural selection was defined as the difference in the exponential growth rate of the two strains (; ). Under this interpretation
column2: the change in the proportion of resistant individuals per unit of time is the logistic curve () with the explicit solution p = est∕(c + est)

Each set of questions was tested using contrasts performed with the glht function in the multcomp package () with a two-sided alternative and sequential Bonferroni procedure. These contrasts were fit to an ANOVA model treating each combination of herbicide and antibiotic as a treatment category. Residual plots confirmed that the assumptions of normality and equality of variance were met. Because residuals were approximately normally distributed we report the arithmetic mean (the maximum likelihood estimator of population mean under the assumption of normality).

As reported previously

From these observations we hypothesized that particular combinations of antibiotics and these formulations alter the MSC or MIC of the antibiotic in a way that favours genetically resistant variants. To test this hypothesis

column1: In this study we report that when bacteria are simultaneously exposed to herbicides and antibiotics
column2: mutants with higher levels of resistance can evolve. In some cases
column3: resistance evolved 100
column4: 000 times faster.

column1: Herbicides can increase the MIC of some antibiotics. At what otherwise would be a lethal concentration of the antibiotic
column2: the bacteria can continue to reproduce. Each reproductive event has a low but steady potential of producing a variant daughter with a higher MIC. We found that these strains have a fitness advantage and accumulate differentially to their low MIC cousins.

column1: Herbicides can also decrease the MIC of some antibiotics. At what otherwise would be a concentration of antibiotic below the MSC
column2: too low to have an effect on the fitness of two bacteria differing in their MICs
column3: we found that the bacteria with the higher MICs replaced the bacteria with lower MICs. The shift in MSC seemed to be of a similar magnitude as the shift in MIC observed for the lower MIC strain.

column1: Finally
column2: herbicides can also alter survival potential at some antibiotic concentrations but not change the concentration to which the entire population is innately susceptible. In the case presented here
column3: MSC was shifted by the herbicides
column4: but MIC was not. The herbicide mitigated the selective pressure caused by the antibiotic
column5: and no genotypes with higher resistance levels established in the population. Although not tested
column6: it is likely that in the reverse case
column7: when MSC is lowered by a substance
column8: antibiotic resistance may arise at higher frequencies.

column1: Our research shows that manufactured chemical products such as the herbicides can have a complex effect on the evolution of antibiotic resistance. They did not replace antibiotics
column2: but could accelerate resistance evolution. Herbicides that reduce the MSC will be more effective at stimulating resistance evolution at the lower ends of the antibiotic concentration gradient
column3: while herbicides that increase the MIC will be more effective at stimulating resistance evolution at the higher ends.

column1: Antibiotics can influence acquired resistance evolution in a number of ways. First
column2: some antibiotics may also be mutagens () and at sub-lethal concentrations could increase the probability of a mutation conferring resistance arising in the population. Second
column3: they discriminate between the reproductive rate of those with higher and lower tolerances to the toxic effects of the antibiotic
column4: leading to different frequencies of genotypes that are less susceptible. At the environmentally relevant concentrations of herbicide formulations that we used
column5: no mutagenic activity was detected. However
column6: the herbicides did contribute to the ability of antibiotics to change the frequency of resistant variants in populations.

column1: Combination treatments using a non-antibiotic to increase an antibiotic’s effectiveness have been suggested to help preserve the usefulness of antibiotics (; ; ). Indeed
column2: various combinations are in clinical use (). While this might improve or enable treatment of an infection
column3: our results indicate that decreasing bacteria’s survival by making an antibiotic more potent may promote resistance evolution by creating more environments in which a resistance genotype is more fit.

column1: Herbicides and different ingredients in product formulations can have antimicrobial activities
column2: with some being more potent antimicrobial agents than are others (). For example
column3: Roundup was more toxic than Kamba to the bacteria that we used. The low level toxicity is likely how they induce adaptive resistance
column4: a source of phenotypic heterogeneity (). In addition
column5: biocidal agents that are mutagens may introduce genotypic heterogeneity.

column1: Antibiotic and herbicide gradients may be viewed as two environmental dimensions concentrating competition between bacteria of different genotypes and physiotypes. The particular concentrations of antibiotic and herbicide at any point of intersection of the gradients above a threshold level increases the frequency of the most fit strains and species
column2: amplifying any linked or associated traits in the more fit competitor. While antibiotics can do this without herbicides (; ; )
column3: the herbicides used in this study increased the range of concentrations under which the antibiotic affects the evolution of resistance.

column1: The concentrations of herbicides we used were below recommended application levels and hence within relevant environmental ranges
column2: suggesting that what we observe in the laboratory has the potential to occur in many places where both antibiotics and herbicides are found together. Simultaneous herbicide and antibiotic exposures are common. Herbicides are used in agriculture
column3: where spray drift or walking through treated fields exposes farm livestock and pets
column4: which may be on therapeutic or prophylactic antibiotics. Most ingested antibiotic is not metabolized and thus excreted ()
column5: becoming mixed with soil as crop fertilizer which in situ may be subsequently sprayed with herbicide. Microbes from these mixes may be carried by blow- and house-flies (). Likewise honeybees may be exposed to herbicide spray or residues as they forage and return to an antibiotic-treated hive. Additionally
column6: herbicides are used in urban environments for purposes like gardening and lawn care
column7: including parks and roadsides (). Worldwide
column8: herbicide use was approximately 1 ×109 kg in 2012 with up to 2 ×108 kg of the active herbicidal ingredients glyphosate
column10: 4-D and dicamba used in the US in 2012 ().

column1: Other chemicals also have been shown to cause adaptive resistance and to increase resistance frequencies (; ; ). Non-antibiotic prescription medicines and food emulsifiers select antibiotic resistant gut bacteria (; ). Approximately 8 million manufactured chemical substances are currently in commerce (; ). According to the US Environmental Protection Agency
column2: annual production of each of the top 3
column3: 000 chemicals is greater than 6 ×1011 kg/year (). They are not regulated for effects on antibiotic resistance and not tested for such effects.

column1: The susceptibility of bacteria to antibiotics must be seen as a non-renewable resource
column2: one that requires careful stewardship worldwide (; ). Evidence that antibiotic resistance evolution is influenced by exposure of bacteria to a wide range of substances may require us to make changes in how we manage both antibiotics and other manufactured and widely distributed chemical products. This is because many facets of the extrinsic environment induce adaptive changes
column3: a complexity frequently ignored in standard studies of resistance. As our results show
column4: complex effects of exposures to non-therapeutic chemicals may undermine strategies to preserve the effectiveness of antibiotics through altering just their use. To our knowledge
column5: there has been no attempt to systematically test common chemicals to which pathogenic bacteria are chronically exposed for effects on antibiotic resistance.

column1: Neither reducing the use of antibiotics nor discovery of new ones may prevent the post-antibiotic era. This is because bacteria may be exposed to other non-antibiotic chemicals that predispose them to evolve resistance to antibiotics more quickly. Herbicides are examples of some of the most common non-antibiotic formulations in frequent global use. More research is necessary to see to what extend other different manufactured chemicals may contribute to this effect. Moreover
column2: depending on how the manufactured chemicals are used
column3: or how they move through the waste stream
column4: there may be combinatorial effects caused by mixtures of different products. Future work should take into account likely combinations as well as different ways that microbes could be exposed to chemical products.

column1: 4|10.7717/peerj.188|Transformative optimisation of agricultural land use to meet future food demands| Koh  Lian Pin |2013-10-24|"By 2050

column11: for a farmer who is currently growing barley
column12: maize
column13: wheat and irrigated rice on his land

column16: such that individual units of different crops within a commodity group (e.g.
column17: cereals or vegetable oil) are mutually substitutable. Therefore
column18: we illustrate our approach by optimising land use within each of two groups of essential and fungible food crops: cereals (barley
column19: maize
column20: millet
column21: rice
column22: sorghum and wheat) and oilseeds (soy
column23: cottonseed

column1: We assessed geospatial information on current land-use and crop-yield for these crops at the farmland scale across 173 countries (). We based our analyses on a published global geospatial dataset at 5 arc-minute resolution (∼10 × 10 km grid cell) that depicts
column2: for the year 2000
column3: the proportion of harvested area and actual yield reported for each crop in each grid cell (). We overlaid these data to produce a new data layer of intersected polygons (i.e.
column4: land areas sharing unique geospatial information on observed yield for each crop; referred to as farmlands in text). For cereals
column5: these data encompass a total area of 651 million ha (∼42% of Earth’s total arable and permanent croplands) () and comprise 788
column6: 557 data polygons (polygon mean area = 826 ± 4.1 ha [±standard error]); for oilseeds
column7: these data encompass a total area of 184 million ha and comprise 426
column8: 000 data polygons (mean area = 433 ± 2.9 ha).

column1: We carried out a three-step procedure to estimate optimised crop production within each farmland (). First
column2: we established a baseline of current total production of cereal or oilseed crops within each farmland. We did so by multiplying harvested area with observed yield of each cereal or oilseed crop (). Unlike cereals
column3: whereby cereal grain is the prime economically-important product
column4: oilseeds are produced for both oil and meal. We calculated vegetable oil and protein meal production amounts by multiplying crop production with an oil- or meal-conversion factor (derived from 2008/09 data on global crop
column5: oil and meal production) ().

column1: Second
column2: we identified an optimal cereal or oilseed crop within each farmland. The optimal cereal crop was the one with the highest observed yield within each farmland. In identifying an optimal oilseed crop
column3: we assessed relative productivity based on the combined quantity of oil and meal produced. Given that global demand for protein meal is higher than that for vegetable oil
column4: in optimising for oilseed production
column5: we ascribed meal a relative weightage of 1.77 tons for every ton of oil produced (derived from 2008/09 data on global crop
column6: oil and meal consumption) ().

column1: Third
column2: we simulated land use optimisation by converting each farmland to a monoculture of the identified optimal cereal or oilseed crop. We calculated the resultant cereal or oilseed production for each optimised farmland by multiplying total harvested area with observed yield of the optimal crop. We calculated the benefits of land use optimisation by comparing this new production volume with the baseline production prior to optimisation.

column1: In our analyses we made the following assumptions. First
column2: we assumed that the reported yield of each crop is uniform within each farmland (mean area = 826 ± 4.1 ha for cereals and 433 ± 2.9 ha for oilseeds). If parts of a farmland had substantially lower (or higher) than the reported yield for the optimal crop
column3: we would have overestimated (or underestimated) the benefits of land use optimisation. Second
column4: we assumed that site-specific biophysical
column5: economic and technological constraints to production are also uniform at the scale of each farmland
column6: such that optimisation of a farmland for any optimal crop would not be limited by
column7: for example
column8: variations in water scarcity or soil nutrient levels across different parts of a farmland.

column1: Globally
column2: our strategy resulted in net increases in annual production of cereal and oilseed crops from 1.9 billion to 2.9 billion tons (46%)
column3: and from 427 million to 481 million tons (13%)
column4: respectively
column5: without any change in total land area harvested for cereals (651 million ha) or oilseeds (184 million ha) (–). Accordingly
column6: annual production of vegetable oil and protein meal (the primary products of oilseeds) increased from 86 million to 94 million tons (10%)
column7: and from 176 million to 228 million tons (29%)

column9: and to 3 billion tons by 2050 (including its use as animal feed) (). As such

We recognise that besides productivity

column1: The specialisation of production systems implies homogenisation of farms and agricultural landscapes. Yet some farmers might prefer to maintain multiple crops for various reasons

column1: Many government policies aim to reduce greenhouse gas emissions

column3: regulating emissions from industrial sources
column4: and encouraging energy-efficient practices. These policies can lead to a reduction in greenhouse gas emissions
column5: which can result in improved air quality. However
column6: some policies aimed to reducing greenhouse gas emissions can also have unintended consequences for air quality. For example
column7: policies that incentivize the use of biofuels may increase the production of crops
column8: leading to increased emissions from agricultural machinery and fertilizers.Moreover
column9: some governmental policies may focus primarily on reducing greenhouse gas emissions and may not take into account their impact on air quality. For instance
column10: policies that promote the use of diesel engines in public transportation to reduce greenhouse gas emissions may lead to increased emissions of particulate matter
column11: a harmful air pollutant. Therefore
column12: it is essential to develop policies that consider both their impact on greenhouse gas emissions and air quality. This requires a comprehensive approach that considers various factors such as the source of emissions
column13: the technology used
column14: and the overall environmental impact. In this way

column1: Ecological niche models are one of the most widely employed methods in ecological studies for identifying the relationship between habitat presence/availability and environmental variables that influence species presence (; ). column1: This study aims to develop ecological niche models of the Andean Ibis throughout its identified geographic range and cover the following: (1) identification of the environmental variables which significantly influence the presence of the species; (2) generation of potential distribution maps based on the developed model; and (3) identification of distribution overlap with protected areas and the existing human threats impacting the potential species distribution. The results of this study enhance the understanding of the ecological requirements of the Andean Ibis and its current species distribution. This study also identifies potential protection areas and human threats to the Andean Ibis that can aid conservation initiatives and decision-making processes.

The scope of the study was restricted to the countries where the presence of the Andean Ibis has been reported (; ; ; ) and the Andean region where previous investigations on the ecology and biology of the species have been conducted (; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; see ).

column1: A path analysis was conducted using structural equation modelling to determine the likely pathways that link anemone size to social group size and structure. column2: as in the case of the relationship between anemone and anemonefish

column4: path analysis allows exploration of the strength of interactions between many interacting factors in a potentially complex model (). Therefore

column1: The path analysis in this study was conducted in R using the Lavaan package and devised using the results from the individual regressions as a starting point. column1: There is increasing evidence that shallow water anemones experience severe bleaching after warm water events (; ). Bleaching not only reduced size and abundance of anemones ()
column2: but further resulted in lower egg production and recruitment of anemone fish (). Higher temperatures have also been linked to breeding disruption
column3: egg degradation
column4: and possible connectivity among anemonefish populations (

column1: This study suggests that the surface area of the host anemone and the length of the largest individual are two critical factors defining the social group size and structure of A. percula. Variation in group structure along spatial gradients such as depth or distance from the coast may also be explained by the effects of these gradients on host anemone size. These results highlight the critical roles of habitat patch size and social hierarchy in governing breeding population size and access to limited resources in these highly specialized fishes. Additional experimental work is needed to fully resolve the direct and indirect effects of anemone size and the size of the largest individual on group size and structure
column2: and how the behavior of this species will be shaped by increasing stresses on the anemone habitat.

column1: The study of obligatory bacterial endosymbionts is complicated by the fact that they cannot be cultured in the laboratory and that only molecular techniques are useful in their detection. Also
column2: only DNA of bacteria that make up at least 1% of the host bacterial community can typically be amplified with broad-range universal primers (; ; ). More sensitive genus- or species-specific primers are required to overcome these problems ().

column1: Temporary ponds are typically shallow aquatic ecosystems that dry out at unpredictable moments due to changing weather conditions. Despite drying
column2: they are often home to diverse aquatic communities of species that have evolved ways to survive the dry periods (). Host-endosymbiont relationships in freshwater invertebrates from such systems
column3: as well as their geographical and ecological distribution have hardly been studied (). These fragile habitats are undergoing a dramatic decline worldwide since they are prone to land alterations and destructions by human activity and climatic changes ().

column1: Invertebrates from temporary ponds are generally characterized by rapid growth and maturation
column2: short-life spans
column3: small sizes
column4: and high reproductive output. All large branchiopod crustaceans (clam
column5: fairy and tadpole shrimps) and many cladocerans
column6: copepods and ostracods are temporary pond specialists. They all have the ability to produce resting (dormant or diapausing) eggs (cysts) that can withstand extended droughts in a state of dormancy (; ).

column1: So far there are no data showing that bacterial endosymbionts of freshwater or marine animals manipulate their host reproduction mode. However
column2: it seems probable that inhabiting temporary ponds could be facilitated in many species by the asexual reproduction (e.g.
column3: ameiotic parthenogenesis) or a modification of the sexual reproduction mode (e.g.
column4: androdioecy as well as masculinization
column5: feminization) associated with bacterial endosymbionts such as Wolbachia (). With this background
column6: we attempted to identify Wolbachia (the most prevalent bacterial endosymbiont of invertebrates) and other related bacteria in taxonomically diverse crustacean species from astatic freshwater ponds using newly designed non-degenerate primers to amplify the bacterial 16S rRNA gene fragment. However
column7: due to the fact that the term “symbiont” in the comprehensive meaning concerns both endosymbiont as well as pathogenic bacteria (since the host-symbiont interactions refer to mutualism

column1: Most aquatic species from temporary waters have a highly specialized life history that allows them to deal with periodic drying (). Many of the species produce drought resistant stages that enter a state of dormancy
column2: which can survive for extended periods of time in the absence of water (). On the individual level
column3: organisms are often time-stressed to complete their life cycle and produce dormant stages before their habitat dries. Under such environmental conditions
column4: shifts in reproductive strategies towards unisexual life cycles seem to be evolutionary beneficial and have been reported by other researchers (; ; ; ). In terrestrial insects
column5: endosymbiotic bacteria such as Wolbachia have been shown to drive such shifts (reviewed in ). However
column6: so far there is a lack of studies on endosymbionts in invertebrates inhabiting temporary waters. Nevertheless
column7: with current high-throughput metagenome analyses
column8: it is possible to study the microbiome and its diversity with a simple probe. This technique is also very useful to identify bacterial symbionts. However

column6: ; ; ). Moreover
