column1: 9|10.7717/peerj.17|Timing of molt of barn swallows is delayed in a rare Clock genotype| Saino  Nicola |2013-2-12|"Organisms are selected to match the timing of life stages to seasonal variation in ecological conditions. A major extrinsic driver of synchronization of phenological or physiological events
column2: such as breeding or migration
column3: to seasonal fluctuations in environmental conditions is variation in photoperiod (; ; ; ; ; ). Rhythmicity of circannual life-cycles can be enforced by so called “circadian clocks” that can sense variation in day/night cycles and cause shifts in physiology and behaviour by triggering a cascade of photoperiodic responses (; ; ). Genes responsible for regulation of such circadian biochemical oscillators are phylogenetically highly conserved and their mechanism of action has been well described (). Clock genes
column4: in particular
column5: seem to play a role in the photoperiodic responses of diverse invertebrates and vertebrates
column6: including migration in fish and breeding in birds (; ; )
column7: but the studies that have investigated photoperiodism in relation to Clock in the wild are still sparse.

column1: A domain of Clock genes which is functionally relevant to photoperiodism is the carboxyl-terminal polyglutamine repeat (Poly-Q)
column2: as the number of Poly-Q repeats is known to affect circadian rhythms in mice (; ). In the Chinhook salmon (Onchorhynchus tshawytscha) latitudinal variation in Clock allele frequencies parallels that of spring migration phenology (; ). Among birds
column3: timing of breeding of the resident/short-distance migratory blue tit (Cyanistes caeruleus) is associated with Clock polymorphism and a cline in ClkpolyQcds allele frequency exists whereby the number of Poly-Q repeats increases with latitude (; ). In the long-distance migratory barn swallow (Hirundo rustica) breeding date is also predicted by Clock genotype (). In all these instances
column4: late phenology has been found to be associated with an increase in the number of Poly-Q Clock terminal repeats. However
column5: other studies of birds (e.g. on great tits
column6: Parus major; swallows of the genus Tachycineta) have found very low polymorphism at Clock and no relationship between Clock and breeding phenology (; ). As huge variation in Clock polymorphism exists even among closely related species (e.g. Tachycineta swallow; blue tit and great tit) (; ; ; )
column7: difficulties in identifying an association between phenology and Clock may in some species stem from rarity of phenologically deviant
column8: potentially counter-selected genotypes
column9: in combination with large environmental “noise” in breeding phenological data.

column1: It has been suggested that photoperiod may have no or minor role in synchronizing circannual rhythms at the tropics where limited oscillations in photoperiod occur through the year (e.g. ; ; see ). On the other hand
column2: even minor changes in photoperiod can be sensed by tropical birds and cause for example gonadal maturation (; see also ;  for effects of tropical photoperiod on other vertebrates)
column3: suggesting that photoperiod can act as a zeitgeber of circannual rhythms also at low latitudes. In addition
column4: at the tropics information from daytime light intensity may be integrated with that of daylength or time of sunrise and sunset to serve as a circannual zeitgeber (; ; ; see also ). However
column5: we are aware of no study where the association between Clock and photoperiodic events has been analyzed at the tropics in vertebrates.

column1: Annual life-cycles are often cadenced by a regular succession of fundamental activities which can be reciprocally constrained by time and/or resource limitation (). The evolution and expression of individual photoperiodic traits therefore occur under the limits set by competing activities. For example
column2: during any single year
column3: migratory birds must switch from breeding to plumage molting and migration
column4: which are all highly demanding activities
column5: both in terms of resources and time (; ). Although in some cases it may be enforced by time constraints (see ; ; )
column6: overlap between molt and migration or breeding can be detrimental to individual performance and viability because of costliness or direct negative reciprocal interference of these activities
column7: as is the case when molt of wing feathers impairs flight performance and thus migration (; ; ; ; ; ; ). As a result of such reciprocal constraints
column8: any shift in timing of these activities can carry-over on the following events in the annual routine (; ). Hence
column9: an association between timing of any specific activity and genotype may arise either as a direct genetic effect on that particular activity or as a consequence of a constraint
column10: set by a genetic effect on a different activity
column11: and mediated by limiting availability of time or resources.

column1: Circannual rhythmicity of breeding
column2: migration and molt in birds is at least partly under the control of photoperiod (; ; ). The timing and speed of plumage molt
column3: in particular
column4: has been shown to be controlled by photoperiod in a number of studies of diverse species
column5: particularly from temperate latitudes (; ; ; ; ). Importantly
column6: large heritability estimates of timing of molt in birds have been found
column7: implying large genetic variance in this trait at least in the few species that have been studied so far (; ). To the best of our knowledge
column8: however
column9: no study has directly analyzed the association between variation in timing of photoperiodic molt processes and Clock
column10: which is known to be a major mechanism mediating photoperiodic responses.

column1: In this study of barn swallows (Hirundo rustica)
column2: our aim thus was to start filling this gap of knowledge by analyzing the progress of molt in the African winter quarters in free-living individuals that were subsequently genotyped at Clock and sexed using molecular techniques. We expected Clock polymorphism to be low (; ). Because female barn swallows with a rare (Q7/Q8) genotype were found to have delayed breeding () and
column3: in general
column4: relatively “long alleles” (e.g. Q8) are expected to be associated with delayed photoperiodic activities (see above)
column5: we had the directional prediction that Q7/Q8 individuals delayed molt relative to the other genotypes. This was expected either because of consistently delayed photoperiodic response by this genotype in both breeding and molt
column6: or because of carry-over effects of timing of breeding on timing of molt (or vice versa)
column7: given the presumably tight annual routines of this long-distance migrant that has up to three breeding attempts during a single breeding season and may take up to 135 days to complete molt (; ; ; ; ).

column1: Because the natural and sexual selection forces acting on males or females can differ
column2: sex is an additional factor expected to affect circannual routines
column3: and thus sex-specific phenology in e.g. migration or molt (; ; ; ). Thus
column4: in a companion study () we investigated variation in timing of molt and showed that no difference in timing of molt between males and females exists for wing
column5: tail or crown feathers.

column1: The barn swallow is a small (ca. 18 g)
column2: long-distance migratory
column3: aerially insectivorous passerine bird. Individuals breeding in Europe winter in sub-Saharan Africa
column4: exhibiting significant migratory connectivity (i.e. individuals breeding in the same geographical area tend also to winter in the same area) (; ; ). A single annual molt of remiges (wing feathers) and rectrices (tail feathers) occurs
column5: mostly in the African winter quarters (; ; ; ; ; ). Primary wing feathers
column6: which are at the focus of the present study because they are the most aerodynamically important
column7: are molted sequentially starting from the innermost one (P1). Barn swallows start arriving in Europe by mid-March but late individuals can arrive as late as May–June. Females tend to arrive later than males but the difference in arrival date is small (ca. 5 days) (). Autumn migration in Europe mostly occurs in September–October.

column5: females with a rare (Q7/Q8) genotype have been observed to delay breeding
column6: with negative consequence on seasonal reproductive success. In addition

column9: phenologically deviant genotype ().

column1: Our major finding was that rare Q7/Q8 heterozygous barn swallows at the Clock gene delayed molt relative to the other genotypes. This result emerged despite very small size of the sample of Q7/Q8 that was obtained from an otherwise large sample of birds.

In a companion study we showed that timing of molt as measured at four plumage regions did not differ between males and females () and this finding was confirmed in the present analyses where we focused on primary wing feathers only and that were based on a slightly different sample. We carefully considered potential sources of bias arising from the conditions under which sampling was conducted. Several arguments against potential confounding effects arising from sex-related
column2: pre-migratory movements that might have occurred by mid-February
column3: well before the start of migration
column4: are presented in the Methods as well as in . As far as the evidence of delayed molt by Q7/Q8 is specifically concerned
column5: a bias could have arisen from sampling effects only if all of the following conditions were simultaneously met: (1) Q7/Q8 individuals had markedly bimodal timing of molt whereby some (a sample of which we could capture) delay molt while others complete molt very early
column6: so that mean molt schedules is similar to that of the other genotypes
column7: and no Q7/Q8 individuals with intermediate molt phenotype occur; (2) pre-migratory movements occur already by mid-February and result in a non-negligible proportion of birds leaving the roost (but see above); and (3) Q7/Q8 individuals were more likely to leave the roost than the other genotypes. Condition (1) and (3) are obviously very unlikely as it is difficult to imagine how such a sharply bimodal frequency distribution of molt phenotypes could arise and be associated with Clock genotype and why such a differential proneness to leave that particular roost should exist among Clock genotypes. Condition (2) is also unlikely to be met according to the arguments we presented in the Methods (see also ). Hence
column8: we are convinced that sampling effects did not confound the present results.

column1: Although photoperiod is deemed the major extrinsic factor ultimately controlling seasonal activities of birds
column2: including molt (; )
column3: no previous study had investigated variation in molt schedules in relation to polymorphism at genes for photoperiodic response. In barn swallows
column4: the single annual molt of wing and tail feathers normally occurs in sub-Saharan Africa
column5: at tropical latitudes (; ; ). Although the role of photoperiod in controlling molt in the tropics may be questioned
column6: birds at the tropics have been shown to respond physiologically to even minor changes in photoperiod ()
column7: and direct evidence for a control of molt and gonadal rhythms by daytime length at low latitudes has been already provided (). In addition
column8: it has been recently shown that synchronization of circannual rhythms at the tropics can be achieved by change in time of sunrise and sunset (). Thus
column9: molt may also be regulated by response to photoperiod and/or daylight intensity variation in barn swallows wintering at the tropics (). Present findings may thus hint at an involvement of Clock genes in decoding photoperiodic/daylight intensity information at the time of molt and thus in giving a cadence to its progress. A potentially concomitant mechanism is that Clock affects timing of other photoperiodic activities that occur earlier at higher latitudes
column10: such as reproduction and initiation of autumn migration. Because of time constraints
column11: these effects may then carry-over on timing of arrival to the wintering grounds and winter molt.

column1: Remarkably
column2: Q7/Q8 individuals not only molted later but were also found to delay breeding in a previous study of an Italian population (). In fact
column3: delay in breeding was observed only among females
column4: as expected because in birds females have ample control over timing of breeding (; ). The delay in molt by Q7/Q8 individuals that could be estimated here based on ptilochronological information on P7 (ca. 8–9 days) is similar in magnitude to the delay that was observed in breeding date of Q7/Q8 females compared to the other genotypes (ca. 13 days).

column1: Hence
column2: Q7/Q8 is apparently associated with a behavioural syndrome that entails delay in both breeding and molt. The cascade of causation between Clock
column3: and breeding and molt phenology
column4: however
column5: is uncertain: owing to tight annual routines
column6: delayed breeding may cause molt retardation or
column7: conversely
column8: delayed molt may cause late migration and thus breeding. Finally
column9: Clock genotype may have an independent effect on both timing of breeding and molt.

column1: In theory
column2: the association between Clock and molt could be due to a second gene linked to Clock. However
column3: the large body of evidence showing Clock involvement in photoperiodic responses in several
column4: phylogenetically distant organisms (see above) which are unlikely to share syntenic chromosomes makes this hypothesis unlikely. A partly alternative interpretation of the present findings is that Clock has an indirect role in controlling molt by determining circadian activity rhythms
column5: including for example foraging behaviour.

column1: Barn swallows from diverse western and central European regions converge to winter in Nigeria
column2: and in the Boje area in particular
column3: as shown by analysis of ring recoveries (P Micheloni
column4: unpublished data). Hypothetically
column5: a spurious association between molt scores and Clock genotype could result from latitudinal variation in both Clock allele frequencies and molt schedules. This interpretation
column6: however
column7: is very unlikely to be correct given the lack of geographical variation in Clock gene frequencies that emerges from two studies of six distant geographic populations and from unpublished information on an additional northern European population (; ; N Saino
column8: M Caprioli & AP Møller
column9: unpublished data).

column1: Finally
column2: a different interpretation is that our results are driven by selection against Q7/Q8. In a previous study it has been demonstrated that the frequency of Q7/Q8 is larger among yearlings than older individuals (). Because yearlings molt later
column3: it is possible that delayed molt of Q7/Q8 results from a larger frequency of Q7/Q8 in the yearlings fraction of the population. In this perspective
column4: the present findings suggest that delayed molt may concur in determining reduced viability of rare Q7/Q8 individuals. This interpretation is also unlikely to be correct
column5: however
column6: because we observed the same pattern of differences in molt scores among genotypes when we considered the subsample of individuals that were still actively molting their wing feathers
column7: which was likely formed mainly by yearlings ().

column1: Admittedly
column2: the present results have little specific bearing for the study of molt phenology of barn swallow populations
column3: because Q8 has been found to be very rare in all populations studied so far. Conversely
column4: the present findings have a potentially major relevance for studies of genetic control of molt and
column5: more generally
column6: for studies of photoperiodism and regulation of seasonality in birds
column7: including the studies on phenotypically plastic/microevolutionary phenological responses to current climate change (see  for a review). While low frequency of Q8 in our model population may be maintained by selection ()
column8: higher polymorphism at Clock in other species (; ; ) may be maintained by selection for variation in photoperiodic response strategies due to e.g. spatial or temporal variation in selection pressures
column9: therefore affording ample opportunity to study the effect of Clock on timing of molt.

column1: Using molecular techniques to identify sex
column2: this study of barn swallows achieved a novel finding of potentially general relevance for the study of the evolution and the control of molt and migration strategies in birds
column3: and potentially in other organisms where the Clock genetic machinery of control of photoperiodic responses is conserved. We uncovered an association between timing of molt and Clock genotype and found that rare Q7/Q8 individuals
column4: which are known to delay breeding
column5: also show delayed annual molt in winter. The pleiotropic temporal shift that Q7/Q8 may cause in fundamental life-history components has obvious general implications for the understanding of regulation of seasonality and annual routines particularly in long-distance migratory species with complex annual cycles
column6: and might disclose research avenues particularly in species exhibiting larger polymorphism at Clock genes.

column1: 10|10.7717/peerj.103|Real-time bioacoustics monitoring and automated species identification| Aide  T. Mitchell |2013-7-16|"Ecologists
column2: conservation biologists
column3: and park and resource managers are expected to make decisions to mitigate or manage the threats of climate change and the high rates of species loss. Unfortunately
column4: they rarely have the information needed to make informed decisions because our understanding of most biological systems is based on very limited spatial and temporal coverage. In most biomes
column5: data collection
column6: particularly of the fauna
column7: is concentrated in a few sites
column8: and this highly aggregated distribution of information
column9: limits our ability to understand large-scale ecological processes and to properly manage fauna in large areas (; ; ; ; ; ). Furthermore
column10: long-term information is needed to understand the implications of land and climate change on biological systems (). From both a conceptual and management perspective there is an urgent challenge to increase biological data collection over large areas and through time.

column1: What is needed are long-term population and distribution data for thousands of species across their range. For some economically important species (e.g.
column2: salmon) we have long-term data ()
column3: but for the majority of species the data is limited to a few years and a few populations. Other areas of science
column4: such as meteorology and land change science have taken advantage of new technologies
column5: such as inexpensive sensors
column6: wireless communication
column7: and satellite images to expand their data sets to the global scale (). Given the urgency of the biodiversity crisis
column8: it is essential that we take advantage of all available tools to improve biodiversity monitoring to thousands of sites around the world.

column1: Traditionally
column2: biodiversity is assessed using a variety of methods that are generally costly
column3: limited in space and time (e.g.
column5: and most importantly
column6: they rarely include a permanent record. Furthermore
column7: most fauna monitoring protocols require the presence of experts in the field because data are often acquired through indirect cues (e.g.
column8: animal vocalizations). This creates various problems. First
column9: in terms of acoustic identification
column10: there are few experts that can confidently identify animals based on vocalization
column11: yet there are many studies that could benefit from this information. Second
column12: experts vary in their abilities to correctly identify species
column13: and this leads to observer bias (). Additionally
column14: these protocols often collect data over a very limited spatial and temporal scale
column15: and these constraints reduce the researcher’s ability to understand the dynamic patterns of animal populations. Furthermore
column16: most traditional sampling methodologies do not include a permanent record and
column17: thus
column18: there is no way to validate the data.

column1: In contrast
column2: automated digital recording systems can monitor animal populations 24 h a day
column3: every day of the year
column4: in stations across a variety of habitats simultaneously
column5: and all recordings can be permanently stored (; ; ; ; ; ; ). This type of monitoring can be effective because in most ecosystems a large proportion of the fauna emits sounds for a variety of reasons including inter- and intraspecific communication
column6: orientation ()
column7: and detection and localization of prey and predators ()
column8: but most importantly
column9: these sounds are species specific.

column1: Automated data collection systems can collect an overwhelming amount of data
column2: creating problems with data management and analysis (). To help solve these problems
column3: researchers have developed algorithms to automate species identification of vocalizations of bats (; ; )
column4: whales (; ; ; ; )
column5: dolphins ()
column6: insects (; )
column7: and birds and amphibians (; ; ; ; ). A limitation with this approach is that most users do not have the programming or math skills to develop these algorithms. Furthermore
column8: most projects have only produced algorithms for one or a few target species.

column1: In this manuscript
column2: we describe the acoustical component of the Automated Remote Biodiversity Monitoring Network (ARBIMON)
column3: a novel combination of hardware and software (cyberinfrastructure) for automating data acquisition
column4: data management
column5: and identification of multiple species of amphibians
column6: birds
column7: insects
column8: and mammals. The main objectives of the manuscript are to demonstrate: (1) how detailed
column9: long-term acoustical data can be collected and managed
column10: (2) how users can create species-specific identification algorithms with no machine learning experience
column11: and (3) how the information created by the system can be used to better understand the activity patterns and long-term population trends of the fauna. To demonstrate this system we present data on the activity patterns of nine species (4-amphibians
column12: 2-birds
column13: 1-mammals
column14: and 2-insects) from an herbaceous wetland in Puerto Rico and a lowland tropical forest in La Selva Biological Station in Costa Rica.

column1: The cyberinfrastructure for collecting and storing the audio recordings includes: (1) the acoustic permanent station
column2: (2) the field base station
column3: and (3) the ARBIMON server (). The permanent monitoring station includes an iPod Touch (2G) with a pre-amplifier
column4: which is powered with a 50 W solar panel
column5: voltage converter
column6: a router
column7: and a 12 V car battery (). A microphone with a frequency response range from 20 Hz to 20 kHz is attached to the iPod via the pre-amplifier. The battery
column8: pre-amplifier
column9: voltage converter
column10: router
column11: and iPod are housed in a water/shock proof case. The pre-amplifier has three gain settings. The gain was set at the intermediate level. Informal experiments suggest that this recording systems will detect the common coqui (Eleutherodactylus coqui) in a forest habitat up to approximately 50 m
column12: suggesting that for this species the sampling area would be approximately 1 ha. An application in the iPod controls the duration of the recording and the time between recordings. Presently
column13: it is programmed to record 1 min of audio every 10 min for a total of 144 1-min recordings per day. The recording schedule can be easily modified depending on the objectives of each project. The application generates a filename for each recording
column14: instructs the software to make the recording
column15: and sends the recording using Secure Copy (SCP) to a MacMini computer at the base station. These files are forwarded by wireless communication from the iPod to a router that is connected to a directional antenna (Avalan Wireless 900 MHz Radio Ethernet extender)
column16: which forwards the file to the receiving antenna that is connected to the base station computer. Our experience shows that this radio/antenna system can maintain a strong connection at a distance of 2 km through vegetation and up to 40 km if there is line-of-sight between the antennas.

column1: The main functions of the base station are to provide internet access
column2: store all data files locally on a 1Tb external hard drive
column3: compress the recordings to reduce the file size
column4: and to forward these files to the project server at the University of Puerto Rico (). These functions are activated every time a recording is received via a folder action and an Applescript. The script converts the recording from stereo to mono
column5: and compresses it using flac format (an open source alternative for lossless compression and decompression of audio files
column6: http://flac.sourceforge.net/)
column7: stores the file locally
column8: and sends a copy to the project server. The project server
column9: an Apple Xserve (2.8 GHz Quad-Core Intel Xeon
column10: 4–12 GB 800 MHz DDR2 FB-DIMM) running MacOS X 10.5.4 Server
column11: Apache 2.2
column12: Php 5.2.5 and MySQL5.0.45
column13: is used for data storage
column14: data backup
column15: data management
column16: analysis
column17: and web hosting. The server also includes a Promise VTrak E610f RAID Subsystem with 12TB configured as a RAID6 for a total of 9TB of available space.

column1: A normalized open source database schema using the MySQL database system is the cornerstone of our web application. The database is general enough so that it can be used for any acoustic project
column2: allowing researchers to work with the data of their specific projects

column1: The centerpieces of the design are the sensors that acquire the data and the methods used to process the data
column2: allowing our system to handle a variety of sensors
column3: use different configurations of these sensors
column4: and to create an efficient way to relate the data with the type of sensor and configuration. Additionally
column5: this database architecture provides easy access to the data at different points in the processing path. This was accomplished by handling the data as both input and output

column7: name

column10: name

column1: Although anyone can view and listen to the recordings on the project website
column2: only approved users can analyze or annotate recordings. To manage projects and users within projects we have developed an administrative interface
column3: which has three sections: administration
column4: project creation and management
column5: and global security. The administration component maintains the databases of all projects
column6: keeps a log of all users’ activity
column7: and documents any security breach or system failure. The project creation and management component allows a new user to (1) create a project
column8: (2) specify site names
column9: location
column10: and time zone
column11: and (3) assign users with different privilege levels of to the project. The global security component manages users and their privileges.

column1: When the audio files arrive to the project server
column2: they are archived
column3: and then sent to a program that extracts the raw data from the wav format to create a spectrogram of the recording. This spectrogram is created using a short-time Fourier transform (STFT) using 512 samples and a Hann window overlapping 256 samples. For one-minute recordings with a sample rate of 44
column4: 100 samples per second each cell of the matrix represents 86 Hz by 0.005 s. This matrix is used to generate the spectrogram image of the recording and is the input for another program that demarks areas of high energy within the recording as regions of interest (ROIs). In addition
column5: an mp3 file is generated using LAME (http://lame.sourceforge.net/) a high quality MPEG Audio Layer III (MP3) encoder licensed under the Lesser General Public License (LGPL). The smaller size of mp3 files makes them more appropriate for the web application
column6: but the quality of the spectrogram or ROIs are not affected because they were generated using the original wav files.

column1: The algorithm to create the regions of interest (ROIs) starts by analyzing the frequency-time matrix to determine the level of background noise within each frequency band. This information is used to define thresholds of audio intensity that the input signals in the recording must surpass to be considered as an acoustic event. For each frequency band
column2: we determine the mean intensity value and keep only the samples that are greater than 10% above the mean. This process greatly reduces the data
column3: making it suitable for storing as a compressed sparse matrix (CSR). We analyze the CSR containing the acoustic events using a depth-first search algorithm to create neighborhoods of pixels into a single region of interest (ROI). Once
column4: the sample is used in a ROI they are removed from the CSR and the algorithm selects another event until all samples that were selected as an acoustic event participates in a ROI. The time and frequency variables that describe the bounding box of each ROI (minimum and maximum frequency
column5: duration
column6: maximum intensity and bandwidth) are the variables that are later used to create the automated species identification algorithms.

column1: To automate species detection
column2: we developed an application that uses Hidden Markov Models (HMMs). The application was designed so that the users can develop their own models using tools to view and listen to their recordings and to create

column4: and validate species-specific identification models. The four major components that make up this interface included: (1) visualizer
column5: (2) species validation
column6: (3) model builder
column7: and (4) model application.

column1: To demonstrate the use of the ARBIMON-acoustic application we created species-specific models for amphibians
column2: birds
column3: mammals
column4: and insects based on recordings from a site in Puerto Rico and a site in Costa Rica. The species were selected to cover a range of taxa with different types of vocalizations. Vocalizations of frogs and birds were confidently identified based on our experience and comparisons with different sources of animal calls. Unfortunately
column5: the two insect species
column6: most likely cicadas
column7: could not be captured and identified

column1: The site in Puerto Rico
column2: Sabana Seca (SS)
column3: is a small (180 ha) wetland near the Caribbean Primate Research Center (CPRC) in Toa Baja
column4: Puerto Rico (18°25′56.01″ N and 66°11′45.62″ W). Typha dominguensis (cattail) is the dominant species in the wetland. This site is the only known locality of Eleutherodactylus juanariveroi (coqui llanero)
column5: an endangered frog species that was recently discovered (). The major motivation for establishing a permanent recording station in Sabana Seca was to improve the information on the calling activity and population dynamics of E. juanariveroi. The station was established in March 2008
column6: and for this study we present the results of species-specific identification models of the endemic frog species
column7: E. juanariveroi
column8: an exotic frog species Rana gryllo (pig frog)
column9: and an unidentified insect (insect #1).

column1: The other study site was La Selva Biological Station (LSBS) in Costa Rica (10°25′ N
column2: 84°01′ W). This reserve encompasses approximately 1
column3: 510 ha of which 64% is primary tropical forest
column4: and contains a high diversity of flora and fauna (). The objective of this project was to conduct broad acoustic monitoring within mature forest for all species that contribute to the acoustic community. For this site
column5: we created species-specific identification models for six species: Tinamus major (great tinamou)
column6: Ramphastos swainsonii (chestnut-mandibled toucan)
column7: Oophaga pumilio (strawberry poison-dart frog)
column8: Diasporus diastema (tink frog)
column9: Alouatta palliata (mantled howler monkey)
column10: and an unidentified insect (insect #2).

column6: Costa Rica

column1: To determine the accuracy and precision of the species identification models we compared the decisions made by the expert (i.e.
column2: validation data set) with the decision made by the models (). The Oophaga pumilio vocalization model had the highest accuracy (99%)
column3: while the model for insect sp#2 had the lowest accuracy (79%). Similarly
column4: the Oophaga pumilio vocalization model had the highest precision (100%)
column5: but the Alouatta palliata model had the lowest precision (76%) due to the high level of false positives. In general
column6: most of the models had relatively low levels of false positives (<5%)
column7: and higher levels of false negatives. For example
column8: the Tinamous major model reported only 1 presence when the vocalization was actually absent (i.e.
column9: false positive)
column10: but 41 times the model reported the species was absent when it was really present (i.e.
column11: false negative). These results suggest that these models are relatively conservative; they rarely confused the species with another
column12: but they do not always detect the species when it is present as determined by an expert through visual and/or aural inspection.

column1: There are two main causes for the false negatives. First

column1: Here we have demonstrated how frequent (sub-hourly) data collection over long time periods (years) can be carried out
column2: and how the data can be managed
column3: archived
column4: and analyzed virtually in real-time. By recording one minute of audio
column5: every 10 min
column6: we were able to achieve fine temporal resolution
column7: covering 24 h a day
column8: seven days of the week over a five year period in Puerto Rico. This fine-scale and long-term temporal sampling
column9: now needs to be matched spatially with many sensors across the landscape.

column1: The detailed and long-term temporal sampling of these sites could not have been accomplished without automating data acquisition
column2: processing
column3: and management. The automation of data collection also provided additional benefits. First
column4: recordings can be inspected visually and aurally in real-time. Recordings from the Sabana Seca station took less than 1 min to be sent from the field
column5: to the base station
column6: and on to the project server where it was processed
column7: stored
column8: and incorporated into the project’s open-access web site. This real-time monitoring can help researchers and managers respond rapidly to important events
column9: particularly when a model that identifies a focal species has been incorporated into the data processing scripts. Another benefit of the real time processing is that we can easily detect any malfunction of the hardware or software by inspecting the recordings
column10: and then respond quickly to limit data loss. The Sabana Seca system collected recordings between 60 and 70% of the time. The major causes of data loss were: (1) loss of power due to extended cloud cover or vegetation growing over the solar panel
column11: (2) loss of power at the base station
column12: and (3) network problems at the base station. Nevertheless
column13: the real cause of missing data was a slow response by our staff to solve these problems. To accelerate the response time
column14: we have developed an application that continuously collects information from each station and generates an alert in the form of an email to the project owner when the station is malfunctioning.

column1: Other benefits of automating data collection include: (1) reduced observer bias and (2) each recording is a verifiable permanent record
column2: equivalent to a museum specimen. Even if observers could stay in the field 24 hr/d throughout the year
column3: there would still be a problem of observer bias (). This is a major limitation especially when it is necessary to sample many sites simultaneously or when data are collected over many years by many different observers. The ability to detect and identify an animal vocalization correctly may require years of experience. But
column4: there can also be high levels of variation among “experts” due to differences in the habitat being sampled
column5: hearing ability
column6: or biases toward certain species (). Another benefit is that each recording is a permanent record
column7: which allows multiple users to review them
column8: leading to more accurate identifications and consequently more accurate estimates of population parameters. All recordings archived in ARBIMON (arbimon.net) are open access
column9: and thus it is the equivalent of an acoustic museum
column10: presently with >1.3 million 1-min recordings.

column1: Our approach is very different in comparison with most other collections of animal vocalizations. For example
column2: the recordings from the Macaulay Library of the Cornell Lab of Ornithology
column3: Xeno-canto
column4: and the Internet Bird Collection are important collections of animal vocalizations and photographs
column5: but their focus is species specific. Furthermore
column6: many species are represented by one or a few recordings. In contrast
column7: our approach is to record the environment (i.e.
column8: soundscape)
column9: frequently and over the long-term. This allows multiple users to take advantage of the recordings. For example
column10: while the initial objective of a project may be to study a specific bird species
column11: the vocalizations of many other species (e.g.
column12: insects
column13: frogs
column14: birds and mammals) are likely to be present. In addition
column15: a soundscape index
column16: an integrated measure of the acoustic environment
column17: can be calculated and measured across time to estimate changes in biodiversity or other factors affecting the acoustic environment (; ). Moreover
column18: given that all recordings will be permanently archived
column19: future users
column20: with new tools and questions will be able to reanalyze these recordings in the future.

column1: Although there are many benefits of a permanent station
column2: the user must consider the costs and other limitations. The initial cost of establishing a permanent recording stations will vary depending on the site and logistics
column3: and could range from approximately US $10
column5: 000. Another important cost is the processing and long-term storage of the audio files. We have estimated the cost at US $0.15 per 1-min recording. Other limitations associated with any monitoring program that depends on audio recordings include: (1) poor or no detection of species or individuals that rarely use acoustic signals for communication (e.g.
column6: females and juvenile)
column7: (2) a single permanent or fixed station will only record biotic activity in a limited radius around the station and this distance will vary among species depending on the sound pressure generated by the calling individual ()
column8: and (3) using models to identify species-specific vocalizations in recordings with varying degrees of intense background noise (e.g.
column9: other species
column10: rain
column11: wind
column12: automobile traffic) could result in misidentifications.

column1: For many studies
column2: presence/absence data or an index of relative abundance can be very useful
column3: but it is not easy to extract this information from thousands of recordings. While some researchers have the programming skills to manage and analyze their recordings
column4: most do not. Typically
column5: researchers resort to listening to a subset of their recordings
column6: which can be very time consuming and leads to a considerable loss of data. In contrast
column7: the ARBIMON-acoustic software allows the user to reduce the time analyzing recordings
column8: while taking advantage of the complete data set. To do this the user must only inspect a subset of the recordings to provide examples of the species-specific vocalization (i.e.
column9: training data) and create the validation data set
column10: which is needed for training the initial model and to evaluate the accuracy and precision of each model
column11: respectively. Our results illustrate that the species-specific identification models created using the ARBIMON-acoustic system worked well for birds
column12: mammals
column13: amphibians and insects
column14: and the models had high levels of accuracy and precision. These models allowed us to process 100
column15: 000s of recordings to generate detailed information on daily and monthly vocalization patterns for these species. Another important feature is that these models can be used in other projects
column16: allowing new users to dedicate their time to producing new models of other vocalizations made by the same species or of other species. Most importantly
column17: these web-based tools greatly simplify the process of extracting useful results for researchers and managers from the raw data (i.e.
column18: recordings)
column19: which should help the users to improve and expand their ecological monitoring programs.

12|10.7717/peerj.15593|Biomes of the world under climate change scenarios: increasing aridity and higher temperatures lead to significant shifts in natural vegetation| Bonannella  Carmelo |2023-6-23|Climate change is one of the biggest threats to human civilization
column2: with slowly accumulating effects and unknown instabilities in front of us and future generations. To assess the potential impacts of climate change on the environment and to help us mitigate and prepare for negative effects
column3: scientists offer predictions of possible futures including global maps of the Earth’s environment in the future (). Global datasets projecting the state of the Earth’s environment include future climate predictions e.g. Representative Concentration Pathways (RCPs) ()
column4: future land use predictions (e.g.
column5: and )
column6: human population scenarios ()
column7: future terrestrial ecosystems maps ()
column8: future ecosystem productivity ()
column9: and future gridded emissions (). Even though the accuracy of these projections in the far future cannot currently be validated
column10: such exercises are deemed useful as they help reveal patterns and assess the impact of scenarios. In essence
column11: there are two main approaches to envision the future state of Earth’s environment (; ):

column1: Process-based mechanistic modeling: simulating evolution of the environment using biophysical process-based Earth System Models (ESM);
Data-based modeling: training predictive models using observations from the past and then extrapolating these models into the future;

Process-based modeling is often preferred by physicists as the relationships between model entities are explicitly defined. Examples of projected changes of land use based on the global Earth System Models are the LUH2 project () and the Lund–Potsdam–Jena managed Land (LPJmL) model (). In the case of data-based modeling
column2: predictions and results of analyses are based on finding relationships between the target property and covariates and then fitting statistical models that are next used to predict values based on unseen combinations of states in feature space. Two common approaches here are: (1) use actual ground observations i.e. monitoring stations to fit spatiotemporal models ()
column3: and (2) use complete Earth observation data cubes and then basically all pixel combinations to visualize and model relationships (). An advantage of the data-based modeling is that it is often computationally less demanding than process-based modeling and it can be extended by adding more covariates (). In addition
column4: process-based modeling requires several assumptions and
column5: in the case of chaotic behaviour or non-linear spatial scaling of features
column6: it is often difficult to produce credible predictions. On the other hand
column7: data-based modeling comes with the risks of producing poor predictions in the extrapolation space and the models are often difficult to interpret (). Yet
column8: strict data-based modeling requires neither subjective parametrization nor model assumptions
column9: and hence it can be considered less complex to start with. It is not to say that the approaches are mutually exclusive and cannot be combined: there is a full spectrum of models from process-based to data-based
column10: which includes hybrid physics-based data-driven models. Different approaches exist in this sense: using data-driven models but constrain the results with boundary conditions derived from physics-based climate models (as suggested by )
column11: including the representation of natural processes in the data-driven model () or using process-based models whose results have been parametrized and calibrated on real data ().

column1: For each pixel we provide class probabilities and prediction errors computed by bootstrapping;
Modeling is done using a consistent set of covariates so that the effects of climate change are controlled purely by the climatic projections.

The paper is divided in four parts: (1) we first describe our predictive mapping framework based on using biome training points (); (2) we evaluate the accuracy of the fitted ensemble model using spatial cross-validation and generate predictions for the three future scenarios; (3) we next aggregate predictions according to the IUCN Global Ecosystem Typology classification system () to make our product comparable with an international standard and (4) we finally highlight the most pronounced changes per continent and biome type.

We modeled the potential distribution of biomes on a global scale for current and future time periods using an ensemble machine learning approach. The model was trained on reference biome data compiled from pollen and fossil reconstructions () along with regional environmental variables describing topography and long-term climatic averages. We used CHELSA climatological data () from the time period 1979–2013 to simulate the baseline potential natural distribution of biomes for the current (2022–2023) time period: since our goal was to model the potential natural vegetation
column2: we tried to predict which PFT-based class of biome would be the dominant one in a specific location based on environmental variables only. Future climatic conditions instead cover the epochs 2041–2060 and 2061–2080. For the future epochs we considered three different climate change scenarios using the concept of “Representative Concentration Pathways” ()

column1: We used an ensemble machine learning approach based on stacked generalization (). Ensemble modeling techniques involve training several independent models with the same input data and then aggregating each of the model outputs into the final predictions. Stacked generalization uses the outputs of the individual models to train an additional model (meta-learner from here on) which then produces the final predictions. We used Random Forests (RF) ()
column2: generalized linear models () with Lasso regularization () and gradient-boosted trees (GBT) () as component models for the ensemble model. To reduce overfitting in the training phase
column3: we used a fivefold spatial cross validation (): the out-of-fold predictions were used to train the meta-learner. Spatial cross validation was implemented by a 100 × 100 km grid and using the tile ID as the blocking variable during the training of the models. We used multinomial logistic regression () as the meta-learner.

column6: no confusion with other classes); in short

column3: from Central Canada to southern Chile and Argentina
column4: including the Caribbean islands) using sequence variation for two mitochondrial genes (COI and 16S). Our results indicate a high level of genetic structure and endemism at a continental scale

We used Artemia-specific primers 1/2COI_Fol-F and 1/2COI_Fol-R () to amplify and sequence a 709 bp fragment of the mitochondrial Cytochrome c Oxidase Subunit I gene (COI). We also amplified and sequenced a 535 bp fragment of the 16S ribosomal RNA gene for a subset of individuals carrying different COI haplotypes to facilitate comparison with other published sequences using primers 16Sar-5′/16Sbr-3′ (). column1: Neighbour Joining (NJ) and Maximum Likelihood (ML) phylogenetic trees were inferred for both COI and 16S gene fragments. NJ trees were constructed using evolutionary distances computed using the Maximum Composite Likelihood method and 1000 bootstrap replicate tests in MEGA5 (). The best-scoring ML trees for COI were estimated using RAxML-VI-HPC v. 7.2.8 () on the CIPRES portal at the San Diego Supercomputer Center (http://www.phylo.org)
column2: optimising free model parameters and executing 1000 rapid bootstraps. Average genetic distances between the main COI lineages – corrected by the K2P + G substitution model – were carried out using MEGA5. Additionally
column3: to identify lineages in our COI dataset
column4: we used the Automatic Barcode Gap Discovery (ABGD) approach () using the webtool (http://wwwabi.snv.jussieu.fr/public/abgd/abgdweb.html).

column1: Intra-population gene diversity Hs (standardized haplotype diversity) for COI was computed using the program RAREFAC () to account for population differences in sampling size. Nucleotide diversity and pairwise ΦST values for COI from all 39 sampled populations were estimated using ARLEQUIN v. 3.1. ().

The significance of correlations between pairwise genetic and geographic distances (isolation-by-distance or IBD patterns) was tested using Mantel tests on IBDWS v.3.21 (). Prior to analyses in this section

column1: We used canonical Redundancy Analysis (RDA) in CANOCO () to estimate the relative contribution of geographic distances
column2: migratory flyways and human introductions on genetic distance between populations. RDA
column3: a multiple linear regression method widely used in community ecology
column4: has recently been applied to infer the role of spatial versus environmental variables in structuring population genetics data (e.g.

Environmental variables were whether the flyway overlapped with the Artemia population (0 or 1 depending on the presence of birds from the Atlantic
column6: Central or Pacific flyways in the area) and introduction history (0 or 1). We modelled spatial variables using latitude and longitude (x and y).

column1: We used the overlap of sampled populations with the three main migratory flyways in America based on two sources (1)  for shorebirds and (2) Birdlife International (extracted from http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/userfiles/file/sowb/flyways/). These data were used as a proxy for bird movements between locations. This approach is likely to be a rough approximation to the probability of bird migration between locations

column3: this would only estimate current bird movements
column4: whereas climate changes since the Pleistocene are likely to have strongly affected bird movements ().

column1: Our analyses revealed that A. franciscana has a strong regional genetic structure in its native distribution range throughout the Americas
column2: with twelve largely allopatric endemic lineages. Such high level of population structure
column3: supported by a very high overall ΦST value
column4: high number of private haplotypes and significant IBD patterns
column5: indicate that the populations studied are not connected by high ongoing gene flow
column6: pointing instead to the effects of genetic drift and persistent founder effects during historical colonisation processes and development of local adaptation (i.e.
column7: the Monopolisation hypothesis; ). The few population pairs with non-significant population differentiation (three lakes from Central Canada
column8: plus one pair from Chile/Argentina) are likely to reflect recent colonisation
column9: rather than ongoing gene flow. Our data also reveals the impact of A. franciscana introductions on the phylogeography of the species
column10: as the lineage including the commercialised SFB and GSL populations has now achieved the widest distribution across the continent
column11: in some cases coexisting – and presumably hybridizing – with pre-existing native populations. In addition
column12: our results suggest that migratory birds have an important role in the colonisation of new habitats and are associated with range expansions both in the history of Artemia colonisation across the Americas
column13: and also at a local scale
column14: where birds facilitate the expansion of introduced lineages.

column1: Our study provides new evidence supporting the key historical role of waterbirds as the main factor shaping the population genetic structure of continental aquatic invertebrates at an intra-continental scale. The patterning of the main phylogenetic lineages
column2: with an Atlantic
column3: Central and Pacific distribution – instead of a North American (Nearctic) vs. South American (Neotropical) division reflecting the recognized zoogeographic regions and the long isolation of the continents (; ) – strongly suggests that historical bird migratory flyways
column4: which occur alongside both the major coasts of this continent
column5: determined the historical spread of A. franciscana genetic lineages. Bird movements also might have allowed the subsequent persistence of this structure by facilitating colonisation along each migratory flyway
column6: which shaped the main East-West division in mitochondrial lineages. RDA showed that the effect of migratory flyways was highly significant
column7: and accounted for 20% of the genetic variation between populations once geographic distance was taken into account
column8: suggesting that the distribution of genetic lineages in A. franciscana is likely to reflect the impact of historical bird flyways on native phylogeographic patterns. In addition
column9: the strong detected IBD pattern suggests that the chances of bird-mediated colonisation are highly distance dependent (see )
column10: although instances of long-distance dispersal and colonisation
column11: for example from Argentina to Chile
column12: or to Colombia from northern Caribbean populations
column13: are also apparent from our data. A corollary of our results is that bird movements must have shown some stability
column14: forming parallel N-S flyways during the time frame of A. franciscana population diversification (i.e.
column15: throughout the Pleistocene) extending into new breeding areas becoming available in the north and new wintering sites in the southern extreme of the continent (). These results are no surprise given the high transport rates of Artemia cysts by waterbirds (; ) and the lack of other dispersal vectors. Migratory waterbirds have existed since the Early Cretaceous (). Although now-extinct migratory mammals were once major vectors of plant dispersal in the Americas ()
column16: the hypersaline habitats used by Artemia are not conducive to dispersal by large mammals.

column1: Our results for A. franciscana agree with other studies in North America pointing to an effect of bird movements on the genetic structure of passively dispersed aquatic invertebrates ()
column2: but extends these findings to the whole of the Americas
column3: and emphasizes the major role of bird movements in facilitating colonisation into new suitable habitats – in agreement with the patterns found in A. franciscana in the invaded Mediterranean range (J Muñoz
column4: A Gómez
column5: J Figuerola
column6: F Amat
column7: C Rico
column8: AJ Green
column9: 2013 unpublished)
column10: and those for A. salina in its native range (). Although at a continental scale our results suggest that bird movements do not promote contemporary gene flow between Artemia populations
column11: such gene flow may still have a role at a local scale or when new areas suitable for colonization become available.

column1: The high level of endemism and population structure in native A. franciscana populations
column2: with low ongoing gene flow and occasional long-distance migration resembles the patterns found in Artemia salina ()
column3: a sexual native Mediterranean brine shrimp species
column4: and in a range of other aquatic passively dispersed taxa including sexual reproduction in their life cycles (; ; ; ; ; ). Our results also expand and confirm the deep phylogenetic breaks found by  in a mtDNA and nDNA study of a more limited number of populations revealing only three lineages. As populations of these passively dispersed organisms can be founded by a small number of propagules
column5: followed by rapid population growth and establishment of large diapausing egg banks
column6: this favours the presence of persistent/long term founder effects
column7: thus reducing the effect of gene flow
column8: possibly reinforced by the development of local adaptation
column9: according to the Monopolisation hypothesis (). Cysts are undoubtedly still regularly dispersed between suitable habitats by waterbirds
column10: but they are unlikely to become established owing to the Monopolisation effects.

column1: Given the range of genetic divergence between lineages (from 2 to 6%) the time frame of their fragmentation can be approximated roughly using a COI molecular clock for other shrimp taxa (1.4% sequence divergence per million year; )
column2: which translates into 1.4–4.3 million years of divergence (reaching the Pliocene)
column3: between A. franciscana lineages. Even a faster rate of 2% per million years will result on pre-Pleistocene divergence times between the main lineages. Therefore
column4: a contribution of Pliocene/Pleistocene climatic oscillations to population fragmentations after range expansions across the continent can be inferred from our data
column5: possibly allowing survival of lineages in separate geographical areas including Caribbean islands and areas in North and South America. Mexico has the highest lineage richness
column6: with five out of the 12 COI lineages being native to this country – including lineages from both Pacific and Atlantic clades. These findings suggest that this region is likely to have supported separate refugia during climatically adverse periods. The occurrence of a highly divergent Central Canadian prairie lineage was unexpected
column7: as an ice sheet covered this area during the last glacial maximum (). However
column8: the 16S data from GenBank suggest that this lineage also occurs in more southern areas in central USA (Nebraska)
column9: where it may have survived south of the ice sheets
column10: and then undergone postglacial colonisation of Central Canada.

column3: long-distance colonisation events must have also occurred. For example

column6: where most of the haplotypes were found in Argentinean populations (see also ) but four of them (three shared across populations) were found in a Chilean Altiplano population (Salar de Llamara)

column9: with private haplotypes distantly related to Caribbean lineages

column13: and have often been implicated in the dispersal of plants between North and South America

column1: The high genetic richness found in our COI analyses

column1: 1. Voting: Token holders in the network can vote to elect delegates from a pool of candidates. The elected delegates then take on the responsibility of validating transactions and adding blocks to the blockchain.
2. Block production: The elected delegates are responsible for creating new blocks. They take turns in producing blocks in a round-robin fashion or based on a predefined schedule.
3. Consensus: Consensus is achieved when a supermajority of elected delegates agree on the validity of a transaction and its inclusion in the blockchain.

column2: it is still considered decentralized because token holders have the power to vote and change the delegates if they are dissatisfied with their performance.

column6: efficiency

15|10.7717/peerj.6107|Climatic niche shift and possible future spread of the invasive South African Orchid Disa bracteata in Australia and adjacent areas| Konowalik  Kamil |2018-12-20|The study of biological invasions has been called “one of the hottest current topics in ecology” ()
column2: mostly because together with habitat destruction and climate change the spread of non-native organisms is considered to be a major threat to biodiversity. In Australasia
column3: invasive (= not native) species are a major problem; for example
column4: the number of species of plants reported as introduced
column5: that have been released and may or may not have become naturalized in Australia exceeds 28
column6: 000 (). A subset of them have naturalized and are a threat to the rich endemic flora of that continent (; ; ) as the populations are self-sustaining and spreading without human assistance. In addition
column7: the eradication of invasive weeds is costly (). Governmental agencies and private landowners invest large amounts of money in controlling the spread of weeds using various methods to assess risk of their further spread. One of the emerging techniques is to evaluate the potential future ranges of invasive species using climatic niche modeling ().

column1: In this research we implemented modeling approach to evaluate the possible further spread of invasive orchid species
column2: Disa bracteata Sw.
column3: which was first reported in Australia relatively recently
column4: in 1944. This plant is listed in the Global Compendium of Weeds (GCW; http://www.hear.org/gcw) and is the only weedy representative of the mainly sub-Saharan genus Disa P.J. Disa bracteata is classified in the GCW as an environmental weed (species that invade native ecosystems; () or a naturalized species (self-sustaining and spreading populations but not necessarily affecting the environment; ; ). D. bracteata is a South African endemic plant found in both the Eastern and Western Cape ()
column5: where it is widespread and common
column6: especially in areas subject to mild disturbance. In undisturbed vegetation it is somewhat less frequent. D. bracteata is included on the Red List of South African plants as a taxon of Least Concern (; ). In the mid-20th-century it was brought to Australia where it became naturalized (). This orchid was first formally recorded near Bacchus Marsh
column7: west of Melbourne
column8: in 1944 (; ) and since 1945 additional reports came from areas of the Great Southern Region in Western Australia (vicinity of Albany). Later it was also recorded in South Australia (in 1988) and Victoria (in 1994). Recently it was found in Tasmania (Viridans Biological Databases). In Australia D. bracteata was probably accidentally introduced and it is now growing along roadsides. Invasive populations are large with up to almost 80 mature individuals in one square meter (; Trees For Life).

column1: The aim of this research was to evaluate the similarities in the bioclimatic niches occupied by invasive and native populations of D. bracteata and to estimate the potential further spread of this species in Australia and adjacent areas. The comparison of the bioclimatic conditions experienced by African and non-native plants was conducted to explain the nature of this invasion by exploring the possible changes in the bioclimatic preferences of D. bracteata in early and present stage of its spread in Australia. Currently it is unclear whether the studied species inhabits similar climatic niches in Australia and Africa or whether it was able to colonise new niches in Australia. The negative effect of the introduction of exotic orchid on the native flora was reported only once so far ()
column2: but the actual impact of invasive Orchidaceae on local plant communities remains poorly recognized. We do believe that results of our analyses of possible future spread of D. bracteata will be valuable information that can be used in planning conservation actions.

column1: A database of localities of D. bracteata was prepared based on the information recorded on the labels of identified herbarium specimens deposited in MO
column2: WAG
column3: S
column4: NY
column5: AD
column6: MEL
column7: CANB
column8: HO
column9: NSW
column10: and PERTH. The herbaria acronyms follow Index Herbariorum (). The process of georeferencing follows . The geographic coordinates provided on the herbarium sheet labels were verified. If there was no geolocation data on the herbarium sheet label
column11: the description of the collection place recorded was assigned coordinates as precisely as possible. In addition
column12: the information provided by the South African National Biodiversity Institute and Global Biodiversity Information Facility (GBIF) with a precision value of less than 1
column13: 000 m was used. A total of 187 native (1863–2013) and 747 invasive records (1945–2016) were gathered ().

column1: The terminology used in this paper follows . The distribution of the studied orchid was evaluated by including in the analyses numerous parameters potentially relevant to its occurrence. While the bioclimatic variables were commonly used as the only predictor in previous studies on invasive plants (e.g.
column3: in our research we also incorporated vegetation
column4: soil
column5: and topographic factors. Due to the lack of data on distribution of pollinators of D. bracteata and insufficient information on the mycorrhizal associations of this species these two ecological aspects were omitted in the analyses.

column1: Nineteen bioclimatic variables from the CHELSA version 1.1 database (; ) were used. The recent study by  indicated that this dataset performs better than other available climatic data in ecological niche modeling. Eighteen soil characters relevant to plant growth were obtained from Global Soil Information Based on Automated Mapping (; http://www.soilgrids.org) with a 250 m2 resolution and upscaled to match the resolution and extent of the bioclimatic variables. Furthermore
column2: several other georeferenced factors were used in the analyses: potential vegetation ()
column3: soil quality () along with six topographic variables based on an altitude raster (). Because some previous studies () indicated that usage of a restricted area in ENM analysis is more reliable than calculating habitat suitability on the global scale the region of our analysis was clipped using a rectangular mask enclosing known populations and surrounding regions in order to estimate possible migration and/or spread. Since this species continues to spread mainly in Australia
column4: the northern border of the Australian continent was set as the maximum extent of spread in this study. To account for co-linearity and select the most important bioclimatic variables
column5: the number of original bioclimatic data was reduced using the R package MaxentVariableSelection (). The following criteria were applied: correlation threshold was set at 0.7
column6: contribution threshold at 1 and beta-multiplayer was tested in the range of 1 to 15 using 0.5 steps and in the range of 1 to 1.5 using 0.1 steps. For each setting
column7: the model was run 10 times and the results were averaged to decrease the possibility of a random selection even though all variables were treated a priori as equal. This algorithm evaluates correlation and contribution mutually and is more objective than selection based on an expert opinion and correlation which may be not repeatable and biased by the specific preferences of a researcher.

column1: The modeling was conducted using the maximum entropy method implemented in Maxent version 3.3.3 k ()
column2: which is commonly used in ecological studies and is known to be reliable ( and references therein). The maximum number of iterations was set at 104 and convergence threshold at 10−5. For each run
column3: 20% of the data was set aside and used as test points (; ; ). The “random seed” option
column4: which provides a random test partition and background subset for each run was used. The run was performed as a bootstrap with 103 replicates and the output was set to cumulative.

column1: To evaluate the possible future expansion of D. bracteata within Australia
column2: climate projections obtained from Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 5 (CMIPP5) were used. Four “representative concentration pathways” (RCPs: rcp26
column3: rcp45
column4: rcp60
column5: rcp85)
column6: which differ in predicted CO2 concentration ()
column7: were analyzed. We only considered the models covering all four representative concentration pathways for the year 2070 (average for 2061–2080). These models were obtained from WorldClim (http://www.worldclim.org) (). To reduce the bias caused by the selection of only one specific model
column8: they were averaged and the ensemble map for each variable
column9: in particular RCP
column10: was computed (). This step simplifies the interpretation as it shows the general trend specific for a given RCP scenario while reducing extremes and uncertainties of particular models. Since many soil variables may be potentially affected by climate warming and due to the absence of such models for the region studied they were not used for future predictions. For the presentation of results
column11: output grids with a cumulative scale were converted to binary grids using maximum training sensitivity plus a specificity threshold (; ).

column1: A range of methods described in previous studies () were used to analyse output from niche modeling and quantify differences between indigenous and invasive populations. To measure the degree of similarity between occupied niches of both groups the niche equivalency test () was calculated using ‘ENMTools’ R package (). Niche overlaps (D and I) were calculated using methods of  and . Schoener’s D statistic uses direct measures of species density
column2: which in this study were changed to measures of densities of occurrence modelled in environmental space. ‘I’ statistic was based on the modified Hellinger distance that compares two probability distributions. These two metrics range from 0 (no similarity) to 1 (high similarity). The bias metric () was calculated as described previously (). It shows differences in ecological tolerance between invasive and native populations and the value of this metric can be either positive (when novel conditions with higher values are experienced by invasive populations) or negative (if conditions recorded in indigenous populations have higher median than those in newly occupied areas). To visualize interdependence of different populations in the simplified (two dimensional) space Principal Component Analysis (PCA) was performed on all available variables ()
column3: as described in  using R (). All GIS operations were done in open source software QGis () and R () using packages ‘raster’ () and ‘rgdal’ ().

column1: The calculated value of the area under the curve (AUC) was 0.98 (SD 0.001)
column2: which indicates excellent model performance. Additional summary statistics included the mean cross-entropy (mxe)
column3: which equaled 0.019 (SD 0.001) and root-mean-squared error (RMSE)
column4: which was 0.073 (SD 0.002). Both of these values were very low

column1: The model of the current distribution of suitable bioclimatic niches for D. bracteata was calculated based on all uncorrelated variables and a model in which only climatic factors were considered exclusively were visually congruent (
column3: and ). The main difference concerns the transitional zone between the western and eastern Cape in Africa and the Nullarbor Plain in Australia. The first region was not indicated as suitable in the model based on climatic factors
column4: whereas the second one was shown as suitable for this orchid in this analysis. Otherwise the difference may be seen in the scale of suitable habitat—while the general trend is very similar

column6: the species is found in most of the suitable habitats in both Africa and Australia. Yet there are some areas in Australia that are not colonized—the Eyre Peninsula and some smaller areas detached from the main distribution. A vast area of Tasmania was predicted to be suitable but so far the occurrence of D. bracteata has been reported only from the northern part of the island. Also
column7: there were no records of this orchid from New Zealand (especially the North and South Islands) although the models indicate the existence of suitable habitat in this area.

column3: the extent of native geographical range of the study orchid can slightly decrease or increase. Two models (rcp26 and rcp60) predict that suitable niches for D. bracteata will become available along the Atlantic coast

column2: each of the future climate scenarios gave specific information on the future potential range of D. bracteata. The rcp26 scenario prediction is that more suitable sites will be located in the south-western and south-eastern part of the Great Dividing Range

column1: The overlap between the studied environmental requirements of invasive and the natural populations is moderate or even low. Statistics calculated according to  were D = 0.35 and I = 0.58. These values were slightly higher using the method developed by : D = 0.44

column3: niche identity tests reveal that the bioclimatic niches occupied by invasive and natural populations were different (p < 0.01). Yet visualization using PCA () indicates a large overlap between the niches with only a small proportion of which is different.

The blue background indicates the whole environment included in the analysis present in Africa and Australia. Native and invasive populations are enclosed by circles encompassing 95% of the data. While native populations occupy a slightly broader niche some of the invasive populations occupy habitats not present in its native niche.

Analyzed environmental conditions recorded at the sites occupied by this species are illustrated in . To measure the dissimilarity between invasive and native populations a bias metric was calculated. It indicates that all invasive populations occupy relatively different habitats compared to populations in Africa (Chi-squared = 46.031
