column1: Orchids are usually not regarded as weeds although some
column2: e.g.
column3: Epipactis atrorubens
column4: E. helleborine and Dactylorhiza majalis
column5: colonize secondary habitats in temperate Europe (; ). Even fewer species are reported as invasive (e.g.
column6: ) and so far only one
column7: Spathoglottis plicata
column8: has been shown to negatively affect native plants ().

column1: The invasive success of D. bracteata has not been thoroughly investigated and the mechanisms of this phenomenon remain unclear. The only predictive models are in Weed Futures (http://www.weedfutures.net; ). Most ground orchids usually do not have great potential for spreading because of their very specific ecological requirements. Orchidaceae often have specific insect pollinators and specific mycorrhizal associations that need to be present in the soil to enable seed germination (e.g.
column2: ; ; ). Many species of orchids will only germinate with the aid of one or a few species of fungus
column3: so their distribution
column4: and hence ecological success
column5: is heavily dependent on suitable conditions for the fungus. Disa bracteata appears to be able to form an association with a large number of fungal partners
column6: especially those that can survive in disturbed soils ()
column7: thus it is much less limited in terms of the places and conditions in which it may become established. For this reason
column8: we did not incorporate the distribution of mycorrhizal fungi in our analyses. It is noteworthy that the incorporation of a fungal factor in any analysis of orchid distribution is extremely difficult. Most orchid mycorrhizal fungi belong to the genus Rhizoctonia
column9: a diverse polyphyletic group that is difficult to classify and molecular methods have become the standard means of assigning these orchid fungi to groups within the Rhizoctonia alliance (). Because of the lack of data on the distribution of specific fungi it is not possible to use such data in ecological niche modeling.

column1: If invasive populations would occupy the same habitat as natural the result will be 0. Negative values indicate occupation of sites below median found in the natural range
column2: whereas positive values indicate occupation of sites above the median. The greater the number is (or lower in case of negative values) the greater is the difference.

column1: As seed production in Disa bracteata is pollinator-independent there was no need to incorporate the potential distribution of any pollinator in order to get a more realistic potential distribution of this orchid. Disa bracteata appears to be self-pollinating as a result of the breakup of pollinia in the anther (). Generally
column2: this is not beneficial for genetic variability
column3: however it does enable it to produce a large number of seeds. High propagule pressure greatly enhances the chances of the establishment of invasive species (). In fact
column4: genetic variability seems to be less important as many successful invaders reproduce vegetatively. This is frequently the only mode of reproduction in some invasive species. This is often influenced by environmental conditions that are not suitable for the full development of a plant e.g.
column5: maturation of seeds. Ideally
column6: even in such cases an invasive species may couple vegetative propagation with occasional sexual reproduction in order to respond to a suite of selective pressures and propagate efficiently ().

column1: The environmental similarity between Australia and South Africa enabled numerous African plants to naturalize in Australia. 15% of naturalized flora of South Australia consists of species native to South Africa (; ). Slightly higher contribution of African plants was observed in Western Australia (17%; ; ). It is worth to notice that the two species considered as major environmental weeds in Australia and New Zealand
column2: Chrysanthemoides monilifera (Asteraceae) and Asparagus asparagoides (Asparagaceae)
column3: are of African origin ().

column1: Invasive species often experience release from biotic interactions and dispersal barriers in their non-native ranges (; ; ). While the visualization using PCA indicate a large overlap between the niches of African and invasive populations of D. bracteata they are not the same and that small difference is important as it influences the results of the niche identity test.
Another interesting result is that the niche of invasive populations has changed over time as the colonization process has progressed. At the time of the first introduction
column2: which was around 1944 (date of the first recorded specimen)
column3: only a few localities were known and the conditions there were similar to those in its natural range. However
column4: over time more populations were established
column5: which eventually gave rise to the colonization of the eastern part of Australia. The first georeferenced specimen collected in 1989 was found on the southern Adelaide Plains. Dispersal to the western part of the continent involved colonizing novel habitats or those that are not available to Disa in its native range (). This shift is congruent with the Köppen–Geiger climate classification system ()
column6: which indicates that the difference in niches may be influenced by the climate availability or different preferences within both ranges. In Africa
column7: six climatic types were occupied while in Australia two of them were not populated and the majority of established populations occurred within the Csb climate (Coastal Mediterranean). The pattern of shifting niche of invasive species was detected in some previous studies in cases when available evidence suggests use of novel environments by alien species in the invaded range (; ; ) when those conditions could be unavailable or inaccessible in the native range (; ; ; ). Additionally
column8: there was a difference between Eastern and Western Australia: in the former second most frequent climate type is the Cfb (Marine With Mild Winter)
column9: while in the latter this position belongs to the Csa climate (Interior Mediterranean; ). Thus it is not an entirely new climate but rather a shift of the climatic preferences that may be attributed to the establishment of invasive populations in areas possessing different composition of available climates. However
column10: it may be related to novel preferences as well since all climates that are present in Africa are present in Australia. Interestingly
column11: areas of some types of climate are less frequent in Africa and apparently are sparsely populated by D. bracteata but mainly by other Disa s.l. species. This may indicate that in its native distribution there are factors that prevent D. bracteata occupying this niche
column12: such as biotic interactions (possibly with other closely related species) while in Australia this constraint is absent and D. bracteata is able to colonize these new sites. Studies on niche shifting species indicate this occurs in various areas (; ) but it is relevant to fewer than 15% of plant invaders (). There are significant differences between the climatic niches occupied by invasive and native populations of another invasive species of orchid
column13: E. helleborine ()
column14: but in this case
column15: no shift within the Köppen–Geiger climate was found. A study of the niche shifts of plants introduced into Australia found that none of 26 species included in this study changed their Köppen–Geiger climatic niche ().

column1: Two types of climate present in Africa are not occupied in Australia. Invasive populations occur mainly within Csb climate with Cfb as the second most frequent in the Eastern Part and Csa as the second one in the Western Part of Australia. Shortcuts follow standard Köppen–Geiger climate classification system: BSh
column2: Hot Semi-Arid; BSk
column3: Cold Semi-Arid; Cfa
column4: Humid Subtropical; Cfb
column5: Marine with Mild Winter; Csa
column6: Interior Mediterranean; Csb
column7: Coastal Mediterranean. Prior to the analysis
column8: occurrences were rarified to match resolution of climate map (10 km2).

column1: In the case of D. bracteata a significant difference in the proportion among climates in areas occupied in Africa and Australia was recorded (Fisher’s Exact Test p = 1.5 × 10−5). The same result is obtained when Africa is compared with two regions of Australia considered separately (Africa vs. Eastern Australia: Fisher’s Exact Test p = 1.4 × 10−4
column2: Africa vs. Western Australia: Fisher’s Exact Test p = 1.3 × 10−3). This suggests that irrespective of invaded region
column3: D. bracteata occurs in Australia in habitats characterized by different climatic conditions (in terms of climatic zones) than these recorded in Africa. Even though both contain the same climatic types
column4: they differ in their abundance and relative areas. This may be influenced by the total available land area which is much smaller in Southern Africa that is stretched along meridians while Australia is rather stretched along parallels.

column1: Disa bracteata may be the first record of such a shift within Australia. When estimating niche conservatism and changes in future range
column2: one needs to bear in mind that correct estimation of the latter is tricky because acquiring a new niche is an active process possibly linked to novel adaptations that may not be known at the time of a study (; ).  suggested that the probability of a niche shift in invasive species depends primarily upon the ecological and genetic processes limiting the species in its native range. Unfortunately
column3: during our studies we did not have access to a sufficient amount of molecular data to explore genetic differences between African and invasive populations of D. bracteata.

column1: A South African D. bracteata has become invasive in Australia and it is already present on a large part of the continent. Created models suggest that area with the suitable niche for this species is larger than that currently occupied by the studied orchid thus; the spread of this species will continue. How this expansion will proceed depends on future changes in the factors influencing its distribution and primarily on the magnitude of the climate modification. As demonstrated here
column2: it is very likely that a niche shift has occurred in this case so the further spread of studied orchid should be monitored. Altogether
column3: the results of this study indicate the need of further research on the spread of D. bracteata
column4: especially analyses of genetic differences between native and invasive populations.

column1: Variables used for modeling are marked with bold. All variables were used for PCA. Topographic variables marked with “Calculated*” as a source were calculated with “raster” package in R using function “terrain” on altitude dataset.

Points are colored according to their finding decade. Groups of a particular decade have different color assignments and are enclosed in a circle encompassing 95% of the data. There is a visible shift in invasive populations which move towards negative axis values.

column1: 16|10.7717/peerj.5792|Impacts of climate change on Capparis spinosa L. based on ecological niche modeling| Ashraf  Uzma |2018-10-16|"In recent years
column2: climate change has been established as a clear and emerging global process

column5: sandy

column8: southern Europe
column9: North and East Africa
column10: Madagascar
column11: Australia
column12: and Oceania (; ). Although sometimes considered a weed
column13: it has a long history as an archaeophyte (). Immature flower buds
column14: unripe fruits
column15: and shoots are consumed as foods or condiments. Flower buds
column16: fruits
column17: seeds
column18: shoots
column19: and bark of roots were traditionally used for pharmacological purposes
column20: especially for treating rheumatism (; ). This plant is widely appreciated for its medicinal properties

column22: particularly diseases of the spleen
column23: kidney
column24: and liver (; ). These properties apparently derive from compounds such as rutin
column25: carotenoid

column27: and tocopherols (). This plant is also potentially important in combating desertification and minimizing soil erosion

column1: Ecological niche modeling (ENM) and the related ideas of species distribution modeling and habitat suitability modeling have gained popularity since they allow evaluation of potential geographic distributions of species under scenarios of climate change (). The approach employs information on environmental variables associated with ecological niche constraints on a species in relation to future distributions of those conditions as approximated by climate model outputs (). The result of these models is the coarse-resolution correlative model of the ecological niche
column2: which makes geographic predictions of the distributional potential of species under present and future conditions. These models are evaluated for predictive ability based on careful testing with independent occurrence datasets.

column1: Although several studies have examined structural and functional traits
column2: phenotypic plasticity
column3: and genetic variation in this species (; )
column4: little information is available on its actual and potential geographic distribution and climate change sensitivity. The present study therefore aims to (1) characterize the species’ ecological niche with respect to climate
column5: and (2) estimate present and future potential distributional areas of C. spinosa worldwide.

column1: Occurrence data were obtained from the Global Biodiversity Information Facility website (https://www.gbif.org)
column2: with an initial total of 6
column3: 371 occurrence points corresponding to 15 infraspecies (; https://doi.org/10.15468/dl.cu3tvo). These data were checked rigorously for georeferencing errors and spatial biases. Outliers and botanical garden-based points were removed through careful assessment of data source and consultation of record metadata
column4: leaving 5
column5: 508 points for further analysis. To estimate the area (M) that has been accessible to the species over relevant time periods ()
column6: which should be used as the model calibration area
column7: we applied a buffer of 1
column8: 000 km around all of the known occurrence points () (). In light of the relatively subtle differences between present and future potential distributions
column9: and considering the problems associated with model transfers to other regions ()
column10: we have opted not to transfer our models globally
column11: but rather to explore implications of climate change within the M region only.

column1: Environmental variables were obtained from the WorldClim climate data archive (http://www.worldclim.org) ()
column2: in the form of 19 bioclimatic data layers summarizing potentially relevant climate dimensions at 2.5′ spatial resolution. Parallel bioclimatic data layers were obtained for five downscaled general circulation model (GCM) outputs at CCAFS Climate (http://www.ccafs-climate.org). We considered 2050 and 2070 as future time slices
column3: under two emission scenarios: representative concentration pathways (RCPs) 4.5 and 8.5. As such
column4: we explored 5 GCMs × 2 time periods × 2 RCPs = 20 future transfers of models for the species.

column1: Occurrence data were rarefied to leave no pair of points closer than five km using SDMTools () in ArcGIS
column2: reducing biases related to spatial autocorrelation. This spatial filtering reduced the number of occurrence points still further to 1
column3: 287. We divided these data into two equal parts at random to permit model calibration and evaluation. We also calculated Pearson correlation coefficients among environmental variables () to avoid using non-independent variables in model calibration. One from each pair of variables showing high correlations (i.e.
column4: r > 0.9) is eliminated (; ). Variable choice from each pair for retention is based on its variable importance in preliminary model runs (). Variables removed were annual mean temperature
column5: minimum temperature of coldest month
column6: temperature annual range
column7: mean temperature of warmest quarter
column8: and precipitation of wettest and driest quarters. We further removed mean temperature of wettest and driest quarters
column9: and precipitation of warmest and coldest quarters
column10: as they present odd spatial artifacts (). Variables used as predictors in final models are presented in .

column3: 000 iterations
column4: 10 random replicate analyses
column5: and 10% test sample size; all other options were left on default. For evaluation of model predictions
column6: we used partial receiver operating characteristic (ROC) approaches (). Partial ROC statistics were calculated using online the Niche Toolbox site
column7: http://shiny.conabio.gob.mx:3838/nichetoolb2/
column8: with 1
column9: 000 replicates and E = 0.1% (). Final models were calibrated based on present-day conditions and transferred to each of the 20 future-climate datasets.

column5: precipitation-related variables in general showed minimal contributions to model quality.

column1: The potential distribution of C. spinosa showed stability in some parts of the world
column2: and mild expansion in others
column3: in the face of changing climates into the future. Being a xerophytic shrub
column4: the species offers several ecosystem services regarding erosion control
column5: and shows remarkable adaptability toward harsh environments (). The species grows mainly in arid and semi-arid environments in tropical regions
column6: and thus can be used to combat desertification and soil erosion (). It is also considered an important plant both economically and medicinally
column7: and has been used in Chinese
column8: Ayurvedic
column9: Siddha

column13: depurative

column16: and has been used to treat gastrointestinal infections ().

column1: This plant is native to Asia
column2: and has a long history farther west
column3: in Mediterranean Europe; it is invasive in North America (). The medicinal properties of this species are appreciated across its native range. Our models indicate that climate change poses few or no direct challenges to its persistence. Anthropogenic pressures such as human population pressure and associated land-use change and livestock grazing

column8: and ecological properties of C. spinose; the review also encouraged studies on this species in relation to climate change in the Mediterranean region

column4: under future climatic scenarios

column6: this species may increase in the arid
column7: semi-arid
column8: and Mediterranean climate regions in the future.

17|10.7717/peerj.55|Cattle and climate in Africa: How climate variability has influenced national cattle holdings from 1961–2008| Lunde  Torleif Markussen |2013-3-19|Since the 1960s there has been a period of climatic change with global land-surface temperature rising about 0.5–0.6 °C (). Although some studies have addressed effect of climate variability on cattle populations (; ; ; )
column2: no studies have described how historical variations in temperature and rainfall influence cattle populations in Africa.

column1: FAO estimates the number of cattle in Africa during the period 2001 to 2010 is twice the estimates for the years 1961–1970. But
column2: how variations in the climate influence cattle depend on the ecological setting
column3: and how variations in cattle influence the population
column4: depend on the availability of alternative energy sources as well as the cultural setting. The role of cattle in developing countries is as a source of high-quality food
column5: as draft animals
column6: and as a source of manure and fuel (; ). Cattle represent important contribution to household incomes ()
column7: and in drought prone areas they can act as an insurance against weather risk ().

column1: Most of the cattle in Africa are in arid and semiarid areas. In the forested humid areas of humid West-Africa
column2: as well as Democratic Republic of Congo
column3: the tsetse tolerant N’Dama and West African Shorthorn breeds are common. The most common cattle breed is
column4: however
column5: the East and West African Zebu
column6: which make up the majority of African cattle (). Close to Lake Chad
column7: the heat tolerant Kuri breed can be found
column8: although the density has declined since the 1950s ()
column9: and in East Africa
column10: the Sanga can be found on the western branch of the Great Rift Valley. In South Africa the Afrikander is common. The different cattle types probably represent mixtures of breeds introduced at various times ().

column1: Many production systems supply water from ponds and rivers during the wet season
column2: and the need for watering increases with higher temperatures (). The IPCC 2007 report concluded that changes in range-fed livestock numbers in any African region will be directly proportional to changes in annual precipitation ().

column1: The coming century
column2: it is virtually certain temperatures will increase
column3: and that the intensity of precipitation will change (). How the cattle has been
column4: and will be influenced directly through climate variability
column5: and indirectly through parasites and vector borne diseases is still uncertain. The lack of certainty in projected absolute changes in precipitation amounts and how cattle respond to climate
column6: makes it difficult to to predict impacts of climate change. It is therefore necessary to understand the historical impact of climate on cattle before projecting future impacts and developing adaptation strategies ().

column1: The growth of plants are directly influenced by the atmospheric CO2 concentrations
column2: with two metabolic pathways; C3 and C4 (). While the productivity is expected to increase for C3 plants
column3: quality
column4: productivity and digestibility is expected to decrease with increasing CO2 concentrations. C4 plants are probably less affected (). In the subtropical Australia it has been hypothesized that lower precipitation can be compensated for by the benefits of increased CO2 (). The compensating effect in tropical Africa is uncertain.

column1: A study by Seif et al. () showed Zebu water consumption increased by 58% when temperature increased from 10 to 31 °C. This is only 2.8% increase per degree
column2: if we consider this as a linear process. At higher temperatures
column3: feed consumption decreases () and fertility increases (). Lower food consumption can be of importance in the dry season. This is in line with the perception of farmers in the savanna zone of central Senegal
column4: who say low temperatures may lead to fodder shortage ().

column1: Although climate influence the vegetation in these semiarid environments
column2: the coupling between climate and animal numbers might not be as straight forward as grass production and the need for watering; in tsetse infested areas
column3: high temperatures might reduce the vector populations and cause a reduction in animal trypanosomiasis (; )
column4: in Nigeria increased rainfall has been linked to outbreaks of blackquarter ()
column5: and in the eastern part of Africa
column6: where east coast fever prevail
column7: climate variability can be related to the survival and reproductive success of the tick Rhipicephalus appendiculatus ()
column8: and as the development of the Theileria parasite (). Livestock also play a role in malaria transmission by creating favourable environments and blood meals for Anopheles arabiensis. We have previously shown that understanding fluctuations in cattle populations is important to assess the historical and future distribution of two of the most efficient vectors of malaria in Africa (; ). Tirados et al. () showed that a cattle herd of 20 heads outside a house reduced the number of Anopheles arabiensis landing on humans by 50%. It has also been speculated that certain malaria epidemics in India and Somalia can be explained by herds of livestock being decimated during drought years (; ).

column2: Chile: a cold-water coral thriving at low pH| Fillinger  Laura |2013-10-29|"Similar to their tropical counterparts and in spite of their lack of photosynthetic endosymbionts
column3: azooxanthellate scleractinian cold-water corals (CWC) provide the structural 3-dimensional basis and habitat for a rich deep-reef associated fauna (; ; ). With an almost circumglobal distribution (; ) these cnidarians occur mostly between 200 and 1000 m depth ()
column4: making investigations in their natural environment logistically challenging ().

column1: One of the main reef-building species
column2: Lophelia pertusa Linnaeus
column4: has been shown to be particularly sensitive to short-term temperature changes ()
column5: and the paucity of CWC in aragonite-limited waters () has raised great concerns regarding their fate in the context of climate change and ocean acidification (; ).

column1: Ocean acidification
column2: a consequence of the uptake of anthropogenic carbon dioxide (CO2) by the ocean
column3: results in pH reduction and alteration of the seawater carbonate chemistry (see e.g.
column4: ; ). As the quantity of CO2 absorbed by the ocean increases
column5: the saturation state of the aragonite carbonate mineral used by scleractinian corals to build their skeleton decreases
column6: leading ultimately to its dissolution (see e.g.
column7: ; ). Besides
column8: as carbonate solubility increases with decreasing temperature and increasing pressure
column9: saturation states are lower in deep and cold habitats than in shallow and warm waters (). Models predict a progressive shoaling of the aragonite saturation horizons (ASH - the limit between undersaturation and supersaturation) (). Hence
column10: deep-dwelling cold-water corals are expected to be the first exposed to aragonite undersaturation ().

column1: To date
column2: few experiments have been conducted on the response of CWC to ocean acidification (; ). Lophelia pertusa exhibited reduced calcification rates under increased CO2 concentrations in short term experiments (; ) but revealed capacity for acclimation in longer term manipulations (). In both cases
column3: calcification rates remained positive even under aragonite undersaturation. Calcification rates in Madrepora oculata Linnaeus
column5: another reef-building species
column6: remained stable under reduced pH but increased under pre-industrial
column7: higher pH levels suggesting that CWC are already suffering from ocean acidification ().

column1: A complementary strategy to manipulation experiments in aquaria to determine the sensitivity of corals to changes in seawater chemistry is to investigate their distribution along natural pH gradients. For zooxanthellate shallow water corals
column2: spatial differences in coral distribution were found around volcanic CO2 seeps in Italy (; ) and Papua New Guinea (). To our knowledge
column3: only three corresponding studies focusing on the qualitative analysis of the presence/absence of some species were published for living CWC. In the Gulf of Mexico
column4: Lophelia pertusa seemed restricted to aragonite saturated waters but given the lack of correlation between this species’ presence and the aragonite saturation level
column5: suggested that depth or other factors were more likely to control the coral’s distribution. On an Australian seamount
column6: two species of colonial scleractinian Solenosmilia variabilis Duncan
column7: 1873 and Enallopsammia rostrata Pourtalès
column8: 1878 persisted near the ASH and a third solitary species Desmophyllum dianthus Esper
column9: 1794 was detected well below this depth in aragonite undersaturated waters (). Pronounced pH and aragonite saturation gradients have recently been described for Comau Fjord
column10: Chilean Patagonia (). There
column11: Desmophyllum dianthus is known to occur in dense aggregations in high-pH near-surface waters
column12: together with two other cup corals: Caryophyllia huinayensis () and Tethocyathus endesa (; ). The deployment of a small Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV) halfway down the fjord (263 m) showed the presence of D. dianthus below the ASH down to at least 255 m depth (; ).

column1: Desmophyllum dianthus (senior synonym of Desmophyllum cristagalli ()) is a solitary azooxanthellate scleractinian often associated with Lophelia pertusa and Madrepora oculata (; ). It can form pseudo-colonies with younger individuals growing on the skeleton of older ones and constitutes the framework-building CWC species in coral banks off New Zealand and Chile (; ). The linear growth rate of deep-sea specimens has been estimated around 0.5–2 mm yr−1 () but might be as fast as 2.2–10 mm yr−1 for Chilean corals (; ) . This cosmopolitan species has been reported over a wide depth range from 35 to 2460 m (). In the fjords of northern Chilean Patagonia
column2: even shallower occurrences of dense D. dianthus banks have been reported (20 m depth)
column3: with scattered individuals occasionally occurring as shallow as 8 m (). SCUBA studies reported D. dianthus abundances up to 1500 individuals/m2 on rocky walls and overhangs with inclination >80° ()
column4: but a notable absence of corals on milder slopes
column5: perhaps due to smothering by terrigenous sediments (; ). The factor limiting the upper distribution of this coral is thought to be the sharp halocline
column6: separating the marine waters from the brackish surface layer (; ). The existence and nature of a lower limit in Comau Fjord has not yet been investigated ().

column1: Individual D. dianthus have been collected in the deep-sea from waters exhibiting various pH ranging from 7.57 to 8.10 (; ). In Comau Fjord
column2: a recent report () described the presence of D. dianthus along a vertical pH gradient of 7.4–8.3 between the surface and mid-depth (230 m). Although the qualitative data suggest that this CWC is less susceptible to low pH than previously assumed ()
column3: concurrent data on D. dianthus abundance
column4: pH and other environmental parameters that might be involved in the control of this CWC are still lacking.

column1: To close this gap
column2: we deployed a novel ROV in Comau Fjord to quantitatively characterize the occurrence of D. dianthus (abundance and size) and simultaneously measure several physico-chemical descriptors of its natural environment.

column1: Our main objectives were to describe the (i) occurrence
column2: (ii) vertical
column3: (iii) horizontal and (iv) size distribution of D. dianthus in Comau Fjord; (v) to measure the corresponding environmental parameters along the ROV survey; and (vi) analyse the resulting distribution patterns to assess the role of pH versus other environmental factors in governing D. dianthus distribution.

column1: Comau Fjord in Northern Chilean Patagonia is a 41 km long
column2: 4.5 km wide and about 480 m deep basin with its mouth shoaling to around 300 m depth (). Little is known about its hydrodynamics but the principal driving processes are probably tidal exchange and mixing in this macrotidal environment with up to 7 m tidal amplitudes ()
column3: and a thermohaline circulation sustained by high precipitation (>5000 mm/year) (). Due to its north-south orientation
column4: the fjord is protected from the main westerly winds
column5: so that wind-driven advection and mixing play only minor roles (). Freshwater entering the fjord from the rivers at its head and its eastern side (; ) strongly influences the surface layer down to 10 m (). Below the shallow brackish water layer with profound seasonal changes in temperature
column6: the lower fully marine environment is less influenced by seasonality (). Surrounded by steep basaltic walls covered with a temperate primary rainforest ()
column7: the fjord is subjected to frequent landslides and avalanches
column8: causing a high input of terrestrial sediment to the aquatic system
column9: which then accumulates at the bottom of the basin ().

column1: From the physico-chemical structure of Comau Fjord ( and ) we could identify three water masses. (1) A layer with low salinity
column2: high summer temperatures
column3: pH
column4: oxygen concentration and primary production at the surface (; ) results from the large freshwater input into the fjord (). The freshwater outflow is compensated by the inflow of (2) Subantarctic oceanic water (; ) constituting an intermediate layer. Below 300 m we found (3) a water mass with a longer residence time in the fjord () showing higher salinity and temperature and lower pH and oxygen due to decomposition processes in the lower water column and fjord bottom (; ). The 300 m isobath is also the one separating the deep Comau Fjord from the shallower parts of the adjacent Golfo de Ancud ()
column5: suggesting a less frequent exchange of these deeper water masses. At the innermost sites
column6: near the head of the fjord
column7: another structure could be detected between the surface and intermediate layers with lower pH and oxygen. This fourth layer could be the consequence of some of the bottom water being washed up by the inflow of oceanic water () or the result of a high decomposition after a large phytoplankton bloom.

column1: Our detailed study confirms anecdotal evidence from the previous shallow ROV survey () that Desmophyllum dianthus may be ubiquitous in Comau Fjord. We found this cold-water coral at virtually all depths below the low salinity surface layer and down to the bottom of the fjord at 395 m (). D. dianthus was also present everywhere between the mouth and the head of the fjord. Most remarkable was the occurrence of a vast coral bank in the direct vicinity of the river at the head of the fjord (Rio Bodudahue)
column2: in striking contrast to the alleged sensitivity of this coral to terrigenous sediments ().

column8: Tasmanian Seamounts ()
column9: and Drake Passage (; ) display changes in the distribution of this scleractinian
column10: most likely due to variations in the surface productivity
column11: the subsurface oxygen concentration and the seawater carbonate chemistry caused by climate-induced modifications of the ocean circulation. The ubiquity of D. dianthus in Comau Fjord demonstrates that no threshold in any of the environmental parameters is crossed there that would completely prevent recruitment
column12: growth or survival of the coral.

column1: The pervasiveness of D. dianthus described above defied identification of an environmental controlling factor. Nevertheless
column2: an important pattern emerged from our quantitative analysis: the significant reduction
column3: in spite of available sediment-free rock

column5: corresponding to the water mass in the deep basin part of the fjord. In the absence of experimental evidence

column1: In Comau Fjord
column2: we found corals within a wide range of pH values (7.71–8.42). Furthermore
column3: measured a pH of 7.6 there in 1995 and the lowest pH recorded by  in 2011 was 7.4 in the same fjord. Given the difficulties in accurately determining pH () and the current lack of information on residence times in the different water masses
column4: it is difficult to decide to what extent the range of values reported reveal seasonal and/or inter-annual variations in pH

column2: the aragonite saturation horizon was located at about 150 m depth (). The presence of D. dianthus below this depth appears to be in contradiction with the restriction of cold-water corals to aragonite saturation states >1 (). However
column3: also found D. dianthus in waters largely undersaturated with regard to aragonite
column4: supporting experimental findings that scleractinian display physiological mechanisms which enable them to increase their internal pH relative to seawater pH
column5: facilitating calcification (). These recent reports
column6: along with the findings of our study
column7: suggest that cold-water corals may be able to cope with ocean acidification better than previously assumed
column8: but likely at a high energy cost (). High primary and secondary production in marine ecosystems of northern Patagonia ()
column9: reflected by the high phosphorus content in the skeleton of D. dianthus individuals collected in Chilean fjords ()
column10: could help this coral sustain the cost of calcification under low pH (; )
column11: at least for the population located above the deep water layer. The individuals growing within the euphotic zone might be exposed to the additional cost of a parasitic relationship with endolithic photo-autotrophs (). It is possible that the corals living in the deepest parts of Comau Fjord might show pre-acclimation or pre-adaptation to life in future lower pH oceans. At present
column12: it cannot be ruled out either that the deeper D. dianthus in the fjord might belong to a genetically divergent population ().

column1: Aragonite saturation state is considered as one of the most important drivers of cold-water coral distribution (). Yet direct measurements of the carbonate chemistry in the vicinity of CWC reefs are very rare (). Furthermore
column2: the few long-term experimental manipulations conducted on CWC showed that at least two of the main reef-building species: Lophelia pertusa and Madrepora oculata remain quite unaffected by pH levels projected for the end of the century (; ) and the skeletal density of several CWC species seems independent of the surrounding carbonate saturation (; ). There is thus a great need to acquire more information in order to be able to define the ecological niche of CWC with respect to the seawater carbonate chemistry ().

column1: Prospering at the lowest limit of seawater acidity and aragonite undersaturation known for cold-water scleractinian in a relatively accessible location
column2: the Desmophyllum dianthus population(s) of Comau Fjord provides a great opportunity to investigate the effect of ocean acidification on CWC.

column1: 19|10.7717/peerj.16524|Cleaning symbiosis in coral reefs of Jardines de la Reina National Park| Corso  Andy Joel |2023-12-6|"Mutualistic symbioses are essential relationships in biological communities. They are closely related to trophic cascades and influence growth
column2: adaptability of individuals
column3: diversity and abundance (). Cleaning mutualisms are defined as those interactions in which one organism
column4: termed “cleaner”
column5: provides another
column6: a “client”
column7: with a cleaning service by feeding on its parasites and significantly reducing its parasitic load (). Ectoparasites negatively affect fish hosts by altering size
column8: growth
column9: larval recruitment success
column10: and survival (). Thus
column11: the presence of cleaners in reefs implies remarkable benefits to the health of communities (; ; ). Although cleaning symbioses in coral reefs have been studied
column12: only a few investigations have employed more holistic approaches that allow the analysis of cleaning mutualistic interaction networks on reefs as complex systems possessing emergent properties with their own study methods (e.g.

column1: Mutualistic ecological networks represent the interactions between two functional groups
column2: such as plants and their floral visitors or as cleaners and clients (). By studying these ecological networks
column3: it is possible to quantify and describe how individuals of different species interact
column4: estimate the diversity of interactions
column5: determine the structural organization of the mutualistic network or analyze the degree of trophic specialization (). The descriptors used to characterize networks are useful tools that facilitate the analysis of the temporal and spatial variation of species and their interactions (). Thereby
column6: descriptors allow the comparison of interactions of the same type in different habitats by describing variations in diversity
column7: structural organization
column8: and specialization ().

column1: The Caribbean Sea is considered one of the most biodiverse regions on the planet (; ). However
column2: Caribbean countries are among the most exposed to the effects of climate change
column3: and therefore the conservation of various habitats and species that are already showing signs of deterioration is also at risk (). Although Cuba does not escape this scenario
column4: biodiversity conservation in the archipelago is certainly superior
column5: which is a consequence of the implementation of successful environmental policies and the existence of an extensive network of protected areas (). Jardines de la Reina National Park
column6: with an area of approximately 950 km2 and a high degree of protection
column7: stands out as one of the best-conserved areas in the Caribbean (; ). Its coral reefs and reef fish communities are in good condition
column8: despite years of sustained fishing pressure in the area ().

column1: However
column2: suggested the existence of different degrees of protection effectiveness in the MPA based on the spatial pattern of abundances of different fish species. The authors attribute this effect to the incidence of illegal fishing pressure at the eastern and western boundaries of the reserve and the greater effectiveness of protection in the central area (; ; ). Thus
column3: the existence of a gradient of protection on the fish community in Jardines de la Reina National Park offers the opportunity to verify its effect on the characteristics of mutualistic interaction networks at various sites that differ in the availability of potential clients. Likewise
column4: although cleaning mutualisms have been extensively studied in the Caribbean Sea ()
column5: no published research includes the Cuban archipelago as a study area. Thus
column6: the present research aims to answer the following questions: what are the characteristics of the interaction networks of cleaning mutualisms in Jardines de la Reina National Park coral reefs? What is the effect of MPA’s different levels of protection effectiveness on coral reef cleaning mutualisms?

column1: The Jardines de la Reina archipelago () currently has more than 950 km2 under the National System of Protected Areas in Cuba
column2: with the management category of National Park () and consists of an extensive chain of cays associated with mangrove islands and stretches of coral reef facing the Caribbean Sea. The reef structure in this area includes a reef terrace between 5 and 15 m deep
column3: followed by a rocky slope culminating in a sandy esplanade
column4: with the formation of large spur and groove area and a reef drop-off ().

column1: Blue dots represent the sampled sites; black lines delimit the three regions and different protection levels.
The sampling effort was concentrated on the largest group of cays in the archipelago

Variables were tested for homoscedasticity (Levene test) and to verify if they fitted to a normal distribution (Kolmogorov–Smirnov test). Non-parametric Kuskal-Wallis test was used to verify statistical differences (α = 0.05) among regions.

column1: Mutualistic interaction matrices between client species and cleaners were generated for each sector and the whole archipelago. Bipartite interaction networks were constructed for each region and for the MPA. The networks were generated from adjacency matrices with values for the number of interactions between pairs of species. The networks
column2: due to their bipartite structure
column3: consist of two groups of species segregated in different columns. Each column is formed by nodes
column4: representing the species
column5: the link between nodes represents the interaction and the thickness of the link is proportional to the number of interactions.

column1: We calculated connectance
column2: nestedness
column3: H2′ specialization ()
column4: niche overlap and complementarity for all networks. Connectance is calculated as proportion of realized interactions given a total number of possible links (). Nestedness was assessed using NODF metric and it describes asymmetrical networks with some species cleaning the most of clients whereas others clean just a subset of those clients (). H2′ measures network-level specialization ranging between 0 (no specialization) and 1 (perfect specialization (). Niche overlap indicates similarities in interaction pattern between species from the same trophic levels whereas functional complementarity measures the ecological niche complementarity between species of the same trophic level as the total branch length of a dendrogram based on differences in interacting species assemblages (). These indices are generally used to describe the characteristics and structure and infer the robustness and the specialization of interaction networks (). Processing was performed using R programming environment and bipartite package (; ).

Functional redundancy and niche overlap are measures of how similar the species are in their roles and interactions within the network. Functional redundancy reflects the extent to which species can be substituted by others without affecting the network structure or function
column6: while niche overlap measures the degree to which species share the same resources or partners in the network. The network of the high protection region had the lowest niche overlap value (0.23) and the highest functional complementarity value (262.4)
column7: suggesting that its species were more distinct and less replaceable than those of the other networks.

Cleaner species detected have been previously reported to occupy that role () (). Most studies in the Caribbean have noted the importance of Elacatinus and juvenile individuals of T. bifasciatum in cleaning stations (; ; ; ). However
column2: in this study B. rufus and T. bifasciatum were the main cleaners.

column1: The absence of significant differences among regions in the variables related to cleaning symbiosis may be explained by the fact that the protection gradient does not have a strong impact on the fish species that participate in this mutualistic interaction. These results are similar to those reported by
column2: who found that cleaner fish density
column3: as well as the number of individuals and species cleaned
column4: did not differ significantly bet ween a fished and a no-take site on the Great Barrier Reef. Therefore

column6: such as habitat complexity
column7: benthic community composition
column8: ectoparasite infection rates
column9: and interspecific interactions.

column1: Habitat complexity is one of the main factors that determine the number and distribution of cleaning stations
column2: as they provide suitable substrates and shelters for cleaners and clients (). The coral reefs sites of JRNP have similar habitat complexity and benthic community composition throughout the park
column3: as reported by  and . This may explain why the density of cleaning stations did not vary significantly among regions. However
column4: the high protection region had a slightly higher density of cleaning stations
column5: which may be related to the higher abundance of fish in this region (). Higher fish abundance may imply higher ectoparasite infection rates
column6: which increase the demand for cleaning services and the availability of food resources for cleaners (; ). This may enhance the trophic niche and abundance of cleaners
column7: especially those that are dedicated cleaners and prefer ectoparasites in their diet
column8: such as Elacatinus cleaning gobies (; ).

column1: We also expected to find higher abundance and species richness of clients in the highly protected region. However
column2: we did not observe significant differences in these variables across the protection gradient. One possible explanation is that the competitive interactions among clients and cleaners at the cleaning stations may have masked the effect of protection. Cleaning stations are not homogeneous in size and structure
column3: but they depend on the species of cleaners that inhabit them (; ). However
column4: this variation does not seem to influence the number of cleaners () or clients that use the stations at any given time (either as cleaners or as recipients of cleaning services). Therefore
column5: the number of clients at the stations may be regulated by the availability of fish from the surrounding community that may seek cleaning services. This is consistent with the finding that the abundance of clients and cleaners is the main predictor of the visitation rate to the cleaning stations (). Another factor that may limit the number of clients at the stations is the presence of predators or competitors that may deter potential clients from approaching the cleaners. This is supported by our results that showed that piscivorous and predatory species were among the most frequent visitors to the cleaning stations.

column1: Another potential reason why we did not find a clear effect of protection on the variables related to cleaning symbioses is that protection may only affect some specific species that are targeted by fishing activities (). These species may not be involved in the cleaning mutualism network
column2: or may have a minor role in it. Furthermore
column3: the cleaner-client networks are not tightly structured or obligatory ()
column4: so the dependence between the two groups is low. Therefore
column5: protection may not have a strong impact on the dynamics of cleaning symbioses in coral reefs. However
column6: we must keep in mind that this work evaluated different degrees of protection in a marine protected area
column7: where even the least protected area also has protection. It would be essential to evaluate and compare with marine areas without any protection and with a marked effect of fishing ().

column2: connectance provides information on how connected the two trophic levels that make up the network are in relation to the maximum value of possible connections between the nodes of both levels (). Higher connectance values mean that the network is more complete. We found that the network in the low protection region had the highest connectance
column3: even though we expected the opposite. This may be related to the species richness in each region. More species means more nodes in the network
column4: and therefore less chance of observing all the potential interactions. Since we recorded more species in the high protection region (23 species)
column5: we would expect that region to have lower connectance than the others. Nestedness shows how similar the patterns of interactions are among different subsets of species in the network (). Higher nestedness values indicate that there is a core group of species at each level that interact frequently with each other (generalists)
column6: and another group of species that interact less often and only with the core group (specialists). This way
column7: the generalists provide a stable source of resources
column8: in this case
column9: cleaning services
column10: that allows for specialization. Our results for nestedness are consistent with those reported for other cleaning networks in the Caribbean ().

column1: 20|10.7717/peerj.16646|Competitive effects of the macroalga Caulerpa taxifolia on key physiological processes in the scleractinian coral Turbinaria peltata under thermal stress| Fu  JianRong |2023-12-14|"The recent effects of climate change and other anthropogenic impacts have caused the severe degradation of coral reefs worldwide (). The first mass coral bleaching event was observed in 1998 and it killed approximately 8% of the world’s coral; an additional 14% of corals were lost between 2009 and 2018 ().
Many studies have asserted that ocean warming is a major factor in the reduction of coral cover (
column2: ; ; ). For example
column3: the successive bleaching events in 2016–2017 devastated Australia’s Great Barrier Reef and resulted in an 89% decline in larval recruitment in 2018 compared to historical levels (
column4: ; ). During this period
column5: 31% of reefs experienced 8–16 degree heating weeks (DHWs
column6: °C-weeks). A decline in coral cover may lead to an increase in the cover of other benthic organisms in the reefs
column7: such as macroalgae (). The impact of herbivorous fishes is mostly ignored
column8: although some studies assert that overfishing and nutrient pollution is the main cause of phase shifts towards macroalgae (). Research had shown that prior to 2011
column9: the estimated global average cover of algae was low (~16%) and stable for 30 years. Since 2011
column10: the amount of algae on the world’s coral reefs has increased by about 20% (). Thus
column11: the coral reef ecosystem is undergoing an ecological phase transition to that of an ecosystem dominated by macroalgae.

column1: Macroalgae are functional communities that are important for stabilizing reef structure ()
column2: generating primary productivity (
column4: maintaining nutrient cycling in reef areas
column5: and providing food sources for herbivores (). However
column6: there is competition between macroalgae and corals. Macrolgae harm corals through direct contact (; ) and allelopathy (; )
column7: weakening the photosynthetic performance of symbiodiniaceae ()
column8: causing the retraction of polyps ()
column9: increasing the number of pathogenic microorganisms (; )
column10: triggering coral bleaching ()
column11: and resulting in the reduced calcification and coral growth
column12: fecundity
column13: survival rate
column14: and settlement rate (; ; ; ). Specifically
column15: macroalgae affect coral feeding
column16: endosymbiont function
column17: tissue recovery
column18: and oxidative stress response.  found that Dictyopteris undulata weakened the particle capture rates of Corynactis californica by redirecting particles around polyps and causing contraction of the feeding tentacles. The dissolved organic carbon (DOC) and terpenoids released by macroalgae decreased photosynthesis and the density of endosymbionts (; ; ).  asserted that the green filamentous macroalga
column19: Chlorodesmis fastigiate
column20: significantly reduced tissue recovery in Acropora pulchra and led to the infection of A. pulchra with ciliates. High levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS
column21: a toxic byproduct of biological aerobic metabolism) could cause damage to cells ().  found that the oxidative stress response of Acropora millepora was activated in response to ROS by altering the transcription factors after contact with the macroalga Chlorodesmis fastigiata and its hydrophobic extract over a short-term period (1 and 24 h). The oxidative imbalance results in rapid protein degradation and eventually to apoptosis and/or necrosis when compensatory transcriptional action by the coral holobiont insufficiently mitigates damage.

column1: In addition
column2: the combined effects of ocean warming
column3: acidification
column4: and macroalgae contact could significantly alter the physiological response of corals (; ; ; ).  have shown that light enhanced calcification (LEC) rates of Porites lobata were negatively affected after contact with Chlorodesmis fastigiata in an ocean warming and acidification scenario
column5: compared to coral under ambient conditions. Typically
column6: the coral-algal competition is related to seasonal and temporal cycles. These
column7: in turn
column8: may be related to the abundance
column9: biomass
column10: and composition of macroalgae
column11: as well as the seasonal dynamics of temperature
column12: pCO2
column13: and light intensity (
column14: ). The sensitivity of various macroalgae to environmental stressors is also different. For example
column15: intermediate levels of ocean warming could enhance the growth and production of Laurencia sp. and Lobophora sp.
column16: which was not the case for Sargassum sp. (; ). Additionally
column17: overfishing and eutrophication have been shown to lead to an increased growth rate of some kinds of macroalgae ()
column18: which may indirectly enhance the competitive ability of macroalgae. Therefore
column19: to better understand the resilience of coral reef ecosystems in the future
column20: it is necessary to determine how coral-algal interactions are impacted by global and local stressors.

column1: According to the China Ocean Climate Monitoring bulletin (www.oceanguide.org.cn)
column2: the average sea surface temperature (SST) in the Xuwen Sea area was 27–30 °C from May to September in 2020. In 2021–2022
column3: the average SST that caused coral bleaching in the Great Barrier Reef from December to April was 28–30 °C (). It is plausible that the physiological responses of corals in the Xuwen Coral Reef National Nature Reserve of China may be affected by thermal stress when SST reaches 30 °C. During thermal stress
column4: coral feeding rates are drastically reduced and more energy is needed to maintain biological processes (DNA repair etc.
column5: ) to resist heat stress (; ). Triggered by thermal stress

The increase in the production of ROS is a stress signaling mechanism that can potentially trigger an oxidative stress and apoptotic cascade in coral cells (; ).

column9: such as monoterpenes and sesquiterpenes (

column17: potentially shifting the competitive interaction between corals and seaweeds in favour of seaweeds. It provides a reliable basis for the evolution of competition between corals and macroalgae under future global changes.

Future studies are needed to explore the interactions of multiple coral-macroalgal species under climate change. Because of the vulnerability and sensitivity of coral reef ecosystem
column6: relevant entities should take urgent steps to prevent CO2 emissions that exceed the goals of the Paris Climate Agreement. Herbivorous fish populations should also be restored to improve macroalgae management in reefs.

21|10.7717/peerj.108|Influence of coral and algal exudates on microbially mediated reef metabolism| Haas  Andreas F. |2013-7-16|Coral reefs
column2: although generally located in oligotrophic environments
column3: are one of the most biodiverse ecosystems on the planet
column4: due largely to their high productivity and efficient nutrient recycling mechanisms (). In recent years coral reefs have suffered many impacts
column5: from local anthropogenic influences such as pollution
column6: fishing
column7: and coastal development
column8: to global climate change including warming and likely future acidification of the oceans (). In many areas
column9: these impacts have led to changes in community structure often resulting in a phase shift ()
column10: in which the benthic communities shift from dominance by corals to fleshy algae (
column11: Smith et al.
column12: unpublished data). These shifts in benthic community structure have dramatic implications for the overall trophic structure of tropical reefs as corals provide habitat and shelter for numerous taxa (; ). In addition
column13: different benthic primary producers influence reef communities in multiple
column14: distinct ways by regulating the availability of various inorganic and organic resources and altering the physical structure of the benthos (; ).

column1: Organic material supplied to the ecosystem by benthic primary producers as exudates is thought to play a pivotal role in community-wide transitions on coral reefs (; ). Exudates may serve different ecological functions depending on their origin. Coral exudates may keep valuable resources in oligotrophic reef systems by trapping particles from the water column
column2: which are remineralized by the benthic microbial communities. In contrast
column3: algae derived exudates have been shown to stimulate rapid growth of planktonic microbies () and community shifts towards copiotrophic and potentially pathogenic microbial communities in the water column (). Despite an increasing focus on microbial cycling of carbon in recent years
column4: little is still known about the variability in microbial uptake of dissolved organic matter (DOM) and the potential influence it may have on biogeochemical cycling.

column1: Previous studies of tropical reef-associated primary producers have shown that all primary producers release a significant portion of their photosynthetically fixed carbon immediately into their environment (; ; ). It has further been established that fleshy macroalgae and especially small (<2 cm) filamentous algal turfs generally have noticeably higher DOC release rates than calcifying primary producers including hermatypic corals. These results are consistent over a wide range of reef systems
column2: comprising the Red Sea (; )
column3: the Caribbean ()
column4: and the Central Pacific ().

column1: However
column2: counter to expectations
column3: demonstrated that in a backreef system dominated by algae rather than corals
column4: DOC concentrations were significantly lower than in the surrounding offshore waters. Other studies () incorporating multiple islands in the central Pacific have shown similar patterns where fleshy algal abundance is inversely related to DOC concentrations in the water column. This surprising inverse correlation may be explained by a significantly more heterotrophic microbial metabolism following initially higher availability of algae derived bio-available DOC. A system wide decrease in DOC concentrations could then be the result of (a) increases in the abundance of heterotrophic microbes () and
column5: (b) a co-metabolism
column6: which occurs when microbes are given an initial surplus of labile carbon
column7: enabling this bacterial community to utilize refractory carbon sources ().

column1: Recent research has shown that macroalgae derived exudates
column2: enriched in the dissolved combined neutral sugar components Fucose and Galactose
column3: facilitate significantly higher rates of bacterioplankton growth and concomitant DOC utilization than coral exudates or untreated seawater (; ). Further
column4: microbial communities growing in different exudates selectively remove different dissolved combined neutral sugar (DCNS) components
column5: whereby the bacterial communities growing on algal exudates have significantly higher utilization rates of the sugar components which were enriched in the respective algal exudates. Analysis of microbial community composition identifies clear differentiation between the communities selected for by algae exudates and those growing on coral exudates or seawater controls. Macroalgae fostered rapid growth of less diverse communities and selected for copiotrophic bacterial populations with more opportunistic pathogens – so called super-heterotrophic communities (; ). In contrast coral exudates engendered a smaller shift in bacterioplankton community structure and maintained relatively high diversity.

column1: The microbial landscape on tropical reefs
column2: however
column3: is not only restricted to the water column directly adjacent to the reef benthos (∼105–106 cm-3) (; ). In addition to microbes associated with benthic macro-organisms (>107 cm-2 surface area) ()
column4: those associated with calcareous reef sands (∼109 cm-3) (; ; ; ) and the vast porous reef structures in the reef matrix () may also play a significant role in biogeochemical cycling (; ; ; ). Surface associated microbes may carry out multiple ecological functions
column5: such as nitrogen fixation or inhibition of potential pathogens () for their host organisms. The benthic microbial communities
column6: living in the reef structure or reef sands
column7: on the other hand have been recognized as important components for the reef community
column8: as they are capable of rapidly reallocating nutrients in the otherwise oligotrophic tropical reef environments (). They also may constitute an essential food source for protists and invertebrates
column9: forming the base of benthic food webs (). Next to remineralization and redistribution of nutrients
column10: recent studies have emphasized the role of the benthic microbial communities as important primary producers in these ecosystems (; ; ; ).

column1: Like the planktonic microbial communities
column2: benthic microbes
column3: although significantly different in community composition ()
column4: are also known to be affected by primary producer derived exudates (; ). However
column5: their responses to these different types of organic matter have rarely been investigated () and only one pilot study addressed these questions in the context of changing reef environments (). Additionally
column6: production
column7: respiration and the contribution to the nutrient pool have been assessed independently for all of the above described groups
column8: but their relative contribution to collective reef metabolism and their effects on each other have not been investigated on a community scale. Although there have been previous attempts to quantify primary production budgets in coral reefs (e.g.
column9: ; ; ; ) they did not account for how microbial metabolism associated with different reef organisms may influence these processes at a landscape scale (; ).

column1: The goal of the present study was to quantify biochemical processes and metabolic rates of both benthic and planktonic microbial communities across different benthic assemblages over diurnal cycles. To verify the findings of our controlled incubation experiments we simultaneously conducted a series of in situ measurements
column2: using collapsible benthic isolation tents (cBITs)
column3: which allow for continuous monitoring of enclosed portions of the reef benthos. These data present the first comprehensive assessment of how benthic primary producers influence surrounding seawater chemistry directly through metabolic processes and indirectly via changing the microbial landscape and metabolism in this ecosystem. Our study thus provides a system-wide overview of potential biochemical alterations facilitated by different primary producer communities on coral reefs.

column1: This study was conducted at the Richard B. Gump South Pacific Research Station located on the north shore of the island of Mo‘orea
column2: French Polynesia (17.48 S 149.84 W) from 1 to 22 September 2011. The high volcanic island of Mo‘orea is encircled by a barrier reef approximately 1 km offshore
column3: thereby creating a semi-enclosed backreef system. The reef ecosystem consists of an outer reef slope and a lagoon system comprising a backreef platform with average water depths of 3 m
column4: and a fringing reef bordering the island (; ). The benthic community on the backreef platform
column5: our main area of investigation
column6: is composed of approximately 68.6 ± 4.9% turf- and fleshy macroalgae
column7: 22.7 ± 4.4% hermatypic coral and 8.1 ± 2.0% sand (http://mcr.lternet.edu/data). Average daytime (06:00 to 18:00 h) PAR availability in the backreef study area during the entire study period was ∼580 µmol quanta m-2 s-1 as measured in LUX in a 5 min resolution with HOBO® Pendant UA-002-64 light and temperature loggers at the water depth were organisms were collected (2.0–2.5 m). Lux were converted to µmol quanta m-2 s-1 PAR according to the approximation established by : 1 mmol quanta m-2 s-1 400–700 nm = 51.2 LUX). Average backreef in situ water temperature at this depth was 26.8 ± 0.6°C with diurnal fluctuations of 2.4 ± 0.4°C. These values were later used as reference to ensure natural light and temperature conditions during incubation experiments.

column1: Samples from four different species of benthic primary producers
column2: each representing one of a major functional group in this backreef system
column3: were collected using SCUBA. The investigated species comprised (1) a hermatypic coral
column4: Pocillopora damicornis
column5: (2) a crustaceous coralline red alga (CCA) Hydrolithon reinboldii
column6: (3) a common fleshy macroalga in the backreef system
column7: Dictyota ceylanica
column8: and (4) a typical mixed consortium of turf algae. All specimens were collected from the backreef platform approximately 500–1000 m east of Paopao Bay (Cook’s Bay) from water depths of 2.0–2.5 m and transferred in coolers to cultivation tanks within 1 h using watertight zippered polyethylene bags. Specimens were collected in replicates of at least 20. Dictyota specimens and CCA
column9: growing as rhodoliths
column10: were collected as whole individuals. For turfing algae pieces of reef structure at least 95% covered by the algae were collected. Fragments of Pocillopora colonies were collected using pliers
column11: with each fragment from a different colony. Pocillopora fragments were then fixed onto ceramic tiles using small amounts of coral cement (Instant Ocean
column12: Holdfast® Epoxy Stick) in such a way that only living coral tissue was exposed to the incubation waters. All samples were collected 5 days prior to the respective incubation experiments and incubated in common flow-through ambient water tanks to allow for healing of potential tissue lesions. Algal overgrowth on the ceramic tiles and glue junction was removed regularly and all specimens were carefully checked for potential infestation of epibionts or endolithic boring organisms to avoid potential confounding effects on experimental results. Samples of all primary producers were chosen in a way that they had comparable surface areas with an average of 87.3 ± 8.8 cm2.

column1: Each benthic specimen was incubated in an individual beaker following  with minor modifications as follows. Primary producer incubations were conducted over a 24 h cycle to assess variations in daytime and night production rates. At dusk (1900 h) each specimen was placed into a randomized beaker containing 920 ml freshly collected filter sterilized backreef seawater (sterilized by passage through pre-flushed 142 mm polyethersulfone filters; 0.2 µm pore size). To measure initial DOC concentration
column2: water was sampled from each beaker with an acid-washed HDPE syringe (60 ml) and filtered through a GF/F filter (Whatman; 0.7 µm nominal pore size) into precombusted glass storage vials with acid-washed Teflon septa. Parallel seawater controls were identical with no organism added. Primary producers were incubated overnight 12 h and DOC samples were again collected at ∼0700 h of the following day. Primary producers were then incubated for another 12 h in the remaining 800 ml seawater. Final DOC samples were taken at around 1900 h. Specimens were then removed from the beakers using acid-washed forceps and the remaining incubation water was processed for the different exudate incubations (see below). Surface area and volume of all specimens (DOC and DO/pH incubations) were determined using the method described in detail by . Specimen volume was always <3% of incubation waters.

column1: Parallel to the DOC release incubations
column2: assessments of primary producer physiology on DO values of the surrounding water column were conducted. These incubations were set up simultaneously with and identical to the above described DOC incubation with the only difference being that beakers were sealed airtight with a low-density polyethylene film (Saran™) over the course of the experiment. The two parallel experiments allowed for reliable measurements of DOC release and algal physiology without the potential for contamination of DOC measurements across beakers (). Initial DO readings were obtained from each beaker using a HACH LANGE HQ40 multiparameter instrument (DO: precision 0.01 mg l-1
column3: accuracy ±0.05%). DO readings were obtained in parallel to DOC sampling (1900 h
column5: 1900 h) following the protocol described by .

column1: To resolve diel effects of different primary producer exudates on the ambient backreef microbial community and water chemistry
column2: 48 h benthic and planktonic dilution culture incubations were conducted under natural light and in the dark. The remaining seawater from each replicate primary producer incubation beaker was filter-sterilized through a pre-flushed (1 L low-organic deionized water; Barnstead Nanopure) 0.2 µm polyethersulfone filter (Pall SUPOR-200) and then inoculated with (a) freshly collected unfiltered backreef seawater (100 mL incubation water : 40 mL inoculum) and (b) with freshly collected sand (15 mL sand : 125 mL incubation water) to add a compositionally-representative ambient planktonic or benthic microbial community to the sample exudate media
column3: respectively. Of the four identical replicates generated in this way for each specimen one incubation vessel was then kept in the dark and a congruent sample under natural daylight conditions
column4: both at in situ temperature over a time period of 48 h. Samples for DOC (DOC plus bacterial carbon) analysis and bacterial cell abundance were taken immediately after combining the exudates with the respective microbial inoculum and again at the end of the experiment. Bacterioplankton abundance was converted to carbon units assuming 20 fg C cell-1 () and total DOC concentrations in the exudate incubation cultures was calculated by subtracting bacterial carbon from the measured DOC. All organic carbon samples (30 mL) were collected in precombusted glass vials and immediately stored at −20°C for up to four months until analysis via high temperature catalytic oxidation according to . Additional samples for bacterial cell abundances (1–2 mL each) in the incubations were collected every 12 h over the 48 h incubation period. Bacterial abundance samples were immediately fixed in 0.5% paraformaldehyde and flash frozen at −80°C. Bacterial samples were stored for up to 2 months
column5: and counted after 1X SYBR Green I (Invitrogen) staining via flow cytometry according to . A parallel incubation with the remaining two congruent samples for each specimen was performed to determine DO fluxes. The remaining inoculated sub-samples were transferred to ground-glass stoppered bottles (Wheaton BOD) and initial oxygen concentration of each sub-sample was determined as described above. Samples were kept airtight under natural light conditions or in the dark at in situ temperature (26.6 ± 0.5°C) alongside DOC/microbial incubations. After 48 h DO values were measured again to assess microbially mediated oxygen fluxes.

column1: Collapsible benthic isolation tents (cBITs) were used to assess effects of specific benthic communities in situ. The triangular pyramids

column8: DO (precision 0.01 mg l-1

column11: and conductivity (accuracy ±1%

column14: connected to a Niskin sampling bottle
column15: served also as a sampling port (). Samples for DO

column17: and bacterial abundance were collected every 24 h and processed as describe above. Five cBITs were set up simultaneously over 3 deployment periods (n = 15). Tents were mounted over selected benthic communities
column18: comprising coral dominated
column19: algae dominated
column20: and sand dominated areas. Locations were chosen such that on each deployment period at least one representative of each of the respective benthic communities was included. After each deployment the benthic community enclosed by the cBITs was photographed for later determination benthic community composition. Photographs were processed using the digital image software Image J

column1: Statistics were performed using SAS within the software package JMP (v10; SAS Institute 1989–2011). All statistical tests were conducted on log-transformed data to meet assumptions of normality. We tested whether derived rates differed from control treatments using analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by Dunnett’s post hoc test with α = 0.05. To test whether rates differed among the treatments we used Tukey’s post hoc tests with α = 0.05. All rates are given in mean ± standard error (SE) where applicable.

Mean rates of oxygen production during daylight by primary producers ranged from 24.5 ± 0.9 µmol L-1 h-1 (Pocillopora) to 35.1 ± 2.5 µmol L-1 h-1 (Dictyota). Rates of DOC release ranged from 0.9 ± 0.3 µmol L-1 h-1 (Pocillopora) to 5.5 ± 1.8 µmol L-1 h-1 (Dictyota) during daytime and 0.3 ± 0.3 µmol L-1 h-1 (CCA) to 1.2 ± 0.3 µmol L-1 h-1 (Dictyota) during night. Previous work has described how antagonistic coral reef associated ecosystem engineers () like coral and non-calcifying algae have the potential to alter biogeochemical cycling and microbial processes (). While previous work has measured rates of benthic primary production (e.g.
