column2: cell density and community composition| Kenkel  Carly D. |2018-12-3|"Cooperation between species has played a fundamental role in the evolution and diversification of life (; ; ; ). In the case of reef-building corals
column3: the intracellular symbiosis between dinoflagellates in the genus Symbiodinium and a calcifying Cnidarian host forms the basis of one of the most biodiverse and productive ecosystems on the planet (; ). The process of host calcification
column4: which builds the three dimensional structure of the reef
column5: is largely powered by symbiont primary productivity (). However
column6: climate change and other anthropogenic processes threaten reefs because of the sensitivity of the coral-dinoflagellate symbiosis to environmental stress (; )
column7: indicating that host-symbiont cooperation is not stable over ecological timescales.

column1: Recent work has suggested that a transition to parasitism may precipitate the breakdown of the host-symbiont relationship ()
column2: but this is not a unique feature of the coral symbiosis. Across taxa
column3: mutualisms are better defined as a spectrum that ranges from negative parasitic interactions to mutually beneficial symbioses
column4: both within the context of a focal inter-species interaction and when comparing relationships across taxa (; ; ; ). Less well-resolved
column5: particularly for the coral-Symbiodinium symbiosis
column6: are the mechanisms that promote and maintain positive interactions between partners (; ).

column1: One major factor predicted to influence levels of cooperation is the mode of symbiont transmission (; ). In corals as in other symbioses
column2: two transmission modes predominate: symbionts can be acquired horizontally from the local environment
column3: usually during a defined larval stage
column4: or vertically from parents
column5: typically through the maternal germ line (reviewed in ). Virulence theory predicts that horizontal transmission allows symbionts to adopt selfish strategies
column6: potentially harmful to the host (). A transition from horizontal to vertical transmission is predicted to align the reproductive interests of partners (via partner-fidelity feedback sensu ) and optimize resource sharing to maximize holobiont (the combination of host and symbiont) fitness (; ; ).

column1: Experimental manipulations of transmission mode in other systems have provided empirical support for a reduction in pathogen virulence under enforced vertical transmission scenarios (; ; ; ). Bacteriophages forced into vertical transmission evolved lower virulence and lost the capacity to transmit horizontally (). Similarly
column2: Symbiodinium microadriaticum under an experimentally enforced horizontal transmission regime proliferated faster within their Cassiopea jellyfish hosts while reducing host reproduction and growth (). However
column3: studies attempting to quantify the evolutionary consequences of natural shifts in transmission mode remain rare
column4: with Herre’s demonstration of a negative relationship between vertical transmission and virulence in nematodes that parasitize fig wasps a notable exception ().

column1: Reef-building corals are a potential system in which to study naturally occurring transitions in transmission mode in a mutualistic symbiosis. Corals (Cnidaria: Anthozoa: Scleractinia) are colonial animals that harbour intracellular populations of dinoflagellate algae in the genus Symbiodinium. This symbiosis is considered obligate as the breakdown of the relationship between host animals and their intracellular Symbiodinium algae
column2: commonly known as coral bleaching
column3: has major fitness consequences for both partners
column4: and can be lethal (reviewed in ). This inter-species partnership is ancient (evolved ∼250 Mya ()
column5: prolific (600+ coral species worldwide ()
column6: and constitutes the foundation of one of the most bio-diverse ecosystems on the planet. The majority of coral species (∼85%) acquire their Symbiodinium horizontally from the local environment in each generation (). However
column7: vertical transmission has independently evolved at least four times
column8: such that both transmission strategies can be exhibited by different coral species within the same genus (; ).

column1: We compared physiological components of cooperation and fitness proxies between horizontal and vertical transmitters (VTs) in a phylogenetically controlled design using three pairs of related coral species exhibiting different strategies: (1) Galaxea acrhelia (VT) and Galaxea astreata (horizontal transmitter
column2: HT); (2) Porites lobata (VT) and Goniopora columna (HT); (3) Montipora aequituberculata (VT) and Acropora millepora (HT). Species comparisons were drawn from the same or sister genera and replicate comparisons from more distantly related clades (). Species also represented a diversity of reproductive modes (e.g. broadcast spawner
column3: brooder)
column4: sexual systems (e.g. hermaphroditic
column5: gonochoric)
column6: and morphologies (e.g. massive
column7: branching)
column8: and host different subclades of Symbiodinium ()
column9: such that mode of symbiont transmission was the only consistent difference between pairs (; ; ). We quantified changes in symbiont cell density
column10: photosynthetic function and translocation of photosynthetically fixed carbon between symbionts and hosts. We defined cooperation as the proportion of photosynthetically fixed carbon translocated to the host
column11: while the degree of symbiont parasitism was calculated as the difference in the proportion of photosynthetically fixed carbon translocated to hosts between control and heat-treated samples at the end of the experiment
column12: sensu . Bleaching
column13: or the reduction in symbiont density in response to sustained thermal stress was used as a proxy for holobiont fitness. While we observed differences in host-symbiont cooperation
column14: both at a baseline level and during the onset of bleaching
column15: vertically transmitting species did not exhibit universally elevated levels of cooperation. Additional post hoc analysis of Symbiodinium ITS-2 diversity among coral species was therefore used to investigate whether symbiont community composition could better explain physiological trait patterns. Symbiont community composition did not explain a significant portion of the variation in physiological components of cooperation or fitness proxies; however
column16: diversity tended to be associated with the degree of symbiont parasitism at the onset of bleaching
column17: suggesting that reduced genetic diversity of symbionts

column1: Understanding variation in the degree of cooperation between corals and their Symbiodinium will be critical for assessing survival potential among species and populations in the face of increasing environmental change (). As in other mutualisms (; ; )
column2: vertical transmission has been proposed as an evolutionary mechanism for enhancing holobiont fitness in the Cnidarian–algal symbiosis (). However
column3: we did not observe universally consistent differences in cooperation between vertical and horizontally transmitting species. The vertically transmitting P. lobata and Galaxea acrhelia exhibited the highest levels of carbon translocation in ambient conditions
column4: which we interpret as symbiont-host cooperation
column5: significantly more so than their respective horizontally transmitting counterparts
column6: Goniopora columna and Galaxea astreata; but cooperation was not different between M. aequituberculata and A. millepora
column7: and tended to be higher in the latter horizontally transmitting species. However
column8: species-specific photosynthesis-irradiance curves were not measured and the potential for interactions between photophysiology and baseline rates of carbon translocation must also be explored. We also expected that the degree of breakdown in the host-symbiont relationship under heat stress
column9: which we interpret as a transition towards parasitism sensu ()
column10: would be comparatively intensified in horizontally transmitting species
column11: but again
column12: this was not the case. P. lobata did exhibit the least change in carbon translocation in spite of showing the same signs of bleaching as other species
column13: but there was no difference in comparison to Goniopora columna which also largely maintained its baseline translocation under elevated temperature. In addition
column14: the greatest relative declines in the proportion of carbon translocated from symbionts to hosts actually occurred in the other two vertically transmitting species
column15: Galaxea acrhelia and M. aequituberculata.

column1: Although we did not find significant support for the role of vertical transmission we still observed significant differences in cooperation among our six focal species

We therefore conducted a series of post hoc analyses to explore other putative drivers of differential cooperation and thermal tolerance: differences in Symbiodinium cell density and/or in symbiont community composition.

column1: The density of symbiont cells was recently proposed as a major driver underpinning the degree of cooperation between coral hosts and symbionts and the functional response of the coral symbiosis to environmental stressors (). Studies in other species have shown that corals with greater initial Symbiodinium cell densities
column2: as quantified by the symbiont to host cell ratio
column3: are subsequently associated with greater bleaching severity in response to elevated thermal stress (; ). This association has been hypothesized to result from the proportional increase in oxidative cellular stress: more symbionts yield more reactive oxygen species when the photosynthetic machinery is overloaded ()

column5: adds another potential explanation. They showed that under non-limiting nutrient conditions

column7: but that the metabolic costs were borne by the coral hosts ()
column8: supporting the prediction that a transition to parasitism precedes unsustainable proliferation of the symbiont community which ultimately results in bleaching ().

column2: conspecific corals hosting clade D Symbiodinium exhibit greater thermal tolerance than those hosting C1 or C2-types (; )

column1: Quantifying cooperation between symbiotic partners in terms of biologically realistic costs and benefits remains an outstanding question for many symbioses (). The transfer of photosynthetically fixed carbon has long been known as a major cooperative benefit to the coral host as up to 95% of a coral’s energy requirements can be met through this mechanism (); however
column2: reciprocal products shared by hosts with their symbionts remain largely unknown (). Similarly
column3: heterotrophic feeding can offset the need for symbiont-derived carbon in some species and in these cases other symbiont-derived metabolites may be more critical for host fitness (). Substantial variation in both intra- and inter-specific bleaching thresholds ()
column4: suggests that levels of cooperation between host and symbiont may also vary. Significant work has gone into investigating coral bleaching over the past three decades
column5: yet fundamental questions remain unresolved (). Ultimately
column6: a greater understanding of both fine-scale interactions between coral hosts and symbionts and the evolutionary and ecological mechanisms that maintain and strengthen cooperation will be essential for managing these ecosystems (; ).

column1: This study investigated whether corals employing vertical symbiont transmission also exhibit enhanced cooperation and holobiont fitness. Contrary to theoretical predictions
column2: we did not find significant support for the role of vertical transmission in spite of significant differences in cooperation among our six focal species. In a post hoc analysis of other drivers
column3: we found that a greater initial symbiont cell density was associated with a greater bleaching intensity
column4: but this association did not appear to result from an alteration of host-symbiont cooperation. Rather
column5: the reduction in cooperation across species at the onset of bleaching was marginally associated with symbiont community diversity. The theoretical benefits of evolving vertical transmission are based on the underlying assumption that the host-symbiont relationship becomes genetically uniform
column6: thereby reducing competition among symbionts. Taken together
column7: our results suggest that it may not be vertical transmission per se that influences host-symbiont cooperation
column8: but genetic uniformity of the symbiont community
column9: though future work is needed to directly test this hypothesis.

column4: and forestry (; ). Species classification models have been a long-standing challenge in remote sensing of forests
column5: with complexity especially in dense forests
column6: due to weakly defined edges among trees
column7: large intra-class variance in tree representation
column8: and high local diversity within a forest. Early work used spectral features
column9: texture-based features
column10: random forests and support-vector-machines
column11: band ratios within the visible and NIR
column12: and other crafted-features (; ; ; ). These initial models often focused on higher-order taxonomic labels
column13: such as ‘Birch’ vs. ‘Pine’
column14: and were limited to less than 10 classes (; ). The emergence of deep learning networks in computer vision
column15: combined with greater data availability
column16: led to a large number of publications combining deep learning with a variety of sensors and data acquisition platforms (; ; ; ). A defining challenge of individual species classification is the fine-grained nature of the task
column17: with subtle differences among co-occurring species
column18: often within the same taxonomic genus. This challenge is compounded by a lack of training data
column19: especially for rarer species
column20: and the natural long-tailed nature of biodiversity leads to a massive imbalance between dominant and rare classes. While there are recent efforts to combat class-imbalance in machine learning for ecology (; ; )
column21: there remains significant areas for improvement before models can be used for operational analysis at the landscape and continental scales.

column1: Data science competitions are a unique way to advance image processing methods for particular applications (). These competitions provide a standardized dataset and criteria for evaluation
column2: and have the potential to draw expertise from different application domains because of the focus on data science tasks that are found in many applications (; ; ). While competitions have allowed for the advancement of many applications in data science
column3: ecology is just beginning to use this format for democratizing method-building (e.g.
column5: largely due to the recent availability of large
column6: openly available ecological datasets such as from the National Ecological Observatory Network (NEON).

column1: The National Ecological Observatory Network is a 30-year effort of the National Science Foundation to collect standardized organismal
column2: biogeochemical
column3: and remote sensing data over 81 sites in the US from 20 distinct ecoclimatic domains (). The data provided by NEON covers a broad array of ecosystem components including field data on trees and associated airborne remote sensing imagery. NEON data is ideal for use in data science competitions because it is openly available
column4: well documented
column5: and part of a massive
column6: continental-scale data collection effort. Therefore
column7: methods and lessons learned from the competition can be applied to a large-scale open dataset being used by large numbers of researchers.

column1: The first competition using data from the NEON was run in 2017 and was aimed at generating species predictions of individual tree crowns in a single temperate forest (). The 2017 competition was instrumental in advancing methods and in establishing a framework for providing data and evaluating submissions. The 2017 competition identified the most effective methods for delineating tree crowns from airborne remote sensing data
column2: aligning delineations to field data
column3: and assigning a species label to delineations. The results of the 2017 competition showed the most successful approaches to species classification require data cleaning to remove noise and outliers prior to analysis
column4: and incorporate uncertainty in predictions show the most promise for future applications.

column1: While the 2017 competition was an important step towards better methods for converting remote sensing to ecological information
column2: it has limited practical application because it was limited to a single site and relied on a small and labor-intensive field dataset collected. Classification methods will be most useful in generating ecological data of individual trees if they achieve high accuracy when: (1) trained on standard forest inventory and remote sensing data
column3: (2) applied across large spatial scales and diverse forest types
column4: and (3) when making predictions in forests where the models have not been trained. The complexity of using standard forest inventory data across multiple sites presents challenges for model performance because of the highly imbalanced multi-species datasets
column5: differences in the species present at different sites
column6: and variability in the remote sensing data due to differences in conditions when the data were collected. While the classification remains critical to the needs of ecologists
column7: an expansion in the diversity of sites and data is required to effectively achieve this task.

column1: To address these needs
column2: a new iteration of the 2017 competition was run that focused on classification using a dataset that allowed for within and cross site evaluation using multiple metrics. This current iteration of the competition uses data from three NEON sites in the southeastern United States to compare how well methods perform on standard forest inventory data at one site
column3: and how well methods perform when applied to a new site. Here we present the results of the competition that includes a comparison of scores from participating teams
column4: a summary of the methods used
column5: and a discussion of how this competition advances our ability to classify individual trees using existing inventory and remote sensing data.

The competition used multiple NEON data products from three sites in combination with data collected by members from our research team. The three NEON sites in the southeastern United States () used in this study are part of three separate NEON ecoclimatic domains and represent distinct environmental

column4: Florida (OSBS

column6: 03) is a mixed forest of hardwood and conifers and is primarily managed for maintaining upland pine forests. The canopy of the pine forests is dominated by Longleaf pine (Pinus palustris) and subcanopy Turkey oak (Quercus laevis) with a grass and forb understory. The forests are on deep sandy soil and are managed with prescribed fires at 3–4 year intervals (). More mesic forests are also present at the site

column9: Appalachians and Cumberland Plateau domain

column1: The competition used NEON data from two standard collections; remote sensing data and field-collected data. The remote sensing data were generated by the NEON Airborne Observation Platform (AOP) and are provided as four different products
column2: each one measuring different properties of the vegetation and the ground surface (
column3: ). The AOP data products are high-resolution orthorectified camera imagery (RGB)
column4: discrete return LiDAR point clouds (LAS)
column5: LiDAR-derived canopy height model raster (CHM)
column6: and spectrometer orthorectified surface directional reflectance—mosaic hyperspectral surface reflectance (HSI). The data products were downloaded using the NEON API and the neonUtilities R package (). We used the most recent data from the start of the competition: April 2019 for TALL
column7: September 2019 for OSBS
column8: and May 2018 for MLBS. NEON aims to collect airborne data for a minimum of 100 sq km at each site during peak vegetation greenness
column9: when the solar angle is above 40 degrees
column10: and with less than 10% cloud cover (). For this competition
column11: 20 m × 20 m image subsets were extracted from the original 1 square km tiles downloaded from NEON. Each 20 m × 20 m image subset is either associated with a NEON field plot or with trees that occur outside NEON field plots but were manually mapped in the field by the research team.

column1: The species labels in the training data were collected through the NEON Terrestrial Observation System (TOS). The data contain information on individual tree identifiers
column2: location of trees relative to sampling locations (i.e.
column3: distance and azimuth from a central location)
column4: species and genus labels
column5: and measures of salient structural attributes. The field attribute that was directly used in the competition was the taxonomic species information that is described by its scientific name
column6: which includes a genus and species classification. To simplify the taxonomic species information
column7: each scientific name is simplified to its unique taxonomic identification code (taxonID). More information about the data products and the field data and the list of species classes and taxonomic codes is provided in .

column1: Participants were given bounding boxes labeled with the taxonomic species in the training dataset and generated species predictions for unclassified bounding boxes in the test dataset. Each bounding box represents an individual tree crown (ITC) and was generated by the research team since they are not a standard NEON product. Because ITC data are time-intensive and difficult to generate
column2: the research team used a combination of two different approaches to produce both a reasonably large number of labeled data for training and precise data for evaluation. Both ITC datasets were generated by experts who are familiar with the ecology of the sites.

column1: To generate ITC data for training the research team in the lab visualized multiple remote sensing datasets and field inventory data from NEON for all tree crowns in the 20 m × 20 m image subsets to draw boxes round individual tree crowns. The 20 m × 20 m subsets were located at NEON field plots (specifically over a subplot of the distributed plots) for which surveys of geolocalized tree stems were available from the NEON vegetation structure dataset. The locations of these plots was determined by NEON through a stratified-random spatial balanced design as to capture the variation of the site and allow for robust statistical analysis of the data (; ; ). In the training data
column2: the average distance from one plot to its nearest plot is 246 m. Plot data and locations are available from NEON and maps of each site and the locations of the plots are included in .

column1: The training data had 409 ITCs from 39 plots at OSBS and 648 ITCs from 46 plots at MLBS (). Each ITC bounding box was assigned a species label based on the location of individual stem data in the NEON vegetation structure dataset that was determined to match the ITC. To minimize the chance of mislabelling bounding boxes we limited assignment of species labels to boxes that: (1) could clearly be linked to a single stem from the field or a single species in cases where multiple conspecific stems could be candidates; (2) where the field stem was not labeled as fully shaded; (3) where the field stem was not labeled as dead; and (4) where the height of the field stem was not more than 4 m lower than the maximum value of the LiDAR-based canopy height model within the bounding box (when field stem height was available). Those bounding boxes that did not qualify were labeled as unknown species and were not used for the species classification task.

column1: Importantly
column2: for this study ITC data can be summarized as polygons that represent the spatial extent of individual tree canopies. We provided 2-dimensional rectangular polygons (i.e.
column3: bounding boxes) with four vertices at the maximum North/South and East/West directions as a proxy of individual crowns. This strategy is different from what is commonly used for forest and remote sensing methods that use more detailed polygons with many vertices to delineate more precise crown boundaries and shape (e.g.
column4: ). We provide bounding boxes rather than multi-vertex polygons because boxes are a common output of most computer vision methods to identify
column5: extract
column6: and classify objects in an image (). In this way the competition allowed for models to be developed on training data from separate crown detection and delineation methods.

column1: All teams were allowed up to four submissions per task. Submissions made prior to the final submission were evaluated and scores were returned. Pre-submissions were allowed to ensure submissions were properly formatted and provide teams with feedback on model performance. The final submission deadline was extended by 2 months after the train and test data were released. This was done to allow teams more time to work with the data given the challenges associated with COVID-19. The number of pre-submissions was limited to reduce the chance of artificially increasing performance indirectly by iteratively learning method performance from the test set. The number of pre-submissions varied by team
column2: with five pre-submissions from the Fujitsu and Intellisense CAU teams (an additional submission allowed due to timeline extension)
column3: four submissions for Jeepers Treepers
column4: and two for Más JALApeñoS.

column1: The data were split into training and testing datasets where the training data allowed for the development and self-evaluation of models and the testing data was used to evaluate the team methods. Training data included ITCs for the OSBS and MLBS sites
column2: which consisted of 1057 ITC delineations with taxonomic species labels for 85 plots and all remote sensing data products (clips of 20 m × 20 m around each plot;
column3: “Train”). Data were split at the plot-level where all ITCs within a plot were assigned as train or test and therefore spatially distant from each other. This was to reduce the effects of spatial autocorrelation in model development due to the similarity of neighboring pixels (). Participants could use any of the remote sensing and field data for training their models since this represents a common scenario where models are developed using data from inventory plots. No TALL data were provided in the training data. The testing data provided to the participants were 353 separate plots with associated remote sensing data
column4: and 585 ITC delineations at the OSBS
column5: MLBS
column6: and TALL sites (
column7: “Test-provided”). The ground truth species labels were withheld from the teams and used for evaluation by the research team (
column8: “Test-ground truth”). Participants submitted the probability of each ITC belonging to one of the taxonomic classes (
column9: “Test-submitted”). The predictions were submitted as a probability from 0 to 100% that the ITC belonged to the associated species class. Providing the ITC bounding boxes kept this task focused on classification methods rather than having participants also incorporate detection and delineation approaches prior to or after classification.

column1: Significant features of this dataset
column2: and forest remote sensing data in general
column3: are class imbalance in the training data
column4: and a difference in species composition and relative abundances between the training data and the test data. Due to the nature of these data
column5: the ability to train on imbalanced data and predict species with species identities and abundances that differ between the training and testing datasets is an important challenge addressed in the competition. The training dataset for the OSBS and MLBS sites had a total of 33 distinct species classes and two genus classes where the species was unknown (Pinus and Quercus)
column6: ranging from 1–302 individuals per class (). This distribution represents the composition and relative abundance of canopy trees in the NEON plots and therefore the data available from forest inventory plots that are used to develop and test classification models. The test data for OSBS and MLBS both show unequal distributions of data among species classes. The test data for both sites include 15 species in the training data
column7: and both sites include species in the test data that are not part of the training data (OSBS: 11 species
column8: MLBS: five species). Furthermore
column9: while the test data for TALL has less imbalance across the species classes than the training data at OSBS and MLBS
column10: it includes only 10 of the species from the training data and introduces 11 new species that are not part of the training data ( as the “Other” class). All new species in the test data have few samples and therefore could not be included in the test and train data. In this way
column11: the external TALL site tests not only the ability of the models to be applied to new remote sensing data
column12: but also to a new site with different species composition.

Two additional challenges for applying methods to the untrained site were differences in species composition and spectral variation among sites. Species that occur in the test data but not in the train data are grouped together in an “Other” class in the test data. Creating a mixed-species “Other” class that contains species with low samples is a common practice in species mapping because there is insufficient data to accurately train and test each class individually (for example ). Participants were allowed to include a species class with the label “Other” in their submissions. The “Other” class can be used to indicate a probability that an ITC is a species that is not represented in the training data and is therefore likely a new species in the test dataset that was not seen in the train dataset. Finally
column2: spectral differences among the sites and training and testing data are also an important feature of the dataset that could impact the ability to apply methods to an untrained site ().

column1: Classification was evaluated with three class-level metrics to assess the performance across teams; two hard-classification metrics that require a single species class label for each crown () and one soft-classification metric that uses the probability of a crown belonging to any trained species. Accuracy
column2: a common metric in remote sensing classification studies
column3: is the number of samples correctly predicted out of the total number of samples. Since accuracy does not take into account the class-level scores
column4: accuracy is influenced by variable performance and sample size of classes. In this dataset
column5: the class PIPA2 (Pinus palustris) is the dominant class so accuracy is heavily influenced by the model performance for that class. Alternatively
column6: a common classification evaluation metric that is not influenced by class sample size is macro F1. Macro F1 is an aggregate F1 metric of class-level performance
column7: where F1 is the harmonic mean of class precision and recall and is given by
column8: where P is precision
column9: R is recall
column10: TP is the number of true positives
column11: FP is the number of false positives
column12: and FN is the number of false negatives. Macro F1 is the unweighted mean of class F1 and is given by
column13: where C is the set of species classes
column14: Pc is the precision of species class c
column15: Rc is the recall of species class c
column16: and |C| is the number of elements of set C. Macro F1 is a useful evaluation metric when there is imbalance in the class size and variable prediction performance. In this dataset
column17: the class PIPA2 has the same influence as each class in the macro F1 score.

$$macro\; F1\; = \; \displaystyle{1 \over {\left| C \right|}}\mathop \sum \limits_{c \in C}^{} F{1_c}\; = \; \displaystyle{1 \over {\left| C \right|}}\mathop \sum \limits_{c \in C}^{} \displaystyle{{2{P_c}{R_c}} \over {{P_c} + {R_c}}}.\; \; \; \; \; \; \; \;$$macroF1=1|C|∑c∈C⁡F1c=1|C|∑c∈C2PcRcPc+Rc.
The final model-level metric is cross entropy loss
column2: which is a metric commonly used to quantify the performance of multi-class classifiers where all samples are predicted with a probability of belonging to each class. The metric measures the degree of uncertainty in the predictions of the model. Cross entropy loss (; ) is a good measure of model robustness particularly in cases where new classes are introduced into the test set because the metric can capture how the model responds to an increase in test data entropy. Models that have a stronger ability to differentiate between learned classes and new classes have lower cross entropy loss scores and can be considered more robust.

column1: Finally
column2: a confusion matrix of all predictions and class-level precision and recall scores were calculated for all team predictions combined to identify classes that are commonly confused across methods. Precision
column3: or user accuracy
column4: is the percentage of instances classified as positive that are actually positive. High precision means a low commission error for the class where there are few predictions that are not true. Recall
column5: or producer accuracy
column6: is the percentage of positive instances correctly classified as positive. High recall means a low omission error for the class where there are few missing predictions. Evaluation scores and confusion matrices were calculated with the scikit-learn package for python (). The evaluation code is available in the .

column1: A gamut of classification algorithms were used in the competition

column3: and then used an ensemble of a random forest classifier and a gradient boosting classifier (). This method was used to generate baseline results.

The crown level features were put through a 3-layer fully connected neural network with Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) activation and softmax output layer for the final species classification. The Jeepers Treepers team fused RGB
column5: HSI
column6: and LiDAR data into a neural network model. Their method first used RGB crown data to train a pre-trained (from ImageNet dataset) ResNet convolution neural network (CNN). The vector of probabilities derived from the ResNet was concatenated with the HSI reflectance pseudo-waveform data from the LiDAR point cloud. The concatenated vector was fed through a two-layer multi-layer perceptron with a customized soft-F1 loss function for final classification
column7: and predictions with high uncertainty were labeled as “Other”. The Más JALApeñoS team’s method applied the Extreme Gradient Boosting decision-tree method to HSI data that was first filtered at a pixel level using LiDAR heights. The height-filtered pixels were further filtered using PCA-based outlier removal before application of PCA based dimension reduction. The dimensionally reduced data was run through the Extreme Gradient Boosted model with parameters chosen with a partial grid search. Pixel class probabilities were averaged for the final crown classification. The “Other” class was generated during training by grouping less abundant species into a single “Other” class. Finally
column8: the Intellisense CAU team’s method was based on a one-dimensional CNN applied to HSI pixels. Small classes were resampled to handle the imbalance. The CNN consisted of a convolutional layer
column9: max-pooling layer
column10: a fully connected layer and output. The output was filtered using LiDAR data to remove ground pixels.

column1: Evaluation in the scientific literature of many remote sensing approaches to tree species classification tends to focus on a method for a single site and where all species classes are mutually exclusive and known. In this competition
column2: participants were asked to grapple with a challenging classification task
column3: specifically building models using forest inventory training data from multiple sites where there is imbalance in the class sizes
column4: and applying those models to a site where the models have not been trained. By establishing a dataset and evaluation process on which participants with different background can apply their methods
column5: we can evaluate and compare relative performance of different methods for this challenging task. While the high model accuracy scores relative to the baseline winning model from the 2017 competition shows an advancement of methods for tree species classification
column6: many species classes were poorly predicted
column7: especially when applying models to the untrained site.

column1: Two classification methods stood out in how they handled the challenges of imbalance data and application to an untrained site. The first-ranked team based on accuracy and macro F1 scores (Fujitsu Satellite) used a convolution neural network pipeline
column2: consisting of both a pixel and crown-level classifier. Stronger performance of CNNs over shallower machine learning methods for species classification of remote sensing data has been documented in many applications partly due to their ability to learn spatial features and reduced reliance on data pre-processing (). The Fujitsu Satellite team implemented a pixel and crown-level classifier and a unique random spatial data augmentation filter
column3: which is likely key to its success (). The first-ranked team based on cross entropy loss (MaS JALApeñoS) used a relatively simple pixel-level decision tree classifier with a partial grid search for best parameters. Despite a more shallow machine learning approach compared to CNNs the Extreme Gradient Boosting method may have been less overfit and while a many species labels may have been incorrect
column4: the certainty of those labels was also low
column5: resulting in a better cross entropy loss score. Finally
column6: while the approaches differed in many ways
column7: both approaches relied on hyperspectral reflectance and reduced the noise and complexity of the data
column8: and extracted relevant features of the 369 hyperspectral bands. This feature engineering was also a key lesson from the original competition in 2017.

column1: An inherent challenge with the classification of ecological data is the imbalance in the data across classes. Most natural forest ecosystems have an unequal abundance distribution of species
column2: with a “hollow curve” shaped distribution where there are a small number of common species
column3: and a large number of relatively rare species (). Ecological datasets often reflect this natural distribution since they are generated by randomly sampling plots in the field. Understanding patterns of taxonomic and functional diversity or evaluating the impact of climate changes and extreme disturbance events on species are examples where poor accuracy of rare species will impact the ability to use the predictions because of the uncertainty in the predictions.

column1: Our results reflect the common outcome of classification on unbalanced datasets
column2: with models generally performing better on classes that have a greater representation in the training data compared to classes with lower representation (; ; ). Evaluation metrics that are weighted by the number of samples per class
column3: such as accuracy
column4: favor models that are most accurate for abundant classes. However
column5: for many ecological questions and applications
column6: having strong predictions across all species
column7: especially the rare species is important (; ; )
column8: and therefore an evaluation score such as macro F1 that equally weighs all species classes
column9: is most appropriate. While model accuracy shows a relatively narrow range in performance among the species
column10: macro F1 shows the distinctly strong performance of two neural network methods (Fujitsu Satellite and Intellisense CAU
column11: ) that can discriminate patterns of some less abundant but taxonomically distinct species. In addition
column12: one of the teams that used a network approach and achieved a high macro F1 score (Intellisense CAU) addressed the imbalance by resampling the common classes
column13: which is a common method to reduce the effect of imbalanced data in model training.

column1: Another challenge addressed in this competition was transferability of the model to an untrained site
column2: where the site will most likely contain new taxonomic classes and introduce spectral variation within species
column3: especially if the untrained site is geographically distinct from the training sites. We found that across metrics
column4: all methods performed worse on the untrained site than the trained sites. While the decreased performance is partially due to the change in species classes
column5: even a dominant species (Longleaf pine
column6: PIPA2) was more poorly predicted at the untrained site. This suggests that regardless of differences in species presence and abundance between sites
column7: the spectral and structural signatures of individual species (caused by sensor calibration
column8: atmospheric conditions
column9: seasonal differences
column10: or inherent differences in species foliar and structural properties) are sufficiently different to hinder model performance.

column1: An encouraging result was the presence of methods with significantly lower cross entropy loss scores than other methods (gradient boosting by Más JALApeñoS and a neural network by Fujitsu)
column2: and that scores were similarly low for both the trained and untrained sites (). A low cross entropy loss score means that a method was confident with its correct predictions and unconfident with its incorrect predictions. Methods that score low in cross entropy loss could be most useful in transferring to new sites because low confidence in a prediction could indicate the presence of a new species.

column1: The imbalance of data and application of methods to an untrained site means classes will be present that are not in the training data. This challenge is often not directly addressed in species classification tasks in the remote sensing fields
column2: but it is studied in computer science as a type of novel class detection (). While this competition design could not fully evaluate the ability to detect untrained species
column3: we did employ the use of mixed-species “Other” class that were present only in the test data
column4: and teams used different approaches to predict this class by grouping trained species with low sample sizes or post-processing based on prediction uncertainty. The F1 scores for the “Other” species class are too low for accurately detecting these new species (~0.2
column6: yet most methods performed better at this task than our baseline approach from the 2017 competition where an untrained site and species were not part of the task. In addition
column7: methods generally performed better at identifying new species in the trained sites (OSBS and MLBS) than the untrained site (TALL). This dataset and competition can hopefully encourage the remote sensing community to continue to confront this real challenge present in individual tree species mapping.

column1: Finally
column2: this classification task was challenging due to limitations and complexities of the data. The complexities reflect the characteristics of data for real-world applications for which robust methods are needed. One common challenge for ecological applications is that the amount of field data for training and testing is often smaller than the optimal amount to train and robustly evaluate algorithms. We believe the most accurate data for training and evaluating crown delineation and classification models comes from laborious field efforts where individual tree crowns are delineated and species are identified in the field. Datasets like these are small and often limited to specific sites and studies. To overcome limited field data and create a sufficiently large dataset for the competition
column3: we generated a large set of image-delineated crowns to use as training data (see ). The certainty of these image-delineated ITCs
column4: especially for classification
column5: is less than for the field data because of uncertainty in associating information from field data on individual trees with the remote sensing data. The results showing confusion between two very different species () suggest that some of the training pixels identified as belonging to one species may in fact belong to another
column6: presenting challenges to classification models. We emphasize that this is an inherent challenge in ecological studies since high-quality data
column7: such as the field-delineated ITCs
column8: will always be limited
column9: and therefore there is a need for methods to account for this source of potential uncertainty. Future efforts should be made to support improved alignment between field and remote sensing datasets (). For example
column10: when collecting data in the field
column11: there could be an attribute that specifies if a tree has a position in the canopy and is therefore viewable in remote sensing imagery. Additionally
column12: tree crowns could be digitized in remote sensing data while in the field to avoid any uncertainty and build robust datasets (). Algorithmic approaches may also help address these issues including research in image analysis in classification and detection with label uncertainty (; )
column13: active learning for adding new labels or reviewing existing labels and the inclusion of a self- or semi-supervised step in the learning processes (; ).

column1: This competition engaged researchers in the data science

column3: and ecology communities to train and apply algorithms for a classification task in the context of individual tree crown species across multiple sites. Participants focused on deep learning approaches
column4: many of which were significantly better at cross-site prediction than the best method from the 2017 competition. By comparing predictions from all teams on the same dataset
column5: we found that the deep learning and more traditional decision tree methods can predict the most common class well
column6: even across sites
column7: but more work is needed in methods that can handle imbalanced data
column8: can predict rare species (i.e.
column9: those with lower relative abundances)
column10: and are robust to identifying the presence of new species when applied to an untrained site.

24|10.7717/peerj.179|Decadal changes and delayed avian species losses due to deforestation in the northern Neotropics| Shaw  David W. |2013-10-8|Deforestation is one of the main threats to biodiversity conservation. Forest loss and fragmentation have caused declines or local extinctions among animal species at many locations (; ; ). Local population declines and extirpations may be the most important leading indicators of biodiversity loss (; ). Bird losses have been documented in many forest systems (e.g.
column2: ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ). Perhaps nowhere has this phenomenon been more noticeable than among tropical forests
column3: where species losses have been documented in numerous taxonomic groups (e.g.
column4: ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ). Species losses can occur at the landscape or patch levels and depend on the intensity of the change in forest cover
column5: the distance to and size of other forest fragments
column6: shape and size of the fragment
column7: and other factors (; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ). Tropical forest species
column8: which often occur in small
column9: low-density populations
column10: may be particularly vulnerable to extirpation (; ; ).

column1: Relatively few studies have assessed changes through decades
column2: however (). And although deforestation and fragmentation can occur over a short period
column3: some time may pass before species begin to disappear from an affected area (; ; ; ; ). Thus
column4: to fully document the impact of deforestation on a forest community
column5: a site must be studied for a substantial period of time after habitat alteration has occurred. Detailing the process of local population decline and extirpation over time provides invaluable information about species’ abilities to cope with habitat fragmentation. It also informs us about how community composition itself may be resistant to change
column6: its degree of resilience following change
column7: and how or if it stabilizes following this disturbance.

column1: Studies of species losses in birds have used a variety of methods
column2: including comparing species richness in different-sized fragments (; ; )
column3: comparison of species composition at a site pre- and post-fragmentation (; ; ; ; )
column4: and experimental fragmentation (; ; ; ; ; )
column5: and have often included scattered survey data prior to fragmentation (; ; ; ; ; ). Many of these studies have relied on qualitative visual and audio survey techniques
column6: with multiple observers
column7: though such techniques can allow cryptic and low-density species to be overlooked (). Additionally
column8: observer skills and intensity of sampling may vary among surveys.

column1: Mist netting offers the most consistent and quantitative method available to sample birds among years (). However
column2: mist nets have documented weaknesses; the most relevant is the limited stratum and size of birds they can effectively sample (; ; ). This is particularly noticeable in structurally diverse habitats such as tropical rainforests
column3: where probability of detection using mist nets is unknown for most species. Mist net studies in the Neotropics are therefore biased toward understory
column4: small- to mid-sized passerines. While mist nets
column5: unlike other methods
column6: are less prone to observer bias and variability
column7: we augmented our analyses of netting data that suggested species losses with presence-absence observational data (daily checklists in later years); this becomes particularly important for low-density species and for those not readily captured.

column1: The Sierra de Los Tuxtlas of southern Veracruz
column2: Mexico provides a textbook case of deforestation. This small range of volcanic mountains is home to the northernmost Neotropical rainforest (; ). The region has lost more than 90% of its forests in the past century
column3: with the majority of that loss occurring in the lowlands over the past fifty years (; ; ). Our study compares eight seasons of mist net sampling from Los Tuxtlas over the course of more than thirty years. This allows us to at least partly answer the question of how species composition and relative abundance changed in and around a conserved core of local rainforest habitat on a decadal scale.

Neither gaps nor monotonic changes were necessary for inclusion
column2: just suggestion of a possible trend. We did this instead of applying statistical tests across all 122 species to minimize Type I and Type II errors either by applying a very large number of tests or a conservative correction (e.g.

column3: suggesting a continuing extirpation of species from the station.  and  found that as surrounding habitat was lost
column4: species richness in remaining fragments increased as individuals displaced from surrounding areas found their way to remaining forest patches. This increased richness was limited by the lifespan of the individual birds (; ). Unlike these studies
column5: in which forest patches were suddenly and completely isolated
column6: the forest of the Estación de Biología Los Tuxtlas was isolated gradually. Because extirpation seems to be continuing
column7: we expect declines and extirpations to continue for some time at the station
column8: even if no further deforestation occurs in the region (; ; ; ).

column1: Mechanisms for tropical bird species losses due to deforestation and fragmentation probably include factors such as greater specialization as compared to temperate birds
column2: reduced dispersal abilities
column3: lower population densities
column4: and patchy distributions (; ; ; ). Our assessment of possible causes for the loss of these species reveals no definite patterns
column5: however
column6: other than the predominant requirement of forested habitat. On Barro Colorado Island in Lake Gatún
column7: Panama
column8: maturation of habitat and loss of open areas was responsible for the decline in the island’s avifauna (; ). This is unlikely to be the case in Los Tuxtlas. Despite major degradation of surrounding forests
column9: the station has remained primary forest with areas of second growth. A loss of sapling and seedling species has been described ()
column10: but the overall structure of the forest appears to have remained fairly stable.  found in a meta-analysis of 30 studies that the effects of fragmentation are not subject to simple generalities
column11: and that they are highly site specific.  pointed to the need to understand extirpations at local scales because responses can differ from predictions made at larger scales.

column1: Los Tuxtlas is at the northernmost extent of the ranges of 13 of the 16 species we found to be declining or extirpated ( and ). Evidence is mixed as to whether populations at the periphery of a species’ range are more vulnerable to extirpation (; ; ). Los Tuxtlas is at the edge of all species’ geographic ranges endemic to Neotropical rainforest
column2: so it is not clear why this subset might be more subject to this phenomenon. The elevational distribution of each of these species encompasses sea level to 750 m or more ()
column3: and we consider this factor unlikely to be responsible for the vulnerability of these particular taxa.

column1: Although insectivores showed a trend toward being disproportionately affected in our study
column2: it was not significant. Elsewhere insectivores have been shown to be particularly vulnerable to severe habitat change (e.g.
column3: ; ; ; ). Additionally
column4: deforestation can negatively impact species found in multi-species foraging flocks ()
column5: which are important to many birds of tropical rainforest communities (; ; ; ).  noted that X. minutus
column6: one of the species showing a significant decline in our study () was a regular member of mixed flocks in the Sierra de Los Tuxtlas.

column1: We considered large-scale range shifts
column2: perhaps from climate change
column3: as a possible cause for species loss
column4: but this seems unlikely. At least some of the species lost in our study appear to have persisted in the southern portion of Los Tuxtlas near Volcán Santa Marta at least into the mid-1990s (K Winker
column5: personal observations). If range shifts were the cause
column6: species would likely have disappeared region-wide and we would not expect only forest-related species to be affected. Habitat loss and degradation seem to be the best explanations for the losses observed
column7: but exactly how these changes affected each species remains unknown.

column1: Another possible influence on mist-net captures
column2: particularly in the most recent
column3: late winter/spring sampling periods
column4: would be seasonal intra-tropical and elevational movements in some of the study species (; ). There is evidence that C. flaveola and E. flavogaster move seasonally within the tropics
column5: seemingly to breed in Los Tuxtlas then departing (M Ramos & J Rappole
column6: personal observations).  found probable elevational movements in M. sulphureipygius. The extirpations of seven of the 16 species are particularly notable. C. flaveola is a widely distributed species known to thrive in manipulated habitats such as gardens and forest edges and is a generalist frugivore and nectarivore (K Winker et al.
column7: personal observations; ). This is not a species we would expect to decline due to forest fragmentation; both its habitat and food preferences are well suited to survival in a mosaic landscape
column8: and it is known to persist in a fragmented landscape elsewhere in northern Middle America (). Intratropical migrations of C. flaveola may partially explain the changing capture rates in this species (M Ramos & J Rappole
column9: personal observations). O. semiflavum and L. amaurocephalus are both edge specialists; thus
column10: limited fragmentation
column11: creating an increase in edges
column12: might a priori seem to benefit these species. Though the habitat protected by the station has remained relatively static
column13: the intensity of lowland deforestation in Los Tuxtlas as a whole () may be too extensive even for these edge specialists. L. souleyetii prefers open forest and partially cleared areas (). The habitat surrounding the station during the 1980s and 1990s was dominated by pasture scattered with isolated trees. In our later field seasons there was a noticeable decline in the number of isolated trees and fences constructed of living trees (K Winker
column14: personal observations). This loss may account for the extirpation of L. souleyetii. G. spirurus apparently disappeared from the station between the 1970s and 1986
column15: the first of the documented extirpations. The majority of deforestation across the region took place during this period. This previously abundant species disappeared from our data in just over a decade. Interestingly
column16: on the slopes of neighboring Volcán Santa Marta the species was present at least into the 1990s and probably still persists there (K Winker
column17: personal observations). Also

column19: indicating at least a decline if not extirpation (). In Brazil
column20: G. spirurus persisted in experimentally isolated fragments well after isolation ()
column21: and the species persists in highly fragmented forest in southern Belize (). H. momotula was collected but not netted in 1974
column22: was captured in substantial numbers during 1986 and 1992–94
column23: but was absent in the last two seasons of sampling. This pattern is mysterious. This species has an elevational range extending to 1500 m and may persist in the forests of the upper slopes of Volcán San Martín. If so

Continued sampling may provide more insight into its abundance patterns. It illustrates the need for improved understanding of species-specific dispersal behavior within and among forest fragments (e.g.

column1: Two other studies provide comparative value to our results. The four species we consider likely extirpated (Taraba major
column2: Formicarius analis
column3: Grallaria guatimalensis
column4: and Schiffornis turdina) were not detected in the much broader census surveys of  in 1990–1992.  conducted the geographically closest long-term study to ours in their analysis of avian declines at Palenque
column5: Chiapas
column6: Mexico. Their results showed only three species that overlapped our results. They found Eucometis penicillata extirpated (to our decline) and two others that declined as our populations did (Xenops minutus and Leptopogon amaurocephalus). Indeed
column7: the species-level heterogeneity between our studies is noteworthy. A key similarity between our studies
column8: however
column9: is the importance of forest in explaining declines and extirpations ().

column1: Our analyses suggest that the Estación de Biología Tropical Los Tuxtlas is too small to maintain its full
column2: historic complement of bird species. If deforestation accelerated region-wide
column3: eliminating other forest refugia
column4: the station alone (640 ha) would be unable to maintain the historical avian diversity of the region or to provide source populations for restored forest habitats for many of its present bird species. Given the scale of deforestation in the region
column5: it is surprising that there are not more species showing declines. Indeed
column6: we may consider it good news that important forest seed dispersers such as Habia tanagers () did not show significant declines. The overall size of the remaining forests
column7: particularly in the highlands
column8: may be ameliorating the effects of lowland deforestation. However
column9: increasing or continued isolation of the station will probably limit recolonization from elsewhere
column10: and species losses will likely continue.

column1: In our study
column2: although several species seemed to quickly succumb to local and regional deforestation
column3: others showed delayed declines and extirpations
column4: a phenomenon also known to happen at larger scales (; ). Moreover
column5: the effects of deforestation were remarkably heterogeneous among forest-related species
column6: with no single clear pattern of why some species experienced declines or extirpation. Our long-term data suggest that predicting which species will be most affected by deforestation in the northern Neotropics
column7: and thus effectively working to ameliorate the effects of forest loss
column8: will be particularly challenging. Nevertheless
column9: as similar long-term datasets accrue
column10: subtle patterns may reveal how species-specific responses reflect underlying commonalities that can be exploited for effective management and conservation.

column1: 25|10.7717/peerj.16675|Uneven demographic consequences of the 2022 disease outbreak for the sea urchin Diadema antillarum in Puerto Rico| Rodríguez-Barreras  Ruber |2023-12-20|"Over recent years
column2: the incidence of infectious diseases affecting marine organisms has increased and resulted in structural and functional impacts on ecosystems (). Even though infectious diseases are common in the marine realm
column3: mass mortalities caused by infectious diseases are rare
column4: yet their effects can be dramatic and long-lasting. Mass mortalities could be particularly damaging in regions characterized by a low redundancy of functional groups
column5: such as the Caribbean reefs (; ). Such events could lead to a functional extinction of a key species (i.e.
column6: the level at which the species no longer fulfills its ecological role) ()
column7: compromising the community assemblage of the entire region and consequently restructuring the services these ecosystems provide to humankind and their ecological roles in the oceans (; ).

column1: The prevalence of disease outbreaks is increasingly impacting the marine environment (). Reports of large-scale episodic events leading to mass mortalities in marine organisms have increased since the latter half of the previous century (). Consequently
column2: documented instances of population crash due to disease outbreak episodes have been reported in many marine taxa. However
column3: population crashes are not uncommon among echinoderms (; ). This phylum is commonly referred to as a “boom-bust” group due to the frequent outbreak episodes and massive die-offs observed worldwide (). Echinoderms play an important ecological role in the Caribbean region
column4: not only as key herbivores
column5: and structuring agents of the benthic community ()
column6: but also due to their history of population crashes (; ).

column1: Several mass-mortality events affecting coral reefs in the Caribbean have been recorded
column2: but the 1980 Diadema antillarum mass mortality event has been the most serious and well-studied of all (). Before the die-off
column3: D. antillarum was among the main herbivores native to Caribbean coral reefs (; ; ). However
column4: a mysterious waterborne pathogen(s) demised over 98% of the D. antillarum population throughout the Caribbean basin (; ). Immediately after
column5: and in subsequent decades
column6: reef-building corals declined
column7: while the coral reefs experienced a significant increase in macroalgae
column8: contributing to a severe decline in coral cover (). Nearly 40 years after the mass-mortality event
column9: D. antillarum has shown variable levels of recovery across the region; however
column10: D. antillarum densities have not reached pre-mortality levels in most localities (; ; ; ).

column1: In early 2022
column2: a new mortality event of D. antillarum was reported on several islands across the Caribbean (Response Network
column3: ). The mortality was first reported in the US Virgin Islands
column4: and subsequently
column5: mortalities were reported in several reefs throughout the Caribbean. Many individuals have been found dead or showing signs of disease
column6: i.e.
column7: sea urchins outside their shelters in midday hours
column8: unable to attach to the substrate
column9: showing slow movement of spines as a response to contact
column10: and loss of spines (). It is known that the species has a diurnal sheltering and nocturnal foraging behavior (). The resurgence of the D. antillarum die-off at a time when populations across the Caribbean have not fully recovered is of great concern for the scientific community
column11: given the poor ecological state of Caribbean coral reefs (). Monitoring demographic changes in keystone species populations is essential for gaining insights into the biological relationships within an ecosystem. Therefore
column12: in this study
column13: we surveyed D. antillarum populations along the eastern
column14: northern
column15: and northwestern coasts of Puerto Rico. At each site
column16: we estimated the population density
column17: size distribution
column18: and disease prevalence. We subsequently compared these parameters to available demographic data collected in 2012 and 2017 for four of these sites. These surveys were driven by three central questions: (1) Is the disease found in all the surveyed reefs? (2) Is the disease prevalence similar in all the surveyed reefs
column19: and therefore are these reefs affected by the disease in a similar way
column20: and (3) Are different sizes of D. antillarum individuals equally affected by the disease?

column9: with variations observed among different locations. However
column10: all the observed diseased sea urchins exhibited external signs of illness similar to those described in the literature by . Furthermore
column11: the concurrent timing of our observations with those reported in other Caribbean jurisdictions strongly suggests that we were indeed dealing with the same disease.

column1: Our data reveals significant variability in the estimated densities of D. antillarum across the surveyed reefs
column2: with notable differences observed among sites. Of particular concern is the considerable decrease in density in 2022 compared to historical data for the Culebra sites. This reduction is significant considering that these sites had the highest densities on the eastern coast of Puerto Rico in the early 2000s (). For instance
column3: these authors reported mean densities of 1.59 ± 0.50 ind m−2 at PTA
column4: whereas no living individuals were detected in 2022. We also documented similar density at PME
column5: where density dropped from 1.04 ± 0.90 ind m−2 in 2017 to no healthy sea urchins detected in 2022. Other sites have experienced similar D. antillarum reductions. For instance
column6: reported densities of 1.10 ind m−2 at ESC
column7: yet our 2022 survey revealed a density drop to 0.1 ind m−2
column8: with nearly half of the individuals being affected by the disease. Given the current disease prevalence observed at ESC
column9: it is anticipated that the outbreak will have a more significant impact if the diseased individuals do not recover
column10: and the disease continues to spread. The other sites exhibited less severe outbreak impact. For instance
column11: PSA and PBL exhibited similar densities
column12: but PSA displayed a disease prevalence of over 10%
column13: while no diseased individuals were detected in PBL. Therefore
column14: the density at PSA would likely have been higher than that of PBL if it had not been affected by the outbreak event.

column1: Densities at PBA and CGO have also experienced decreases even though evidence of disease was not found. For instance
column2: a study conducted in 2017 by () estimated the density at PBA at 1.26 ind m−2
column3: while the estimated current density decreased to 1.05 ind m−2. A similar declining trend was observed in CGO
column4: where densities decreased from 1.55 ind m−2 in 2017 to 1.09 ind m−2 in 2022. respectively. In subsequent visits to these sites in February
column5: May
column6: and August 2023
column7: we found no evidence of the disease
column8: further suggesting that the disease may not be the primary cause of these declines (unpublished data). Instead
column9: habitat degradation may have influenced the observed declines as multiple coral heads
column10: including the dominant species in this area e.g.
column11: Pseudodiploria spp.
column12: were either recently dead or exhibited signs of Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (). Alternatively
column13: the absence of diseased or dead sea urchins among the studied reefs may result in some populations being more resistant to the disease than others. It is also noteworthy to mention that from 2017 to 2022
column14: three hurricanes onslaught Puerto Rico. These hurricanes caused significant damage to the reefs and seagrass beds
column15: which
column16: when combined with natural low recruitment
column17: may have resulted in the observed low density in 2022 when compared to the 2017 densities (; ; ; ).

column1: The current size distribution of D. antillarum in the surveyed reefs was dominated by the large-size class individuals
column2: with fewer medium-sized class individuals and even fewer small-size class individuals. The absence of juveniles may have multiple explanations. For instance
column3: lower frequencies of small-size individuals may suggest a generally low recruitment given the relatively low abundance of mature and
column4: therefore
column5: larger D. antillarum individuals when compared to 80s pre-mortality events. In fact
column6: most authors argued this as the main reason for the slow recovery after the massive mortality (; ; ). It could also suggest a high mortality among the recently settled and juvenile sea urchins due to predation
column7: as several studies have argued that D. antillarum predation by reef-fishes may have a profound effect on the sea urchin size structure (; ). Nonetheless
column8: it is difficult to conclude that the reason for the low observations of juveniles and sub-adults was related to being more susceptible to the disease than mature individuals. Therefore
column9: longitudinal studies
column10: which include a collection of D. antillarum in the larval pool and recruit monitoring across several reefs
column11: are required to better comprehend the demographic dynamics of D. antillarum under the current outbreak scenario.

column1: Overall
column2: the prevalence of the new outbreak was still relatively low in the surveyed reefs
column3: as only 4.3% of the 665 counted sea urchins were diseased (). It also shows an erratic geographic distribution
column4: with some reefs showing high prevalence and others with moderately to low prevalence and hence presumably low impact. A recent study conducted in the Dominican Republic also reported variable impact across different reefs
column5: although the outbreak in the Dominican Republic seems to have had a higher impact than in Puerto Rico (). Nonetheless
column6: the outbreak in Puerto Rico just started
column7: as judged by the lack of conclusive evidence of disease in some of the surveyed reefs. Therefore
column8: it is still premature to capture the real magnitude of the impact
column9: and if the ongoing outbreak is as destructive as the 80s mortality event when populations were decimated throughout the western Atlantic
column10: including the Bermudas
column11: in a relatively short period of time (; ). Instead
column12: the spatial-heterogeneous nature of this new outbreak and the variable mortality of individuals resemble the mortality event occurring in the sibling urchin species D. africanum
column13: from October 2009 to April 2010 in the subtropical eastern Atlantic (). Nonetheless
column14: long-term monitoring programs at these reefs may help disclose size class patterns of disease susceptibility.

column1: Populations of D. antillarum at surveyed reefs have not fully recovered since the mass mortality event in the 1980s (; ) and are now facing a second outbreak that is causing further damage. This study sheds light on the current state of D. antillarum populations in Puerto Rico’s reefs amid the Scutico ciliatosis outbreak
column2: revealing different degrees of impact across different locations
column3: with some reefs experiencing a drastic decline in sea urchin density
column4: particularly concerning for sites like Culebra
column5: which once boasted relative high densities. Nonetheless
column6: the limited available data makes it difficult to determine which factors (abiotic and biotic) may favor the infection either by compromising the individual’s immune system or by favoring the proliferation of the biotic agent(s) or both. This 2022 outbreak’s complexity mirrors past events
column7: emphasizing the importance of establishing long-term monitoring programs where key abiotic and biotic components known to cause stress to other coral reefs-associated organisms are regularly surveyed. This is especially critical in the face of climate change and changing marine conditions
column8: which may weaken the immunity of marine organisms and increase the frequency and severity of disease outbreaks.
