column1: 26|10.7717/peerj.16510|Genomic DNA extraction optimization and validation for genome sequencing using the marine gastropod Kellet’s whelk| Daniels  Benjamin N. |2023-12-6|"Rapidly advancing next generation sequencing technologies such as whole genome sequencing
column2: genotyping-in-thousands by sequencing (GT-seq)
column3: and restriction-site-associated DNA sequencing (RAD-seq)
column4: are becoming more available and affordable for non-model organisms (; ; ; ). However
column5: applying these technologies to marine invertebrate species
column6: which are nearly all non-model organisms
column7: is hindered by challenges in extracting and purifying their genomic DNA (). DNA extraction of marine invertebrate species can be riddled with contaminants that result in poor sequencing results (; ; ; ). The contaminants are commonly attributed to mucopolysaccharides that co-precipitate with genomic DNA
column8: increasing the viscosity of the sample during extractions and preventing the activity of enzymes as well as proper homogenization of mixtures (). As a result
column9: researchers studying marine invertebrates species have had to dedicate extensive time and resources to troubleshooting and optimizing their genomic DNA extraction methods (; ; ; ).

column1: This study aims to develop and test DNA extraction protocols and generate an optimal protocol for the marine gastropod
column2: Kellet’s whelk (Kelletia kelletii)
column3: for supporting genomic research on the species and marine invertebrates in general. Kellet’s whelk is a subtidal gastropod distributed along the North American west coast ()
column4: a significant predator in kelp forest ecosystems ()
column5: and a species of interest to commercial fisheries (). Kellet’s whelk currently supports the second-largest molluscan commercial fishery in California in both landings and ex-vessel value (). Furthermore
column6: Kellet’s whelk recently exhibited a ∼ 300 km northward range expansion ()
column7: potentially driven by intensifying El Niño Southern Oscillation (ENSO) oceanographic conditions due to climate change (; ). Current research on the species seeks to use genomic-based assignment testing (sensu ) to investigate this hypothesis and
column8: more broadly
column9: elucidate spatio-temporal patterns of gene flow and population dynamics of Kellet’s whelk. Such information holds significant promise in addressing pivotal marine ecological and evolutionary inquiries and providing valuable support for the sustainable management of the Kellet’s whelk fishery
column10: as well as that of other ecologically and economically important coastal species with analogous life history traits (e.g.
column11: large populations
column12: slow growth
column13: sedentary demersal adults
column14: and pelagic larvae) such as spiny lobster (Panulirus interruptus)
column15: kelp bass (Paralabrax clathratus)
column16: sheephead (Semicossyphus pulcher)
column17: and some rockfish (Sebastes atrovirens) (; ).

column1: An experimental design for revealing genomic features of Kellet’s whelk could be supported by applying next generation sequencing technologies to a large sample size of individuals in parent and recruit (1–2 year old Kellet’s whelk individuals) populations across the species’ biogeographic range and over multiple years (). For instance
column2: the application of GT-seq and RAD-seq techniques targeting neutral loci
column3: along with GT-seq focusing on geographically-associated loci harboring putatively adaptive alleles
column4: can offer insights into the underlying adaptive genetic mechanisms and historical population dynamics within the species (). Moreover
column5: these approaches can delineate spatially distinct population units characterized by unique genotypic patterns
column6: facilitating the assignment testing of recruits to adult populations
column7: thereby revealing population connectivity in the species (; ). To support such genomics research
column8: a reliable method for generating high-molecular weight (HMW) genomic DNA is required from each sample. Furthermore
column9: analysis of a large sample size (e.g.
column11: 000s of individuals
column12: necessary for generating a complete population connectivity matrix; ) requires an optimized high-throughput HMW DNA extraction methodology. To help meet this need for Kellet’s whelk and potentially other non-model marine invertebrate species
column13: we tested and compared the efficacy of DNA extraction protocols on Kellet’s whelk tissue. We evaluated four DNA extraction methods from marine invertebrate studies: (1) Qiagen Blood and Tissue kit (QIAGEN
column14: MD
column15: USA) (; )
column16: (2) Zymo Quick-DNA HMW Magbead kit (Zymo
column17: CA
column18: USA) ()
column19: (3) Phenol Chloroform protocol (PCI)(Thermo Fisher Scientific
column20: MA
column21: USA) ()
column22: and
column23: (4) an optimized version of a Salting out protocol (; ). For each
column24: we conducted DNA extraction on 4–5 tissue samples
column25: then compared the quality of DNA extractions produced
column26: as well as the difficulty
column27: toxicity and cost of the extraction method. We then used our test results and lessons learned from other marine genomic research projects to develop a modified protocol intended to optimally balance efficiency
column28: cost
column30: and quality of extracting Kellet’s whelk DNA. We validated our protocol by evaluating DNA yield
column31: DNA quality
column32: and DNA purity generated by DNA extractions on an independent set of 16 tissue samples. We then applied our protocol to over three thousand tissue samples. We conducted the DNA extractions in batches using a group of trained student researchers
column33: and assessed the quality of the DNA extractions produced on a random subset of the samples. We also tested a subset of the extractions for both RAD-seq and GT-seq methods. Finally
column34: we tested our protocol by applying DNA extractions generated from it to whole genome sequencing using both long read sequencing (ONT MinION) and short read sequencing (Illumina Novaseq).

column1: Portions of this text were previously published as part of a preprint (https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2023.07.31.551321v2). We tested four DNA extraction methods used in previous marine invertebrate studies. Each method was applied to tissue samples dissected from adult Kellet’s whelk (between 60–120 mm in length) collected in the wild and maintained prior to dissection in flow-through filtered seawater aquaria at the California Polytechnic State University research pier
column2: located in Avila Beach
column3: California
column4: USA. The adult whelks were collected from sub-tidal reefs located near Monterey (36.6181670N
column8: Diablo Canyon (35.2244500N
column10: and Point Loma (32.665333N
column11: 117.261517W) California in 2019
column12: transported live to the aquaria
column13: and maintained under ambient conditions with food in the form of frozen seafood provided ad libitum (CDFW Scientific Collection Permit 8018 to C.W.). Tissue samples were dissected in March 2021. Approximately 200 mg tissue was dissected non-invasively from the foot of the individual
column14: immediately frozen in liquid nitrogen
column15: then transported to the Center for Applied Biotechnology at California Polytechnic State University and stored at −80 °C. A portion of each tissue sample was used for DNA extractions as specified in each method.

column1: Five Kellet’s whelk individuals were dissected for the Qiagen and Zymo kit extractions with each sample having a duplicate (10 total extractions conducted using the Qiagen kit and 10 total extractions conducted using the Zymo kit) in March 2021. Four Kellet’s whelk individuals were dissected for the Salting out and PCI protocols with duplicates for the Salting out protocol but not for the PCI protocol (eight total extractions conducted using the Salting out protocol and four total extractions conducted using the PCI protocol) in May 2021.
Each method was evaluated based on protocol difficulty
column2: cost per extraction
column3: DNA yield
column4: DNA quality
column5: and toxicity. Difficulty was measured on a three-level qualitative scale. Easy: requires very little wet bench expertise - simple pipetting volumes and reagents as well as basic techniques. Medium: requires some wet bench experience—simple pipetting volumes and reagents with more advanced techniques (e.g.
column6: bead binding
column7: DNA washing and drying). Hard: requires more extensive wet bench experience—precise pipetting and handling of toxic reagents (e.g.
column8: pipetting layered solutions
column9: working in fume hoods and more extensive use of personal protective equipment (PPE)). Cost per extraction was calculated by the sum of costs per method (e.g.
column10: price of Qiagen kit
column11: cost of all reagents for PCI method) divided by the number of possible DNA extractions. DNA yield was measured by a Qubit fluorometer using the DNA Broad Range assay (Invitrogen
column12: USA). DNA quality was evaluated based on gel electrophoresis using ∼250 ng DNA per sample (or 10 µL sample if concentration was under 25 µg/µL) on a 1% agarose gel at 80V for 45 min with a 1kb DNA ladder. Gel electrophoresis has been shown to be a robust method for quantifying DNA quality (; ; ). Quality was measured as good: high molecular weight (HMW) band; medium: HMW band with degradation; or
column13: bad: digested. Toxicity of each method was measured by the reagent classification in accordance with Occupational Safety and Health Administration(OSHA)
column14: International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC)
column15: or National Toxicology Program (NTP). Low toxicity: no ingredients are registered as carcinogenic
column16: aspirationally or reproductively toxic. Medium toxicity: no ingredients are registered as carcinogenic
column17: aspirationally or reproductively toxic
column18: but some ingredients are registered as causing skin irritation
column19: serious irritation
column20: or damage to eyes. High toxicity: ingredients are registered as carcinogenic
column21: aspirationally or reproductively toxic.

Investigating the spatial and temporal scales of genetic variation among populations of non-model organisms
column2: such as marine invertebrates
column3: can reveal emergent
column4: novel patterns and drivers of population connectivity in a marine system
column5: with significant implications for science and management (; ). Recent advances in genome sequencing technologies hold tremendous potential for genomic research across the tree of life
column6: particularly in under-represented organisms. While these new technologies hold the capacity for sequencing numerous non-model species. It is essential to recognize that sequencing many organisms
column7: especially marine invertebrates
column8: remains inherently challenging (). Marine organisms retain chemical composites within their tissue that interfere with both common DNA extraction methods as well as many downstream sequencing techniques (). Moreover
column9: they often exhibit notably high heterozygosity and large numbers of repetitive regions within their genomes (e.g.
column10: transposable elements)
column11: underscoring the need for DNA extraction methods capable of producing high molecular weight DNA for long-read sequencing to overcome these genome assembly limitations (). Our objective was to test four common DNA extraction methods using the non-model organism Kellet’s whelk
column12: generate an optimal protocol for producing HMW DNA for next generation sequencing
column13: apply this protocol to a large collection of samples
column14: and test its suitability for whole genome sequencing as well as RAD-seq and GT-seq platforms across large sample sizes. Among the four tested DNA extraction methods
column15: the Salting out protocol stood out by producing the highest quality genomic DNA
column16: while also being cost-effective and high yielding (). The Salting out protocol was improved by the use of simple low agitation mechanical tissue homogenization
column17: a set duration for chemical tissue lysis with proteinase K
column18: and an overall lack of DNA disruption by minimizing the use of pipette mixing
column19: vortexing
column20: and filter columns (). The necessity of these improvements became evident during initial testing
column21: as they played a vital role in producing HMW DNA
column22: given that different methods for homogenization and tissue lysis yielded varying outcomes (). It is unclear why the Salting Out method was most successful in extracting consistent HMW DNA; it is possible that secondary metabolites specific to Kellet’s whelk may interfere with the efficacy of these extraction methods/kits differently. Importantly
column23: we validated this method using samples collected 3–5 years prior to extractions and stored at −80 °C
column24: which resulted in genomic DNA with high purity
column25: as evident by A260/280 and A260/230 ratios suitable for population genetic platforms (). Notably
column26: any signs of degradation observed in these samples collected years ago from disparate locations () were not evident in samples produced in the lab from fresh tissue (). This finding strongly suggests that compromised DNA quality stems not from the extraction method but rather from DNA degradation during tissue collection or storage. Conducting extractions on all 3
column27: 325 tissue samples using the Salting out protocol produced DNA with sufficient quality for RAD-seq and GT-seq
column28: despite some variability among samples (). We attribute this variability to potential factors such as human error
column30: and suboptimal storage conditions (frozen on dry ice and subjected to multiple transfers before being stored at −80 °C). Nevertheless
column31: the Salting out method yielded a majority (81.3%) of DNA extractions of optimal quality for RAD-seq and GT-seq platforms. We interpreted the quality of the DNA as measures of the potential success in RAD-seq and GT-seq platforms (81.3%) by requiring HMW DNA be present (as shown in gel electrophoresis ) and this was reflected in the overall results of the subset testing on GT-seq (75% successful) and RAD-seq (78.9% successful) (). We anticipate that further improvements can be achieved by collecting samples under more consistent conditions and employing robotics rather than manual operators (trained students
column33: which may elevate the overall DNA quality across samples. While the Salting out protocol produced HMW DNA extraction
column34: it likely did not remove all contaminants
column35: as indicated by the failed ONT rapid sequencing library and initial suboptimal GT-seq libraries (
column36: ). These contaminants may have potentially interfered with the polymerase activity of the MinION Nanopore technology and enzyme activity during library preparation for the Illumina technologies (). With the introduction of additional cleanup steps
column37: the ONT ligation sequencing libraries and Illumina Novaseq library successfully achieved high-quality genome sequencing. Moreover
column39: 80% of the failed GT-seq samples from the initial run produced successful genotyping (>90% call rate) (). The bead cleaning steps in combination with the PCR step employed in the library preparation of the RAD-seq libraries appeared to exhibit resistance to inhibition by co-precipitants during sequencing. This observation implies a cleanup step is necessary before conducting PCR library amplification in order to facilitate successful sequencing.

column1: The issue of inhibition or blockage of Nanopore pores was also observed in the black tiger prawn
column2: Penaeus monodon
column3: and believed to be caused by repetitive regions in the genome (). Successful Nanopore sequencing occurred after PCR-based library preparation
column4: suggesting that there may be unknown contaminants associated with mollusc DNA that are removed or destroyed during PCR (). Similarly
column5: we encountered comparable challenges in producing successful genome sequencing using the Nanopore MinION without added cleanup and purification. Our cleanup
column6: which did not include PCR steps and maintained the integrity of our HMW DNA
column7: allowing secondary structures to form prior to sequencing
column8: is believed to remove the unidentified contaminant responsible for the failed Nanopore MinION sequencing runs (–). Our comparison of DNA extraction methods could have benefited from a method for isolating or quantifying co-precipitants. These co-precipitants
column9: most likely a mucopolysaccharide ()
column10: were not significantly implicated by the A260/280 ratio (A260/280 = 1.85 ± 0.046) or the A260/230 ratio (A260/230 = 1.81 ± 0.27) and were only clearly evident after sequencing
column11: not being implicated by PCR failure (). To prevent future failed sequencing runs
column12: we recommend implementing a method for detecting these co-precipitants (2D gel-electrophoresis) or ensuring their removal (using the added clean-up
column13: HMW selection kits
column14: or PCR). Other methods known for removing polysaccharides
column15: such as the CTAB method (; )
column16: should also be explored
column17: as well as other modified methods being published regularly
column18: given the increasing number of non-model marine invertebrates under genetic analysis (; ; ).

column1: By applying whole genome sequencing using GT-seq and RAD-seq to the extensive collection of Kellet’s whelk adult and recruit samples from populations spanning the US and Mexico coasts
column2: southern and central California
column3: and the species’ historical and expanded range
column4: over multiple years covering ENSO and non-ENSO oceanographic conditions
column5: researchers can hope to increase understanding of population connectivity in this and other coastal marine species with extensive larval dispersal (). However
column6: accomplishing such large-scale sequencing data on a non-model marine invertebrate species of this scale is challenging. This study serves as an important contribution towards enhancing our understanding of the performance and potential use of DNA extraction methods for future large-scale genomics.

column1: 27|10.7717/peerj.6042|Drivers and assemblies of soil eukaryotic microbes among different soil habitat types in a semi-arid mountain in China| Zhao  He |2018-12-5|"Eukaryotic microorganisms comprise most of the natural microbes and they are closely linked with the sustainability of the soil-based ecosystem and biogeochemical processes (; ; ). The soil eukaryotic microbial group plays a key role in litter decomposition
column2: nutrient cycling
column3: and soil structural formation (). However
column4: the majority of microorganisms are still unknown and cannot be isolated from complex environmental matrices or are not cultivable on microbiological media (; ; )
column5: so they require detailed studies. Particularly
column6: knowledge of the role and dynamics of soil eukaryotic communities in semi-arid areas is rather fragmentary.

column1: Species richness and diffusion capacity are features of eukaryotic microbial communities
column2: which lead them to inhabit a variety of habitat types (; ; ). Scholars have argued that the abundance of nematode
column3: amoeba
column4: and fungal communities generally increase in aquifer environmental habitats (; )
column5: and several studies have demonstrated that organic fertilization strongly affects eukaryotic community composition in agricultural habitats (; ; ).  showed that substrate characteristics affect the abundance/biomass of eukaryotic microbial communities but do not influence diversity in sandstone habitats. Most previous studies only focused on changes in communities of a single habitat type (; ). However
column6: the diversity and composition of eukaryotic microbial communities may differ among various soil habitat types
column7: and comparative analyses of eukaryotic microbial communities are still rare in different semi-arid soil habitats.

column1: Elucidating the composition and diversity of eukaryotic microbial communities is a crucial step for conserving soil productivity and sustainability (; ; ). Thus
column2: it is necessary to understand the processes responsible for the distribution of eukaryotic microorganisms. Most attempts to identify the structure and coexistence mechanism of natural communities are the joint result of niche theory
column3: which focuses on deterministic processes based on habitat patterns but is restricted by various factors (; ; ). Niche theory emphasizes that species composition is correlated closely with changes in environmental factors (). Some studies have supported niche differentiation and confirmed that abiotic soil factors structure microbial communities (; ). Thus
column4: environmental heterogeneity and spatially structured differences are key factors to explore the distribution of eukaryotic microorganisms. Although ecologists have conducted a series of explorations (; )
column5: the major environmental drivers of soil eukaryotic microorganism distribution in semi-arid regions remain unclear.

column1: Obtaining detailed and accurate data is key to studies of soil eukaryotic microorganisms. However
column2: traditional soil biodiversity assessments are fundamentally flawed
column3: and the time consumed and taxonomic expertise severely limit the sorting and identification of eukaryotic microbiota (; ). Thus
column4: new measurement and evaluation methods are constantly being updated. High-throughput sequencing (pyrophosphate sequencing with the Illumina Genome AnalyzerIIx) of soil samples affords an interesting opportunity to confirm microbial genetic variation and classification (; ; ). This technology provides more comprehensive and accurate sequencing data () that are beneficial to accurately identify microbes in a variety of vegetation types (; ). Applying new technologies provides very useful insight
column5: but more explicit microbiological information in a variety of research areas is still needed.

column1: The area south of the Taihang Mountains in China has a typical semi-arid climate
column2: and soil degradation is common (). Vegetation restoration is a traditional practice widely used to prevent ecosystem degradation in this region of China
column3: and many areas have been planted with artificial shrubs and trees (). Hitherto
column4: most studies of local vegetation restoration have focused on soil physicochemical properties
column5: enzyme activities
column6: root characteristics
column7: and plant growing conditions (; ; )
column8: but few studies have considered the characteristics of local microorganisms. We hypothesized that the presence of soil eukaryotic microbes is closely related to soil habitat types. To test our hypothesis
column9: we used 18S rRNA gene (V4) region to analyze the sequenced data from the Illumina MiSeq platform. The test aimed to (1) identify the composition and richness of the current soil eukaryotic microorganisms in the area south of the Taihang Mountains; (2) illustrate any differences in soil eukaryotic microbes among soil habitat types
column10: including forest
column11: shrub
column12: grass
column13: and farmland soil habitats; (3) and assess the drivers of environmental factors on soil eukaryotic microorganism assembly in this semi-arid region.

column1: The area south of the Taihang Mountains (112°28′–112°30′E
column2: 35°01′–35°03′N) is a semi-arid region in China. The study area has a continental monsoon climate in most parts
column3: with an average annual sunshine rate of 54%
column4: average air temperature of 14.3 °C
column5: and annual precipitation of 440–860 mm. The most common soil is Ustalf (United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) classification). Local vegetation restoration began in 1958. The main land-use methods are grass soil
column6: forest soil
column7: shrub soil
column8: and farmland. Most of the forest soil habitat types are artificial forests
column9: including Platycladus orientalis (L.) Franco

column12: Vitex negundo L.

column14: Setaria viridis (L.) Beauv.
column15: and Arthraxon hispidus (Thunb.) Makino are the dominant herbaceous plants in the natural grass soil. All farmland species were planted by local residents and include Zea mays L.

Previous studies have reported the soil eukaryotic taxa by molecular techniques at other sites (; ). Our present study fully examined the soil eukaryotic microorganism data across different soil habitat types in the area south of the Taihang Mountains. The results show that the Shannon and chao1 indices were significantly different among the different soil habitats (P < 0.05); the highest value were in the forest soil habitat type
column2: followed by shrub and farmland. This result may be because forest soil has more substrate diversity leading to species-rich microbes
column3: and human disturbance might reduce the diffusion and growth processes of microbes (; ). Indeed
column4: it is generally accepted that a single cropping system and excessive fertilization are applied to Chinese farmland ecosystems ()
column5: so the microbial community structure tends to be less diverse in farmland. The forest and shrub soil habitat types provide sheltered habitat for small animals
column6: and their activities and excreta increase soil nutrient contents to enrich the soil microbe species (; ).

column1: Sequencing results have elucidated that the dominant composition of eukaryotic groups among the different soil habitat types was roughly similar. Within the eukaryotic communities
column2: the SAR (Stramenopiles
column3: Alveolates
column4: Rhizaria) supergroup (comprising about 39.76% of the sequences) was the dominant group at the phylum level
column5: followed by Metazoa (37.78%). Fungi was another highly represented group (12.82%). A similar distribution was reported in the Loess Plateau area of northwest China ().  analyzed the eukaryotic microbes using a metatranscriptomic approach in a coastal sand dune. Those authors assumed that sequence number was closely related to cytoplasmic volume (biologically active)
column6: and most protists are single-celled organisms
column7: compared with fungi
column8: with a high copy number per haploid genome (). Therefore
column9: using ribosomal sequences leads to a far higher ratio of protist to fungal sequences.

column4: our results showed that a large number of eukaryotic communities (25.6% of all OTUs) were shared by the different soil habitat types. These results may have occurred because soils have more complex chemical and physical features than water and the adaptive strategies of eukaryotes in soil differ from those in the aquatic environment (; ). Our results also show that few OTUs existed in one soil habitat type
column5: possibly because certain microbes exist in specific habitats and need a specific climate
column6: nutrients
column7: soil texture
column8: or other factors (; ). Interestingly
column9: the number of protozoan (Alveolates 29.1%; Rhizaria 33.6%) OTUs was larger than the fungal OTUs (18.3%)
column10: suggesting that the protozoa were better adapted to the semi-arid mountain environment than fungi. The network results also indicate that the eukaryotic microbes with different sequences may have different environmental adaptation strategies. However
column11: we studied the distribution of eukaryotic communities only from the habitat angle. Other possible factors

column1: The db-RDA indicated that the variation in species composition of the soil eukaryotic microorganisms was mainly explained by soil properties and geographical factors. Our study assessed the relationship between variations in the soil eukaryotic community and multiple environmental factors in the area south of the Taihang Mountains. The results showed that the composition of soil eukaryotic communities was closely related to water content
column2: SOM
column3: the O-elements
column4: and elevation and the O-elements was the most significantly related (P < 0.05). The large number of litters in the O horizon soil indicated greater nutrient cycling and the accumulation of a considerable pool of nutrient elements in soil for use by the microbes (; ). The results also indicated that elevation was the second most influential factor (P < 0.01). The effect of elevation on biological distribution was integral to terrestrial ecosystems (; ). The climate change caused by changes in elevation alters the living space of microbial communities
column5: resulting in the close association between microbes and elevation (; ).

column1: Furthermore
column2: the water content and SOM also significantly influenced the eukaryotic groups (P < 0.05) in this study. Water is an indispensable substance for life and can directly affect reproductive or metabolic processes
column3: and indirectly influence the ecological niches of local microorganism or their physiological status (; ). In addition
column5: and ) confirmed that soil SOM is an essential energy source for microbial activities including reproduction. In general
column6: our research considered substrate variables as well as soil properties and environmental factors based on niche theory
column7: but further studies on the relationship between geographic isolation or other factors and eukaryotic groups will be conducted in the future.

column1: Elucidating the drivers and assemblages of soil eukaryotic microbes is a critical step to reflecting the trends in recent ecological restoration. Species diversity and composition of soil eukaryotic microbes in the area south of the Taihang Mountains was delineated for the first time in this study. Our results indicate that the soil eukaryotic microbes were closely related to different soil habitat types. The highest eukaryotic richness and diversity were found in the forest soil habitat type; the SAR supergroup and Metazoa were the dominant soil eukaryotic groups
column2: about 26% of total OTUs were shared in each soil habitat type. Our study also show the relationship between soil eukaryotic microbes and different environmental factors. The results suggest that the O-elements
column3: water content
column4: SOM
column5: and elevation were significant driving factors in the soil eukaryotic microorganism communities. There findings confirm that forest soil habitat is an efficient means to restore local vegetation and shed new light on the distribution of local soil eukaryotic microbes in semi-arid areas.

28|10.7717/peerj.15222|Mapping the global distribution of invasive pest Drosophila suzukii and parasitoid Leptopilina japonica : implications for biological control| Nair  Rahul R. |2023-4-24|Over recent decades
column2: the world has witnessed significant increases in agricultural production
column3: but increases in crop yields have often been reduced by diverse insect pests (; ). Assessment of all of the components of agricultural productivity and food security must include consideration of insect pests
column4: as they are an integral part of anthropogenic crop ecosystems (; ). Global warming and economic globalization accelerate development of new routes of pest invasion ()
column5: presenting new challenges. As pests pose serious threats in the functioning of global food systems ()
column6: various strategies have been developed for insect pest management
column7: each with its own advantages and disadvantages (). Improvement in the management of invasive pest populations includes consideration of sustainable and eco-friendly approaches
column8: with the goal of achieving long-term benefits ().

column1: A broad (fruit) host range (; )
column2: combined with an ability to infest ripening soft fruits ()
column3: has made Drosophila suzukii (Matsumura) (Diptera: Drosophilidae) an economically damaging
column4: globally invasive fruit pest of serious concern (). Preference for not-quite-ripe or just-ripe fruits over damaged or decaying fruits ()
column5: and the presence of a sclerotized ovipositor of females () with serrations to pierce undamaged fruit epicarps for laying eggs
column6: are two notable traits () that contribute significantly to economic threats imposed by D. suzukii. Bacterial and fungal pathogens can cause secondary infections in fruits after infestation by D. suzukii
column7: augmenting economic losses (; ; ).

column1: Drosophila suzukii is native to eastern and southeastern Asia (); it was initially detected in Japan in 1916 () and described as a distinct species in 1931 (). In 2008
column2: D. suzukii was identified as an invasive species for the first time with populations in both North America () and Europe (). Its host range covers 13 angiosperm families ()
column3: and its invaded geographic range has now extended to South America (; ) and Africa (). As D. suzukii larvae feed inside of fruits ()
column4: and the fruit export trade strictly follows zero-tolerance towards infestations ()
column5: much high-value fruit is rendered unsellable every year. Economic impact assessments in the United States (; ; ; ; ; )
column6: Europe ()
column7: and South America ()
column8: have indicated losses on the order of US$550M per year.

column1: Various preventive and post-infestation control measures (; ; ; ; ; ) have been developed so far
column2: but none with complete efficacy (; ). Management strategies for D. suzukii can be classified broadly into four categories: (1) chemical control (; ; ; )
column3: (2) microclimate manipulation (; )
column4: (3) RNA interference biopesticides ()
column5: and (4) biological control (; ; ; ; ). Extensive use of chemical methods to control D. suzukii infestations can lead to increased pest resistance
column6: and concerns regarding food and environmental safety (). Microclimate manipulation approaches to control D. suzukii are more likely to perform well in hot and dry regions ()
column7: as D. suzukii is sensitive to high temperatures and low humidity (). RNA interference methods involve higher development costs and involve much labor (). Finally
column8: biological control involves release of enemies of D. suzukii from the region of its origin (Asia) in invaded areas
column9: as a means to reduce its population growth (). This method is recommended (; ) in view of improved food safety
column10: environment-friendly characteristics
column11: economic feasibility
column12: and long-term control solutions that are established ().

column1: Parasitoid wasps of the genera Asobara (Braconidae)
column2: Ganaspis (Figitidae)
column3: and Leptopilina (Figitidae) have been studied extensively as biological control agents with potential to suppress growth of D. suzukii populations (; ; ; ; ; ). In particular
column4: the species A. japonica
column5: G. brasiliensis
column6: and L. japonica are potential biocontrol agents (). However
column7: some researchers do not recommend A. japonica for biological control programs aimed at D. suzukii (; ; )
column8: owing to its broad host range (; ). Indeed
column9: given its host specificity
column10: G. brasiliensis has been proposed as a candidate for biological control of D. suzukii (); yet
column11: in a scenario when these three wasps coexist
column12: L. japonica is unique in being able to outcompete the other two species thanks to its relatively faster egg-hatching potential (). Relatively high host specificity ()
column13: demonstrated competence in multi-parasite systems ()
column14: and recent range expansions into areas invaded by D. suzukii in Europe () and North America (; )
column15: make L. japonica an intriguing candidate biocontrol agent for D. suzukii that can be tested for overall effectiveness.

column1: Ecological niche modeling (ENM) has been used extensively to identify potential distributions of species for a variety of purposes (; ; ; ; ; ; ; ). In pest-parasitoid systems
column2: identifying and comparing relative habitat suitability of pest and parasitoid can help to guide effective biological control programs (; ; ). The utility of ENM in applications to biological control of pests can be attributed to two factors: alien parasitoid species must survive and reproduce in the geographic regions where they are released (; )
column3: and unfavorable abiotic factors can reduce the long-term efficacy of biological control measures (). Modeling climatic preferences of deliberately introduced parasitoid species can also provide insights into possible range expansions
column4: an important aspect to be tested in improving effectiveness of classical biological control programs ().

column1: In this study
column2: we used ENM approaches to explore
column3: discuss
column4: and highlight five aspects of a biological control strategy for D. suzukii that can directly benefit producers
column5: extension agents
column6: and policy makers. (1) We estimated the potential distribution of the invasive pest D. suzukii
column7: and (2) that of the parasitoid L. japonica. (3) We assessed the degree of overlap in the potential distributions of D. suzukii and L. japonica
column8: and (4) estimated the biocontrol potential of this system for each country. Finally
column9: (5) we assessed parasitoid model performance to see if incorporating distributional information for the pest improves model performance for the parasitoid.

column1: In conclusion

column1: 29|10.7717/peerj.16368|An ecological transcriptome approach to capture the molecular and physiological mechanisms of mass flowering in Shorea curtisii| Suhaimi  Ahmad Husaini |2023-11-29|"General flowering (GF) is a unique intermittent flowering phenomenon that occurs at irregular intervals of 1–10 years in Southeast Asian mixed-lowland dipterocarp forests (; ). During GF
column2: dozens of plant families
column3: including Dipterocarpaceae
column4: exhibit synchronous flowering (; ) which lasts for about four weeks. Typically
column5: GF is followed by mast fruiting (; )
column6: resulting in a massive pulse of resources in the form of seeds and fruits that are consumed by nectarivorous and frugivorous fauna (; ). The interval and magnitude of GF are proposed to be influenced by climate extremes such as El Nino-Southern Oscillation (ENSO)
column7: which leads to fluctuations in temperatures
column8: drops in cumulative rainfalls and drought events (). The unpredictable nature of GF presents challenges for seed collection for the conservation of GF dipterocarp species ()
column9: which are among the most abundant trees in the tropical rainforests (). Hence
column10: determining the cues that govern flowering timing of GF dipterocarps is critical for conservation management of these species
column11: in a region that is constantly threatened by deforestation.

column1: The absence of clear seasonality in the aseasonal tropics poses difficulties when it comes to identifying the proximate cues and underlying physiological and molecular mechanisms of GF dipterocarps. While photoperiod or day length is a key factor in the reproductive initiation of temperate plants ()
column2: the minimal day length variation in the tropics () suggests that this may not be the case for tropical plants. Studies in Citrus spp. (; ; ) have shed some light on the trigger of flowering for plants in this region. Unlike their subtropical counterparts
column3: which rely on low temperatures as floral cue
column4: tropical citrus has been found to initiate flowering after a brief period of drought (; ). These observations align with earlier reports that drought can mediate or accelerate flowering in various plants including Arabidopsis thaliana () and wheat (). The aforementioned studies in Citrus spp. have also unveiled potential molecular mechanisms linking drought signals to flowering (; ). These studies found elevated expression levels of key proteins in floral regulation
column5: including GIGANTEA (GI)
column6: following an increase in endogenous abscisic acid (ABA) levels in these trees (; ). Subsequently
column7: there was an upregulation of the major floral promoter
column8: FLOWERING LOCUS T (FT)
column9: expression in leaves and buds
column10: indicating floral initiation.  also reported a decrease in gibberellin (GA) levels in lemon buds prior to flowering
column11: aligning with earlier studies that reported GA as a floral repressor in woody plants (reviewed in ). While these trees are not GF species
column12: the knowledge on the physiological and molecular aspects in floral regulation of these trees could provide an insight into flowering time control in dipterocarps. The question of whether a similar mechanism is employed in GF dipterocarps remains to be investigated.

column1: Progress to understand the floral regulation of dipterocarps has been hindered by limited sample availability for frequent sample collection and challenges associated with accessing the dense and tall canopies of tropical forests. Despite sampling challenges
column2: several studies have utilized gene expression and ecological data to infer the environmental factors regulating floral initiation in GF dipterocarps. For example
column3: transcriptome sequencing of bud samples from Shorea beccariana
column4: coupled with rainfall data
column5: supported drought as a significant floral trigger (). In a separate study
column6: the expression of two key flowering genes
column7: FLOWERING LOCUS T (FT) and LEAFY (LFY)
column8: in two Shorea species
column9: Shorea curtisii and Shorea leprosula
column10: measured using real-time PCR
column11: showed a strong correlation with floral initiation (). The same study also showed that the synergistic effect of drought and low temperature best predicted the occurrence of flowering in the study period (). This report was followed by our recent research using RNA-seq data from S. curtisii leaf samples collected at three time points
column12: including before and after floral initiation (). In the study
column13: homologs of genes associated with drought response were found to have a significantly higher representation among genes that were differentially expressed during flowering time ()
column14: thus supported the involvement of drought in floral initiation of GF dipterocarps. The studies thus far
column15: nevertheless important
column16: were conducted on a small number of key flowering genes () or a single tissue (; ). Since physiological changes during flowering span across multiple organs () with both leaves and buds playing direct roles in the production and transportation of proteins that regulate floral initiation
column17: a concurrent study of both the organs is needed to capture the missing link and gain a comprehensive view of the gene expression dynamics that occur between the two organs. Moreover
column18: leveraging information available on model species
column19: A. thaliana and tropical citrus
column20: coupled with the ecological transcriptome approach that combines transcriptome and meteorological data ()
column21: offer a means to elucidate the cues and regulatory mechanism of flowering in GF dipterocarp species
column22: thus enabling the prediction of flowering time.

column1: In our current study
column2: we aimed to capture the transcriptomic changes in a dipterocarp species
column3: S. curtisii
column4: across a flowering year and infer the physiological and molecular mechanisms leading to flowering in this species. To achieve this objective
column5: we utilized the transcriptome of both leaf and bud tissues
column6: and more than double the number of time points compared to our previous study (). Our current study provided a greater resolution of the molecular events leading to flowering in a GF dipterocarp species. We identified and characterized differentially expressed unigenes (DEUs) by comparing them to publicly available databases and A. thaliana gene sets that are associated with responses to biotic and abiotic stresses
column7: nutrient levels
column8: and hormonal treatments. We also integrated meteorological records to uncover the molecular pathways interacting with the environmental signals and finally discussed the possible mechanisms involved in the floral regulation of this GF dipterocarp species.

column4: transcript quantification and principal component analysis (PCA) were performed. Subsequently

column1: Our study found high transcriptional variation between leaf and bud of S. curtisii during GF
column2: including the expression of A. thaliana homologs involved in flowering. Despite the inter-individual differences in the expression profiles
column3: we were able to identify and characterize transcriptional profiles of unigenes that are differentially expressed in the vegetative and reproductive stages of the trees. We interpreted the outcome of our transcriptome analysis along with available meteorological data and proposed a preliminary framework of floral initiation and development in this GF dipterocarp species.

column1: The increasing rate of climate change
column2: characterized by higher temperature fluctuations () and more frequent and severe droughts (; )
column3: among others
column4: is expected to have a disproportionate impact on the phenology of plants and animals in tropical regions (). The fitness and abundance of tropical communities
column5: including GF dipterocarp species
column6: can be adversely affected by a slight temperature increase (; ). As these plant species is a valuable source of sustenance for a variety of seed and fruit predators including wild pigs and rodents (; )
column7: alteration in their phenology could have potential cascading effects on the tropical forest communities.

column1: Long term phenological records of tropical rainforests in the Malay Peninsula have shown that flowering events are always preceded by a drop in daily minimum temperature after a brief period of drought (; ). In fact
column2: mathematical models that take into account the synergistic effects of both drought and a drop in daily minimum temperature have been found to be reliable at predicting GF occurrences (; ). The cumulative rainfall data ()
column3: along with the expression profile of DEU clusters that were enriched with homologs of drought-responsive genes ( and ) indicate that the S. curtisii in our study experienced water stress due to a moderate drought lasting from before TP-D until TP-E. The upregulation of ScFT1 (Unigene: TRINITY_DN6921_c0_g2_i2) in leaf shortly after drought began (TP-D; ) and the upregulation of ScFT1 in bud post-drought (TP-E; ) further corroborated the hypothesis that drought is one of the floral triggers in dipterocarps (; ; ). Moreover
column4: the observed expression changes in homologs associated with elevated levels of ABA
column5: IAA
column6: and JA hormones () that coincides with the occurrence of drought also supported the aforementioned hypothesis. Roles of these hormones in the floral regulation and signaling pathways of plants responding to stress
column7: including drought
column8: have been established in many other species such as A. thaliana
column9: rice and Citrus spp. (reviewed in ; ). Among these hormones
column10: ABA is particularly important in the hormonal response to drought stress () as it regulates stomatal opening to reduce water loss (). Interestingly
column11: ABA has also been reported to function in drought-induced flowering in A. thaliana () and Citrus spp. (; ). Auxin (IAA) plays a role in modulating leaf water uptake and regulating the expression of antioxidant enzymes to detoxify reactive oxygen species (). Similarly
column12: JA contributes to abiotic stress responses by increasing resistance to oxidative stress () and maintaining downstream drought-responsive pathways such as those related to modulation of shoot biomass and photosynthetic rate ().

column1: Despite a sharp drop in daily minimum temperature observed prior to TP-D ()
column2: we did not find any gene set associated with differential regulation in response to decrease in temperature to be overrepresented in any of the DEU clusters ( and ). However
column3: several studies have shown that temperature drops always precede GF (; ; )
column4: and that the occurrence of drought alone does not always result in GF (; ). Therefore
column5: it is possible that S. curtisii employs different temperature-responsive mechanisms than A. thaliana. This scenario is not unlikely
column6: as similar divergence has been reported in other floral regulatory pathways. For example
column7: FLOWERING LOCUS C (FLC) encodes a key floral repressor in the vernalization pathway of A. thaliana
column8: grasses have developed vernalization response that is completely independent of FLC
column9: relying instead on four central genes: VERNALIZATION 1–3 (VRN1–3) and VEGETATIVE TO REPRODUCTIVE TRANSITION 2 (VRT2) (reviewed in ). Further studies using transcriptome profiles of GF dipterocarp trees treated with low temperature and drought under controlled environments could help to verify the potential of low temperature as a flowering cue and further prove that a short period of drought can induce flowering in the species. However
column10: this would be a practically challenging and costly endeavor for tall tropical trees that flower irregularly.

column1: Additionally
column2: several homologs of flowering-time genes were found to be differentially expressed after the short dry spell ( and ; –). Given the accumulating evidence of organisms and populations adapting to climate change through phenotypic plasticity and genotypic evolution (reviewed in )
column3: further research focusing on these homologs could help us understand how these rapid environmental changes will impact the reproductive phenology of GF dipterocarp species and how this phenomenon will evolve in the future.

column1: Accumulated nutrients have been proposed as a limiting factor in GF
column2: which could explain why some mature trees do not flower during GF (; ). In the present study
column3: we found molecular evidence showing changes in the expression levels of homologs associated with a decrease in phosphorus level prior to flowering (). This finding is consistent with a previous report that investigated mineral nutrient storage dynamics prior to mast reproduction in another dipterocarp species
column4: Dryobalapnos aromatica (). Although we identified homologs of A. thaliana genes responsive to nitrogen to be overrepresented in the DEUs of S. curtisii ()
column5: these unigenes did not show any specific expression pattern ( and ). In addition to phosphorus and nitrogen
column6: carbohydrates have been demonstrated to be utilized for floral initiation and subsequent development of floral organs (). The results of our cluster analysis indicate changes in the expression level of unigenes correlated with an increase in sucrose level in leaves during the brief period of drought (). An elevated level of sucrose in leaves during drought have been reported in other trees such as East Asian white birch () and tropical starfruit (). Furthermore
column7: the cluster analysis identified changes in expression levels of homologs correlated with a reduction in carbon concentrations in bud prior to flowering time (). Previous studies have suggested that flower bud act as carbohydrate ‘utilizing sinks’
column8: metabolizing rather than storing carbohydrate (; ).

column1: In the current study
column2: we inferred the fluctuation of nutrient levels based on associated or correlated transcript expressions. To gain a more comprehensive understanding of the roles and dynamics of plant resources during flowering time
column3: future studies should consider integrating nutritional content information such as nitrogen
column4: phosphorus and carbon concentrations. The collection of sufficient tissues from carbohydrate sinks such as stems and trunks () without damaging the branches was not feasible in this study due to the need for frequent repeated sampling to capture the floral initiation time points of these trees with unpredictable flowering intervals. Furthermore
column5: canopy access for tall tropical trees has thus far been limited
column6: hence observations and sample collections in our study were done by climbing approximately 40 m above the ground canopy. It is also important to acknowledge the low statistical power of this study due to the limited number of biological replicates and the absence of technical replicates. In addition
column7: due to logistical constraints
column8: rainfall measurements in the current study were obtained from the nearest hydrological station located adjacent to the forest reserve
column9: rather than being recorded at the specific location of each individual tree. Therefore
column10: to address these limitations
column11: it would be essential to employ improved monitoring and sampling techniques e.g.
column12: by utilizing technology such as unmanned aerial vehicles (drones) for sample collection
column13: to facilitate future research. Notwithstanding these challenges and limitations
column14: we were able to conduct the present study on two individual trees and obtain clear and consistent results.

column1: To date
column2: this is the first genome-wide transcriptome sequencing study conducted concurrently on both leaf and bud of a dipterocarp species over a flowering season. The characterization of S. curtisii transcriptome in this study has led to the identification of numerous unigenes that are homologous to A. thaliana flowering-time genes
column3: including unigenes that were differentially expressed during the flowering season. We have also identified unigenes that are important during the switch from vegetative to reproductive stages but are not known to be involved in the A. thaliana flowering mechanism
column4: hence warrant further investigation. Our ecological transcriptome approach
column5: which involved cluster analysis of DEUs in S. curtisii and enrichment analysis with A. thaliana gene sets and climatic data
column6: suggests that the trees perceived proximate flowering cues
column7: such as drought
column8: approximately three months prior to floral initiation. This was followed by changes in expression of genes associated with hormone levels
column9: as well as phosphorus and carbohydrate contents
column10: as the trees entered reproductive stages. The outcomes of this study provide insight into the molecular and physiological mechanisms underlying floral regulation in a GF dipterocarp species and highlight the need for further research in this area to fully elucidate these complex processes.

column1: Currently

column1: In this paper
column2: we used Harbin

column4: as an example and hypothesized that urban forests have largely different landscape metrics on the basis of the forest type

column6: and urban–rural gradients

The following questions have been answered in this paper: (1) How large are the variations in the characteristics of urban forest landscapes in Harbin City? What are the differences in forest types
column8: administrative districts
column9: and urban–rural gradients? (2) How large are the differences in urban forest biomass and underground soils? Which landscape metrics are most significantly associated with these carbon sequestration differences? (3) Is there any management suggestions from the view of landscape configurations for promoting carbon storage in urban forests? What type of landscape-related suggestions for the exact evaluation of urban forest carbon storage could be derived? The solutions will favor landscape-carbon sequestration sciences and help to define a suitable strategy for urban afforestation and management.

column1: Urban forest fragmentation was induced and influenced by many factors
column2: such as urban building density (); deforestation and reforestation processes during urban sprawl (); socioeconomic factors such as urban structure change
column3: industry-related economic boom
column4: increase in migrant resident population; and increased income of city residents (); economically driven intense anthropogenic activities; and the absence of a sustainable environmental management and conservation strategy (). The fragmented landscape may degrade habitat quality; threaten species richness
column5: abundance
column6: and composition (; ); and affect phylogenetic diversity (). This may be a huge risk to forest management in cities such as Harbin. Harbin has experienced an exponential economic growth since the 1980s and population boom since the 1950s ()
column7: resulting in insufficient greenery services in urban regions. The largely fragmented forest landscapes and uneven distribution in different regions of Harbin City require new efforts for urban forest protection
column8: new plantation afforestation
column9: and sustainable management activities for maximizing urban forest services.

column1: Urban forests can provide various ecosystem services and values to a city and its residents
column2: like the removal of air pollutants ()
column3: alleviation of the urban heat island effect ()
column4: and reduction and offset of carbon emissions (; ). These ecological functions are linked to landscape characteristics based on the common consensus that environmental patterns strongly influence ecological processes (; ). Our findings highlighted that carbon storage function and landscape pattern of urban forests in Harbin are linked and landscape regulation is possible to improve urban vegetation and soil carbon storage. The carbon storage function of urban forests could contribute to the alleviation of climate changes () and reduction of the negative effects of fast urbanization (). The increase of carbon storage at individual tree and patch scale is possible through tree health promotion

column7: and soil improvement (). With respect to landscape pattern
column8: it is important to increase carbon storage through landscape regulation
column9: which should be an aspect of promoting forest ecological function in limited urban areas (; ).

column1: The landscape of urban forests in Harbin was highly fragmented when compared with other local cities
column2: and the fragmentation was different in different forest types
column3: administrative districts
column4: ring road- and urban history-related urban–rural gradients. The fragmentation of the landscape was strongly associated with carbon storage functions in trees and soils. LSI increased along with the urban–rural gradients
column5: and its positive relationship with SOC indicates that LSI contributes to the underlying mechanism of urbanization-induced carbon accumulation in highly urbanized regions. The relationships between carbon storage and landscape metrics manifested possible ways to improve the urban forest carbon storage
column6: such as improvement of the whole urban carbon storage by increasing the afforested area. An increase in the largest patch percentage and patch aggregation could increase the soil carbon storage per hectare. This study would help to promote carbon-oriented management practices for urban vegetation and exact evaluation of urban forest carbon sequestration by including landscape metrics and urbanization intensities.

column1: 31|10.7717/peerj.16367|Enhancing restoration success of rare plants in an arid-tropical climate through water-saving technologies: a case study of Scalesia affinis ssp. brachyloba in the Galapagos Islands| Plunkett  Esme |2023-12-6|"Despite occupying less than 5% of the global terrestrial area
column2: oceanic islands are home to >20% of the planet’s terrestrial plant and vertebrate species (). The Galapagos archipelago and its flora are no exception
column3: with 37% of its plant taxa as endemic (). Despite its globally recognized significance
column4: the Galapagos flora is currently under high levels of degradation () and extinction risk () which has led to Galapagos flora becoming the focus of major restoration activities in the last decade (). In the Galapagos
column5: as in other arid tropic regions
column6: dry forest species are some of the most threatened species due to land-use change and stochastic rainfall seasonality
column7: which can undermine regeneration (; ; ; ).

column1: The scarcity of water and high summer temperatures in arid environments are some of the main challenges of restoration programs in these climates (; ). Although tropical climates have wet summers
column2: climate change has altered rainfall predictability
column3: making plants more prone to senesce when water is scarce (; ). Water-saving technologies (WSTs) (i.e.
column4: equipment or tools that can reduce the changing levels of water balance in soil) can improve seedling survival by providing regular supplies of water and have been useful in restoration programs in other arid regions around the world (). WSTs have the potential to accelerate Galapagos plant species’ restoration by making them less susceptible to rainfall seasonality. For example
column5: given that water limitation has been identified as a key driver of seedling survival
column6: preliminary restoration activities with these technologies have shown promising results (; ).

column1: We focused on Scalesia affinis ssp. brachyloba as a model taxon to investigate the effect of WSTs on improving species restoration. In 2013
column2: the Charles Darwin Foundation (CDF)
column3: via the Galapagos Verde 2050 (GV2050) program
column4: began an initiative to increase populations of Scalesia spp.
column5: with a particular focus on S. affinis ssp. brachyloba which only grows in arid-tropical conditions () in the south-west border of Santa Cruz Island (). The species’ has evergreen leaves and a long taproot
column6: functional characteristics for a dry environment ()
column7: making it an ideal candidate for testing the potential value of WSTs. Results from this study may also be useful for understanding other species in this genus. Scalesia (Asteraceae) is the most diverse of seven endemic genera of the Galapagos
column8: with 15 species and more than 20 taxa
column9: and is often cited as a prominent example of adaptive radiation (; ; ) from colonizing barren lava fields
column10: to forming a forest canopy (; ; ; ; ). Many Scalesia spp. are threatened by anthropogenic habitat changes
column11: introduced species
column12: and the changing climate (; ). The population of S. affinis ssp. brachyloba (hereafter S. brachyloba) on Santa Cruz Island has dramatically declined (). The historical distribution of the taxa was in the same area where the human settlements have established and expanded on Santa Cruz Island (; ; ). By the year 2000 the population of S. brachyloba within the main town of Puerto Ayora was recorded at five individuals (; )
column13: and by 2007 all areas with previously large populations of S. brachyloba had seen population declines (). Low natural and assisted regeneration of the species hinders its restoration. For example
column14: the species may reach only 17% survival 3 months after germination
column15: and 19% one year after transplanting to the wild ().

column1: Here we aim to use S. brachyloba both as a study taxon for evaluating the effect of a water-saving technology (Groasis Waterboxx®
column2: hereafter just “Groasis”) on dry forest species
column3: but results may also inform future restoration efforts of this species on Santa Cruz Island
column4: Galapagos. Through this process we also account for the influence of the two main factors that shape arid-tropical climates: precipitation and maximum temperature.

column1: Through regular monitoring over six years
column2: we collected data on the survival of S. brachyloba seedlings that were planted (under National Park permission N° PC-10-21) using treatments that included Groasis and controls (no technology). We also used available data from associated environmental conditions ().

column1: Our study sites were based on the southwest border of Santa Cruz Island (−0.7351
column2: −90.3089)—the second largest island on the Galapagos archipelago ()
column3: in the dry lowland zone of the island. The climate in this zone is arid tropical with a median annual rainfall of 227 mm
column4: though 71% of this precipitation occurs during the wet season months of January through May and mean monthly temperature ranging from 19° to 30.5 °C (; ). Vegetation community is mostly xeric
column5: composed by S. affinis
column6: Oputia echios and Croton scouleri (). Soils are mainly clay or clay-loam (). We studied S. brachyloba at six sites which totaled an area of one hectare and were all locations where S. brachyloba had been historically found—locations that were confirmed by samples in the CDF Herbarium ()

Two sites correspond to natural populations: one site was an enclosed area of national park land in an area called ‘Garrapatero’ in the southeast of the island and another site called ‘Mirador’ was on public land in Puerto Ayora (). The other four sites were all small gardens located in Puerto Ayora

column1: Our results suggest that water limitation is an important factor for the early-stage survival and restoration of S. brachyloba. We found that water availability through precipitation increases the survival of this species and the use of Groasis consistently improved survival (
column2: and )
column3: probably by providing a balanced input of water. However
column4: survival differences between control and Groasis narrowed two years after planting (). This coincided with when most Groasis boxes were removed
column5: suggesting that S. brachyloba plants may benefit from a longer application of this technology. Additionally
column6: as the study was part of a large-scale restoration project framework
column7: there were certain limitations in the experimental design because not all combinations of treatments could be applied (e.g.
column8: applying the same volume of water to Groasis and controls)
column9: thus
column10: effects of water availability presented here confounded the application of the Groasis technology.

column1: Individuals of S. brachyloba usually exhibit resilience in their survival during extended periods of reduced water-availability. Having evolved with regular drought
column2: arid-tropical species such as S. brachyloba may be resilient to such conditions (; ). The Galapagos archipelago’s location is within the El Niño Southern Oscillation (ENSO) region making it subject to drought and rainfall cycles that are especially pronounced during strong events
column3: with some years seeing up to 3 m of annual precipitation (). Many plant species in Galapagos
column4: including Scalesia spp.
column5: benefit from short-term increases in rainfall associated with El Niño events ()
column6: as seen in other arid-tropical plant populations following an El Niño event (). This period is followed by a large spike in mortality of adults from long-term exposure to water via precipitation. This may explain why the plants grown with Groasis showed a weak negative trend with increasing precipitation ()
column7: indicating that the water added to the technology plus high levels of rainfall may be more than the amount that S. brachyloba can withstand. Nonetheless
column8: even the lowest values of predicted survival in Groasis were close to the maximum predicted survival for controls ()
column9: indicating that even in extremely humid events
column10: too little water has a more detrimental effect on S. brachyloba than too much water
column11: providing a support to the value of using Groasis despite intense rainfall events.

column1: The Galapagos hot season (i.e.
column2: warmer land and sea surface temperatures from January to May and high rainfall variability) principally determines dry-zone species’ annual productivity
column3: such as that of S. brachyloba (; ; ). Our results similarly show that predicted survival was associated with rainfall
column4: and that under lower maximum temperatures (in the June-December cold season) this interaction is more evident (). Although the test of temperature above or below 27 °C in this study was arbitrary and only for visualization purposes
column5: one possible explanation is that S. brachyloba may be more active below certain temperature thresholds. Many tropical forest species are known to have a threshold around 27–29 °C where photosynthesis starts to decline but respiration increases (). Further research addressing temperature threshold for Galapagos’ plant species is needed to evaluate this hypothesis.

column1: Overall
column2: most high mortality rates in S. brachyloba appeared either after periods of cold temperature and high rainfall (>50 mm per week
column3: maximum temperature <27 °C) () or after periods of warm temperature and low rainfall (<50 mm per week
column4: maximum temperature >27 °C)
column5: indicating two extreme conditions that may constrain species development. The link between high water availability and other Scalesia species’ mortality has been attributed to root rot
column6: high winds
column7: and vine overgrowth (; ; ). While temperature and rainfall influence the species’ survival
column8: the causal mechanism was not addressed in this study. Further research is needed to establish how weather conditions directly shape S. brachyloba seedling survival. Research on S. pedunculata has shown that large-scale
column9: rainfall-induced mortality makes way for the next cohort of young plants (; ; ; ; ; ). Rainfall
column10: especially during extreme weather events
column11: may thus influence the natural regeneration cycles of related species. Similar patterns
column12: though less pronounced
column13: have been found in the mortality and regeneration of five dry-zone Scalesia species in response to the high rainfall of El Niño events (; ; ). All in all
column14: the results of this study are informative in addressing potential planting windows for S. brachyloba by avoiding the most extreme conditions.

column1: Our study only applies to the first few years of growth in young S. brachyloba
column2: a period less than both the species life expectancy and ENSO events; therefore
column3: future research should consider longer-term demographic changes in response to weather conditions. Other challenges of the current study include the large variability between sites. Evaluating site differences were beyond the scope of the study since our goal was to extract general trends on the use of WSTs for the focal species. Nonetheless
column4: future work should seek to further examine the importance of local conditions. For example
column5: no plants survived more than 851 days at the Garrapatero site
column6: which may be due to its much more rural location
column7: where plants may be more susceptible to pest attacks (P Jaramillo
column8: pers. obs.
column9: 2019). The overall low sample and imbalanced design is also a fundamental limitation of this study. This is largely due to the focus on conservation of a rare species that is already limited in sample size (i.e.
column10: restoring S. brachyloba)
column11: and retrospective nature of the study (the study was conducted using data that were collected prior to a central experimental design). These limitations reduce the strength with which our results can be generalized
column12: and it is imperative that additional prospective studies are conducted for further assessing the best tools for effectively restoring S. brachyloba and other species of conservation concern in Galapagos.

column1: Galapagos arid-zone rainfall is largely determined by sea-surface temperatures
column2: and global climate change is expected to create a wetter and warmer climate in the Galapagos (; ). It is more important to understand how these changes may affect endangered species in the Galapagos
column3: especially by examining the effect of water availability and temperature on their survival and growth. This study gives insights into the effects of both factor on the early growth of S. brachyloba and shows promise in the use of water-saving technologies to improve survival in the face of climatic fluctuations. However
column4: further investigations into the life cycle of S. brachyloba across more conditions and larger time frames are necessary to inform a more complete restoration plan for this species.

column1: The restoration of endemic species in tropical dry forest ecosystems is needed to prevent the continued degradation of these habitats (). Water-saving technology has the potential to increase the survival of dry forest plant species
column2: such as Scalesia affinis ssp. brachyloba. This study investigated S. brachyloba’ relationship with two main weather conditions in arid-tropical climates: precipitation and maximum temperature
column3: and from this
column4: has been able to determine the effectiveness of a WST on its survival. Application of similar experimental efforts using WSTs on endangered dry forest species may produce similar positive successes
column5: but understanding other factors that impact the species’ early establishment is essential for contributing to the recovery of a globally threatened ecosystem.

column2: exotic plants distort native bee (Hymenoptera: Apoidea) assemblages in high elevation Andes ecosystem| Henríquez-Piskulich  Patricia |2018-11-7|"Native bee species are not only important as native plant pollinators
column3: but also 15–30% of productive crops require their pollination services (). Declines of these insects have been reported at a global scale (; ; ). Human activities are the main drivers of native bee’s downturn through: fragmentation

column6: ultimately
column7: destruction (; ). Furthermore

column9: degradation of biodiversity
column10: and reduction of food production from negative effects on native flowers and crops of economic importance that depend on pollinators to achieve reproduction (; ). Pollinator declines may not be easy to recover from if they continue progressing from the continuous anthropogenic pressure on pollinators (; ).

column1: This situation could be even more critical for native insects adapted to extreme environments
column2: including bees. At a local scale
column3: insects may be especially sensitive to human impact in habitats with extreme climate fluctuations (; ). This could be the case of high elevation environments
column4: such as montane ecosystems
column5: also recognized as hotspots of biological diversity (). Under these conditions
column6: in addition to overcoming current human impacts
column7: native bees must face extreme environmental variation such as severe changes in temperature
column8: elevated levels of ultraviolet radiation
column9: less partial pressure of atmospheric gases
column10: drastic oscillations in the amount of precipitations
column11: strong wind speed
column12: among others (). All these abiotic factors have been associated to comparatively reduced diversity and specialized insects in high-altitude habitats (; ). In general
column13: studies have found lower richness and abundance of insects as altitude increases (; ; )
column14: and
column15: regarding native bee species
column16: the same pattern has been reported (; ).

column1: With economic development
column2: it often increases environment degradation in favor of urbanization
column3: especially in countries with unsustainable development policies (). This problem may also impact high-altitude ecosystems (; ). Regarding the consequences of urbanization on native bee habitats
column4: it has been demonstrated that this affects bee richness and evenness
column5: and also
column6: delays peak abundance during the bee season and decreases temporal turnover (; ). Also
column7: the landscape modifications have been found to affect bee assemblages due to species-specific responses such as: body size
column8: nesting habits
column9: feeding behavior and sociality level
column10: among others (; ; ; ). Considering this
column11: there could be an array of different responses: Some species could respond positively while others could be under threat of disappearing from an ecosystem (; ). Bees with feeding specializations (also called “oligolectic”) gather resources on floral species of one family or genera of plants
column12: and therefore
column13: are less flexible to changes in their habitats due to human-derived modifications (; ). For instance
column14: changes in the landscape decrease the proportion of parasitic ()
column15: solitary and also larger-sized native bee species (). Contrastingly
column16: urbanization may favor cavity-nesting species () probably due to the higher nesting resources for these species in urbanized areas (). Therefore
column17: knowledge of the pollinator’s resources and life-history traits is required to correctly assess the most likely pollination responses under the effects of human activities ().

column1: The impact of exotic plants over native bee species has been poorly studied (; ; ). To the best of our knowledge
column2: the effects of exotic plant species on mountain native bee assemblages are yet to be revealed. Their influence could be particularly relevant if they are capable of modifying the landscapes and native plant communities
column3: due to cascading effects on different trophic levels (; ). In high elevation environments it has been suggested that exotic plants may jeopardize native pollination services () and potentially affect native bee populations through modifying the relative abundance and the diversity of native plant species (). Previous studies have found a negative relationship between the presence of exotic plants and the abundance
column4: species richness
column5: and evenness of native bees (; ). However
column6: contrary to general trends in landscape studies
column7: there are no works on the effect of exotic plants over the species-specific response of native bees due to the threats previously mentioned. If exotic plant species could alter mountain bee assemblages
column8: this may have significant effects on pollination of native plant species. Since their effect could vary depending on which bee species is considered
column9: some native bee species could benefit from the introduction of exotic plants
column10: as providers of additional resources (; ). Nonetheless
column11: it has been recently demonstrated that the introduction of exotic plants could be even more problematic that changes in the landscape
column12: affecting not only insect assemblages but also complete plant-pollinator networks (). If exotic plants replace the majority of native plant species
column13: not only bees would face the consequences of this introduction
column14: but also the whole ecosystem services may be hampered due to alterations to the biota ().

column1: Despite above-mentioned problems
column2: we found no published studies that focus on the response of native bee assemblages towards the effects of the landscape’s changes and the introduction of exotic flora in high elevation ecosystems. These dimensions could be essential for the understanding of bee declines
column3: and preventing further losses not only of these insects
column4: but also the rest of pollinating animals (). In this work

column6: we focused on the influence of the abundance of exotic plant species and the response of native bee assemblages in order to evaluate species-specific responses. In general
column7: there is still much to explore from montane biomes (). In this context
