column12: that the introduction of exotic flora that comes along with this
column13: could also play a role in changing the assemblage due to the close relationship between bees and their floral resources. Our objectives were to assess the effect of urbanization over high-altitude native bee diversity and of exotic floral abundance over the assemblage.

33|10.7717/peerj.15188|The influence of climate warming on flowering phenology in relation to historical annual and seasonal temperatures and plant functional traits| Geissler  Cole |2023-4-21|Since the late 19th century the average global temperature has risen by 1.1 °C ()
column2: and in the contiguous United States
column3: the yearly average temperature has increased by 0.09 °C each decade since 1895
column4: surpassing the 1895–2020 average in 1956 (). In the region in which this study was conducted
column5: the yearly average temperature increased by 0.11 °C each decade since 1895
column6: surpassing the 1895–2021 average in 1958 (). This change in climate has the potential to influence many biological and ecological processes
column7: particularly changes in phenology (timing of biological events) in plants and animals
column8: given the potential for temperature-dependence of such traits (). Moreover
column9: the effects of climate change on phenology may in turn have further implications in terms of the consequential impact on ecological interactions. Among these is the potential impact of climate change on pollination due to the differential temporal response of plants and their pollinators to warming seasonal temperatures resulting in asynchrony between flowering time and the emergence of pollinators (; ; ; ; ; ). Similarly
column10: a differential response to warming between prey emergence and predator demand has been shown to significantly impact different trophic levels in the same community (). Additionally
column11: independent shifts in the phenology of invasive plants and their herbivores in response to warming suggest that climate change will influence biological invasions (; ; ).

column1: Given the potential for climate warming to influence plant flowering phenology and the broader ecological consequences of such changes
column2: this has become an important area of study. The effect of warming on flowering phenology has been addressed experimentally through the physical warming of plots in natural settings (; ; ; ; ; ; ); in field studies over temperature gradients with elevation (; ; ) and latitude (; ; ; )
column3: and along urban-rural temperature gradients resulting from heat island effects in cities (). Another approach has been to use historical data from herbarium collections to assess the effects of warming on flowering phenology. Herbarium specimens are most often collected during blooming
column4: indicating flowering dates and location
column5: and many such collections span hundreds of years
column6: offering a unique long-term historical perspective of the relationship between flowering time and historical trends in warming ().

column1: The potential limitations of using herbarium specimens to document long-term changes in flowering time stem from collection biases due to non-random collecting practices related to location
column2: sampling time
column3: and collector preferences for certain species
column4: as well as duplicate sampling of individual species from the same location on the same date (; ; ; ; ). Although these biases can limit the value of phenological information of individual specimens
column5: the use of large sample sizes while avoiding duplicates; and including plants from a large number of families
column6: and with different functional traits and flowering seasons can allow for a more accurate assessment of the long-term shifts in phenology at a given location (; ). Bias can also come from inconsistent assessment of phenophase or the timing of onset
column7: peak
column8: and end flowering dates
column9: which becomes more problematic for species that flower over longer parts of a season. This can be mitigated by using a consistent application of flowering time based on the percentage of open flowers
column10: and by using plants with similar durations of flowering (). Finally
column11: the use of the collection date of flowering herbarium specimens as a proxy for actual flowering time has been validated in studies that show no significant difference between specimen collection dates and flowering dates observed in the field in the same year (; ; ; ; ; ).

column1: Prior studies on long-term trends in flowering phenology in relation to historical temperatures revealed changes in flowering time by as much as 2–6 days earlier per 1 °C increase in temperature (; ; ; )
column2: and 8–16 days earlier per 1 °C in more recent decades (; ). However
column3: not all species show the same temporal response
column4: with some blooming earlier and others later over time (; ; ; ; ; ). One possible reason for observed interspecific differences in response to warming is that individual species may respond differently to seasonal temperatures within a year
column5: such that winter or spring temperatures could independently or divergently influence flowering time depending on species specific cues for germination
column6: and leaf or flower emergence (; ). Many prior studies of climate warming on flowering phenology have been based solely on the temporal trends using collection year as the only explanatory variable. While this may be well suited for examining long term trends in phenology with warming
column7: since temperatures have risen during the collection periods; linking collection dates to concurrent temperature data from the same locations allows for the consideration of seasonal temperatures within a year as drivers of phenological change. Use of temperature data can also more accurately reveal year-to-year variation in phenological response to temperature (; ; ; ; ; ; ; ).

column1: Another reason for mixed results among species in prior studies of warming on flowering phenology may be due to differences in plant functional characteristics known to be related to phenology (). Interspecific character differences that likely influence temporal responses to warming include native status (native versus non-native species)
column2: growth habit (woody versus herbaceous species)
column3: fruit type (species with dry versus fleshy fruit)
column4: and seasonality of blooming (spring-blooming versus summer-blooming species). The native status of species could influence phenological responses to temporal warming because non-native species
column5: especially those that are invasive
column6: tend to exhibit more phenotypic plasticity than native species (; )
column7: and thus may be more likely to exhibit changes in flowering time in response to warming temperatures especially over the short term (; ; ). Growth habit also has the potential to influence the temporal changes in flowering time. There is some evidence that in woody species phenology may tend to be cued by photoperiod more than temperature
column8: so woody species may be less likely to exhibit changes in flowering time with temperature deviations (; ; ). Fruit type may influence the phenological response to warming since plants with fleshy fruits may be more constrained by fruit and seed development time and disperser abundance than timing for pollination (; )
column9: which in turn could influence the response to warming. Also
column10: accounting for the season in which different species bloom can shed further light on interspecific divergent responses to warming ().

column1: The objective of this study was to use herbarium specimens for 36 species from 28 families collected from 1884–2015 to determine general patterns of long-term changes in flowering time in relation to concurrent historical temperature data in eastern Pennsylvania
column2: USA. We also assessed whether yearly average temperature
column3: and winter and spring temperatures elicit different responses across species. Additionally
column4: our goal was to determine the extent to which differences in native status
column5: growth habit
column6: fruit type
column7: and the seasonality of blooming are related to the response of flowering phenology to warming. We predicted that flowering time will tend to occur earlier in response to warming over the time period considered
column8: and that there would be differences among species in the predictive strength of yearly average
column9: and winter and spring temperatures on flowering phenology trends. We also hypothesized that the relationship between flowering phenology and warming temperatures would be stronger in non-native species in contrast to native species
column10: in herbaceous compared to woody species
column11: in species with dry fruits relative to those with fleshy fruits
column12: and in spring blooming species compared to those that flower in summer.

column1: We studied historical flowering times in relation to temperature using herbarium specimens from Berks
column2: Bucks
column3: Lehigh
column4: and Northampton counties in the Lehigh and Delaware River watersheds in eastern Pennsylvania. This area is characterized by fragmented mature deciduous forest nested within a largely agricultural
column5: suburban matrix. Typical of oak-hickory
column6: mesophytic forests in this region
column7: the dominant tree species are Quercus and Carya spp.
column8: Acer rubrum
column9: Fagus grandifolia
column10: Liriodendron tulipifera
column11: and Juglans nigra (; ). Development in these areas has been restricted primarily to agricultural land along the urban corridor within the study region with the greatest suburban expansion occurring from the mid-1990s to the present (). The growing season in this region averages 177 days with the last frost occurring in early April and the first frost in mid-October ().

column1: We obtained historical climate data from the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) through the Climate Data Online service and Global Summary of the Month CSV datasets (). To best represent temperatures across the region over time
column2: and because data from individual weather stations are often incomplete
column3: we collected the daily average temperatures between 1884 and 2015 from 11 individual climate stations located in the study area with the most extensive historical coverage. Then
column4: because of minimal differences in temperatures among stations
column5: we averaged the daily temperature data values across all climate stations in the four counties. We then calculated the monthly averages of the daily temperatures (TAVG) across the counties in our study
column6: and used them to calculate average annual temperatures (YearTAVG). We calculated winter temperatures for each year as an average of the December (prior year)
column7: January and February temperatures (WinterTAVG)
column8: and the average spring onset temperatures for each year as an average of March
column9: April
column10: and May temperatures (SpringTAVG) to determine the extent to which these seasonal temperatures are related to phenological change (; ; ). Although we recognize that the lack of continuity of temperature data across weather stations could potentially be problematic in that different stations contribute data in different years
column11: we minimized this potential by using a large number of weather stations with overlapping data and by averaging across stations on any given date. This allowed for a more accurate representation of annual variation in phenological response to temperature and the consideration of seasonal temperatures within a year as drivers of phenological change (; ; ; ; ; ).

column1: We obtained historical flowering times using physical and digital herbarium specimens from the herbaria at Muhlenberg College
column2: The Academy of Natural Sciences
column3: and The Morris Arboretum. We sourced digital specimens from the Mid-Atlantic Megalopolis Project through the Mid-Atlantic Herbaria Consortium. We selected insect-pollinated species from these collections that had at least 30 specimens collected in our study area at peak flowering after the removal of duplicate flowering specimens with identical date and location. Peak flowering was determined as the presence of buds
column4: flowers and fruits with ≥75% fully opened flowers (). We excluded herbarium specimens from before 1884 because of limited climate data for that time period
column5: and after 2015 due to reduced collection rates. None of the specimens used were collected from large urban centers or industrial sites. Based on the above criteria
column6: we were able to include 36 species with large enough sample sizes across our categories of interest (native status
column7: growth form

column9: and blooming season ().

column1: Notes. *Species with flowering periods longer than 5 months excluded in some analyses. We converted each specimen’s collection date to an integer reflecting the ordered day within a year. We classified each species with regard to native status

column1: In order to predict changes in flowering date in response to changes in temperature over time
column2: we created three linear mixed-effects models. Each model used a different climate variable as the predictor: average yearly temperature (YearTAVG)
column3: average winter temperature (WinterTAVG)
column4: and average spring onset temperature (SpringTAVG). In all models
column5: species was treated as a random effect to account for the variability of flowering dates between species. We calculated confidence intervals for the change in predicted flowering date based on a unit change in temperature as indicated by the slopes of each model. In instances of non-normal residuals or heteroscedasticity

column1: We have shown that in eastern Pennsylvania flowering phenology has significantly advanced in response to rising temperatures. The species in this study bloomed on average approximately 2.26 days earlier per 1 °C increase in the yearly average temperature from 1884–2015 (). This is consistent with other similar studies showing plants flowering 2–6 days earlier (; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ). The plants in this study are on the low end of this range for earlier flowering with warming. One possible explanation for this is that much of the area of study remained as undeveloped agricultural land through the early 1990s () and none of the specimens used in this study were collected from large urban or industrial centers
column2: perhaps in contrast to other areas in which this type of study have been conducted. This may have reduced any heat island effect thereby amplifying warming trends and is known to influence flower phenology (; ). Another potential issue is the general decline in herbarium collection rates in more recent decades which could result in an under estimate of phenological response to warming in more recent years. This is the case for a few of the species in our study
column3: which we attempted to address through the use of temperature rather than year as an independent variable in analyses. However
column4: it is possible that this resulted in an underestimation of the of the phenological response to warming in more recent years. Also consistent with prior studies showing early flowering with warming for a number of species
column5: is that not all species flowered earlier and some actually flowered later (). Furthermore
column6: differences in the plant species between studies of this sort could explain variation in results due to possible phylogenetic constraints on the phenological responses to warming.

column1: To further explore this variation among species and to shed additional light on the possible mechanistic bases of changes in flowering time with climate change
column2: we also considered the influence of seasonal temperatures within a year and a number of plant functional characteristics that could potentially influence the response of flowering time to warming. In our analysis of the influence of seasonal temperatures on flowering time across all species considered here
column3: we found that warmer spring temperatures were a strong driver of phenological response with plants on average flowering 2.93 days earlier per 1 °C ()
column4: which was greater than the response to increases in annual temperature (). This makes sense given the conventional thinking that earlier spring warming would initiate the termination of dormancy
column5: germination
column6: and leaf and flower emergence; and is consistent with prior studies that examined this (; ; ; ; ; ; ). However
column7: the question remains why some plant species appeared not to respond to warming temperatures.

column1: The lack of response of some species to warming average may actually be driven by warmer winter temperatures which can delay dormancy or chilling requirements for germination via vernalization
column2: which in turn could delay spring events such as flowering or leafing out in some species (; ; ). This is consistent with our finding that increasing winter temperatures were not significantly related to flowering phenology
column3: and the lack of a negative slope in this relationship
column4: suggests that some species may actually be flowering later in response to warming winter temperatures (). This supports the suggestion by  that species that appear not to respond to warming average annual temperatures seen in this and other studies may actually be responding to warming
column5: but the opposing effects of winter and spring warming cancel each other out. Sampling error and limited sample sizes may also be a possible explanation for the varied response among species.

column1: We expected the native status of species to influence phenological responses to temporal warming because of the potentially greater phenotypic plasticity of non-native species (; )
column2: but we found no difference between native and non-native species in the relationship between flowering time and rising annual and seasonal temperatures (). Other studies that considered this found similar or mixed results (; ; )
column3: with only one other that found non-native species to flower earlier in response to rising temperatures ().

column1: There are a number of possible explanations why we did not see non-native species flowering earlier in response to warming than natives. First
column2: not all introduced species are invasive
column3: and whether or not a plant is invasive may be a more important determinant of plasticity than native status. This however is difficult to discern as most studies of plasticity in non-native plants focus on those that are invasive. Since only species with specimens that span the time range over which warming has occurred and that the rate of introduction has increased exponentially during the time of this study ()
column4: the proportion of non-native species that are invasive may have been under represented in this and similar studies. In fact
column5: only five of the non-native species used in this study
column6: Alliaria petiolata
column7: Datura stramonium
column8: Hesperis matronalis
column9: Lonicera japonica and Lythrum salicaria
column10: and Lonicera japonica (ital) () are designated as invasive (). If invasive non-natives exhibit greater phenotypic plasticity than those that are not invasive
column11: the underrepresentation of invasive species in our study could explain the lack of response to warming of the non-native species seen here and in other studies. Also
column12: most studies of invasive plants assess plasticity characters such as reproductive output
column13: growth rate
column14: and photosynthesis ()
column15: and plants with greater plasticity may also respond to warming by investing more in early vegetative growth. Finally
column16: adaptive selection on phenological advance also plays a significant role in response to warming ()
column17: potentially equalizing differences in the response of native and non-native plants attributed to plasticity. If we are to better understand how phenological changes may influence biological invasion with climate warming
column18: we will need to advance our understanding of the underlying mechanisms.

column1: We found that the relationship between flowering time and warming was significantly stronger in woody plants than that in herbaceous plants
column2: contradicting our own hypothesis. We are unaware of other studies that directly make this same comparison. There is some evidence that in woody species phenology tends to be cued by photoperiod rather than temperature; but this tendency varies within particular growth forms
column3: and with latitude
column4: flowering season
column5: and other factors (; ; ). Thus
column6: to better predict the consequences of climate warming on flowering phenology
column7: direct comparisons among species with different growth forms and life histories are needed
column8: as is a more comprehensive understanding of the relative effects of temperature and day length on phenology across species. Our finding of no differences in phenological response between species with dry or fleshy fruits () is also in conflict with our own hypotheses and prior work (). This may be due to the low number of fleshy fruited species in our study
column9: or the possibility that fruit type may or may not be related to flowering time or other functional characteristics.

column1: We found that spring-blooming species show a greater advance in flowering phenology than summer-blooming species. Most past studies found the same (; ; ; )
column2: with  finding the opposite. The greater advance in flowering time with warming of spring blooming species can simply be explained by the earlier cue of warming on phenological processes. Also
column3: selection on spring blooming plants to flower earlier to decrease competition for pollinators
column4: particularly for male function
column5: is typically balanced by the risk of late winter damage (; ). As that risk decreases with climate warming
column6: selection to flower earlier should become stronger ().  demonstrated this effect in a much warmer climate where spring blooming species exhibited a stronger phenological response to warmer spring temperatures than species that bloom later in the season; and that significantly warmer summer temperatures actually delayed flowering in summer and autumn flowering species. Additionally
column7: found that the phenological advance in spring flowering was dampened in years with high levels of spring precipitation independent of temperature.

column1: It is clear from our study and others like it that climate change is influencing flowering time in ways that could negatively impact community level ecological processes. However
column2: it also clear that the phenological responses to warming varies greatly among species due to the multiple factors and mechanisms at play. These include differential responses to winter and spring warming
column3: and different functional characteristics among those species. Future work should focus on these possible mechanisms through both historical and experimental studies that take into account the complexities of seasonal differences in warming
column4: and multiple factors that likely contribute to the variation in the response of individual species and how they might interact or be confounded with each other. Consideration of other factors related climate change such as seasonal precipitation
column5: snow melt
column6: the frequency and intensity extreme weather events
column7: and comparisons among sites with greatly different climates will further enhance our understanding of how climate change influences flowering phenology (; ; Peterson
column8: 2016; ; ; ). Additionally
column9: analysis of species specific factors that cue flowering phenology ()
column10: the consideration of the influence of urban heat island effects related to urban and suburban development (; ; )
column11: potential interactions among the plant characteristics that we considered
column12: as well as taking into account any phylogenetic signal in flowering response to warming could help to make sense of observed differences in the phenological response to warming.

column13: Palo Alto

The response variable used in subsequent analyses of treatment effects was soil respiration for the entire sampling period (Day of Year [DOY] 191-273) each year; this value was estimated by linearly interpolating soil respiration measurements between sampling days for each collar and then summing (integrating) all values over the 82-day sampling period.

column1: Total soil respiration is the sum of two belowground components: heterotrophic (microbial and microfaunal respiration) and autotrophic (root respiration). Measurements of soil respiration in the control plots represent the sum of these belowground processes. Thus
column2: to a first approximation
column3: differences in soil respiration between control and either logged or girdled treatments reflect the contribution of eastern hemlock to autotrophic respiration. Decreased soil respiration due to treatment effects were calculated by taking the pre-treatment soil respiration over the sampling season and subtracting from it the post-treatment seasonal soil respiration. These decreases represent a conservative estimate of autotrophic soil respiration in treated plots. Potential limitations to this method include the loss of root biomass
column4: which could reduce heterotrophic respiration of soil organic matter via lack of priming
column5: and that the newly severed roots may temporarily increase carbon available for heterotrophic respiration.

column1: Losses of individual species can have cascading effects on system-wide biological diversity and ecosystem function
column2: but whether specific species have different effects on ecosystem structure function remains an open question that has been addressed much more in theory than in practice (; ; ). It is important to distinguish between effects of loss of dominant (in terms of basal area or biomass) or abundant species and effects of loss of foundation species. For example
column3: American beech is declining rapidly due to beech-bark disease (; )
column4: but because beech resprouts readily
column5: one consequence of beech-bark disease has been to change the size structure of these forests. Large beech trees are now uncommon
column6: but the number of saplings (sprouts) and even the amount of beech’s basal area in a stand may be much greater than before the occurrence and spread of the disease (). Similarly
column7: American elm
column8: once a co-dominant in many eastern North American forests
column9: continued to recruit from small trees
column10: which can reproduce before they are killed by Dutch elm disease (). Understory composition changes rarely in beech stands infested by beech-bark disease ()
column11: and several authors have failed to find expected changes in invertebrate or mammal abundance associated with widespread decline in beech nuts formerly produced by large trees (; ). Effects of beech decline on energy and nutrient cycling varies with co-occurring hardwoods
column12: rates of resprouting
column13: and intensity of infestation ().

column1: In contrast
column2: eastern hemlock has distinctive assemblages of understory plants and animals (; ; ; ; ; ; ; )
column3: and affects carbon cycling and hydrological processes differently from both co-occurring hardwoods and co-occurring conifers (; ; ). Hemlock
column4: unlike hardwoods
column5: does not resprout
column6: and the hemlock woolly adelgid feeds on all ages and size-classes of hemlock. Thus
column7: there is neither opportunity for rapid regeneration through resprouting nor is there an opportunity for hemlock seedlings to reach maturity and fruit before they are killed by the adelgid. Eastern hemlock
column8: therefore
column9: is a better candidate for a foundation tree species than many other forest dominants. Its decline and death have been hypothesized to lead to both short- and long-term changes in ecological dynamics and ecosystem processes (; ).

column1: The Harvard Forest Hemlock Removal Experiment (HF-HeRE) examines this hypothesis by quantifying these changes and testing explicit predictions about how the magnitude and rate of these changes are functions of the mechanism by which a foundation species is lost. In general terms
column2: we predicted that rates of change in biological diversity and ecosystem function would parallel the rate of foundation species loss: slowly when hemlock was girdled (to mimic decline due to infestation by the hemlock woolly adelgid; ) but more rapidly when hemlock was cut and removed (to simulate a commercial logging operation; ). We hypothesize that despite differences in initial rates
column3: changes in vegetation structure and ecosystem function caused by different mechanisms of hemlock loss will converge and come to resemble those seen in the young hardwood stands that represent a plausible scenario of our forests in the future
column4: after hemlock has disappeared from the landscape (). Our results provide strong support for this hypotheses with respect to most measures of vegetation structure
column5: but for fewer measures of ecosystem function.

column1: Decline and loss of T. canadensis in the logged and girdled plots at HF-HeRE led to changes in overstory densities and basal area () that were similar to those seen in sites long infested by the adelgid (; ) or that have been salvage logged (; ). Light availability near ground-level increased gradually over time following girdling but abruptly after logging
column2: followed by a decline with regrowth in the logged treatment (). Average daily soil and air temperatures in logged and girdled plots were 2–4 °C warmer in summer and cooler in winter relative to the hemlock or hardwood controls
column3: and both diurnal and seasonal variances in temperatures were highest in the logged treatment (). Such changes in light and temperature can strongly impact both vegetation community composition (; ) and associated ecosystem properties including decomposition ()
column4: soil respiration ()
column5: and nutrient cycling ().

column1: Removal of T. canadensis by girdling or logging resulted in a 2- to 3-fold increase in species richness after either treatment. Consistent with our first prediction
column2: understory cover in the girdled treatment plots increased slowly () because overstory trees died slowly and the majority of snags were still standing and providing partial shade 4–5 years after the canopy manipulation treatment had been applied. We anticipate that understory vegetation in this treatment will continue to increase in cover and species richness. In contrast
column3: understory vegetation cover in the logged treatment plots increased rapidly and matched total cover in the hardwood control plots by 2009 (). Shade intolerant species including Rubus spp.
column4: Aralia hispida
column5: and Carex spp. initially were absent in all six T. canadensis-dominated plots
column6: but established from both the seed bank and the seed rain in soils scarified by logging () and grew quickly in these scarified areas. Similar increases in total richness and cover have been observed following girdling (; ) or salvage logging (; D Orwig
column7: unpublished data) of T. canadensis elsewhere. However
column8: the heavy recruitment of birch (Betula spp.) into the sapling layer within four years of girdling has not been observed in other girdling studies (; )
column9: perhaps due to lower deer browsing and lack of rhododendron (Rhododendron maximum L.) cover that limit rapid recruitment south of our study areas. These results overall highlight the fact that healthy hemlock act as an ecological filter
column10: limiting seedling and understory plant establishment (; ; ). Now that the adelgid has colonized the hemlock control plots
column11: however
column12: they are also poised for change
column13: and will provide important comparisons with responses observed following girdling.

column1: We caution
column2: however
column3: that it may take much longer than a decade or two for changes in soil dynamics resulting from the loss of hemlock to be manifest (DJ Lodge
column4: personal communication
column5: 24 September 2004). One of the dominant drivers of soil dynamics – decomposition of large fallen boles and other coarse woody debris – is very different in hemlock (and other conifer-dominated) stands than in hardwood dominated stands. In the former
column6: brown-rot fungi dominate
column7: and they primarily decompose cellulose. In the latter
column8: white-rot fungi dominate
column9: and they primarily decompose lignin; in general
column10: white-rot fungi are much more efficient (and rapid) decomposers (; ). We predict that soil nutrient availability will decline significantly only once dead hemlock boles and smaller coarse hemlock woody debris have decomposed and brown-rot fungi disappear
column11: but this may take one or two centuries.

column1: Loss of the foundation tree species
column2: T. canadensis
column3: by either girdling or logging
column4: leads to short- and long-term changes in vegetation structure and ecosystem function. Rapid removal by logging leads to abrupt
column5: rapid changes
column6: whereas girdling (and by inference
column7: the adelgid itself) causes slower but no less important responses of similar magnitude several years later. Vegetation richness
column8: cover
column9: and density increase continuously following hemlock removal and exert strong
column10: potentially stabilizing
column11: biotic control on the fluxes of nutrients. Thus
column12: these ecosystem processes exhibited short-term fluctuations following T. canadensis removal but recovered to near pre-treatment levels within four years
column13: highlighting the resilience – at least in the short-term – of some forest ecosystem processes to disturbances (; ). Results from HF-HeRE
column14: together with results from observations and experiments on other foundation species suggest that their continued losses
column15: together with human responses to ongoing environmental changes
column16: may have profound impacts on the structure and function of forested ecosystems for decades to come.

column1: 35|10.7717/peerj.6087|The recent expansion of Fox Sparrow ( Passerella iliaca iliaca ) breeding range into the northeastern United States| Lloyd  John D. |2018-12-10|"Fox Sparrow (Passerella iliaca) is a polytypic species that breeds in montane and boreal forest across western and northern North America. Eastern Fox Sparrow
column2: (P. i. iliaca)
column3: part of the Red Fox Sparrow subspecies group (along with P. i. zaboria)
column4: nests in early-successional coniferous or mixed forests; shrubby thickets along waterways and wetlands; and stunted conifer forests on mountaintops or cool coastlines from Manitoba eastward to the Maritime Provinces of Canada (; ; ; ). Most general references identify the breeding range of Eastern Fox Sparrow as extending into the eastern United States only in the northernmost part of Maine (; ; ). However
column5: anecdotal reports from birders and observations submitted to eBird (http://www.ebird.org)
column6: a web platform that documents bird distributions using observations submitted by citizen-scientists ()
column7: suggest that Fox Sparrow now occurs regularly during the breeding season throughout northern and western Maine and as far south and west as the mountains of central and northern New Hampshire. This would constitute a fairly rapid southward expansion of the species’ breeding range
column8: on the order of several hundred kilometers
column9: yet this phenomenon has remained undescribed in the ornithological literature. Here
column10: in an effort to address this gap and to clarify the status of Fox Sparrow as a breeding species in the northeastern US
column11: I review historical and current literature describing the distribution of Fox Sparrow in this region and describe temporal changes in occurrence using data submitted to eBird by citizen scientists.

These accounts formed the basis for understanding the historic distribution of Fox Sparrow in Maine and New Hampshire.

column1: Analyzing the regional occurrence of Fox Sparrows in eBird checklists posed two problems. First
column2: because of the species’ rarity and its localized distribution in the region
column3: many checklists were submitted from the same locale on approximately the same date and presumably recorded the presence of the same individual or individuals. For example
column4: when an individual was located on an accessible mountaintop
column5: many birders would actively seek out that individual and submit a checklist recording its presence. As these are not independent observations
column6: this phenomenon would potentially lead to an overestimate of the frequency with which the species is observed in the region. At the same time
column7: the localized regional distribution of Fox Sparrows resulted in an exceedingly small proportion of checklists reporting the species.

column1: To address both issues
column2: rather than use the raw proportion of checklists with Fox Sparrow
column3: I instead used as a dependent variable the proportion of 10 km2 grid cells within the region that had at least one complete checklist containing Fox Sparrow. To accomplish this
column4: I first overlaid a 10 km2 grid atop the study area within the QGIS version 2.18 Geographic Information System () and then determined whether each grid cell contained at least one complete checklist with a record of Fox Sparrow for each year. Doing so avoided the problem of treating multiple observations of the same bird as independent and also yielded clearer insight into large-scale changes in the occurrence of Fox Sparrow. The choice of the grid size was essentially subjective
column5: but reflected a trade-off between cells that were too large to accurately quantify changes in distribution over time and cells that were too small to accurately record the location where Fox Sparrows were detected during traveling counts submitted to eBird (i.e.
column6: situations where an observer recorded birds in multiple grid cells on a single checklist; the median length of a traveling checklist used in this analysis was 3.3 km and the mean length was 6.2 km). To test the sensitivity of the results to the choice of a size for the grid cells
column7: I repeated the analysis described below using a 5 km2 grid and found that the results were nearly identical (supplemental results of this analysis are available at https://doi.org/10.6084/m9.figshare.7300031.v1).

column1: I analyzed changes in the proportion of grid cells occupied by Fox Sparrow over time using a generalized linear model
column2: with the conditional distribution of the response variable assumed to be binomial (i.e.
column3: a grid cell did or did not contain a record of Fox Sparrow in that year). I included year as the predictor variable in the model. Any change in the proportion of cells with Fox Sparrow records could be due in part to increased observer effort over time
column4: so I analyzed a second model that included both year and a measure of observer effort
column5: which I quantified as the summed value of observation hours spent on each complete checklist in the grid cell. I used analysis of deviance to choose the preferred model for inference. I tested for overdispersion via a chi-squared test of the ratio of the squared sum of Pearson residuals to the residual degrees of freedom (). None of the models showed evidence of overdispersion (i.e.
column7: and as such I made no adjustments.

column1: Although the measure of observer hours spent birding can help address the confounding effect of effort on the apparent frequency with which a rare species like Fox Sparrow is detected
column2: it does not address changes in observer behavior that may increase the likelihood of encountering the species. For example
column3: over time
column4: birders may have become more adventurous in their explorations and more likely to visit the locales—remote mountaintops or commercial forestlands with limited access—potentially inhabited by Fox Sparrows. To address this potential source of confounding
column5: I repeated the above analysis for two other species that occupy similar habitat as Fox Sparrows: Bicknell’s Thrush (Catharus bicknelli) and Blackpoll Warbler (Setophaga striata). Bicknell’s Thrush populations have likely been declining slowly in the region (; ) in recent decades
column6: whereas Blackpoll Warblers have shown no significant trend in numbers ()
column7: so any increase in the proportion of grid cells occupied by these species in the study area might reflect increased observer activity in habitat suitable for Fox Sparrows.

column1: Eastern Fox Sparrows appear to be in the midst of a significant southward expansion of their breeding range. I found no evidence that the species was present in the northeastern US during the breeding season until the late 1970s or early 1980s
column2: yet they are now widely reported in both krummholz forest and in sites at lower elevations—presumably dense stands of young conifer—across New Hampshire and Maine during June and July. Observers have confirmed nesting in both states. This amounts to an approximately 400 km breeding range extension during the span of approximately 30 years.

column1: Given that Fox Sparrows nest in remote or difficult-to-access locales—old clearcuts and mountaintops—it is difficult to rule out the possibility that they may have been present in small numbers in the northeastern US in the past. However
column2: the avifauna of the region’s mountains has been well-described since the early 1900s
column3: yet no breeding season records for the species exist in the historical literature. Also
column4: during the last decade
column5: the increased frequency of breeding season eBird reports of Fox Sparrow in Maine and New Hampshire appears to reflect a real increase in the species’ abundance and extent of occurrence
column6: not an increase in observer effort in the right habitat; Bicknell’s Thrush and Blackpoll Warbler
column7: which co-occur with Fox Sparrow during the breeding season
column8: have shown no such increase.

column1: During the mid-1980s
column2: birders in southern Quebec began noting what were then considered extralimital records of Fox Sparrow
column3: at about the same time that observers in far northwestern Maine first discovered evidence that Fox Sparrows were nesting in the state. The breeding range for Fox Sparrows continued to expand south and west out of southern Quebec and northern Maine over the next decade
column4: reaching northern New Hampshire by the mid-1990s. Although Fox Sparrows have not spread substantially further west since then—only a handful of breeding season records exist for Vermont
column5: and only one for New York—they have continued moving south and now occur regularly during summer throughout the White Mountains of New Hampshire.

column1: Fox Sparrows found in the northeastern US during the breeding season tend to occur either in krummholz forest at high elevations or in young stands of spruce and fir regenerating after harvest
column2: which is consistent with descriptions of nesting habitat occupied in the core of the species’ range in Canada (). Other locales with potentially suitable nesting habitat include the Green Mountains of Vermont and the Adirondack Mountains of New York
column3: both of which contain areas of krummholz forest. Extensive stands of young spruce-fir forest at lower elevations are uncommon outside of the commercial forestlands of western and northern Maine
column4: although parts of far northeastern Vermont and northern New Hampshire support lowland spruce-fir forests that might provide suitable conditions for nesting Fox Sparrows following harvest.

column1: Why the distribution of Fox Sparrows has expanded so rapidly is not clear. Equally puzzling is that the direction of the expansion is unexpected given contemporary climate change; in general
column2: species have responded to rising temperatures by shifting to higher latitudes or higher elevations (; ). The southern range limit of 44 species of breeding birds in New York
column3: for example
column4: shifted northward by nearly 6 km per decade between 1980 and 2005 (). Indeed
column5: models that attempt to predict the future distribution of habitat for boreal birds in general
column6: and Fox Sparrows in particular
column7: suggest a northward retraction of the southern range limit in response to anthropogenic climate change. For example
column8: and  both predicted that by the end of the century the southern limit of the distribution of Fox Sparrow would extend no further south than central Quebec and southern Labrador. Despite the general trend for species to shift their distributions poleward and upslope in response to climate change
column9: a substantial minority of species examined have shown the opposite pattern (; ). Explanations for range shifts downslope or towards the equator are varied
column10: ranging from sampling error to indirect effects of climate change on biotic interactions such as competition or facilitation ()
column11: but  argued that aspects of climate other than temperature might be a key driver of unexpected range shifts. In particular
column12: their analysis of century-long changes in the elevational limits of montane birds in the western US suggested that whereas rising temperatures favored upslope movement of some species
column13: increasing precipitation favored downslope movements among others. Although precipitation has not been demonstrated to produce similar effects on latitudinal distribution
column14: precipitation across most of the northeastern US has increased by >15% over the last century ()
column15: which presumably could have mitigated any precipitation-related limit to the southern edge of Fox Sparrow breeding range.

column1: Alternatively
column2: the southward extension of the breeding range of Fox Sparrow may be unrelated to climate change.  demonstrated that the northeastern quarter of the US
column3: from the Great Lakes region through New England
column4: had climatic conditions putatively suitable for Fox Sparrow even though nearly all of the region was unoccupied by the species. This would suggest that either the global population was limited by factors other than habitat availability or that necessary features of the environment were absent from areas that were climatically suitable. To the latter point
column5: Breeding Bird Survey data reveal no evidence of population increases in eastern Canada ()
column6: as might be expected if growth in the core of the range was facilitating the southward expansion of the breeding range.

column1: However
column2: the expansion of breeding Fox Sparrows into Maine coincides approximately with the extensive salvage logging that occurred during and after the last outbreak of eastern spruce budworm
column3: which created vast areas of young spruce-fir forest in northern and western parts of the state. The epidemic began in the 1960s and continued through the 1980s
column4: and was accompanied by extensive salvage harvests from the 1970s into the 1990s (). As regeneration proceeded
column5: the area of young spruce-fir forest expanded significantly
column6: from < 4
column7: 000 km2 in 1982 to nearly 10
column9: with nearly all of the increase occurring between 1982 and 1995 (). Furthermore
column10: the increase in the area of young spruce-fir forest was greatest in the northern counties of Aroostock
column11: Somerset
column12: and Piscataquis
column13: where breeding Fox Sparrows were first detected (). Given that Fox Sparrows will nest in young conifer stands ()
column14: the species’ southward expansion may have been in part due to the increased availability of nesting habitat created by logging; under this scenario
column15: climatic conditions for breeding Fox Sparrows may have always been suitable in the northeastern US
column16: but the lack of dense
column17: short-statured stands of conifers limited their ability to successfully nest in the region.  suggested a similar role for logging in the spread of Fox Sparrows onto the western slopes of the Cascade Mountains of Oregon
column18: where a combination of clearcutting and burning created extensive new areas of shrubby
column19: early-seral vegetation in an area that was previously dominated by mature coniferous forests. Increased availability of stands of young conifer may also explain the higher incidence of observations of Fox Sparrow at low elevations in Maine. Maine has substantially greater acreages of young balsam fir (; ) and for practical and regulatory reasons nearly all of the timber harvest that creates young fir forest in Maine occurs at elevations <800 m
column20: which corresponds to the peak in observations of Fox Sparrow evident between 250 and 750 m. However
column21: the role of early-successional forests in promoting the range expansion documented here is speculative given the lack of data on habitat conditions at any of the occurrence locations at lower elevations. Targeted surveys across a range of forest types would likely prove useful in clarifying the connection between young forest and breeding Fox Sparrows in the northeastern US.

column1: Although this study did not address changes in the northern limit of the breeding range of Fox Sparrow
column2: some climate models predict a northward expansion of Fox Sparrows into the arctic as shrub cover increases in arctic tundra () even as the southern range limit contracts. Empirical observations appear to confirm this prediction;  reported
column3: based on surveys conducted from 2008–2016

column1: Eastern Fox Sparrows were historically a passage migrant through the northeastern US during late spring and early fall
column2: but within the past few decades have become widespread and regular during the breeding season. Although confirmed nesting records remain scarce
column3: the consistent presence of singing males suggests that the southern limit of the breeding range now extends several hundred kilometers south of where it is placed on most published range maps. In particular
column4: the species’ breeding range now encompasses the northwestern half of Maine and the highlands of northern New Hampshire from the White Mountains to the US-Canada border. Scattered recent records in Vermont and New York
column5: along with the presence of suitable habitat in the higher mountains of both states
column6: suggests that Fox Sparrow may continue expanding its range to the west.

column1: The aim of this study is to determine existing differences between the three disjunct areas of occurrence for X. gilli (). Specifically we hypothesised that sea-level rise (or fall) brought about isolation which contributed to divergence in Xenopus gilli. We examined these hypotheses using both morphometric data and molecular markers of X. gilli from multiple sites within each of the three disjunct areas. We dated the divergence time across the geographic gaps using a coalescent approach with sequences from two mitochondrial genes. Lastly
column2: we compared size-corrected morphometric data from each genetically defined population.

column1: Xenopus gilli
column2: the Cape platanna
column3: inhabits lowland acid blackwater pools characteristic of the fynbos biome (). Anthropogenic disturbances
column4: intense urbanisation and the spread of alien vegetation has resulted in declines of populations of X. gilli
column5: so that this species is now considered Endangered (). Xenopus gilli was initially known from only two localities (the Cape Peninsula and adjoining Cape Flats) but in the 1970s a population was discovered approximately 50 km to the east
column6: on the opposite side of False Bay (see ). Lastly
column7: in 1987 a third disjunct cluster of sites was found near the town of Pearly Beach (

column12: X. gilli is threatened through introgression from its sister species
column13: Xenopus laevis (Daudin) (see ) which is known for its invasive abilities on four continents (). In South Africa
column14: X. laevis is considered to be a domestic exotic
column15: readily invading disturbed waters

column1: Xenopus gilli was first described from the Silvermine River () and is usually characterised by a venter marked with bright yellow-orange and black mottling; although this is more distinct in some animals than others (). Dorsally

column3: down the length of the body and breaking up into patches on the lower back and legs. They exhibit sexual size dimorphism with the larger females attaining a snout-vent length of approximately 60 mm and having distinct cloacal folds (; ).

column1: Total genomic DNA was extracted from the tissue samples using a standard salt extraction procedure () and fragments of two mitochondrial (mt) markers (ND2 and 16S) were amplified. However

column4: ). Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) annealing temperature was optimised at 57.1°C for 40 cycles and magnesium concentration was 3 mM MgCl2. The 16S marker was amplified from primers 16Sa to 16Sb () of which 517bp were used for analysis (mtDNA BP 4052 to 4559
column5: ). PCR annealing temperature was optimised at 48.1°C for 40 cycles and magnesium concentration was 2.5 mM MgCl2. Electrophoresis was used to examine the quality of PCR products on a 2% agarose gel stained with Goldview and the high quality products were sent to Macrogen Inc. (Seoul
column6: Korea) for sequencing.

column1: Geneious Pro 5.0.4 was used to edit and align the DNA sequences from each sample using a global alignment () and the alignment was used to define haplotypes at each site. One animal from Cape Point was removed from all further analyses as it fell into an eastern haplotype population. Similarly

column1: In order to determine whether sampling sites clustered on a population level
column2: a spatial analysis of variance (SAMOVA) was conducted ()
column3: using ND2 haplotype data and geographic co-ordinates of each of the 9 sample sites. The SAMOVA was run for K = 2–9 putative populations to determine the maximum FCT value
column4: the highest proportion of differences between populations due to genetic variation (). The results of the SAMOVA allowed for the consolidation of the DNA data into a Cape peninsula population and a combined Kleinmond and Pearly Beach population for further genetic and morphometric analyses (henceforth combined and referred to as the “eastern population”). Network 4.5.1.6 () was used to consolidate the haplotype data into a Median-joining network. Arlequin 3.5 () was used to provide a quantitative measure of haplotype and nucleotide diversity within localities. Sequence divergence between populations was estimated with uncorrected net p-distances using MEGA v. 5 ().

column1: To examine whether any population had experienced historical demographic changes
column2: Tajima’s D and Fu’s FS was used to test for departures from mutation–drift equilibrium (; ) using Arlequin 3.5 (). For populations out of equilibrium
column3: we then applied a model of demographic expansion (; ; ). For populations that showed signs of expansion
column4: we estimated the timing of the demographic shift; t = τ/(2u)
column5: where t is time in generations
column6: τ is the age of the expansion in mutational units (estimated in the model of demographic expansion) and u is the sum of the per nucleotide mutation rate for the region sequenced (; ). For the present study
column7: generation time was estimated at 2 years () and u was obtained from the same estimate of mutation rate as described for coalescence analysis (below).

column1: Coalescent analysis was used to investigate time of divergence (t) between all the populations identified in SAMOVA (Cape Peninsula vs. eastern)
column2: using IMa2 (). IMa2 runs Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) simulations using a likelihood based analysis
column3: under a Felsenstein framework (). MCMC included a burn-in duration of 3 million steps
column4: 10 000 genealogies saved and a geometric heating model with 20 chains
column5: 0.96 as first and 0.9 as second chain heating parameters. A geometric heating scheme was applied to ensure sufficient rates of chain swapping to speed up the calculation process. The mutation rates for the ND2 and 16S regions of the mitochondrion were determined using the estimated time of divergence reported for the dated time of divergence between the two X. gilli lineages (8.5 Mya) in . Mutation rate was estimated to be 0.52% change per lineage per million years
column6: and compares favourably with other mutation rates published for ND2 (e.g.
column8: we used the same calculation to give us a rate of 0.10% change per lineage per million years
column9: which is in the same general range as other published rates for this marker (e.g.
column10: 0.15% ). The software IMa2 uses the geometric mean of all mutation rates with upper and lower confidence intervals which were calculated from the upper and lower divergence bounds for X. gilli: 4.8 and 13.4 Mya (). Preliminary runs in IMa2 were used to establish settings for priors
column11: ensure adequate mixing and sufficiently high effective sample size (see )
column12: this included setting migration to 0 between CoGH and other populations due to an absence of shared haplotypes between populations. Three final duplicate runs were made with the same parameters using IMa2 at Cornell University via internet upload (http://cbsuapps.tc.cornell.edu/IMa.aspx).

column1: In the absence of sexual dimorphism for the variables measured (see Results)
column2: data from both sexes were pooled for all further analyses. To examine population level differences in morphology
column3: a multivariate approach was taken. Size corrected residuals from the Cape peninsula (n = 50) and from the eastern population (n = 59) were used as input variables for a principal components analysis (PCA) to reduce the 13 morphometric variables into linear combinations of correlated variables (principal components-PCs). The Keiser-Meyer-Olkin (KMO) measure of sampling adequacy was applied to determine whether the dataset was adequate to conduct a PCA. The analysis included a varimax rotation to minimise the number of variables with high loadings on each PC

column5: and ANOVA was carried out on the PCs extracted using R ().

There is considerable genetic and morphological variation between the two identified populations of Xenopus gilli distributed on either side of False Bay. However
column2: for the two disjunct clusters of sites in the East (Kleinmond and Pearly Beach)
column3: our analyses did not suggest that they represent separate populations. Despite the disturbance to population distributions caused by sea-level change
column4: our results from coalescent analysis of the deep genetic division between the two populations
column5: dates back to 4.63 Mya (3.17 to 6.38 Mya). This is considerably younger than the estimate provided by  (

column1: The original description of X. gilli by  was presumably from the same population that now resides on the Cape peninsula (see ). These authors referred to head shape and size as defining characters in separating X. gilli from X. laevis. Relative to their size
column2: the Cape peninsula animals had significantly broader and higher heads than those from the eastern population. In addition
column3: animals from the eastern population had relatively shorter legs. These characters may relate to differences in performance between animals from the different habitat types (see ). On the Cape peninsula
column4: natural water bodies are seasonally inundated wetlands
column5: while eastern sites have typically larger
column6: more permanent water bodies. We acknowledge that although our findings show no obvious sexual dimorphism
column7: examination of a larger dataset should be conducted to confirm this result.

column1: In addition to the quantitative morphometric study
column2: a retrospective qualitative examination of the markings of each population revealed some consistent differences. There were no apparent differences between the animals with regards to their dorsal markings

column4: down the length of the body. However
column5: the ventral surfaces were different with respect to positioning of the lateral line ‘stitch’ marks; these were more lateral in animals caught on the Cape peninsula
column6: while those collected in eastern sites were more ventral. For eastern animals
column7: the intensity of the yellow-brown and black marbling was much less apparent although some animals were more brightly coloured than others. Animals from the east also had transparent webbing between the toes on their hind feet

column11: especially with regards to the lateral line organs should be quantified in future research.

column1: Inundation of the Cape Flats was previously speculated to have caused the deep genetic division in the Cape Xenopus lineage ()
column2: but repeated inundations both pre- and post-date our estimated divergence time ()
column3: and so it seems unlikely that sea-level changes can be the sole factor responsible for the divergence. Sea-level change may have produced two different scenarios for these two Xenopus lineages: sea-level rises which contracted populations and sea-level falls which may have allowed a range extension and migration (cf ). Furthermore
column4: we suggest that the effects of climate change from 5 Mya are of key importance
column5: when substantial uplifting led to significant reduction of rainfall in this area ()
column6: reduction in temperature () and a massive expansion in regional floristic diversity ().

column1: The Agulhas Bank was last exposed as recently as the last glacial maximum
column2: some 20 Kya with a sea-level fall of around 140 m (). The change in climate and land area since then is predicted to have had a major reduction on the distributions of Cape frogs (). At the last glacial maximum
column3: much of the exposed land would have been sand fynbos vegetated dunes similar to that seen
column4: and not occupied by X. gilli
column5: on the Cape Flats today (see ). East of False Bay
column6: the extension of the rivers across the exposed southern coastal plain would have in part coincided with bedrock supporting both Renosterveld and lowland sand-stone fynbos
column7: vegetation types on which X. gilli are found today (). On the Cape peninsula
column8: it seems that only a small amount of land to the West and South would have been exposed
column9: and this would also have been the currently uninhabited sand fynbos. Due East of the Cape peninsula (5–10 km) lies a large (approximately 50 km2) outcrop of sandstone which may have been able to support a population of X. gilli. It is not known whether X. gilli would have been capable of movement across the intermediary habitat
column10: and there is a similar distance from this outcrop to the habitat extensions in the east. If dispersal was possible over these distances
column11: there is the potential for movement of individuals across False Bay at each glacial maximum. Similarly
column12: speculated about the dispersal of Proteacae across the same bridging habitat.

column3: as did  ( see below). If single animals are capable of dispersal on this scale
column4: our interpretation that this resulted from the release of captive animals would be erroneous. We suggest that in the east
column5: as continuous appropriate habitat would have been uncovered by the retreat of the sea during the last glacial maximum ()
column6: that this Xenopus lineage would have had a larger distribution. This may explain the star-shaped haplotype network ()
column7: and the significant results of neutrality tests
column8: which suggest that this lineage has undergone recent expansion. Using mutation rates already described for ND2
column9: we date this expansion to around 150 Kya
column10: a period when the Agulhas bank was exposed with sea-level some 140 m lower than today ().

column1: Our approximate dates for divergence between the populations coincide with a period when sea-level is hypothesised to have been around 25 m higher (also see ). However
column2: unless this was the first marine incursion in the area
column3: there is no reason to think that the substrate immediately prior (or after) to this time was any more suitable than it is today. Certainly the sea also covered the Cape Flats 1.5 Mya ()
column4: and was even 20 m higher than current levels around 130 Kya (). Thus
column5: if sea-level has repeatedly covered the Cape Flats and uncovered the Agulhas Bank throughout the Pleistocene period
column6: we look to other explanations for the continued separation of these lineages.

column1: We consider that composition of the underlying substrate may be more likely to have had a lasting effect on the distribution of these acidophilic species. Distribution records for X. gilli suggest that this species is not tolerant of alkaline substrates
column2: such as those currently in place over much of the Cape Flats area today (see
column3: ). The alkalinity of this area is thought to be in part due to repeated marine incursions
column4: but with additional calcareous wind-blown sands resulting from the erosion of uplifted material (; ). This suggests that areas that had been suitable for the ancestor of these populations were continually reduced over the last 5 My.

column1: Climatic change also followed this period of substantial uplifting on the eastern side of the subcontinent and is thought to have led to significant reductions of rainfall in the west and aridification of the interior of South Africa (; ). The start of these substantial changes
column2: around 5 Mya
column3: coincides with our estimated split within Xenopus gilli and the rapid diversification and establishment of the fynbos biome ca. 3–5 Mya (; )
column4: on whose acid-blackwaters X. gilli now depends (). This same period is known to have resulted in radiations of other faunal groups in this specific area of the south-western Cape (e.g.
column5: ; ; ; ). Thus
column6: the tectonic uplift began a series of processes (spread of alkaline substrate and drying) which reduced the range of the most recent common ancestor of these distinct lineages
column7: eventually leading to their divergence around 4.63 Mya. Further
column8: we postulate that these processes
column9: which have been ongoing
column10: continue to reduce the ranges of these lineages (and other species such as Microbatrachella capensis)
column11: and this might explain why there was not a more recent genetic expansion during the last glacial maximum. Most recently however
column12: anthropogenic factors have further reduced the ranges of these X. gilli populations
column13: raising concern for the conservation of these species.

column1: On the Cape peninsula
column2: there has been a reduction in the known range of Xenopus gilli over the past century. The type series was said to be collected from the Silvermine River and adjacent Cape Flats ()
column3: and in the same publication
column4: it was noted that frogs collected from these localities were already outnumbered by X. laevis (see also ). The last record of this species outside of the CoGH is a specimen collected at Zeekovlei in the mid-1970s (in the Iziko Museum
column5: Cape Town: ). This dramatic and rapid reduction in range may in part explain the low haplotype diversity observed in population sampled from the Cape peninsula (see also ). The disparity between haplotype (h) and nucleotide (π) diversity between the two lineages is indicative of a bottleneck in the Cape peninsula
column6: with a reduction in the number but not in the diversity of the remaining haplotypes. The distribution of pairwise differences between haplotypes () is also suggestive that intermediary haplotypes have been lost. The scenario is similar to that described in other historically documented cases of population bottlenecks (; ).

column1: Natural hybridisation between Xenopus laevis and X. gilli was reported as far back as the 1970s (; ; ; ; )
column2: although continuing investigations showed that hybrid males were not fertile
column3: and hybrid females had a reduced fecundity (). The first genetic investigations into the frequency of hybrids found only a single (nuclear) hybrid in 32 X. gilli (and 38 X. laevis) sampled in the eastern area
column4: while none were found from 59 sampled from the CoGH reserve (). Thus
column5: these authors concluded that introgression was not the greatest obstacle to X. gilli conservation. Our study
column6: sequencing mtDNA only
column7: avoided sampling any obvious X. laevis or putative X. laevis-gilli hybrids
column8: but still found substantial numbers of animals which were morphologically identified as X. gilli (see methods for characters) but result from a X. laevis maternal lineage. From relatively low levels (2.5%) on the Cape peninsula
column9: to 27% in ponds near Kleinmond where X. laevis and hybrids have come to dominate some historically known X. gilli sites. Ponds near Pearly Beach
column10: which were previously thought to contain only pure X. gilli (see ; )
column11: were found to have 8% hybrids
column12: even though no X. laevis-like animals were seen. Our results suggest that hybridisation has greatly increased in both lineages and this aspect requires better investigation using nuclear markers and larger sample sizes.

column1: Although we decided to remove the single individual found in CoGH with the eastern haplotype from our analysis
column2: there remains the question surrounding the provenance of this individual with a haplotype also found by . There are two possibilities: first that this individual was anthropogenically introduced into CoGH
column3: or second that it represents ancient migration from the eastern population to the west. We reason that if it was possible for animals to migrate east to west
column4: then the return route should also have been possible
column5: and we might therefore expect to see instances of peninsula haplotypes in eastern populations. Given that the last time that the Cape Flats were covered was some 1.5 Mya ()
column6: migrants from that time should be discernible from modern movements through further genetic testing such as micro-satellites. It is important to conduct such a test as otherwise these animals present another introgression threat to the remaining population on the peninsula.

column1: Lastly
column2: we suggest that the eastern lineage of X. gilli requires taxonomic attention through data on calls
column3: patterning and lateral line configuration. Despite recent conservation work at CoGH removing hundreds of Xenopus laevis
column4: hybridisation poses an ongoing threat (see ). The eastern population occurs over a much wider area
column5: but all of this is threatened by invasive alien plants (as well as hybridising X. laevis). Despite good and clear documentation on the threats to this species
column6: all appear to continue unabated.

column1: 37|10.7717/peerj.16585|Genetic variability and population structure of the Montezuma quail ( Cyrtonyx montezumae ) in the northern limit of its distribution| Sánchez-Murrieta  Eduardo |2023-12-8|"Geographical patterns of genetic variation are influenced by a species ability to disperse () and low dispersal ability may reduce local genetic variation in isolated populations (). If dispersal is limited
column2: then the concomitant reduced genetic diversity can limit local adaptability ()
column3: and may result in a detriment of the long-term survival of the metapopulation (). Similarly
column4: genetic variation loss reduces population viability by decreasing average individual fitness (). Population size reductions in harvested wildlife species have disrupted their geographical distribution
column5: increased their isolation
column6: and reduced gene flow (). Under this metapopulation dynamic scenario
column7: persistent harvest may further exacerbate the loss of genetic variability (). The Montezuma quail (Cyrtonyx montezumae Vigors 1830) is a small game bird with limited dispersal by flight
column8: that has a naturally disjointed geographic distribution. Montezuma quail inhabit temperate woodlands and savannas associated with mountains ranges in southeastern Arizona
column9: southern New Mexico
column10: and west Texas in the United States (). The Montezuma quail’s geographic distribution extends southwards into Mexico along the Sierra Madre Occidental and Oriental to the Trans-Mexican Volcanic Belt and the Sierra Madre del Sur. Montezuma quail often occupy habitat patches in isolated mountain ranges that are widely separated by unsuitable arid lands (). Dense grass is a necessary habitat component for cover ()
column11: nesting ()
column12: and forage (). Montezuma quail are sedentary
column13: with home range sizes averaging 50 ha (). The Montezuma quail nesting season is from June to October and is largely influenced by summer precipitation. Females produce a single clutch of around 10 eggs (). Montezuma quail live in coveys during the non-breeding season
column14: but form pairs for nesting by as early as late February. The species feeds primarily on underground plant structures
column15: such as bulbs and tubers of woodsorrel (Oxalis spp.)
column16: and sedge (Cyperus spp.)
column17: but also feed on acorns (Quercus spp.)
column18: and a large variety of seeds and insects ().

column1: The Montezuma quail has a patchy distribution with numerous disjunct populations in Arizona
column2: New Mexico
column3: and Texas (). This habitat fragmentation may have an additive effect with genetic drift on the loss of genetic variation of isolated populations. For instance
column4: naturally fragmented habitat has led to reduced levels of genetic variation in the Mexican spotted owl (Strix occidentalis lucida) in southeastern Arizona. Differences in management by state agencies may have also affected quail genetic structure. Montezuma quail are hunted in Arizona and New Mexico (; )
column5: but not in Texas
column6: where populations are restricted to the Trans-Pecos region and the Edwards Plateau (). Furthermore
column7: geographic genetic variation of Texas populations might have been disrupted in the mid 1970s when a series of reintroductions occurred in west and central Texas using quail from Arizona (; ). The success of these reintroductions
column8: and their contribution to the gene pool in those populations
column9: has not been confirmed ().

column1: A population genetic survey is a convenient way to evaluate the effect of isolation
column2: fragmentation

column5: New Mexico and Texas using DNA microsatellite markers.

column1: Tissue samples were extracted from specimens hunted in Arizona and New Mexico under numerous hunting licenses issued by Arizona Game and Fish Department and New Mexico Department of Game and Fish. Tissue samples from Texas originated from specimens collected under Scientific Permit Number SPR-0410-139 issued by Texas Parks and Wildlife Department. We initially allocated 119 individual samples to four populations: Arizona (AZ)
column2: Central New Mexico (CNM

column4: Western New Mexico (WNM

column7: we re-allocated 14 samples from the two easternmost collection locations of the WNM population to the CNM population. Ultimately
column8: we allocated the 119 samples as follows: 32 to AZ
column9: 36 to WNM
column10: 26 to CNM and 25 to WTX (). We used 25 mg of muscle tissue from the right wing to extract genomic DNA using a Qiagen® DNeasy Blood and Tissue extraction kit.

column1: Twenty DNA microsatellite loci developed by  for Colinus virginianus were evaluated. The choice of these was made based on heterozygosity and the number of alleles reported by the authors. For microsatellite amplification

column6: which varied between 45 °C and 57 °C. The program used was as follows: a cycle of 95 °C for 2 min; five cycles of 94 °C for 30 s
column7: 45 °C for 30 s and 72 °C for 30 s; then 35 cycles of 94 °C for 45 s

A post-PCR multiplex array for fragment reading was performed. This was performed on an ABI 3730xl sequencer (Applied Biosystems
column9: Waltham
column10: MA

We did not run fragment analyses on individual samples more than once and we could not estimate error rates associated with allele scoring. Loss of alleles
column12: null alleles and excess homozygotes were assessed using program Micro-Checker v 2.2.3 (). Genetic variability estimator’s locus alleles (A) expected heterozygosity (He) and observed heterozygosity (Ho) were obtained for each locus and population using GenAlEx v 6.5 (). Using the Arlequin v 3.5 software ()
column13: possible deviations from the Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium (HWE)
column14: were calculated for each locus in each population. Linkage disequilibrium for each pair of loci across populations was tested using the Fisher’s method as implemented by Genepop on the Web (; ).

column1: Population genetic differentiation index RST was calculated using Arlequin v 3.5 () to determine the degree of genetic differentiation among populations. Population structure analysis was conducted using software STRUCTURE ()
column2: which estimates the posterior probability of the data given existence of K clusters or groups under Hardy–Weinberg equilibrium and estimates the individuals’ posterior membership probability to each of the K clusters. Individuals are then assigned to the cluster that holds the highest probability. Parameters established for the analysis were 10
column3: 000 burnins
column5: 000 repetitions of Monte Carlo Markov Chains
column6: 25 iterations
column7: a K value (number of clusters) between 1–4
column8: an ancestral mixing model
column9: and a correlated allelic frequency model. Outputs from program STRUCTURE were analyzed using the method of  as implemented by software STRUCTURE HARVESTER () to determine the most probable number of genetic clusters. This method estimates Delta K (ΔK)
column10: which is the difference between the values of the logarithmic likelihood in each analysis iteration for the four clusters.

column1: A Mantel test was conducted using program Genepop 4.3 () to determine if there was a distance isolation pattern among Montezuma quail populations. Statistic RST was used as a measure of genetic distance between populations. We performed 10
column2: 000 permutations to estimate the statistical significance (P < 0.05) of the null hypothesis of independence between genetic and geographic distance.
