column1: Previous genetic work () found that Montezuma quail from Arizona and Texas were not genetically distinct from one another based on mitochondrial DNA sequences. In this study
column2: slight population structure was shown through the low RST. Nonetheless
column3: pairwise RST values indicate a low to moderate differentiation in some study populations. For instance
column4: AZ is different from CNM (RST = 0.061; P = 0.011) and WTX (RST = 0.094; P = 0.001). In addition
column5: AZ is not differentiated from WNM
column6: suggesting an extant or recent corridor for Montezuma quail between these two study populations. Montezuma quail occur through the forested Mogollon Rim
column7: which may connect WNM to central Arizona and to mountain ranges to southern Arizona (AZ) as both eBird sightings () and GBIF () records suggest (). The Montezuma quail’s ability to disperse this distance may not be as limited as presumed. A radiotelemetry study conducted in Texas found that Montezuma quail move up to 15 km (). Furthermore
column8: the lowest genetic differentiation occurred between neighboring WNM and CNM (RST = 0.014; P = 0.063). Although some CNM individuals appear to be geographically isolated from the rest of the population (for example
column9: individuals collected in the Lincoln National Forest)
column10: Montezuma quail can occur in areas with riparian vegetation near oak forest ()
column11: hence
column12: they may be using riparian corridors to transit between the WNM and CNM populations. Sightings on the Rio Grande () also suggest connectivity between WNM and CNM through this river.

column1: Despite a low genetic differentiation among our study populations
column2: patterns of genetic variation from all our analyses suggests isolation by distance
column3: which arises from limited geneflow between distant populations (). RST values between distant populations AZ and WTX (RST = 0.094; P = 0.001) and WTX–WNM populations (RST = 0.059; P = 0.013) were the highest among all pairwise comparisons. In contrast
column4: the previous mtDNA survey by  found no genetic differentiation between Montezuma quail populations in Arizona and Texas. Differentiation between neighboring WTX–CNM populations (RST = 0.033) was suggestive (P = 0.096) that gene flow occurs between these populations >200 km apart. This area is approximately 20 km away from the Guadalupe Mountains
column5: which is near the Texas–New Mexico state line
column6: thus representing a possible route of exchange between the two study populations. However
column7: the current existence of a large extension of unsuitable arid land between the two mountainous areas and the RST may suggest recent divergence. Furthermore
column8: our Bayesian analysis suggested three clusters
column9: whose assignment probabilities among Montezuma quail followed a longitudinal gradient ()
column10: although mixing between clusters was evident. However
column11: a Bayesian assignment by  did not detect separation between Arizona and New Mexico using genomic-wide single nucleotide polymorphism. Nevertheless
column12: also found lower differentiation between the populations of AZ–WNM and WNM–CNM. Thus
column13: these populations may represent a single cluster
column14: while the WTX population would be isolated from the rest. Approximately 40% of WTX Montezuma quail were assigned to the NM Cluster
column15: suggesting recent admixture between clusters. Results from this work support the idea
column16: proposed by
column17: that the Montezuma quail is a “ring species”
column18: where AZ and WTX populations were colonized by divergent populations dispersing northbound from the ancestral population in central Mexico through the Sierra Madre Occidental and Sierra Madre Oriental
column19: respectively. Alternatively
column20: our results may also suggest gene flow has recently stopped. Connectivity between Montezuma quail populations in the American southwest and northern Mexico may have been more widespread before the late 19th century when extensive ranching began reducing and depleting the grass cover () needed by Montezuma quail to disperse.

column1: Evidence suggests isolation by distance in Montezuma quail populations. Changes in temperature and precipitation patterns apparently expanded in altitude and latitude boundaries of deserts surrounding Montezuma quail habitat in the southwestern United States (; ; ). Thus
column2: constant fragmentation
column3: and predicted reduction of the extent of currently suitable habitats for the Montezuma quail () may reduce corridors between populations
column4: causing complete separation of populations and increase the risk of survival of the species in the mid and long term.

column1: Given the little genetic structure found in this study and the resolution offered by DNA microsatellites
column2: we were unable detect effects of Montezuma quail reintroductions from Arizona to Texas on genetic structure. There is no evidence that the reintroductions were successful and that any genetic structure was disrupted. However
column3: we found a significant genetic difference between AZ and WTX. In addition
column4: also found that extant populations in Arizona and Texas are genetically distinct from one another
column5: having diverged approximately 17
column6: 000 years ago.

column1: The presence of isolated populations in mountain patches of habitat or “sky islands” is frequent along all edges of its geographic distribution
column2: which may be embedded in arid
column3: subtropical
column4: or tropical vegetation. The existence of these isolated populations poses a conservation challenge for managers. These isolated populations have most likely differentiated
column5: as those of numerous vertebrates inhabiting sky islands systems (; ; ; ). The degree to which isolated Montezuma quail populations have differentiated deserves investigation. Montezuma quail in sky islands may have a similar degree of differentiation that led to the special management status of the Mount Graham red squirrel (Tamiascirus fremonti grahamensis) in Arizona (). Furthermore
column6: isolation in sky islands can also lead to local adaptation
column7: which may also promote persistence of a species. Therefore
column8: a genomic survey through the Montezuma quail geographic distribution is a research priority. Genome sequencing has been undertaken for the species (; ; )
column9: but with minimal representation of Mexican and sky island populations. Genomic surveys will unveil genetic structure patterns relevant for conservation. For instance
column10: translocations from one island population to another by federal and state game management agencies in the United States may have irreparably disrupted local genetics in such isolated populations. Like the Mount Graham’s red squirrel
column11: genomic studies of Montezuma quail populations in Texas
column12: southeastern Arizona
column13: and southwestern New Mexico sky islands might reveal that they may warranted special management status.

column1: The subtle genetic structure and low levels of genetic variation detected in our study is valuable for the future management of this charismatic game bird. This subtle genetic structure consists in clusters in Arizona
column2: New Mexico
column3: and Texas. Despite the differentiation
column4: the analysis suggests some mixing between populations
column5: which may indicate infrequent
column6: long-distance dispersal
column7: especially between New Mexico and Arizona populations. Migration between New Mexico and West Texas is also possible. In this context
column8: our results support isolation by distance between populations
column9: due to the non-independence between geographical distance and genetic distance. Montezuma quail populations in the southwestern United States may not be fully isolated
column10: despite habitat loss and fragmentation in this region as some corridors may open or close periodically depending on annual precipitation and its effect on herbaceous vegetation cover.

column1: The study was conducted at the SANBI National Zoological Garden (NZG)
column2: Pretoria
column3: South Africa (25.73913°N
column4: 28.18918°E) from the 24th of November 2017 to the 5th of December 2017. The study animals

column9: green vegetables were provided daily to all individuals. Prior to the start of the study

column1: Furthermore
column2: the mean absolute deviation (MAD) was calculated for the baseline sample set (pre-injection). The MAD of the particular dataset shows the average distance between each baseline period data point and the calculated mean thereof
column3: which represents the variability of the baseline samples collected. Thus

column1: In the current study
column2: the defecation rate of study animals were prolonged and varied substantially within and between individuals. Extended defecation rates have been observed in a number of reptile species such as the Italian wall lizard (Podarcis sicula
column5: veiled chameleon (Chamaeleo calyptratus
column8: six striped runner (Cnedmidophurs sexlineatus
column10: () and a variety of snake species (45–3
column12: (). Additionally
column13: these studies have also shown high levels of individual variability in terms of gut retention times; for example
column14: observed an individual excretion rate in C. calyptratus ranging from 48–120 h
column15: while  found the excretion rate in C. sexlineatus to range from 20–72 h. Understanding species-specific differences and individual variability in faecal and urinary defecation rates are important for a number of reasons. Firstly
column16: the infrequent and extended excretion rate of urinary and faecal material in reptiles may complicate data interpretations (). Furthermore
column17: the movement of urine into the cloaca (urodeum) before moving into the intestines
column18: where urinary and faecal material can be excreted in unison ()
column19: can further complicate the distinction between matrix-specific retention time and steroid hormone metabolite excretion routes. As it is difficult to collect frequent faecal and urine samples consistently in S. giganteus and other reptile species
column20: it may be advisable to monitor GC metabolite patterns over a longer time period to successfully determine the possible effect a defined stressor may have on an individual or species ().

column1: The MAD values for the four EIAs used in the fGCM analysis indicated low levels of variation from the predetermined baseline values. In contrast to this
column2: the MAD values calculated for the four uGCM EIAs showed high levels of variation from calculated baseline levels. As such
column3: GC metabolite excretion via faeces may be less prone to regular fluctuation than urine
column4: although further research is required to confirm this.

column1: Following ACTH injection
column2: the peak fGCM response was observed in the first faecal sample collected from all study animals.  found peak fGCM concentration
column3: following the ACTH injection
column4: in the first collected faecal sample from C. niloticus. Similarly
column5: observed peak fGCM concentrations in the first collected faecal sample in Harlequin frogs (Atelopus certus) following a biological stressor. The pooling of faecal material in the reptile gut
column6: over an extended period of time
column7: may explain why peak fGCM responses are observed in the first sample post-injection in reptiles and other infrequent defecators. However
column8: the available literature on reptile fGCM monitoring is limited
column9: with a number of studies failing to highlight when the peak fGCM levels were observed or choosing to pool samples into larger time periods (). Although all four EIAs displayed considerable peak fGCM responses for both sexes
column10: the tetrahydrocorticosterone and 5α-pregnane-3β-11β-21-triol-20-one EIA performed best in our study
column11: based on (i) EIA stability as seen in the low MAD values and (ii) the magnitude of peak percentage fGCM response following the ACTH injection. As such
column12: both EIAs seem to be suitable for monitoring alterations in fGCM concentration in S. giganteus faecal material.

column1: The peak uGCM concentrations following ACTH administrations were observed in the first and third collected urine sample for the females and male respectively. To our knowledge this is the first study to quantify the uGCM response following the activation of the HPA axis through physiological or biological stressors. In reptiles
column2: the movement of urine into the intestine
column3: and the resulting pooling effect along with faeces over time
column4: may explain why peak uGCM responses were observed within the first collected samples for females and third sample for males. Similar to the fGCM analysis
column5: all four EIAs used during the study were able to monitor alterations in uGCM concentrations following the ACTH administration; the tetrahydrocorticosterone and 5α-pregnane-3β-11β-21-triol-20-one EIAs again showed the highest uGCM response in this regard. With all uGCM MAD values considerably higher than observed for the fGCM analysis
column6: the peak uGCM response values were used to determine EIA suitability; in this regard
column7: both the tetrahydrocorticosterone and 5α-pregnane-3β-11β-21-triol-20-one EIAs were deemed suitable for monitoring alterations in uGCM concentration in S. giganteus urine.

column1: The ability to monitor physiological stress patterns in endangered reptile species
column2: through non-invasive hormone monitoring techniques
column3: offers conservationists an ideal tool which can be implemented within both free-ranging and captive setups with limited effort. With the increase in human-driven factors leading to substantial decreases in reptile populations
column4: the need for such techniques are becoming more important. This study has successfully validated such a technique for monitoring the stress response in S. giganteus in both urine and faeces by using the 5α-pregnane-3β-11β-21-triol-20-one or tetrahydrocorticosterone EIA. Both assays showed low MAD values as well as a considerable response in fGCM and uGCM concentrations following ACTH injection. As such
column5: both sample matrices can be used to monitor physiological stress in S. giganteus. Despite the results of this study
column6: a number of uncertainties need to be addressed by researcher conducting further studies on the topic. Of greatest concern is the observed gut passage time and time to peak fGCM and uGCM concentrations between individuals. Although the time to peak fGCM (24 h) and uGCM (27 h) responses were similar in both females
column7: the monitored male showed a prolonged gut passage time with peak fGCM and uGCM concentrations 81 h and 70 h later
column8: respectively. However
column9: if in fact differences in gut passage time or GC metabolite patterns between individuals or sexes of the species exist is yet to be determined by examining larger study populations. Currently
column10: we recommend collecting only the faecal or urine component for GC metabolite monitoring in S. giganteus. Despite the limitations of this study the findings increased our understanding of stress hormone production
column11: metabolism and excretion pattern in the species. We hope this will encourage and stimulate future research not only on this species
column12: but reptiles in general
column13: especially concerning the non-invasively examining the physiological stress response linked to a host of anthropogenic and natural factors.

column4: and antioxidant capacity of common beans ( Phaseolus vulgaris ) grown under water deficit| Abd El Mageed  Taia A. |2023-6-22|"Plants are subjected to recurring abiotic stresses during their growth
column5: threatening the production of vegetable crops. Water stress and salinity alone or in combination are the main yield-limiting factors (; ). Forecasts based on the integration of crop growth models and climate change estimated further yield losses ()
column6: associated with population growth that would require an increase in food production
column7: consequently increasing the demands for irrigated agriculture
column8: which requires heightening the irrigation use efficiency (IUE) (; ).

column1: Soil water deficit alters many vital processes and causes severe damage to the plant. A plant’s primary response to soil water reduction is a decrease in cell turgor pressure
column2: which causes osmotic stress
column3: resulting in several cellular signaling pathways that disrupt different physio-biochemical activities in plant cells (; ). Later
column4: drought/osmotic stress leads to excessive accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS; e.g.
column5: OH −
column6: H2O2
column7: and O2•−) produced from different plant organelles; mitochondria
column8: chloroplasts
column9: and peroxisomes (; ). Hyperproduction of ROS destructs the normal balance between ROS formation and scavenging that not only inhibits several enzymes activity but also triggers cellular oxidative damage like protein
column10: DNA
column11: and lipids even can lead to cell damage (; ). Simultaneously
column12: ROS provokes chlorophyll degradation and membrane lipid peroxidation
column13: decreasing membrane stability
column14: selectivity
column15: and fluidity
column16: and disturbing cell redox homeostasis (; ). Under water stress
column17: the lower tissue water content becomes limiting for physiological and biochemical processes in the plant (; ). Besides stomatal closure is a primary factor that decreases photosynthesis
column18: ROS generated in the chloroplasts can damage the photosynthetic pigments
column19: thylakoid membrane
column20: and enzymes
column21: as well as decrease or inhibits the photosynthetic capacity of photosystem II (PSII) (; ).

column1: The plant has adapted several regulatory signaling mechanisms to withstand water stress
column2: including activating the plant’s defense system
column3: which helps the plant modulate metabolism
column4: maintain protective regimes
column5: and redox homeostasis (). The abundance of osmoprotectants in plant tissues involves osmotic adjustment
column6: the maintenance of cell turgor
column7: and the control of water influx/efflux (; ). Furthermore
column8: increasing the activity of antioxidants both enzymatic and non-enzymatic helps to reduce ROS and protect membrane lipids from peroxidation under drought stress (; ; ). However
column9: a continuous water deficit can trigger an imbalance among scavenge and the production of ROS
column10: which inhibits the antioxidative machinery’s scavenging action. In that case
column11: the endogenous plant defense mechanisms are unable to completely protect plants from the harmful effects of drought stress
column12: thus impairing plant growth and reducing yield (; ). Therefore
column13: exogenous use of auxiliary compounds such as antioxidative compounds or another effective approach to support plant defense system
column14: allows plants to perform well under water deficit.

column1: Glutathione (GSH) is a major free thiol tripeptide with a low molecular weight that acts as an antioxidant to relieve environmental stresses in several ways (; ). GSH participates in the antioxidant defense
column2: hormone or redox molecule signaling
column3: transmembrane of amino acids transport
column4: and detoxification of ROS
column5: methylglyoxal
column6: and xenobiotics (; ). GSH is a small molecular weight of a thiol group that presents the GSH as a powerful ROS scavenger ()
column7: it can also scavenge ROS indirectly through the AsA-GSH cycle
column8: which removes harmful peroxides (; ). GSH is an active redox compound that is typically found in reduced glutathione (GSH) form which may be oxidized by ROS to disulfide glutathione (GSSG) form that is recirculated to GSH by NADPH-dependent glutathione reductase (). The balance of GSH and GSSG in the cell is related to its redox state
column9: in the sense that a higher GSH or GSH/GSSG ratio is important for many physiological mechanisms and regulates various adaptations to abiotic stress resilience (; ). Furthermore
column10: GSH contributes to plasma membrane stability by reducing lipid peroxidation
column11: as well as osmotic adjustment for abiotic stress tolerance (; ). Exogenous GSH has been shown in recent studies to improve plant growth
column12: leaf water status
column13: and photosynthesis
column14: and reduce oxidative damage indicators
column15: along with up-regulated some osmolytes
column16: the antioxidant capacity
column17: and induced cellular redox hemostasis under abiotic stress such as drought stress ()
column18: high temperature () and salt stress ().

column1: The Phaseolus vulgaris
column2: a common bean
column3: is among the world’s largest legume crops

The experimental layout was a randomized complete block design with three replications. The total experimental included 27 plots; each one was approximately 9 m2 (15 m in length × 0.6 m row width) each plot included 2 planting rows placed 30 cm apart with a distance of 10 cm between plants within rows. Two drip lines were placed 0.3 m apart in each elementary test plot. Phaseolus vulgaris (cv. Bronco) seeds were planted on 6 September and harvested on 28 November in both growing seasons. All treatments were separated and surrounded by a 1m non-irrigated area. Plants were adequately watered during the first irrigation. One week after complete germination
column2: irrigation treatments were started. All experimental units received identical doses of N
column3: P2O5
column4: and K150 kg N ha−1
column5: 60 kg P ha−1
column6: and 70 K kg ha−1 orderly. The other cultural practices for commercial bean production were carried out according to the instructions of the Ministry of Agriculture and Land Reclamation.

column1: 40|10.7717/peerj.5886|First steps towards assessing the evolutionary history and phylogeography of a widely distributed Neotropical grassland bird (Motacillidae: Anthus correndera )| Norambuena  Heraldo V. |2018-11-21|"Geological processes and past climatic changes have been determining factors in generating complex patterns of genetic diversity (). Both the spatial heterogeneity and the temporal variation of environmental conditions have influenced both intra- and interspecific diversification (; ). As such
column2: historical changes in habitat connectivity resulted in an increase in beta diversity
column3: both at intra- and inter-specific level. The phylogeographic context of southern South America (central Andes to Patagonia) is complex
column4: with different historical scenarios depending on latitude and habitat type. For example
column5: in Patagonia a series of refugia have been described in glacial valleys
column6: lowlands
column7: and periglacial zones (e.g.
column8: ; ; ). These areas maintained high levels of genetic diversity and worked as reservoirs for post-glacial colonization (; ). Our knowledge of the historical processes driving diversity patterns in southern South America has increased in recent decades
column9: mostly based on phylogeographic studies of organisms associated with temperate forest (; ). However
column10: this has limited our understanding about the role of other important ecosystems
column11: like grasslands
column12: in generating biodiversity patterns and processes. This is a relevant issue given the dominance of grasslands in southern South America. Studying grassland specialist species will increase our understanding of the processes acting across different environments and their relative importance in causing regional patterns of biodiversity.

column1: The study of diversification in Neotropical birds has been centered largely on the rich Amazonian and Andean forest biota
column2: as illustrated by an abundance of recent phylogenetic and phylogeographic studies (e.g.
column3: ; ; ). However
column4: approximately 15% of South America is covered in various types of natural open lowland and montane grasslands (). The Central Andes are considered a strong vicariant force for lowland-adapted taxa
column5: isolating ancestral populations on both sides of the cordillera (; ; ; ; ). At the same time
column6: the Andes are a source of diversification for highland taxa
column7: particularly during glacial cycles (; ; ; ; ; ). This suggests that the diversification of certain widely distributed species inhabiting both montane environments and lowland grassland could be due to a set of processes including orogeny and changes in the distribution of habitats. Unlike in the Holarctic region
column8: Neotropical grasslands are not distributed continuously and constitute a mosaic of different types of grasslands in the temperate (pampa)
column9: montane (highland moor)
column10: and (sub)tropical (closed
column11: field
column12: plains) zones (). The South American grasslands were more extensive during the Last Glacial Maximum (hereafter LGM
column14: 000 years ago; ) and probably during all glacial periods since ca. 1.5 Ma () according to several lines of evidence including palynology (; )
column15: climate models () and fossil deposits () studied for the Amazonian region. This evidence suggests that many of the grasslands
column16: and their fauna
column17: were probably connected during the Pleistocene and became isolated only recently ()
column18: with the degree of connectivity likely varying with altitude and geographic location. In addition
column19: genetic diversification can also be driven by differences in environmental conditions (). For example
column20: differences in extreme temperatures and precipitation have been shown to drive genetic divergence between populations of white-breasted Nuthatches from the sky islands (montane forest habitat islands in the USA) () and recent theory have emphasized the potential role of climatic differences in speciation ()
column21: especially in mountainous habitats where climatic conditions can shift abruptly across a smaller geographical space.

column1: Phylogeographic studies on organisms inhabiting grasslands in Southern South America are scarce (e.g.
column3: limiting our understanding about the potential historical scenarios that characterized these ecosystems as well as the potential processes shaping genetic diversity across taxa. Therefore
column4: new information about the phylogeographic history of taxa that specialize in this type of habitat is crucial to understand the historical and geological processes that have shaped the biodiversity of this particular biome (; ).

column1: The Correndera Pipit (Anthus correndera) is a grassland bird
column2: with a large breeding distribution in southern South America
column3: present in a variety of grassland habitats including páramo and puna (both on the Andean Altiplano)

column10: southeastern Peru

column12: A. c. correndera from southern Paraguay

column16: dark appearance and bigger size ()

column1: Considering the complex history and geography of the habitat of A. correndera in southern South America and the uncharacterized evolutionary relationships within the species
column2: the aim of this study is to evaluate the phylogeographic structure of A. correndera across most of its distributional range in order to test the following hypothesis predictions: (i) The degree of phylogeographic differentiation across the distribution of the species is not related to current morphology-based subspecific designations; (ii) the genetic structure is associated with environmental differences as represented by climatic variables; and (iii) the demographic history of lineages is related to the development of different types of grasslands across the South American landscape.

column1: We used both Bayesian inference (BI) and maximum likelihood (ML) approaches for phylogenetic reconstruction. We reconstructed a tree using only the ND2 gene and a species tree using both ND2 and ACOI9 for a subset of individuals. We identified the best-fit nucleotide substitution model for each gene using jModeltest 2 () which indicated HKY+ Γ as the best-fit model for ND2 and also for ACOI9 under the Akaike information criterion. BI analyses were conducted using MrBayes 3.2.1 () with the ND2 sequences
column2: by means of two runs with four chains each. We performed two different runs; one using the HKY+ Γ evolutionary model for the aligned matrix of ND2 and the other using an evolution model partitioning each position of the codons in the ND2 matrix. We ran all analyses for 100 million generations and we sampled every 1
column3: 000 steps; the first 25% of the data was discarded as burn-in. The convergence of MCMC analysis was examined visually in Tracer v1.6 (). We then compared log-likelihood of each matrix model by Bayes Factor for model selection. We chose the partitioning model because it returned a score 81 log-likelihood units greater than the HKY+ Γ evolutionary model.

column1: Principal Component Analyses (PCA) and a background test of niche divergence were conducted to investigate whether subspecies exhibit differences in climatic space. For each locality in the rarefied coordinate dataset (see the SDM section above)
column2: we collected the climatic data values from the same bioclimatic layers used in the SDM after highly correlated bio-climatic layers were removed. Statistical significance between groups was assessed using a PERMANOVA test. For the background test we followed . The background test compares niche divergence (differences in climate space) to a null model of background environmental differences and offers a powerful approach to test niche divergence while accounting for spatial autocorrelation. We used this test to evaluate whether there was evidence of niche divergence between the two main clades (A and B) resulting from the genealogical analysis. For a detailed description of this test see Supplementary material. All PCA analyses

column1: The ENM results suggest that suitable habitat for A. correndera was less extensive in the LGM relative to the present. This is contradictory to the general idea that grasslands increased their extension during the LGM (). An increase of grasslands was only evident in the Amazon Basin and the northern Andes (; ; ; ) but our results suggest that in southern South America the process apparently was different
column2: with a slight reduction or displacement of grassland habitat. This idea is also supported by studies of other Patagonian endemic taxa such as lizards () and shrubs () that showed displacement of suitable habitat
column3: rather than an increase in habitat during the LGM. These vegetational and climatic changes are explained by the latitudinal shifts and changes in intensity of the southern atmospheric circulation (). The lack of grassland expansion can explain why our historical demographic results suggest only modest increases in population size ( and ). However
column4: for the highland group the low sample size and their distribution could also be affecting these results (; ). The possibility that some individuals of clade A are present in the correndera subspecies range was not detected due to low geographic coverage of this area. More analysis of the demographic history of the highland group is necessary. The discontinuities seem not to heavily impact patterns of genetic and lineage differentiation
column5: at least within the widely distributed clade B. Nevertheless
column6: there seems to be a historic association between the Andean Altiplano
column7: that appears relatively isolated from southern areas
column8: with the phylogeographic split between clades A and B.

The lack of congruence between morphology-based taxonomy and genetic differentiation in the Anthus correndera complex can be explained by a rapid colonization of grassland environments
column2: which may have promoted a strong selection of certain morphological characters. However
column3: a re-evaluation of morphological differences between subspecies is necessary
column4: because the description of many of these was based on a low number of samples. Our results suggest that the grassland biome experienced distributional changes during the last glacial maximum
column5: which affected the distribution of specialist grassland fauna. This distributional change was interrupted only by altitudinal differences that could influence the genetic population structure of a specialist species like A. correndera. Future analysis based on greater genomic coverage and the use of complementary information like vocalizations would be useful to complement these results.

column1: 41|10.7717/peerj.5659|Greenhouse gas flux with reflooding of a drained salt marsh soil| Wollenberg  Jan T. |2018-11-15|"Salt marshes provide valuable ecosystem services including habitat provision and storm protection (e.g.
column2: ; ). In addition
column3: salt marshes have been recognized for their value as highly effective carbon (C) sinks (; ) and the term “blue carbon” has been coined for the C stored in salt marshes
column4: mangroves and seagrass beds (). Carbon dioxide (CO2) is fixed by marsh vegetation through photosynthesis and stored in both aboveground and belowground biomass. The persistence of anaerobic conditions results in low rates of decomposition (; ) and over time salt marshes accrete both vertically and laterally with the accumulation of organic matter and sediment delivered by tidal floodwaters (; ). Unlike freshwater wetlands which can be significant sources of CH4 ()
column5: salt marshes are typically negligible sources of CH4 and N2O. The former is due to the presence of abundant sulfate in marsh soils that hinders CH4 production (). Where the supply of N as NO3− is minimal
column6: N2O emissions are also negligible ().

column1: Over the past 50 to 100 years
column2: human activities including land reclamation through drainage have resulted in the loss of 25–50% of salt marsh area globally (; ). On Canada’s St. Lawrence River estuary
column3: 135 km2 (63%) of pre-existing marsh has been drained (). Once drainage infrastructure is in place
column4: the water table of the reclaimed marsh area recedes causing the decay of organic matter to accelerate as the formerly saturated soil is aerated (). Net C losses are thus likely over time as the land subsides (; ) and the former marsh soil loses its capacity to act as a C sink; losses as high as 80% have been reported for drained marshes in the San Joaquin Delta in California ().

column1: Yet while the global decline of this important C sink continues today
column2: in certain countries this trend has now been reversed with efforts to restore salt marshes through a process known as managed realignment (MR) (; ). The process of MR involves the landward movement
column3: breaching
column4: or removal of a sea defence structure to restore tidal influence and promote the creation of new intertidal habitat (). Managed realignment projects have demonstrated that even with relatively limited management or pre-treatment
column5: allowing tidal flow to return to a drained and low-lying agricultural field will quickly produce new habitat dominated by salt marsh plants (; ; ).

column1: Although restoring marsh habitat can help reduce atmospheric concentrations of CO2
column2: under some conditions salt marshes can be sources of N2O and CH4. In marshes with elevated nutrient inputs
column3: for example
column4: N2O emission can be substantial and could reduce the value of soil C storage from the perspective of climate change mitigation (; ; ).  observed significant N2O emissions from Prince Edward Island marshes in watersheds subject to N runoff from intensively farmed watersheds. N2O flux is of particular concern as it has a global warming potential (GWP) 265 times that of CO2 over a 100-year time horizon (). When extrapolated to the entire growing season with the sustained-flux global warming/cooling potential (SGWP/SGCP) developed by
column5: the N2O flux offset ∼20% of the average annual C sequestration of the marshes.

column1: Restored agricultural marshes
column2: however
column3: may exhibit patterns of GHG emissions that differ from natural marshes. Since agricultural management often includes application of manure or inorganic fertilizer they could temporarily become sources of N2O even in the usually N-limited estuarine environment. To investigate these differences in GHG dynamics
column4: experimentally flooded soil cores collected from a MR marsh approximately 6 months after MR and a natural marsh in the upper estuary of the River Torridge
column5: UK. Prior to the realignment the MR site consisted of abandoned agricultural land reclaimed from the salt marsh approximately 200 years ago (). In contrast to the natural marsh
column6: they found that the soil cores from the formerly drained MR had N2O emission even when floodwater was N limited
column7: presumably due to an intrinsic source of NO3−. Similarly
column8: studied the rates of C burial and GHG emissions (CO2
column9: N2O and CH4) in natural and MR salt marshes of the Blackwater estuary
column10: UK and found that GHG emissions
column11: particularly N2O
column12: were highest in the MR marshes. The highest N2O emissions observed originated from an MR site formerly used as pasture which also had the highest pre-breach soil N content (mean of 0.94 wt. % N) of the five MR sites. Due to its large global warming potential (GWP)
column13: N2O flux reduced the net cooling effect of C sequestration by as much as 49% in some of the MR marshes investigated ().

column1: Previous studies have shown that MR and soil flooding can result in rapid changes in soils of recovering marshes (e.g.
column2: ; ) which can contribute to the multiple pathways for production of N2O ().  flooded soil cores from a historically drained salt marsh on Cape Cod and found a large NH4+-N mobilization immediately after re-flooding which resulted in a 40 to 60-fold increase in porewater NH4+-N concentration. Similar research following the MR of abandoned agricultural lands in the River Torridge estuary
column3: UK
column4: revealed that MR quickly transformed the site’s topsoil (0–10 cm) from an oxidized to a reducing environment and increased topsoil pore water NH4+-N concentrations from 0.1 to 10.1 mg L−1 (). Studies employing stable isotopes have provided evidence that ammonia oxidizing bacteria can significantly contribute to soil N2O emissions in flooded soil (; ).

column1: Examining the impact of flooding would require sampling at high tide which presents logistical challenges. Static chamber measurements are thus often taken during low tide when nutrient concentrations and N2O fluxes are likely to be lower ().  found N2O flux varied with the tidal cycle and observed the highest N2O fluxes immediately after the high tide receded. Simply using an average of high tide and low tide fluxes extrapolated over 24 hours may thus overestimate or underestimate daily N2O fluxes (). To accurately model the soil’s response to flooding
column2: it is important to verify the effect of flooding on N2O emissions at high tide
column3: low tide
column4: and mid tide (halfway between high tide and low tide).

column1: Although restoration of salt marsh can mitigate climate change
column2: considerable gaps in our knowledge with regards to GHG dynamics in recovering marshes remain
column3: particularly immediately after estuary water is returned (). Soil core incubation experiments are an alternative to field sampling given the logistical challenges of capturing emissions at the exact moment of re-flooding. The purpose of our study is to assess the GHG flux of the soil immediately after flooding. This data will help to establish a baseline for evaluating the potential changes in GHG flux following the restoration of estuarine flooding to dykelands.

column1: In the Kamouraska region
column2: which spans from Saint-Roch-des Aulnaies to Trois-Pistoles on the southern shore of the St. Lawrence River
column3: the drained marsh soils are acid sulfate soils belonging to the de l’Anse series in Quebec (). Surface soils (0–35 cm) are composed primarily of silty clay ().

column1: Soil cores were collected from a farm on a drained and dyked salt marsh (47°41′36.54″N
column2: 69°42′38.95″W) located in the Kamouraska region ().

column1: Map Data: Google/DigitalGlobe.
The dyke was constructed in 1986 (A Parent
column2: pers. comm.
column3: 2016) and conventional drainage infrastructure was employed. Several drainage ditches approximately 1 to 2 m deep allow runoff from the upland and field to drain into the river. Tidal gates were installed where the ditches intersect the dyke to prevent the inflow of tidal water at high tide and allow water from the farm and the upland to drain out to the river at low tide. The site was also subject to an additional modification known as crowning
column4: or landforming
column5: which involves transferring the soil adjacent to the ditches to the mid-ditch area
column6: creating a crown and a slope from the crown towards the ditches to promote drainage ().

column1: The farmland is used to grow alternating grain and hay crops in mixed rotation (A Parent
column2: pers. comm.
column3: 2016). The field is plowed to a depth of approximately 30 cm at which a plow pan is found. The reduced permeability of the compacted plow pan slows the percolation of water at depth causing water to drain horizontally into the adjacent ditches.

column1: The Kamouraska region has a cool and wet climate with a yearly annual average temperature of 4.5 °C (). The daily average temperature in July is 19 °C and the daily average maximum in January is −12 °C (). The soil freezes in mid to late November and thaws in late April to early May. The region receives an annual average of 1
column2: 140 mm precipitation (). Tidal amplitudes at nearby Riviere-du-Loup are between 3.5 and 5.6 m ().

column1: Soil cores were collected at three elevations within the field: at the low-crown
column2: mid and crown top (). The first location (low-crown) was placed near the drainage ditch and represents the section of the field from which soil was removed during crowning modifications. The second location (mid-crown) was selected approximately 25 m towards the upland edge between the ditch and the center of the field; this location was chosen to represent an area which was less likely to have had any soil added or removed during crowning modifications. The third location (high-crown) was placed near the center of the field to represent the top of the crowned area where soil was added.

column1: The cores were collected on October 31
column2: 2015. Five replicate cores were collected from low
column3: mid and high crown locations by driving 40 cm sections of a 10 cm-diameter HDPE pipe into the soil using a truck-mounted hydraulic borer. The pipes were driven approximately 30 cm into the soil
column4: or until refusal
column5: and the cores were then dug out by hand. A denser and more compacted soil was encountered at the mid-crown and high-crown locations; cores collected from these locations were thus subject to compaction as the pipes were driven into the soil. The mean % compaction (calculated from the length of the outside and inside of the core after insertion into soil) measured for cores collected in the low-crown
column6: mid-crown
column7: and high-crown locations were 3.9% (n = 5)
column10: respectively. Cores were sealed at each end using fitted HDPE caps and stored under refrigeration.

column1: A hole was dug adjacent to the cores at each of the three locations in the field to collect bulk soil samples for NH4+ and NO3− analysis. Three samples were collected from the wall of each hole from depths of 0–10 cm
column3: and 20–30 cm. At high tide 32 L of estuary water (salinity 9 psu) was collected near La Pocatière approximately 50 km upstream of the study site. The water was frozen following collection.

column1: Soil cores previously collected across the farm field by  were used to determine surface soil (0–30 cm) bulk density and organic C content. All samples were oven dried at 60 °C until there was no additional mass lost. Samples were then ground using a mortar and pestle. Representative sub-samples of ∼2.5 g were placed in crucibles and dried at 60 °C until all moisture was lost and weighed in preparation for loss on ignition (LOI). In a programmable muffle furnace
column2: the sub-samples were heated to 350 °C for 1 hour followed by 4 hours at 550 °C (). Samples were removed from the muffle furnace and placed in a desiccator to cool to room temperature prior to weighing. If the difference in mass loss between the two replicate samples exceeded 10% additional replicates were run. The organic C content of each sample was estimated using a conversion factor developed by : Organic C fraction = 0.40(LOI fraction) + (0.025 * LOI fraction)2. The NH4+ and NO3− content were determined using potassium chloride extraction and the automated colorimetric method ().

column1: To prepare cores for the gas flux experiment
column2: holes were drilled in the center of each bottom cap and through the wall of the core tube approximately 1 mm above the soil surface. Clear plastic tubing was inserted into the holes and sealed in place with silicon caulking to allow both surface water and percolating water to drain after each flooding cycle. The bottom caps of the soil cores were lined with cheese cloth
column3: 20 µm filter paper
column4: and a thin layer of fine gravel to prevent the loss of soil as water drained. A control was prepared using the same materials (river water
column5: HDPE piping
column6: silicon caulking
column7: gravel
column8: filter paper
column9: and cheese cloth) to confirm that any changes in gas flux observed were due to microbial activity in the soil cores rather than the composition of the materials used in the experiment. Headspace sampling of river water in the control tube yielded negligible gas flux of just −0.014 µmol CH4 hr−1
column10: −0.010 µmol N2O hr−1
column11: and 20.89 µmol CO2 hr−1. Changes observed during soil flooding were thus attributable to differences in soil microbial processes as the influence of the different materials were negligible. Gas concentrations measured in the ambient air in the lab at the beginning of the experiment were 0.331 ppm N2O
column12: 1.991 ppm CH4
column13: and 608.18 ppm CO2; concentrations of each gas were within the expected range based on current atmospheric GHG concentrations ().

column1: Cores were frozen at −10 °C for one week prior to use to simulate the natural freezing that occurs in the region over winter. Following freezing
column2: the cores were incubated at room temperature (ranging from 23 to 25 °C) for five days prior to beginning the experiment. Cores were flooded by gently pouring the estuary water over top of the soil column until 3 cm of standing water was present above the soil surface and the water level remained constant. The flooding pattern is modeled after that of  to simulate semi-diurnal tidal flooding.

column5: and 60 min). Each sample was collected through the septum using a gas tight syringe with a stopcock. Prior to sampling

The GWP has been used to compare the radiative forcing of different GHG (). In our study
column2: we apply the sustained-flux global warming/cooling potential (SGWP/SGCP) developed by  (). The SGWP/SGCP metrics have been proposed as more representative for ecosystem studies. We used the SGWP and SGCP metrics over a 100-year time horizon to determine how flooding changed the climate role of the dyked soils; SGCP values were used for uptake (i.e.
column3: negative flux) and SGWP were used for emission (i.e.
column4: positive flux). The 100-year time horizon was chosen as C registries typically require that C offset projects be evaluated for a minimum period of 100 yr.

Peak fluxes were 540% and 350% higher than background levels for high-crown and mid-crown locations

column1: Mean CO2 fluxes from cores collected at the three different locations in the field significantly decreased with flooding. Soil physical properties which affect the diffusion and availability of oxygen can exhibit a strong control on decomposition rates and CO2 production. In well drained and aerated soils
column2: oxygen diffusion is rapid and upland soils have O 2concentrations near 21% up to depths of 1 m (). When soils are flooded
column3: increasing the water filled pore space (WFPS) can slow O2 diffusion by a factor of 104 compared with air (). As soils become saturated
column4: some air-filled pore spaces can eventually become completely separated from gaseous exchange with atmosphere (). Increasing water content in the soil can thus slow aerobic decomposition and associated CO2 production (; ; ).

column1: Changes in CO2 flux upon flooding in our experiment were consistent with other studies which have indicated that restoration of tidal flooding reduces decomposition in organic soils and prevents on-going C losses to the atmosphere (). In another study carried out before and after the MR of a drained marsh in the UK cotton strip decomposition assays revealed rates of cotton tensile strength loss (CTSL)
column2: a proxy for decomposition rates
column3: were greatly reduced following soil flooding ().

column1: Across the three locations sampled in the field
column2: flooding resulted in a 62% decline in mean CO2 emissions. Extrapolated over 24 hr
column3: this decrease is equivalent to a reduction in soil CO2 flux of 338 mmol CO2 m−2 day−1
column4: nearly seven times the mean global C sequestration rate of 48 mmol CO2 m−2 day−1 reported by . In the short term
column5: flooding dyked marsh soils could thus have a net cooling effect by reducing CO2-C losses and preventing the daily release of CO2 equivalent to one week of typical marsh C sequestration.

column1: Negative CH4 fluxes occur when CH4 oxidation within the soil exceeds production and the soil acts as a sink (). Prior to flooding
column2: CH4 fluxes were negative for all cores
column3: with larger negative fluxes observed for mid-crown and low-crown cores. As soil CH4 flux is determined by a combination of gas diffusivity and methanotroph and methanogen activity ()
column4: differences in soil compaction may explain part of the variation in background fluxes between locations. Poor aeration and CH4 diffusion below the soil surface may thus have resulted in smaller CH4 uptake in the more compacted high-crown soils.

column1: The negative pre-flooding fluxes for CH4 measured in our experiment range from −0.2 to −4.4 mg CH4 day−1 and are consistent with the net uptake of CH4 observed in many agricultural soils (). Although soil flooding did not result in positive CH4 flux
column2: CH4 uptake was significantly reduced. The magnitude of the change in flux should be approached with caution
column3: however
column4: as the background measurements were taken after the soils cores had been refrigerated and removed from the influence of rainfall or moisture. Given that differences in rainfall have been shown to shift agricultural soils from an annual sink to an annual source of CH4 ()
column5: refrigeration and removal of the cores from the influence of precipitation may thus have yielded larger negative fluxes than would have been expected in the field.

column1: CH4 fluxes were negligible after flooding
column2: presumably due to the abundance of sulfate remaining in the soil (). The highest hourly flux during the experiment (0.41 µmol m−2 hr−1) was measured during the simulated low-tide on the first day of flooding in the low-crown cores. Even if this maximum flux was maintained throughout the annual period when marsh soil is not frozen
column3: the SGWP of CH4 emissions would offset <0.5% of the mean global C sequestration reported for salt marshes by . Thus
column4: although the loss of the small CH4 sink is equivalent to an effective increase in the supply of CH4 to the atmosphere
column5: the effect remains small when compared to the immediate reduction in CO2 emission as well as the future C sequestration once the marsh redevelops.

column1: Nitrous oxide (N2O) is produced primarily during three microbial processes in the soil nitrogen cycle: (1) the nitrification of ammonium (NH4+) to nitrate (NO3−)
column2: in which N2O is a non-obligate by-product
column3: (2) the denitrification of NO3− in which N2O is an obligate intermediate in the reduction of NO3 to nitrogen gas (N2)
column4: and (3) the dissimilatory reduction of NO3− to NH4+ (DNRA) (; ). These processes depend strongly on many inter-related factors including the availability of O2
column5: NH4+ (for nitrification)
column6: NO3− (for denitrification)
column7: and organic C (). N2O emission is thus expected to increase when NO3−-N and NH4+-N in the soil are higher (). The data from our experiment
column8: however
column9: showed a significant relationship between N2O flux and soil N content for post-flood measurements only; no significant relationship was observed between pre-flood N2O flux and soil N content.

column1: Differences in soil compaction offer one possible explanation for the variation in pre-flood fluxes among cores collected from the three different locations in the farm field. Denitrification occurs when the consumption of O2 in the soil by plant roots and microorganisms exceeds the rate of replenishment from the atmosphere and creates anoxic microsites in the soil (). Oxygen is the energetically preferred electron acceptor in denitrifying bacteria and if present will result in the suppression of denitrification enzyme synthesis (). Still
column2: as NO3− readily accepts electrons in the absence of O2
column3: denitrification can begin rapidly once anaerobic conditions develop (). Compaction decreases gas diffusivity and oxygen delivery to the soil () and may thus increase the proportion of anaerobic microsites in which N2O is produced ().

column1: Field experiments and models have shown higher N2O emissions in compacted soil ().  observed
column2: however
column3: that increases in N2O emission from soil compaction in the field became non-significant when soil was broken up and sieved. As they found the effect of compaction on N2O flux to be reversible
column4: suggest that it was the physical effects of compaction that drove the increase in N2O flux rather than a permanent increase in the biological potential for N2O production (). This is consistent with the St. Lawrence cores for which % soil compaction was positively correlated with background N2O flux. It should be noted that although mechanical compaction occurred during core collection
column5: it is likely that the soil in the mid-crown and high-crown locations was already more compacted in situ. In the low-crown location
column6: piping was easily driven into the soil whereas considerable resistance was encountered when the same force was applied by the hydraulic borer in the mid and high-crown locations. The effects of compaction are thus likely attributable to both pre-existing compaction within the farm soils as well as the additional compaction that occurred during sampling.

column1: After 5 days of flooding only low-crown cores showed positive N2O flux with the more compacted soils in the mid-crown and high-crown cores showing fluxes near zero. Given that agricultural lands tend to have higher N2O emissions ()
column2: these results suggest that in highly compacted soils
column3: which are frequently encountered in agricultural settings due to tractor and machinery traffic ()
column4: returning tidal flooding may in fact quickly transform a former source of N2O into a sink. For the less compacted low-crown soils
column5: if the mean daily flux of 1.01 µmol m−2 hr−1 were to persist for the entire period of the year during which soils are not frozen
column6: the SGWP of the additional N2O emissions would offset <10% of the mean global C sequestration reported for salt marshes by . The recovering marsh would thus maintain a global cooling potential.

column1: After flooding
column2: the 5-day mean SGWP fell by 72

column5: mid-crown
column6: and high-crown soil cores

column1: Notes. No measurements were made on days 3 and 4. Gas flux on days 3 and 4 were thus assumed to be equivalent to the mean of fluxes measured on days 2 and 5. The substantial cooling effect associated with the reduction in CO2 flux far exceeded the warming potential associated with the small increases in N2O and CH4 flux. Restoring tidal flooding to the historically dyked soils thus had an immediate net cooling effect and does not appear to pose a risk of offsetting the global cooling potential of the C that would be sequestered as the salt marsh recovered. Going forward
column2: the successfully restored marsh would then be expected to resume new soil C storage at rates typical for the region. In addition
column3: although we did not consider the C sequestration associated with the growth of farm vegetation
column4: salt marsh production is comparable to crop production (; ).

column1: Previous studies have raised concerns that re-flooding drained salt marsh soils may temporarily create conditions favorable for denitrification and N2O production. Due to its high SGWP
column2: even small fluxes of N2O can offset a portion of the blue carbon benefit associated with a marsh restoration project. Here we show that despite small increases in CH4 and N2O flux following flooding
column3: the accompanying large decline in CO2 emission suggests that re-flooding has a strong and immediate net cooling effect. The daily SGWP of both CH4 and N2O were very small when compared with the decline in CO2 and CH4 and N2O contributed <5% of the total mean daily SGWP when expressed in terms of CO2 eq.

column1: The use of soil cores allowed us to measure emissions at the exact moment of reflooding. Further monitoring beyond the first week of flooding is still warranted to confirm the persistence of low N2O flux
column2: particularly at times such as spring thaw when flux is expected to be higher. In addition the N-content of the soils tested in this experiment were low compared with those measured in other agricultural soils and further study of N2O emissions following restoration may be necessary for areas which have been heavily fertilized. Still
column3: our findings suggest that N2O emissions following re-flooding are likely to be minimal in non-fertilized dykelands and provide further support for the effectiveness of marsh restoration as a climate mitigation strategy.

column1: 42|10.7717/peerj.45|Does haemosporidian infection affect hematological and biochemical profiles of the endangered Black-fronted piping-guan ( Aburria jacutinga )?| Motta  Rafael Otávio Cançado |2013-2-26|"Infectious diseases can greatly impact local species populations by causing temporary or permanent declines in host abundance. The effects of infection interacting with other driving forces
column2: such as habitat loss
column3: climate change
column4: overexploitation
column5: invasive species and environmental pollution
column6: contribute to local and global extinctions (). Only recently have the negative effects of parasites on the population dynamics of wildlife been recognized and emerged as a critical issue in the conservation of threatened species (; ).

column1: For a long time
column2: most avian haemosporidian parasites have been considered harmless in the wild because they appear in clinically healthy birds (). However
column3: researchers have shown that these parasites can have negative impacts on their hosts
column4: especially during energy-demanding or stressful phases by delaying their arrival to breeding grounds
column5: reducing clutch sizes and nest defense behavior
column6: increasing probability of clutch desertion
column7: reducing hatching and fledging success and weaning nestlings with poorer body conditions (; ; ; ; ; ). In captivity

column9: including threatened species such as the recent report of severe and acute mortality in the masked Bobwhite quail Colinus virginianus ridgwayi at breeding facilities (; ). All of this evidence has deemed avian haemosporidian infections to be important for studies related to wildlife conservation and management programs in-situ and ex-situ  (; ).

column1: Most studies have focused on the need to account for infectious diseases in management plans for threatened species
column2: including those maintained in captivity (; ). These studies recommend improving the understanding of the prevalence and diversity of parasites found in such species as a first step
column3: mainly in populations at risk from several other stressors. In addition to surveillance

column1: The Cracidae family of the order Galliformes contains 11 genera
column2: 50 species and approximately 60 subspecies (). Black-fronted piping-guan
column3: Aburria jacutinga
column4: which is endemic to the Brazilian Atlantic rainforest

The species was widely distributed at altitudes between 0 and 1000 m and could be found from the northeast to the south of Brazil and in some areas of Argentina and Paraguay. Aburria jacutinga is facing the risk of extinction due to predatory hunting and habitat loss. The Brazilian Atlantic rainforest has been altered and reduced due to deforestation for agribusiness purposes. No reports of the species have occurred in the last 20 years in any of the Brazilian states. Wild populations of A. jacutinga are at present found mainly in conservation areas in only two Brazilian states (; ).

column4: the evaluation of the presence as well as the effects of avian haemosporidians on the health of endangered species
column5: such as A. jacutinga
column6: is still essential because
column7: even though clinical signs of haemosporidian infection are mild or absent in the chronic phase
column8: some studies have shown that haemosporidian infections that are associated with other etiological agents

column1: Aburria jacutinga is an endangered cracid species with a scarcity of information concerning aspects of its physiology and health
column2: mainly due to the high difficulty of finding individuals both in their natural habitat and in captivity. The presence of undescribed Plasmodium and Haemoproteus lineages in A. jacutinga
column3: indicates the need to characterize the haemosporidian diversity occurring not only in endangered species such A. jacutinga but also in all captive birds maintained in rehabilitation centers or zoos
column4: as well the wild birds that inhabit the vicinity of these centers

Parasites that are able to infect multiple hosts coupled with cross species transmission pose the greatest threat to disease-mediated extinction because parasites can maintain high prevalence in alternative hosts even if the density of host populations is very low (; ).

column1: The Brazilian management plan for conservation of cracids proposes reintroduction of endangered species that were born in captivity into the wild (). Therefore
column2: knowledge about physiological parameters and etiological agents that occur in such birds would help to establish guidelines for conservation acts. Considering that the occurrence of haemosporidian parasites was determined and might serve as reference

column5: using insect-proof nets

column7: would reduce the impacts of the disease in early cracid life stages. Additionally
column8: birds to be reintroduced should be assessed for infectious and parasitic etiologies to avoid the introduction of diseases in previously absent environments ().

column1: Any conservational action may have parasitological consequences. For instance
column2: programs for reintroducing captive-bred animals that do not take infection risks into account could compromise such programs (). The conservation of A. jacutinga species might depend on the evaluation of the impact of the diseases and other threats such as food availability
column3: predation and competition (). As the chronically infected individuals do survive and reproduce normally
column4: they can be used for breeding in captivity for reintroduction projects and should be assessed for infectious and parasitic agents.

column1: 43|10.7717/peerj.5725|Testing the heat dissipation limitation hypothesis: basal metabolic rates of endotherms decrease with increasing upper and lower critical temperatures| Khaliq  Imran |2018-10-31|"Understanding how organisms cope with their ambient climatic conditions is crucial for assessing whether and how species may be able to respond to ongoing and future climate change. Organisms’ energy budgets play a key role in this context
column2: and studies on how energy budgets vary in relation to environmental factors as well as to the species-specific characteristics will contribute to our knowledge about species’ responses to varying and changing climatic conditions.

column1: Life history theory predicts that the energy available to organisms is limited and thus has to be utilized economically among different biological processes (). An endotherm animal (i.e.
column2: a bird or a mammal) normally utilizes 30–50% of its daily energy expenditure just to maintain a stable body temperature (homeostasis) (). In order to be able to invest sufficient energy into processes such as reproduction
column3: dispersal or predator avoidance
column4: animals should seek to live in environments with conditions allowing them to keep the energetic cost for homeostasis low ().

column1: A non-reproductive endotherm at rest utilizes a minimal amount of energy within a range of temperatures known as thermal neutral zone (TNZ
column2: ). Under the assumption outlined above
column3: i.e.
column4: assuming an energetic trade-off between homeostasis and other biological processes
column5: endotherms should live in areas with ambient temperatures falling within the TNZ (). However
column6: studies have shown that for the majority of investigated endotherm species this expectation did not receive strong support (; ; ). This indicates that other physiological and ecological constraints shape the evolution
column7: survival and distribution of endotherm animals (; ). In fact
column8: in recent decades the identification of the factors that limit the energy availability of animals has become one of the most pursued topics in the field of eco-physiology (; ; ; ; ; ). Researchers have proposed a plethora of factors that influence the energetics of animals (; )
column9: in particular their basal metabolic rate (BMR)
column10: which is the minimal level of energy invested for homeostasis. On the one hand
column11: several intrinsic factors
column12: i.e.
column13: factors inherent to the animal’s morphological or physiological characteristics have been shown to be associated with the variation in BMR (; ; ). For instance
column14: species with small organ size tend to have lower BMR than species with larger organs (). As another example
column15: the number of uncoupled mitochondria in individual mice have been shown to be positively associated with BMR (). On the other hand
column16: BMR has been shown to be related to extrinsic factors
column17: i.e.
column18: factors related to species’ ambient environments. For example
column19: BMR was negatively associated with ambient temperatures and rainfall variability (; ) and positively associated with the primary productivity (; ).

column1: Traditionally
column2: most studies have focused on extrinsic factors influencing the energy level in organisms
column3: such as food sources (; ) or environmental conditions (; ; ). However
column4: a few studies have also focused on intrinsic factors (; ; ). Regarding the latter
column5: several hypotheses have gained attention. First
column6: the central limitation hypothesis predicts that energy intake is constrained by the alimentary canal’s capacity to process food
column7: i.e.
column8: energy levels should be directly linked to the efficiency of the alimentary canal to convert food into energy (; ). In other words
column9: available energy is constrained at the level of energy generation. However
column10: this hypothesis has been questioned because it was shown that under different climatic conditions animals (n = 15) can alter the rate of food processing beyond the upper limits set by the central limitation hypothesis (; ). Second
column11: the peripheral limitation hypothesis predicts that it is the internal organs’ capacity to utilize energy that constrains the energy level in animals (; ). However
column12: the observed possibility to alter energy utilization within the same individual animal (containing the same organs) under different climatic conditions has questioned this hypothesis as well (; ).

column1: Recently
column2: as a third hypothesis on which intrinsic factors constrain the energy budget of endotherms
column3: the heat dissipation limit (HDL) hypothesis has been put forward (). It has received support from different lines of evidence; e.g.
column4: from observations that endotherms are able to alter energy levels under different climatic conditions () or that basal and field metabolic rates decrease with increasing ambient temperatures (; ; ). These findings point towards the role of animals’ heat dissipation capacity in limiting their energy levels. Further support for this comes from  who showed that mammals inhabiting hotter environments are confronted with the endogenous heat load challenge due to their inefficiency to dissipate heat. When ambient temperatures rise
column5: the difference between body and ambient temperatures decreases and it becomes increasingly difficult for endotherms to dissipate heat from the body. Consequently
column6: it becomes a challenge to keep body temperatures constant. When temperatures rise beyond the upper limit of the TNZ (upper critical temperature
column7: Tuc)
column8: the amount of heat generated at BMR exceeds the capacity of the body to dissipate heat passively
column9: and other cooling mechanisms (e.g.
column10: evaporative cooling) must be employed to maintain body temperature and avoid hyperthermia.

column1: To avoid an increased risk of hyperthermia under high and even increasing temperatures
column2: endotherms may have two non-exclusive avenues of adaptation: changes in their thermal conductance (i.e.
column3: the ability to passively dissipate heat) or changes in their BMR as one crucial source of endogenous heat (). All else being equal
column4: to keep their body temperatures constant
column5: species with a relatively high BMR must invoke active cooling mechanisms at a lower environmental temperature
column6: i.e.
column7: have a lower Tuc
column8: than species with a relatively low BMR. Similarly
column9: species with a relatively high thermal conductance are able to dissipate more heat at a given environmental temperature than species with low thermal conductance. Thus
column10: by increasing thermal conductance
column11: decreasing BMR
column12: or both
column13: endotherms may increase their Tuc ()
column14: hence being able to cope better with rising global temperatures (). Similarly
column15: when environmental temperatures decrease
column16: the difference between body temperatures and environmental temperatures increases and as a consequence the animal loses heat. When environmental temperatures reach the lower critical temperature (Tlc)
column17: the heat generated at the BMR level is not sufficient to keep body temperatures constant; therefore
column18: the animal must invoke active heat generation (e.g.
column19: by increasing its food intake). Animals with relatively high BMR should therefore be able to extend their Tlc towards low temperatures.

column1: The HDL theory proposes that the overall energetics of endotherms are dependent on their abilities to dissipate heat from the body (). There are a few studies that show lack of support for the HDL theory (; ); however
column2: several studies in the recent past have empirically supported the HDL theory (; ; ; ; ; ; ; ). Still
column3: a cross-species test of the HDL is lacking. Here
column4: we test this prediction
column5: using a data set of Tuc
column6: Tlc
column7: body mass
column8: and BMR of 146 endotherms (88 mammals and 58 birds)
column9: in order to evaluate the influence of heat dissipation limits on species’ metabolism. Following the above line of arguments
column10: we expect
column11: if heat dissipation is a limiting factor of endotherm energy levels (i.e.
column12: BMR)
column13: a negative relationship between critical temperatures and BMR.

The compiled data were obtained from physiological experiments conducted to measure BMR under different temperature conditions. Data quality and suitability for macro-physiological analyses have been extensively debated recently (; ; ; ). Here
column4: the focus on critical temperatures and BMR requires the use of data fulfilling the strictest quality criteria

column6: a sufficient sample size (at least three individuals measured) and an experimental temperature range sufficient for determining Tuc and Tlc (at least three measurements of metabolic rate beyond TNZ) (see Table S1 in ;  for details). Thus

column8: and 88 mammal species belonging to 40 families and 16 order (see ). To account for the evolutionary non-independence of data in comparative analysis
column9: phylogenetic information for all species were compiled from published supertrees for birds () and mammals (; ).

column1: Our analyses accounts for the effect of body mass
column2: which plays a central role in thermal physiology. Large endotherms tend to dissipate or lose metabolic heat slowly because of their small surface area relative to their body volume (; ). Thermal conductance scales with body mass with an exponent of around 0.57
column3: while both BMR and body surface area scale with body mass with exponents around 0.66–0.74 (; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ). This indicates that thermal conductance increases more slowly with body mass than BMR. Therefore
column4: larger endotherms are better able to withstand colder temperatures than smaller endotherms (). Similarly
column5: small-bodied endotherms have a higher thermal conductance than larger species. Thus
column6: small endotherms may be able to cope better with rising ambient temperatures than larger species (). Overall the interplay of body size
column7: BMR and heat dissipation capacities allows endotherms to occupy a variety of environmental conditions ().

column1: The findings of the present study support the hypothesis that the capacity to dissipate heat is an important constraint for the energetics of endotherms (). Recently
column2: highlighted the importance of rodents’ ability to dissipate heat in order to manage endogenous heat load and suggested that endotherm species (particularly non-tropical rodents) tend to increase their thermal conductance during the summer season (e.g.
column3: by seasonal changes in their insulation via fur). Such an increase in conductance should also be a means to increase their Tuc. Other studies also suggest that endotherm species do indeed raise their Tuc during warmer months by lowering their basal metabolic rates (). Without such plastic changes in Tuc
column4: endotherms may find it difficult to cope with the temporal variability in dry and hot conditions and as a result would frequently experience hyperthermia
column5: which may have serious consequences for their survival (; ). Therefore
column6: hot and dry conditions
column7: as they are projected under future climate change scenarios for many regions
column8: would probably favour species with lower mass-independent BMR and higher Tuc

column1: All else being equal
column2: it should be advantageous for endotherms to achieve extreme metabolic critical temperatures (i.e.
column3: very low Tlc and very high Tuc) because wide thermal limits will allow endotherms to maintain BMR across a wide range of environmental temperatures
column4: and thereby conserve energy (; ). However
column5: TNZs in endotherms are not infinitely wide; instead
column6: Tlc and Tuc are positively correlated (; ; ). This indicates that there are constrains for changing between low minimum conductance (i.e.
column7: low Tlc) and high maximum conductance (i.e.
column8: high Tuc) and thereby to alter the TNZ dynamically or adaptively to suit variable or changing climatic conditions. Specifically
column9: in order to tolerate cold temperatures an endotherm requires a high energy level and low thermal conductance which is disadvantageous under higher ambient temperatures (). This trade-off is supported by findings that the breadth of the TNZ of small endotherms varies seasonally which may be a mechanism to conserve energy (; ).

column1: Our findings on the relationship of metabolic critical temperatures with BMR may contribute to a better understanding of the relationships between species’ energy budget and their environment
column2: especially under changing climatic conditions. To off-set the negative effects of rising temperatures
column3: endotherms may have at least two avenues for adaptation: (i) the alteration of heat dissipation capacities e.g.
column4: via altering body insulation ()
column5: or (ii) achieving higher levels of metabolic critical temperatures. Endothermic species
column6: particularly those in the tropics
column7: might face the challenge of hyperthermia during periods of high ambient temperatures (see also ). Therefore
column8: considering the heat dissipation capacities of endotherms may significantly improve assessments of species’ vulnerability to climatic change.

column1: 44|10.7717/peerj.5919|Geophysical upheavals and evolutionary diversification of plant species in the Himalaya| Manish  Kumar |2018-11-7|"Mountain regions comprise the large majority of the global biodiversity hotspots and it is argued that species diversification is associated with mountain building through changes in landscape and climate followed by formation of varied and heterogeneous habitats along the elevational gradients (). It is equally well established that nearly all the mountains on the Earth have experienced a variety of geophysical upheavals in the geological past (). For relatively younger mountain systems such as the Himalaya and Mount Kinabalu
column2: their progress as biodiverse landscapes has also been shown to be the result of various geophysical upheavals (; ). Formation of the Himalaya started in early Cenozoic Era around 55–50 million years ago (Mya) with the collision of the Indian and the Eurasian plates (; )
column3: an event considered as one of the greatest geophysical episodes in the Earth’s history (; ; ). The periodic orogenic events led to physiographic and environmental changes (e.g.
column4: formation of land bridges
column5: development of monsoon
column6: formation of glaciers and establishment of an elaborate perennial river drainage system) and served as key drivers of the newly evolving ecosystems resulting in geographical isolation of taxa
column7: vicariance
column8: and evolutionary divergence of life forms (). Thus
column9: the interconnectedness between geophysical and biological components of the Himalayan ecosystems needs to be unraveled to develop insights into understanding of the build-up of its biodiversity.
