column8: the Himalaya extends from Namcha Barwa mountain range in India’s east to Nanga Parbat massif in the west forming an arc of about 2
column9: 400 km (). Geologically
column10: Himalaya is divided into four distinct litho-tectonic and physiographic units from north to south
column11: namely Outer Himalaya or Siwaliks

column21: 000 m (; ). Eco-climatically
column22: the Himalaya is broadly classified into Eastern and Western Himalaya. The Eastern Himalaya (EH) stretches from 21°–25°N latitudes across the east of Kali Gandaki valley encompassing eastern Nepal
column23: north-eastern Indian states of Sikkim

column27: Indian states of Uttarakhand

column31: 000 mm while the WH is comparatively drier with an annual average rainfall of 75–150 mm (see ). The rainfall plays a major role in determining the east–west bioclimatic gradient of the Himalaya. Some authors have designated a part of the mountain range as the Central Himalaya (; ; ). CH extends from river Kali in the east to river Tons (largest tributary of Yamuna river) in the west encompassing central Nepal and central Uttarakhand (India).

column1: The total number of higher plant species in the Himalaya varies from 8
column3: 000 with about 40% of these taxa as endemics (). It is well known that majority of the Himalayan flora consists of immigrated plant taxa that have evolved and diversified over millions of years following the Himalayan formation (; ; ; ). It is
column4: therefore
column5: of significant interest to evolutionary biologists as to how starting with an immigrant flora
column6: the Himalaya now harbors such a high number of plant endemics. This question
column7: though fascinating
column8: has not been much investigated or discussed in ecological literature. To address this knowledge gap
column9: we need to develop an understanding of the intricate relationship between geodynamic processes of the Himalayan mountain building and its varied biodiversity gradients. To the best of our knowledge
column10: there are only limited studies that have attempted to understand this relationship in an integrated manner (; ; ; ). The large majority of studies on the Himalaya have mostly focused on the evolutionary consequences of a specific geological period () or concentrated on a specific geographic region (; ). Hence
column11: a broader and a more comprehensive understanding of the evolutionary diversification of Himalayan flora is warranted. In this contribution
column12: we seek to address this knowledge gap by evolving a sequence of plant species divergence episodes during major geological periods in the Himalaya and identifying their relationship with the environmental changes in the region. An overarching goal of this study was to analyze the existing published information on Himalayan plant diversity and understand the intricate relationship between the build-up of plant diversity and physical-climatological variations produced by the geophysical changes during various phases of the Himalaya’s formation.

column1: Indian continent was once a part of Gondwanaland—a supercontinent formed nearly 600 Mya (). Gondwanaland covered much of the Southern Hemisphere comprising the present day South America
column2: Africa
column3: Madagascar
column4: Seychelles
column5: India
column6: Australia
column7: and Antarctica. Gondwanaland split around 180 Mya as a result of sea-floor spreading and development of a series of oceanic deep-seated mantle plumes resulting in Western Gondwana (comprising Africa and South America) and Eastern Gondwana (comprising Madagascar
column8: India
column9: Seychelles
column10: Australia and Antarctica). Around 120 Mya
column11: the split of Western Gondwana led to separation of South America from Africa
column12: and another fragment containing India-Madagascar-Seychelles (IMS) separated from Antarctica and Australia (). The newly separated IMS fragment migrated northward across the Tethys ocean towards the Eurasian continent at varying speeds ranging between 5–40 cm/year (). The drifting IMS fragment carried along a host of primitive flora of Gondwanan origin such as seed ferns (Glossopteris
column13: Dicroidium
column14: Sphenobaiera
column15: Linguifolium)
column16: conifers (Heidiphyllum
column17: Voltziopsis
column18: araucarians and podocarps) and lycopods (Cyclomeia) (). Widespread seafloor spreading around 80–90 Mya further widened the central Indian Ocean and resulted in detachment of the Madagascar block from the IMS fragment (). The Indian-Seychelles plate that drifted at ∼5 cm/year suddenly tripled its speed to ∼15 cm/year
column19: the fastest recorded migration speed for any tectonic drift in the geological history (). During its northward traverse around 65 Mya
column20: the Deccan flood basalts erupted and repositioning of the western Indian Ocean spreading ridge occurred that led to the separation of Seychelles from the Indian plate (; ). Subsequently
column21: the Seychelles block stationed close to Africa while India continued migrating northward. Around 55–50 Mya (Early Eocene)
column22: the drifting Indian plate collided with Eurasia along the northeastern corner of Greater India with the collision progressing westwards until 40 Mya (; ; ). The India-Eurasia collision led to extensive deformation of the northern margin of Indian plate and a major portion of the Indian plate subducted underneath the Asian plate. The collision also led to the draining of the Tethys Sea and upliftment of the long settled Tethyan geosyncline coastal sediments as meta-sedimentary formations. The continental collision also laid the foundation of the youngest and loftiest mountain system of the world—the Himalaya.

column1: From the above account
column2: it is reasonable to guess that post separation from Gondwanaland
column3: the Indian plate may have been an isolated island continent for nearly 45 million years
column4: which could have created conditions for the evolution of a high endemic biodiversity. The fossil record of India
column5: however
column6: provides equivocal evidence on the extent of pre-Himalayan biotic endemism (see ). Fossil records belonging to the Upper Cretaceous to Lower Tertiary in the Deccan Intertrappean beds of Southern India reveal a mixed flora with wide geographical affinities ranging from disparate regions
column7: namely Africa (Palmocaulon hyphaeneoides
column8: Palmoxylon hyphaenoides)
column9: Australia (Eucalyptus dharmendrae
column10: Tristania confertoides)
column11: Madagascar (Palmoxylon ghughuense)
column12: and South America (Rodietes
column13: Cyclanthodendron) (see ; ). Presence of fossil taxa with such varied geographical affinities indicates the likelihood of biotic exchanges between these disjunct landmasses and also that India may not have been isolated in strict sense to induce high endemism (). Isolated or connected
column14: some researchers have reported presence of high endemic biodiversity on the Indian plate that likely developed during 45 million years of its isolation (see ). As such
column15: much of the diversity of Indian plate was decimated due to eruption of Deccan volcanoes around the Cretaceous-Tertiary boundary with bulk of these eruptions occurring in the early Paleocene between 67–65 Mya (; ). Notwithstanding these catastrophic events
column16: many ancient Gondwanan lineages did manage to survive and disperse into Asia when India collided with Eurasia (). The proponents of “out-of-India” hypothesis have referred to the Indian plate as a ‘raft’ for ferrying a number of taxa from Gondwanaland to mainland Asia (; ). The “out-of-India” hypothesis has received support from investigations of the plant family Crypteroniaceae suggesting that the family originated in west Gondwanaland and subsequently reached Asia by rafting on the Indian plate (). Recent fossil leaf impression data from genus Alphonsea (Annonaceae) from the Tertiary sediment deposits of Assam suggests that the genus originated in India during Late Oligocene and migrated to South East Asia via Myanmar during Early Miocene ().

column1: An immediate consequence of the collision of the Indian and Eurasian plates was the establishment of a contiguous landmass connecting Indian Peninsula with the Sino-Japanese regions in the north and the Malayan Archipelago in the southeast (). This landmass connectivity was the result of cessation of marine deposition and the beginning of terrestrial sedimentation in the suture zone of India-Eurasia collision (). A biological vacuum was created in the erstwhile nascent Himalayan ecosystems as a result of extinctions caused by Cretaceous-Tertiary volcanism event. This vacuum was gradually filled by large-scale floral migrations from the adjacent connected regions in the east
column2: north and south (). Thus
column3: the newly evolved Himalayan landscape started to serve as a ‘intercontinental biological highway’ for migrating flora from all directions (; ).

column2: Dipterocarpaceae

Majority of the early migrants which first crossed through the northeastern route (via present day Arunachal Pradesh) were the ones with largely Sino-Japanese and Malayan affinities such as Dalbergia

This first phase of plant migration lasted for almost 30 million years and it gradually stopped when connections were lost due to the uplift of Himalaya with a dissected topography as a result of climatic and morpho-tectonic changes in the subsequent epochs ().

column1: The monsoon played a decisive role in the landscape evolution of the Himalaya. The ensuing heavy rainfall along the frontal ranges of the Himalaya gave rise to numerous streams and rivers which transported vast quantities of sediments and regularly denuded the exposed rock surfaces leading to the formation of deeply incised valleys (). The landscape incision was more prominent in the EH than the WH because of its tropical latitudes ensuring prolonged and more intense period of rainfall (). As a result
column2: the EH region was transformed into a “region of extreme relief” due to closely clustered high
column3: steep mountains that were dissected by numerous river divides and deep valleys (). The monsoon
column4: therefore
column5: was key to transforming the Himalaya into a highly dissected landscape aided by the tectonic processes that accelerated weathering
column6: denudation and sediment transport driven mainly by precipitation (). Differentiated into numerous valleys dissected by rivers
column7: the fragmented Himalayan landscape after the uplift provided novel physical and physiological barriers for gene flow between once continuous plant populations (; ). The combined result of these geographic events and formation of geographic barriers to gene flow culminated in large-scale allopatric speciation and evolutionary diversifications of the Himalayan flora (; ). Various molecular phylogenetic studies provide evidence that many plant species complexes (Caragana
column8: Cyananthus
column9: Koenigia
column10: Meconopsis
column11: Rheum and Rhodiola) originated and diversified in the period after the onset and intensification of modern-day monsoon system in the Himalaya (see ). However
column12: argued that most of the time-calibrated molecular phylogenetic studies that link clade age of species between 15–0.5 Mya with specific uplift phases of the Tibetan plateau and its associated environmental effects assume that the Tibetan plateau underwent most major uplift during the Miocene
column13: whereas the Tibetan Plateau had already reached the height of 4
column15: 000 m in Paleogene (∼40 Mya). Extensive weathering
column16: erosion and detritus transport along the Himalayan slopes due to monsoon also aided in the formation of a foreland basin and transformation of the vegetation profile of the Himalayan from tropical wet evergreen forests of C3 plants to tall grassland ecosystems comprising predominantly of C4 plants around 8–6 Mya (). This shift from C3 to C4 dominated ecosystem was due to widespread global environmental changes in the Late Cenozoic Era such as global cooling and significant drop in global carbon dioxide (CO2) levels due to increased rates of chemical weathering and trapping of CO2 in the ocean sediments (; ). It has

column2: and geographic isolation and speciation through vicariance. Once these species complexes originated and diversified in the Himalaya

column4: Central Asia
column5: Mongolian plateau and Europe producing derivative species therein (; ).  proposed the term “Central Asiatic highland corridor” through which the flora originating in the Himalaya migrated northward to the Central Asian highlands. Examples of such emigrant taxa from the Himalaya are: Anaphalis (migrated to eastern Asia
column6: South East Asia and North America) ()
column7: Hippophae rhamnoides (migrated to Central Asia and Asia Minor) ()
column8: Lagotis (migrated to Central Asia and Arctic highlands) ()
column9: Leontopodium (migrated to European mountain ranges) ()
column10: Rhodiola (migrated to Northern Hemisphere regions such as Europe and Central Asia) () and Solms-laubachia (migrated to Hengduan Mts) (). The advent of the monsoon system is believed to have led to the formation of a mesic corridor in the Indian sub-continental region through which plant species such as Begonia migrated from Africa to Southeast Asia via the Himalaya ().

column1: Following the development of SW monsoon system
column2: aridity in the Central Asian region increased due to blockage of moisture laden winds by the uplift of the Himalaya and the Tibetan Plateau (). The aridification triggered the diversification of many plant species with xerophytic adaptation and colonization of the Tibetan Plateau. Specific examples of species divergence include split of arid palmate Frutescentes section from its sister clades in the Caragana species complex around 8–7 Mya (). Similarly
column3: Phyllolobium
column4: a genus diversified as a result of intense uplift
column5: cold climate
column6: and ensuing aridity in the Tibetan Plateau (; ). Central Asian aridification is also reported to have triggered the divergences of three lineages of Ephedra (eastern Tibetan Plateau
column7: southern Tibetan Plateau
column8: and northern China) (). Likewise
column9: the dominance of Artemisia in the present day vegetation of southwest and southeast regions of Tibetan Plateau is the result of rapid uplift of Tibetan Plateau and consequent prevalence of dry climate during and after the Late Miocene ().

column1: The Quaternary Period starting 2.6 Mya was characterized by expansive climatic fluctuations including repeated advance and retreat of glaciers with intermittent warming stages (; ). Almost the entire Northern Hemisphere including Europe
column2: North America and North-west Asia witnessed widespread glaciations with ice sheets descending as far south as to 40°N latitude (). However
column3: in the Himalaya
column4: glaciations were confined to mountain peaks and high elevations (). More importantly
column5: unlike other regions of the world
column6: there is no evidence of a uniform ice sheet covering the entire Himalayan range during the Quaternary glacial stages (). In fact
column7: most of the glaciers are reported to have advanced up to 10 km from their present-day ice margins during various stages of glaciation (; ). Thus
column8: the extent of glaciations in the Himalaya has been rather restricted as compared to the other mountain systems such as Alps and Andes
column9: most likely due to the tropical location of the mountain range ().

column1: Overall
column2: 3–4 significant glacial advances and retreats have been documented in different regions of the Himalaya during the Quaternary period: Tibetan Plateau (4)
column3: Zanskar ranges (3)
column4: Swat valley (3)
column5: Kanchenjunga ranges (4) and Khumbu mountains (4) (see  and references therein). Despite the limited extent of glaciation
column6: the advance and retreat of glaciers had a significant effect on the evolutionary diversification of plant species in the Himalaya (). Some species got extinct
column7: some dispersed to new warmer habitats in the south while some survived in glacial refugia in non-glaciated habitats. Habitats with patchy landscape distribution and diverse environmental conditions offered spaces as refugial habitats for numerous plant species. Examples of refugial habitats include low elevation areas of Tibetan Plateau (Aconitum gymnandrum
column8: Juniperus tibetica
column9: Primula secundiflora)
column10: south-eastern edge of Tibetan Plateau (Potentilla fruticosa
column11: Metagentiana striata)

column13: Tsuga dumosa
column14: Juniperus przewalskii
column15: Sinopodophyllum hexandrum) (; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ). Moreover
column16: multiple refugia for a single plant species have also been reported such as for Pomatosace filicula ()
column17: Hippophae neurocarpa ()
column18: Hippophae tibetana ()
column19: Rhododendron simsii () and Aconitum gymnandrum (). Development of multiple refugia was facilitated by the steep elevational gradient of the Himalaya that allowed various taxa to rapidly disperse to lower elevation habitats after traversing short geographic distances (). Multiple recolonization events from numerous refugia have been reported after the glacial retreat (; ; ). As a result of episodic glacial advances and retreats
column20: the geographic ranges of the refugial plant taxa underwent repeated contractions and expansions. Consequently
column21: the multiple recolonization events resulted in frequent mixing of floral taxa from different refugia followed by periodic hybridizations
column22: adaptive radiations and speciation resulting in greater species diversity (). Hybridizations post recolonization events from refugia have been cited as an important mechanism for the colonizing success of Rhododendron (; )
column23: Meconopsis ()
column24: and Pinus densata (). Earlier studies have reported that when previously isolated populations come in contact with each other due to large scale glacial dynamics
column25: hybridization followed by polyploidy is a common phenomenon resulting in species divergence (). There is evidence of colonization success and dominance of polyploids in post-Pleistocene plant taxa in the Himalaya (; ).

column1: Pollen data from the Pleistocene epoch of Uttarakhand region in the WH indicates that evergreen oaks (Quercus semecarpifolia) and alder (Alnus) dominated the Himalayan landscapes around 0.0078 Mya when climate conditions were cold and wet with moderate monsoon and were subsequently replaced by conifers (Pinus and Abies) around 0.0066 Mya when climate became warmer (; ). This indicates that repeated climatic oscillations in the Quaternary influenced the vegetation composition of the Himalaya. It has also been postulated that during each glaciation phase

column1: The present-day Himalayan ecosystems exhibit a pronounced post-Pleistocene biodiversity
column2: but retain the mixed biotic character of having immigrant elements from different biogeographic regions. Biogeographically
column3: the Himalaya is a transitional zone located at the cusp of three biogeographic realms
column4: namely Palearctic
column5: Afrotropical and Indo-Malayan and Oriental realms in the north
column6: southwest and southeast
column7: respectively (see ). As a result of this unique geographic location
column8: the Himalaya is home to numerous Austro-Polynesian
column9: Sino-Tibetan
column10: Euro-Mediterranean and Malayo-Burman biotic elements. A simplified plant biodiversity profile occupying different Himalayan zones
column11: viz. Western
column12: Central and Eastern Himalaya is presented in . The elevational limits of different vegetation zones are slightly higher by 300–400 m in the Eastern Himalaya than the Western Himalaya (). The principal reason for such an elevational zone shift is the latitudinal difference of nearly 10° between the Eastern and Western Himalaya (see ).

column1: Overall
column2: Western Himalaya is characterized by coniferous forests of deodar
column3: pines and silver fir
column4: while Eastern Himalaya shows conspicuous presence of broad-leaved forests of oaks
column5: Rhododendrons and maples (Source: Adapted from ; ; ; ).

column1: The development of present-day plant biodiversity in the Himalaya has largely been shaped by the climate of the region. In the tropical and sub-tropical zones (up to 1
column3: temperature varies from 6 °C to 35 °C in various areas while rainfall variation is from 1
column5: 500 mm. These climate conditions are well suited for growth of deciduous and semi-deciduous vegetation. The temperate zone (1
column7: 500 m) experiences an average annual rainfall of 2
column8: 400 mm while temperature varies between 5–26 °C. Thus
column9: mainly coniferous forests dominate the temperate zone in the Himalaya. Low temperatures characterize the sub-alpine and alpine zones (3
column10: 500 m and above) and the precipitation takes place mainly in the form of snowfall
column11: except in the summer
column12: which lasts for only three months. Thus
column13: only plant communities with specialized adaptations to these harsh conditions can survive in the sub-alpine and alpine zones
column14: such as cushion forming communities (Arenaria polytrichoides
column15: Anaphalis cavei)
column16: tussocks or tufts (Kobresia schoenoides
column17: Carex parva)
column18: solifluction acrobats (Gentiana urnula
column19: Eriophytum wallichii)
column20: and dense woolly forms (Saussurea graminifolia
column21: Glechoma nivalis).

column1: The geophysical upheavals associated with the formation of the Himalaya led to significant climate changes
column2: new geophysical environments
column3: novel ecological niches and formation of physical and physiological isolation barriers that acted as natural selection sieves. These geophysical changes including onset of monsoon
column4: glaciation and glacial advance
column5: and retreat brought about adaptive radiations in the plant taxa. The Himalayan landforms were initially colonized by migrant plant taxa from the neighboring biogeographic regions and in due course
column6: these early taxa established gene exchanges among them and formed new variants that were highly adaptive to the changing physiography and climate of the area. Once the final phase of the Himalayan uplift concluded
column7: the environmental conditions became suitable for the migrant species and the variants to evolve and diversify into new taxa. Disjunct taxa distributions were brought about by the fragmented landscape
column8: while new physical and physiological barriers limited the expansion of species ranges. Newly formed steep gorges and valleys imposed isolation barriers on the recently migrated species enhancing the processes and rates of evolutionary divergence. As a net result
column9: many endemic taxa evolved and the Himalaya became a repository of unique assemblages of plant species. Thus
column10: having started as a ‘sink’ and a ‘biological highway’
column11: the Himalaya turned into a geographical barrier
column12: promoting vicariance
column13: species diversification and endemism. However
column14: only a few studies have empirically tested the above hypotheses and those that have are based on only specific taxa. It is well known that different patterns of diversification may exist for different taxa. Therefore
column15: many of the stated hypotheses here may appear as conjectures. Many unanswered questions still remain
column16: that include: (i) what was the nature of early migrants in the Himalaya; (ii) what were the ancestral distributional areas of these migrants; (iii) what were the precise time periods during which maximum migrations and endemic diversification occurred in the Himalaya; and (iv) were the migrations and diversifications widespread across phylogenies? A complementary and integrative collaboration between researchers from varied backgrounds in earth sciences
column17: atmospheric sciences
column18: palaeobiology
column19: biogeography and ecology are needed to solve these evolutionary riddles in an important global biodiversity hotspot—the Himalaya.

column1: In the present study

column4: we analysed morphological data to test for congruent variation between genetic groups. We hypothesized that H. ascotanensis would show high genetic structure due to geographical and environmental influences

column1: The Ascotán saltpan is one of the closed basins located in the Chilean Altiplano (Región de Antofagasta) between 21.2–21.4°S and 68.1–68.2°W at 3
column2: 760 m a.s.l. The hydrological system extends around 33 km long by 16 km wide at its maximum range

column4: whose main water body (18 km2) is generated by a series of springs located mainly in the eastern area of the system (). These springs are fed by underground water coming from the contiguous basin Pastos Grandes Caldera located at 4
column5: 600 m altitude (; ; ). On the eastern margin
column6: twelve springs are isolated or partially isolated by vast areas of evaporitic lands that constitute topographic barriers difficult to cross for the aquatic organisms
column7: so connectivity among springs and populations that inhabiting them is reduced. These springs are considered thermal since the water temperature varies among 16–22 ° C

column1: The results of the current study revealed that H. ascotanensis has a high phylogeographic structure
column2: even when its distribution encompass only a few kilometres. Using diverse analysis to examine spatial and temporal patterns of mtDNA sequence variation and morphological differentiation
column3: a complex pattern of historical and demographic events was uncovered in this endemic snail. Three main findings were revealed from the present study: (i) a pronounced mtDNA sequence differentiation at small spatial scale; (ii) complex historical and demographic influences on sequence divergence and diversity within and among springs and; (iii) an incipient morphological differentiation coupled to genetic divergence.

column1: Whilst some springs contained highly similar genetic snails

This pattern suggests that in these springs exist two highly genetically structured groups that probably have different evolutionary histories. We propose two main hypotheses to explain this pattern. The first and most probable hypothesis suggests that occurred an allopatric divergence followed by a range expansion leading to secondary contact among the two genetic groups detected. The second hypothesis proposes a local adaptation accompanied by genetic divergence (i.e.
column3: an in situ differentiation); but considering that the saltpan was a continuous hydrological system in the past
column4: and that afterwards the passing of humid periods to a dry season the system was divided into isolated or partially isolated springs suggest that this is a less feasible scenario. The range expansion considered in the first hypothesis may be due to dispersal mediated by high rainfalls seasons during SASM () that could connect springs close enough to allow genetic flow between populations (as proposed by )
column5: subterranean hydrological connections undetected or passive dispersal mediated by birds as has been reported in similar freshwater snails from desert hydrological systems in Australia (; ; ). The results suggest that genetic differentiation is not only a response to a physiographic imposition
column6: but also an active reaction to climatic changes
column7: and additionally
column8: modulated by biological properties (such as dispersal capacities) of the organism. This pattern has been described previously in diverse freshwater taxa confined to hydrological systems located in arid regions (; ; ; ).

column1: Finally

column3: and environmental impositions (such as aridity) influence the genetic pool or structure in different taxa

column1: In this study
column2: we provide the first phylogeographical investigation on a species of the genus Heleobia from the Southern Altiplano. We present evidence of a high genetic structure in H. ascotanensis. Molecular analyses detected three main haplogroups well differentiated and related with the saltpan physiography. Gene flow and demographic analyses showed different connectivity and histories among populations
column3: indicating that each haplogroup responded independently to the fragmentation of the aquatic system occurred during humid-arid transitions that characterized the Plio-Pleistocene period. Morphometric analyses detected a subtle differentiation
column4: and this variation is concomitant with genetic divergence found in Spring 11
column5: the southernmost spring. The genetic structure found in H. ascotanensis is similar to the pattern detected in co-distributed taxa; however
column6: temporal and spatial differences among species populations indicate that each taxon (or population) responded differently to the same historic and contemporary climatic changes. Further work in H. ascotanensis should focus on increasing analysed loci (besides markers with a uniparental mechanism of inheritance as COI)
column7: a strategy that would be helpful for evaluating in detail the mode and tempo of the genetic differentiation detected in this species.

column2: South Africa| Cowling  Richard M. |2023-12-12|"The sedimentary deposits associated with Holocene coastal dunes are regionally and globally rare habitats ()

column6: notably coastal protection
column7: erosion control
column8: water retention and purification

column11: with human pressure on coastal environments increasing drastically in recent decades (). This surge in human activities—urbanization
column12: agriculture

The composition of fixed coastal dune vegetation of the Cape south coast and elsewhere is influenced by several geographical contingencies
column2: namely dune area and topography (; )
column3: composition and ecology of hinterland vegetation (; )
column4: sediment age (; ) and the extent of available space for accommodating dune habitat on land exposed during glacial periods (). Long-distance oceanic dispersal plays a relatively minor role in structuring the vegetation of fixed Holocene dunes; most species are derived via colonization from hinterland floras (; ).

column1: The delimitation of vegetation units on coastal dunes on the Cape south coast is ripe for re-analysis. The current vegetation map (; ) is not based on formal floristic analysis and the mapped units frequently combine vegetation on Holocene dunes with that on older (Pleistocene and Neogene)
column2: more weathered and calcium-poor sands. Producing a defensible classification of the dune vegetation is important for habitat protection
column3: since conservation priority status in South Africa is based on the extent to which area-based targets can be or have been achieved in untransformed habitat (; ). Thus
column4: vegetation types where there is no longer sufficient intact habitat to achieve a target (say 20% of the original extent of habitat) emerge as conservation priorities
column5: whereas those where untransformed habitat greatly exceeds the target
column6: are regarded as “Least Threatened”. It follows that an accurate map of vegetation types influences significantly their conservation status.

column1: The extent of Cenozoic sediments in the CFR is inadequately delimited (). The assignment of stratigraphic units was traditionally biased towards bedrock in the compilation of geological maps in South Africa (e.g.
column2: see the 3322 Oudtshoorn sheet: ). The recent availability of remote-sensing datasets to assist mapping ()
column3: a clearer understanding of onshore-offshore depositional processes influencing coastal sedimentation (e.g.
column4: ; ) and integrated approaches in research (; ; )
column5: have greatly improved our ability to map these sediments and understand their sedimentation dynamics. The high specialization of Cape plants to the edaphic idiosyncrasies of these Cenozoic deposits () offers a hitherto neglected opportunity to use vegetation as an indicator of sediment age. We explore this theme here
column6: using the highly distinctive flora and vegetation of unconsolidated
column7: calcareous sediments of the Cape south coast as a proxy for delimitation of Holocene dunes.

column1: Mindful of the deficiencies in the current delimitation of the coastal dune vegetation of the CFR
column2: our primary aim for this paper is to provide a defensible scheme and map of units based on detailed floristic analysis. Our methodology for identifying
column3: describing and mapping Cape south coast dune vegetation differs from that of previous attempts in three important ways: (1) we provide the first unified treatment of dune vegetation on the southern coast of the CFR; (2) our vegetation units are based on quantified floristic composition
column4: with numerically defensible distinctions based on rigorous analysis; (3) our spatial delimitation of units is not reliant on geological maps. Further details on our approach are provided below. A secondary aim of the study is to interpret these vegetation patterns in terms of geographical gradients
column5: exposure to fire
column6: soil moisture status and sediment age. We also discuss the conservation implications of our findings.

column1: Available depositional ages and lithologies of correlated geological formations of the Algoa Group and Bredasdorp Group are indicated. Modified from .

The study area extends from Cape Recife in the Eastern Cape to Cape Hangklip in the Western Cape and is nested within the Cape Floristic Region (CFR) (). It coincides with coastal parts of the Southeastern and Bredasdorp-Riversdale centres of endemism of the CFR. Geologically
column2: the area incorporates the western sector of the Algoa Group and the full extent of the Bredasdorp Group
column3: both comprising Cenozoic coastal sediments (; ).

column1: Coastal dunes occupy ca. 2
column2: 200 km2 in the CFR
column3: constituting just over 2% of the region (). In the study area
column4: coastal dunes cover ca. 680 km2
column5: although only ca. 620 km2 of this comprises stable
column6: vegetated dunes (). The largest vegetated dune systems occur in the embayments
column7: where sediment accumulation is more favourable than the cliffed swaths of coast. These dune sediment depocentres are Walker Bay
column8: the Wilderness-Sedgefield embayment
column9: Oyster Bay to St Francis Bay
column10: and Sardinia Bay to Algoa Bay. Generally
column11: the Cape south coast half-heart bays coincide with underlying Bokkeveld Group shales
column12: whereas the erosion-resistant headlands that bound them are composed of Table Mountain Group sandstones and quartzites.

column1: The unconsolidated sediments of the Holocene dune landscapes comprise coarse to medium grained
column2: freely draining sands with a high carbonate content (; ). These attain thicknesses up to 100 m or more but are often shallower coastwards where the underlying geology—mostly Pleistocene aeolianites accreted on the basal bedrock—is often closer to the surface. Two formations are recognised: the Strandveld Formation (Bredasdorp Group) between Walker Bay and Plettenberg Bay ()
column3: and Schelm Hoek Formation (Algoa Group) between Tsitsikamma River and Cape Recife () (; ).

column1: These deposits were laid down primarily since the Last Glacial Maximum (LGM) during the Postglacial Marine Transgression (). Most of the current extent of these sediments
column2: however
column3: is currently underwater on the continental shelf (; ). The neocoastal deposits currently preserved onshore date back to ∼13 ka () but mostly started accumulating after the Holocene highstand
column4: at ca 6 ka (; ; ). Optically Stimulated Luminescence (OSL) dates from the seaward dune ridge at Sedgefield yield ages ranging from 6.1–0.5 ka
column5: suggesting continuous dune building during this period (). However
column6: coastal dunes on the Agulhas Plain postdate the highstand
column7: having dates of 4.1 to 4.7 ka
column8: with one site dated to 0.8 ka (). Continuous dune building is evident today where most of the coastal dunefields of the Cape south coast include tracts of mobile and semi-mobile dunes
column9: many being headland-bypass dunes that were active prior to artificial stabilization between the mid-19th and mid-20th centuries (; ).

column1: Throughout the region
column2: parabolic dunes with long trailing arms aligned with the prevailing wind directions (W-E) are the dominant dune morphology; running parallel to the coast
column3: these form a series of parallel ridges (“barrier dunes”) and troughs that cover the dune landscape. In the Wilderness-Sedgefield embayment and west of Oyster Bay

column5: respectively (; ).  calls these wind rift dunes. Hummock-blowouts and playa lunette dunes are uniquely found in the high wind energy environment west of Cape Agulhas (). More detail on dune morphology is given in the vegetation descriptions below.

column1: The soils of Holocene coastal dunes are relatively deep
column2: except in the few places where calcarenites
column3: cemented duricrusts and Table Mountain Group rocks outcrop. The soils are alkaline (Ph 7-8)
column4: and well- to excessively-drained
column5: coarse- to medium-grained sands with high content of shell material (; ; ). Phosphate levels are high (10-40 ppm available P) in comparison to most other CFR soils
column6: and base saturation is also high
column7: driven mainly by calcium carbonate. Organic carbon and total nitrogen is considerably higher under thicket than fynbos vegetation
column8: owing to the higher input of litter and higher shade beneath the thicket canopy (; ).

column1: Coastal dune soils differ markedly from those of the Pleistocene and Neogene coastal sediments exposed landward of the Holocene cordon (; ). These older dune substrates comprise two main groups: skeletal sands (0–0.3 m) on calcarenites
column2: and deep (>1.0 m)
column3: unconsolidated sands. The skeletal sands
column4: like those of coastal dunes
column5: are calcareous
column6: alkaline and relatively rich in P. They differ in that they are everywhere shallow. The unconsolidated sands differ from coastal dune sands in that they are more weathered
column7: finer grained
column8: and with better water holding capacity. They differ further in being neutral to weakly acid and having lower levels of all soil nutrients. As we detail below
column9: the vegetation on the older sediments is different floristically and structurally from coastal dune vegetation
column10: there being very high to complete turnover (beta diversity) of species across the edaphic boundary (; ).

column1: The climate varies from winter-rainfall (Cape Hangklip to Arniston) to a bimodal regime for the remainder of the study area that shows peaks in spring and autumn () (; ). While the proportion of warm-season rainfall (summer –autumn) increases eastwards
column2: this period corresponds with the highest drought stress for plants throughout the region. Thus
column3: the rainfall regime can be characterised as Mediterranean to sub-Mediterranean. Mean annual rainfall for Holocene dune cordons is highest in the Wilderness-Sedgefield area (ca. 800 mm at Goukamma) and between Cape St Francis and the eastern Tsitsikamma (700–900 mm). Elsewhere
column4: it is lower: 550–600 mm between Cape Recife (Gqeberha) and the Gamtoos River
column5: and <500 mm between the Groot Brak River and Danger Point. Temperature regimes show little annual variation: mean midsummer temperatures range from 18 to 22 °C and mean midwinter temperatures from 14 to 16 °C
column6: and the region is frost free. Wind regimes are fierce with numerous strong- to gale force winds in summer (from the east) and in winter (from the west). The central region
column7: between Mossel Bay and Tsitsikamma
column8: has a calmer wind regime ().

column1: This region forms part of the CFR
column2: the second richest plant biodiversity hotspot globally (). Coastal dune floras of the region are rich
column3: despite the low ecological heterogeneity of dune landscapes as well as their small areas (). Indeed
column4: they are as rich in species as some floras of the coastal hinterland that are associated with ancient sediments
column5: and considerably richer than dune floras from other Mediterranean Climate Regions
column6: except for some sites in the Mediterranean Basin (). Like Holocene dune floras of other Mediterranean Climate Regions
column7: dominant families on CFR dunes are Asteraceae
column8: Poaceae and Fabaceae; the high incidence of species belonging to Iridaceae
column9: Aizoaceae and Scrophulariaceae is a distinctive feature of Cape dune floras (). Almost half the species are endemic to the Greater Cape Floristic Region (CFR plus the Succulent Karoo)
column10: and ca. 40% are endemic to the CFR. Species endemic to calcareous substrata (dunes and calcarenites) comprise up to 30% of local floras and are overwhelmingly of Cape affinity. Most are dwarf shrubs and geophytes.  have attributed the surprisingly high diversity and endemism of the floras south Cape Holocene dunes—relative to the small and fragmented contemporary area of dune landscape—to the extensive distribution of coastal dunes on the Palaeo-Agulhas Plain that was variously exposed during the glacial periods that prevailed during the Pleistocene.

column1: The major biomes associated with Cape Holocene coastal dunes are Fynbos and Subtropical Thicket; there are also minor occurrences of Grassland (in the east)
column2: Forest and Wetland biomes (; ). Most species (40–60%) in local floras are associated with the Fynbos Biome
column3: followed by the Subtropical Thicket biome (10–20%). The vegetation of Holocene coastal dunes comprises a mosaic of open-canopy fynbos and closed-canopy subtropical thicket. The former is dominated by ericoid shrubs and restioids of Cape affinity and prevails in fire-exposed and edaphically dry sites; the latter is dominated by broad-leaved shrubs and low trees of tropical affinity and grows in moist and fire-protected sites where it can attain forest stature (; ; ; ). Biomass of dune fynbos is dominated by species endemic to Holocene dunes
column4: although non-endemics comprise most of the species growing there (). Dune thicket
column5: comprising lineages of tropical origin
column6: is a distinctive formation that includes several species endemic to the coastal dunes of the CFR (; ).

column1: Like fynbos of inland habitats ()
column2: the fynbos component of southern Cape dune vegetation is highly fire-prone and comprises numerous non-sprouting species that recruit from seed only after fire (; ; ; ). While dune thicket (and forest) is best developed in fire-free sites
column3: most component species
column4: especially dune endemics
column5: resprout readily after fire (; ) and are resilient to even high-intensity burns (; ).

column1: In this study
column2: we employed a phytosociological approach to identify and describe the different vegetation units that occur on coastal dunes of the Cape south coast and to inform mapping their distributions in the study area. Recent attempts at delimiting these plant associations (; ; ; ) did not include formal floristic analyses
column3: resulting in disparate classification at the biome level and inappropriate combination/splitting of vegetation units across/within edaphic substrata and physiographic units. While ’s () treatment of Fynbos Biome vegetation recognised the Walker Bay to Oyster Bay part of our study area as belonging to a coherent unit—South Strandveld—this treatment excluded coastal dunes occurring eastward to Cape Recife that share several characteristic species with the remainder of this region (; ). Similarly
column4: ’s (; see also ) treatment of coastal dune vegetation considered only that stretch of coast between the Duiwenhoks River and Cape Recife and excluded the coastal dunes westward to Cape Hangklip. This currently fragmented treatment of southern Cape coastal dune vegetation is further marred by inherent methodological differences: circumscription of Fynbos Biome vegetation units (“Western Strandveld”; )

column1: Phytosociological studies provide major advantages for classifying vegetation over an approach based on expert opinion and geology
column2: as was used by  for mapping and describing the vegetation of Cape coastal dunes. Firstly
column3: these studies offer a more objective and systematic approach to vegetation classification by relying on quantitative data and statistical analyses
column4: thus reducing biases and inconsistencies that may arise from relying on individual expert judgments. Secondly
column5: phytosociological studies provide a more comprehensive and detailed description of vegetation communities by undertaking a thorough inventory of species and their relative abundances
column6: thus facilitating more accurate and precise classification. Thirdly
column7: phytosociological studies can provide a more repeatable and standardized approach to vegetation classification
column8: allowing for consistent application of methods and criteria across different sites and regions
column9: and facilitating comparisons and generalizations across studies (). Overall
column10: phytosociological studies offer a rigorous and scientific approach to vegetation classification that can yield more reliable and informative results compared to expert-opinion approaches.

column1: Our data on floristic composition of coastal dune vegetation stems from two primary sources
column2: namely: (1) relevé data collected as part of previous phytosociological studies in the Cape south coast that included vegetated coastal dunes; and (2) relevé data collected by us during a recent vegetation survey of the study area (during the austral spring to early summer season of 2018 and 2019).

column1: After collating the above data
column2: we identified seven dune areas along the Cape south coast—an area that has hosted relatively few phytosociological studies ()—for which floristic data were conspicuously lacking. These included the dune cordons at the following locations: Cape Recife south of Gqeberha; between the Gamtoos and Kabeljous rivers; Hartenbos east of Mossel Bay; Vleesbaai west of Mossel Bay; Geelkrans near Still Bay; Arniston east of Cape Agulhas; and Brandfontein west of Cape Agulhas (). Approval to access the dune cordon near Arniston was granted by the Denel Overberg Test Range. These areas represent samples of dune cordons of various sizes and configurations
column3: but all include moderate to large areas of stable
column4: vegetated dunes. In line with the generally accepted protocol for phytosociological classification and description of vegetation in South Africa (; )
column5: we conducted vegetation surveys at each of these sites following the Zürich-Montpellier (Braun-Blanquet) approach (; ). Stratified random placement of sample plots was employed to ensure that floristically and environmentally homogeneous stands of vegetation were sampled
column6: with plots primarily stratified between stands of the fynbos and thicket cover states. Plots were located in vegetation without recent disturbances
column7: although this was not possible for Cape Recife as the entire dune landscape burned during two successive wildfires in 2018 and 2019. For fynbos
column8: we sampled plots of 5 × 5 m (25 m2)
column9: and 10 × 10 m (100 m2) for thicket (). Within each plot
column10: the total vascular plant flora was recorded and cover-abundance values
column11: using the modified Braun-Blanquet scale ()
column12: were estimated for each species. We sampled a total of 58 plots during our survey
column13: including 45 in fynbos and 13 in thicket ().

column1: Our final dataset
column2: combining vegetation plot data from our field survey with those gleaned from the literature

column4: 73 thicket) from 10 coastal dune sites (). In total

column1: We used non-metric multidimensional scaling (NMDS)
column2: implemented with the ‘metaMDS’ function of the vegan v. 2.6.2 () R v. 4.2.1 statistical software () package
column3: to ordinate our vegetation samples. Prior to ordination
column4: data were subjected to Wisconsin double standardization (scaled to zero mean and unit variance) and square-root transformation. The NMDS used Bray-Curtis dissimilarity as a distance metric along two dimensions (k = 2). We then used standard procedures (; )
column5: as previously described in
column6: to complete the ordination analysis. Specifically
column7: the ordination was run 999 times with random starts to prevent the NMDS from becoming trapped in local optima
column8: and the solution with minimal stress was then selected. To facilitate interpretation of the results
column9: the final NMDS solution was centred and rotated by principal components so that the variance of points was maximised along the first NMDS axis
column10: which allows inference about major gradients between ordinated samples. The resulting ordination plots were examined to identify any clustering or separation of vegetation samples.

Synoptic tables indicating species constancy

column1: In characterising Holocene age dune vegetation of the Cape south coast
column2: we have invoked the Strandveld concept (). Strandveld (literally “beach vegetation”) is a term embedded in the Cape Afrikaans vernacular dating back to the Dutch settlement of the Cape west and south coasts
column3: starting in the 17th Century. An alternative term is Duineveld (“dune vegetation”). As it appears on maps and from published accounts
column4: Strandveld refers to areas of coastal
column5: calcareous
column6: white (“wit”) sands along the western part of the south Cape coast
column7: and the beige sands of the eastern part. It is distinct from Sandveld (“sand vegetation”) which refers to the vegetation on coastal Pleistocene sands immediately inland of the Strandveld
column8: where the neutral to acid sands are mostly weathered yellow to reddish brown and the vegetation is invariably Sand Fynbos (; ; ). We suggest using Strandveld to denote the vegetation of the Holocene calcareous sands of the Cape coast. Strandveld has poorly developed
column9: quick draining soils with high levels of exchangeable cations and relatively high levels of P and N; these support a vegetation dominated by shrubs comprising mosaics and mixtures of fynbos and thicket species. The older sands supporting Sand Fynbos are more weathered
column10: finer-grained
column11: more water retentive and less fertile than the Strandveld sands. While Sand Fynbos west of the Breede River mouth has a minor thicket component
column12: this is not the case to the east where dune thicket with forest patches in moist
column13: fire-protected sites
column14: forms an important component of the vegetation (–).

column1: Thus defined
column2: our Strandveld concept excludes the vegetation termed Strandveld by  that is associated with granite
column3: calcarenite and Pleistocene sands on the west coast but includes the vegetation on Holocene sands (Witsand Formation of the Sandveld Group). Being floristically dominated by fynbos taxa
column4: and thus belonging to the Fynbos Biome
column5: the concept also excludes vegetation of the Holocene dunes north of Elands Bay (where succulent karoo taxa replace fynbos lineages) () and east of Algoa Bay (where thicket prevails and where depauperate fynbos is confined to ecologically demanding sites
column6: such as dune slipfaces and excessively-drained ridges) (; ). This vegetation is best placed in the Succulent Karoo and Subtropical Thicket biomes
column7: respectively. The vegetation on coastal cliffs composed of granites
column8: sandstones and cemented aeolianites
column9: that are mapped as Strandveld by
column10: are also excluded from our Strandveld concept. These sites
column11: which are under strong maritime influence and support a wind-pruned vegetation comprising a diverse array of species tolerant of exposure to salt-laden winds ()
column12: are best included in the azonal Cape Seashore Vegetation concept
column13: a heterogeneous assemblage of soft- and hard-substratum communities (; ).

column1: Amongst Fynbos Biome vegetation types
column2: Strandveld has the highest cover of Subtropical Thicket Biome species
column3: leading to some suggesting that it should be included in the latter biome (; ; ). However
column4: while closed-canopy clumps of thicket may be locally dominant in Strandveld
column5: the most extensive vegetation is fynbos
column6: albeit with an admixture of thicket species; moreover
column7: Strandveld floras are dominated by Cape taxa (; ).

column1: Our focus is on South Coast Strandveld

column3: calcareous sands of Holocene age that comprises a matrix of closed-canopy thicket and asteraceous (ericoid shrub-dominated) fynbos () that occurs along the Cape south coast. It corresponds to ’s () South Coast Strandveld Bioregion of the Fynbos Biome and to the south coastal vegetation units of ’s () Western Strandveld intrazonal vegetation group. Also included by us in South Coast Strandveld is the Holocene dune vegetation between Oyster Bay and Cape Recife
column4: which ’s () and ’s () map as Algoa Dune Strandveld

column6: we propose two strandveld bioregions for the Cape Floristic Region: West Coast Strandveld and South Coast Strandveld. Justification for this delimitation is provided later in this section.

column1: A recent revision of the  vegetation map of South Africa to incorporate Subtropical Thicket biome concepts () is not helpful regarding the delimitation of South Coast Strandveld as defined here. A major problem is that this revised system attributes what we define as Strandveld to the Subtropical Thicket biome when
column2: as we have mentioned above
column3: Strandveld is a Fynbos Biome vegetation. Also
column4: many of these revised vegetation units occur on both Holocene and Pleistocene sediments. Thus

column1: The boundary between South Coast Strandveld and the adjacent Sand Fynbos and Limestone Fynbos units () requires some discussion
column2: since some species are shared amongst these three vegetation units ().  suggest that higher fire return intervals in Sand Fynbos are of prime importance in floristically and structurally differentiating it from Strandveld
column3: with soil nutrients playing a lesser role. They do point out
column4: that “[W]ith time
column5: the alkaline sands [supporting Strandveld] become leached and are invaded by sand fynbos…”. While there is no evidence in support of the fire return interval hypothesis
column6: there is good evidence that Sand Fynbos occupies soils that are more acidic and of lower nutrient status than Strandveld ()
column7: a consequence of nutrients leaching from the older sediments. Moreover
column8: Strandveld thicket species are fire-resilient
column9: resprouting vigorously after fire ()
column10: even at high fire return intervals ().

column1: An alternative hypothesis is that the higher incidence of thicket in Strandveld relative to Sand Fynbos may be a consequence of higher water tables owing to the impervious bedrock approaching the surface in these coastal settings. These conditions would favour thicket over fynbos
column2: especially in dune swales and the immediate coastline
column3: where water tables are near the surface (; ). Where the water table is deep
column4: such as inland settings and on the crests and upper slopes of high dune ridges
column5: thicket incidence is much lower. However
column6: more research is required to test this soil moisture hypothesis. In a later section we speculate how soil moisture and fire exposure interact to determine the coexistence of thicket and fynbos cover states in South Coast Strandveld.

column1: Limestone Fynbos is easily differentiated edaphically from Strandveld based on the former being confined to skeletal sands overlying calcarenite as opposed to the deep unconsolidated sands of Strandveld (). Differences in soil nutrients between the two types are slight (; ).
As pointed out above
column2: the flora of south coast Holocene dunes is dominated by species associated with the Fynbos Biome
column3: and these provide the basis for delimiting the five Strandveld units we recognise in this study. Owing to the more widespread occurrence of their component species
column4: dune thicket communities extend over wider geographical ranges (see below) and are embedded in the Strandveld units (hence the term “dune fynbos-thicket mosaic” used in earlier accounts) (; ). Below
column5: we briefly describe each of these five fynbos-dominated units found on the Cape south coast.

column1: Southeastern Strandveld () occurs on the southeastern and northeastern sectors of the St Francis Bay coastline as well as the dunes between the Groot Brak River and Mossel Bay (). It thus includes part of ’s () St Francis Dune Thicket and Hartenbos Dune Thicket
column2: as well as ’s () Groot Brak Dune Strandveld (
column3: ). In total
column4: Southeastern Strandveld covers an area of ca. 119 km2. It is poorly differentiated from the other strandveld types showing strongest floristic links to Southwestern Strandveld and St Francis Strandveld (). The western enclave near Mossel Bay lacks several species prominent in the eastern sector.

column1: The climate of Southeastern Strandveld is warm temperate
column2: subhumid to semi-arid and sub-Mediterranean. Annual rainfall is highest in the extreme east at Cape Recife (550–600 mmyr−1) and lower elsewhere (500 mmyr−1) (). The mid-summer months (Dec–Feb) are the driest and the time when plant moisture stress is most severe. Rainfall reliability is highest in the equinoctial months. The proportion of warm season rainfall decreases from east to west. The temperature regime is equable: mean midsummer temperatures are ca 20−22 °C
column3: and midwinter temperatures ca. 16−18 °C. The coast is everywhere windy but notably so at Cape Recife. West-southwest winds prevail in winter and east-southeast winds in summer.

column8: these aeolianites support a grassy form of Sand Fynbos with a strong thicket and forest component (). The enclave of St Francis Strandveld at Maitland is also partly perched on Nanaga sediments.

column1: Throughout its range

column5: this is not to say that frequent fire coupled with browsing by megaherbivores would not have limited the extent of thicket in suitable habitats in the dune landscape ().

column1: Based on soil features
column2: dune morphology and vegetation
column3: what we term Holocene dunes represent a coherent geo-botanical unit. As we pointed out above
column4: geology maps classify much of what we identify as Holocene dunes as Late Pleistocene or even Neogene sediments. OSL-dated sediments are rare in our study area but do confirm Holocene age for the sediments at the sites we sampled at Goukamma (; ; ) and on the Agulhas Plain (). We suspect that the fixed
column5: calcareous dunes west of Cape St Francis and at Grootbos are of early Holocene to terminal Pleistocene age
column6: but this hypothesis remains to be tested.

column1: Problems with the assignation of sediments to deposition sequences may arise because of the stacking of serial dune depositions as has been described in the Wilderness-Sedgefield embayment by  and  but also evident at Cape St Francis () and elsewhere (e.g.
column2: Walker Bay). Globally
column3: sea-level fluctuations of the Pliocene and Pleistocene have shifted palaeoshorelines between elevations of over 10 m above present
column4: to ∼130 m below present base level (). South Africa’s tectonic position and resultant geological record during this period generally mirrors these glacio-eustatic trends (). With the highstand deposits preserved along the modern coast
column5: and sampling and observational bias allowing us to work on these and less so under the sea
column6: neo-coastal deposits show that it is apparent that shorelines have repeatedly returned to the same elevations. When a stable shoreline ensues and sediment is supplied to that coast
column7: it tends to either prograde outward in a seaward direction
column8: or accumulate on top of existing deposits where accommodation space is limited. In the Cape
column9: the latter is often the case as accommodation of sediment is hindered by steep bedrock cliffs and mountains in the immediate hinterland. As such
column10: stacking can occur when there is no room for adjacent packing of younger sediments. Thus
column11: Holocene deposits may overlie older aeolianites but this does not mean that they have weathered from these deposits.

column1: However
column2: as already pointed out above
column3: Holocene sands are physically and chemically different from the Pleistocene sands in many ways
column4: most of which can be identified in the field. The vegetation of the Cape coast is highly responsive to these differences
column5: with alkaline Holocene sand supporting a floristically distinct Strandveld vegetation with a different structure and sharing few species with the Sand Fynbos and Limestone Fynbos of the older sediments (). In the absence of OSL dates
column6: geology could learn much from soil science and plant ecology when mapping Cenozoic sediment along the Cape coast.

column1: What is clear is that coastal dune landscapes are dynamic
column2: accommodating in some places successive phases of accretion throughout the Quaternary. These processes would juxtapose floras associated with different-aged sediments
column3: producing novel plant communities and genotypes because of invasion onto newly available edaphic surfaces. Importantly
column4: these communities
column5: along with their high incidence of endemics
column6: have been assembled over the past 6 ka. There may well be correlates between Strandveld community structure and sediment age that could be explored. However
column7: this will have to await comprehensive dating of Holocene and Late Pleistocene coastal sediments of the Cape south coast.

column1: As we have shown above
column2: South Coast Strandveld comprises a mosaic of open-canopy fynbos and closed-canopy thicket
column3: each comprising a floristically and phylogenetically distinct assemblage. The relative abundance of these two formations is a function largely of fire and moisture regimes
column4: and their interactions; however
column5: browsing by megaherbivores in the precolonial era likely also played an important but undocumented role.

column1: Under high moisture regimes
column2: for example dune troughs where the water table is close to the surface
column3: or high annual rainfall regimes (>ca. 750 mm)
column4: and where fire is excluded or occurs at century-long intervals
column5: the Holocene dune landscape would likely be dominated by Mesic Dune Thicket and Dune Forest () (). The corresponding dominant architectural types of canopy species are lateral spreaders (e.g.
column6: Sideroxylon inerme) and vertical growers (e.g.
column7: Chionanthus foveolatus) (). At the other extreme
column8: where fire frequency is high (ca. 20-year intervals) and the moisture regime is low (annual rainfall <ca. 450 mm)
column9: the dune landscape is dominated by fynbos with patches of Xeric Dune Thicket comprising low
column10: multi-stemmed (hedge-forming)
column11: geoxylic (with underground stems) shrubs (e.g.
column12: Robsonodendron maritimum
column13: Olea exasperata) that are restricted to relatively moist sites such as high-water table dune troughs and steep
column14: south-facing dune slopes. Under a high-moisture
column15: high-fire frequency combination
column16: hedge thicket is replaced in the most favourable sites by a taller thicket dominated by lateral spreaders such as Pterocelatrus tricuspidatus and Mystroxylon aethiopicum (). The extent to which this occurs will depend on the frequency of fires with sufficient intensity to consume these taller thicket patches. Such fires
column17: by reducing canopy height and promoting vigorous sprouting of thicket species from below-ground branches
column18: are key to maintaining the relative mix of thicket dominated by hedge formers and lateral spreaders (; ). Our model predicts that in fire-free dune landscapes of uniformly low soil moisture
column19: hedge thicket would dominate.

column1: Fynbos is predicted to be a stable cover state on Holocene dunes where high-intensity fires occur at ca. 20-year intervals
column2: irrespective of soil moisture regime; only on sites of low soil moisture does fynbos persist at ca. 80-year intervals but is outcompeted by Xeric Thicket where fires occur at centuries-long intervals (). This is consistent with the requirement for fire-stimulated recruitment of the generally short-lived fynbos lineages both in the Holocene dune landscape (; ; ; ) and elsewhere in the CFR ().

column1: Coastal dunes are particularly fragile ecosystems
column2: as even minor disturbances can cause long-term alterations and compromise biodiversity (). Though specific impacts depend on local conditions ()
column3: rising sea levels driven by climate change are likely to result in the inland migration of the beach–dune profile (; ; ). Dune ecosystems may remain intact and functional even after such a displacement
column4: but this is entirely dependent on the existence of sufficient accommodation space on the landward margin (). Should this not be the case
column5: a “coastal squeeze” will ensue
column6: leading to the extirpation of dune habitats and their component species (; ; ). The risk of this is especially high along developed coasts
column7: where hard infrastructure effectively limits the extent to which coastal ecosystems can migrate inland in response to sea-level rise. Ecological niche modelling
column8: considering predicted climate change and sea-level rise
column9: suggests that the distribution of coastal plant species in Mexico will become severely limited
column10: and especially so for local endemics (). Similarly
column11: fixed-dune habitats in Italy
column12: being more sensitive to direct climate change effects
column13: are projected to lose most of their distribution in the coming decades ().

column1: While much more research is needed to understand the potential impacts of climate change and sea-level rise on coastal dune vegetation on the southern coast of the CFR
column2: we expect similar ramifications to those predicted for dunes elsewhere. All South Coast Strandveld units support dune endemics with small ranges ()
column3: many of which are already threatened by coastal development and invasive alien plants
column4: especially Acacia cyclops (); given projected global change
column5: these localised edaphic endemics are most likely to suffer further range contractions (). Adding to this
column6: ramifications of coastal squeeze are likely to be exacerbated by the fact that inland habitats (e.g.
column7: sand fynbos) are unlikely to be a reliable source of propagules for impacted strandveld vegetation as these non-dune habitats largely lack calcicolous species
column8: which constitute about 40% of dune floras in the Cape (; ).

column1: Nearly half of South Africa’s coastal habitat has been degraded
column2: while 60% of coastal ecosystem types (or 55% by area) are considered threatened (). Impacts have been especially severe along the dune-supporting sandy coasts of the country
column3: including along the Cape south coast ()
column4: yet the conservation importance of these coastal dune habitats has historically been underappreciated (; ).  classified South African vegetation types according to their ecosystem status
column5: a measure based on the extent of remaining untransformed area of a vegetation type in relation to its biodiversity target (% area). In this classification
column6: all vegetation types occurring on southern Cape coastal dunes were listed as “Least Threatened”. An updated status assessment
column7: based on the latest classification of South Africa’s vegetation () and implementing the IUCN Red List of Ecosystems V. 1.1 protocol ()
column8: classified most Cape south coast dune vegetation as “Least Concern”
column9: although Overberg Dune Strandveld and Hartenbos Dune Thicket were classified as “Endangered” and Grootbrak Dune Strandveld as “Critically Endangered” ().

column1: As we have explained
column2: however
column3: the delimitation of vegetation units on coastal dunes of the Cape south coast has hitherto been problematic
column4: which has introduced inherent errors in the threat status assessments of these ecosystems. There is thus an urgent need for the reassessment of transformation and protection levels and threat statuses of these ecosystems to identify regional conservation priorities and to implement effective measures in these areas to prevent further degradation and loss of dune vegetation. We propose that our classification of South Coast Strandveld be used for this purpose as it allows for a more accurate understanding of the distribution and composition of coastal dune vegetation units
column5: thereby providing a more reliable foundation for spatially explicit conservation assessments. Such assessments should further consider the regional and global rarity of these coastal dune vegetation units (): relative to the more extensive ecosystems of the southern Cape hinterland ()
column6: they occur as small (mostly <10 km2; ) and fragmented patches in a dynamic landscape. Thus
column7: given the continuing threat of coastal development and encroachment by invasive plants
column8: as well as the exacerbating effects of climate change and associated sea-level rise
column9: we propose that all remnant South Coast Strandveld vegetation be protected to facilitate the persistence of these habitats and their component species.

column1: The vegetation of the Holocene dunes of the Cape south coast form a coherent geo-botanical unit (South Coast Strandveld) that can be further divided into six floristically differentiated units comprising mosaics of fynbos and thicket. These Strandveld units
column2: despite their small extent
column3: are rich in species including numerous dune endemics. They are floristically distinct from the vegetation on older
column4: mostly Late Pleistocene sands
column5: with which they have been conflated in previous studies. We recommend that our study be replicated on these older sediments
column6: thereby enabling a comprehensive floristic assessment of Cenozoic sediments in the study area. We also recommend that this study be extended to the Cenozoic sediments of the Cape west coast and further afield in Namaqualand. This research should include comprehensive data on sediment ages to test the hypothesis that botanical composition of vegetation can predict accurately sediment age. Botanical transects across the full spectrum of Cenozoic coastal sediments in the Cape offer great potential for understanding the evolutionary underpinnings of Cape coastal clades
column7: representing the youngest radiation in the hyperdiverse Cape flora ().
