column1: 47|10.7717/peerj.183|Citations and the h index of soil researchers and journals in the Web of Science
column2: Scopus
column3: and Google Scholar| Minasny  Budiman |2013-10-22|"Scientific impact measures are increasingly being used for academic promotions
column4: grant evaluations and evaluation of job vacancy candidates. They are also being used for the evaluations of university departments and research centres. Traditionally
column5: the impact factor of a journal has been used – a metric developed by  whereby the citations and number of papers published over a given period are divided. For most journals it shows considerable inter-annual fluctuation and it provides no information on individual papers nor individual authors. Since 2005
column6: the h index has been used as an index for quantifying the scientific productivity of scientists based on their publication record (). It is a personal index and provides information on the number of publications of an author and the number of citations: A scholar with an index of h has published h papers with at least h citations each. The h index can also be calculated for journals
column7: departments
column8: universities or countries.

column1: The three widely used bibliometric databases for analysis and evaluations of citations and the h index are Web of Science (Thomson Reuters)
column2: Scopus (Elsevier)
column3: and Google Scholar. Some papers have compared citations between these three databases. Although Google Scholar and Scopus seem to provide higher numbers of citations ()
column4: there is mixed information on the h index. For example
column5: compared the h index for 47 highly-cited Israeli researchers across the three databases and concluded that the results from Google Scholar are considerably different from Web of Science and Scopus.  looked at 4
column6: 600 publications from three UK business schools
column7: and found that Web of Science poorly covers the management discipline compared to Google Scholar.  examined 31 faculty members from nursing faculty in the Midwestern USA
column8: and concluded that more than one database should be used to calculate the h index. They further recommended that since the h index rankings differ among databases
column9: comparisons between researchers should be done only within a specified database.

column1: The difference between the three databases has been fairly well established and the three databases will calculate different citations and h indices. As far as we know
column2: the relationships between the three databases have not been investigated and derived. The aims of this paper are therefore: (i) to compare citations and h index across the three databases
column3: (ii) to derive transfer functions to convert metrics from one database to the others
column4: and (iii) to compare impact factors for journals and the h index. Hereto we have compared the data from 340 researchers and 31 journals. Since we are soil scientists
column5: we have used only soil researchers and journals in this study.

column1: Soil science is a study of soil as a natural phenomenon and resource (). It is a relatively small discipline in terms of number of researchers
column2: number of papers per annum
column3: and citations. The IUSS (International Union of Soil Sciences) database lists about 50
column4: 000 soil scientists worldwide
column5: but only a fraction of these are in research and actively publish
column6: with a guesstimate of 5
column8: 000 publishing researchers. The “soil” topic has lower number of papers and citations when compared to other subjects of natural resources such as “air” and “water” (). The number of published papers in 2011 according to Scopus with “soil” in the abstract and keywords is 39
column10: with a rate of increase of about 2
column11: 000 papers per year. In comparison the number of papers in 2011 on “air” is 1.4 times larger and the number of papers on “water” is 3.5 times larger. The h index ratios for water
column12: air
column13: and soil (for the papers published in 2011) are 1.7
column15: and 1.0. Nevertheless soil is becoming more important with strong links to global issues of food security
column16: biodiversity
column17: land use change
column18: and climate change (). While this study only used soil researchers
column19: the bibliometric results are illustrative to other agricultural
column20: environmental
column21: earth science and biology disciplines
column22: and to small scientific disciplines in general.

column1: Google Scholar (GS) is a bibliographic database freely available from Google. It was introduced in 2004 and contains scholarly works across many disciplines and sources
column2: including theses
column3: books
column4: reports
column5: abstracts
column6: peer-reviewed and non-reviewed articles
column7: and web pages that are deemed scholarly. Google Scholar lists these automatically from its search engine activities (; ). An individual Google Scholar page was featured in 2012
column8: where a researcher can create a webpage
column9: with fields of interest. Google Scholar automatically searches and populates the individual’s publications
column10: calculates and displays the individual’s total number of citations
column11: h index
column12: and i10 index. Scopus
column13: or SciVerse Scopus
column14: is a bibliographic database from Elsevier which contains abstracts and citations for academic journal articles
column15: conference papers
column16: and book chapters. Inclusion in the database is through the Scopus Content Selection and Advisory Board. Although its record goes back as early as 1823
column17: its citations are reliable after 1995. The Web of Science is a bibliographic database from Thompson Reuters which only contained abstracts and citations for articles listed in the Web of Science indexed journals since 1900 ().

column1: Data from researchers who listed their areas of interest as: “soil science”
column2: “soil”
column3: “pedology”
column4: “soil physics”
column5: “soil biology”
column6: “soil chemistry”
column7: “soil fertility”
column8: “soil erosion”
column9: “soil ecology”
column10: and “soil carbon” were retrieved from the Google Scholar author pages. The same researchers were located in Scopus and the Web of Science. In Scopus
column11: the ‘Author Identifier’ tool was used to locate the researcher. In the Web of Science
column12: the author’s surname and first name’s initial was used
column13: together with “soil” in the search subject. When the name and publication record were inconsistent across all three databases
column14: the researcher was not included in our analysis. At the end
column15: we collected data from 340 researchers and this included: number of total citations
column16: h index
column17: number of papers
column18: and year of the first publication. These data were obtained for each researcher and from each of the three databases. The publications and citations are until June 2013.

column1: shows the statistics of h index
column2: number of publications
column3: number of citations
column4: and year of the first paper for 340 soil researchers in the three databases. Our data encompass a wide range of researchers from early-career to well-established and highly-cited researchers. The database is much larger and more diverse than previous studies where a small and focussed group of researchers was used to compare citation metrics between the databases (e.g.

column1: The median number of papers for the 340 soil researchers ranged from 23 (Web of Science) to 79 (Google Scholar) with Scopus having intermediate values. The number of citations is also highest in Google scholar
column2: with a median of 866 citations per author whereas it is 291 in the Web of Science. The h index and its annual increase are lowest in the Web of Science. This pattern holds for all of the metrics presented here: Google Scholar has the highest numbers and the Web of Science the lowest whereas the Scopus numbers are in between. Part of this may be the different types of publications included and also the periods of time covered by the 3 databases are slightly different.

column1: A simple linear regression without intercept was performed between the citation indices of the three databases (). Google Scholar has on average 2.3 times more articles and 1.9 times more citations than the Web of Science. The Scopus database (all years) has 1.1 times more papers than the Web of Science but a similar number of citations compared to the Web of Science. Since the citations are more correct and complete after 1995
column2: a revision was made to the relationship for post 1995 authors; it shows that Scopus has about 1.2 times more citations than the Web of Science. The 20% higher citations are consistent with the findings by  in the field of medicine. Similarly
column3: for articles in medical journals
column4: found that Google Scholar and Scopus retrieved more citations compared to Web of Science (1.22 and 1.20 times respectively).

column1: The relationship between number of papers and citations is scattered
column2: especially for the number of papers ()
column3: but the relationships between h index values across the 3 databases appear to be quite linear. The h index in Google Scholar is on average 1.4 times larger than Web of Science
column4: and the h index in Scopus (post 1995 authors) is on average 1.1 times larger than Web of Science. However
column5: for pre-1995 authors
column6: their Scopus h index is similar and sometimes can be smaller when compared to Web of Science. While Google Scholar contains more grey literature (informally published written material) and its citations may contain errors ()
column7: the h index appears to be quite robust and comparable with Web of Science and Scopus. This is due to the fact that h index does not vary greatly if the number of articles increases (e.g.
column8: book chapters or unrefereed articles). In addition
column9: extra citations do not have a large effect on the h index
column10: as once a paper has reached h citations additional citations to that paper do not affect its value (; ).

column1: Our results are different from the study by  who evaluated 13 computer scientists from his university’s department and found that on average Google Scholar had five times more papers
column2: eight times more citations and a three-fold larger h index. Our results from 340 soil researchers are more in line with  who looked at 30 researchers from nursing and found that the h index from Google Scholar is 1.3 times larger than the Web of Science
column3: and Scopus is 1.1 times larger than the h index in the Web of Science. Similar results were obtained by  who evaluated 22 human–computer interaction researchers from the UK and found that the h index in Google Scholar is on average 1.6 times higher than Web of Science.  compared publications and citations for 195 Nobel Laureates in Physiology and Medicine using the three databases. They found no concordance between the three databases when considering the number of publications and citations count per Laureate. However
column4: the h index was the most reliably calculated bibliometric index across the three databases.

column1: We calculated the Spearman’s rank correlation (ρ) of the h index of the 340 researchers from the three databases. The three databases show excellent correlation for the h index
column2: with WoS and GS as having the largest concordance. Unexpectedly
column3: the rank correlation in terms of no. citations and no. papers () also indicates that the three databases are comparable. This implies that the ranking of individuals within a database is comparable with the other databases. Our correlation is also much higher compared to the 13 computer scientists studied by  who only obtained ρ = 0.65. We used a much larger dataset
column4: and the GS data came from the page that was created by the researcher
column5: thus the listed papers and citations are assumed to be more complete.

column1: In an earlier paper () we investigated the relationship between the h index of 228 soil researchers and found that the index was 0.7 times the number of years since the first publication (which we called scientific age
column2: or t). That means if a researcher has been publishing for 10 years his/her h index should be about 7. We calculated this index using the Web of Science database
column3: and now we repeated this using analysis to the 340 soil researchers in this study (which are different from the previous list) (). Although the data are scattered the relationship holds:

column1: \begin{eqnarray*} \displaystyle h\text{ index}=0.73\times \text{ scientific age}\quad ({R}^{2}=0.72).&&\displaystyle \end{eqnarray*}h index=0.73× scientific age(R2=0.72).
The Web of Science database shows that the average rate of h index increase over time (m) is 0.7
column2: with the lowest value of 0.06 and highest value of 2.9 (). The average m value for Scopus is 0.7 and for Google Scholar it is 0.8 ().

column4: and 0.36 for biochemistry
column5: water
column6: economics
column7: and anthropology

column1: For selected researchers

column4: soil biology
column5: biogeochemistry and ecology have the highest m values (median of 0.8). This is followed by soil physics

column1: Although the number of citations for researchers across the three databases can be quite different
column2: the relationship between the number of citations and the h index is quite consistent across the three databases ():

column1: \begin{eqnarray*} \displaystyle h\text{ index}=\unicode{x00BD} {n}^{\unicode{x00BD} }\quad ({R}^{2}=0.95).&&\displaystyle \end{eqnarray*}h index=½n½(R2=0.95).
This relationship follows the function postulated by  where the number of citations is about 3 to 5 times h2
column2: and it appears the h index follows an absorption-type relationship ()
column3: increasing rapidly at low numbers of citations with the rate decreasing with increasing number of citations.

column1: Black dots are data from Web of Science
column2: green squares are from Scopus
column3: and blue triangles are from Google Scholar.

column1: shows the relationship of the average number of papers and citations per year for the 340 soil researchers. This can be interpreted as: on average
column2: a soil researcher produces 5 articles per year
column3: 2 articles in international refereed journals
column4: 1 in a conference proceedings
column5: and 2 other unrefereed publications. The researcher receives 65 citations per year from journal articles
column6: an additional 13 citations from conference proceedings and another 44 citations from other publications.

column1: A way to boost the h index is by self-citation.  found that self-citation is 12% of all references in biology
column2: engineering and physics
column3: compared to 4% in sociology
column4: philosophy
column5: linguistics
column6: or marketing. For soil science journals
column7: we found a mean of 12% self-citations but it differs between the sub-disciplines (). High rates of self-citation were accompanied by high journal impact factor ranking; China and the USA had the highest rates of self-citation whereas Egypt
column8: Algeria
column9: Ukraine
column10: and Indonesia have low levels of self-citations in soil science ().

column1: So high rates of self-citation may influence the h index and the Scopus database allows calculation of the h index with and without self-citation. Self-citation here is the so-called diachronous kind ()
column2: which is self-citation from the citations received by the author. The other type is called synchronous which is more difficult to calculate
column3: i.e.
column4: author’s self-referencing relative to the total number of references cited in a paper.

column1: The relationship for the 340 soil researchers is consistent and the average h index without self-citation is about 12% lower ():
\begin{eqnarray*} \displaystyle h\text{ index without self-citation}=0.88\times h\text{ index }({R}^{2}=0.97).&&\displaystyle \end{eqnarray*}h index without self-citation=0.88×h index (R2=0.97).

We found a weak relationship between percentage of self-citation and scientific age (t) (). It suggests that some younger authors appear to have high rates of self citation as their works were not known widely and their citations mainly come from themselves
column2: as the researchers mature their papers are more widely known and more external citations were gained thus a lower percentage of self-citations:

column1: \begin{eqnarray*} \displaystyle \text{Percent self-citation}=42-5{t}^{0.5}\quad ({R}^{2}=0.18).&&\displaystyle \end{eqnarray*}Percent self-citation=42−5t0.5(R2=0.18).
We retrieved 31 Soil Science journal impact factors (IF) and other metrics from the 2012 Thompson Reuters Journal Citation Reports (JCR
column2: released in June 2013). Google Scholar also has measures of the journal’s metric
column3: the h5-index
column4: which is the h index for articles published in that journal for the last five years. The list of journals for the soil science discipline in Google Scholar is slightly different from the Thompson Reuters Journal Citation Reports (JCR)
column5: and therefore we used the journals listed in JCR as the basis for comparison. We searched for the h5-index for the journals in Google Scholar metrics for 2012 (released July 2013).

column1: shows that Google Scholar h5-index has a better correlation with the five year IF (impact factor) than the two year IF
column2: and  shows the comparison between GS h5-index and the five year IF. While the h5-index and five year IF have a high rank correlation (ρ = 0.90)
column3: the ranking is different for different journals. The journals ‘Soil Biology and Biochemistry’ and ‘Plant and Soil’ both consistently ranked no. 1 and 2 in JCR and GS while other journals appear to be slightly different in their ranking (1 to 3 places difference). The top two journals are able to maintain a large number of citations relative to the number of papers they publish.

column1: There are 4 journals that are ranked much higher ( >  =  4 difference in rank) in Google Scholar compared to the IF: ‘Soil Science Society of America Journal’
column2: ‘Journal of Plant Nutrition and Soil Science’
column3: ‘Pedosphere’
column4: and ‘Revista Brasileira de Ciência do Solo’. All these journals are published by national soil science societies (USA
column5: Germany
column6: China and Brazil). In the case of ‘Revista Brasileira de Ciência do Solo’ which ranked 12 in GS and 25 in JCR
column7: Google Scholar includes more citations from non-English articles. Contrarily
column8: there are four journals that are ranked much lower ( <  =  4 difference in rank) in Google Scholar: ‘European Journal of Soil Science’
column9: ‘Soil Use and Management’
column10: ‘Journal of Soil and Water Conservation’
column11: and ‘Soil Science’.

column1: The Thompson Reuters Journal Citation Reports suffers from a miscalculation
column2: for example
column3: ‘Australian Journal of Soil Research’ was reported to have a 2 year IF of 3.443. This is a miscalculation
column4: as the journal changed its name to ‘Soil Research’ in 2011
column5: and the IF calculation for Australian Journal of Soil Research only accounts for papers published until 2010. ‘Soil Research’ was again listed as a separate journal in JCR. We have recalculated the actual impact factor for this journal in our analysis.

column1: While there is a positive correlation between cites (citations in 2012 to papers published from the previous 5 years) and IF
column2: we can see that there are 2 trends (). For journals that published <700 papers between 2007 and 2011 (or on average less than 140 papers per year) IF tends to increase rapidly with increasing citations (1.2 increase in IF per 1000 citations). For the other 7 journals that published more than 700 papers
column3: the slope is half as much (0.6 IF increase per 1000 citations). So there is a drawback for journals that publish more papers. Meanwhile the h5-index is mostly controlled by number of citations following an absorption relationship (). Although the citations come from WoS
column4: the h5-index still holds the square-root relationship supporting its robustness.

column1: also shows that the GS h5-index is more correlated to the Eigenfactor metric compared to IF. The Eigenfactor metric is based on the Google PageRank algorithm which calculated the “influence” of the journal based on the citations weighted by the “quality” of the journal (). A citation from a highly cited journal will have a higher weighting than a lower cited journal. We showed that this metric is less vulnerable to self-citation than the impact factor (). Interestingly
column2: Google Scholar does not apply its PageRank for citations.

column1: and   established that the h index is robust and is relatively unaffected by grey literature and errors in citations such as in Google Scholar. Most of the errors (and distortions) in citation databases are found in the ‘long tails’ of the citation distribution and they tend not to affect the h index much. For the journals considered here
column2: we calculated the ratio between h5-index and number of papers
column3: and it shows that only 1–9% (median 5%) of the total papers that contributed to h5-index. In other words
column4: less than 10% of the cited papers are influencing the h5-index. We also demonstrated that the h5-index keeps its relationship even when using WoS citations.

column1: recommended the use of the GS h index for Management and International Business journals. We also concur that the h5-index is a better measure of a journal’s citation performance than the impact factor as it is more robust and less affected by citation manipulation. It is now acknowledged that there are ways of manipulating impact factor
column2: which include self-citation
column3: and editorials that listed references to previously published articles (). The h index is less sensitive to the increase in number of citations
column4: while individual highly cited papers can artificially increase the impact factor. In addition
column5: it only considers the top influential h papers in the journal
column6: thus it does not penalise a journal for publishing a larger number of papers. Although h index can also be manipulated by self-citation
column7: in order to increase the h index considerably
column8: a journal has to be more tactical by increasing a significant number of citations to certain papers.

column1: From this analysis the following can be concluded:
There is a large difference between the number of citations
column2: number of publications and the h index using the three different databases.

column1: On average
column2: Google Scholar gives the largest number of publications
column3: largest number of citations and the highest h index. The Web of Science gives the lowest averages.

column1: There are solid relationships between the h indices in these three databases.
The h5-index has a correlation with the five year impact factor
column2: but it is more robust and less affected by citation manipulation. It should be considered as an alternative to the journal’s impact factor.

column1: This analysis has shown that the choice of the database affects the assessment of scientific impact for academic promotions
column2: grant evaluations
column3: job vacancy candidates or the evaluations of university departments and research centres. It is recommended that we should quote these bibliometric indices for all three databases as they reflect different types of publications. Web of Science uses mostly refereed journal articles
column4: Scopus includes conference proceedings and book chapters
column5: whereas Google Scholar includes other publications (including software). The established relationships between the databases () can be used as bibliometric transfer functions by anyone interested in relating databases. We are not aware of whether these functions have been established for other scientific disciplines but assume they will be similar. As a test
column6: we applied our function relating the h index of WoS and GS to the 30 nursing faculty data of   and the function gives a good prediction with a Spearman rank correlation of 0.852. We envisage that these functions would work better for science than socio-economical disciplines. However
column7: this needs to be investigated.

column1: Although we focussed on the relatively small discipline of soil science
column2: the reported researchers has a lot of cross-over with other disciplines
column3: in particular earth science
column4: agricultural science
column5: biogeochemistry and ecology. Many researchers in ecology and microbiology work with soil as a medium
column6: while they do not necessarily study soil as a natural body. Their contributions elevated the citations as compared to pure soil research. The trend of h index for soil researchers appears to be in between the water and biochemistry disciplines (). However
column7: soil science publication rate (on average 2.5 papers per year per researcher) is lower compared to water and biochemistry (on average of 3.1 and 3.8 papers per year
column8: respectively).

column1: 48|10.7717/peerj.16419|Riding the wave of innovation: immunoinformatics in fish disease control| Razali  Siti Aisyah |2023-12-8|"Millions of farmers
column2: food processors
column3: traders
column4: researchers
column5: technical experts
column6: and leaders all over the world are engaged in the daunting challenge of feeding a projected nine billion global population by 2050. Fish and other aquatic products from aquaculture play a significant role in meeting the dietary needs of all people
column7: as well as the requirements of the poorest for food security (). Aquaculture accounts for 49.2% of total aquaculture and fisheries production on a global scale
column8: with proportions varying by region and production sector. Aquaculture is essential to meet the world’s need for fish for several reasons
column9: including overfishing
column10: habitat degradation
column11: climate change
column12: pollution
column13: and unsustainable fishing practices (). However
column14: the sustainable development of the aquaculture sector is hindered by many factors
column15: with the control of infectious diseases being one of the most significant challenges
column16: as fish disease outbreaks have caused enormous economic losses in the aquaculture industry (; ; ; ). Losses in fish production
column17: revenue
column18: livelihoods
column19: and international trade (citation) are major components of economic losses caused by fish disease outbreaks in aquaculture
column20: emphasizing the need for effective fish disease management strategies.

column1: Despite antibiotics or chemotherapeutics being used for fish disease treatment in aquaculture
column2: drug resistance issues and safety concerns become obstacles to resolving fish disease outbreaks (; ). Thus
column3: fish vaccinations have been extensively employed in the aquaculture industry. Prior to deployment
column4: fish vaccines
column5: like those used in human and veterinary medicine
column6: must pass stringent tests for safety and efficacy. In safety assessments
column7: potential adverse effects on vaccinated fish
column8: non-target species
column9: and the environment are evaluated. These tests ensure that the vaccine does not cause excessive damage to the fish
column10: has no negative effects on non-target organisms
column11: and does not introduce harmful substances into the environment (). The effectiveness of fish vaccines is evaluated both in the laboratory and in the field. The immune response of the fish is monitored in the laboratory to corroborate that the vaccine induces an adequate immune response. Trials are conducted in the field to ensure that the vaccine provides protection against the targeted pathogen under real-world conditions.

column1: Notably
column2: although vaccines considerably reduce the likelihood of disease outbreaks
column3: they do not guarantee complete immunity. Individual fish may respond differently to vaccination
column4: similar to other animals
column5: due to a variety of factors
column6: including genetic variability
column7: age
column8: nutritional status
column9: stress
column10: and concurrent infections. This is why continuous monitoring of the efficacy and safety of vaccines is necessary (). Vaccines are an essential component of the sustainable management of aquaculture
column11: as they contribute to disease control and fish welfare while reducing the use of antibiotics. They provide a proactive and preventative approach to health management
column12: which aligns with the overarching goal of assuring global food security. As is the case with all medications
column13: the key to their successful application rests in their application in accordance with scientific research and established guidelines.

column1: A fish vaccine typically contains a substance derived from pathogenic microorganisms in non-pathogenic forms that act as an antigen. By stimulating the fish’s immune system to combat a specific pathogen
column2: the system is permitted to create a response
column3: as well as a “memory” to cause the acceleration of the response
column4: when the specific organism that causes the disease creates future infections (; ; ). Traditional methods were used to develop a variety of vaccines
column5: including killed whole-cell
column6: live-attenuated
column7: recombinant DNA
column8: subunits
column9: and toxoid vaccines (; ; ). However
column10: most authorized and commercial vaccines currently in use in the aquaculture industry are killed whole-cell vaccines while other vaccine groups are still being studied in live animals or are in the experimental phase (; ).

column1: The killed whole-cell vaccine
column2: also known as bacterin
column3: is among the oldest vaccination technologies indigenously manufactured by many developing countries (). To make such a vaccine
column4: it requires organisms that must be inactivated or that have died through physical or chemical procedures like inactivation with heat
column5: irradiation with UV
column6: or inactivation through formalin or chloroform (; ). When administered to the host
column7: they induce strong protective humoral immune responses against those pathogens (). Using killed whole-cell vaccines can prevent a number of viral disease outbreaks
column8: which include infectious necrosis of the pancreas
column9: spleen
column10: and kidney; and pancreatic disease viruses; as well as bacterial diseases such as Vibriosis
column11: enteric septicaemia of catfish
column12: and Streptococcus infections (; ).

column1: Live-attenuated vaccines are types of vaccines that contain live microorganisms whose virulent properties were disabled under specific cultivation conditions to generate a broad immune response (; ). Many scientific studies have focused on these vaccinations
column2: which are being investigated for commercialization as fish vaccines due to their capacity to combat 209 infectious diseases caused by recognised and unknown pathogenic microorganisms still under investigation (). Unlike killed whole-cell vaccines
column3: live-attenuated vaccines are able to induce both cell-mediated and humoral immune responses (; ). These vaccines with a minimum dosage are adequate to elicit long-lasting protective immune responses as they mimic the real infections caused by pathogens. This incident preferentially evokes T-cell proliferative responses relative to B-cell responses (; ). Thus

This demonstrates that recombinant DNA vaccines are useful tools in investigating the key factor in the pathogenicity of the etiological agent to the fish and are economically viable in animals with extremely high value (; ).

Immunoinformatics bridges the gap between computer science and immunology by employing computational resources and methods to manage and comprehend immunology data. It contributes to the management of large datasets and aids in the creation of new hypotheses regarding immune responses (; ; ).

column1: While the practical implementation of immunoinformatics in aquaculture has been established
column2: it is essential to recognise that there are significant differences between fish and human immune systems (). Understanding these differences is crucial for the efficient application of immunoinformatics in the development of vaccines and treatments for fish. The innate immune system of fish
column3: which is recognised as the first line of defence against a variety of pathogens
column4: plays a more significant role than its homologue in mammals. Notably
column5: primitive fish species with no jaws
column6: such as lampreys and hagfish
column7: have a profoundly different immune system than jawed vertebrates. This system lacks the typical B and T cells and Major Histocompatibility Complex (MHC) molecules observed in humans and advanced fish species. For adaptive immunity
column8: these jawless species rely on a unique system of variable lymphocyte receptors (VLRs). Jawed fish
column9: including cartilaginous (such as sharks) and bony (such as trout) species
column10: have evolved B and T cells and MHC molecules
column11: heralding the “modern” emergence of adaptive immunity (; ; ). Despite this
column12: fish immune systems are less sophisticated than those of mammals
column13: including humans. For instance
column14: fish have fewer subsets of T cells
column15: and their B cells are less diverse. The disparity also extends to MHC molecules. Fewer MHC class II molecules are present in fish than in humans
column16: and their function is not as well understood. Some species
column17: such as the Atlantic cod
column18: lack MHC II molecules but possess a larger number of MHC I molecules (). Essentially
column19: these differences highlight the need for predictive models and algorithms that are uniquely tailored to the immune response pathways of fish. Ideally
column20: the data used to train these algorithms should be derived from the immune responses of fish. Taking into consideration these differences will permit the effective application of immunoinformatics to the development of more effective preventative and therapeutic measures for aquaculture health management.

column1: Immunoinformatics has been applied in many research studies

column4: modelling immune responses

column8: addressing major obstacles and accelerating progress. Identification of immune epitopes within fish pathogens is one of the most important applications. Using immunoinformatics
column9: researchers can effectively identify these epitopes: sections of the pathogen’s genetic sequences with the potential to elicit an immune response in fish (; ; ). This essential knowledge guides the development of vaccines based on epitopes
column10: allowing for targeted immunisation that induces a specific protective immune response. In addition
column11: immunoinformatics contributes substantially to the development of epitope-based vaccines by enabling scientists to select the most immunogenic and conserved epitopes (; ). This strategic approach paves the way for the development of broad-spectrum vaccines
column12: thereby protecting against multiple pathogen strains or variants.

Proteins that are aligned and have a higher percentage of identical residues imply evolutionary relationships. This method consists of several steps: (1) template identification and initial alignment

column1: Threading
column2: also known as fold recognition
column3: is an alternative method if homology modelling cannot be applied. By comparing a template sequence to a collection of structural folds
column4: this approach returns a list of scores. A known peptide–MHC complex structure is utilised to predict the binding structures of other peptides to the same MHC molecule by maximising the alignment of the amino acid sequence and their 3D structural patterns. I-TASSER ()
column5: Phyre2 ()
column6: and RaptorX () servers are used for threading modelling. Ab initio protein modelling predicts 3D structures based on novel folds and can be utilised if the structure of interest is unavailable or if the sequence identity between the template and the protein of interest is less than 30%. Based on physical principles
column7: this method involves computing all energy parameters of protein folding and determining the state with the lowest free energy. ROBETTA ()

column1: Structural validation is an important step in protein modelling to assure the quality of the models. Energy minimisation and structural refinement are applied to each model to enhance the three-dimensional structures’ quality so that structural errors and steric conflicts in the structure of each protein can be eliminated. Several structure validation tools (; ) such as PROCHECK (http://www.ebi.ac.uk/thornton-srv/software/PROCHECK/)
column2: Verify3D and ERRAT (https://saves.mbi.ucla.edu/) can be used to assess model quality before and after refining. PROCHECK provides a Ramachandran plot that calculates phi–psi torsion angles for each residue and analyses the overall stereochemical quality of 3D structures of protein models. Verify3D evaluate the compatibility of an atomic model (3D) with its amino acid sequence (1D) while the ERRAT server assesses the overall quality factor for nonbonded atomic interactions. The model quality assessment is critical for determining the overall correctness of the structure as well as the local accuracy of each protein fragment. Selecting the best protein modelling approach is dependent on the availability of known homologues
column3: the folds of known structures
column4: and the quality of 3D structures.  depicts a schematic illustration of protein structure decision-making prediction based on several modelling methodologies.

column1: Self-assembling immunogens
column2: such as virus-like particles (VLPs) and self-assembling protein nanoparticles (SAPNs)
column3: are used in the development of fish vaccines to enhance their immunogenicity and stability (; ; ). These immunogens are formed from the self-assembly of viral or bacterial proteins
column4: which mimic the shape and size of native virions. Self-assembling peptides can also act as adjuvants themselves by forming an antigen depot
column5: directing vaccines to antigen-presenting cells (APCs)
column6: and enhancing immune-cell priming (). These self-assembling structures
column7: often designed as nanoparticles
column8: mimic the native size and shape of viruses or bacteria and present multiple copies of an antigen or epitope on their surface. This form of antigen presentation stimulates a robust immune response by imitating the repetitive antigenic patterns of many pathogens. The use of self-assembling immunogens in fish vaccines offers several advantages
column9: including precise antigen display
column10: enhanced immunogenicity
column11: and stability ().

column1: Within the field of aquaculture
column2: the potential benefits of self-assembling immunogens are multifaceted. Primarily
column3: these structures could significantly enhance the immune response in fish. Due to the repetitive
column4: ordered array of antigens that these nanoparticle vaccines present
column5: they can stimulate the immune system more effectively
column6: leading to a more potent and enduring response than traditional vaccines. Moreover
column7: the versatility of self-assembling immunogens opens doors to designing broad-spectrum or multivalent vaccines. Such vaccines could potentially combat multiple strains or species of pathogens
column8: addressing the substantial challenge of pathogenic diversity in aquaculture. On a practical note
column9: self-assembling immunogens might present advantages in terms of stability and scalability. Assuming the initial design and production processes are fine-tuned
column10: these vaccines could potentially be produced on a large scale and might exhibit enhanced stability under a variety of environmental conditions
column11: an essential consideration for aquaculture operations worldwide. However
column12: the development of self-assembling immunogens for fish vaccines is not without challenges. The design and production processes for these vaccines can be complex
column13: necessitating further research to streamline them. Furthermore
column14: experimental validation of the safety and efficacy of these vaccines in target fish species remains paramount. In this context
column15: computational methods could play a significant role.

column1: Self-assembling immunogens can be developed using computational methods such as AlphaFold and RosettaFold (; ; ). These computational methods enable the design of self-assembling immunogens by predicting the structure of proteins and their interactions with other proteins. AlphaFold uses deep neural networks to predict protein structures with high accuracy
column2: while RosettaFold uses a combination of computational methods to predict protein structures. These computational methods can be used to design self-assembling immunogens with specific epitopes or antigens
column3: which can enhance their immunogenicity and specificity.

column1: The use of computational methods in the development of self-assembling immunogens offers several advantages
column2: including the ability to design immunogens with specific properties and the ability to optimise immunogenicity and stability (; ; ). These tools could help design self-assembling immunogens by predicting the structures of antigens and then guiding the design of self-assembling proteins or peptides that best present these antigens. Overall
column3: self-assembling immunogens provide a promising avenue for fish vaccine development. Their potential
column4: coupled with the burgeoning capabilities of machine learning and deep learning tools
column5: underscores an exciting frontier in aquaculture vaccine design. Nevertheless
column6: it is crucial to maintain a balance between this optimism and a realistic understanding of the extensive validation and optimisation these novel approaches require.

column1: Compared to conventional vaccinology
column2: epitope-based chimeric (subunit) vaccines using an immunoinformatics approach offer many advantages such as not requiring microbial culturing
column3: being less expensive to develop
column4: taking less time to produce
column5: outperforming numerous wet-lab experiments
column6: and being specific and stable because they do not contain the entire organism (; ; ). Due to the occurrence of MHC variants
column7: an epitope-based vaccine targeting limited MHC alleles typically does not have the desired or equivalent effect on the fish population. Consequently
column8: very promiscuous epitopes can simultaneously bind different alleles
column9: allowing for the immunological response sought in a diverse fish population (; ; ; ). This approach
column10: termed a multi-epitope vaccine
column11: consists of a set of peptides that overlap
column12: and it induces an immune response according to a short immunogenic sequence ().

column1: A multi-epitope vaccine also uses certain design principles
column2: such as TH
column3: B-cell

column6: which a TCR from a different subset of T-cells can recognize; and many epitopes from different forms of antigens
column7: which increases the number of bacteria and viruses that can be targetedFurthermore
column8: multi-epitope vaccines involve the introduction of an adjuvant-capable element able to boost immunogenicity and offer durable immune responses. Moreover
column9: undesirable elements that might cause an abnormal immune response or a detrimental side effect can be eliminated (; ; ; ).

column1: Attention should be given to the potential of immunoinformatics and multi-epitope vaccine design to improve vaccine manufacturing capacity
column2: particularly in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs). These computational approaches
column3: which rely on accessible and affordable digital resources rather than costly laboratory infrastructure
column4: have the potential to revolutionise vaccine development in environments with limited resources. Immunoinformatics can reduce the cost of vaccine development
column5: a crucial factor for LMICs. It facilitates the antigen discovery procedure by enabling researchers to predict antigenic components of a pathogen that are likely to elicit a robust immune response using computational methods. This avoids the costly and time-consuming process of empirical experimentation
column6: making the development of vaccines more affordable for institutions with limited resources. Moreover
column7: multi-epitope vaccines
column8: which contain sequences from multiple epitopes
column9: provide additional benefits. They are typically manufactured synthetically
column10: allowing LMICs to potentially produce their own vaccines instead of relying on imports. This factor could result in substantial cost savings and increase local biotechnology expertise.

column1: In addition
column2: the reduced cold chain requirements of multi-epitope vaccines
column3: as a result of their increased thermal stability
column4: could alleviate the logistical challenges associated with vaccine distribution in LMICs. In these regions
column5: where maintaining the necessary low-temperature conditions is often challenging
column6: overcoming cold chain constraints is a pressing necessity. Lastly
column7: the adaptability of multi-epitope vaccine design can pave the way for the development of custom vaccines. These could be modified to target specific pathogen strains prevalent in certain geographic regions
column8: enabling more effective and targeted immunisation strategies. Implementing these strategies could considerably increase collaboration and information exchange in the field of immunoinformatics-based fish vaccine design. By doing so
column9: we can foster a global
column10: collaborative research community with the common objective of developing more effective and sustainable aquaculture health management solutions.

column1: Vaccination is fundamental to the sustainable management of aquaculture
column2: playing a crucial role in preventing disease outbreaks and the overuse of antibiotics in fish. However
column3: research and development on vaccines for aquatic animals are still in their infancy
column4: highlighting the pressing need for enhanced strategies. The development of species-specific vaccines requires a comprehensive understanding of fish immune systems
column5: including B-cells
column6: T-cells
column7: MHC molecules
column8: and TLR signalling pathways; however
column9: traditional approaches are often costly and time-consuming. Immunoinformatics is a prospective alternative for more effective vaccine design
column10: capable of addressing the complexities of emerging and re-emerging diseases
column11: antigenic diversity
column12: and personalised immunisation requirements. This strategy employs high-performance tools for identifying multi-epitope vaccines
column13: thereby providing a platform for examining variations in immune adaptations among fish species. However
column14: there are gaps and limitations in the discipline.

column1: Despite the promise of immunoinformatics
column2: the accuracy of these predictions is inextricably linked to the precision of the data and the sophistication of the algorithms employed. Inaccuracies in high-throughput sequence data highlight the need for robust data cleansing methods and verification protocols; without them
column3: subsequent analyses may be erroneous. Future research should concentrate on refining the computational techniques used in immunoinformatics and developing better methods for integrating these techniques with conventional laboratory testing. This will not only result in a more precise vaccine design based on epitopes
column4: but it will also promote a targeted
column5: cost-effective
column6: and safer approach to fish vaccination. Moreover
column7: the establishment of a comprehensive database of fish immune responses at the individual
column8: species
column9: and population levels could provide valuable insights for vaccine design and delivery strategies. Although immunoinformatics is a promising instrument for vaccine development
column10: it must be continuously refined and validated. In order to push the boundaries of aquatic animal health management in the coming years
column11: it will be essential to adopt this technology and simultaneously address its gaps and limitations.

column1: Considering the undeniable fact that the increase in immunoinformatics studies following COVID-19 has broadened our understanding and presented numerous potential vaccine candidates
column2: the true value of these findings cannot be confirmed without laboratory validation. Computational analysis and in silico modelling are essential instruments
column3: but they are only the first step in a multi-step process leading to the development of a viable vaccine. The majority of published immunoinformatics studies are indeed predominantly computational
column4: and their contribution to actual vaccine development can be limited in the absence of experimental validation. The prediction of potential antigens or epitopes is based on our current knowledge of protein structure and immune response
column5: both of which are still active research areas. In addition
column6: immunoinformatics tools are imperfect and frequently operate based on assumptions that may not always be true. Therefore
column7: experimental data must always validate bioinformatics predictions.

column1: Determining the protective efficacy of identified vaccine candidates in aquaculture is difficult due to the complexity of immune responses in aquatic organisms and the difference between their immune systems and those of mammals. Important is experimental validation in the form of laboratory and field evaluations. These include
column2: but are not limited to
column3: epitope mapping
column4: evaluating for immune response in cell cultures or fish
column5: and observing the progression of disease resistance in vaccinated populations. Although the redundancy of theoretical vaccine papers can be a cause for concern
column6: it is essential to remember that these studies still contribute to our collective knowledge and may serve as the foundation for future experimental research. Increasing accessibility to computational tools and techniques has democratised scientific research
column7: allowing more scientists to contribute their findings. As these findings are tested and validated in the laboratory
column8: we will be able to enhance the predictive power of immunoinformatics by refining our tools and models.

column1: To ensure that immunoinformatics research effectively contributes to the development of essential vaccines
column2: it is crucial to encourage the laboratory application of these computational findings. This can be accomplished by encouraging collaboration between computational and experimental biologists
column3: promoting funding for validation studies
column4: and emphasising the publication of studies that include both computational and experimental components. In conclusion
column5: although the influx of theoretical vaccine papers provides a plethora of potential vaccine candidates
column6: it is essential to take these findings to the next level by conducting the necessary experimental validations to advance vaccine development in the aquaculture field.

column1: 49|10.7717/peerj.11|Assessing insect responses to climate change: What are we testing for? Where should we be heading?| Andrew  Nigel R. |2013-2-12|"Attempts to assess and predict the biological impacts of climate change
column2: are two of the most important and intensive research endeavours of our time (; ; ; ). The knowledge base for the information relating to biological impacts of climate change is highly integrative
column3: covering broad disciplines of behaviour
column4: biogeography
column5: ecology
column6: evolution
column7: genetics
column8: genomics
column9: phenology and physiology (; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ). In spite of the large amounts of data that are being accumulated addressing biotic responses of organisms to climate change
column10: we are still a long way from understanding whether there are generalities across space
column11: taxa
column12: and time in terms of responses and adaptability to rapid change
column13: or whether most organisms will respond idiosyncratically
column14: and the implications of this on species interactions both within and among trophic levels. In addition
column15: the lack of long-term records (>50 years) means that much of the research today is being conducted without a strong baseline to assess species and community responses to climate change
column16: with the exception of a few charismatic species in some regions (; ; ; ; ; ; ; ).

column1: Insects are the most dominant groups of organisms on the planet in terms of species richness
column2: abundance and biomass (). The crucial roles that they play in all terrestrial and many aquatic ecosystems
column3: are well understood and are critical for ecological function and food web structure (). However
column4: our understanding of individual species is extremely limited. Most species of insects are yet to be identified (; )
column5: and many have had little
column6: if any
column7: biological information collected on them
column8: yet many species are potentially highly vulnerable to the impacts of climate change and extinction (). Nevertheless
column9: there has been a substantive amount of research dedicated to assessing potential insect responses to human-induced climate change. One of the major challenges we face is to determine the impacts of climate change on insects
column10: broadly
column11: and gather enough information to develop mechanistic models on single species (; ) and species interactions (e.g. game theory ) across a range of species to assess multi-trophic community responses to climate change.

column1: Assessing how the research community is attempting to measure climate change impacts can be done by identifying research agendas within publications and identifying trends and gaps across published literature. Here we assessed published research on climate change and insects to assess the type of research being carried out
column2: its geographic location and identify the Orders
column3: variables and factors being tested. Our intention was not to review the findings of the studies themselves
column4: as has been done by successive high profile publications and meta-analyses (; ; ; ; ; ; )
column5: but to identify questions posed and assumptions made in the published literature on insect responses to climate change.

column1: A literature search was conducted on all available published literature up to the 2nd August 2012 in Scopus (http://www.scopus.com) for literature with the keywords: “clim* change and insect”
column2: “clim* change and [each of the insect order names and generally used common names]”. Citations and abstracts were downloaded for 4704 studies. Studies that had these keywords ranged in years from 1924 to online early publications (August 2012). Each abstract was then examined to determine if it was appropriate for further assessment
column3: and a PDF reprint was then downloaded. Scopus also adds keywords in addition to the authors’ keywords
column4: with many articles not actually referring to climate change
column5: in any part of their study. If climate change (or equivalent term) was not referred to in the study (abstract or main body) it was not included in the assessment. The number of publications was then culled down to 1703 ranging in years from 1985 to online early (2nd August 2012). If studies were deemed appropriate
column6: a range of information was extracted as shown in . Number of species in each order that have been formally identified as of 2010 was generated from . The papers assessed and data extracted was uploaded onto FigShare http://dx.doi.org/10.6084/m9.figshare.105599.

Temperature was the most studied climate change factor (40% of publications; ). Surprisingly

The variables that were used to measure insect responses to climate change in the published literature could be broken into four groups (). Most publications on insect responses to climate change (more than 390 publications in each category

This analysis of current climate change research

column10: and this gap could be limiting the ability for broad generalisations to be developed quickly (). The importance of insect physiology and behaviour in the distribution and abundance of species has been known for a long time ()
column11: as has the strong association between ecology and physiology (; ). Understanding responses to climate change is a crucial area where knowledge across disciplines is vital (). This information is required to identify how scientists can build mechanistic models of responses to climate change ()

column1: Climate change studies with insects have primarily focused on changes in abundance
column2: and change in the distribution of species
column3: as these are the easiest measures of change to test
column4: especially in field conditions. However
column5: populations may have annual fluctuations and keep within their distribution
column6: but be impacted in other ways including a reduction in fitness and population viability (). Surviving insects may be of a particular genotype
column7: or have been pushed closer to their physiological tolerance. An analogous impact of this can be seen in agricultural production areas where prophylactic sprays have been used to control insects
column8: killing off the majority of individuals
column9: but a small number of a resistant individuals survive (). The resistant animals then increase in numbers and return to high abundances over a relative short time (a few seasons in some cases) with higher resistance to the chemical spray
column10: but with a much reduced genotypic variation (). From a climate change perspective
column11: more thermally tolerant populations within a species may be more resistant than others (; ; ). Such population changes exemplifies the critical role that genetic and genomic assessments of population responses to climate change play (e.g. ; ).

column1: Physiological tolerances of thermal extremes vary for different species and for different species across their range. Indeed even within a population
column2: males and females may exhibit different responses to thermal stress. A non-insect example of this is the pseudoscorpion Cordylochernes scorpioides () put under a 3.5 °C temperature stress (compared to average temperature) using a split brood experiment; the higher temperature reduced development time but also reduced size
column3: particularly of males and thus reducing sexual dimorphism. The males produced 45% less sperm and females failed to produce embryos. However
column4: when males were re-acclimated back to the ambient temperature seven days before mating
column5: sperm count did not change. However
column6: females which did not produce embryos at the higher temperature were able to produce embryos after a seven day acclimation at control temperatures
column7: and no females that were moved from the ambient temperature to the higher temperature became gravid with seven days exposure. Such cases exhibit the crucial importance of understanding fundamental biology of organisms if we are to make accurate predictions about population responses to a rapidly changing climate.

column1: Ectotherms may also stay within their habitat when there are a variety of microhabitats to choose from. Most species modelling is based on average temperature readings that are not directly in the habitat of the organism being studied. For example
column2: WORLDCLIM derived climate data () are from weather stations located a few metres off the ground
column3: in open
column4: clear microhabitats not directly associated with the primary habitat of organisms. Therefore the use of such data may lead to very different interpretation than from data collected from an organism’s habitat. For example
column5: the warming tolerance (WT) and thermal safety margins (TSM) of the meat ant Iridomyrmex purpueus (Andrew et al. unpublished data) based on weather station annual average temperatures were 25.8 °C (WT) and 13.78 °C (TSM). These values declined to 19.5 °C and 7.51 °C when based on summer average temperatures
column6: and declined further to 7.81 °C (WT) and −4.2 °C (TSM) when based on temperatures recorded at the nest site during summer between 10 am and 4 pm when the ants are under the highest thermal stress.

column1: There is a fundamental difficulty in interpreting biological responses to rapid climate change when the biology of species is not well known and experiments are only carried out over short time periods. Most studies which are considered long term usually have no more than 10 years of accumulated data
column2: with older morphological and potential DNA data for single species found in museum collections (). Very few directly comparable collected datasets have data constantly recorded over multiple decades and centuries
column3: keeping consistent methodologies
column4: collecting sufficient data
column5: or sampling comprehensively across all biota (). Two of the most comprehensive datasets we currently have for insects are from Rothamstead in the UK
column6: and of locust outbreaks in China. In Rothamstead
column7: moth records date back to 1933 with light traps at 80 sites around the UK
column8: and aphid populations have been monitored via suction traps since 1964 when a national network of sixteen aphid collection sites were dispersed throughout the UK (). In China
column9: migratory locusts records were haphazardly collected over a 1
column10: 910 year period () from historical documents. The general lack of long-term datasets globally and across taxa challenges our ability to make strong comparative generalisations about species responses and the biological impacts of climate change.

column1: It is clear from the current assessment that changes in abundance and range/distribution shift of single species are the main focus in insect responses to climate change. Therefore for future studies
column2: it is vital that researchers identify the type of habitat they are working in to continue this type of work (‘non-specified’ habitats accounted for 28% of publications in this assessment). We also recommend an emphasis on the causes of these changes is required by the assessment of insect population dynamics
column3: ecology
column4: physiology
column5: behaviour
column6: and genetic/genomic analyses. Many species will more likely adapt to a changing climate in-situ
column7: and what impact this has on their recruitment and population dynamics is unclear. When single species studies are conducted it would be instructive to explore modeled predictions in research papers to identify the impacts of a changing climate on insects. In its most simplistic form species predictions could be undertaken using species distribution modeling or vulnerability indices (). When assemblages are assessed then community change predictions would also be of use. This can be done using relationship changes between trophic levels of species and competition among species using existing tools such as game theory ()
column8: or using statistical relationships based on changes in assemblage composition (fitting predictive models in mvabund ) or functional traits (Brown AM
column9: Warton DI
column10: Andrew NR
column11: Binns M
column12: Cassis G & Gibb H
column13: unpublished data).

column1: Even today we are still just beginning to understand how insects will respond to a rapidly changing climate
column2: but the current trends of publications give a good basis for how we are attempting to assess insect responses. There is a crucial need for a broader study of ecological
column3: behavioural
column4: physiological and life history responses to be addressed across a greater range of geographic locations
column5: particularly Asia
column6: Australia/Oceania
column7: Africa
column8: and South America
column9: and in areas of high human population growth and habitat modification.

column1: We are not advocating that research should be done on under-represented taxa to merely equilibrate the research done on each taxa relative to the number of identified species. A wider range of taxa should be studied to attempt to identify how generalised trends in response to climate change are across taxa. This also exemplifies the need for understanding the role of phylogenetic relatedness and functional/morphological traits across a range of species and within/among communities (; ; ). Future work should consider these issues critically
column2: and some exemplar questions to pose include: Are the range movements measured in European butterfly’s in native habitats
column3: an umbrella response to other more-poorly understood taxa from Europe (including Coleoptera
column4: and Hymenoptera) from those same habitats? Is this trend applicable in other parts of the world? Will individual species
column5: taxa or functional groups respond in a highly variable way within and among regions? Rigorous testing of such predictions are required globally to develop a better understanding of biotic responses to climate change.

column1: The implementation of an IoT-based sustainable environment management system in poultry farms involves the deployment of various sensors to monitor and regulate environmental conditions. These sensors play a critical role in ensuring optimal conditions for poultry health
column2: growth
column3: and overall farm productivity. However
column4: there are specific concerns and constraints associated with using sensors in such environments
column5: particularly due to potential animal interactions and their impact on measurement precision.

column1: (a) Animal interaction with sensors: Poultry’s natural curiosity and behavior might lead them to interact with or peck at sensors. This interaction could potentially disrupt sensor functionality or damage the sensors themselves. Steps need to be taken to design sensor enclosures or mounting strategies that minimize such interference.
(b) Measurement precision and accuracy: Poultry interactions or environmental factors might affect the accuracy and precision of sensor measurements. For instance
column2: animals crowding around a temperature sensor might block proper airflow
column3: leading to inaccurate readings. Careful sensor placement and calibration are essential to mitigate these issues.

column1: (c) Data noise and interpretation: Poultry movement or interactions could introduce noise into the sensor data
column2: making it challenging to distinguish between normal behavior and potential anomalies. Advanced data filtering and analysis techniques may be required to extract meaningful insights.

column1: (d) Sensor maintenance and replacement: In an agricultural setting
column2: sensors can be exposed to dust
column3: debris
column4: and moisture
column5: potentially affecting their longevity and reliability. Regular maintenance and timely replacement of damaged sensors are crucial to ensure continuous monitoring.

column1: (e) Energy efficiency: Ensuring sensors operate efficiently without causing stress to the poultry is important. Bright LED lights on sensors
column2: for instance
column3: might disturb the birds
column4: affecting their behavior and health.

column1: (f) Integration with farm practices: The deployment of sensors should align with the overall management practices of the poultry farm. Sensors should complement existing protocols and practices rather than disrupt them.
Addressing these constraints requires a combination of thoughtful sensor design
column2: strategic placement
column3: data analytics techniques
column4: and a deep understanding of poultry behavior. Despite these challenges
column5: IoT-based sustainable environment management holds significant promise for improving poultry farm operations and animal welfare.

column1: The diverse effects of mast seeding - the synchronous intermittent production of large seed crops (; ) - on ecological communities around the world are illustrated well using rodent populations in forests. Yellow-necked mice (Apodemus flavicollis) have increased winter survival and rapid population growth following mast seeding in oak (Quercus robur) and hornbeam (Carpinus betulus) in Biolowieza Primeval Forest in Poland
column2: with a corresponding increase and peak in predator populations such as the pine marten (Martes martes) a few months to a year later (; ). When rodent prey becomes scarce following an irruption
column3: pine martens compensate by increasing consumption of alternative resources including birds and berries (). Forest bird populations are also limited by predation by raptors
column4: e.g.
column5: buzzards
column6: which undergo prey-switching from rodents to birds following a crash in rodent abundance (). In oak (Quercus spp.) forest in the eastern U.S.
column7: white-footed mice (Peromyscus leucopus) respond similarly to acorn masts
column8: with increased winter survival and breeding success (). However
column9: in this case mice are also predators
column10: playing an important role in suppressing gypsy moth (Lymantria dispar)
column11: an invasive and outbreaking species. Defoliation by gypsy moths can delay and reduce mast production
column12: creating a negative feedback loop for the mast-driven mouse populations with catastrophic consequences for tree growth and survival as well as reduced mouse abundance (). However
column13: high densities of mice lead to increases in tick (Ixodes scapularis) populations
column14: and the associated spread of Lyme disease in humans ().

column1: In South America
column2: rodent outbreaks are associated with emerging viral diseases such as hantavirus (). These outbreaks occur after bamboo (Chusquea sp. and Merostachys sp.) masts
column3: but may also follow heavy rainfall events ()
column4: emphasizing the need for ecologically sound pest forecasting models. Introduced bamboo (Melocanna baccifera) is a well-used non-timber forest product in India
column5: but rodent (Rattus sp. and Mus sp.) migration towards agricultural areas following mast events can have devastating effects on standing crops and stored grains
column6: as well as increasing the risk of infection for rodent-borne diseases (). It has also been suggested that masting by introduced Asian bamboos into North America
column7: particularly the Pacific Northwest
column8: poses a health risk to humans as a result of population irruptions and subsequent dispersal of deer mice (Peromyscus maniculatus
column9: a hantavirus carrier) following mast seed depletion ().

column2: invasive house mouse (Mus musculus) populations undergo episodic outbreaks in response to high seedfall
column3: particularly in the heavy masts of native beech forest (Nothofagus species) (; ; ) or rimu (Dacrydium cupressinum) (). Irruptions in beech forest occur predominantly in late summer to autumn (i.e.

column1: Models for irrupting mouse populations require good predictions of the size and timing of masts. Temperature and rainfall in the years before the mast event are almost always the primary cue for mast seeding ().  proposed a generic and widely applicable model using the change in temperature in a set period over the previous two years (ΔT) as the sole predictor. This model offered much improved predictions over other simple models and has been shown to be applicable to a wide range of plant species around the world
