column1: Management to mitigate the adverse effects of irrupting small consumers should be optimised to ensure that the limited resources available to managers are implemented to have the greatest impact and to meet control objectives. In this work
column2: we offer insights into the dynamics of an irrupting rodent population undergoing broad-scale aerial control in an ecosystem with pulsed resources. It is clear from our results that the timing and frequency of control can affect the time spent above the plague threshold. While we have focused on mouse irruptions in beech forest
column3: insights from this work have clear ramifications for dynamics of predators
column4: prey and disease spread in other systems with climate-driven pulsed resources and outbreaking consumers worldwide.

column1: There is currently no broad-scale control of mice alone in mainland New Zealand
column2: due
column3: in part
column4: to the higher prioritisation of other vertebrate pest targets. In addition
column5: achieving a large reduction in mouse abundance at large spatial scale is difficult with the control tools currently available on the mainland (). As a result
column6: considerably less is known about the optimal control practices when targeting mice
column7: compared to other small mammals. In a year of high seedfall and in the absence of control
column8: our model predicts that mouse populations exceed the plague threshold and drive a rapid decline in the amount of available seed. As seedfall is depleted
column9: mouse density decreases but still remains above the plague threshold by the end of the mast year. As well as the local impacts of this prolonged high mouse density
column10: there is a risk that after seedfall depletion mice may disperse out of beech forest into other adjacent areas or habitats (; ). Dispersal of outbreaking rodents has been reported globally and contributes to disease spread (; )
column11: damage to agriculture (; ; ) or declines in indigenous biota (). However
column12: conducting high level mouse control in mast years can successfully reduce mouse density to below plague level and to an extent where seed abundance is closer to that observed in the absence of mice. This should provide more food resource for other indigenous consumers competing for seedfall
column13: and reduce the risks of dispersal and predation by mice and stoats.

column1: Our model quantifies relative mouse abundance as an index of captures per 100 trap nights. Measuring actual mouse density in the field is difficult and costly
column2: therefore indices such as C/100TN or a rodent tracking index (i.e.
column3: mean percentage of ‘run-through’ tracking tunnels containing mouse prints per line) are commonly used (; ). Quantifying relative abundance in this way facilitates model parameterisation and validation of model predictions using observation data that is more readily available to managers. Our model assumes logistic-type density dependence in the mouse population
column4: which has been shown to be a good description of density-dependent dynamics in small rodent populations (see e.g.
column5: ). The best-fit parameters were similar to those used in other models of house mouse population dynamics
column6: for example the model of  also had a positive density-independent growth term and a negative density-dependent growth term.

column1: The three values chosen for peak bait availability to model low
column2: moderate and high control levels
column3: gave control success rates (measured as percentage kill) in the ranges 50–60%
column5: respectively. How these ranges relate to success for real control operations
column6: that aim to suppress as opposed to eradicate mouse populations
column7: will depend on control objectives. To date
column8: very little has been published on the density-impact relationships for mouse abundance and biodiversity in New Zealand. These knowledge deficits currently present a major barrier for managers
column9: both in terms of setting conservation aims and measuring success of mouse control operations. However
column10: our modelling framework provides predictions of mouse abundance and kill rates that can be readily applied to real control operations
column11: as research advances in this area and new thresholds for successful suppression of mouse populations on mainland New Zealand are set.

column1: Our model predicts that the optimal timing for mouse control in beech forest
column2: with respect to minimising plague time
column3: is mid-September. This timing fits within the recommended range for broad-scale aerial 1
column4: 080 control targeting rats and stoats (). Across all the regimes considered here
column5: the seedfall driven biannual-biennial regime gave the greatest reduction in plague time and plague years for low and moderate control levels
column6: although the differences between regimes were relatively small. Managers will need to take additional factors into consideration when selecting an optimal approach
column7: for instance the benefits of seedfall driven control need to be weighed against the cost and effort associated with collecting the necessary seedfall data
column8: while temperature data required for the climate driven regime is readily available.

column1: An important advantage of this modelling approach is its simplicity and generality. This work considered a case study of optimal control for mouse populations in New Zealand hard beech forest
column2: where the aim is to minimise plague time. Different compositions of masting species in other forests will drive slightly different seedfall and mouse dynamics. For example
column3: reported a later onset of mouse population increase due to heavy rimu (D. cupressium) masting occurring two to three months later than in beech forest. Therefore
column4: optimal control conditions will likely differ for other forest compositions. Nonetheless
column5: our model and approach could be easily adapted for application to other habitats with climate-driven pulsed resources
column6: for which temperature
column7: resource
column8: and consumer abundance data is available for parameterisation. Similarly
column9: it would be straightforward to alter our model to account for additional drivers of population irruptions
column10: such as the effect of rainfall alongside bamboo masts on rodent outbreaks in South America ()
column11: or to adjust the thresholds for conducting control.

column1: Interactions between mice and their competitors or predators are captured implicitly in the model via the density-independent and density-dependent growth rates. However
column2: making these interactions explicit by including rate of change of equations for other interacting species in the system
column3: could offer additional insights
column4: e.g.
column5: into cascading responses across different trophic levels. For instance
column6: an equation for stoat density could be included in the model to consider the effectiveness of targeting mice as vectors for secondary poisoning of stoats. In addition
column7: this work could be extended to relate the mouse densities expected under different control regimes to outcomes for indigenous biota
column8: as this will be another key factor for determining the optimal approach and assessing whether conservation objectives are being met.

column1: In this work
column2: we have attempted to maintain approximately equal costs across each control scenario by comparing regimes with similar long-term frequency (e.g.
column3: annual vs. biennual-biannual). We assume that low
column4: moderate and high effort poisoning will likely have similar overall costs since the operational costs (e.g.
column5: aerial transport and/or application
column6: labour costs) are relatively large and constant compared to equipment costs (e.g.
column7: traps
column8: bait). However
column9: a cost-benefit analysis of higher and lower frequency control regimes could also be undertaken.

column1: Our results assume the same distribution of summer temperatures over a 1
column2: 000 year time-series; however the effects of climate change could be investigated by relaxing this assumption. If consumer-resource dynamics are altered due to climate change
column3: our model could be useful for guiding how management timing and intensity should be modified to still be effective in reducing the mouse population. For example
column4: our model would be compatible with a recently developed framework
column5: based on the  model
column6: that uses climate projections to assess whether climate change might affect the frequency or spatial extent of beech forest masts ().

column1: With large-scale predator control campaigns causing pest control to ramp up across New Zealand’s mainland ()
column2: there are opportunities to answer increasingly complex questions around the impacts of broad-scale invasive pest management for ecosystems
column3: and to determine optimal control practices. We have attempted to fill an important knowledge gap concerning broad-scale control of irrupting mouse populations in masting beech forest
column4: however a deeper understanding of climate-driven consumer-resource dynamics and control outcomes will benefit managers globally. In a rapidly changing world
column5: having the modelling tools in place to make good predictions about the behaviour of such systems
column6: puts us in a stronger position to anticipate and mitigate the potential adverse effects of change.

column1: 52|10.7717/peerj.16516|Landscape predictors influencing livestock depredation by leopards in and around Annapurna Conservation Area
column2: Nepal| Lamichhane  Saurav |2023-12-13|"Addressing the global challenge of human-wildlife conflict is an urgent issue faced by managers and policymakers (; ). This conflict arises from the shared use of resources by humans
column3: livestock
column4: and wild predators (; ; ; ). In cases of human-large carnivore conflict
column5: animals kill livestock (; ) and occasionally pose a threat to humans (; )
column6: leading to their persecution by people (). Carnivore habitats are increasingly fragmented and resource-scarce due to human population growth
column7: anthropogenic activity reduced wild prey
column8: and changing land use
column9: resulting in human-carnivore conflicts (; ; ; ). For instance
column10: rapid human population growth and habitat encroachment on common leopard (Panthera pardus
column11: referred to as “leopard” hereafter) habitats have led to reduced prey availability and habitat fragmentation. Those pressures drive leopards to forage closer to human settlements
column12: leading to frequent human-leopard conflicts (; ; ).

column1: The leopard is categorized as Vulnerable by the International Union for Conservation of Nature and Natural Resources (IUCN) Red List () and listed as  in CITES law. The National Red List of Mammals categorizes it as an endangered species. The legal status of this species in Nepal is Protected () under the National Parks and Wildlife Conservation Act 1973 (). Its populations are declining throughout most of their range (; )
column2: leading to isolation
column3: with some Asian subspecies now assessed as endangered and critically endangered on the IUCN Red List. They are widely distributed across Africa and Asia
column4: but their historical range has seen a significant 61% reduction (). The leopard is distributed in Nepal from the lowland Terai region to the mid-hill areas (; )
column5: and recent observations indicate sightings at elevations of up to 4
column6: 500 meters in Annapurna Conservation Area (Bikram Shrestha
column8: pers. comm.). However
column9: their population status and occupancy are poorly understood ().

column1: Numerous studies have been conducted to examine the spatio-temporal patterns and frequency of leopard predation on livestock in Nepal (; ; ; ; ; ; ). The Department of National Parks and Wildlife Conservation () reported that leopards were responsible for 78% of livestock kill incidents in Nepal. Furthermore
column2: identified the mid-hills regions as the areas with the highest risk for leopard predation on livestock. Likewise
column3: similar studies have been conducted outside of Nepal (; ; ; ; ).

column1: Few studies have been conducted on the influential factors affecting leopard predation on livestock (; ; ; ) and mapping conflict hotspots (; ). However
column2: all of these studies were conducted outside of Nepal. So far
column3: studies that employ a robust sampling and modelling framework have not been conducted in Nepal. Identifying zones prone to leopard predation on livestock is crucial for efficient conflict mitigation and leopard conservation. Focusing resources and efforts on these areas allows for targeted livestock protection
column4: reduces retaliatory killings of leopards
column5: and engages communities in sustainable coexistence strategies (). This approach addresses the complex challenges of human-leopard conflicts while safeguarding both local livelihoods and leopard populations (; ; ). Reckoning this fact
column6: we have chosen the Gandaki Province of Nepal as our research site for these unexplored studies
column7: particularly as it overlaps with the Annapurna Conservation Area. This region
column8: situated in the mid-hills of Nepal
column9: is a notable focal point for human-leopard conflict (; ).

column1: To address the aforementioned research gaps
column2: we aim to determine important landscape variables that can influence leopard predation on livestock by employing a binomial generalized linear model and map high-conflict-prone areas by utilizing significant environmental
column3: anthropogenic
column4: and topographic variables through MaxEnt modeling. In this study
column5: we established hypotheses to investigate how different landscape factors influence leopard predation on livestock in Nepal. The study’s primary objective was empirically assessing and confirming these hypotheses through data analysis and statistical methods. In addition
column6: we conducted descriptive analyses to understand the spatio-temporal and predation patterns of leopards on livestock. The study’s results are expected to enhance efforts to reduce human-leopard conflicts and promote coexistence between humans and leopards.

column1: The Annapurna Conservation Area (ACA)
column2: established in 1992
column3: is Nepal’s largest protected area
column4: covering 7
column5: 629 sq. km. It is located in the hills and mountains of west-central Nepal (83057′E
column6: 28050′N) and is managed by the National Trust for Nature Conservation (NTNC). The Annapurna Himalayas have various ecosystems
column7: from subtropical woods to trans-Himalayan freezing deserts. The ACA is rich in biodiversity
column8: home to 1

column18: and keep their traditional rights and access to the usage of natural resources. The area is also a popular trekking destination for visitors from all over the world. To ease its management
column19: ACA is divided into seven-unit conservation offices: Ghandruk

column1: To model the spatial spread and extent of livestock depredation

For binomial distribution
column3: the presence/absence of the conflict incidents (response variable) was recorded on a binary coding basis (1 = presence of a conflict incident in a grid
column4: 0 = absence of a conflict incident in a grid). The absence of conflict was taken as the reference variable when running the models. We selected the GLM due to its suitability for modeling binary response data () and its balance between simplicity and interpretability

column8: we used all the variables for model construction ().

column1: To select the most parsimonious model among the set of models

column3: all possible models were constructed and ranked based on small-sampled AICc (). The final model was obtained by averaging the top candidate models (delta AIC ≤ 2) (). To test the predictive performance of the dominant model
column4: we generated the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve and area under the curve (AUC) value using the package ‘ROCR’ (). We obtained high model uncertainty among the selected predictors indicated by similar model weight. Thus
column5: we did full model averaging ( and ) to compute the effect of predictors. Additionally
column6: we created an odds ratio plot to visualize the effect sizes of the predictors. The plot displayed the odds ratios with 95% confidence intervals
column7: aiding in the interpretation of predictor variable impacts on the presence or absence of conflict incidents using the package ‘ggplot 2’ ().

column1: We generated the conflict probability map through the maximum entropy (MaxEnt) modelling. MaxEnt is a widely used approach (; )
column2: demonstrating the best predictive power across all sample sizes (; ). The occurrence points of conflict were filtered by keeping at least 100 m of the distance between locations
column3: thereby minimizing spatial autocorrelation (; ). For this purpose
column4: the georeferenced points were spatially thinned using the SpThin package (). Using this technique
column5: we utilized the spatially filtered conflict occurrence points alongside the variables with high significance in the GLM models to predict the conflict probability in the study area (). The MaxEnt program (version 3.4.4) was set up to use 70% of the data points for training and 30% for model validation. A maximum iteration limit of 1
column6: 000 was chosen
column7: and models were replicated ten times to generate average model information (). We assessed the model accuracy through the threshold-independent (AUC-ROC) method and the threshold-dependent true skill statistics (TSS) method. AUC value ranges from 0 to 1. Similarly
column8: TSS ranges from −1 to +1
column9: with the model with a higher value in both representing good predictive performance (). The continuous probability map obtained from MaxEnt was reclassified in ArcGis 10.5 to obtain a distinct probability of conflict (low = <0.40
column10: medium = 0.4–0.6
column11: and high = >0.6).

column1: During the three years
column2: 439 livestock were reportedly killed by leopards in three districts. Goats (Capra aegagrus hircus) were the most predated livestock (66.5%)
column3: followed by sheep (Ovis aries) (22.5%)
column4: cows/oxen (Bos taurus) (7%)
column5: and buffalo (Bubalus bubalis) (4%). The analysis of data across slope and elevation revealed that about 80% of kill sites occurred within a 30-degree slope
column6: and about 90% occurred within 3
column7: 000 m elevation. In inside conservation areas
column8: there was a lower percentage of livestock depredation (41%) than in outside conservation areas (59%). It is apparent from the kill sites that about 65% of livestock depredation in the study area was recorded within 1 km of a road. The number of livestock depredation events decreased with distance from roads. Similar patterns were observed when conflict locations were analyzed in relation to distance from rivers and settlements. About 70% of livestock depredation was observed within 1 km of a river and 85% within 1 km of a settlement ().

column1: In our study
column2: we examined the significance of various variables regarding the presence or absence of livestock depredation by leopards. River length exhibited a significant positive association with livestock depredation (ß = 0.3348; p = 0.0405)
column3: indicating that areas with longer rivers were more susceptible to leopard attacks on livestock. Additionally
column4: slope displayed a significant negative relation with livestock depredation (ß = −0.5934; p = 0.0113)
column5: suggesting that areas characterized by steeper terrain experienced fewer instances of leopard predation. The proximity to the protected area demonstrated a significant negative correlation with livestock depredation (ß = −0.3768; p = 0.0486). This suggests that areas closer to protected areas experienced elevated risks of livestock depredation.

column1: Moreover
column2: elevation demonstrated a significant positive relation (ß = 1.1444; p = 0.0255)
column3: implying that higher elevations were linked to an increased likelihood of leopard attacks on livestock. Furthermore
column4: the forest area exhibited a significant positive relation with livestock depredation (ß = 0.5033; p = 0.0462)
column5: indicating that larger forested areas heightened the risk of leopard predation. Similarly
column6: the area of agricultural land demonstrated a significant positive association with livestock depredation (ß = 0.3335; p = 0.0229)
column7: suggesting that larger agricultural areas were more vulnerable to leopard attacks on livestock. The distance to settlements variable exhibited a statistically significant negative relationship with livestock depredation (ß = −1.1678; p = 0.0127) (). This suggests that areas closer to human settlements experienced a higher risk of leopard predation. The dominant model (GLM with binomial structure) had a receiver operating curve value of 0.85 (83.55% accuracy). We also generated an odds ratio plot to provide a visual representation of the effect sizes of our predictor variables. This plot displayed odds ratios along with their corresponding 95% confidence intervals
column8: serving as a valuable tool for interpreting the impacts of these predictor variables on the presence or absence of conflict incidents ().

column3: bare ground

column6: elevation

column8: and slope appeared as the dominant model for leopard attacks on livestock in the study area (.

column1: A total of 289.11 km2 (approximately 5% of the total study area) was considered high risk for livestock depredation by leopards. Kaski district had the largest high conflict-prone area (approximately 122.28 km2)
column2: followed by Lamjung (approximately 107.01 km2) and Myagdi (approximately 59.82 km2). Similarly
column3: 18% of the high-risk area was incorporated within Annapurna CA (). The model accuracy was quite good
column4: with an average AUC of 0.82 ± 0.09 and an average TSS 0.68 ± 0.08.

column1: Our study comprehensively analyzes the multifaceted interactions between leopards and livestock within our research area. By delving into various aspects of leopard predation patterns and the environmental factors that influence them
column2: we aim to contribute significantly to understanding human-leopard conflicts and developing effective mitigation strategies.

column1: This study revealed that leopards primarily preyed on goats
column2: followed by sheep and cattle. This predation pattern aligns with the livestock population ratios in the study districts
column3: as the Ministry of Agricultural and Livestock Development Nepal () reported in 2021: 50% goats
column4: 7% sheep
column5: 29% cattle
column6: and 14% buffalo. In contrast
column7: sheep were the second most vulnerable prey livestock to leopards in the study area despite constituting the smallest proportion of the population compared to cattle and buffalo. Goats and sheep
column8: as opposed to cattle and buffalo
column9: exhibit less defensive behavior ()
column10: making them easier prey for leopards to capture. Although the diet of the leopard ranges mainly from small to some very large prey (>100 kg) ()
column11: they generally prefer species weighing between 10–40 kg () and 2–25 kg (). As a result
column12: they exhibit similar size preferences when preying on goats and sheep. Moreover
column13: the lesser killing of cattle and buffalos in the study area might be attributed to their large body size
column14: which is not preferred by leopards (; ).

column1: Our analysis showed strong variations in livestock losses among seasons
column2: months
column3: and times. Most leopard attacks on livestock occur during the dry season (winter and summer). Our results coincide with studies conducted by  in the Himalayan region of India and  in the Terai region of Nepal. The dry season corresponds with the planting and harvesting of agricultural crops. During these months
column4: farmers are busy with crop harvesting and production
column5: leaving their livestock ranging freely (). Due to the cold temperatures in the winter
column6: managing livestock guarding also becomes difficult for herders. Negligence by herders is regarded as one of the main contributing factors to livestock losses by predators ().

column1: Moreover
column2: the months of the winter season (November and December) receive less rainfall and thus require free grazing of livestock in forest areas due to the limited availability of fodder for stall feeding
column3: making them more vulnerable to leopard attacks (). Several studies have reported leopard behavior as nocturnal (; ; ). However
column4: some studies also reported higher livestock depredation by leopards during the daytime (; ). In our study
column5: the timing of leopard attacks on livestock also varied significantly
column6: with peak occurrence during the evening time (4 PM–8 PM)
column7: similar to the studies conducted by  at Machiara National Park
column8: Pakistan. Multiple factors
column9: such as animal husbandry practices and the availability of alternate prey
column10: influence the timing of leopard depredation on livestock (). To sum up
column11: our study emphasizes the significant impact of seasonality and daily timing on leopard predation of livestock
column12: echoing findings from various regions. These insights underscore the complex interplay of factors
column13: including animal husbandry practices and prey availability
column14: in shaping the patterns of leopard attacks on livestock.

column1: This study revealed that forest area
column2: agricultural area
column3: slope
column4: distance to protected areas
column5: distance to settlements
column6: length of the river
column7: and elevation affect livestock kills by leopards (). In India
column8: the availability of water resources emerged as a spatial factor influencing leopard predation rates
column9: as previously highlighted by . This observation resonates with similar research conducted in arid ecosystems of Africa
column10: where water availability has consistently been identified as a primary driver of human-carnivore conflicts (; ). Our study corroborates these established findings. Specifically
column11: we found a statistically significant positive correlation between the length of the river and the probability of livestock depredation by leopards. Rural villages heavily rely on livestock rearing as a prominent profession in our study area
column12: which is characterized by higher-sloped geographical terrain and lush forests. Previous studies
column13: such as
column14: have reported leopard predation on livestock in rugged areas of Bhutan. Our study revealed a contrasting pattern where the probability of livestock depredation decreased with increasing slope. This finding can be attributed to the already prevalent sloped geographical terrain in our study area
column15: which may act as a natural deterrent for leopards
column16: making it more difficult to access and prey upon livestock. Interestingly
column17: the study conducted by  also observed high occurrences of leopards on gentle slopes
column18: indicating that leopard behavior and habitat preferences can vary across different regions. These findings highlight the complexity of the relationship between slope and leopard predation and emphasize the need for further research to understand better the factors influencing livestock depredation in diverse geographical settings.

column1: With increasing distance from protected areas
column2: we observed a decreasing trend in leopard predation on livestock. Our findings are consistent with studies conducted by  in South Africa. However
column3: documented contrasting results and found that conflicts with leopards were higher as they moved farther away from reserves. Factors such as habitat availability
column4: prey availability
column5: and local community dynamics can play significant roles in shaping the patterns of leopard predation on livestock. Further research is needed to understand the underlying mechanisms driving these variations and develop context-specific strategies for mitigating human-leopard conflicts.

column1: This study revealed that elevation was a significant factor for leopard attacks on livestock
column2: implying that higher elevations were linked to an increased likelihood of leopard attacks on livestock. Leopards can be found throughout Nepal
column3: from the low-lying Terai (100 m) to the Himalayan peaks (4
column4: 000 m) (). This finding aligns with recent research conducted by
column5: which suggests that leopards are experiencing a shift in their habitat preferences in response to the effects of climate change. As temperature rises and ecosystems undergo transformations
column6: leopards may be compelled to explore higher-elevation areas in search of suitable habitats and prey resources. Consequently
column7: the increased presence of leopards at higher elevations may bring a higher incidence of livestock attacks in these regions.

column1: In the rural villages inside the Annapurna Conservation Area (ACA)
column2: livestock grazing practices are prevalent throughout the year
column3: with cattle sheds strategically positioned at different elevation ranges within the forest. This availability of prey
column4: facilitated by the existing livestock husbandry practices
column5: may be one of the reasons why the probability of leopard predation on livestock is positively associated with the forested area. The mid-hills region of Nepal has witnessed an increase in forest area in recent decades
column6: which could further contribute to higher carnivore-related livestock depredations
column7: as found by .

column1: Despite this trend
column2: studies have also shown that leopards exhibit adaptability to human-modified landscapes (; ; ). A study conducted by  in Nepal’s Kathmandu district further strengthens this assertion
column3: revealing a substantial leopard presence in areas characterized by high human population densities. This underscores the leopard’s capacity to thrive in proximity to human settlements. Furthermore
column4: the settlements in our study area are mostly scattered and not so densely populated
column5: allowing leopards to dwell nearby. This adaptability
column6: in conjunction with the easy availability of livestock near the settlements
column7: likely contributes to the observed increase in livestock depredation with a decrease in distance from settlements.

column1: Our findings support a positive association between livestock depredation and agricultural land
column2: consistent with previous studies by  and
column3: which reported higher leopard predation on livestock in human-use landscapes
column4: especially in areas with agricultural activities. Agricultural lands provide a potential food source for leopards
column5: including domestic livestock like cattle
column6: buffalo
column7: goats
column8: and chickens. The remarkable adaptive nature of leopards () allows them to exploit these human-modified habitats for hunting and acquiring additional food resources.  even reported a higher contribution of livestock to the leopard diet from human-use landscapes. The ability of leopards to cover large home ranges and their highly adaptive nature () enables them to persist in areas with varying degrees of human presence (). Despite their adaptive behaviors
column9: leopards may avoid areas with higher human settlement density due to potential conflicts and increased human activity
column10: thereby limiting their predation on livestock in such regions. Hence
column11: our results suggest that while leopards may exploit agricultural land for hunting opportunities
column12: they may also exhibit caution and avoid areas with denser human populations. This dual pattern intricate interplay of ecological and behavioral factors underscores the complex dynamics of human-leopard interactions
column13: necessitating further research on spatial dynamics of settlements and their impact on human-leopard conflicts.

column1: Our findings offer valuable insights for managing human-leopard conflicts in the study area and beyond. The conflict risk maps generated are instrumental in pinpointing high-risk regions where resources and policy changes can be strategically directed. Key mitigation strategies include enhancing corral structures
column2: reinforcing stock guarding
column3: and discouraging livestock grazing in vulnerable areas. Collaborative efforts among stakeholders are essential for successful coexistence between communities and leopards. Effective guarding is crucial in the winter and summer months
column4: especially in December and March when leopard attacks on cattle are at their worst. Building safer or predator-proof corrals and emphasizing stall-feeding techniques
column5: particularly in locations with forest fringes
column6: are necessary. It is also necessary to avoid grazing livestock inside dense forests and near water bodies. If grazing is unavoidable in such areas
column7: we advise using communally coordinated herding practices. Developing a conflict mitigation strategy that incorporates cooperation between divisional forest offices
column8: local populations
column9: and conservationists will be essential to encourage coexistence between people and leopards. We recommend a detailed and extensive study to understand the habitat utilization pattern of leopards at a finer scale through intensive camera trapping and radio telemetry methods in the study area.

column1: 54|10.7717/peerj.16629|Impact of livestock grazing management on carbon stocks: a case study in sparse elm woodlands of semi-arid lands| Tang  Yi |2023-12-12|"Global warming
column2: which is primarily caused by excessive emissions of CO2
column3: has attracted worldwide attention (). One important strategy for reducing anthropogenic CO2 emissions is carbon storage (). The terrestrial biosphere
column4: a repository of approximately 2
column5: 000 Gt of carbon
column6: plays a crucial role in carbon storage (). Vegetation
column7: as a major component of the terrestrial biosphere
column8: is responsible for a large proportion of carbon storage (; ). Trees
column9: as the largest and longest-lived plants
column10: are particularly important for carbon storage (). Vegetation plays a pivotal role in regulating the carbon cycle and storing carbon primarily by absorbing CO2 through photosynthesis and subsequently releasing some of it back into the atmosphere through respiration (). Therefore
column11: strategies that enhance vegetation carbon storage can effectively mitigate global warming (; ).

column1: Arid and semi-arid areas together constitute almost one-third of the world’s land area (). In these regions
column2: livestock grazing is a prominent human activity. It is considered as an effective approach for regulating carbon storage in soil and vegetation (; ). The effects of grazing exclusion and its duration on carbon storage in soil and vegetation have been studied in previous studies (; ; ). For example
column3: the effects of fencing on carbon stocks were explored in degraded alpine grasslands ()
column4: and the duration of exclosure practice was investigated in a degraded grassland in China ().

column1: Grazing management plays a pivotal role in shaping the carbon dynamics of terrestrial ecosystems (). At the most fundamental level
column2: grazing alters plant biomass
column3: which directly impacts the amount of carbon that vegetation can sequester from the atmosphere (). For instance
column4: moderate grazing can stimulate plant growth due to the removal of old plant material
column5: which can pave the way for new shoots. This often results in a net increase in photosynthetic activity and
column6: thus
column7: increased carbon capture. In contrast
column8: the balance of different plant species
column9: dictated in part by grazing intensity
column10: can also influence the carbon storage potential of an ecosystem
column11: as different plants possess varying carbon sequestration capacities (). Therefore
column12: understanding the intricacies of grazing management is paramount not just for ecosystem health
column13: but also for optimizing the carbon sequestration potential of landscapes.

column1: Recently
column2: the difference in the effects of livestock grazing management on ecosystems has attracted much attention (). Studies have reported on the effects of seasonal prohibited grazing and grazing intensities on soil organic carbon stock in Mongolian grasslands (; ). Other management practices
column3: including grazing period
column4: number of burns
column5: and slashes
column6: have been considered in a dry tropical region of western Mexico (). Although the effects of several grazing management practices have been reported separately in case studies
column7: the role of grazing management in carbon stock
column8: especially in vegetation
column9: has not been fully compared.

column1: Given the fragmented nature of current research on grazing management’s effect on carbon stock
column2: there’s a pressing need for a more comprehensive approach to these investigations. The model simulation approach presents a robust avenue to address this gap. Through modeling
column3: we can holistically evaluate the impacts of varied livestock grazing management practices against a unified benchmark
column4: enabling more thorough comparisons. Firstly
column5: models in plant population and vegetation dynamics have been widely reported
column6: providing a framework for models (; ). Secondly
column7: the quantitative relationship between carbon stock and plant biomass has been explored (; ). Lastly
column8: experimental studies have provided some evidence to verify the simulating models (; ).

column1: Understanding the impacts of grazing management on vegetation carbon storage is not only of academic interest but also holds profound implications for policy formulation and the evaluation of ecosystem services. Firstly
column2: optimizing carbon storage in ecosystems serves as a countermeasure to rising atmospheric CO2 concentrations
column3: potentially mitigating the impacts of global warming (). Moreover
column4: the retained carbon stock is intrinsically linked to the broader spectrum of ecosystem services
column5: underpinning their value and functionality (). As grazing practices directly modulate the carbon balance of an ecosystem
column6: they become pivotal tools in enhancing ecological services.

column1: Carbon sequestration rates serve as a crucial aspect in understanding the role of grazing management in determining carbon fixation within vegetation. The carbon sequestration rate is an insightful metric to represent the annual carbon accumulation in vegetation (; ). Calculating vegetation carbon sequestration rates is conventionally achieved by examining the average change in carbon stock over a designated time span (). In recent studies
column2: it has been proposed that the relationship between vegetation carbon stocks and carbon sequestration rates can be effectively described using logistic functions (). Utilizing logistic functions to determine carbon sequestration rates proves advantageous as it encompasses non-linear growth patterns
column3: offering a comprehensive perspective on carbon accumulation dynamics over time.

column1: The primary objective of this study was to assess the impacts of livestock grazing management on vegetation in semi-arid regions from two perspectives: carbon stock and carbon sequestration rates. Our approach involved establishing a system dynamics model with multiple life-history stages to simulate the effects of grazing managements on plant biomass. By understanding the relationship between biomass and carbon stock
column2: we could determine the carbon stock. Subsequently
column3: by employing a logistic model linking carbon stock to carbon sequestration rates
column4: we estimated the latter. This research provides a holistic methodology to comprehend the multifaceted repercussions of grazing management on carbon dynamics
column5: thereby offering valuable insights for sustainable land-use strategies in semi-arid environments.

column1: This study was conducted in the Wulanaodu region (42° 29′–43° 06′N
column4: located in the Horqin Sandy Land
column5: one of the largest sand lands in China (). This region is a typical landscape composed of active sand dunes
column6: stabilized sand dunes
column7: and inter-dune lowlands. The region has a semi-arid climate
column8: with a mean temperature of 23 °C in July and –14 °C in January (). Sparse elm woodlands are the original vegetation community in this region
column9: and Ulmus pumila is the dominant tree species in these woodlands (). Besides U. pumila trees
column10: there are also sparsely distributed shrubs and herbs
column11: whose biomass is far less than U. pumila trees ().

column1: We evaluated the effects of grazing management on carbon stock in sparse elm woodlands. In this study
column2: we considered four scenarios of grazing management
column3: i.e.
column4: rotational grazing every 5 years (RG5)
column5: prohibited grazing (PG)

column7: SPG)
column8: and continuous grazing (CG). Continuous grazing increases elm seed production and decreases elm seedling densities in sparse elm woodlands in a previous study (). Seasonal prohibited grazing influences elm seedlings but does not influence seed production
column9: as seed dispersal mainly occurs in May (). Meanwhile
column10: rotational grazing every 5 years works as continuous grazing once every 5 years. The specific values are shown in .

column1: In RG5 and PG management
column2: the carbon stock in elm trees is faster and in greater quantities than in CG and SPG management. This suggests that RG5 and PG are the preferred choices for maintaining steady carbon storage in elm trees. This result is consistent with a previous study
column3: where elm seedlings and sapling densities were found to be significantly higher in fenced plots than in grazed plots (). This is likely due to grazing threats to the growth of seedlings and saplings (). Similar results were also found in a similar landscape
column4: the tropical savanna
column5: where long-term grazing was found to have reduced vegetation biomass ().

column1: According to this study
column2: the carbon stock of elm trees is 10–15 M g ha−1 in mature-stage sparse woodlands under prohibited grazing management. This result is consistent with an experimental study in sparse woodlands
column3: where the carbon stock of elm trees was found to be 15.57 M g ha−1 in fenced plots (). These consistent results suggest that the system dynamic approach is effective in simulating changes in the carbon stock of plant populations. In previous studies
column4: the system dynamic approach has been used to simulate various aspects of plant populations
column5: including the carbon cycle
column6: hydrological processes
column7: and population dynamics (; ; ). This study expands the application of the system dynamic approach by incorporating it in the calculation of vegetation carbon sequestration and evaluating the effects of specific factors.

column1: The results reveal a notably low carbon sequestration associated with the SPG treatment (). A plausible rationale for this observation hinges on the period spanning March to July
column2: which is likely the predominant growth phase for regional vegetation. By prohibiting grazing during these crucial months
column3: an excessive proliferation of herbs may ensue
column4: which could subsequently impede the germination and establishment of elm tree seeds (). Such a reduction in the available seed source inevitably results in a decline in the population of elm trees. This chain reaction
column5: commencing from the lowered elm population
column6: can be linked to the observed decrement in vegetation carbon sequestration within the SPG treatment.

column1: Meanwhile
column2: the carbon accumulation observed in the PG treatment appears to surpass that of the RG-5 treatment between 70 and 80 years. This can potentially be attributed to the enclosure practices
column3: which tend to bolster biomass accumulation
column4: yielding a slight elevation in carbon sequestration compared to RG5 within that timeframe. Yet
column5: as the duration of enclosure prolongs
column6: an elevated population density ensues
column7: inciting heightened interspecific competition. This amplified competition paves the way for increased individual mortality
column8: leading to a reduction in the aggregate population biomass
column9: and
column10: in turn
column11: a subsequent decline in carbon accumulation.

column1: Grazing management can influence soil carbon stock by regulating soil organic carbon and soil microbial carbon (). The effects of grazing exclusion on soil carbon stock are more commonly reported compared to other grazing management techniques (). A possible reason for this is that grazing and grazing exclusion are the most commonly used grazing management techniques in grasslands and degraded lands
column2: and it is relatively easier to establish designed experimental plots for these methods. Rotational grazing management has great potential for maintaining vegetation carbon stock
column3: and its effects on soil carbon stock should be fully considered.

column1: Elm trees exhibited varying carbon sequestration rates under different management practices: 0.15
column3: 0.13 and 0.09 Mg C ha−1 year−1 in RG5
column4: PG
column5: CG
column6: and SPG
column7: respectively. This clearly highlights RG5 management as the most efficient in terms of carbon sequestration
column8: while SPG management lagged behind the others. Although both CG and PG management exhibited the same carbon sequestration rate
column9: the carrying capacity (K value) was notably higher under PG management. This could account for the observed higher carbon stock in PG as compared to CG throughout the duration of the study. This data suggests a hierarchical efficiency in carbon sequestration by elm trees under different management practices: RG5 being the most efficient
column10: followed by PG
column11: CG
column12: and then SPG.

column1: Management practices can significantly shape vegetation carbon sequestration rates by influencing the structural and functional attributes of plant communities (). For instance
column2: practices that promote plant diversity and maintain a dense vegetative cover tend to enhance the overall photosynthetic capacity of an ecosystem (). A diverse plant community
column3: with species that have varied photosynthetic rates and growth patterns
column4: can capture carbon more efficiently throughout the year (). Moreover
column5: management interventions that optimize nutrient availability
column6: such as controlled grazing or periodic soil amendments
column7: can boost plant growth and thereby enhance their carbon intake ().

column1: Conversely
column2: mismanaged practices can have deleterious effects on vegetation carbon sequestration. Overgrazing
column3: for example
column4: can result in the dominance of less productive plant species
column5: diminishing the overall carbon capture capacity of the vegetation (). Practices that lead to soil compaction or erosion can hinder root growth
column6: thereby reducing the plant’s ability to access water and nutrients essential for photosynthesis (). Additionally
column7: the loss or suppression of certain plant species due to specific management choices can disrupt the synchrony between plant phenology and climatic patterns
column8: potentially leading to decreased photosynthetic periods (). Hence
column9: tailoring management practices to the ecological needs of the vegetation is vital to maximize carbon sequestration rates.

column1: Our findings
column2: indicating carbon sequestration rates for elm trees ranging between 0.09 and 0.15 under various management measures
column3: align with those reported by  in Central Nepal.  documented a carbon sequestration rate of 0.14 Mg C ha−1 year−1 for trees within the Ulmaceae family. This further attests to the appropriateness of employing logistic models to capture the accumulation dynamics of biomass in vegetation. It is worth noting
column4: however
column5: that the carbon sequestration rates derived from our logistic models represent an average value across the entire life cycle of the vegetation and do not imply that the annual carbon sequestration rates for the plants consistently maintain this state.

column1: In addition to grazing management
column2: the duration of grazing and grazing exclusion management can also influence carbon storage in soil and vegetation (). For example
column3: ecosystem carbon storage in an alpine meadow steppe showed a hump-shaped pattern in response to the duration of grazing exclusion with a 6-year threshold (). In this study
column4: the results provide evidence that the duration of grazing management influences vegetation carbon storage in sparse woodlands. Moreover
column5: longer periods of grazing exclusion management do not necessarily lead to more vegetation carbon storage in over-mature trees. While our study emphasizes the importance of considering the duration of grazing management in decision-making processes to enhance carbon sequestration
column6: it is pivotal to also scrutinize the specific strategies implemented during grazing. This encompasses analyzing the length and timing of rotations
column7: as the efficacy of grazing management on carbon storage in sparse woodlands might be intricately linked to these variables. Moreover
column8: integrating auxiliary strategies such as selective cutting
column9: especially when trees exhibit diminished carbon storage capabilities
column10: could serve as a pragmatic approach to sustain and potentially augment carbon sequestration in these ecosystems (). Thus
column11: a holistic approach
column12: examining both the temporal and strategic facets of grazing management alongside supplementary woodland management practices like selective cutting
column13: should be adopted to cultivate a robust framework that optimally supports carbon sequestration.

column1: Climate changes
column2: especially changes in precipitation and temperature
column3: can affect vegetation carbon stock as biomass accumulation is influenced (). Precipitation plays a vital role in regulating plant growth and vegetation patterns in terrestrial ecosystems (). In sparse elm woodlands
column4: population densities
column5: but not the population age structure of elm trees
column6: are greatly influenced by precipitation (). The effects of precipitation on carbon storage in sparse elm woodlands are worth exploring in future research.

column1: Livestock grazing can decrease the herb layer in terms of biomass
column2: coverage
column3: and spatial distribution (; ). Subsequently
column4: the changes in herbs might affect the growth of elm trees
column5: especially in the early stages of their life history. However
column6: the effects of herbs on elm tree growth are relatively difficult to evaluate. Firstly
column7: herbs play different roles in influencing the secondary dispersal of elm seeds. Herbs with vegetation coverage lower than the threshold can promote seed secondary dispersal
column8: while herbs with vegetation coverage higher than the threshold can impede seed secondary dispersal (). Secondly
column9: herbs have little effect on elm seed germination and seedling growth
column10: as their effects might be mixed with other factors
column11: such as light and litter biomass (). Thirdly
column12: microbiomes might connect the interaction between herbs and elm trees
column13: yet the role of microbiomes on plants in sparse elm woodlands is only recently being considered (). Therefore
column14: considering the interaction between herb layers and elm trees could promote the accuracy of tree growth models.

column1: In our research
column2: we crafted an advanced system dynamics model that integrates multiple life-history stages to explore the impact of livestock grazing management strategies on carbon storage
column3: both in terms of carbon stock and sequestration rates. Importantly
column4: for a nuanced estimation of carbon sequestration rates
column5: we employed logistical models.

column1: In this study
column2: we examined four distinct grazing management strategies: RG5
column3: PG
column4: CG
column5: and SPG. Our findings indicate that the carbon stock of elm trees under RG5 and PG management surpassed those observed under CG and SPG management. Moreover
column6: our research highlights the differences in carbon sequestration rates of elm trees across these managements
column7: with RG5 standing out as the most effective. As a result
column8: our data suggests that rotational grazing management may offer a superior strategy to enhance vegetation carbon storage in sparse elm woodlands in semi-arid landscapes. Furthermore
column9: in line with previous research
column10: our logistic model outcomes showed that the carbon sequestration rates for elm trees ranged between 0.09 and 0.15 Mg C ha−1 year−1.

column1: Our assessment sheds light on the carbon sequestration capabilities of vegetation in sparse elm woodlands
column2: offering valuable insights for grazing management in semi-arid regions. For a more refined understanding of carbon storage capacities in the future
column3: it will be imperative to integrate soil carbon sequestration studies and delve deeper into the interactions between the herb layers and elm trees within these sparse woodlands.

55|10.7717/peerj.5671|Growth and feeding of deep-sea coral Lophelia pertusa from the California margin under simulated ocean acidification conditions| Gómez  Carlos E. |2018-9-27|Global mean values of atmospheric carbon dioxide (CO2) have increased dramatically since pre-industrial times from 280 ppm (parts per million) to about 405 ppm in 2017. This increase in atmospheric CO2 is unprecedented in at least the past 650 thousand years during the last four glacial cycles (). As the oceans come into equilibrium with the atmosphere
column2: there is an alteration of carbonate chemistry with an increase in the concentration of hydrogen ions and a decrease in carbonate saturation states (Ω) (; ). The aragonite saturation horizon (ASH) is a boundary in the water column at which the carbonate saturation state is equal to 1 (Ω = 1)
column3: and is found at two different depths corresponding to the different carbonate polymorphs of aragonite (Ωarag) and calcite (Ωcal). If the water is supersaturated (Ω >  1) calcification is favored
column4: whereas if it is undersaturated (Ω <  1) dissolution is favored over calcification (; ). In a biological context
column5: the lower the saturation state
column6: the more energy required for calcification. Although global ocean calcium carbonate saturation states remain above 1 for most shallow portions of the ocean
column7: models forecast a significant shoaling of the saturation horizon (ASH) by mid-century (). This will have negative impacts to deep-sea ecosystems
column8: since deep-water organisms live in habitats that already experience lower saturation states than their shallower counterparts.

column1: Lophelia pertusa is the most well-known cold-water coral (CWC) with cosmopolitan distribution at depths normally between 40 and 800 m (). Hard substrate
column2: local topography
column3: temperature
column4: current flow
column5: and food supply have been cited as factors that can affect its distribution (; ). Aragonite saturation (Ωarag) has also been proposed as an important factor due to the evidence that >95% of CWCs are distributed in places where the saturation of calcium carbonate is above 1 (Ω > 1) (). Nevertheless
column6: recent deep-sea explorations have led to new observations of scleractinian corals at aragonite undersaturation
column7: such as the Central North Pacific (
column8: ASH < 550 m)
column9: Chilean Fjiords (;
column10: ASH < 200 m)
column11: and the Central South Pacific (
column12: ASH < 1
column13: 000 m). The mechanisms underlying this apparent ability to live under conditions that are not favorable for calcification remain unknown
column14: since experimental studies with coral species from those places are not common. However
column15: the capacity to alter the internal carbonate chemistry in favor of calcification has been proposed as a plausible explanation (). Due to the significant ecological role of these deep-water habitats
column16: understanding the complex relationship among the biology
column17: physiology
column18: and ecology of cold-water corals with their realized distribution is of prime importance.

column1: The potential effects of future levels of ocean acidification on the physiological performance of CWCs have shown some contrasting evidence
column2: nevertheless
column3: there is good agreement about its potential negative effects (; ; ; ). On one hand
column4: studied the short and long-term response of Lophelia pertusa from the North Atlantic grown under different levels of pH and aragonite saturation states (Ωarag). They found that in the long-term
column5: corals were able to maintain and even increase calcification rates under high CO2 conditions.  found no significant effects of low Ωarag on growth rates of L. pertusa in the long-term
column6: although they observed decreased calcification. On the other hand
column7: showed significant detrimental effects of ocean acidification on calcification of L. pertusa in the long-term (6 months)
column8: and that in the short term (∼2 weeks) some of the fragments experienced net dissolution rates at undersaturated levels of aragonite
column9: although these differences were not significant due to a high variability in the response.  found that different populations of L. pertusa from the Gulf of Mexico and Norwegian Skagerrak had different responses at undersaturated levels of aragonite
column10: with the Norwegian population capable of elevating feeding rate to maintain growth at low pH
column11: while populations of the Gulf of Mexico reduced feeding rate and calcification rates
column12: presumably to save energy. Moreover
column13: showed that L. pertusa can survive undersaturation but with the cost of losing framework stability.

column1: Upwelling systems are characterized by naturally higher CO2 concentrations
column2: and lower pH and Ωarag than non-upwelling areas (). These are also areas with high primary productivity
column3: which has been shown to affect the structure and composition of the benthic fauna
column4: including increasing deep-sea coral diversity (; ). The California Current System (CCS) is a key Pacific Ocean current that moves equatorward throughout the year along the west coast of North America ()
column5: and is one of the four major Eastern Boundary Upwelling Systems
column6: which has been associated with ocean acidification processes (; ). Modeled data have suggested that since preindustrial times
column7: the CCS has already experienced a dramatic decrease in pH of ∼0.1 and Ωarag of ∼0.4
column8: with the current surface mean pH and Ωarag of 7.95 ±  0.04 and 1.67 ± 0.16 respectively (). This is especially relevant given the ecological
column9: biological
column10: and economical importance of the CCS (; ). In summer
column11: when the system experiences strong upwelling
column12: models predict undersaturation throughout the water column for coastal as well as off-shore waters ().

column1: In order to understand the physiological response of L. pertusa from the California margin
column2: we conducted a series of short-term experiments to examine the calcification and feeding behavior of corals collected from the Southern California Bight. Fragments of L. pertusa were grown under controlled conditions that simulated the present-day scenario in situ (aragonite undersaturation) hereafter referred to as “unfavorable” vs. saturated aragonite conditions (corresponding to >95% of the present-day global distribution of L. pertusa) hereafter referred to as “favorable”. We hypothesized that corals growing in unfavorable conditions (Ωarag < 1) will calcify less than corals grown in favorable conditions (Ωarag > 1)
column3: but we still expect calcification rates to be positive (accretion > erosion). The present conditions in the California Current System are similar to those that are expected to occur by the end of the century in other areas ()
column4: so the information regarding how these corals are responding is crucial to understanding the whole ecosystem response in the CCS and beyond.

column1: Lophelia pertusa fragments were collected in the Southern California Bight (SCB) between April and May 2015 (33°55′7.6794″N; 119°28′18.84″W) at ∼300 m depth under permit number CINMS-2015-002

column1: Yellow dots refer to the CalCOFI stations used to complement the characterization of the carbonate chemistry of the area where L. pertusa was collected.

The California Current System (CCS) is an offshore water mass characterized by waters with low temperature and salinity that flow equatorward along the west coast of the US (). North Pacific Ocean water masses that influence this system are relatively low in pH and hence carbonate saturation (; ). Due to the periodic upwelling of deeper water masses
column2: the CCS experiences frequent periods of aragonite undersaturation
column3: which are similar to the future effects of ocean acidification in other areas (; ; ; ). In order to assess the carbonate chemistry near the L. pertusa coral collection location
column4: two inorganic carbon parameters (pHT and AT) were measured from two different sources: (1) Discrete water samples obtained from seven CTD casts (August 2014
column5: March 2015
column6: August 2015) and (2) CTD data from the California Cooperative Oceanic and Fisheries Investigation program (CalCOFI) stations that were sampled forming a grid within the northern part of the Southern California Bight (SCB) that includes the sample collection sites (). Refer to CalCOFI.org for more information about the grid and stations.

column1: The CTD rosette was deployed in the area of major coral aggregations at the Piggy Bank and Footprint coral sites (33°55′7.32″N; 119°28′19.5594″W and 33°57′48.95″N; 119°29′22.2″W respectively) in order to characterize the carbonate system in the environment in which L. pertusa grows (). The CTD rosette was composed of Niskin bottles and a Sea-Bird CTD unit that varied according to the year of collection. In 2014

The other carbonate parameters were calculated from pHT and AT using the software CO2calc ().

column1: This study clearly suggests that VIS/NIR spectroscopy is an effective method to detect wetland SOC content of soils in arid regions. Our work provides a comprehensive evaluation of models and algorithms for their power to identify relevant feature wavelengths to estimate SOC. Such endeavor is of critical and practical importance. Increasing population and intensive human activities have put ever increasing pressure on wetland in arid regions. Changes in the SOC content in fragile ecosystems can be drastic even with slight increase of human activities (; ). Such changes could have significant impact on local climatic and ecological systems
column2: and even contribute to large-scale carbon equilibrium (). The proposed VIS/NIR spectroscopic approach plus the relatively mature classification and prediction models provide effective means to the local ecological and environmental management authorities.
