column1: 57|10.7717/peerj.15685|Major biotic stresses affecting maize production in Kenya and their implications for food security| Njeru  Faith |2023-11-30|"Maize (Zea mays L.) is an important cereal crop in Sub-Saharan Africa (SSA) critical for food security as well as a source of income for millions of small-holder farmers (). The importance of maize is evidenced by the fact that 90% of the Kenyan population depends on maize for food (annual maize consumption of 4.3 million tons reported in 2018)
column2: income
column3: and employment (; ). The total maize yield in Kenya ranged from 1.43 to 1.82 t/ha from 2010 to 2018 () (). Similar results have also been reported from data collected from farmers in Kenya who reported average maize yields of 1.48 t/ha for the 2020/2021 cropping season ().

column1: Maize is cultivated under diversified climatic and agroecological zones mostly by resource-poor farmers with limited access to inputs such as fertilizers and pesticides (). Despite the low maize production
column2: potential exists for increasing maize yield to over 6 t/ha through increased use of improved seeds and good crop husbandry (). Therefore
column3: there is need to invest in innovative agricultural practices to increase maize yield to be able to meet the demand.

column1: Maize production is threatened by several factors
column2: where biotic factors are of a significant challenge. Biotic factors such as stem borers
column3: and weeds such as Striga
column4: have been shown to contribute up to 30% yield loss in maize production (). Pathogens such as maize dwarf mosaic virus
column5: sugarcane mosaic virus
column6: and wheat streak mosaic virus have also been reported to cause significant reductions in maize yields in the affected farms.

column1: Globalization and international trade have increased significantly in recent years
column2: subsequently increasing the spread of transboundary pests and diseases. This has led to the emergence of new diseases such as maize lethal necrosis (MLN) and the introduction of new pests such as fall armyworm (FAW) Spodoptera frugiperda in Kenya. These new pests and diseases further contribute to low maize production and puts a strain on the agricultural sector.

column1: The population in Kenya is expected to continue increasing in the coming decades reaching 66 million in 2030; however
column2: food production is not expected to be on pace. Hence
column3: the purpose of this review article is to present the major biotic stresses affecting maize production in Kenya
column4: with a focus on two pests namely: fall armyworm and desert locusts
column5: two fungal pathogens: gray leaf spot (GLS)
column6: and Turcicum leaf blight (TLB) and one viral disease: maize lethal necrosis (MLN). These biotic stresses are very common
column7: occurring every maize growing season and have the potential to cause yield losses of 100%.

column1: This review article targets plant breeders
column2: pathologists
column3: scientists in the seed companies and students in agricultural research. Several previous review articles have been written on different aspects of biotic stresses affecting maize production such as (; ; ). These previous review articles have focused on a single specific biotic stress. The current review article combines several biotic stresses affecting maize production.

column1: The studies used to write this review article were identified from science direct
column2: research gate and web of science. Only articles written in English were reviewed. The inclusion criteria included original articles and previous review articles on biotic stresses. The search terms used included maize lethal necrosis
column3: fall armyworm
column4: desert locusts
column5: gray leaf spot; turcicum leaf blight. The search terms were used with the Boolean operator “AND” maize production. This enabled to focus the search to articles written in reference to maize cultivation.

column1: Productivity in plants is reduced by biotic stresses caused by living organisms such as pests
column2: bacteria
column3: fungi
column4: weeds and viruses. Economic losses to biotic stresses vary depending on the stress and the affected crop. A study by  reported that biotic stresses in Kenya cause losses of between 3.8 to 123.6 million USD.

column1: Maize is cultivated in 40% of the total crop area in Kenya and its production is often affected by biotic stresses. These biotic stresses affect maize during growth and also after harvest and they not only impact the yield but also the quality of the crop harvested.
Below we discuss in detail the main biotic stresses of maize which are of national importance in Kenya as they cause significant yield losses disrupting food security.
Fall armyworm (FAW) Spodoptera frugiperda () is a type of caterpillar that disperses to long distances annually (). The female moth lays eggs in masses on the leaves of many plants (). After hatching
column2: the larvae
column3: which is the most destructive stage of the pest
column4: disperses throughout the crop field consuming vegetation they come across (). FAW is a polyphagous pest that attacks over 60 plant species
column5: but its major effects have been on a few crops such as rice
column6: millet
column7: sorghum
column8: and maize ().

column1: (A) and (C) is the photo showing the larval form of FAW (taken by Paul Njau at Naivasha
column2: Kenya in 2023). (B) Photo of maize plants affected by FAW in Kenya 2020 (photo taken by Faith Njeru).

column1: FAW is native to the tropical regions of the western hemisphere from the United States to Argentina (). FAW is relatively new in Africa
column2: where its first report was in 2016 in West Africa following distress calls by maize producers of high armyworm populations (). Subsequently
column3: FAW was reported in other African countries including Kenya in 2016 () and by 2018
column4: FAW had affected 86% of the maize farmers in Kenya ().

column1: The fast spread of FAW is attributed to its natural distribution capacity (strong migratory pest
column2: producing many eggs) and the increase in international trade (). Additionally
column3: its preference for maize
column4: the major cereal crop in Africa also aids in the fast spread of this pest which has been reported in all Sub-Saharan African countries except Lesotho ().

column1: Damage on maize by FAW is observed on all plant parts and at all maize developmental stages
column2: with the pest reported to affect the stem base of maize plantlets
column3: the leaves of maize at the vegetative stage
column4: and also on grown maize plants with the pest able to feed on tassels or bore into ears (). It has been identified mostly in the Southeastern U.S. as a regular and serious pest destroying maize crops () causing yield losses of up to 57% in Latin America
column5: depending on the crop season and the maize variety planted ().

column1: Results based on socioeconomic surveys and farmer estimates of yield loss
column2: undertaken in different countries in Africa have estimated maize yield loss due to fall armyworm to be in the range of 22–67% (). A community based survey done in Kenya in 2017 and 2018 by  reported average yield losses of 33% to FAW. These losses due to FAW are similar to results reported by a farmer survey in Ghana and Zambia which reported yield losses of 26–40% and 35–40% respectively () and 32% in Ethiopia ().

column1: reported FAW-induced maize yield reductions of 32% (Ethiopia) and 47% (Kenya)
column2: based on a survey of maize farmers while maize yield reductions of 27% and 35% were reported in Ghana and Zambia respectively (). However
column3: in Zimbabwe
column4: a study by  estimated FAW-induced maize yield reductions of 12% based on a rigorous field scouting method.

column1: In addition
column2: FAW has been shown to significantly increase the vulnerability of maize to other secondary plant pathogens since it destroys the protective husk of maize. The larvae of FAW causes damage to corn ears and the kernels (). The damage caused by FAW to maize kernels has been shown to increase the growth of certain fungi that cause mycotoxins (). Mycotoxins are toxic metabolites with mild to chronic health effects to humans and animals ().

column1: The presence of this new pest in most SSA countries adds to the threat caused by native lepidopteran maize stalk or ear borers such as African maize stalk borer (Busseola fusca). Besides its direct effect on agricultural production
column2: FAW also has the potential to significantly affect access to foreign markets (). Maize is an important crop as a source of food and income to millions of smallholder farmers in SSA
column3: FAW
column4: therefore
column5: poses a significant threat to food security in SSA and the attainment of sustainable development goal 2 to end hunger by 2030.

column1: An assay based on loop‑mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) has been reported for the species specific diagnosis of FAW (). However
column2: for the small scale farmer
column3: scouting protocols which looks for the signs of egg hatch and feeding by egg larvae instar have been developed to allow early detection of FAW for better management (). Besides scouting
column4: sex pheromone traps have been developed to track the presence and movement of FAW in a certain region ().

column1: FAW is new to the African continent
column2: therefore control measures are limited and little is known about the most effective agronomic practices for FAW control (; ). Therefore
column3: most farmers are left with the option of using chemical pesticides for FAW control. This option is expensive and poses risks to health and the environment (). A study done across five African countries of Ghana
column4: Rwanda
column5: Uganda
column6: Zambia and Zimbabwe showed that use of pesticides for FAW control was the most preferred management option among the surveyed farmers ().

column1: The common pesticides used by the farmers in Africa include profenofos and cypermethrin traded as rocket and lambda-cyhalothrin (Karate). These are broad spectrum non systemic pesticides having contact and stomach action. Unfortunately
column2: some farmers reported using pesticides such as monocrotophos
column3: dichlorvos
column4: methomyl
column5: and methamidophos which are highly toxic and prohibited products (). Due to the devastating effect of FAW on maize plants and the limited know how on its management
column6: farmers resulted in indiscriminate use of pesticides with some farmers applying fungicides such as mancozeb ().

column1: In addition
column2: the use of pesticides has been reported to be ineffective due to the larval behavior of this pest; larval stage bores into the host plant developing under a protected environment
column3: making it difficult to reach with target insecticidal sprays (). FAW has also been reported to have evolved resistance to several insecticides used including carbamates
column4: organophosphorus
column5: and pyrethroids (). FAW have been shown to develop resistance to insecticide by metabolic detoxification mechanism (). The increased activity of glutathione S-transferases (GSTs) resulted in FAW resistance to pyrethroids. Another mechanism that has been reported for FAW resistance to insecticides is the target resistance mechanism ().

column1: In Brazil
column2: the control of FAW has been through the use of transgenic plants encoding the Cry1F gene from Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) (). However
column3: some studies done in Brazil have shown Bt maize plants expressing the Cry1 proteins to be affected by FAW as a consequence of field evolved resistance to Cry1 proteins (). Maize plants expressing the Vip3A gene product from Bt were shown to be effective against FAW due to the high toxicity of Vip3A protein to FAW ().

column1: Though the use of Bt maize expressing the Cry1 and Vip3A proteins have been shown to have significant success in the control of FAW
column2: the use of this technology need to be monitored and integrated with other control options to control the evolution of resistance against the Cry1 and Vip3A proteins by FAW.

column1: However
column2: the ethical concerns for use of genetically modified (GM) plants still restrict the use of such plants where legal restrictions are imposed on GM plants. Therefore
column3: in most SSA countries
column4: more research is being carried out to identify alternative control strategies that are effective
column5: adaptable
column6: and applicable to the smallholder farmer’s in SSA.

column1: These mitigation strategies include cultural
column2: the use of bioagents
column3: and the use of resistant plant genotypes as extensively reviewed by . Natural enemies of FAW which include parasitoids that are small insects that develop attached to the host and eventually kill the host
column4: have been reported for FAW.  reported several species of parasitoids for FAW in Ethiopia
column5: Kenya and Tanzania with parasitism ranging from 4 to 45.3%. The parasitoids that have been reported for FAW include Cotesia icipe
column6: tachinid fly
column7: Palexorista zonata
column8: Charops ater and Coccygidium luteum (). It has also been shown that intercropping with plants such as Tephrosia or desmodium which produce repugnant chemicals help to repel the adult female moths
column9: reducing the number of eggs laid on host plants ().

column1: In addition to the parasitoids which are natural enemies of FAW
column2: entomopathogenic microbials which include fungi
column3: bacteria
column4: viruses and nematodes have also been reported to be effective against FAW through laboratory assays and in the field (). Entomopathogenic fungi Beauveria bassiana
column5: Metarhizium rileyi
column6: and Metarhizium anisopliae have more than 50% mortality rate of FAW eggs and larvae (). The nematode Steinernema feltiae
column7: when sprayed on maize ears in the field was shown to effective against FAW ().

column1: Microbials have also been shown that when applied in combination can lead to more efficient FAW control. Simultaneous application of Metarhizium rileyi strains and nucleopolyhedroviruses (NPV) had a better control on FAW (). Microbials have been used to develop biopesticides for FAW control
column2: which are less hazardous to the environment and the farmers. Few biopesticides are registered for use in Africa
column3: therefore more research should be done to encourage the use of biopestides in Africa ().

column1: A study by  in Zimbabwe
column2: reported that FAW damage was frequently reduced by frequent weeding and by minimum and zero tillage. However
column3: pumpkin intercropping was found to significantly increase FAW damage. Further research is needed to determine which crops are the most efficient in controlling FAW and acceptable to farmers. Farmers’ have also been shown to employ a range of cultural and physical practices
column4: based on indigenous knowledge e.g.
column5: hand picking of egg masses and caterpillars
column6: and application of ash/sand on the larvae
column7: some with considerable levels of success ().

column1: Some of the cultural practices used by the farmers have been studied and shown to be effective. For example
column2: the use of neem oil was reported to have 70% mortality of FAW larvae (). Rabbit urine has also been shown to be effective against FAW by reducing larval feeding (). Therefore
column3: potential exists for the application of indigenous knowledge in the control of FAW especially in small scale farming.

column1: Some maize varieties were found by  to be tolerant to FAW damage. Therefore
column2: there has been research to identify quantitative trait loci (QTL) for application in marker-assisted selection (MAS) to facilitate the breeding process.  identified two important QTL explaining 37% of the phenotypic variance of leaf-feeding damage by FAW in maize. The resistant variety used
column3: Mp705 was responsible for the leaf-feeding damage-reducing alleles for both large-effect QTL and most of the small-effect QTL identified in the study by .

column1: There is a need for the development and implementation of evidence based efforts to control this pest. Development of alternative control and preventive methods for FAW
column2: based on agronomic management would be more affordable to resource-constrained small holder farmers with less risk to health and environment. But studies are still ongoing to understand the unknown dynamics of establishment
column3: spread
column4: and environmental conditions favoring the survival of FAW in the continent.

column1: The desert locusts
column2: Schistocerca gregaria () are mainly found in arid and semi-arid areas spanning regions from West Africa to India. Desert locusts’ outbreak and invasion date back to 1860. Since then
column3: different outbreaks have been recorded over the years some even lasting for over 22 years (). The recent outbreak can be traced back to the 2018 cyclones coupled with the warm weather and heavy rainfall experienced at the end of 2019 (). In this outbreak
column4: Kenya has experienced its worst invasion in 70 years where a large swarm occupied an area of about 2
column5: 400 square kilometers ().

column1: Current outbreaks are worsened by the current state of political instability
column2: under-financed control centers
column3: the COVID-19 pandemic and poor early detection strategies (). Therefore
column4: with the recent advances in technology and remote sensing
column5: there is need to devise better and effective early sensing tools for arresting the situation before much damage is done.

column1: At a local level
column2: desert locusts can cause desertification due to the raged soils caused by depletion of vegetation cover. Development of irrigation schemes in SSA have also aggravated the situation by providing a favorable environment for breeding sites of the desert locusts
column3: consequently leading to recurrent outbreaks (). Desert locusts can migrate over very long distances attacking all types of vegetation cover hence posing great threat to agro-sylvo-pastoral production (). In times of favorable weather conditions of high rainfall (favorable environment for laying eggs)
column4: outbreaks
column5: upsurge and invasions usually occur threatening food security.

column1: On the flip side
column2: insects have over the years been used as an alternative source of high-quality proteins
column3: energy
column4: fats and mineral elements. Together with migratory locusts
column5: desert locusts are consumed for proteins and fats although research on its actual nutritive value has not been reported (; ). There is
column6: therefore
column7: need to investigate the actual nutritive value and the data obtained used to sensitize the society on the importance of such sources of food. Moreover
column8: specific elements of the same can be used in fortification of staple foods like maize and rice in poor communities where malnutrition is rampant.

column1: Over the years
column2: the control for desert locust outbreaks and invasion has been the use of organo-phosphorous insecticides such as the widely used chlorpyrifos. These compounds are not only harmful to humans and other living organisms but also pollute the environment contributing to the chlorofluorocarbon (CFCs) and global warming at large (). Therefore
column3: researchers are advocating for better monitoring and alternative measures to synthetic pesticides to control these insects.

column1: First in the list of alternatives
column2: are biopesticides. Research had shown that a fungus
column3: Metarhizium anisopliae can kill desert locust by growing in the insect’s body.  and his team reported that these entomopathogenic fungi kill the desert locust around day four and five of infection. Therefore
column4: this bio control strategy although it is environmentally friendly
column5: does not offer immediate results but can be used in combination with other control methods. Hence
column6: further research on the appropriate combination of control strategies need to be done to establish the ones that will give optimal results.

column1: Recently
column2: characterized the microbial community present in the desert locust’s body. Using morphological data and molecular identification techniques
column3: nine bacteria species were identified from the S. gregari adult body. The identified bacteria species were E. faecalis
column4: L. paracasei Bacillus sp.
column5: S. epidermidis
column6: Escherichia sp.
column7: Salmonella sp.
column8: Pluralibacter sp.
column9: Shimwellia spp; while one species was unclassified. However; further research on the role of the identified microbes on the survival of the locusts can give an insight on whether the said microbes can be used in the control of the locusts. In addition
column10: further studies are needed to establish the inter-relationship between these microbes and the entomopathogenic fungi Metarhizium anisopliae.

column1: Another environmental friendly control strategy is the use of crude extracts from Jatropha (Jatropha curcas L.) and neem (Azadirachta indica A. Juss) which have been demonstrated to be toxic
column2: antifeedant
column3: and growth regulating compounds against nymph stage of the desert locusts (). However
column4: further investigation and fractionation of the extracts is required to characterize the specific compounds which are effective against desert locusts in these plants. There is also need to establish the effectiveness of these plant extracts on hopper stages of the locusts. Moreover
column5: studies on the effect of combined extracts from both plants on the effect on the nymph and hopper stages of the locusts are yet to be reported.

column1: Gray leaf spot (GLS) () caused by Cercospora zea-maydis (C. zein-maydis) ()
column2: is a maize leaf disease that is a global threat to maize production. Molecular research shows that C. zea-maydis is mainly distributed in Brazil and African countries
column3: and is predominant in the United States of America (). In Kenya
column4: GLS was first reported in 1993 in the Western region and has since spread to other maize growing areas in the country (). Turcicum leaf blight (TLB) ()
column5: is distributed widely around the world and is caused by Helminthosporium turcicum affecting maize plants from seedling to harvest stages (). It is a serious problem in North Eastern United States
column6: sub-Saharan Africa
column7: and areas of China
column8: Latin America
column9: and India (

column1: Photo taken by Paul Njau at Naivasha
column2: Kenya.

column1: Documented yield losses attributed to GLS range from 11% to total yield loss in cases of severe infection (). GLS disease is characterized by necrotic lesions leading to leaf senescence hence reducing the photosynthetic capacity of the plant. This reduced photosynthetic capacity is linked to poor grain filling and stalk lodging which ultimately lead to poor maize yield ().

TLB is characterized by elliptical grey-green lesions which turn to tan with dark spots of sporulation as maturation continues and eventually leads to defoliation affecting grain yield as seen in GLS (). Yield losses range from 15% to 70% depending on the onset and severity of the infection (). Wet and humid weather conditions favor the spread and increase the severity of GLS and TLB (). Crop yield losses threaten global food security especially in SSA where farming is the main source of livelihood.
The common control methods deployed for these two maize diseases are mainly cultural
column2: chemical and host plant resistance. In the cultural approach
column3: crop rotation and effective burying of the infected plants debris
column4: have reduced the severity of infection in the subsequent round of planting. In addition
column5: effective removal of the over-wintering infected debris
column6: reduced the inoculum available for the next season hence reducing the infection pressure. Fungicides have also been used to control TLB and GLS though it’s less cost effective
column7: leads to fungicide resistance and the continued use of chemicals has raised environmental concerns. Due to these drawbacks
column8: cultural and chemical methods have proved to be uneconomical and unreliable especially for the poor small scale farmers.

column1: Host plant resistance therefore
column2: remains the most viable and reliable method for the management of TLB and GLS. Breeders and researchers around the world have hence embarked on research aimed at identifying host resistant varieties for different biotic and abiotic stresses in different economically important crops. For instance
column4: identified three maize hybrid lines (053WH54
column5: ZS225 and SR52) which were resistant to TLB diseases. In addition
column6: GLS resistant genes were established in various researches (; ; ).

column1: Due to increasing population and climate change
column2: the demand for food is expected to increase. Therefore
column3: there is need to search for more resistant maize lines for both biotic and abiotic stresses and give farmers a wide variety of maize lines. In recent decades
column4: the invention of next-generation sequencing (NGS) technology and high throughput phenotyping platforms have paved ways for fast and effective means of identifying host resistance
column5: molecular breeding and marker assisted selection. In addition
column6: new biotechnological tools have made it possible to perform gene editing and produce superior breeds.

column1: Maize lethal necrosis (MLN) () is a disease of maize that was first reported in Peru in 1973 and then in USA in 1976 (). It is a viral disease caused by the synergistic interaction of Maize Chlorotic Mottle Virus (MCMV) and any of the viruses belonging to the potyviridae family (). The first report on MLN in the African continent was in Kenya in 2012 (). The disease was later reported in several other African countries () including Uganda
column2: Tanzania
column3: Ethiopia
column4: Rwanda
column5: D.R Congo
column6: and South Sudan (). These countries are known as MLN endemic countries.

column1: (A) MLN infected field under artificial inoculation at CIMMYT-KALRO MLN screening facility. (B) maize harvested from an MLN infected field in Kenya. Photo credit: Faith Njeru.

Maize dwarf mosaic virus (MDMV) and wheat streak mosaic virus (WSMV) are the main potyviruses combining with MCMV to cause MLN in the USA () while sugarcane mosaic virus (SCMV) has been implicated in MLN infections in Africa (). Recently
column2: a study by  showed the Johsongrass mosaic virus (JGMV) to cause MLN in co-infections with MCMV in East Africa. Maize yellow mosaic virus (MaYMV) has been discovered in deep sequencing studies in MLN-infected maize plants. However
column3: it was shown that it does not cause MLN but it significantly enhances MLN symptoms such as stunting ().

column1: Maize is the main natural host for MCMV; however
column2: it has also been detected in millet
column3: Johnsongrass
column4: Digitaria sp.
column5: sedge
column6: Setaria sp.
column7: and sugarcane (). Common SCMV hosts reported in Africa include sugarcane
column8: maize
column9: sorghum
column10: Kikuyu grass as well as other Poaceous plant species.

column1: A study conducted on the metagenomic analysis of viruses causing MLN in Kenya showed MCMV as the most prevalent virus in maize-growing regions in Kenya (). SCMV population in Kenya was shown to be diverse
column2: consisting of numerous strains distantly related to isolates from other parts of the world (). Limited sequence divergence among MCMV isolates has been reported in population genetics studies (). Phylogenetic analysis has shown similarity between MCMV isolates found in East Africa to those found in China ().

column1: Maize plants infected with MLN show more severe symptoms than plants infected with MCMV or SCMV alone (). MLN-infected plants show a wide range of symptoms depending on the maize variety
column2: time of infection
column3: and prevailing environmental conditions (). The symptoms are: chlorotic mottle on leaves
column4: developing from the base of young whorl leaves upward to the leaf tips; mild to severe leaf mottling; and necrosis (). Necrosis of young leaves leads to a “dead heart” symptom
column5: and plant death (). Severely affected plants have small cobs with little or no grain set ().

column1: Results from a community-based survey done by
column2: estimated the proportion of maize lost in the community
column3: at 0.5 million tons per year
column4: or 22% of the average annual production before MLN
column5: with an estimated economic loss of $180 million (). The most affected region by MLN in maize production is Western Kenya with more than half of the farmers affected and with a 58% loss of maize (). Central and Eastern Kenya
column6: had up to a third of the farmers affected and with 19% maize yield loss (). Many studies have estimated the losses due to MLN in different maize agro-ecological zones in Kenya to be between 23–100% ().

column1: Different studies have shown than MLN disease can completely wipe out maize plants leading to 100% loss in yield. This is especially devastating to millions of families and small holder farmers who depend on maize as a source of food and income. The effect of MLN is also felt by small and medium enterprises (SME’s) seed companies and processors (). Demand for seed of commercial seed varieties declined when MLN was major epidemic with losses of sales for the companies ().
Due to the significant impact of MLN on the maize sector
column2: International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center (CIMMYT) in collaboration with National Agricultural Research System (NARS) and National Plant protection Organization (NPPO) have put in place several mechanisms to prevent its further spread to the MLN non endemic countries where MLN/MCMV has not yet been reported (). The mechanisms that have been put in place include diagnosis of MLN-causing viruses in maize seed
column3: monitoring and surveillance of MLN across Africa
column4: production and exchange of MLN pathogen free commercial maize seed ().

column1: Sustainable control of plant diseases requires an understanding of the disease including the biology of the pathogens causing the disease
column2: suitable environmental factors favoring host-pathogen interactions and vectors or any other means involved in the spread of the disease. Epidemiological studies on the spread of MLN have identified the spread of the disease from plant to plant and from field to field to be mainly through insect vectors ().

column1: Several insect vectors such as thrips
column2: maize rootworms
column3: leaf beetles and leaf hoppers have been associated with the spread of MCMV (). Aphids on the other hand are the main insect vectors associated with the spread of SCMV (). Seed transmission of MCMV has been reported though at very low rates (0.03–0.33%). This may partly explain how MCMV has managed to travel across continents and countries. MLN has also been shown to be in the soil
column4: irrigation water
column5: and infected plant debris (; ).

column1: To detect MLN
column2: you have to be able to detect both MCMV and SCMV (causative viruses for MLN). Commercial enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) kits based on polyclonal antibodies are available for MCMV and SCMV detection. Monoclonal antibodies have also been developed and shown to have sensitive and specific detection of MCMV (). Gene amplification techniques including reverse-transcriptase polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) () and reverse transcriptase loop mediated isothermal amplification (RT-LAMP) () have been developed for MCMV and SCMV detection.

column1: Use of symptoms to tell the presence of MLN is challenging because symptoms caused by MLN viruses vary depending on the age of maize plant and environmental conditions. In addition
column2: symptomless plants have been found to be MCMV positive. Therefore
column3: diagnostic methods are significant to validate the presence of MCMV and SCMV.

column1: A study conducted by CIMMYT in collaboration with Kenya Agricultural and Livestock Research Organization (KALRO) between 2012 and 2014 found that
column2: most of the maize germplasm had low levels of resistance to MLN (). Therefore
column3: more than 95% of the inbred lines and hybrids grown by farmers in Kenya are susceptible to MLN (). With the continuous cultivation of maize throughout the year
column4: this exacerbates the problem caused by MLN. Therefore
column5: more research is needed to generate and release resistant hybrids to MLN.

column1: Breeding for resistance following the conventional method is time consuming and costly. Therefore
column2: many researchers are applying genomic selection as a promising breeding tool to improve the efficiency and speed of the breeding process. MCMV resistance has been shown to occur in different chromosomal locations and five QTL for MCMV resistance have been found in chromosomes 3

column1: Both major QTL effects and multiple minor effects QTL for MCMV resistance were identified (). Similar observations were made in a subsequent study by  that identified QTL for resistance to MCMV in seven bi-parental populations where some QTL showed major effects in some populations and minor effects in other populations.
To further understand the resistance mechanism of MCMV
column3: tested systemic leaf tissue from five inbred lines for the presence of MCMV and determined that ELISA responses for tissue of inoculated plants from the five lines and the susceptible control (Oh28) were similar. Therefore
column4: only tolerance to MCMV have been identified rather than resistance. The responses of MCMV and SCMV viruses are not linked as the SCMV-resistant line Pa405 had no resistance to MCMV ().

column1: MLN is a complex disease with multiple reservoirs and transmission pathways. There is need for continued investigation on the molecular basis for resistance to MLN
column2: interactions between MCMV and SCMV for deployment of resistant varieties especially in areas where subsistent farmers’ depend on continuous maize crop for food.

column1: Maize is a key cereal crop in Kenya and it associated with food security not only in Kenya but also in Sub-Saharan Africa. The average maize yield in Kenya is far below the global average because of biotic and abiotic stresses which puts a strain on production. Transboundary diseases and pests are significantly increasing putting a strain on food and income security of millions of small-holder farmers.
In as much as these new diseases have previously been reported elsewhere
column2: research is needed in Kenya so that the extent of the economic losses can be clearly defined and also new strategies to control and prevent these disease which are adaptable to the small holder setting in Kenya can be adapted.

column1: With the lifting of the ban on use and cultivation of GMO in Kenya on October 2022
column2: it is expected that researchers will use this technology to fast-track the release of improved maize varieties resistant to pests and diseases.

column1: Maize is an important crop not only in Kenya but Sub-Saharan Africa at large. Therefore
column2: the effect of biotic stresses has become a burden to especially the small-scale farmers. Hence
column3: sustainable means of dealing with these are important for a food secure country. First
column4: the available new technological advances and breeding methods should be applied to provide farmers with plant host resistance breeds and environmentally friendly control measures. Also
column5: in most instances
column6: these biotic stresses are detected too late. Improved technologies should therefore be used to develop early detection methods.

column1: There is also a need to build a regional collective response to invasive pests and trans-boundary diseases.
Finally
column2: there is a need to sensitize society on alternative crops to maize which is more tolerant to the pest and have superior nutritive value such as sorghum and millet.

58|10.7717/peerj.16561|Mass mortality event of the giant barrel sponge Xestospongia sp.: population dynamics and size distribution in Koh Phangan, Gulf of Thailand| Mueller  Jasmin S. |2023-12-12|Various marine sponges play an important role as engineers of coral reefs (; )
column2: have a positive impact on the habitat complexity and the local biodiversity ()
column3: provide home for invertebrate taxa ()
column4: and function as a food source (; ). Due to their ability to filter large amounts of seawater ()
column5: they have been recognized as an important component of benthic-pelagic coupling. In recent years there has been increasing evidence that sponges play an important role in biochemical cycles ()
column6: in particular the uptake and transformation of dissolved organic matter (DOM) to particular organic matter (POM) via the so-called sponge-loop (; ; ).

column1: The barrel sponge Xestospongia sp.
column2: a genus of the family Petrosiidae
column3: is a key species in tropical
column4: shallow coral reefs (). This sponge is an ecological important member of the reef community
column5: due to its large size
column6: its importance as a habitat engineer
column7: and its function as a big seawater filterer (; ). Until recently
column8: the most abundant species of the genus Xestospongia were identified as X. muta in the Caribbean and X. testudinaria in the Indo-Pacific. However
column9: recent genetic studies documented that these different Xestospongia species are part of a more diverse species complex (; ). Morphologically similar species occur sympatrically and might feature differences in their physiology. Some individuals of the Caribbean reefs may be older than 2
column10: 300 years and can grow several meters in height and diameter (; ; ).  found contrary to McMurray twice as high growth rates for Xestospongia spp. from Wakatobi National Marine Park
column11: Indonesia
column12: compared to the Caribbean counterparts.

column1: The main habitat-forming organisms
column2: the scleractinian corals
column3: are threatened worldwide by multiple stressors such as rising water temperatures
column4: ocean acidification
column5: and pollution (; ; ). With the decline of hard coral cover in shallow waters
column6: coral reefs can undergo phase shifts from coral-dominated states to reefs dominated by algae or other invertebrates
column7: including sponges (; ; ; ; ; ). Reefs dominated by sponges have mainly been reported from the Caribbean but also from certain locations in the Indo-Pacific (; ; ; ).

column1: Xestospongia spp. have been reported to undergo cyclic bleaching like reef-building corals (). During this time
column2: the sponges lose their reddish-brown coloration due to the loss of their photosynthetic symbionts and appear creamy-white (; ). Unlike corals
column3: the affected Xestospongia spp. can survive long-term bleaching without mortality. According to
column4: this fact leads to the assumption that the host benefits just a little or not from its photosynthetic symbionts.

column1: With increasing sponge research and benthic monitoring in the last decades
column2: also more bleaching events
column3: diseases
column4: and mortality events were reported for sponges around the world (; ; ; ; ; ). Sponge mortality is often caused by fatal bleaching with massive tissue loss due to diseases () or other environmental stressors such as changing environmental conditions and harmful algal blooms (HABs) (; ).

column1: Our knowledge of Xestospongia spp. demography and size distribution in tropical regions like the Indo-Pacific is still limited. Further research is needed to fill this gap
column2: especially since sponges generally could play an increasing role for tropical reefs in the future. This study aimed to monitor the population dynamics and the size distribution of Xestospongia sp. in the lower Gulf of Thailand. When the bleaching and massive die-off occurred during the monitoring
column3: the aims were complemented with the documentation of the sponge health status to identify the population consequences of this event.

column1: The surveys were conducted under a Memorandum of Understanding with the Department of Marine and Coastal Resources Thailand and the Phuket Marine Biological Center
column2: and with a research permit with permit number B8425313.

column1: Surveys were performed at different time points in March and April of the years 2015 and 2016. To determine the size and age structure of the Xestospongia populations at the study sites
column2: surveys were carried out in March 2015 before the mortality event took place
column3: once the mass mortality was recognized during the ongoing mortality event in April 2015
column4: and during the next field season in March and April 2016. All data were collected by snorkeling.

column1: Four coral reefs were chosen in the shallow waters of the lower Gulf of Thailand. Two of them were located on the island of Koh Phangan
column2: and the other two were in the Mu Ko Ang Thong Marine National Park (). By choosing these study sites
column3: the observed reefs were divided into reefs with different human impact levels: anthropogenic impacted (Koh Phangan)
column4: hereafter abbreviated with AI
column5: and anthropogenic non-impacted (Marine National Park)
column6: hereafter abbreviated with ANI.

column1: The study sites within the Mu Ko Ang Thong Marine National Park (ANI) are shown in (A)
column2: and (B) shows the two study sites on the island of Koh Phangan (AI). Figure source credit: The visualization of this map was done by using the ERSI ArcGIS® World Light Gray Base (basemap) version 7.2. Sources: Esri

column1: The investigated reefs of Koh Phangan were located in the northern and northwestern part of the island at the beaches Haad Khom (AI) (9°47′5″N
column3: 99°58′35.3″E) (). Both were impacted by tourism. Several bungalows and bars are located at Mae Haad (AI)
column4: snorkelers got easily to the reef on foot
column5: and fishing was recognized in front of the reef during our surveys. Haad Khom (AI) was less touristic
column6: and snorkelers just got to the reef by boat. The reef of Mae Haad (AI) was during the surveys less degraded than the reef of Haad Khom (AI)
column7: which showed lower live coral cover and higher cover of dead coral overgrown by filamentous algae (JS Mueller
column8: P-J Grammel and E Schoenig

column1: This study documents a locally restricted massive die-off of the sponge Xestospongia sp. While the northwestern reefs of Koh Phangan
column2: Mae Haad (AI) and Haad Khom (AI) were subject to a population loss of 75–83%
column3: the close by reefs in the Mu Ko Ang Thong Marine National were not affected by this phenomenon. The massive mortality occurred regardless of the survey depth ()
column4: leading to a rapid decline in sponge abundances by 98.4% at Mae Haad (AI) and 80.6% at Haad Khom (AI) from year 2015 to year 2016.

column1: Affected sponges showed a decrease in their usual brown pigmentation with these “bleached” parts becoming covered by whitish to grey mats within a day or two. Once the sponges were completely covered by these presumably microbial mats
column2: the sponge tissue disintegrated further leading to the collapse of especially large Xestospongia individuals. Disintegration of the tissue continued until only bare skeletal fibers of dead sponges were left
column3: making dead sponges difficult to detect during surveys. Based on our observations it cannot be determined if the microbial mats were the cause or rather a secondary infection of opportunistic microorganisms
column4: following the initial bleaching of affected sponges.  described a similar mass mortality in several arctic sponges following an abnormal heatwave in the area. Initial infected parts showed discoloration followed by tissue necrosis and further loss of natural coloration. In some species the tissue became also covered by mucus mats. Similar to our study they could not determine the cause of the mortality event
column5: whether the observed heatwave directly led to the mass mortality or if it acted in synergy with waterborne agents.

column1: Although a heatwave could not be confirmed in our case from historical data
column2: affected Xestospongia sp. sponges revealed similar disease progression leading to mortality as described by .

column1: Based on
column2: fatal bleaching of Xestospongia sp. in the Caribbean usually does not result from cyclic bleaching
column3: but can be caused by e.g.
column4: diseases and other pathogens. According to our observations
column5: the mortality event in April 2015 was not a specific sponge disease as numerous Xestospongia individuals
column6: other sponge species and corals adjacent to affected sponges started to bleach. During the surveys
column7: the mortality of further marine fauna was documented
column8: including numerous individuals of the marine snail Tectus sp.
column9: fish
column10: and crustaceans (P-J Grammel and E Schoenig
column12: personal communication). Taken these observations into account
column13: the mass mortality event could have had various reasons. Having numerous other species from different phyla affected
column14: could most likely indicate three possible scenarios for this mortality event: local higher water temperatures
column15: a local harmful algal bloom (HAB)
column16: or a combination of both factors.

column1: Mass mortality events affecting species from various phyla have been reported from the Mediterranean Sea as a result of marine heatwaves with increased sea water temperatures (
column2: ; ). Studies from other locations have reported increased water temperatures due to El Nino Southern Oscillation in 2015/2016
column3: which affected marine organisms and resulted likewise in bleaching and mortality (; ).  could also demonstrate in laboratory experiments with the temperate model sponge Crella incrustans
column4: that experimentally induced heatwaves led to disease and mortality in the exposed sponges. Sponge mortality events have also been reported by  from the Florida Keys. They observed widespread sponge mortality of various sponge species which occurred together with cyanobacterial blooms
column5: the likely cause of the observed mortality. They also documented long-lasting ecological consequences for the recovery of sponge communities for more than one decade.

column1: There was no regional marine heatwave detected during the observed Xestospongia sp. mass mortality event between February and April 2015 based on the SST satellite dataset for the area around Mae Haad (AI) and nearby Haad Khom (AI). Therefore
column2: it is highly unlikely that a marine heatwave directly caused the observed mass mortality in April 2015. However
column3: heatwaves after the investigation in April 2015 could have reduced the chance for recovery or could have increased further mortality of Xestospongia sp. by April 2016.

column1: The mass mortality event was only a local phenomenon. Only sponges at the two monitored reefs of Koh Phangan were impacted
column2: while no affected sponges were found at the same water depths at the monitored reefs of Koh Yippon (ANI) and Hin Yippon (ANI). Considering these circumstances
column3: it becomes most likely that the mass mortality event may have been triggered by a local HAB.

column1: In general
column2: HAB events are increasing rapidly in density
column3: distribution
column4: and intensity
column5: and most coasts worldwide are nowadays threatened (; ; ). This is caused amongst others by climate change and increasing nutrient pollution (; ). HABs are caused by toxic microalgae or cyanobacteria
column6: which can lead during outbreaks to illness and death of fish
column7: other marine invertebrates
column8: seabirds
column9: and even humans (; ). Sponges are important for benthic-pelagic coupling as they filter large amounts of water (up to 1
column10: 000 L*h−1*kg−1 body mass
column11: ()). This in turn could make them also very susceptible to HABs
column12: by retaining the microalgae and thereby accumulating the produced toxins
column13: leading ultimately to the death of the affected sponges (). In 2015
column14: HAB events were reported for other regions around the world
column15: including the U.S. West Coast
column16: coastal areas in Northern Europe
column17: and Canada (; ; ). For example
column18: the HAB at the U.S. West Coast was a coastwide event
column19: negatively impacting fisheries and initiated by anomalously warm water conditions (; ).

column5: could be possible triggers for a HAB outbreak resulting in the observed mass mortality of Xestospongia sponges at the study sites Mae Haad (AI) and Haad Khom (AI). But it is difficult to determine the reason for the sponge mortality event because environmental parameters such as nutrients

column4: and PO43−) were rather small between the bays ranging in pH from 7.95 to 8.12

column1: The reefs of Koh Yippon (ANI) and Hin Yippon (ANI) showed just little changes in the abundance of Xestospongia sp. between the years 2015 and 2016. These small variances could be either caused by growth effects over the course of one year
column2: or by monitoring artefacts
column3: since the position and the number of the transect tapes differed between the two survey years. However
column4: these anthropogenic non-impacted study sites represented in both survey years populations with a higher number of observed Xestospongia sp. than at the anthropogenically impacted locations. A decline in sponge health was not detected during the surveys at both Marine Park locations.

column1: Regarding the size distribution of Xestospongia sp.
column2: sponge sizes (according to volume classes) showed a similar Gaussian distribution at all study sites in both survey years. The highest number of Xestospongia sp. were found for all study sites at the middle class 1
column4: 000 cm³. The lower number of small individuals could possibly be caused by a higher risk of being eaten by predators. Larger individuals are probably more often affected by environmental factors over time due to their higher age (). The mass mortality event also showed its effects on the size distribution. Only two sponges of the previously dominant middle class were still alive at Mae Haad (AI) in 2016. At Haad Khom (AI)
column5: Xestospongia individuals within the small and middle classes survived after the massive bleaching. The results show that sponges of all sizes were affected by the mass mortality event. This observation suggests an exceptional event occurred in the northern region of Koh Phangan
column6: as large sponges
column7: known for their resilience and ability to withstand cyclic bleaching
column8: disappeared in 2016.

column1: To estimate the age of sponge individuals
column2: size-at-age models have been used in earlier studies. Reports from Caribbean Xestospongia muta revealed ages of 127 years
column3: and estimated the largest sponges on these reefs to be more than 2
column4: 300 years old
column5: reflecting Xestospongia spp. as a long-lived and slow-growing sponge species (). Contrary and more recently
column6: found Indo-Pacific Xestospongia spp. from Sulawesi
column7: Indonesia
column8: to be faster growing than their Caribbean relatives. We used the parameter from the growth model from  to estimate the age of our sponges
column9: since they are both Pacific populations and represent more likely the same species. The largest measured individuals of the investigated Xestospongia sp. (March 2015 at Haad Khom (AI)) were up to 24 years old (). One year later
column10: the oldest sponge at the same location was only 9 years old. Sponges of the middle volume classes
column11: which were most abundant and survived the bleaching
column12: were around 3–7 years old.

column1: Although three species of Xestospongia
column2: namely X. muta
column3: X. testudinaria
column4: and X. bergquistia
column5: are recognized globally
column6: recent studies suggest that these species are part of complexes consisting of cryptic species (; ).  analyzed 10 Xestospongia samples from Koh Tao (only ~40 km away from our sampling sites) and identified three different haplotypes. However
column7: the limitations of the genetic markers made it impossible to determine the number of species. The current species consensus of all these samples was X. testudinaria. Despite being cryptic species and challenging to differentiate
column8: different Xestospongia species can exhibit varying growth performances. For instance
column9: explored intraspecific variation within a single population of X. muta in Florida
column10: and identified two distinct
column11: co-occurring genetic clusters. Furthermore
column12: they found that each genetic cluster of X. muta exhibited a preference towards different sponge sizes
column13: highlighting the variation in growth performance among these cryptic species. We can hypothesize that in addition to differences in growth performance among different Xestospongia species
column14: these variations may also be attributed to other ecological differences
column15: such as their susceptibility to diseases or heat stress. Dealing with potentially cryptic species
column16: it becomes essential to consider that factors like disease resistance or tolerance could contribute to the observed dissimilarities in growth patterns. Understanding how various ecological factors interact with genetic variations can provide valuable insights into the complexities of Xestospongia populations and their overall ecological dynamics. Further research is necessary to explore these aspects comprehensively and gain a more comprehensive understanding of the factors influencing the growth and ecological dynamics of Xestospongia species.

column1: This study documented an extraordinary massive mortality event of Xestospongia populations from two anthropogenic impacted reefs of Koh Phangan
column2: Thailand. Xestospongia sp. mortality ranged from 98.4% at Mae Haad (AI) to 80.6% at Haad Khom (AI) (between monitoring events in 2015 and 2016). Anthropogenic non-impacted reefs at the Mu Ko Ang Thong Marine National Park were not affected. This underlines the importance to establish and enforce protected Marine Parks for maintaining healthy ecosystems. The localized mortality event was probably triggered by a local HAB
column3: pathogens
column4: undetected local higher water temperatures
column5: or a combination of these factors. Whereas no marine heatwave was detected during the observed mass mortality event in 2015 from sea surface temperature data. Xestospongia sp. of all sizes were affected. The lack of Xestospongia sp. following the mass mortality event in the two surveyed bays
column6: could lead to a decrease in biodiversity
column7: a weaker filter efficiency and loss of 3D complexity and function (e.g.
column8: habitat and shelter) of the reefs. The enormous loss of Xestospongia sp. in Mae Haad (AI) and Haad Khom (AI) could also have serious long-term consequences for the sponge populations and for the coral reefs in these locations
column9: as reproduction and recruitment patterns for this species are unknown. Therefore
column10: it will be important to determine and reduce triggers of such mass mortality events
column11: especially for essential habitat-forming organisms such as Xestospongia spp. Long-term monitoring programs are needed to document the condition of reef systems and its ecological parameters
column12: and to detect and act on the causes of decline.

column2: Antrozous pallidus
column3: is widespread in western North America in arid to semiarid
column4: rocky habitats
column5: open deserts
column6: and oak and pine forest. Previous studies have shown that this species is generally a predator on arthropods
column7: especially insects. However
column8: pallid bats are opportunistic and flexible
column9: occasionally taking fruit from organ pipe cactus (Stenocereus thurberi) and cardón (Pachycereus pringlei) in the Southwest
column10: and at least incidentally
column11: they also take pollen and nectar from flowering columnar cacti (; ; ; ; ; ; ) and probably agaves (). Most often
column12: pallid bats prey upon relatively large
column13: flightless arthropods; occasionally they also eat small vertebrates (; ; ; ; ; ). Some of these prey items are taken during a brief touchdown or are gleaned from foliage during flight. Pallid bats use a characteristic searching flight that usually involves relatively slow and maneuverable flying about 0.5–2.5 m above the ground while making rhythmic rises and dips interspersed with swoops and glides when the bat detects prey (). Occasionally the bats hover near low or thorny vegetation
column14: or land on the ground where they are quite agile at using a variety of gaits and strides to pursue prey. This foraging style carries high risks of injury and predation for the bats
column15: whose wing membranes and bones heal but show scars and deformities (). While preying upon scorpions and centipedes they also endure venomous stings to the face and other body parts (). Previous authors () showed that pallid bats eat arthropods that share at least two of four characteristics: (1) large size; (2) either obligatorily or primarily active on the ground surface; (3) fly weakly at low heights; or (4) fly strongly but often land on vegetation. The bats frequently retreat to a night roost to rest or manipulate and eat the prey they have caught. They alight on the ceilings of rock shelters
column16: overhangs
column17: or small grottos temporarily to process their prey. The bats drop undesired parts of the arthropods such as wings
column18: elytra
column19: and legs. The discarded items provide qualitative data on pallid bat diets (e.g.
column21: although  found that the insects represented in the culled parts were biased toward the hardest and largest prey species eaten relative to species represented in fecal pellets. In this study
column22: we investigated food habits of pallid bats in southeastern Arizona and made observations on their interactions with the predominant insect in their local diet
column23: the mesquite bug Thasus neocalifornicus. We add to the list of arthropod and vertebrate prey known to be taken by pallid bats. Importantly
column24: we document observations relevant to an example of these bats feeding opportunistically on large numbers of mesquite bugs
column25: as well as the first record in the wild of their feeding on another species of bat in southeastern Arizona. We also provide observations of certain mesquite bug behaviors that may increase their susceptibility to the foraging style of pallid bats.

column1: As gleaning bats that hunt arthropods moving or resting on plant or ground surfaces
column2: pallid bats have well-developed acoustical
column3: olfactory
column4: and visual senses. They mainly locate their prey by sound
column5: either through active echolocation or passive detection of the faint sounds made by moving prey (). In experiments with insects and their pheromones other than those of mesquite bugs (Thasus spp.)
column6: pallid bats are also sensitive to olfactory cues and are able to distinguish prey odors from controls and from non-prey species (). The bats show selectivity upon closely approaching certain prey insects versus a non-prey noxious insect (the Pinacate beetle Eleodes: Tenebrionidae) or paper balls impregnated with their odors (). The bats also have relatively large eyes and high visual acuity at low light levels ().

column1: Pallid bats previously were reported to take certain leaf-footed bugs (Heteroptera: Coreidae) only occasionally. Coreids are globally distributed but mostly subtropical and tropical insects
column2: with about 80 species in the continental United States and Canada (). One member genus
column3: Thasus (Coreinae: Nematopini)
column4: has eight species primarily distributed in the Neotropics (). Like many other Hemiptera
column5: Coreidae are herbivores that suck the contents of plant tissues (). Many coreids are also host specific
column6: feeding on one or two families of plants (). The only species of Thasus in the United States is Thasus neocalifornicus (giant mesquite bug); the species also occurs in Baja California and Sonora and Chihuahua
column7: Mexico (). The closely related species Thasus gigas and Thasus acutangulus occur further south in Mexico and Central America; these two species were once considered synonymous with T. neocalifornicus but have since been shown to be distinct (Brailovsky & Barrera in ; ). Thasus neocalifornicus is ecologically tied to mesquites (Prosopis
column8: Fabaceae; ; ; ; ). Mesquite trees have spread widely in the United States during the last two centuries along with cattle ()
column9: providing the potential for the bugs to expand their geographic range.   speculated that the United States populations of T. neocalifornicus in Arizona might have been an accidental introduction by humans. The species has been recorded in Arizona since at least 1876 () and is now also known north of Mexico from California to Texas (https://bugguide.net/node/view/20163).

column1: As a brief synopsis of the known ecology and annual phenological cycle of T. neocalifornicus relative to their host plant
column2: mesquite bugs are univoltine (having one generation per year and overwintering as eggs; (). Eggs eclose in February
column3: and nymphs aggregate around the eggs to use up the rest of the egg reserves and feed. Nymphs aggregate using specialized pheromones (adults do not respond to the pheromones in tests)
column4: and secrete malodorous
column5: toxic compounds in self-defense and possibly as alarm chemicals to alert conspecifics against predator attacks. The nymphal toxins are effective on insect predators in tests; tests do not seem to have been made on vertebrate predators (). Nymphs feed on mesquite leaves and pods (once available)
column6: and molt through their first-fifth instars from January–July; they often migrate to the base of their host trees in summer
column7: probably in response to high afternoon temperatures (). Mesquites flower from February-March
column8: and bear fruits (bean pods) from July–October. As nymphs
column9: mesquite bugs are unable to fly and are aposematically colored red
column10: white
column11: and brownish or blackish to advertise their noxious secretions. The coloration probably deters visually oriented diurnal predators such as birds and larger invertebrate predators. Although the warning coloration might be visible to bats during twilight hours
column12: night-active bats might be repelled by the nymphs’ noxious secretions. The nymphs often stay under the foliage on the spiny branches of the mesquite canopy; they also form defensive aggregations that secrete noxious fluid from their abdomens
column13: similar to a related species
column14: T. acutangulus in Central America (). The adults first start to appear from the fifth nymphal instars around July–August
column15: with the highest proportion of adults between May–September. By contrast with the nymphs
column16: the adult mesquite bugs are large and dark colored (blackish brown and dark reddish) and no longer secrete the chemicals that are toxic to small insect predators. Instead
column17: the adults secrete a different set of noxious chemicals from those of the nymphs to trigger aggregations and in response to a simulated predator disturbance. These chemicals include hexyl acetate
column18: hexenal
column19: and hexanol (; ). Adult pheromones are not toxic or deterrent to insect predators but might deter vertebrates such as birds
column20: a major group of predators on adult heteropteran insects (). Adult mesquite bugs feed in the mesquite trees until late summer
column21: then breed and lay eggs on mesquite stems and under bark in late summer-early fall. Females start ovipositing around August and continue through October
column22: when the mesquite trees start dropping their leaves (October–January). They overwinter only in the egg stage ().

column1: Insect parts were first noted by one of us (WDP) in 1994 in one of the soil-piping cavities at Cienega Creek. In 1996
column2: WDP discovered two additional cavities with accumulations of insect fragments and made sightings of A. pallidus. We chose to study the culled insect parts discarded by the pallid bats as a non-intrusive method of determining the macro-arthropodophagous diet in this bat population. Pallid bats are sensitive to disturbance at their roosts (; )
column3: thus we collected insect remains at a night roost not used by the bats during the daytime as a way to avoid interference in their activity. The pallid bats were usually absent from the soil-piping cavities when we collected samples in the daytime except on one occasion in September 2002 when we observed two individuals. We visited the cavities and collected insect parts once in November 1996

column6: in February and September 2002
column7: and once in September 2004. These did not represent seasonal samples but were merely times at which we were able to visit the soil-piping cavities and collect the remains that had accumulated since our previous visit. We collected all pieces from the larger concentrations of pallid bat prey that could easily be picked up by hand for later identification and to sample the overall diversity of species eaten

column9: our study reinforces previously published data about the contribution of prey brought into night roosts to the total diet of pallid bats. We identified insect parts by comparison with intact museum specimens in the Oklahoma Museum of Natural History

column1: On 11 September 2002 we made observations and photographed mesquite bug behavior in the mesquite bosque in late afternoon and early evening. We observed apparent end-of-season mating and mortality of adult insects. We also recorded air temperatures and relative humidity with a handheld electronic sensor outside one of the pallid bat night-roosting cavities during the sundown-to-dark transition period to investigate the relationship of temperature and humidity on adult activities late in the season. On the same date
column2: we collected several of the dead and dying mesquite bugs as voucher specimens. We inferred the sex of the mesquite bugs eaten using sexually dimorphic hind leg parts ().

column1: Pallid bats used only three of six available soil-piping cavities in the CCNP mesquite bosque as night roosts during our study

The soil-piping cavities () offered several characteristics that make them suitable as night roosts for pallid bats: (1) enclosed space providing protection from the weather and nocturnal flying predators; (2) easy access with from one to three entrances of relatively large dimensions; (3) relatively spacious interior (in this aspect the cavities were somewhat like the daytime roosts described by ) mostly unobstructed except for occasional exposed mesquite roots; (4) high ceilings and steep walls

column5: respectively; (5) rough ceiling surface texture providing secure grip for the bats’ claws while processing prey; (6) proximity to the bats’ foraging area
column6: and to at least one observed day roost.

and subsequent authors have compiled a long list of arthropod prey species taken by pallid bats. Our results add 17 taxa not previously recorded as pallid bat prey to the overall list. Pallid bats in our study fed upon large moths as well as large beetles;  indicated that beetles were about 3.2 times harder than moths of the same body size

column6: especially for a bat species that depends on being able to continue echolocating to fly. Some bats might capture prey that are too tough to process in flight and must land to process them. column1: In our study
column2: the higher numbers of large
column3: armored
column4: and cumbersome legs and other body parts of mesquite bugs found beneath roosts relative to other taxa of insects suggests that mesquite bugs are more difficult for pallid bats to process than other kinds of insects. Mesquite bugs have a small head
column5: thorax
column6: and abdomen with large legs relative to most of the beetles and moths in the bats’ diet. Perhaps the relative ease with which mesquite bugs are located or secured in late summer or early autumn counterbalances the energy and time needed to commute to a night roost to process them. The size and hardness of the insects eaten by pallid bats suggests there is a large upper size limit to what insects pallid bats are capable of processing and eating. Mesquite bugs (Thasus) are among the largest terrestrial heteropterans known () and are 28–43 mm in body length. The relative abundance of their body parts recovered in the soil-piping cavities leads us to hypothesize that the availability of the cavities and their proximity to the mesquite bosque enhanced their usage by pallid bats as a place to process a seasonally abundant source of food.

column1: As adults
column2: mesquite bugs do not secrete the same compounds as a defense against insect predators that they do as nymphs. Most insect predators are not interested in the non-toxic adults because the bugs are so big. For the mesquite bugs
column3: it might not be evolutionarily worthwhile to invest energy in producing toxic compounds against other insect predators when it is unnecessary. The adult bugs switch to a defense of muted colors (thus being more cryptic to visually-oriented aerial predators)
column4: and a physically more armored exoskeleton (spiny hind legs
column5: tougher wings)
column6: but less noxious chemicals than nymphs. We hypothesize that this is not necessarily a change to prevent predation as much as a trade-off of putting less investment in defense (producing energetically expensive coloration and toxins) and more investment into reproduction (wings provide mobility to find mates
column7: less toxic chemical investment for short period of mating and death).

column1: As noted earlier
column2: in laboratory experiments pallid bats showed an aversion to the odor of a Pinacate beetle Eleodes (); however
column3: at least one species of Eleodes
column4: E. acuticauda
column5: has been reported as a prey item for pallid bats (:232)
column6: and the genus also appeared as prey in our study. Perhaps the bats are able to process and discard the noxious parts of certain insects as well as the armored hard parts. Although the chemicals secreted by adult mesquite bugs differ from those of nymphs (chemical components frequently change after metamorphosis; )
column7: the adult compounds have not been tested with vertebrates
column8: so it is unknown whether the chemicals produced by the adult bugs actually deter vertebrate predators (). Of the secretions produced by the adult bugs (hexyl acetate
column9: hexanal
column10: 1-hexanol and possibly others)
column11: hexyl acetate and hexanal might be aggregational pheromones directed toward other mesquite bugs (; ). Given that pallid bats in our study never ate nymphs
column12: the nymphal secretions might be effective not only against insect predators but also against bats. And given the frequency with which pallid bats ate the adults
column13: either the adult bugs are non-noxious to pallid bats or else the bats are not susceptible or averse to their secretions.

column1: The only non-insect prey item found at a pallid bat night roost in this study was another bat
column2: the flower-visiting phyllostomid Choeronycteris mexicana. As noted above
column3: C. mexicana utilized separate but adjacent soil-piping cavities at CCNP in summers during our study. There is one previous record of pallid bats eating a Mexican free-tailed bat
column4: Tadarida brasiliensis
column5: although the predation occurred while the two species were in captivity
column6: being held together in the same cage from which the smaller free-tailed bats were unable to escape (). Thus
column7: the C. mexicana at CCNP is the first recorded instance in the wild of predation by A. pallidus on another species of bat.

column1: Many kinds of animals take advantage of situations arising as they acquire food (). Like many predators
column2: various species of bats are opportunistic on hatches of insects (e.g.
column3: Myotis [Vespertilionidae]
column4: ; Lavia frons [Megadermatidae]
column5: ; ; Taphozous melanopogon [Emballonuridae]
column6: Hipposideros sp. [Hipposideridae]
column7: and Scotophilus temminckii [Vespertilionidae]
column8: ; Hipposideros gigas [Hipposideridae]
column9: ; Nycteris grandis [Nycteridae]
column10: ) and also passively use sounds produced by the insects rather than actively echolocating them. Several of the large insects preyed upon are noisy fliers
column11: and pallid bats might thus detect them easily. Some
column12: like antlions
column13: are poor fliers as adults (). At CCNP
column14: opportunistic feeding was associated with high selectivity for a single prey species that could make wide searches for patches of food energetically worthwhile.

column1: The body parts discarded versus parts eaten indicates that pallid bats take the most easily digestible and probably most nutritious parts of mesquite bugs. The abdomens of gravid female bugs filled with egg masses in particular might provide additional protein. When mesquite bugs are clustered and immobile on the periphery of the mesquite canopy
column2: bats can likely capture the bugs easily compared to within the thorny canopy. Mated females move from the periphery deeper into the mesquite tree canopy to find appropriate places to deposit their eggs
column3: and thus are less susceptible to being located and preyed upon compared to males
column4: which might explain the male bias in our samples. The noxious and aposematic defenses of the nymphs
column5: which are unable to fly
column6: render them relatively immune to attack at night by the bats perhaps due to their odor and quieter movements
column7: and to visually-oriented predators like birds during the day (or bats during twilight). By becoming immobile overnight after they alight on mesquite foliage at evening twilight
column8: the bugs might avoid detection by bats. The noisy flight and possibly other movements and activities of the mesquite bugs
column9: beetles
column10: grasshoppers
column11: and other large insects has been little studied and could be an important aspect of the bat-insect relationship. Similarly
column12: the influence of anthropogenic noise (e.g.
column13: automobile traffic
column14: railroad noise
column15: air traffic) on a passive-sound-using predator limits the pallid bats’ foraging efficiency and potentially their ability to utilize certain areas for foraging (; ).

column1: Unfortunately
column2: in this study we were unable to collect data seasonally or regularly
column3: but a seasonal or monthly collection of dietary data would provide a good future study to pursue this ecological relationship in greater depth. Moreover
column4: the bats might select mesquite bugs as prey only when the bugs are the most vulnerable: in late summer or early autumn after the adult females laid the eggs for the overwintering generation
column5: and when falling nighttime air temperatures
column6: local cool air drainage from the adjacent mountains and foothills
column7: and high evapotranspiration might slow the insect’s activity or mobility. Finally
column8: the overwhelming majority of insects consumed in the night roosts were mesquite bugs locally derived from the mesquite bosque; the bats consumed other kinds of insects almost incidentally. In addition to their ability to endure injuries and heal ()
column9: the dietary plasticity shown by pallid bats across the species’ broad geographic range might help to lessen their risk of extinction () in the face of anthropogenic environmental upset and climate change.

column1: The diet of pallid bats can be investigated non-intrusively by visiting their temporary-use night roosts during the day while the bats are away at separate day roosts. However
column2: the night roosts possibly yield evidence only of those foods that are large enough to require transport to a temporary night roost for processing of edible versus inedible parts. Adult mesquite bugs formed the predominant prey for pallid bats at the CCNP. We found no evidence of pallid bats feeding on toxic
column3: aposematically colored nymphal stages of mesquite bugs. Adult mesquite bugs are possibly non-toxic to pallid bats
column4: or perhaps the bats are able to tolerate the less-toxic compounds of the adult bugs. Late-season breeding and postbreeding adult mesquite bugs are exposed near the edges of the mesquite canopy and provide prey for opportunistic
column5: foliage-gleaning pallid bats. After breeding and laying eggs that overwinter in the mesquite trees
column6: moribund adult mesquite bugs begin to become immobile in the trees or drop from the canopy when the nighttime low temperatures at CCNP fell below 21 °C. Mesquite bugs are considered to be mostly subtropical-tropical insects that may have invaded the southwestern United States during historic times with the bringing of cattle and spread of mesquite trees; pallid bats at the CCNP are providing an important natural control on the local mesquite bug population. Their exploitation of mesquite bugs is probably enhanced by the proximity of soil-piping caves to the mesquite bosque as a place to process the insects. Pallid bats at the CCNP ate numerous taxa of large-bodied insects
column7: consistent with their diet in many other portions of the bats’ range. When mesquite bugs are observable in the local mesquite trees
column8: their procurement by pallid bats can be determined by the presence of large reddish guano pellets 2.5–3.5 mm in diameter beneath local bat night roosts. Insects parts discarded beneath pallid bat roosts can be distinguished from insect parts culled by birds or other predators by distinctive tooth marks on the discarded insect parts. Bats usually ate the abdomen and thorax of mesquite bugs and most consistently discarded the wings and legs. At the CCNP
column9: pallid bats left the remains of no arthropods other than insects. Seventeen taxa of insects were newly identified as prey for pallid bats
column10: and reflect a diversity of local habitats of the CCNP as foraging habitat for the bats. In addition
column11: pallid bats ate an individual of one other local species of bat
column12: the Mexican long-nosed bat
column13: another first recorded instance of such predation for pallid bats.
