column1: 60|10.7717/peerj.47|Microbial secondary succession in soil microcosms of a desert oasis in the Cuatro Cienegas Basin
column2: Mexico| López-Lozano  Nguyen E. |2013-3-5|"In ecological theory
column3: succession is defined as the predictable manner by which communities change over time during the colonization of a new environment or following a disturbance (primary and secondary succession respectively) (). Bacterial succession has been explored in a variety of environments and over different timescales (). However
column4: there are many theoretical and methodological questions that remain unanswered. For example the order of occurrence and species turnover in successional stages can differ and be explained by stochastic or functional factors depending on the use of ecological or functional classifications instead of 16S rRNA taxonomic classifications (; ). Notwithstanding
column5: there are theoretical models for microbial community succession and assembly
column6: most of them derived from those already well defined for macroorganisms (). Due to the complexity of the patterns observed in the succession of microbial communities
column7: alternative explanations started to appear based on carbon (C) inputs to the systems
column8: suggesting that species richness and the biomass of specific ecological groups change along successional stages ().

column1: In recent years
column2: the role of deterministic and stochastic processes in community assembly has been tested. It has been shown that bacterial community assemblages can be regulated by the local environment (species sorting) ()
column3: massive immigration that can prevent competitive exclusion of species (mass effect) ()
column4: and neutral process
column5: which assumes that all species are similar in their competitive ability and in dispersal. In the neutral process scenario
column6: changes in community composition result from neutral drift over time (; ). These mechanisms can co-occur
column7: resulting in microbial communities being structured by more than one process (). However
column8: the development of such theoretical models are based mostly on work done in laboratory environments
column9: and little is known of what happens in natural systems such as soils (; ; ; ). Data on how microbial communities change through time in natural environments are needed in order to refine these theoretical models.

column1: Soils are among the most diverse microbial environments analyzed to date (; )
column2: which makes the identification of differences in community diversity patterns between stable and disturbed soils challenging. Arid soils are thought to have lower productivity and diversity than other soil habitats
column3: and therefore may provide a better opportunity to validate molecular ecosystems approaches to examine the genetic and functional organization of native microbial consortia. Earth’s arid regions represent today nearly one third of total continental ecosystems () and are thought to be more vulnerable under most scenarios of global climate change (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change
column4: 2007 report).

column1: The Cuatro Cienegas Basin (CCB) in Coahuila
column2: Mexico
column3: is an arid zone that has recently been threatened by aquifer overexploitation. The gypsum-based soil system at CCB is one of the most oligotrophic environments in the world despite the high diversity in comparison with other arid soils (). Nevertheless
column4: due to recent and ongoing overexploitation of the deep aquifer in this oasis
column5: we decided to evaluate the community sensitivity after disturbance. In this study we experimentally sampled and disturbed soil microcosms with gamma rays and sterilization. Samples were restored to their original site in a mesh bag that allowed migration. Then
column6: community composition as well as nutrient dynamics were followed and compared to neighboring undisturbed soil samples. The objective of this study was to evaluate the recovery of microbial biodiversity and biogeochemical characteristics of the Churince within the CCB during a process of microbial secondary succession over one year period after disturbance and to find out if microbial succession the shift pattern behaves like any of the previously proposed models. Our approach was designed to be experimentally analogous to sections of a forest cleared by removing the biota of an area
column7: and tracking recolonization by migration.

column1: Site description and experimental design. The sampling sites are located at the Churince oasis system on the west side of the Cuatro Cienegas Basin (CCB) at 740 m above sea level. This valley has <15.0 cm of annual precipitation falling mainly during the summer. The dominant soil is gypsisol
column2: and the predominant vegetation types are halophile and gypshophile grasses. As described before ()
column3: the Churince system consists of a spring
column4: an intermediate lagoon
column5: and a desiccation lagoon (Dry Lagoon)
column6: connected by short shallow creeks. Two sites separated by one kilometer were selected. One site borders a small river creek (River) (26°50′43″N/102°8′18″W)
column7: and has 60% of plant coverage. The grass Sporobolus airoides is the dominant plant. The other site is closer to a dry lagoon (26°50′53″N/102°8′52″W) and has only 10% of plant coverage. At this site the gypsophile grass
column8: Sesuvium erectum
column9: is the dominant plant species. The sites have alkaline soils with slight variation in the pH of 8.5–8.8.

column1: In January of 2007
column2: 20 kg of soil were collected from each site. The soil was mixed and used to construct microcosms of 1 kg of soil in permeable bags of nylon mesh (25 × 25 cm). The microcosms were sterilized by autoclaving followed by a dose of 25 kGy of gamma rays (at the Instituto Nacional de Investigaciones Nucleares
column3: La Marquesa
column4: México). One microcosm sample from each site was immediately used for DNA extraction as a control.

column1: In February 2007
column2: plots of 8 × 8 m were established at each site. The plots were divided in 64 quadrants of 1 m2
column3: and 40 microcosm bags were placed randomly within the plots as replicates. Every three months
column4: three microcosm bags were collected randomly during a period of one year (3
column6: 9 and 12 months)
column7: 12 in total for each site. The remaining microcosms were deposited in the site in order to guarantee enough samples in each sampling period but not analyzed for this experiment. In addition
column8: three samples from 15 cm3 of undisturbed soil next to the microcosms in each plot were randomly collected at the beginning and end (12 months) of the experiment to describe the community that potentially could colonize the microcosms. 50 g of each sample were stored in liquid nitrogen until DNA extraction. The remaining soil was stored in black plastic bags at 4 °C during one month until processing in the laboratory for biogeochemical analysis.

column1: Soil biogeochemical analyses. Separate samples were oven-dried at 75 °C to constant weight for soil moisture determination by the gravimetric method (). Measurements of (C) forms were determined with a Total Carbon Analyzer (UIC Mod. CM5012)
column2: while nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) forms were determined using a Bran-Luebbe Auto Analyzer III (Norderstedt
column3: Germany). Total and inorganic C were determined by dry combustion and coulometric detection (). Organic C was calculated as the difference between total and inorganic C. N and P concentrations were determined following acid digestion; N was determined using a modified Kjeldahl method ()
column4: and P was determined with the molybdate colorimetric method following ascorbic acid reduction (). Microbial C (micC) and microbial N (micN) concentrations were determined from field-moist samples by a chloroform fumigation extraction method ().

column1: Inorganic N (NH4+ and NO3-) was extracted from fresh sub-samples with 2 M KCl
column2: followed by filtration through a Whatman No. 1 paper filter ()
column3: and determined colorimetrically by the phenol-hypochlorite method. Dissolved organic C
column4: N and P were extracted in two grams of fresh material with deionized water after shaking for 1 h
column5: and filtering through a Whatman #42 filter and a 0.45 µm nitrocellulose membrane (). Dissolved organic C (DOC) was determined by combustion and coulometric detection (). Dissolved organic N (DON) and dissolved organic P (DOP) were determined after acid digestion. DON was calculated as the difference between digested soluble N and NH4+ in deionized water extracts. The DOP was calculated as the difference between digested dissolved P and inorganic P (as orthophosphate).

column1: Statistical analyses. To evaluate significant differences in biogeochemical soil parameters between sites and through the time during the microcosms experiment
column2: we analyzed biogeochemical variables with a repeated measures analysis of variance (RMANOVA) with one between-subject factor (site: Dry Lagoon and River) and one within factor (sampling date: 3
column4: 9 and 12 months). In order to compare biogeochemical soil parameters between sites
column5: and between disturbed and undisturbed soil in the last sampling date (12 months)
column6: we carried out a two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) (factor 1 levels: sites Dry Lagoon and River; factor 2 levels: undisturbed soil and microcosm).

column1: The relationship between microbial community composition in terms of the most abundant bacterial families
column2: soil characteristics and samples (date and site) was analyzed by canonical correspondence analysis (CCA). In this analysis
column3: the samples are represented by a centroid. Its position is indicative of the relationship between a specific sample and either of the ordination axes. Soil characteristics are represented by vectors. Vectors of greater magnitude and forming smaller angles with an ordination axis are more strongly correlated with that ordination axis. High scores of absolute value for a given family or a given site on a CCA axis indicate that it is highly related to the axis and to the environmental variable exhibiting high correlation to the axis. All soil characteristics were tested for significant contribution to the explanation of the variation in bacterial family community composition with an ANOVA like permutation test to assess the significance of constraints. Only variables that were significant by the permutation test at the P ≤ 0.05 level were included in the CCA biplot. The CCA was performed using the package Vegan in R (http://www.r-project.org/). In the supplementary material
column4: we added a DCA analysis without the restrictive variables for comparison ().

column1: DNA Extraction. Total DNA was extracted using the Soil Master DNA Extraction Kit (EPICENTRE Biotechnology) according to the manufacturer’s instructions
column2: with an additional step of bacterial isolation using the fractionation centrifugation technique described in . This step was performed on frozen 50 g soil samples before DNA extraction as a way of reducing the remaining concentrations of salts
column3: polysaccharides and secondary compounds. DNA was stored at −20 °C.

column1: In order to confirm sterilization in the control microcosms
column2: the DNA region coding for 16S rRNA was PCR amplified using universal primers ( and ). Since no band was recovered in a PCR gel run using the sterilized soil
column3: the sterilization was assumed to be effective.

column1: Pyrosequencing of 16S rRNA tags. 16S rRNA genes were amplified from each sampling date (pooled DNA from the three subsamples was used as template) using the 939F (TTGACGGGGGCCCGCAC) and 1492R (TACCTTGTTACGACTT) paired primers. Sequencing was undertaken using the standard Roche 454 Titanium LIB-A kit with multiplex identifier sequence (MID) tags () () at the Research and Testing Laboratory (Lubbock
column2: TX).

column1: Bioinformatic analysis of barcoded 16S ribosomal RNA gene libraries: Mothur open-source software package (v 1.15.0) () was used for processing
column2: clustering
column3: and classification of the quality screened sequence data. Raw sequences were screened for potential chimeric reads using Chimera.slayer () and the linked SILVA template database (26%). Sequences containing homopolymer runs of 9 or more bases
column4: those with more than one mismatch to the sequencing primer and those longer than 565 bp were eliminated. Group membership was determined prior to trimming of the bar-code MID and primer sequence. Sequences were aligned against the manually curated bacterial SILVA 16S rRNA gene template using the nearest alignment space termination (NAST) algorithm (; )
column5: and manually trimmed for the optimal alignment region (start = 28596
column7: which yielded aligned fragments of 270 bp long
column8: including both the V6 and V7 regions. Pre-clustering
column9: based on the SLP clustering algorithm ()
column10: was used to reduce the effect of pyrosequencing errors on subsequent analysis. A pairwise distance matrix was calculate across the non-redundant sequence set
column11: and reads were clustered into operational taxonomic units (OTUs) at 3% distance using the furthest neighbor method (). This matrix was used to calculate the similarity based on the Bray-Curtis and Jaccard Index between the samples in each site and to create a cluster diagram of sample similarity. The sequences and OTUs were categorized taxonomically using Mothur’s Bayesian classifier and the SILVA bacterial reference set. A representative sequence from the center of each OTU grouping was classified using a naïve Bayesian approach (). The taxonomic reference database was composed of unique
column12: full-length sequences from the SILVA SSU Ref v.106 database (http://www.mothur.org/wiki/Silva_reference_files) (). To account for the effects of different sequencing depths on the alpha-diversity measurements

column16: Shannon indices

We found that the CCB soil at the Dry Lagoon site was more oligotrophic (C:N:P ratio of 125:5:1) than the River site (C:N:P ratio 300:16:1) and in comparison with a general “average” soil C:N:P of 186:13:1 (). Microbial diversity and secondary succession in a gypsum based soil. In this study we analyzed the bacterial secondary succession of experimentally disturbed soil microcosms at two sites over one year. While each site differed in both biogeochemistry and biodiversity
column2: both present similar community composition at the beginning of the experiment. However
column3: successional patterns differed in later stages. This suggests an species sorting effect at the beginning of the experiment
column4: followed by a more neutral process in later stages. At the end of the experiment
column5: comparing the adjacent soil samples with the experimentally disturbed soil
column6: the microbial communities at either site do not resemble adjacent undisturbed microbial communities. Both disturbed soils were most closely related to each other ().

column1: Four factors contribute strongly to differences in our sites: humidity
column2: Total N
column3: Total Organic C and Total P. The first three factors were identified as the most correlated variables with the relative abundance of the bacterial groups. These factors have been considered as part of the best group of soil variables for predicting microbial community composition in arid lands (; ). We also found that Total P in the soil explained some variability. This was expected
column4: as P is the most limiting nutrient in CCB ().

column3: we still only captured 52% to 81% of the microbial communities in our samples ( and ). This was surprising
column4: since in more fertile and wet soils analyzed with similar sequencing effort
column5: the maximum number of OTUs at 97% of identity seldom exceeds ∼5
column6: 600 OTUs ()
column7: while in our more diverse sample
column8: we estimated ∼9
column9: 000 OTUs at 97% identity. However
column10: the sites analyzed in this study differed in diversity
column11: with soil adjacent to the River being more diverse than the Dry Lagoon if we compare the OTUs at 97% (). In the previous clone library study the same pattern was observed (). We also evaluated the community composition in terms of “ecological groups”
column12: considering groups in which abundance correlated with high or low C mineralization rates (copiotrophs or oligotrophs
column13: respectively) in general soil surveys (; )
column14: and widely known autotrophs (phototrophs such as Cyanobacteria and Chloroflexi). Also we determined some metabolic capabilities by comparison of the sequences with the closer cultivated organisms. Despite the broad ecological classification based only in 16S rRNA gene sequences
column15: our results showed interesting trends in groups considered as oligotrophs
column16: copiotrophs (organism that need more nutrients) as well as heterotrophs and autotrophs.

column1: The 16S rRNA libraries of both sites are dominated by Acidobacteria in all samples. This is one of the most common phyla found in soil libraries worldwide (). The Acidobacteria are in general oligotrophic (; ) and have been shown to comprise 50% of clone libraries in arid soils (; )
column2: but they are less abundant in more nutrient-rich agricultural soils (; ). Hence
column3: it is not surprising that they represent ∼30–60% of bacteria in all our libraries in CCB sampling sites. In contrast
column4: Bacteroidetes have been classified as copiotrophs in general (). In our libraries Bacteroidetes become much more abundant at the 6 months sampling
column5: and it is possible that these phyla appear when the accumulation of organic material is enough to sustain the mineralization rate of this group. In fact
column6: between 6 and 9 months there was a drop in the Dissolved Organic C while the microbial C increase in the soil microcosms. Autotrophic groups
column7: such as Cyanobacteria are abundant at both sites. This phylum is common in most environments including soil and biological soil crust of arid zones (; ). The abundance of yet another autotrophic group
column8: the Chloroflexi
column9: increased during the initial stages of succession (3–9 months) but decreased in the last sampling date (12 months). With the exception of high Chloroflexi in the River sample during the first samplings
column10: the majority of the colonizers are opportunistic heterotrophs (a greater part members of Firmicuttes
column11: Betaproteobacteria and Gammaproteobacteria). It is possible that especially in the Dry Lagoon site
column12: the initial colonizers were benefited by the Dissolved Organic C
column13: released during the sterilization process. The nutrient pulse could have facilitated colonization by heterotrophs that benefited from available resources in the disrupted microcosm. The finding of preferential growth of opportunistic heterotrophs during early succession agrees with the findings from another study using outdoor sterile microcosms seeded by rainwater bacteria (). These authors found that neutral and species sorting processes interacted during the assembly of bacterial communities
column14: and the importance of each depended on how many generalists and specialists were present in the community. We suggest that in our microcosms
column15: the initial faster growing community (generalists) depleted the nutrients
column16: and then were out competed by groups that have alternative energy sources (specialists). At 6 months the abundance of groups with more specialized metabolic capabilities increased
column17: and the taxa with low abundances before the sterilization
column18: “the rare biota” that were not present after three months returned to the community from this sampling date. The observed nitrate accumulation in the Dry Lagoon site could be due to an increase in nitrification
column19: an alternative mechanism of obtaining energy that could persist under those conditions (). Also
column20: the higher DOP in the Dry Lagoon site suggests that P mineralization is not occurring as efficiently as in the River site
column21: perhaps because of limited energy for exo-enzyme production necessary for acquisition (F Garcia-Oliva
column22: personal communication). Hence
column23: the Dry Lagoon site might be supporting a more oligotrophic microbiota than the River site. Conversely
column24: changes in DOC at the River site correlated with changes in microbial C. Additionally
column25: ammonium correlated with microbial N
column26: suggesting that the initial surge of nutrients could have facilitated a faster migration of the neighboring heterotrophic biomass in this site than in the Dry Lagoon site. Photosynthetic autotrophs (such as Chloroflexi) seem to be sufficient for nurturing the community in the River site providing C sources
column27: since nitrates and DOP are scarce
column28: indicating low nitrification rates.

column1: Regarding the diversity shifts
column2: both sites showed similar patterns. There is an initial increase in the Shannon index
column3: followed by a decrease at 9 months and an increase at 12 months. We do not discard the possibility that these convergent patterns only in the diversity index shifts can be related to climatic factors. At nine months a decrease in diversity was observed
column4: which coincided with an unusually heavy precipitation event ().

column1: The Bray-Curtis similarity index and Jaccard similarity coefficient analyses
column2: suggest that even though the initial communities are similar in composition
column3: they differ in later stages. In addition
column4: the analysis by phylogenetic and ecological groups
column5: suggest that the communities respond in a similar trajectory of initial colonization first by heterotropic generalists and later specialists. However the River site was characterized by higher nutrients and diversity
column6: together with the continuous presence of Cloroflexi in high abundance suggest an early food web based on primary production. This pattern is similar to the early stages of the autotrophic succession suggested by . Our experimental design cannot be used to test this autotrophic model
column7: but our data show that the colonizers were a mix of heterotrophs and autotrophs.

column1: In this study we found community composition of the soil microcosms after perturbation did not recover to similar communities found in the undisturbed soil community after one year. We are not assuming that there is only one “climax” community in terms of composition. What we suggest is that a community would be “recovered” when it has similar performing conditions to the neighboring undisturbed community. The performance of the community can be inferred by the soil nutrient content and characteristics. Due to the significant differences in physicochemical parameters between microcosms and control sites at 12 months
column2: we can conclude that despite the diversity found at the Churince soils
column3: the small patches (1 kg mesh bags) did not recover to resemble undisturbed soil in either site
column4: after a year of migration and succession. Nevertheless
column5: it seems they followed a parallel paths for such recovery at the beginning of the succession and diverged in later stages.

column1: This is the first study on bacterial soil dynamics conducted at the CCB. Our results provide important insight into microbial community dynamics in response to a disturbance (secondary succession). In this study we found changes in community composition across time that were indicative of the successional process. Our data showed evidence of similar initial colonizers in both sites
column2: but the divergence in later successional stages reflects stochastic factors suggesting a species sorting and neutral effects. While community descriptions based only on 16S rRNA gene analysis do not reflect the full metabolic plasticity of the communities
column3: this study provides important insight into microbial succession patterns. For further research metagenomics and measures of the rates of the physiological traits are necessary to corroborate our results. Notwithstanding
column4: it is interesting that succession of small
column5: perturbed spots is very slow; this reveals the temporal scale is important for this community in terms of resilience
column6: but general long-term monitoring is necessary to better understand the temporal patterns and natural variability of this area. Changes in microbial communities due to disturbance may directly affect ecosystem processes
column7: which are vital in a protected natural area
column8: threatened by over-exploitation of aquifers such as is occurring in the CCB.

column1: 61|10.7717/peerj.163|Microgeographic maladaptive performance and deme depression in response to roads and runoff| Brady  Steven P. |2013-9-17|"Identifying informative scales and levels of biological organization for investigation has long been regarded as a critical challenge in ecology
column2: limiting our ability to describe variation in the natural world (). Population level approaches—which are bringing to light profound variation that occurs across small spatial and temporal scales ()—offer a promising advancement on this challenge. Variation observed at this level can reveal dramatic differences among local populations
column3: even across microgeographic spatial scales (; ). For example
column4: spatially heterogeneous selection pressures coupled with heritable phenotypic variation has often been found to result in local adaptation (; ). Evidenced by a specific form of gene-by-environment interaction (G × E)
column5: local adaptation results when the local population has higher fitness than a foreign population measured within the local environment (). However
column6: gene flow and genetic drift can constrain local adaptation
column7: rendering populations equivalently adapted between environments
column8: or even maladapted to their local environment (; ; ; ). Though evident in wild populations ()
column9: such empirical examples of population level maladaptation are rare (). Rather
column10: most examples of maladaptation in general describe reduced fitness across higher levels of organization
column11: typically in host-parasite or host-pathogen systems
column12: where coevolution can generate reciprocating dynamics of maladaptation and adaptation ().

column1: In human-altered contexts
column2: where novel selection pressures and reduced gene flow can yield adaptive responses () and/or genetic drift ()
column3: and the rates of phenotypic change are high relative to undisturbed settings (; )
column4: the potential for population divergence should be elevated. Given this increased potential
column5: population level approaches should be viewed as essential for fulfilling conservation imperatives aimed at understanding the long-term fates of populations influenced by human activities. Here
column6: I report the population specific responses of a pool-breeding amphibian (the wood frog
column7: Rana sylvatica  =  Lithobates sylvaticus) occupying a landscape fragmented by roads. Given the suite of novel selection pressures associated with road fragmentation and runoff—along with previously documented contemporary local adaptation to temperature by the wood frog in this landscape ()—I expected that road adjacent populations of this species would be locally adapted to the roadside environment. In particular
column8: I expected road runoff contaminants—especially road salt ()—to act as the selective pressure conducing local adaptation in these populations. This outcome seems especially likely in light of recent evidence of local adaptation to roads by another amphibian (the spotted salamander
column9: Ambystoma maculatum) breeding and dwelling in the same locations ().

column1: I hypothesized (1) that the roadside environment would induce negative effects on the aquatic life stages of the wood frog
column2: and (2) that within a common roadside environment
column3: roadside populations would outperform woodland populations (i.e.
column4: the local adaptation hypothesis). I used a field-based reciprocal transplant experiment ( and ) coupled with a series of lab-based exposure experiments to test these hypotheses across a total of 10 populations (five roadside
column5: five woodland). Each of these populations occupied a distinct seasonal pool
column6: which comprises natural
column7: typical breeding habitat. To evaluate demographic consequences of roads in this system
column8: I surveyed wild population size and female reproductive effort. Finally
column9: to investigate potential mechanisms by which road proximity influences wood frog performance
column10: I characterized the environment at each pool by estimating a suite of abiotic variables associated with amphibian distribution and performance (). I expected that these characteristics might vary with road proximity
column11: and thus act as potential agents of natural selection in roadside pools.

column1: The wood frog is widely distributed throughout eastern North America and much of Canada
column2: with a range extending from within the Arctic Circle to the southeastern United States. Within the study region
column3: populations of this species breed in late March or early April
column4: when adults migrate from upland terrestrial habitat into ephemeral wetlands to reproduce. The wood frog is an explosive breeder
column5: with the majority of oviposition for a given population occurring in a single night (). Populations in this study region typically breed synchronously within several days of one another
column6: when each female oviposits one egg mass containing approximately 800 eggs per mass. Embryos develop over two-three weeks before hatching
column7: and continue to develop as aquatic larvae throughout spring and early summer until they metamorphose into terrestrial juveniles.

column1: My method for selecting populations for this study is previously reported in a reciprocal transplant study that indicated local adaptation to roads in the spotted salamander (). Briefly
column2: I selected the five roadside pools with the highest conductivity (µS) values (an indicator of road salt runoff) and with a breeding population comprising at least 10 wood frog families. For each roadside pool
column3: a nearby woodland pool was then selected to minimize confounding effects. Specifically
column4: I selected woodland pools based on the criterion of maximizing similarity in the abiotic conditions of canopy cover
column5: pool size
column6: and the presence and type of emergent vegetation. Reciprocal transplants (described below) were conducted within each of these five pool pairs (see  and  for overview and schematic). Eight of the 10 pools selected during the aforementioned spotted salamander study () were also selected here; the remaining two pools did not have breeding populations of the wood frog and thus two different pools were selected for the present study. Inference from these overlapping populations provides an especially strong basis for comparison of responses between two amphibians occupying identical habitats. When referring to the full complement of roadside or woodland populations
column7: I use the term ‘deme’. Thus
column8: ‘roadside deme’ refers collectively to the five roadside populations while ‘woodland deme’ refers collectively to the five woodland populations.

column1: In spring 2008
column2: I collected from each of 10 pools (five roadside
column3: five woodland) a subset of embryos from 10 egg masses less than 36 h old. To ensure collection within this timeframe
column4: pools were monitored daily as the breeding season approached. Roadside pools were located <10 m from a paved road while woodland pools were located >200 m from the nearest paved road and at least 100 m from the nearest dirt road (). Dirt roads in this region typically see low traffic use and do not receive winter deicing salts. While roadside egg masses appeared somewhat more compact than woodland egg masses ()
column5: there were no conspicuous differences in the embryos themselves between sites
column6: and selection of embryos from within each egg mass was haphazard. Thus
column7: any potential carryover effect of the <36 h exposure did not bias the selection of the subset of embryos employed in the experiment. The consequences of such potential effects are elaborated upon in the Discussion. Each egg mass was collected whole in a plastic 710 ml container. From each egg mass
column8: I separated out two clusters of embryos. Each cluster was photographed and stocked into one of five experimental enclosures in the origin pool and the transplant pool (). (Details of the experimental enclosures are described elsewhere () and an image can be found in .) For a subset of egg masses
column9: this procedure occurred in the field. However
column10: heavy rains impeded photography; remaining egg masses were instead transported to the lab on ice for processing
column11: stored overnight in an 8°C incubator
column12: and returned for stocking the following day. Each pool contained five experimental blocks. I targeted stocking approximately 50 embryos from each of two egg masses per enclosure
column13: yielding one unique pairing of egg masses (hereafter “clutch pair”) that was replicated across
column14: but not within pool pairs. This design was chosen to maximize family level diversity while maintaining an additional level of replication at the clutch pair level
column15: while at the same time balancing logistics of resources and spatial constraints within pools. Enclosure assignment was haphazard for each clutch pair. In total across 10 pools
column16: I stocked 100 enclosures containing approximately 100 embryos each. Breeding began first at the two most southerly pools on 2 April 2008. 8 April 2008 was the last day that breeding began in a new pool. Given the proximity between each pair of pools (i.e.
column17: the complement of a woodland-roadside pairing)
column18: breeding began within a one-day period across all pairs. Stocking of enclosures occurred in a pairwise manner between each roadside and woodland pond. In this way
column19: there were no systematic differences in the timing of collection or stocking. I used a dissecting stereoscopic microscope to estimate the developmental stage of each egg mass upon collection from the field. For each embryo cluster (n = 200) I estimated egg size from photographs. Specifically
column20: I used ImageJ () to measure the two-dimensional surface area of 15 embryos per cluster represented by a best-fit ellipse (). At the conclusion of the experiment
column21: when all eggs had either hatched and reached feeding stage or died
column22: I estimated survival
column23: developmental stage ()
column24: and snout-vent length (SVL) at the container level. One enclosure was removed from analysis due to predator intrusion.

column2: nine were haphazardly assigned into groups of three (hereafter “clutch triad”). From each egg mass
column3: three separate clusters—each of approximately 30 embryos—were separated out and haphazardly assigned by clutch triad to each of three road salt treatments. Each cluster (n = 270) was photographed and embryo size was estimated as described above. Each experimental unit comprised one clutch triad

Road salt comprising at least 95% NaCl obtained from the Connecticut Department of Transportation was added to the medium and high treatments to achieve specific conductance values of 1

Embryos were reared in 11.3 l plastic containers (42 × 28 × 18 cm) filled with approximately 8 l of assigned treatment water. Each container was fitted with mesh hardware cloth to suspend embryos in the water column off the bottom of the container. Containers were housed on tables in an outdoor facility under 50% shade cloth. Concentrations of roadsalt were maintained in response to evaporation and rain by adding lower or higher concentration treatment water respectively

column1: Following assignment of embryo clusters to reciprocal transplant and chronic exposure experiments described above
column2: egg masses were retained within collection containers and placed haphazardly within 150 L plastic wading pools that served as incubators
column3: located in an outdoor enclosure covered with 50% shade cloth. When embryos reached developmental stage 18
column4: I separated out approximately 45 embryos from each egg mass and placed them into a common garden environment consisting of a 150 L wading pool filled with aged
column5: conditioned tap water. Embryos were grouped by origin such that each wading pool contained embryos from each of the 10 egg masses representing a single natural pool. Upon approaching feeding stage (Gosner developmental stage 25 ())
column6: larvae were fed a mixture of 3:1 ground rabbit chow and fish flakes at a daily ration of 10% average body mass. When all individuals appeared to reach feeding stage

A 5.1 L container (33 × 20 × 11 cm) filled with 4 L of treatment water comprised each experimental unit. Larvae in each experimental unit were given a four-day food ration at the start of the experiment. After 96 h of exposure

column4: three woodland) and haphazardly assigned male–female combinations grouped by origin pool. Pairs were placed in 11.3 L plastic containers filled with 2.5 L of aged spring water

column1: In each pool

column1: All statistical analyses were conducted in R V. 2.15.0 (). I employed AIC based selection procedures to compose a suite of mixed effects models to evaluate performance variables across the G × E interaction both for transplant and exposure experiments. I analyzed survival and malformation each as bivariate responses of successes and failures. Growth and developmental rates were derived as exponential functions of change in size and stage
column2: respectively
column3: in relation to the number of days elapsed (e.g.
column4: [ln(final size)− ln(initial size)]/period). Initial size was defined as embryo diameter derived from estimates of embryo area
column5: while final size comprised hatchling snout-vent length (SVL). These derived rates were characterized by Gaussian distributions in the statistical models. All models of performance variables were composed with and without embryo size as a covariate in order to estimate the potential influence of egg size mediated maternal effects (). Each response variable was evaluated across a suite of models that differed in random effects structure (see –). For each response variable
column6: I evaluated for inference the most parsimonious model with respect to both the significance of interacting main effects and the random effect structure as indicated by lowest AIC score (see –). I also used mixed effects models to evaluate the influence of pool type on embryo size and fecundity. I used a combination of MCMC randomization methods and log-likelihood approaches to conduct inference (). I used MANOVA to evaluate the suite of abiotic variables characterizing the roadside and woodland environment. I used a standard linear model to evaluate the influence of pool type on population size
column7: which was scaled to pool area and log-transformed to meet model assumptions. The full details regarding these analyses are described in the . Data for this study are deposited in the Dryad Repository: http://doi.org/10.5061/dryad.fb8tk.

Despite strong negative effects associated with the roadside environment

column8: the presence of a maladaptive response was not limited to these roadside contexts—even when reared in woodland pools
column9: survival of the roadside deme remained lower than that of the woodland deme (). This suggests that the maladaptive performance induced by the roadside environment further spurs a generalized depression effect on the roadside deme
column10: which manifests independently of the immediate negative influences of both the roadside environment and road salt exposure.

column1: Because adult wood frogs exhibit high site fidelity () and have shown phenotypic differentiation across microgeographic environmental gradients ()
column2: I assumed that the embryos I collected represent a lineage of wood frogs specific to the collection pool (i.e.
column3: ‘population’). While I later discuss the implications of relaxing this assumption
column4: for now I assume that the offspring entered into this experiment represent an independent population potentially connected by some level of dispersal and gene flow. With this in mind
column5: the pattern of maladaptive performance seen here suggests that negative effects associated with exposure to the roadside environment are transmitted from parent to offspring prior to birth. In this way
column6: embryos may be predisposed to reduced performance prior to any direct encounter with the roadside environment. Alternatively
column7: maladaptive performance may have been caused by early embryonic exposure to roadside pools. Specifically
column8: the first 36 h following natural oviposition but preceding collection—during which time embryos were exposed to their natal environment—may have had strong negative effects on embryos. Though such early exposure alone could explain the putative maladaptive response
column9: preliminary evidence suggests that early exposure has no such carryover effect in this system (SP Brady
column10: unpublished data).

column1: In addition to any potential effect caused by early exposure
column2: the overall pattern of parentally mediated maladaptive performance described here is consistent with several potential mechanisms
column3: including maladaptation
column4: non-genetic inherited effects (e.g.
column5: maternal effects)
column6: and demographic processes such as phenotype-biased habitat-oriented dispersal. Maladaptation can be defined most generally as a deviation from an adaptive peak caused by genetic processes (). As an empirical phenomenon
column7: maladaptation is less common than local adaptation (; ). Yet even in reciprocal transplant studies where local adaptation was hypothesized and evaluated
column8: maladaptation frequently occurs. In a review of this literature
column9: found the frequency of maladaptation to be 0.29
column10: as compared to the frequency of local adaptation
column11: which was 0.71. Where it has been described
column12: maladaptation at the population level is typically the consequence of migration load ()
column13: resulting when asymmetrical gene flow from non-adapted source populations yields genotypes of sub-optimal fitness in the recipient population (). In the present study however
column14: given the relative survival advantage of the woodland deme of wood frogs
column15: regardless of environment
column16: it seems unlikely that asymmetrical gene flow (i.e.
column17: from the woodland deme to the roadside deme) is limiting the local adaptation capacity of the roadside deme. If this were the case
column18: we would expect similar phenotypes between populations grown out within the roadside environment. Likewise
column19: phenotypic homogeneity would be expected if we view maladaptation through the lens of the adaptive phenotypic landscapes framework ()
column20: which predicts that maladaptation is a ubiquitous outcome of dynamic environments because the response to selection always lags one generation behind selection itself; therefore
column21: the adaptive high point is constantly shifting before phenotypes can reach their adaptive peak (). However
column22: this process alone does not explain the maladaptation seen here. Specifically
column23: if we assume that the roadside deme did not differ from the woodland deme prior to the recent influence of roads
column24: then we would again expect comparable performance declines between populations when exposed to the roadside environment.

column1: In addition to the potential role for genetic based maladaptation
column2: non-genetic processes may be responsible for the observed maladaptive performance of the roadside deme. For example
column3: maternal effects
column4: broadly defined as “a direct effect of a parent’s phenotype on the phenotype of its offspring” () may be associated with the roadside deme’s reduced ability to survive. In this system
column5: if we assume that parents were born in a roadside pool
column6: maternal effects could originate from their exposure to roadside water during their period of aquatic development
column7: which spans newly laid embryos through metamorphosis. Similarly
column8: aspects of the terrestrial roadside environment might propagate negative maternal effects. Yet
column9: I found no evidence that egg size—a maternally mediated trait known to influence amphibian performance (reviewed by )—varied across deme or had any effect on performance variables. Still
column10: maternal effects in this system may be mediated by mechanisms independent of size. For example
column11: nutrient availability and/or allocation of resources to egg quality may differ between these two environments. Alternatively
column12: roadside embryos might accrue chemical contaminants as in utero eggs through maternal transfer. This possibility is supported by outcomes from studies in which coal combustion waste contaminants such as strontium and selenium are maternally transferred
column13: negatively affecting offspring survival and development in two species of toads (; ).

column1: Demographic processes might also lead to maladaptive performance of the roadside deme. For example
column2: if we assume that roadside adults face higher mortality risk—either through carryover effects from the aquatic environment
column3: or through terrestrial road pressures such as roadkill—then the average age of roadside breeders should be lower
column4: a condition associated with poorer offspring provisioning in wood frogs (). On the other hand
column5: demographic processes might also explain how the roadside deme can persist in spite of the negative effects of the roadside environment and given a predisposition for lower survival. Specifically
column6: across demes
column7: I detected no difference in population size of breeding females. As one possible explanation of this demographic pattern
column8: I found that on average
column9: and adjusted for mean body size
column10: females from the roadside deme lay 10.5% more eggs than females from the woodland deme. Thus while average survival is 15% lower for roadside embryos
column11: the differential in numerical reproductive output per female is less substantial. For example
column12: based on the overall average female size
column13: roadside wood frogs lay 890 eggs per clutch while woodland wood frogs lay 795 eggs per clutch. If we apply the deme respective survival rates (73% versus 86% respectively)
column14: we can estimate that for the average sized female
column15: 684 embryos survive to feeding stage in the woodland deme as compared to 650 embryos in the roadside deme. This amounts to just a 5% decrease in reproductive success per female of the roadside deme. Thus
column16: differential reproductive efforts may potentially offset average reductions in offspring survival
column17: mitigating the detriment to demographic success. This increase in reproductive effort may serve to buffer the population against stochastic effects in the face of intense of selection. Ultimately
column18: given the interacting effects of female size and deme ()
column19: the adaptive significance of this increased reproductive effort will depend upon the distribution of female size. Similarly
column20: the potential for a tradeoff between quality and quantity of eggs will affect the utility of this apparent strategy.

column1: A second demographic possibility is that roadside pools may serve as sink habitats
column2: wherein roadside populations are augmented through immigration from woodland populations
column3: bolstering the chance of persistence. However
column4: here again
column5: if this were true
column6: we might expect that the migration of woodland individuals into roadside pools would yield homogenized performance responses between the roadside and woodland deme
column7: which is inconsistent with the observed pattern. Still
column8: source–sink dynamics could occur if we assume that putative migrants arriving to breed in roadside pools are themselves depressed in some way. For example
column9: younger or otherwise misfit adults may breed more frequently in the suboptimal conditions found in roadside as compared to woodland pools
column10: potentially through competitive exclusions or the process of ‘matching habitat choice’ (). As an alternative explanation of persistence
column11: it is possible that the negative effects on the aquatic stages of roadside wood frogs might be reversed at later life history stages. For example
column12: the surviving individuals from the roadside deme may produce inferior embryos and larvae
column13: but superior adults.

column1: It is surprising to consider that the maladaptive response of the roadside deme was unabated in woodland pools
column2: where survival is typically high. While this generalized depression effect (hereafter ‘deme depression’) is not well described in the literature (but see )
column3: there are conceptual analogues worth considering. As one such example
column4: inbreeding depression can arise in the context of reduced effective population size () that could result from intense selection () in the roadside environment. However
column5: inbreeding depression is predicted to be minimal in the context of intense maladaptation (). Still
column6: roads can act as barriers to migration
column7: which can potentially spur inbreeding depression. However
column8: the types of roads in this system do not appear to limit gene flow between wood frog populations in surrounding landscapes separated by similar or larger distances of space (). A similar argument for gene flow could therefore be made against genetic drift as a mechanism driving these patterns. As an alternative possibility
column9: genetic mutations or epigenetic modifications could lead to maladaptive responses and overall deme depression. This may be especially likely in harsh roadside environments
column10: where mutagens such as heavy metals are known to accumulate from road runoff ().

column1: Regardless of mechanism
column2: the maladaptive performance of the roadside deme of wood frogs occurs in direct contrast to the local adaptation of the roadside deme of spotted salamanders breeding and dwelling in the very same pools (). That deme divergence has been detected for two different species occupying identical habitats suggests that population differentiation may be a common consequence of landscape modification. That these outcomes occurred in opposing directions highlights the complex nature of population level responses across species—even when they occupy identical habitats—suggesting that more generalized approaches may be inadequate
column3: and reinforcing the imperative for population specific investigations. Though microgeographic population outcomes have received little attention
column4: there is reason to believe that maladaptive responses may be an emerging and prominent consequence of human environmental modification. For example
column5: phenotypes of species harvested for human consumption have undergone rapid and substantial change () in directions believed to oppose adaptation to the increased environmental variation predicted to occur with climate change (). Similarly
column6: hatchery-reared fish have become locally adapted to the hatchery environment at the cost of maladaptation to the wild (). It is worth noting how the conservation implications of these cited examples of maladaptation differ from that of the maladaptive responses reported here. Wherein harvest regimes and hatcheries induce maladaptation through selection as a consequence of a specific management strategy
column7: roads induce maladaptive performance as a collateral effect of global change and for a species that is a bystander to human actions
column8: not a direct target. Thus
column9: even indirect human activities in relatively undeveloped settings can induce maladaptive responses in wild populations.

The proportion of fossils provides a measure of influence for each site when assessing the pie chart of total diversity from microvertebrate localities

column12: and Shannon–Wiener biodiversity index calculated for the entire dataset.

column1: Due to variations in collection and cataloging methods between the NCMNS and the OMNH
column2: in addition to the raw Shannon–Wiener indices calculated for each locality
column3: we also created several datasets in which we subsampled the COI locality to a sample size equal to each of the OMNH fossil localities. Subsampling and subsequent calculation of biodiversity indices was carried out in R version 3.5.0 () using the vegan package v. 2.5-2 (). Within the sample function of R
column4: probability weights for the selection of each taxon was accomplished by simply taking the proportion of a given clade within the known COI database. This means that if a clade was not present in the COI site then it received a zero probability of being selected in the subsampled dataset. The resulting COI subsampled dataset was then used to calculate the Shannon–Wiener index. Afterward
column5: we subtracted the OMNH locality index value from the subsampled COI index in order to assess the difference in biodiversity calculation between the two sites. Difference in Shannon–Wiener values is the quantity of interest in our case
column6: not the calculated value of each subsampled COI index. Instead we focus on the comparison between the COI and a respective OMNH site (whose Shannon–Wiener index does not change)
column7: which requires the difference in values. This process of subsampling
column8: recalculating the Shannon–Wiener index
column9: and determining the difference was repeated 100 times for each OMNH locality. Additionally
column10: since the sample sizes between the COI site and the OMNH sites were equal in the subsampled datasets
column11: we calculated the Morisita–Horn similarity index in order to assess how similar the subsampled COI fossils were to the original OMNH preserved faunas. Again
column12: this calculation was performed 100 times per OMNH locality
column13: except using the R package fossil v. 0.3.7 (). Complete results for these analyses and the R code used to derive them are available in the .

column1: Rarefaction curves were produced to assess the likelihood of recovering additional higher-level taxa from each locality. As mentioned
column2: the database used for this calculation is based on family-level taxonomy and above. As such
column3: the rarefaction is calculating the likelihood of finding new representatives at those taxonomic levels
column4: not at the species level. Both the Shannon–Wiener diversity indices and the rarefaction curves were produced using PAST (version 3.19; ).

column1: Finally
column2: to assess the impact of geology and stratigraphic position on the preserved fauna
column3: we used multivariate techniques to draw comparisons with the other vertebrate microfossil sites in the Mussentuchit Member included in . We produced a correspondence analysis (CA) in PAST version 3.19 ()
column4: using the same faunal data from the paleoecology analyses (). CA is a multivariate ordinal technique used to explore relationships among a matrix of categorical or continuous variables ()
column5: and has been used by other studies to understand diversity changes across gradients ()
column6: because of the method’s robust nature to missing data (). A total of 11 of the 12 OMNH microsites were associated with one of four types of depositional environment
column7: categorized by  as either fluvial oxbow
column8: floodplain
column9: crevasse splay
column10: or channel deposits. In our analysis
column11: each site was categorized according to Goldberg’s assessment whereas COI was categorized as an ox-bow lake
column12: in accordance with our independently derived geologic interpretation. These category labels were then overlain on the plotted data to qualitatively assess the role of depositional environment on preserved faunal remains.

column1: First

column3: followed by subsequent analyses of biodiversity
column4: paleoecology
column5: and paleoenvironmental influences
column6: based on comparisons of the COI assemblage with other microvertebrate assemblages from the Mussentuchit Member.

column2: 1758 TESTUDINATA Klein
column3: 1760 HELOCHELYDRIDAE (SOLEMYDIDAE) Lapparent de Broin and Murelaga
column4: 1996 The majority of turtle materials from the COI are referable to a new species of helochelydrid
column5: previously referred to Naomichelys speciosa (). Helochelydrid shell fragments
column6: characterized by a dense arrangement of raised
column7: cylindrical tubercles
column8: are common throughout the Mussentuchit Member (). Tubercles are typically 0.5–1 mm tall with planar apices and constricted bases ( and ). Microstructurally
column9: COI helochelydrid shell samples are similar to Solemys vermiculata and Solemys sp. previously reported from Spain
column10: and Naomichelys sp. previously reported from Canada and USA (). All samples possess a dipole structure with cortices of relatively equal thickness (). The external cortex is characterized by an outer zone of ornamental tubercles composed of parallel-fibered bone (). The lower zone of the external cortex is composed of coarse
column11: interwoven structural bone fibers (). The cancellous bone is comprised of long slender trabeculae with large intertrabecular regions (). Secondary lamellar bone is often present along the walls of the trabeculae. The internal cortex is made of parallel fibered bone and occasionally grades into lamellar zonal bone ().

column1: ADOCIDAE Cope
column2: 1870 The COI contains shell fragments likely representing a new species of adocid (–).  describes adocid shell sculpturing as having a diagnostic arrangement of small and regular grooves and pits or dots. COI shell fragments ascribed to Adocidae are similar to those described by  and  and are characterized by a smooth surface with small pits and rhomboidal sulci. A few shell fragments are sculptured
column3: bearing small grooves and pits (–). The identification of these specimens as adocid is supported by similarities of internal microstructure (). All samples possess thick
column4: densely packed external and internal cortical bone with well-developed interior cancellous bone (). The external cortex is divided into upper and lower zones. The lower zone (bordering the internal cancellous bone) is made of interwoven structural fibers and is characterized by a reticular vascular pattern. The upper zone (bordering the external surface) is characterized by highly birefringent growth marks exhibiting a wavy pattern
column5: and diagonally oriented Sharpey’s fibers (). The cancellous bone is less spongy than found in Helochelydra
column6: and is made of coarse
column7: dense
column8: short trabeculae with round to oval intertrabecular regions (). Secondary lamellar bone is often present along the walls of the trabeculae. The internal cortex is made of parallel-fibered bone and occasionally grades into lamellar zonal bone ().

column1: MESOEUCROCODYLIA indet. Teeth we identify as similar in morphology to bernissartids are apico-basally short
column2: mesio-distally elongate
column3: labiolingually compressed
column4: and slightly reniform in occlusal view (–). The crowns often possess an elliptical apical wear facet. The mesial and distal margins have poorly defined carinae and the labial and lingual faces are ornamented with widely spaced
column5: well-defined
column6: apicobasally oriented striations that converge toward the apex. Teeth matching this description have been recovered from the Lower Cretaceous Cloverly Formation of Wyoming and Montana and the Lower Cretaceous Rabekke
column7: Jydegård

column1: Teeth resembling atoposaurids from the COI are proportionately taller
column2: labiolingually compressed
column3: and reniform in occlusal view (–). They are constricted toward the crown base and have well-developed
column4: sometimes crenulated
column5: mesial
column6: and distal carinae. They are typically triangular in mesio-distal view
column7: with a strongly convex labial face and a weakly convex lingual face. Tooth crowns range from strongly rounded to moderately acuminate and have varying degrees of recurvature. Striations run apicobasally
column8: are sometimes anastomosing
column9: and are typically more pronounced on the lingual face. Recent descriptions of similar teeth have come from the Lower Cretaceous Cloverly Formation of Wyoming and Montana and other sites in the Mussentuchit Member of the Cedar Mountain Formation (; ; ).

column1: Teeth morphologically similar to those of pholidosaurids are considerably taller
column2: strongly conical
column3: and have nearly circular bases (–). Smaller teeth are tall and slender
column4: with slight lingual recurvature and sharp apices (–). Larger teeth tend to be more stout with rounded apices (–). Mesial and distal carinae extend from the base to the apex and are weakly defined. Striations are strongly defined and run apicobasally
column5: sometimes anastomosing towards the apex. Larger
column6: stouter teeth have numerous
column7: thin
column8: well defined striations that run apicobasally (–). These larger teeth have been previously reported from the Cedar Mountain Formation as a fifth group of teleosaurid teeth similar to Machimosaurus ()
column9: which display a nearly complete sinusoidal curve outlined by the primary carinae in apical view (R. Nydam
column11: personal communication).

column1: NCSM 33362 and NCSM 33384 are mesoeucrocodylian osteoderms ( and ). Due to breakage there is no evidence of an anterior bar or posterior suture to aid identification and so we assign these osteoderms to Mesoeucrocodylia indet. NCSM 33384 possesses a random array of approximately equal-sized round pits
column2: generally similar to the morphology on pholidosaurids and goniopholids. One feature on NCSM 33362 is a low median keel that barely rises above the level of the dermal body ().

column1: cf. DAKOTASUCHUS sp. Mehl (1941) Teeth we identify as similar to Dakotasuchus are conical
column2: sometimes slightly lingually recurved
column3: and typically sub-circular in occlusal view (–) (). They are somewhat labiolingually compressed
column4: with the labial side slightly more convex than the lingual. Tooth bases are circular to somewhat elliptical and the crown apices are strongly pointed. Both the mesial and distal carinae extend from the base to the apex and are less pronounced than those of similar to atoposaurid-like teeth. Enamel ornamentation is characterized by multiple
column5: weakly defined
column6: apicobasally oriented
column7: parallel striations. Teeth of Dakotasuchus are morphologically similar to goniopholid and pholidosaurid teeth (; ; ).

column2: 1906 NCSM 33276 (–) is a premaxillary tooth and the only specimen that can be confidently referred to as a tyrannosauroid based on a suite of diagnostic characteristics (; ; ; ; ). These include a D-shaped cross section
column3: a lingually oriented carina that arches around the lingual side of the tooth

column5: a lack of denticles and serrations
column6: and a prominent ridge on the lingual surface. The tooth has a crown height of 6 mm and part of the lingual surface
column7: near the apex is worn away.

column2: 1986 DROMAEOSAURIDAE Matthew and Brown
column3: 1922 Two teeth are assigned to Dromaeosauridae from the COI. Diagnostic characteristics of dromaeosaurid teeth are outlined in  and . Maxillary and dentary teeth are laterally compressed and recurved in lateral view
column4: lack basal constriction
column5: and possess a flattened
column6: oval cross section. Distal denticles are thin
column7: sometimes point apically
column8: and larger than mesial serrations
column9: and the distal carina is often positioned toward the lingual margin. In both datasets
column10: the dromaeosaurid convex hulls have wide regions that overlap with Tyrannosauridae and Richardoestesia. Thus
column11: several teeth may in fact belong to other groups and should be considered tentatively assigned.

column1: NCSM 33267 (–) has a crown height of 10.15 mm
column2: a FABL of 5.03 mm
column3: and a basal width of 3.32 mm. The tooth lacks recurvature in mesial view and has an incrassate figure-eight-shaped cross section (sensu ). Wear facets extend along the apical portion of the distal carina and across the smooth enamel of the labial surface. The mesial carina lacks serrations and twists lingually near the base. The distal denticles are subrectangular and the distal carina is positioned towards the lingual margin. When considered as part of the  database

column1: NCSM 33275 (–) has a crown height of 5.83 mm
column2: a FABL of 3.23 mm
column3: and a basal width of 1.36 mm. The tooth is strongly compressed laterally

column5: and has a weakly figure-eight-shaped cross section. The enamel texture is smooth and a depression is present on the lingual face. The mesial carina is centrally positioned and the distal carina is positioned centrally at the apex

column1: Rarefaction of the sampled faunas across all localities () shows that only four of the 13 localities (OMNH 239
column4: 794) have plateaued. In other words
column5: these sites have been sampled enough that it is unlikely a new vertebrate clade will be discovered with increased sampling. The COI site does not appear close to leveling off; therefore
column6: a high probability exists to discover new clades not currently documented in the site fauna from further sampling. After estimating the rarefaction over 4
column7: 000 specimens
column8: COI appears equal to OMNH V695 in clade representation. The direction of the rarefaction line for the COI locality is quite different from the OMNH localities
column9: rising at a gradual pace to the end of the graph. This behavior is due to the high number of mesoeucrocodylian teeth and osteichthyan fossils in our sample along with the rarer individual numbers of additional clades. In short
column10: the rarefaction of the COI database continuously provides excessive individuals of mesoeucrocodylians and fish along with occasional other clades
column11: which when amalgamated across the entire resampling protocol produces the slowly increasing line. Compared to other OMNH localities there are key clades that are missing from the COI site
column12: and recalculation of rarefaction curves at lower taxonomic levels might have different outcomes than presented here
column13: yet more refined taxonomic classifications are not yet possible. Given the higher potential for COI to yield additional clades through further sampling
column14: the COI locality could be one of the most taxonomically rich microsites yet described from the Mussentuchit Member.

column1: Rarefaction curves for the 12 OMNH microfossil localities listed in the  dataset and the Cliffs of Insanity locality. Colors of site names correspond to the rarefaction line of the same color. The pie chart represents the combined percentage of all 13 site abundances used in the present study.
In contrast
column2: the biodiversity as calculated via the Shannon–Wiener index produces a low value
column3: (0.41; ) compared to other OMNH sites. The Shannon–Wiener index takes into consideration both the number of clades present and the number of individuals per clade in order to calculate biodiversity among sites. Sampling localities that have a larger number of clades and a relatively equal number of individuals per clade will receive a higher biodiversity score than sites that have either low number of clades or those sites that possess a large number of clades
column4: yet a very few of those clades possess nearly all of the individual abundance. Therefore
column5: the high number of crocodylomorph fossils from COI compared to all other clades in that site creates an uneven assemblage that effectively lowers this biodiversity measure. Locality OMNH V868 is the most diverse site by this measure
column6: with a biodiversity index of 1.61.  shows the results of the Shannon–Wiener COI subsampling. There is an obvious spread of biodiversity values for the COI locality under all subsampling simulations. However
column7: the most distinguishing pattern is that in all cases the biodiversity of this site remains lower than every OMNH locality.

column1: Shannon–Wiener biodiversity index differences between the COI locality and each OMNH locality are shown as orange circles. The black circles show the original Shannon–Wiener value for the COI deposit after subtraction of each OMNH locality. This circle is also the average for the subsampled differences. Morisita–Horn similarity values are shown as blue circles. Each column in the figure contains 100 circles
column2: above which are the averages for each column.

column1: Interestingly
column2: there is no correlation between the number of fossils collected and the Shannon–Wiener biodiversity index. The slope of the correlation was essentially zero (−5.77 × 10−5). The four sites that had plateaued rarefaction curves (OMNH V239
column5: and V794) ranged in biodiversity from 1.05 to 1.40
column6: which were all below the peak biodiversity of 1.61 (OMNH V868). This indicates that neither extensive sampling nor abundance of fossils can adequately predict the biodiversity of an individual site.

column1: Similarity
column2: as calculated by the Morisita–Horn index
column3: of the subsampled sites has a much smaller distribution than the Shannon–Wiener values in every comparison
column4: meaning that despite wide differences in biodiversity among subsamples
column5: the similarity of faunas is not sensitive to subsampling techniques. In this index a value of 1 means that all of the clades are shared between localities and the proportions of those clades are also essentially equivalent. Sites OMNH V213 and OMNH V868 have a near identical array of clades compared to COI. The site that is least similar to COI is OMNH V696.

column1: Prior studies of microvertebrate localities in the Mussentuchit Member of the Cedar Mountain Formation reportedly processed 49
column2: 295 kg of matrix in total
column3: with individual sites contributing between 775 and 14
column4: 580 kg of matrix. These studies have resulted in a total of 13
column5: 036 identifiable specimens (). By comparison
column6: the COI microvertebrate locality preserves a uniquely high density of fossils
column7: with over 6
column8: 342 specimens recovered from only 183 kg of sediment.

column1: Baenid
column2: helochelydrid (solemydid)
column3: glyptopsid
column4: and trionychid turtles have been reported previously (; ; ) from the Mussentuchit Member. Our histological analysis as well as qualitative assessment of turtle shell fragments from the COI add the presence of Adocidae
column5: representing the first occurrence of this taxon from the Cedar Mountain Formation and the earliest occurrence in North America.

column1: The majority of Mussentuchit Member sites sampled
column2: including the COI locality
column3: are dominated by mesoeucrocodylian teeth (; )
column4: with a large percentage of sites alternatively dominated by fish materials (osteichthyans and chondrichthyans). The remarkably high abundance of mesoeucrocodylians (90% of recovered fossils) warrants further investigation as a potential paleoenvironmental signal within the Mussentuchit Member itself. We hypothesize that the general predominance of mesoeucrocodylians in the upper Cedar Mountain Formation likely represents an authentic paleoecological signal tied to transgression of the WIS during this interval. If specimen NCSM 33308 is indeed an enchodontid tooth
column5: then this would support our hypothesis because members of Enchodontidae are predominantly marine (). However
column6: shed teeth can often over-represent a population density
column7: and the fluvial nature of many of the sampled localities
column8: (including the COI) can lead to the overrepresentation of aquatic faunal elements compared to terrestrial vertebrate elements.

column1: A diverse assemblage of dinosaurian species are known from the Mussentuchit Member based on previous microfaunal studies (; ; ; ; ). We find support for some of these identifications in the COI sample; however
column2: we take a more conservative approach to isolated tooth identifications based on our knowledge of the unique taxonomic diversity of Mussentuchit Member macrofaunal remains (i.e.
column3: generally an undescribed macrofauna consisting of new dinosaurian species; ; ) and previous studies cautioning on the assignment of isolated dinosaur teeth to refined taxonomic levels (). Importantly
column4: we provide some of the first detailed descriptions and photographic documentation of dinosaurian teeth from the Mussentuchit Member.

column1: The previous oldest occurrence of adocid turtles in North America derives from the Smoky Hollow Member of the Straight Cliffs Formation ()
column2: dated to the Turonian (∼92 Ma; ). Therefore
column3: the presence of adocids in the Cenomanian aged (∼97 Ma) Mussentuchit Member (; ) extends the presence of the clade in North America by approximately 5 million years and provides further evidence for Laurasian interchange between Asia and North America at or prior to the dawn of the Late Cretaceous.

column3: suggesting a Beringian dispersal route in concordance with the majority of other vertebrate records. It has been suggested that the high-latitude connection between eastern Asia and western North America acted as a climatic barrier
column4: resulting in a dispersal filter that restricted smaller members of faunal assemblages such as turtles (). The appearance of adocids in North America by at least the Cenomanian

The recovery of adocid turtle remains marks a new addition to the Mussentuchit Member assemblage and adds to a growing body of evidence refining the pattern and tempo of faunal interchange between Asia and North America prior to and post the Early/Late Cretaceous boundary. It also represents the earliest occurrence of Adocidae in North America

The inconsistencies between where a tooth may plot is likely due to the inability of existing databases to account for subtle shape variation and ambiguous taxonomic referrals due to their reliance solely on a limited series of linear measurements. Future attempts to refine this methodology will incorporate a larger sample set of theropod teeth from the Mussentuchit Member utilizing landmark data and three-dimensional shape analyses.

column2: L. helicina were formerly widely distributed in the Arctic and the Pacific
