column1: 64|10.7717/peerj.5644|Conceptual and empirical advances in Neotropical biodiversity research| Antonelli  Alexandre |2018-10-4|"The Neotropical region (also referred to as tropical America or the American tropics) extends today from central Mexico to Argentina
column2: including the Caribbean (). The region encompasses a vast range of biomes and habitat types
column3: each with a particular history of landscapes and biotic evolution (; ). For many groups of organisms
column4: the Neotropics are home to outstandingly high levels of species richness
column5: when compared to other major biotic realms (; ). As such
column6: understanding Neotropical biodiversity patterns and the processes associated with its origin and maintenance represents a major scientific priority.

column1: Map of the Neotropical region
column2: spanning from Central Mexico to central Argentina (red dashed line) and including all Caribbean Islands. The figure shows examples of the large diversity of Neotropical habitats and the taxa that inhabit those habitats. We also outline a few of the many topics in Neotropical biodiversity that can be studied in the “trans-disciplinary biogeographic approach” advocated here. (A) Eastern slopes of the Bolivian Andes
column3: where the Amazonian and Andean biotas meet; (B) Patagonian mountains of southern Chile
column4: which despite being in the temperate zone of South America is home to many Neotropical-derived lineages; (C) Iguazu waterfalls
column5: where increased humidity create gallery forests within the South American open diagonal; (D) Southern grasslands of the Pampas
column6: a naturally open habitat now largely influenced by human activity; (E) One of the ca. 338 known species of hummingbirds
column7: a conspicuous clade currently restricted to the American continent and particularly diverse in the Andes; (F) Epidendrum ibaguense
column8: a widespread species in the orchid family in which many new Neotropical species are discovered each year; (G) An unidentified fly in the inselbergs of southern French Guiana
column9: where basaltic rocks emerge several hundred meters above the surrounding Amazonian rainforest; (H) The large dogtooth characin fish Hydrolycus scomberoides
column10: exemplifying the world’s richest ichthyofauna in the Amazon drainage basin; (I) Ameerega flavopicta
column11: a rock-dwelling frog species adapted to a region of high seasonality of precipitation; (J) A columnar cactus of central Mexico
column12: near the northwestern limits of the Neotropical region where low-canopy forests and succulent vegetation build vegetation mosaics across the landscape. Map generated through the remote-sensing ESA GlobCover 2009 project and colored by biome assignments (©ESA 2010 and UCLouvain; http://due.esrin.esa.int/page_globcover.php). Photo credits: A–G
column13: I and J: A.A.; H: J.A.

column1: Biodiversity refers to the diversity of life across all levels of biological organization (; ). Biodiversity is unevenly distributed across Earth
column2: and varies among and within geographic regions
column3: between terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems
column4: and among different groups of organisms. Biodiversity increases from the poles to the equator
column5: reaching the highest values in tropical regions
column6: a pattern termed the latitudinal diversity gradient (; ). However
column7: this pattern is complex
column8: with numerous non-diverse tropical or diverse non-tropical areas and taxa constituting exceptions. In addition
column9: there are still numerous uncertainties in the underlying data used to generalize overall patterns. Most importantly
column10: these patterns remain far from properly understood
column11: and we are still struggling to identify their main determinants. As a result
column12: researchers tend to focus on different aspects of biodiversity such as taxonomic
column13: phylogenetic
column14: and functional diversity (FD) (; –). Each of these aspects of biodiversity may vary among regions and taxa
column15: and each must therefore be assessed by independent criteria (; ).

column1: In April 2017
column2: we gathered scholars from several countries and scientific backgrounds to discuss Neotropical biodiversity during the “Origins of Biodiversity Workshop” organized by Chalmers University of Technology and the University of Gothenburg (Sweden)
column3: under the auspices of the Gothenburg Centre for Advanced Studies. We spent one week outlining the topics presented here
column4: focusing primarily on recent advances and the future of the field. We continued to work remotely toward the conclusion of this publication. The overall goal of this review is to summarize the knowns and unknowns about Neotropical biodiversity
column5: with focus on terrestrial taxa and ecosystems
column6: and to discuss the many opportunities and challenges of this research field. We acknowledge that the wide breadth of the topics discussed here are in part due to this being a summary of ideas produced at a workshop of diverse participants
column7: and that each topic contains a depth that cannot be simply synthesized. For instance
column8: more comprehensive reviews on the theories proposed to explain the origin of Neotropical diversity can be found elsewhere (; ; ; ). We focus here on topics that we think require further development within this research field.

column1: Relatively good estimates of taxonomic diversity are only available for well-studied Neotropical taxa
column2: as in other parts of the world. These estimates have been used to identify the best predictors of diversity at large scales (; ). Although sampling across taxa is comparable or even greater in the Neotropics than in other tropical regions (; )
column3: taxonomic diversity is generally underestimated within the Neotropics
column4: especially for poorly sampled organisms such as fungi
column5: invertebrates
column6: and micro-organisms.

column1: One difficulty in assessing taxonomic diversity is that taxonomic units may vary according to the preference of the taxonomist revising a particular group (e.g.
column2: whether a “splitter” or a “lumper”)
column3: and by the data and methodologies underlying taxonomic revisions and species circumscriptions. This issue becomes obvious when taxonomic treatments of the same group are produced by different researchers independently. For example
column4: the Neotropical palm genus Attalea included 29 species in one monograph ()
column5: and 65 species in another taxonomic treatment published just 4 years later (). Similarly
column6: the Caribbean palm genus Coccothrinax included 14 species in one taxonomic treatment () and 53 species in another (). Personal preferences to “lumping” vs. “splitting” among taxonomists may have large consequences for biodiversity estimates and have been shown to strongly affect diversification rate estimates (). Taxonomic practices should therefore be considered when comparing taxonomic biodiversity at any scale
column7: and whenever adequate
column8: researchers should take advantage of explicit and reproducible criteria for species delimitations.

column1: Here
column2: we propose that the best way to fully understand the complexity of Neotropical biodiversity is by conceptualizing and implementing a novel holistic framework. We define trans-disciplinary biogeography as “a holistic framework that takes advantage of the methods and data in multiple disciplines
column3: in order to solve complex questions about the evolution
column4: maintenance
column5: and distribution of biodiversity through time and scape. By doing so
column6: each individual discipline transcends its traditional borders.”

column4: phylogeography

Successful examples of trans-disciplinary research include the archaeology-ecology synergy that led to the elucidation of pre-Columbian effects on the distribution of Amazonian plants (); the genetic-geology synergy that led to the discovery of an earlier and more prolonged biotic interchange between South and North America since the Miocene (; )

column19: and “geodiversity” (). We envision the integration of a high number of additional synergistic sub-disciplines.

column1: In practice
column2: we want to encourage young students and researchers to invest time in learning more about disciplines that might fall outside their general curriculum
column3: but which could contribute to creating fruitful synergies. Obviously
column4: not every project or publication has to (or should) be trans-disciplinary

column1: To showcase the benefit of these interactions
column2: we provide some background on the emergence of Neotropical biogeography as a research focus—which was integrative from its early days
column3: but successively lost much of its cross-disciplinarity. We then discuss how trans-disciplinary biogeography may help address the interactions between landscape evolution
column4: climate change and biotic diversification at its multiple levels (see also ).

column1: The Prussian naturalist Alexander von Humboldt was among the first to realize that biotic and abiotic processes interact to constrain species distributions
column2: and to place these influences into a geological and climatic framework. He came to this notion in the Neotropics
column3: most famously during his study of the Chimborazo volcano in Ecuador
column4: where he carefully documented the location of different species along elevational zones (). It was in this trip that he first observed that physical parameters such as topography and climate were key for geographic distributions.

column1: A century later
column2: advanced the incipient field of historical biogeography with the theory of continental drift
column3: based in part on past geographic distributions of biotas linked by previously connected continental plates. The striking fit between the coastlines of South America and Africa was one of the pieces of evidence inspiring Wegener’s theory of dynamic
column4: non-static landmasses. In the 1960s
column5: a geophysical mechanism for plate tectonics was proposed ()
column6: placing studies of plant and freshwater fish biogeography into a plate tectonic framework
column7: where vicariance was assumed as a major biogeographic force (; ).

column1: At first
column2: the explanatory power of vicariance biogeography was the ability to predict biogeographic distributions of individual taxa and that of whole biotas from knowledge of how landscapes changed through time (). The paradigmatic example is the geological fragmentation of the Gondwana supercontinent
column3: and the resulting fragmentation of the resident Gondwanan biotas. The vicariance biogeography approach satisfies the scientific impulse of systematists and biogeographers for general explanations of organismal distributions
column4: rather than ascribing each distribution to the vagaries of idiosyncratic evolutionary histories ().

column1: Soon after
column2: the challenge to vicariance biogeography as a general theory was the commonplace observation that vicariant cladogenesis (i.e.
column3: allopatric speciation) is only one of three general macroevolutionary processes
column4: along with dispersal and extinction (; ). Indeed
column5: ecologists have long understood dispersal to be a pervasive process influencing biogeographic distributions (). Long-distance dispersal has been documented in the formation of many biotas worldwide (; ) including those in the Neotropics (; ; ; ).

column1: Neotropical historical biogeography increasingly relies on geological models that specify the landscape configurations on which species originate
column2: disperse
column3: and go extinct. Understanding phylogeny and biogeography in the context of landscape evolution requires assessment of geological data
column4: including sedimentary environments
column5: sedimentation rates
column6: palaeontological records
column7: and geochronological ages
column8: among others (; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ).

column1: Some recent reconstructions of the Neogene landscape in Amazonia are based on dynamic topography
column2: in which mantle movements through time are quantified (). The effects of these movements are estimated on surface subsidence and are then related to environmental and landscape changes
column3: such as the model applied to explain the genesis of the Pebas wetland in western Amazonia (). Another approach is to use numerical modeling and create reconstructions from physical parameters such as rates of erosion and mountain uplift. An example is the reconstruction of the flow of the Amazon River which incorporates surface processes
column4: flexural isostasy and crustal thickening due to orogeny into a mathematical model to explain the drainage reversal in the Miocene (). However
column5: this study did not incorporate the synergic effects of plate movements and surface dynamics
column6: that are known to have impacted on the formation of mega-wetlands and ecosystems ().

column1: Landscape evolution models (LEMs) can be useful in a biological context but often lack spatial and temporal precision. Biological data can help to infer past landscapes
column2: by testing alternative geological models and increasing their precision. However
column3: we caution that the evolutionary history of one clade might represent an idiosyncratic story
column4: rather than inform the general evolution of an entire landscape in which the clade occurs (; ).

column1: In recent years
column2: integrated approaches have built integrative LEMs based on geological
column3: climatic and biodiversity data (; ; ). Some studies make use of geographic information systems and combine these with well-dated palynological databases
column4: such as Neotoma (https://www.neotomadb.org/). These models are mainly applied to reconstruct landscapes across Quaternary time scales (i.e.
column5: the past 2.6 million years). For example
column6: reconstruction of changes in connectivity across the northern Andes enabled the inference of cyclic phases of biotic dispersal and speciation vs. extinction (). Molecular phylogenetic data can be used to statistically evaluate the likelihood of competing geological models on longer time scales
column7: such as the closure of the Central American Seaway dividing South and Central America ()
column8: and the roles of the Caribbean plate margins as dispersal corridors between South and Central America (). Similar approaches based on both terrestrial () and aquatic taxa () may provide important insights when geological data are insufficient or ambiguous.

column1: Neotropical biodiversity can only be properly understood when considering the Andean uplift and the effects of this orogeny on the landscape () and regional climate (; ; ). Although the Andes are entirely confined to South America
column2: their formation has led to far-reaching effects across the Neotropics
column3: and there are clear links with orogenies in Mesoamerica caused by plate tectonics.

column1: The 7
column2: 000 km long Andes is positioned perpendicular to the principal global atmospheric currents and traps the humid air of the Intertropical Convergence Zone. This configuration enhances precipitation along the Andean slopes and in western Amazonia
column3: making them wetter than they would be in a low Andes setting. Moreover
column4: the high Andes also redirects the atmospheric flow inducing the southward deflected South American low-level jet (; ; ).

column1: The situation is reversed in southern and in northwestern South America. In these regions
column2: the Andes trap the humid air of the Southern Hemisphere westerlies (). In contrast to the Amazonian settings
column3: the eastern margin of the Andes at its northern and southern extremes forms a rain shadow where semi-desert conditions prevail
column4: and on the western flank there is increased precipitation with more humid conditions (; )
column5: although this situation is partially reverse during El Niño events. The monumental Andean barrier has thus imposed strong impacts on both the climate and landscapes of South American lowlands
column6: resulting in the modification of river systems and drastic changes in the climate and habitats of many Neotropical regions.

column1: The rise of the northern Andes had a major impact on Neotropical biodiversity
column2: as documented for many taxa (; ; ; ; ; ; ; ). Recent studies that explicitly integrate surface uplift and climatic changes as a function of speciation and extinction include work on the Andean bellflowers ()
column3: Neotropical orchids ()
column4: and Neotropical hummingbirds ().

column1: The Andean uplift affected Neotropical regions in different ways. Over the course of the Miocene
column2: it led to a humidification of Amazonia and aridification of Patagonia (; ; ; ). This contrast is reflected by the history of New World monkeys (Platyrrhini)
column3: whose geographic expansion and morphological and taxonomic diversification is tightly linked with climatic changes (). Platyrrhines were once widely distributed in Patagonia from early to middle Miocene
column4: including the southernmost non-human primates that have ever lived (; ). However
column5: those primates were later extirpated during regional aridification and global cooling after the Middle Miocene. The platyrrhine record of the high Chilean Andes indicates that the connection between Patagonia and the northern Neotropics possibly persisted on the western part of South America
column6: as the southern Andean cordillera was not an important barrier in the Middle Miocene (). This scenario provided primates and other animals with a migration route to the north
column7: facilitating faunal turnover (). This connection may also have contributed to the subsequent Amazonian diversification of crown platyrrhines
column8: including some Patagonian lineages ().

column1: A major question in the study of biodiversity in general
column2: and Neotropical research in particular
column3: is how ecological communities have been assembled over time and how abiotic factors and species interactions have influenced this process. Approaches for the study of biodiverse communities have employed a wide range of models with diverse conceptual roots. Over the last 20 years
column4: there has been an expansion from studies focusing on contemporary community structure and spatial patterns of physical properties of ecosystems (; ; ; ; ; ; )
column5: to studies focusing on historical aspects of community structure and evolution (; ). Early approaches () focused on indices of diversity
column6: descriptions of community membership
column7: as well as flow charts of energy and nutrients through the community. Key variables influencing community structure consistently emphasized classic Hutchinsonian processes such as resource use
column8: competition
column9: and niche partitioning. However
column10: as ecologists adopted new techniques
column11: the resolution of the niche increased from simple variables to also include high-resolution data on climate
column12: soil chemistry
column13: microbiomes
column14: and other physical and biological properties. These approaches have guided several recent analyses of tropical groups
column15: including microorganisms and plants (; ; ; ; ). Additionally
column16: the availability of spatially explicit online global datasets of climate and environmental parameters has helped spawn a generation of studies using large-scale spatial biodiversity surveys and inventories. When analyzed with statistical approaches
column17: these data allow the interpolation between sampled sites and estimation of diversity in non-sampled areas (

column1: The general idea that species are adapted to their environment (i.e.
column2: have different niches) has two important consequences. First
column3: species distributions are expected to reflect the distribution of suitable habitats. Second
column4: species composition in local communities should reflect the environmental characteristics of the site
column5: as unsuitable environmental characteristics or biotic interactions make it impossible for a species to establish and/or survive. Along these lines
column6: many studies have aimed to characterize the edaphic associations of tropical plant species (; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ) and the elevational ranges of many taxa (). If there are more species adapted to some environmental conditions than others and dispersal is generally not a limiting factor
column7: a species richness gradient should result. However
column8: it is also possible that some environmental conditions may allow more species to coexist than others. Several studies have analyzed species richness gradients along environmental gradients such as elevation (; )
column9: rainfall (; )
column10: and soil fertility (; ; ). In general
column11: these studies have shown that Neotropical plant species richness tends to be highest in warm
column12: humid
column13: and aseasonal environmental niches at low to middle elevations.

column2: spatial patterns in the abundance of anurans from Central Amazonia have been shown to conform to the expectations of Hubbell’s neutral theory of biodiversity and biogeography (NTBB; ; ). More recently
column3: a study demonstrated that the incorporation of population genetic dynamics into NTBB support the hypothesis that biodiversity dynamics are out of equilibrium (). Additional research is needed to assess the relative roles of niche constraints
column4: neutral
column5: and non-neutral processes
column6: in explaining and predicting Neotropical biodiversity.

column1: Early theoretical ecologists conceived the role of ecological interactions (e.g.
column2: hervivory
column3: pollination

column6: theoretical ecology shifted to a broader perspective

column8: positive interactions such as mutualism and symbiosis) was envisioned as a mechanism that affects processes in both population and community levels (). This broad spectrum linked many ecological concepts and type of data (such as geo-referenced occurrences and DNA sequences)

column1: The explosion of molecular phylogenetics and dating analyses allow the inference of time-calibrated trees
column2: where branch lengths are measured as units of time or rates of molecular evolution. Fortunately
column3: a massive increase in the availability of genetic information is being driven by high throughput sequencing technologies. Novel genomic data are likely to significantly improve our understanding of genetic diversity and evolutionary relationships among and within species (). Furthermore
column4: these data will also greatly improve our understanding of largely under-studied groups
column5: such as soil microbes ().

column1: The integration of time-calibrated trees into phylogeographic and biogeographic analyses now enables the establishment of links with external sources of temporal information such as landscape evolution
column2: geological history
column3: fossil record
column4: and climate history. Therefore
column5: phylogenies constitute the strongest and most concrete “bridge” across scales and disciplines as outlined here (). Given the complexity and challenges with the reconstruction of reliable phylogenetic trees
column6: we refer to an accompanying review in this volume ().

column1: Detailed reconstructions of the temporal and spatial evolution for individual clades are obtained through “single clade” approaches. These approaches focus on contingencies or events that are idiosyncratic to the group under study
column2: instead of generalities across groups. Methodological advances in single lineage approaches have undergone major developments with parametric methodologies (; ; ; ; ; ).

column1: Typical biogeographic analyses use time-trees and parametric models of biogeographic evolution to estimate ancestral ranges of lineages (branches) and speciation events (nodes)
column2: and to infer rates of biogeographic processes (e.g.
column3: dispersal
column4: speciation
column5: and extinction). To date
column6: probably hundreds of studies have examined the biogeographic history of particular Neotropical clades in this way. Biogeographic hypotheses or models about the relative role of biogeographic processes in the geographic evolution of particular groups can be compared statistically using methods for model selection in phylogenetics
column7: such as the Akaike information criterion or Bayes factors (). Moreover
column8: the rates of these processes may be modified (scaled) to reflect the changing connectivity among the areas of analysis over time (). These advances have contributed to the integration of landscape dynamics and geological history into taxon biogeography in the Neotropics (; ; ; ).

column1: These approaches (sometimes under the umbrella of “comparative biogeography”; ) aim to extract generalities on the evolution of a biogeographic region or whole biota
column2: or generalities on the relationships among biogeographic regions or biotas
column3: by reconstructing the history of their individual components. The focus of this approach is on inferring shared biogeographic histories
column4: such as general patterns of colonization and diversification or a common response to climatic and landscape changes. A recent cross-taxonomic analysis for six major clades of terrestrial plants and animals
column5: across all major Neotropical biomes and regions
column6: was presented by . That study showed an unexpectedly high number of dispersal events across the entire Neotropics
column7: which took place for tens of millions of years and often involved shifts in major environmental types (in particular from forests to savannas). The high frequency of dispersal events identified in that study reflects patterns reported for tree communities across Neotropical rain forests () and between rainforests and savannas ().

column1: Multi-clade approaches were traditionally known as “area biogeography” and were the focus of the cladistic biogeographic school for decades (; ). The first methods used for cross-taxonomic biogeographic approaches were based on parsimony

column3: such as those underlying the assembly of freshwater fish faunas in South American river basins (). Recently developed parametric approaches (; ) offer now a powerful way to obtain generalities about patterns of dispersal and diversification in biotas
column4: allowing us to test between alternative geological or spatial scenarios (). An interesting methodology bridging community ecology and cross-taxonomic biogeographic analysis is the phylogeographic concordance factor analysis ()
column5: which uses Bayesian concordance analysis () to test for shared evolutionary history among co-distributed species and the existence of strong ecological interactions or dependence (

column1: The Theory of Island Biogeography (TIB; ) introduced parameters such as rates of colonization (immigration) and extinction within a mathematical framework
column2: allowing the prediction of the number of species present on an island based solely on its distance from a mainland species source and its area (; ). New models inspired by the TIB are now attempting to integrate additional parameters
column3: such as speciation and island age ()
column4: population abundances ()
column5: and trophic interactions ().

column1: Community ecology and an expanded TIB are now also adopting a more evolutionary approach by integrating phylogenetic data to the study of community assembly and dynamics
column2: including the role of in situ adaptation or speciation vs. dispersal in community assembly
column3: the temporal sequence of species interactions
column4: and the role of abiotic and biotic factors in diversification of specific lineages (; ; ; ;
column5: ; ). Importantly
column6: this requires denser voucher sampling of specimens
column7: which in turn will lead to denser phylogenies
column8: better estimates of species boundaries under multi-species coalescent approaches and tackling the common problem of cryptic species (; ). By adopting a more historical focus
column9: community ecology methods are explicitly trying to reconstruct the sequence of events leading to modern-day communities
column10: such as the island-like patches of white-sand savannas in Amazonia (). These approaches relax the assumption of ecological neutrality
column11: and focus on the distinctive properties of individual lineages
column12: historical contingency
column13: and particularities of present-day outcomes (; ). In their most recent forms
column14: these models incorporate ecological parameters such as competition and species interactions () or landscape dynamics ().

column1: The original goals of community ecology
column2: as established in the early 20th century
column3: were to predict species distributions and abundances
column4: species richness and equitability
column5: community productivity
column6: food web structure
column7: predator-prey dynamics
column8: succession
column9: and community assembly. However
column10: this discipline has not yet succeeded in meeting most of these goals (; ; ; ; ). The reasons are many
column11: but may be especially associated to the non-equilibrium condition of most local assemblages
column12: in which the effects of historical contingencies of dispersal
column13: extirpations
column14: and other stochastic processes override the equilibrium expectations generated by local functional processes such as predation and competition (). In other words
column15: the species composition and equitability of most local assemblages are more strongly governed by regional and historical factors than by local ecological interactions (; ; ; ).

column1: The regional species pool (light gray box) is defined as the sum of all the local species assemblages (darker gray box). Black arrows indicate processes that increase species richness
column2: red arrows processes that reduce species richness. Note the hierarchical organization of processes resulting in species richness
column3: with evolutionary processes occurring over regional to continental spatiotemporal scales and ecological processes occurring over local scales. Speciation and dispersal contribute new species to the regional pool
column4: while extinction removes species. Dispersal mediated by abiotic habitat filtering and biotic facilitation () increase the richness of local assemblages by enhancing establishment of species preadapted to local conditions
column5: or aiding in the establishment of other species. Biotic interactions such as predation and competition may serve to reduce local richness. Diagram modified from  and .

column1: This “crisis” in community ecology has fueled the rise of alternative functionally-neutral theories
column2: like the NTBB ()
column3: and the metacommunity theory (). However
column4: neutral theories have also been criticized for their simplistic assumptions and lack of predictive power under the non-neutral conditions frequently observed in nature (). In general
column5: the field of community ecology appears to be ripe for a paradigm shift ().

column1: While many studies conducted at continental to global scales aim to test broad hypotheses about drivers of biodiversity gradients (; )
column2: others rely on analyses of region-wide field data collected over decades (). These surveys set the stage for analyses on the environmental and historical correlates of diversity (; ; ). Detailed explanations of the heterogeneity found at multiple scales remain a major challenge.

column1: One recent topic of concern is whether Neotropical biodiversity patterns documented today have resulted from purely “natural” processes
column2: or have been largely influenced by human activities (). Evidence from archaeological
column3: remote sensing
column4: biodiversity data
column5: and modeling approaches suggest that humans may have had a much deeper impact on Neotropical biodiversity
column6: both in time and space
column7: than traditionally conceived ().

column1: The multi-layer analytical framework developed by recent eco-genetic research granted the combination of multiple data layers from three different scale dynamics (local mechanisms
column2: macro-evolutionary processes
column3: geographic patterns). The synergy of those layers illustrates a tripod that gathers ecological
column4: evolutionary and biogeographical factors of populations
column5: communities
column6: and meta-communities
column7: respectively (; ; ). Detecting ecological signals across multi-layered data
column8: such as the contribution of mutualisms in biogeographic processes (speciation
column9: extinction
column10: migration) remains a major challenge. Tackling this challenge will require linking spatio-temporal data with models that detect common signatures of ecological interactions across layers (). Although recent theoretical advances have unveiled phylogenetic signals from community processes (; ; )
column11: we urge for new models that can identify ecological signal from multiple layers. The exploration of ecological factors that are associated to positive and negative interactions (i.e.
column12: network structure
column13: taxonomic associations) might reveal important insights on the dynamics and complexity of ecological interactions for producing and maintaining Neotropical biodiversity.

column1: One important shortcoming of molecular-based biogeographical analyses in general
column2: and parametric models of range evolution in particular
column3: is the fact that it is almost always based on extant data alone. Because of the effects of extinction
column4: the pattern of geographic distribution we observe today may be a poor representation of the actual biogeographic history
column5: especially if extinction rates have been unequal among areas () and taxa (). One way to solve this issue is to include extinct lineages in biogeographic analyses ()
column6: or to use their past distribution inferred from the fossil record to constrain inferences of ancestral ranges (). This approach has often revealed different biogeographic histories for the study group as compared to analyses based on extant data only (; ). A recent development is the development of the dispersal-extinction-sampling model
column7: to infer rates of dispersal and area extinction exclusively from fossil data (
column8: ). Under this approach
column9: a separate sampling parameter is used to account for the unevenness of the fossil record both spatially and temporally. When the fossil record is sufficiently abundant
column10: it provides more accurate measures of changes in rates of geographic evolution and less biased extinction rates
column11: than when exclusively extant taxa are used ().

column1: Another challenge to understanding current patterns of evolutionary diversity is the absolute dating of phylogenies
column2: which relies heavily on fossils. This shortcoming complicates a detailed understanding of the ages of tropical taxa
column3: especially those from rainforests (). New methodological developments to directly integrate fossil (extinct) lineages into phylogeny reconstruction (; ; ; ) offer new hope in the quest to retrieve more accurate depictions of evolutionary patterns.

column1: Finally
column2: estimating the tempo of diversification is difficult without fossil constraints. In simulated phylogenies
column3: the resulting shape of lineage-through-time plots vary significantly when the fossil record is added as compared to phylogenies that incorporate extant taxa exclusively (; ). The inferred macroevolutionary dynamics estimated from molecular phylogenies may thus be misleading if fossil taxa are neglected
column4: or when macroevolutionary tools do not acknowledge the rare sampling of fossil lineages. Clearly
column5: fossils are crucial to not only understand past dynamics
column6: but also for an improved understanding of current patterns (). We therefore urge for a much tighter integration between the palaeontological and neontological research communities in the Neotropics.

column1: A potential problem with single clade and cross-taxonomic biogeographic analyses as discussed above is that areas are treated as traits of organisms evolving along phylogenetic trees. Geology is often used to inform the model but does not form its core. For instance
column2: area connectivity is often used in parametric methods to constrain or scale migration rates but not as an actual part of the model. A new generation of methods that use the power of LEMs to study the full panoply of evolutionary processes
column3: at both microevolutionary (population) level (; ) and macroevolutionary (interspecific) scales (; ) are now being developed. For example
column4: uplift of a dissected landscape and river capture are two landscape evolution processes with great power to generate high species richness (). Both of these processes simultaneously and continuously merge and separate portions of adjacent landscape areas
column5: allowing biotic dispersal and larger geographic ranges
column6: vicariant speciation and smaller geographic ranges
column7: and extinction when range sizes are subdivided below a minimum persistence threshold ().

column1: There are often mixed definitions and questions related to the timing and mode of biotic evolution in the Neotropics. The “origin of Neotropical biodiversity” encapsulates at least two contrasting subjects: the timing of origin of hyperdiversity (i.e.
column2: “when did the Neotropics reach globally outstanding levels of species richness?”)
column3: and the actual age of extant species (i.e.
column4: “when did the species that we see today split from their most recent common ancestors
column5: as defined by their stem age?”; ). It is clear that there have been unusual periods of time throughout geological history
column6: both in terms of biotic and abiotic events (; ; ). However
column7: all periods of time have contributed to the current biodiversity
column8: if seen in the perspective of the “evolutionary continuum” that bridges the core biodiversity disciplines ().

column1: Examples of studies that have sought for “special” periods of time often come from time-calibrated molecular phylogenies. For instance
column2: butterfly species-pairs seem to be relatively young in origin (i.e.
column3: <2 Ma)
column4: suggesting that the Pleistocene and Holocene may have represented “extraordinary times” for Neotropical butterfly speciation () and following the refugium theory (). However
column5: time-calibrated phylogenies may not fully address the potential impact of extinction and species duration (). In other words
column6: if we were able to travel back in time to any period and sequence species around us
column7: the odds are that most species alive might also be around two Ma old or less. In addition
column8: the definition of “species” may vary considerably depending on data source (e.g.
column9: based on the fossil record and extant populations) and across taxa. Highly structured populations with considerable genetic divergences may be seen as “incipient species” that have not yet completed the speciation process (). Variable species concepts and adequate sampling of extant and extinct taxa represent a serious barrier for our understanding of Neotropical diversification (– and ).

column1: Comparative biology has experienced major advancements in the theory and practice of biogeography and molecular phylogenetics during the past decades. However
column2: we still need to increase sampling of organisms drastically in order to advance our knowledge on the patterns and processes underlying Neotropical biodiversity (). However
column3: fieldwork in the Neotropics
column4: especially in pristine areas
column5: is time consuming and logistically demanding. Research funding for exploratory inventory projects is also becoming increasingly harder to obtain
column6: despite the fact that highly successful projects (i.e.
column7: sequencing the first human genome and creating the Amazon Tree Diversity Network) were initially discovery-driven
column8: rather than focused on testing specific hypotheses. Furthermore
column9: obtaining permits to collect and export biological samples is also challenging
column10: involving many differences across national legislations. Finally
column11: some authors have seen the need of fieldwork as less relevant in this era of museomics (; ).

column1: Despite all these obstacles
column2: fieldwork remains absolutely essential for biodiversity data generation and monitoring (). Fieldwork also provides students and researchers with a deeper understanding of their study systems
column3: often providing new ideas and questions
column4: while facilitating the establishment of new collaborations
column5: enabling the exchange of knowledge
column6: fueling the development of new methods
column7: and increasing the possibilities of major discoveries (). We should seriously consider new strategies for the generation of new biodiversity data
column8: as well as for the syntheses of the already available data.

column1: Multi-taxon field campaigns could provide unique opportunities for intensive sampling
column2: while optimizing resources
column3: bureaucratic
column4: and logistic efforts. However
column5: this vision requires a radical re-thinking and re-organization. We need to provide young generations with the training
column6: tools and resources needed to carry out research on all aspects of biodiversity. We also need to support taxonomic specialists and institutions in order to adequately study
column7: archive and facilitate free access to biological collections and associated data. In addition
column8: we also need to join forces across nations and disciplines for mutual benefit and joint scientific growth. Clearly
column9: these investments would be worthwhile from a global perspective. The future of Neotropical biodiversity research depends on extensive collaborations and coordinated efforts.

column1: Five main take home messages can be taken from this review
column2: namely:

column1: Neotropical biodiversity is exceedingly high
column2: regardless of the axis explored (e.g.
column3: taxonomic
column4: phylogenetic
column5: functional
column6: ecosystems);

column1: Understanding the origins
column2: evolution
column3: maintenance
column4: and distribution patterns of Neotropical biodiversity is a grand scientific challenge with many remaining unknowns;

column1: “Trans-disciplinary biogeography” aims to better integrate the seemingly disparate disciplines required to explore the biotic and abiotic evolution of the Neotropics;
Many methodological advances will be required to deal with the increasing wealth of biodiversity data and associated environmental and geological variables;
There is an urgent need to fill the many gaps in biodiversity knowledge
column2: including extant and extinct taxa and their interactions. This calls for a “renaissance” for fieldwork.

column2: Pacific
column3: Caribbean
column4: and Andean regions of Colombia and Venezuelan Andes (; ; ). It boasts numerous species
column5: making it one of the most essential areas in the Neotropics (; ; ). Its varied climate and resource availability have fostered human settlements over time
column6: turning the NAB into one of the most populated regions in South America (). However

column12: PNN Bahia Portete-Kaurrele; ; ). However
column13: this approach does not consider the history and evolutionary information contained in a phylogeny (; )

column1: From an evolutionary perspective
column2: it is crucial to consider the distinction between species when prioritizing conservation areas. One way to accomplish this is to use phylogenetic diversity indices that evaluate the distinctness between species by quantifying the information contained in a topology (; ; ). These indices can also be used to compare phylogenetic values in different areas (; ; ).

column1: Phylogenetic diversity indices can help protect not only the most phylogenetically distinct species but also a more significant number of species in fewer areas (; )
column2: mainly when used in conjunction with a complementarity measure (). Despite the potential benefits of using phylogenetic diversity indices for conservation
column3: most studies since 1991 have focused on their applicability and statistical properties (; ; )
column4: rather than on providing specific recommendations for conservation purposes (; ). Therefore
column5: developing recommendations for the effective protection of these areas using phylogenetic information is crucial.

column1: Although studies on phylogenetic diversity indices have primarily focused on their applicability and statistical properties since 1991 (; ; )
column2: there has been limited exploration of their potential application in conservation (). Thus
column3: in this study
column4: our objectives were to (1) present a plausible priority scheme in the NAB for conservation purposes
column5: (2) quantify the contribution of endemic species to the phylogenetic diversity of the NAB
column6: and (3) assess the phylogenetic diversity within the PAs.

We primarily chose families if at least 70% of the species in the family had a minimum of three genes available in GenBank. We used the family as a “taxonomic group”; if not

column1: Our analysis of the protected areas in the NAB revealed some interesting patterns. The overall species richness and phylogenetic diversity values were higher outside the protected areas (64% and 67%
column2: respectively). Although we found more species and higher phylogenetic diversity values outside protected areas
column3: they still held a significant amount of the region’s total evolutionary information in a relatively small area (9.2% of the NAB’s area).

column1: The Andean region harbors the highest phylogenetic diversity in the NAB ()
column2: is considered an essential area for biodiversity (i.e.
column3: hotspot; ; )
column4: and is a primary target for conservation efforts (e.g.
column5: ; ). Furthermore
column6: found more evolutionary information within the Andean hotspot than outside
column7: highlighting its importance for phylogenetic diversity. Although the Andean region stands out as a priority area
column8: our study also revealed other areas outside this region with high AvTD values
column9: such as the Andean valleys and the Amazonian and Pacific lowlands (). Notably

column1: Conservation analyses based on a single taxon or specific species groups
column2: such as endemic
column3: small-range
column4: or threatened species
column5: may yield different results. Endemic species are generally characterized by recent diversification
column6: which can result in short branch lengths (; ). Our study found that endemic species had significantly shorter terminal branch lengths (mean = 0.029
column7: SD = 0.056) than non-endemic species (mean = 0.070
column9: and represented only 0.4% of the accumulated branch lengths
column10: resulting in a low contribution to the phylogenetic diversity of the NAB. Although endemic species are more sensitive to environmental disruption and are strong predictors of local extinction risk (; )
column11: they may not accurately predict phylogenetic priorities because of their short branches
column12: which may lead to the underestimation of phylogenetic diversity in a given area. Therefore
column13: it is not advisable to use endemic species as the sole estimator of phylogenetic diversity. It is essential to consider a broader range of taxa and phylogenetic information to accurately identify conservation priorities.

column1: Other phylogenetic diversity indices consider the threat of species extinction
column2: such as the EDGE index (). This index scales phylogenetic diversity based on the IUCN category of the species. We found that approximately 53% of the 1
column3: 252 species included in our study were not evaluated by the IUCN ()
column4: rendering the EDGE index impractical. Additionally
column5: 55% of the species classified as critically endangered (CR) or endangered (EN) were endemic species with low phylogenetic diversity values and shorter branch lengths. According to the IUCN
column6: high threat levels do not always correlate with high evolutionary distinctiveness (). Therefore
column7: considering that the threat of extinction is vital for conservation
column8: it is essential to consider other factors
column9: such as evolutionary distinctiveness and phylogenetic diversity
column10: to ensure comprehensive and effective conservation planning.

column1: PAs on the NAB have effectively preserved species richness and evolutionary history. However
column2: our study found that species richness and phylogenetic diversity were higher outside PAs than within PAs. This pattern has also been observed in other studies of species richness
column3: phylogenetic diversity
column4: and ecoregions (; ; ; ). However
column5: the PAs in the NAB cover a small geographic area (only 9.2% of the NAB area). Although species richness is commonly used in PA delimitation
column6: evolutionary considerations remain limited (; ; ).

column1: Given that PAs are the primary strategy for conservation () and a crucial tool for mitigating the impact of climate change (; )
column2: it is necessary to delineate new areas for PAs and improve connectivity between existing ones. Phylogenetic diversity also plays a significant role in this process. Well-connected PA networks can increase species persistence by allowing the movement and flow of different ecological components (; ). Areas with high phylogenetic diversity should be considered when expanding and connecting PAs. For example

66|10.7717/peerj.208|Effects of ocean acidification on the dissolution rates of reef-coral skeletons| van Woesik  Robert |2013-11-21|As humans continue to burn fossil fuels at an unprecedented rate
column2: the concentration of carbon dioxide (CO2) in the atmosphere is presently higher than it has been for the last 420

Yet the tropical oceans are changing at a more rapid rate and are acidifying more quickly than the cooler waters most likely because of the relationship of rapidly increasing ocean temperature and reaction rates (). column5: the Pacific Ocean is more acidic than the Atlantic Ocean

column1: Species delimitation is crucial in various biological disciplines

column4: and evolutionary biology (; ). Species serve as a metric in biology and are essential for conservation
column5: as species help conservationists develop effective strategies for targeted conservation management (). Accurately defining species allows the study of patterns of genetic diversity and population structure within certain categories
column6: as well as the factors driving speciation (). For example
column7: the discovery and precise description of Ostryopsis intermedia B. Tian & J. Q. Liu sparked a series of research projects focused on its homoploid hybrid speciation. These findings provided insights into how Quaternary climate change triggered its demographic history and biogeography pattern
column8: as well as the underlying genetic mechanism of its speciation (; ; ). Similar studies have been conducted on species exhibiting hybrid speciation
column9: such as Allium przewalskianum Regel
column10: Picea purpurea Mast.
column11: and Cupressus chengiana S.Y. Hu (; ; ; ).

column1: Since Ernst Mayr proposed the concept of species
column2: it has sparked a heated debate and resulted in a variety of definitions (). The traditional morphological (taxonomic) species concept is based on discontinuous phenotypic differences across species at the population level (; )
column3: but it can be inaccurate when cryptic species
column4: phenotypic polymorphism
column5: or adaptive convergence are involved. Advances in molecular biology and phylogenetic analytic improvements have led to increased popularity of the phylogenetic species concept
column6: which refers to a species as an independent evolutionary lineage
column7: and sidesteps the non-universal criterion of reproductive isolation
column8: making it applicable to both extant and extinct organisms
column9: as well as to both sexual and asexual reproductive creatures (; ). As a result
column10: DNA-based approaches have become increasingly important in resolving taxonomic uncertainties and identifying evolutionarily significant units (ESUs; ).

column1: In an era marked by widespread species extinction and ongoing environmental crises
column2: DNA barcoding has emerged as a standardized tool for species delimitation due to its ability to facilitate accurate and direct comparison among different users (; ; ; ). DNA barcoding is based on the principle of barcoding gap
column3: which refers to the difference in genetic distances between intra- and inter-species. Previous studies have assessed different combinations of nuclear internal transcribed spacers (ITS) with the seven leading candidate plastid DNA regions () as potential core DNA barcodes discriminating closely related plant species (; ). However
column4: these combinations may not always provide adequate barcoding gaps and efficiency compared to the CO1 barcode used in animals
column5: especially in plants with ancestral polymorphisms
column6: recent speciation
column7: or hybridization (; ). Previous studies using traditional DNA barcodes for the M. squamosa complex showed poor species resolution due to insufficient specimen sampling and genetic diversity; M. squamosa
column8: M. bracteata and M. paniculata shared haplotypes and tended to cluster into one clade (; ). This highlights the need for more extensive taxon sampling and the development of more discerning super-barcodes to identify the phylogenetic relationships and remove the taxonomic ambiguity within this genus.

column1: The chloroplast
column2: a high-copy organelle in plants
column3: is one of the most technically accessible regions of the plant genome. With the development of next-generation sequencing technologies and the reduction of sequencing costs
column4: the plastome has gained recognition as a “super-barcode” with versatile applications in plant phylogenetics
column5: species delimitation and population genetics
column6: especially in groups with poor morphological differentiation among species (; ; ; ; ).

column1: Although previous studies have analyzed plastome features with a few Myricaria species available in GenBank
column2: there is currently no report on species-specific identification and determination of species boundaries within the M. squamosa complex (; ). Plastome sequences and genome skimming can also be used for nuclear ribosomal DNA (nrDNA) assembly
column3: enabling the inference of phylogenetic relationships using both uniparentally-inherited plastomes and biparentally-inherited nuclear genes (;

column1: To explore the features and structural differentiation of plastomes among the species in the M. squamosa complex
column2: and to establish a well-supported phylogenetic framework of Chinese Myricaria
column3: 25 individual samples
column4: representing three recognized taxa in the M. squamosa complex through a dense taxon sampling strategy
column5: were used in this study. Complete plastomes and nrDNA sequences via genome skimming were used to test their discriminatory power in the M. squamosa species complex
column6: an evolutionarily young lineage where the traditional DNA barcoding approach has been insufficient. The main purposes of this study were to: (i) gain insight into the structural features of multiple Myricaria plastomes; (ii) determine whether plastome sequences can provide a more detailed resolution of the shallow-level relationships within the M. squamosa complex compared with nrDNA sequences; and (iii) evaluate the utility of proposed ‘Myricaria-specific’ plastid barcodes for species discrimination.

column3: M. wardii

column5: spanning years 2019 to 2021 (). Additionally

column8: with each species having more than two populations. Data from Tamarix and Reaumuria L. were used as outgroups. Myricaria samples sequenced in the present study were not included in the list of national key protected plants and not collected from national parks or natural reserves. The research methods were approved by the Ethical Experimentation Committee of Zunyi Medical University (Identification Code: ZMU-BO-1903-169) and followed the legal and ethical standards of the local government. Voucher specimens from each population were then deposited at the Herbarium of Zunyi Medical University Life Science Museum with specific voucher numbers. Detailed information of the species and datasets are listed in .

column1: Four datasets were independently used for species delimitation by a tree-based method: (1) the complete plastome sequences
column2: (2) standard nrDNA barcodes (ITS)
column3: (3) the plastid DNA barcodes (matK+rbcL+trnH-psbA+trnL-F) recommended by  and (4) the group-specific DNA barcodes selected from hyper-variable regions in plastomes. Each dataset containing outgroups was aligned using the MAFFT online service with default parameters () and manually checked using MEGA 7.0 (). The best-fit substitution model for each dataset was then determined using Jmodeltest 2.1 (; ). ML phylogenetic analyses were then performed through the IQ-TREE web server with 1
column4: 000 bootstrap replicates using UFBoot2 and the collapsing near-zero branches option (; ). Bayesian inference (BI) analyses were inferred using MrBayes v3.2.7 with two simultaneous parallel analyses employing the MC3 algorithm (). For each run
column5: four independent Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) chains (one cold and three heated) were propagated for 1
column7: 000 generations each and sampled every 500 generations. The log file was checked to ensure that the potential scale reduction factor (PSRF) was reasonably close to 1.0 and the estimated sample size (ESS) was larger than 200 for all parameters. The first 25% of samples were conservatively discarded as burn-in. The remaining trees were used to generate a 50% majority-rule consensus tree. The ML and BI trees were annotated and visualized using ITOL 6.5.8 ().

column1: Each species of Pogona has its own distinct range () (). Pogona barbata is found in the east and prefers a woodland to dry sclerophyll forest habitat; however
column2: P. barbata is commonly seen around cities. P. henrylawsoni is found in the inland north-central region of Queensland
column3: and their habitat is a treeless cracked clay plain with Mitchell grass as the only vegetation. P. microlepidota is restricted to the northwest of Kimberley in Western Australia
column4: and prefers a woodland habitat with a more humid climate. P. minor is found in the west and prefers a woodland to shrubland habitat. P. nullarbor is restricted to the Nullarbor Plain and prefers a shrubland habitat with minimal to moderate humidity. P. vitticeps is found throughout the eastern interior of Australia
column5: and it prefers a semi-arid to arid woodland habitat with low humidity.

69|10.7717/peerj.77|Some poleward movement of British native vascular plants is occurring, but the fingerprint of climate change is not evident| Groom  Quentin J. |2013-5-28|Among animals
column2: numerous studies have shown recent poleward movement and upward altitudinal shift of distribution (; ; ; ; ). In the case of plants there is evidence of movement towards higher altitudinal ranges
column3: but in contrast to animals
column4: the evidence for poleward shifts of plants is scant (; ; ; ; ; ).  and  are often cited
column5: but
column6: they concern a tiny number of species in small areas of the Arctic and Antarctic.

column1: These poleward and altitudinal range shifts have been interpreted as the fingerprint of recent climatic warming on the biosphere (; ; ). So
column2: why is there a lack of evidence for poleward range shifts among plants? One reason may be the disparity between rapid declines in temperature with elevation over a short distance in mountains
column3: compared to the gradual change with latitude.  called this the “altitude-for-latitude disparity”. In elevation change the dispersal rate of plants is not such an impediment to migration. Poleward movement is often assumed to be the expected response of animals and plants whose range has warmed
column4: though there are many reasons why this may not be the case ().

column1: Another complicating factor in migration is that there are many other environmental changes that are causing range shifts among plants
column2: such as atmospheric nitrogen deposition
column3: grazing changes
column4: anthropogenic dispersal
column5: disease
column6: general eutrophication and many forms of land use change. These other migrational pressures are not necessarily in a poleward direction. Therefore
column7: to uncover a climatic component to latitudinal migration in plants one needs to look at many species over long distances.

column1: Range boundaries are often used to measure rates and directions of migration (). It is argued that the edges of a range will be more sensitive to change than the core range of a species. Yet
column2: a range boundary is difficult to define unless it occurs along a physical barrier. At the edges of a species range the populations will be more diffuse and the measurements of range boundaries will be sensitive to small gains and losses
column3: whether these are real or due to differences in recorder effort.

column1: Another problem of interpreting range shifts in bounded areas
column2: such as an island
column3: is that species can only extend their range in unbounded directions. So migration can be substantially misdirected from the course it would have taken without barriers. Furthermore
column4: if the population of a species increases in its core area it will almost inevitably extend its range boundary
column5: even if it is no more adapted to the environment in this new range. This is the concept of sink populations which would not survive were it not for replenishment from the core (). Such apparent range shifts might be expected to be greater for species with effective long distance dispersal mechanisms that can replenish sink populations. In Great Britain there is a gradient of vascular plant diversity with latitude
column6: with fewer species in the north
column7: therefore a general northward movement might be predicted among species with expanding ranges just because there are more species in the south.

column1: In this study I have chosen to determine the centre of mass of a population based upon the occupancy probabilities of that species across the area. The unevenness of raw biodiversity data was corrected by using detection/non-detection data in 4 km2 grid squares and by smoothing spatial differences in recording effort with spatial interpolation. The migrational change and direction are based upon the movement of the centre of mass between time periods
column2: not the range boundary. Unlike measurements of range boundaries this measurement uses a much larger proportion of the available data and because it is generated from detection/non-detection data the recording effort is better controlled and not so sensitive to local differences at the boundary. Centre of mass changes take into account
column3: the core distribution
column4: leading and trailing edges of a population’s migration.

column1: The centre of mass of species with expanding ranges will move in the direction of migration
column2: whereas the centre of mass of declining species will move away from the area of greatest decline. As these mechanisms are different
column3: the results are analysed separately for species with expanding and declining ranges.

column1: The aim of this research was to examine the natural migration of plants in Great Britain. That is
column2: the migration driven by the plants’ own dispersal mechanisms and not by the deliberate dispersal by mankind through horticulture and forestry. For this reason
column3: I have concentrated on examining the migration of native species. I aim to establish whether there is a poleward migration of plants within Great Britain and whether the movements of plants can be explained by climate change and dispersal syndrome.

column1: Species occupancy maps were created for all but the rarest species in Great Britain as has previously been described in . In summary
column2: records were used from the Distributions Database of the Botanical Society of the British Isles (BSBI). All dated records from 1978 to 2011 were used in this study from all parts of Great Britain
column3: except for the islands of the Outer Hebrides
column4: Shetland
column5: the Channel Islands and the Isle of Man. A snapshot of the database was taken in November 2011 (). Any records of subspecies and variety were amalgamated with those of the species to which they belong. Microspecies in the genera Taraxacum
column6: Hieracium
column7: Euphrasia and Rubus were combined with records of the aggregate species
column8: as were some other taxa such as Arctium
column9: Cotoneaster
column10: and Rosa canina agg. Taxonomy follows . The grid system used is that of the Ordnance Survey of the United Kingdom.

column1: Estimation of species ranges was done by selecting well-surveyed grid cells (4 km2) from a pool of all records and generating detection/non-detection data from these. The spatial distribution of occupancy is modelled using variograms and these models are used to interpolate the occupancy probability across the whole area in a process called kriging. To avoid kriging over landscapes with different spatial structures Great Britain was separated into four partitions
column2: Scotland
column3: Wales
column4: northern England and southern England. Northern England was defined as is traditional for the BSBI as vice counties including
column5: and northwards from
column6: South Lincolnshire
column7: Leicestershire
column8: Derbyshire and Cheshire (for biological recording purposes Great Britain is divided into 113 vice counties with fixed borders). For convenience these regions and countries will be referred to as the partitions. Partitioning the area is not entirely necessary
column9: but does allow statistical comparison between partitions and species.

column1: Species without at least 50 occupancies in a partition were not considered in the analysis. Rare species tend to have the greatest biases in their recording and in the conservation efforts used to preserve them in their localities.

Interpolated maps were created for two time periods 1978 to 1994 and 1995 to 2011. These periods were chosen because they are of equal length and both periods contain a national sample survey of 4 km2 grid squares. Selection of sample squares
column2: creation of detection/non-detection data and the resulting estimates of recording effort
column3: average occupancy and occupancy change are all described in detail in . Well-surveyed grid cells are defined as those that have had at least two days of surveying conducted in them and a minimum threshold of species recorded in each of those surveys. Details of these thresholds and discussion of the differences in recording effort between the different time periods is given in . Essentially
column4: the process of kriging balances the spatial differences in recording and the selection criteria of grid cells balancing temporal differences. Furthermore
column5: although the selection threshold is important
column6: the method is insensitive to its precise value.

column1: Given that northern migration of the centre of mass cannot be easily explained by climate and that there is no obvious influence of dispersal syndrome on the magnitude of movement it is informative to look at examples of species with large movements in their population’s centre of mass ().

Species with large changes in their centre of mass were selected by having the highest product of their absolute change in occupancy probability and the distance that their centre of mass moved. Directions are from zero at grid north. Distance is the distance moved by the centre of mass. Mean occupancy probability and absolute change are taken from . Details of all species are available in .
The most obvious group among these species are the halophytes e.g.
column2: Atriplex littoralis
column3: Beta vulgaris
column4: Cochlearia danica
column5: Puccinellia distans and Spergularia marina. Yet
column6: there are no common directions in the movement of these plants. Other common features are far less clear. Orchids are quite well represented e.g.
column7: Dactylorhiza maculata
column8: D. praetermissa and Goodyera repens as are other wind dispersed plants such as Acer campestre
column9: Lactuca virosa
column10: Phragmites australis
column11: Polystichum setiferum
column12: Populus nigra
column13: Sonchus asper and Typha latifolia. Yet there are several other dispersal strategies represented
column14: including animal dispersed species (Bryonia dioica
column15: Rosa caesia
column16: Rubus caesius and Solanum dulcamara) and water dispersed plants (Oenanthe crocata and Comarum palustre).

column1: The centre of mass in bounded ranges will tend to move parallel with the long axis of the area. For example
column2: the south of England is very roughly a right-angle triangle with the acute angle in the west. If a species has its core range in the west
column3: but for climatic reasons is able to grow further north
column4: its centre of mass will move north-eastward as it occupies more northerly territory
column5: because it is blocked from moving directly north by the sea and the boundary with northern England. This explains why the majority of centre of mass movements are on a north-easterly to south-westerly axis for southern England (). The other
column6: more rectangular
column7: partitions show a north-south axis.

column1: Recent poleward migration has been repeatedly claimed for animal species in a number of countries including Great Britain. Yet
column2: in this study it can only be confirmed for plants with expanding ranges and then only weakly. Furthermore
column3: recent reanalysis of avian range margin shifts has shown that changes largely disappear when recording effort is correctly accounted for .

column1: Among the plants that are moving northward there is no evidence that these are from warmer climates. Perhaps it is unrealistic to already expect a northward movement of plant species due to climate change. The mean annual temperature for the UK has only risen about 0.25°C over the period of this study ()
column2: which is perhaps too small to have a significant impact. Also
column3: a lag is to be expected in the reaction of plants to climate change. At the leading edge of migration a lag will occur because of the limitations of natural dispersal (). While at the trailing edge
column4: a lag will occur because of the persistence of perennial species in otherwise unfavourable climates ().

column1: Furthermore
column2: the effects of climate change are more complicated than a simple northward shift of range. For example
column3: there are examples of species migrating in the opposite direction to that originally predicted from temperature changes (; ; ). Modelling of the climatic niche of Australian birds showed that a complex interaction of temperature and precipitation change predicted a wide range of migration directions. If only poleward movement was considered the impact of climate change was seriously underestimated (). In Great Britain
column4: migration could occur towards the more temperate coasts to avoid greater temperature extremes in the centre of the country. In which case
column5: some species could move southward in response to temperature change.

column1: Great Britain
column2: like many temperate countries
column3: has many latitudinal gradients. These gradients are a consequence of geology and geography and only partially related to the climatic gradient. For example
column4: human population and soil fertility decrease towards the north
column5: while altitude increases. The greater population in the south means more disturbance in the south
column6: greater introduction of alien species and more transportation. The lower human population in the north
column7: higher elevation and infertile soils means more extensive farming methods and forestry. These non-climatic gradients might act as barriers to the northward migration of species. Also
column8: Britain has a mild oceanic climate
column9: which could soften the impact of climate change.

column1: Perhaps it is counterintuitive
column2: but different dispersal strategies made no difference to the migration of plants in Great Britain during this period. Species traits were also a poor predictor of range shifts in North American Passeriformes and British Odonata (). Even though many wind-dispersed species were among the top migrating species
column3: halophytes
column4: with no obvious morphological dispersal strategy
column5: moved just as rapidly. Halophytes have the advantage of an uninterrupted habit
column6: free from competitors as they spread along roads where salt is strewn in the winter (). Clearly
column7: the dispersal strategy is not always the rate limiting factor in migration and habitat availability is important.

column1: The fingerprint of climate change is not yet obvious on the migration of plants in Great Britain. Even though climate change is affecting British plants in other ways
column2: such as changes in phenology ().This is not to say that migration due to climate change will not
column3: or has not occurred
column4: however
column5: its traces in Great Britain are obscured by other manmade changes to the environment and will require more sensitive analyses to uncover.
