column1: 71|10.7717/peerj.5909|Postmortem transport in fossil and modern shelled cephalopods| Yacobucci  Margaret M. |2018-11-27|"Biogeographic distributions strongly influence the ecology
column2: evolution
column3: and extinction of clades. While scientists studying modern organisms can collect data on the observed locations of sampled living specimens
column4: paleontologists must assume that fossil localities provide an accurate estimate of the group’s living geographic range. For most marine animal groups
column5: especially benthic invertebrates
column6: that assumption appears to work well
column7: as their hard parts tend to be buried and fossilized at or near their life locations (; ; ). However
column8: cephalopod mollusks with chambered shells present a special challenge—once their soft parts are removed
column9: cephalopod shells can float after death and therefore be picked up and transported by surface currents (). This postmortem transport or “drift” has been observed in living nautilid species
column10: but how commonly it occurs in ancient cephalopod groups has been debated in the scientific literature for over 100 years (; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ). If postmortem transport was frequent and extensive
column11: the geographic distributions of fossil cephalopods should be considered unreliable proxies for living ranges. Biogeographic studies of fossil cephalopods require some assurance that locality data are meaningful
column12: especially as paleontologists investigate paleobiogeographic processes with more quantitative analytical methods (; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ). Also
column13: as modern nautilids experience greater fishery pressure and habitat disruption due to climate change and other anthropogenic impacts
column14: conservation efforts will need to accurately assess population distributions (; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ). It is therefore imperative to determine the likely frequency of postmortem transport in both fossil and modern shelled cephalopods.

column1: Living Nautilus in Palau. Photo source: image accessed from https://pxhere.com/en/photo/660195 under a Creative Commons CC0 license.
In the 19th and early 20th centuries
column2: some workers noted that the small geographic ranges and concentrations of undamaged fossil cephalopod shells indicated little postmortem transport
column3: while others argued that the lack of preserved soft parts (particularly for ammonoids) indicated that the shells must have drifted for some time before sinking to the seafloor (; ); it should be noted that this lack of soft parts could potentially also be explained by a high level of ammonia in the soft tissues
column4: which prevents mineralization of the decaying tissues
column5: see (). The “drift” argument was bolstered by observations in the mid-20th century of varyingly broken and encrusted shells of modern Nautilus on beaches and other coastal settings in Australia ()
column6: Japan ()
column7: Thailand (; )
column8: and the Bay of Bengal (). These authors inferred from these observations that Nautilus shells were being transported by ocean currents away from living populations
column9: likely in the Philippines
column10: Indonesia
column11: and New Caledonia.  provided a review of much of this earlier literature. Reyment claimed that postmortem drift was common in both modern and fossil shelled cephalopods (which he described as the minority viewpoint at the time) and investigated the buoyancy of empty shells to determine which shell characteristics were related to greater buoyancy and therefore postmortem transport (see “Cephalopod shell buoyancy” below). Reyment continued throughout his career to argue vigorously that postmortem drift in cephalopods was “the rule rather than the exception” (; ; ) and influenced the position of many other paleontologists (e.g.
column12: ; ; ; ) as well as biologists (e.g.
column13: ). For example
column14: claimed that modern nautilid shells “often” rise to the surface after death and are “often dispersed by currents–sometimes on an impressive scale...thousands of miles from the habitat of the animals that build them...and similar behavior is inferred for many fossil cephalopods...” (

column1: On the other hand
column2: the extent to which postmortem transport has affected the distribution of fossil cephalopods has been questioned. Many fossil localities include very large numbers of well-preserved
column3: intact shells
column4: which is hard to explain by postmortem transport (). It is also well-established that many cephalopod taxa or morphotypes are strongly tied to particular depositional environments and sedimentary facies
column5: implying fidelity of dead shells to their living habitat (; ). Paleontologists have noted that postmortem transport is likely to remove drift shells from the fossil record entirely
column6: as they degrade over time and end up destroyed when carried by currents into high energy shoreline environments (; ; ; ; ; ; ). Fossil cephalopods
column7: therefore
column8: likely represent shells that sank soon after death.

column1: used buoyancy calculations to argue that most ammonoids
column2: especially those with shells less than 5 cm diameter or found in deeper water settings (where ambient hydrostatic pressure is high)
column3: would quickly sink to the sea floor after the removal of soft parts
column4: as water flooded the phragmocone (the chambered portion of the shell). A similar argument has been made for Paleozoic and Mesozoic nautiloids (; )
column5: although  suggested that the Cenozoic nautilid Aturia () experienced unusually extensive drift because its very long septal neck would have slowed the rate of flooding.  conducted experiments on modern Nautilus pompilius () and showed that the phragmocone floods quickly once the mantle tissue is detached
column6: especially if the shell is small. They argued that only shells over 20 cm in diameter were likely to experience significant postmortem transport
column7: consistent with their claim that observations of long-distance postmortem drift in modern nautilids—while sometimes dramatic—are actually quite rare.

column1: pointed out that we typically only encounter modern nautilid shells that have floated into nearshore settings and have little firsthand information about what happens to nautilid shells that sink quickly to the sea floor. Remarkable data from dredged specimens of Nautilus macromphalus () show nautilid shells do indeed accumulate on the sea floor in deep water
column2: near their living habitats (; ; ; ; ; ). Evidence of bioerosion and encrustation has been used as evidence for the duration of transport and exposure at the sea floor in both modern (; ; ) and fossil nautilids () and in ammonoids (). Such detailed taphonomic analyses are necessary
column3: as workers have argued that one must not assume postmortem transport but rather search for evidence of it in specific cases (; ; ; ).

column1: For a cephalopod shell to experience postmortem drift
column2: it must first become positively buoyant after the death of the animal. Numerous workers have investigated the buoyancy of cephalopod shells
column3: both during life and after death
column4: via physical experiments and mathematical models (; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; )
column5: with new imaging and computational techniques (; ; ; ) enabling ever more sophisticated analyses. Workers have established that
column6: in life
column7: modern nautilids are generally neutral to slightly negatively buoyant (; ). Newly formed chambers of the phragmocone are emptied of liquid slowly
column8: at a rate of about 1 mL per chamber per day
column9: via the siphuncle
column10: a tube of tissue extending back from the soft body of the animal through openings in the septal walls that define the chambers (). Modern nautilids are capable of partially refilling empty chambers in 10 to 30 h (for a rate of about 2 mL/day) in response to sudden increases in buoyancy
column11: for instance
column12: if some of its shell is removed by a predator (). This partial refilling
column13: though
column14: may be insufficient for the animal to regain neutral buoyancy.

column1: After a shelled cephalopod dies and some or all of its soft parts are removed
column2: and assuming it died at a shallow enough depth not to sink immediately (; )
column3: the shell will become positively buoyant and float until the phragmocone is filled by seawater moving through the siphuncular opening (assuming the phragmocone is intact) (). A critical question is how quickly this flooding of the phragmocone happens.  conducted taphonomic experiments in which freshly killed N. pompilius in the Philippines were set on deep (320 m) and shallow (50 m) seafloors. Soft parts were left within the shells. They found that flooding of the phragmocone did not occur immediately postmortem
column4: but that most shells at both depths were flooded within 3 (shallow) to 7 (deep) days
column5: as the mantle’s attachment to the last septum failed. In the few days before flooding of the phragmocone began
column6: the dead cephalopod presumably remained neutral to slightly negatively buoyant
column7: as in life; the dead animal would therefore not be transported by surface currents during this interval
column8: but some transport via deeper-water currents might occur (although ’s  experimental design did not test for this). Flooding began when decaying mantle tissue pulled away from the back of the body chamber; presumably removal of soft parts by predation or scavenging would hasten the initiation of chamber filling.  further argued that the rate of flooding would be a function of the radius
column9: thickness
column10: and length of the siphuncular tube. Since fossil ammonoids possessed a siphuncle 1.5 to 3 times as long as that of modern N. pompilius
column11: they predicted that ammonoid phragmocones would flood faster than modern nautilids.

column1: explicitly connected variations in shell form to the likelihood of positive postmortem buoyancy in ammonoids. He used modern nautilid shells and plastic models of a variety of fossil cephalopod shell morphotypes in laboratory experiments to identify how differing shell shapes produced different degrees of buoyancy in the empty shells. Plastic models were constructed based on molds of actual fossil specimens. These included three ceratite ammonoid taxa (Ceratites (Acanthoceratites) spinosus
column2: Ceratites nodosus ()
column3: and Discoceratites sp. (no author given))
column4: which are similar in most shell parameters and ornamentation
column5: but show a continuum of more compressed (Discoceratites sp.) to more inflated (C. (A.) spinosus) whorl shapes. Flotation experiments were conducted on these models to determine how much weight would need to be added to cause the floating empty shells to sink.  found that the ceratite shells with more inflated whorl sections were more buoyant
column6: that is
column7: more weight needed to be added to the shell to counteract buoyancy forces and cause the shell to sink. The ceratite models showed that these shell shapes would have required more water in their phragmocone chambers when alive than modern Nautilus pompilius does
column8: in order to maintain neutral buoyancy.  attributed this difference to the ceratites’ shells being more evolute and with a longer body chamber that Nautilus.

column1: Having found experimentally that shell parameters like coiling
column2: inflation
column3: and body chamber length are important determinants of postmortem buoyancy
column4: argued that quantitative analysis of cephalopod shell shape would provide a more efficient way of investigating the hydrostatics of fossil shells than the time-consuming and expensive physical modeling and tank experiments he conducted. To demonstrate this
column5: he performed a principal coordinates analysis on five shell shape parameters (shell diameter
column6: maximum inflation
column7: body chamber length
column8: ventral inflation
column9: and umbilical width) for 42 Mesozoic ammonoid species. The position of each species on a plot of the first two principal coordinates indicates its overall shell shape (). Based on his experimental results
column10: Reyment then mapped out on the plot which shell shapes would have more or less buoyancy postmortem
column11: that is
column12: need more or less water flooding into the phragmocone to sink. He concluded that serpenticones (narrow shells with a wide umbilicus
column13: plotting high on the second principal axis) and oxycones (disc-shaped shells with a high whorl expansion rate
column14: plotting high on the first principal axis but low on the second principal axis) were less buoyant while the more inflated spherocone shells (plotting low on the first principal axis) showed higher postmortem buoyancy. Hence
column15: it can be predicted that spheroconic ammonoids would be more likely to experience extensive postmortem transport.

column1: While Reyment himself insisted that postmortem drift was pervasive among all fossil cephalopods
column2: his own work  suggested a more complex relationship between shell form
column3: postmortem buoyancy
column4: and transport potential. ’s  morphospace for Mesozoic ammonoids () closely resembles the ternary Westermann Morphospace devised by  to capture morphological variations in regularly coiled ammonoids ()
column5: with three end-member forms representing serpenticones
column6: oxycones
column7: and spherocones. This resemblance is understandable given the overlap in shell shape parameters that the two approaches use to construct their morphospaces. Westermann Morphospace has the advantage of being expressed in a standardized frame of reference
column8: making comparisons among any taxa possible
column9: in contrast to more traditional factor analytic techniques that define axes that apply only to the specimens used to define them. Westermann Morphospace
column10: then
column11: becomes a useful framework to assess the link between shell form and postmortem transport potential.

column1: Schematic representation of Westermann Morphospace () as a ternary diagram. Each corner of the diagram represents the maximum value for one of the three shell shape parameters used to construct the morphospace: umbilical ratio U (serpenticones)

column1: Given the ongoing debate about the frequency of postmortem transport in both fossil and modern shelled cephalopods and the importance of this question to our understanding of the geographic distributions of these groups
column2: the present research has three goals:

column1: To determine the relationship between cephalopod shell form and postmortem transport potential
To test the hypothesis that fossil cephalopods with higher postmortem transport potential will show artificially larger geographic ranges than cephalopods with lower transport potential
To assess the extent of postmortem transport in modern nautilid species.
Ultimately
column2: the intention of this work is to enable fossil cephalopod workers to use shell forms to better predict the likelihood of postmortem transport and to evaluate their confidence in the use of geographic ranges derived from fossil localities as a proxy for the living geographic ranges of the groups under study. In addition
column3: analysis of the geographic distribution of modern living and dead drift nautilid shells will help researchers better evaluate the geospatial extent of these charismatic but threatened species.

These normalized parameters were used to construct ternary plots of Westermann Morphospace. Each corner of the ternary plot represents the maximum end member value for one of the three shell shape parameters. Individual shell forms plot within the triangular morphospace based on the values of these three parameters.
Westermann Morphospace provides a standardized framework for comparing the morphology of any normally coiled cephalopods in a reproducible way

column1: Two approaches were used to assess the prediction that taxa with more buoyant postmortem shell forms would be more likely to drift and therefore have larger geographic ranges. First
column2: the parameter Th was determined to be predictive of whether a taxon was likely to be buoyant or non-buoyant postmortem (see Results below
column3: ). Correlations between Th and geographic range were calculated separately for Late Cenomanian and Early Turonian genera and tested for significance (). To check for the influence of taxa with small geographic ranges
column4: which could potentially represent sampling or collecting failures
column5: correlations were calculated for the full datasets and after excluding taxa with smaller ranges (<1
column6: 000 sq km). It should be noted that many of these taxa with small geographic ranges come from well-sampled regions (such as the Western Interior Seaway of North America and the European Platform) and therefore are more likely to reflect truly restricted ranges than sampling bias; in any case
column7: these narrow ranges do not support the notion of extensive postmortem transport. Second
column8: the distributions of geographic ranges for buoyant vs. non-buoyant genera were visualized and Mann–Whitney U-tests used to test for significant differences in the medians of these distributions (). All statistical tests were conducted in PAST 3.15 ().

column1: One complicating factor in assessing the causes of geographic range size is the potential swimming ability of the taxa investigated (). In  construction of Westermann Morphospace
column2: they mapped different interpreted modes of life onto their ternary diagram
column3: with good nektonic swimmers represented in the oxyconic portion of the morphospace
column4: vertical migrants with poor horizontal swimming ability in the spherocone region
column5: and planktonic drifters in the serpenticone region. Demersal groups fell in a curved band separating the serpenticone corner from the rest of the morphospace. In order to control for the possible confounding relationship of geographic range size with mode of life
column6: Late Cenomanian and Early Turonian genera were assigned to one of three categories (nektonic
column7: demersal
column8: or vertical migrant) based on their position within Westermann Morphospace. No genera fell within the planktonic region; 19 genera falling near the center of this morphospace were excluded as their mode of life could not be unambiguously assigned. The non-parametric Kruskal–Wallis test was used to determine whether median geographic range was significantly different among modes of life.

column1: It is interesting to note that Reyment’s own buoyancy experiments () showed that fossil cephalopods had a range of postmortem transport potentials
column2: contradicting his persistent claim that most cephalopods experienced extensive transport (). The underlying rationale for Reyment’s insistence that postmortem drift in shelled cephalopods is the norm was his claim that modern nautilids are known to frequently experience postmortem drift across long distances. This claim is based on observed occurrences of living nautilid specimens and beached shells interpreted as having drifted away from living populations as reported in  and . As noted in the Introduction
column3: while drift has been observed
column4: actual evidence for long-distance transport of nautilid shells is actually relatively rare ().

column1: The results presented here support the claim that the majority of ammonoid taxa (including both Paleozoic and Mesozoic groups) would have been considerably less likely to experience postmortem transport than modern nautilids. Only the subset of ammonoid taxa with relatively inflated shells would experience sufficient postmortem buoyancy to float for any length of time
column2: a prerequisite for extensive transport away from their living habitat. Further
column3: this conclusion does not consider that other factors
column4: such as the depth at which the animal died
column5: could further diminish the likelihood of postmortem drift
column6: as animals in deeper water are more likely to become waterlogged and sink more quickly (; ). Based on evidence from Late Cretaceous ammonoids
column7: genera with shell forms conducive to postmortem floating did not have larger geographic ranges ()
column8: indicating that even those fossil cephalopods with a greater chance of postmortem transport did not likely actually experience extensive movement away from their living habitat. It should be noted that such shell forms are associated with a vertical migrant life mode (). The lack of significantly larger geographic ranges in such taxa indicates that neither transport during life nor postmortem drift markedly increased their observed geographic distributions. These results are consistent with previous workers’ arguments for the rarity of extensive postmortem transport of fossil cephalopods
column9: based on taphonomic evidence (; ; ; ; ; ; ; ) and buoyancy considerations (; ; ; ). They also provide a measure of confidence that fossil locality data are a robust proxy for living biogeographic ranges.

column1: The notion that postmortem transport was common in fossil cephalopods ultimately derives from the claim that modern nautilids show frequent and extensive postmortem drift. However
column2: drifted nautilid shells are actually fairly rare () and only one extant nautilid species
column3: N. pompilius
column4: shows widely distributed drift shells. The limited geographic distributions of most nautilid species are consistent with evidence for genetic divergence of geographically separated populations (; ; ; ; ; ; ). The wider distribution of N. pompilius may be real or the result of pooling multiple genetically distinct populations into single named species. N. pompilius does appear to contain more genetic variation than other nautilid species
column5: and may be paraphyletic
column6: with N. stenomphalus and N. belauensis falling within N. pompilius sensu latu (; ; ; ). Hence
column7: the wide distribution of N. pompilius drift shells could actually be caused by the conflation of smaller drift ranges of separate populations.

column1: The abundance of drifted N. pompilius shells in the western Indian Ocean is particularly remarkable
column2: as these sites are quite distant from known living populations (). Both  and  argued that the N. pompilius shells that wash up in some numbers on beaches in Kenya and Mozambique must have drifted across the entire Indian Ocean from living populations in the Philippines. Under this view
column3: floating shells would need to move from the Philippines into the eastern Indian Ocean basin
column4: where they could then be picked up by the westward flowing North or South Equatorial surface currents. This mechanism could conceivably bring floating shells across the Indian Ocean to Africa
column5: but would require the shells to remain relatively undamaged (so as to continue floating) for a journey of over 6
column6: 000 km and to avoid being caught up in the eastward flowing Equatorial Countercurrent or the Indian Ocean gyre lying south of the equatorial currents (). In addition to these obstacles
column7: it is difficult to understand why this one species of nautilid would show such different postmortem behavior than its close relatives. As an alternative
column8: it might be possible that these African drift shells are actually being sourced from living populations much closer
column9: in the western Indian Ocean basin (; ; ). Live Nautilus (presumed to be N. pompilius) have been reported from Madagascar () and from Mauritius and the Seychelles (by Dr. Anna Bidder
column10: cited in )
column11: and late Pleistocene fossils of Nautilus (likely N. pompilius) have been identified on the Bajuni Islands off the Somali coast (). The distances from the isolated islands of the western Indian Ocean to the African mainland are more consistent with the distances traveled by dead shells of the other nautilid species. The possibility of living N. pompilius populations within the western Indian Ocean basin is therefore worthy of further investigation

column13: Mauritius

column18: like those described by  from Somalia

column21: which would allow the placement of these shells in the larger phylogenetic context of nautilids throughout the Indo-Pacific region.

column1: While postmortem transport of cephalopod shells in modern and fossil contexts is frequently assumed to have been both frequent and extensive
column2: evidence supporting this view is lacking. Most fossil ammonoids had shell forms that resulted in low postmortem buoyancy; only highly inflated shells were likely to float for significant periods after death. An analysis of early Late Cretaceous (Cenomanian-Turonian) cephalopod genera shows that observed geographic range size was not related to likelihood of postmortem transport
column3: indicating that these observed fossil ranges are adequate proxies for living geographic ranges. Most living nautilid species have relatively small geographic ranges with limited dispersal of drift shells. The exception
column4: Nautilus pompilius
column5: is widespread throughout the Indo-Pacific
column6: with drift shells found apparently great distances from known living populations. However
column7: drift shells found along the east African coast may in reality be derived from cryptic living populations among the isolated islands of the western Indian Ocean.

column1: 72|10.7717/peerj.5899|Trait-based predation suitability offers insight into effects of changing prey communities| Weigel  Benjamin |2018-11-6|"Community reorganizations
column2: due to natural and anthropogenic pressures on the environment
column3: not only reshuffle species compositions
column4: but also affect species interactions and trophic dynamics (). What makes prey species suitable and/or favourable for their predators has been a central question in ecology for over half a century (; ) and with accelerating rates of changing communities and shifting interactions (; ; )
column5: it is still a relevant one.

column1: Besides purely size-structured approaches (; )
column2: the ‘who eats whom’ is usually described through empirical observations such as gut content analysis (; ; )
column3: stable isotope measurements (; )
column4: or fatty acid measurements (; )
column5: resulting in species-based trophic links. This type of data is usually temporally restricted and often limited to commercially relevant species due to high sampling and analysis efforts as well as costs. Although a pure taxonomy-based approach is the fundament of linking predator and prey
column6: it neglects the key ecological information on why a species appears relevant as prey in the first place. Instead of being determined by taxonomic classifications
column7: predation is more likely to be driven by the morphological and behavioural characteristics of a prey species than by its name (). Hence
column8: taxonomic affiliations of species offer only restricted information regarding their suitability as prey or their ecological role in general. During the past decades
column9: the perception of taxonomic limitations in answering ecological questions has led to a steep increase in studies applying different measures linked to functional traits of species
column10: rather than taxonomic related indices
column11: when investigating ecosystem processes or species interactions (; ; ; ).

column1: Although it seems straightforward to include morphological and behavioural attributes describing prey
column2: this approach has so far been applied in relatively few studies. Using trait-based approaches in the framework of feeding ecology has contributed significantly to a more complete understanding of predator-prey (PP) relationships and prey selectivity (; ; ; ) through prey vulnerability traits
column3: predator foraging traits
column4: and the interaction of both prey and predator traits (; ; ).

column1: In dynamically changing ecosystems
column2: strong environmental drivers
column3: human impacts
column4: and invasive species may rapidly alter community characteristics and biotic interactions (; ; ; ). Bottom trawl fishing
column5: for example
column6: has been highlighted as one particular human pressure that can impact the composition of benthic prey communities
column7: leading to altered predator conditions by changing the prey availability (; ). Trait-based approaches seem to be a promising tool to assess changing food conditions on community levels (). Yet to our knowledge
column8: there is no available framework to evaluate how changing prey may affect the suitability of food resources of a predator community over time and/or space. Here we suggest a novel concept to assess how changes in prey communities may influence food resource conditions for their predators based on a trait-suitability measure linked to the community weighted mean (CWM) traits of prey. We apply our approach on a marine coastal system in the Baltic Sea
column9: to find out if changes in zoobenthos community structure influenced the food resource suitability (RS) for the benthic feeding fish community. While we focus on the link between zoobenthic prey and benthic-feeding fish in this study
column10: the concept is applicable to all PP systems. The broad spectrum of phenotypic variation in zoobenthos makes it an ideal group to illustrate our approach. For example
column11: many zoobenthos species can be inaccessible for their potential predators as they may be buried too deep in the sediment
column12: have strong physical protection or are not within the right size spectrum to be consumed. In contrast
column13: other zoobenthos may serve as easy prey items for visual hunters when occurring epibenthically
column14: that is
column15: on or just above the sediment surface
column16: and being motile with no physical protection such as hard shells.

column1: In a system where the link between zoobenthos and fish is strong (; )

column1: Among the first monuments to be established through the federal Antiquities Act of 1906 was Grand Canyon National Monument by President Theodore Roosevelt in 1908 (). Not long after the establishment of that monument

column2: President William J. Clinton designated 1.9 million acres in southern Utah as Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument (GSENM) (). The justification for creating this monument was in part a recognition that this area houses unique geological
column3: paleontological

column2: Kaiparowits Plateau

column2: President Donald J. Trump issued a proclamation that reduced the size of GSENM by nearly 50% and divided the monument into three distinct management units ()

column1: The typical capture site for A. palloni is rocky reefs located largely within the general 40–80-m depth range along the Swedish Skagerrak coast. Capture sites are also matching the route of the main currents bringing water from the North Sea. The bottom layer of the deeper parts of Skagerrak have a salinity similar to the North Sea and is substantially more saline than the water originating from the Baltic Sea in the upper layers and in Kattegatt to the south of Skagerrak (). This likely makes the conditions in these areas suitable for marine species with a main distribution area in more saline waters
column2: such as A. palloni.

column2: Canary Islands
column3: Madeira and Cape Verde) suggests that there is capacity for dispersal in the species. However
column4: the temporal aspect of dispersal appears largely unknown. The angled specimens are all captured between May and October
column5: which represents the time when the angling activity is highest. Given paucity of data from the winter-months
column6: we cannot exclude that the species is a seasonally migratory species in this geographic area. An additional unverified underwater observation was made in November during a scientific expedition at Persgrunden (record #4; ); but November-temperatures at >25 m depth largely matches spring- to early-summer temperatures ()
column7: so such an observation does not indicate a non-migratory behavior. However

column11: which is relatively inefficient for long-distance swimming ()
column12: a seasonal migratory life-style seems unlikely. Notably

column1: The recent surge in records could be an indication of a range extension. One hypothetical factor that could lead to range extensions is the changing climate which leads to higher seawater temperatures (). While increased sea water temperature was also noted from 1939 to 1960
column2: the warming effect has been particularly strong in the north–east Atlantic during the last decades (). The estimated temperature increase in Skagerrak
column3: from 1990 to 2014
column4: is 1.6 °C (). Range extensions has indeed been indicated in several European marine areas
column5: including Scandinavian waters (; ; ).  estimated that more than 500 marine benthic species expanded their range northwards in Norwegian waters
column6: in the range of 750–1
column7: 000 km
column8: between 1997 and 2010. Furthermore
column9: several new species were found to establish along the Norwegian Skagerrak coast during this time (; ). Even though the angling records are located south of the previous northern range limit
column10: the eastern Skagerrak is still at the edge of the species’ northern distribution range (AquaMaps presents an estimated native distribution map that extends to the Faroe Islands
column11: but this is not supported by actual records of the species; ; ). Hence
column12: a climate-related population increase at the northern distribution range may cause spillover effects into the Swedish waters of Skagerrak
column13: following the main currents in this area (cf.  and ). However
column14: the paucity of historical data on the species makes it impossible to conclude whether this is the case or not. The fact that the species was recorded in south-western Norway already in 1869 ()
column15: with further observations along the south coast of Norway noted prior to 1942 ()
column16: suggests that it was present
column17: at least temporarily
column18: close to Swedish waters prior to the recent increase in the seawater temperature. It is also very hard to distinguish climate effects from other effects such as fishing or meta-population dynamics ().

column1: This study presents a case where citizen generated data can be used for increasing the knowledge about a rare species. It is possible that recognition of the angler community as a valuable source of information may increase anglers’ interest in continuing and increasing the frequency and detail of their reports. Anglers are often keen to participate in the management of fish stocks

column3: so this type of recognition may lead to a further increased interest in fish conservation issues (; ). From a conservation and management perspective

column1: In this article
column2: we summarize the present knowledge about the current distribution of A. palloni in Swedish waters
column3: at the edge of the distribution range of the species. The records of A. palloni presented here in particular highlight the importance of citizen-generated data in the form of anglers’ reports and angling records as useful contributions for ichthyological investigations of presence and distribution of non-commercial fish species.

column1: The rise in shark-human interactions has also led to the emergence of many new personal shark deterrents. The rapid commercial availability of these deterrents has preceded rigorous and peer-reviewed studies to test the effectiveness of these devices
column2: meaning that manufacturers are making claims about their products without rigorous scientific evidence to back them up. If deterrents were not as effective as advertised
column3: it could potentially give users a false sense of security
column4: leading some people to put themselves at greater risk of shark interactions than they normally would because of their reliance on these devices. For example
column5: some surfers and spearfishers might ignore other mitigation measures
column6: such as beach closures
column7: because they feel safe when wearing these products. Whether they are or not is what we aim to demonstrate in this study.

column1: Surfing has been suggested as an activity that exposes people to sharks more than others
column2: because many ideal surfing locations are regions that overlap with the habitats of potentially dangerous sharks
column3: the amount of time surfers spend in the water relative to most other bathers
column4: surfers’ distance from shore
column5: isolation

column11: USA between 1982 and 2013 occurred during surfing activities
column12: while surfing has also been implicated in 53% of shark bites in Brazil since 1992 () and two-thirds of shark bites in Reunion Island (). This has resulted in recent development of personal deterrents to decrease the risk of shark bites to surfers.

column1: Our aims were to test the effectiveness of surfing-specific personal shark deterrents and quantify the behavioural response of sharks exposed to these deterrents. We tested the effects of five deterrents (two electric
column2: two magnetic
column3: and one olfactory-based) on the behaviour of white sharks (Carcharodon carcharias) and determined if these deterrents reduce the likelihood of white sharks consuming a bait. Specifically
column4: we assessed and compared the effects of each deterrent on (1) the number of passes to a bait
column5: (2) the minimum distance between a bait and the sharks
column6: (3) the percentage of bait taken
column7: and (4) whether shark behaviour changed with increased exposure to the deterrent (e.g.
column8: whether sharks became habituated to the deterrent). We also assessed the effect size that the trials could detect statistically (power analysis) to gauge the experiment’s ability to identify small behavioural changes if present.

column1: In Australia
column2: the white shark is responsible for the most unprovoked bites (19.5% of all shark bites) and the most fatalities (34%) (). Our study focused on white sharks because interactions with this species are considered to be a worst-case scenario during which the deterrent is subjected to the most dangerous species. We did all deterrent-testing trials at the Neptune Islands Group Marine Park (35°149S
column4: ∼30 km off the southern coast of South Australia. The area supports the largest colony of fur seals (Arctocephalus forsteri) in Australia (; )
column5: and is considered an aggregation site for white sharks (). Commercial shark-cage diving has taken place here since the late 1970s and is the only location where such activity is permitted in Australia (). We chose this area because of the high likelihood of shark interactions. We did the deterrent testing over 18 days and five separate trips between September 2017 and January 2018.

column1: This project was done under the Department of Environment

column3: we tested each deterrent a total of 50 times within a series of trials. A series of trials consisted of testing each of the five deterrents and the control board (total six trials) in a randomised sequence. This led to a total of 300 trials (50 trials for each of the five deterrents and control).

column1: We attracted white sharks by disbursing into the water a mix of fish oil and minced southern bluefin tuna Thunnus maccoyii (i.e.
column2: berley or chum) and by deploying sections of tuna attached to a float ∼15 m from the stern of the vessel. Trials commenced after a white shark was sighted near the vessel at least twice within five minutes or when a shark showed consistent interest in the tuna section. We removed and replaced the tuna section with the deterrent set-up
column3: which we deployed when the shark had left the proximity of the vessel. Each trial consisted of the deployment of a tuna gill (∼2 kg)
column4: referred to as the ‘bait’
column5: which we attached ∼30 cm beneath the board to replicate the typical distance between a surfer’s foot and the board when surfers sit on their board to wait for a wave. The deterrent set-up was between 5 and 15 m from the vessel and varied depending on wind
column6: swell
column7: tide
column8: and glare conditions
column9: to ensure that observers could identify sharks and record behaviour. We ran trials for 15 minutes or until a shark touched the bait or board with an intent to consume the bait. We repeated trials during which a shark did not approach the board with an intent to take the bait to ensure that the results were not biased by trials during which sharks did not attempt to consume the bait.

column1: We determined sex based on clasper presence and measured total body length using EventMeasure. We did not include the effect of shark sex or size in the analysis because potential differences here were beyond the scope of our study. Instead
column2: we included individual shark (shark ID) as a random effect in the models (see below) to account statistically for individual shark behaviour that was independent of deterrent effects (i.e.
column3: some sharks might be more inclined to approach closer or more frequently than others). Prior to analysis

column1: The data we collected had two potential analytical biases: temporal correlation and pseudo-replication when the same shark interacted with the deterrent set-up several times within and across trials. For example
column2: sharks might become habituated to the deterrent through repeated exposure. Sharks that consumed the bait might also become less likely to respond to the deterrent because of the positive reinforcement provided by the bait. We investigated whether the effectiveness of the deterrents changed throughout the study (e.g.
column3: sharks becoming habituated to the deterrents) by including ‘trial’ as a fixed integer covariate in the models (i.e.
column4: ignoring the real elapsed time between successive exposures
column5: but including the information indicating relative serial time; e.g.
column6: 2 followed 1) and by plotting the mean distance between the shark and the board
column7: and the number of passes across trials for sharks that interacted with the board for 15 trials or more.

column1: We minimised potential pseudo-replication by testing the effects of deterrents on all response variables using a generalised linear mixed-effects model (GLMM) with individual shark coded as a random effect
column2: and the deterrent used as the fixed effect. We also included trial as fixed-integer effect and the interaction between trial and deterrent to account for potential temporal effects. We included individual shark as a random effect to account for the potential lack of independence in behaviour within individual sharks. We excluded those passes for which we could not identify the shark from this analysis (117 out of 1
column3: 413 passes; 8.2%). We determined the most appropriate statistical family and error distribution for each analysis by examining the distribution of the response variable and visually inspecting the residuals for the saturated models. We ran all models for all possible combinations of factors
column4: and compared their relative probability using Akaike’s information criterion corrected for small sample size (AICc) (). The bias-corrected relative weight of evidence for each model
column5: given the data and the suite of candidate models considered
column6: was the AICc weight; the smaller the weight
column7: the lower the probability the model was ‘true’ (). We also calculated the marginal R2 of each resampled GLMM (Rm) as a measure of goodness of fit and the contribution of the fixed effects to explaining variance in the response variable (). Behavioural studies on large predatory sharks like white sharks can be logistically challenging and expensive because it can be difficult to do a large number of trials given the inherently low abundance of sharks and their unpredicatibility in interacting with experimental equipment. Power analyses like ours will enable future studies to consider the number of trials required to assess the efficacy of shark deterrents.

column1: The rise in the number of shark bites worldwide and in Australia has led to global development of shark-mitigation measures and the commercial availability of several personal deterrents. Our study clearly shows that while some products are capable of affecting the behaviour of sharks and can reduce the risk of a shark bite
column2: others did not have the advertised outcome. Manufacturers should consider these results to assess the suitability of their products and gauge whether changes are required to ensure their intended performance. Our results will allow private and government agencies to make informed decisions about the use of these devices for occupational activities and enable the public to make appropriate decisions about the use and suitability of these five products.

column2: snowfall is ubiquitous. Specifically
column3: along the Front Range of eastern Colorado
column4: the mean annual snowfall from 2010 to 2016 is reported as 248 cm in Boulder
column5: Colorado (). Despite snow’s prevalence
column6: little is known about the biological composition of this massive annual hydrological deposit; meteoric snow is an essential part of yearly moisture input in Boulder
column7: Colorado given that it represents 43% of the total precipitation (by liquid water volume) from 2010 to 2016 (). Both snow and rain require an initiation surface for atmospheric water to condense into a droplet of water or ice particle
column8: respectively. Typically
column9: these are thought to be airborne particles of particulate matter
column10: both organic and inorganic of various colloidal sizes (e.g.
column11: 0.5–8 μm) (; ). Bioaerosols
column12: defined as particles of a biological nature released from both terrestrial and marine ecosystems into the receiving atmosphere
column13: are considered to be capable of acting as initiation surfaces (). Recently
column14: bioaerosols have also been suggested as an understudied component of both atmospheric processes and biogeographical fate ().

column1: Bioaerosols are most famous for their potential detrimental effects upon human health from contaminated indoor or other built-environments (e.g.
column2: mold and fungal spores) (). However
column3: even in a benign
column4: typical
column5: outdoor/atmospheric environment
column6: bioaerosols are common and the literature reports source environments ()
column7: seasonal effects (; )
column8: and diurnal shifts () as factors in atmospheric bioaerosol composition. Furthermore
column9: bioaerosols are known to be components of the estimated three Pg annual dust traffic around the globe (; ; ). Work has approached the theory that bioaersols are important to climate as a whole () by examining in silico models of microbiological dispersion in the atmosphere (). With respect to precipitation
column10: the effects of rainfall additions to soil communities has already been shown to be of microbiological community significance at the soil/atmosphere interface (). Additionally
column11: these works suggest that snowfall
column12: in particular
column13: is even more important in the deposition of some strains of ice-nucleating (IN) bacteria Pseudomonas syringae (also a plant pathogen) ()—from which the man-made snow-making additive Snomax™ derives (). In vivo studies of both atmospheric (suspended as an aerosol) and hail (within the hailstone) microorganisms have shown them to be metabolically active (; ;
column15: suggesting that the biomass in the environment from which snowfall derives is not of inconsequence and that microbiota can remain metabolically active throughout the rain/ice/snow nucleation process. Save for some work on liquid precipitation ()
column16: what microbiota within ice/snow look like when they contact soil and surface receiving waters remains unknown. Beyond the community structure of precipitation microbiota
column17: it is especially unknown how these structures changes as a result of geography and atmospheric events.

column1: Bioaerosol literature
column2: in general
column3: is best represented by reports of in silico bioaerosol movement
column4: aerosol microbial composition and studies of canonical surface constituents such as glaciers and snowpack (; ; ; ; ). There exists little specific information
column5: however
column6: on the microbiology of snowfall precipitation. The biological composition of snowfall—a global meteorological event—should be well understood vis-à-vis magnitude and community structure
column7: especially given that precipitation is key to uncovering atmospheric/surface interactions. An aid to such study is the preponderance of weather information available that tracks storm events in multiple-spectral analyses; these data further inform storm/biome/hydrology source locations.

column1: To this end
column2: we sampled fresh snowfall throughout the entirety of the 2016–2017 snow season (snow seasons are typically October–May) along the Front Range of eastern Colorado. Our investigations include disparate sampling sites (one rural mountain
column3: and one urban plain) to help in the characterization of snowfall with respect to both sampling location—within the same storm—and direction of origin of the storm. We hypothesized that the biological content of fresh snowfall would be uniquely distinguishable with respect to sampling location and that storm tracking could provide novel insight into other factors that may affect biogeography. Here
column4: we apply ecological investigative approaches together with remote sensing based atmospheric storm analyses to assess the magnitude of biological deposition
column5: community structure
column6: and abiotic solute associations in fresh snowfall.

column1: Snow storms associated with sample collection were tracked with the NOAA ARL (Air Resource Laboratory) HYSPLIT Trajectory Model (; ). A trajectory ensemble was generated for each storm by creating a set of tracking vectors centered about the latitude and longitude of each sampling site. Models are backward trajectories and encapsulate storm tracking up to 24 h prior to the day of the snow storm event. Backward trajectories began at 500 m AGL (above ground level) from sampling sites.

Filtrate from melted snow (0.45-μm pore size) was subsampled to two 15 mL aliquots for ion chromatography (IC; major anions) and inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES; major cations) analysis. ICP-AES samples were acidified with three drops of trace metal grade 10% nitric acid. IC analysis was performed on a Dionex ICS-900 IC system running an AG14A-5 μm RFIC (3 × 30 mm) guard column and an AS14A-5 μm RFIC (3 × 150 mm) column
column2: while ICP-AES was performed on a Perkin–Elmer 8300 ICP-AES.

column1: Herein
column2: we have chosen to use the total estimated biomass of annual snowfall over more traditional measures like copy numbers of 16S rRNA because we believe that it is a more tractable unit when assessing large-scale mass transport and deposition. It is our assessment that the importance of our work is best demonstrated by the surprising bio- load of meteoric snow (and its associated genetic bank)—and that whether total estimated cell count or total biomass is the unit of discussion (both of which we report) is tangential to the overall finding that microbiota and their genomes are a substantial component of fresh snowfall.

column1: Broad questions on the impact of snowfall-deposited microbiology on both public health and ecosystem function can be generated by the work we describe herein. With respect to public health

column3: rain

column1: The flux in genetic diversity brought about by fresh snowfall is in much need of greater study. The amount of gDNA deposited by snow per hectare of land suggests that gene flow
column2: via known mechanisms of horizontal gene transfer ()
column3: is likely widely seeded by continual deposition from storm events. While our work does not specify the live/dead state of microbiological communities in fresh snow
column4: these communities possess genetic banks irrespective of their activity. Mechanisms of horizontal gene transfer are dependent on live vs. dead cells
column5: but it’s important to note that natural transformation can occur under either the live/dead state. Though we cannot specify any magnitude to which horizontal gene transfer occurs between live/live or live/dead pairs due to snow
column6: natural transformation is indeed known to be possible (). Ecosystem in situ genetic diversity is thus likely to be in a state of higher flux than previously acknowledged
column7: with receiving ecosystems likely more capable of responding to dynamic environmental conditions with updated “genetic banks
column8: ” literally always raining upon them. As we argue that the biomass within meteoric snow is ecologically impactful
column9: we acknowledge that one may posit that what is deposited in snow would be just a fraction of the biomass already existing at the soil/hydrologic interface. We point out that meteoric snow would certainly be less consequential if it were uniformly distributed to all surface communities. However
column10: microbiota within snow are highly heterogeneous (even within the same storm) and alternate genetic banks are deposited in different regions
column11: some of which may be native to an area already—but many are likely to be non-local. Within oceanic systems
column12: show that up to 47% of culturable bacteria are confirmed gene recipients by known mechanisms of horizontal gene transfer; if 1 year of snowfall biomass represents even 0.1% of the cells within the top one cm of receiving soil (assuming 1 × 109 cells cm−3)

column14: a substantial portion of that entire one cm layer (and more) would be the beneficiary of new genetic material—accelerating genome innovation (). Bacteria/archaea genomic innovation and resiliency has largely been driven by horizontal gene transfer () and we expect that environmental systems comprised of these organisms are likely to depend upon this mechanism for survival. Since lateral gene transfer increases genomic diversity at rates much higher than in situ evolution alone ()
column15: communities that receive higher fluxes of genetic material are likely to be more resilient than those that receive very little. We demonstrate in our investigation that geographic areas receive distinct biological input. This suggests that “genetic banks” received by surfaces during snow storms send different locales on alternate genetic uptake and evolutionary pathways. Genetic transport by snowfall (and rain/ice/humidity) disallow microbial communities at any given geography to remain isolated. In an Earth system increasingly affected by climate change
column16: the ability of local microbial communities to adapt will become paramount; atmospheric microbial transfer and snow/rain/icing/humidity events should be considered essential components in delivering novel genetic material to geographic areas. The ability for soil/water receiving microbial systems to remain resilient will have demonstrable effects on downstream ecological pathways such as macroscopic life and the mediation of soil/water chemical equilibrium—including the management of contaminants. The “everything is everywhere” notion of microbial distribution across the globe can likely be better understood by the interaction of Earth’s compartments (lithosphere
column17: hydrosphere
column18: and atmosphere) via transport. On-going work with metagenomics
column19: transcriptomics and single-cell genomics should better inform the surface ecosystem-altering effects of the genetic potential of storm delivered events.

78|10.7717/peerj.5904|Soil moisture dynamics under two rainfall frequency treatments drive early spring CO 2 gas exchange of lichen-dominated biocrusts in central Spain| Baldauf  Selina |2018-11-16|Biocrusts are communities dominated by cyanobacteria
column2: algae
column3: fungi
column4: lichens
column5: and bryophytes living on the soil surface. They are a major feature in drylands worldwide
column6: with an estimated cover of around 12% of the terrestrial surface ()
column7: and act as a boundary layer between the soil and the atmosphere (; ). Biocrust constituents
column8: such as lichens and mosses
column9: are poikilohydric organisms
column10: which are dormant when desiccated and
column11: unlike vascular plants
column12: cannot actively control their water balance. Their activity time is thus limited to hydration events with water inputs from the atmosphere in the form of rain
column13: fog
column14: dew
column15: water vapor
column16: or melting snow (; ). Despite their restricted activity time
column17: biocrusts play an important role in the carbon cycle of drylands through their photosynthetic and respiratory activity (; ). The duration of moisture availability thereby controls the ratio of respiratory losses to photosynthetic gains (; ). Therefore
column18: larger rainfall events are generally associated with higher carbon gains
column19: whereas small rain events can result in a carbon deficit
column20: because the initial respiratory losses cannot be compensated by subsequent photosynthesis (; ; ).

column1: In addition to the importance of individual event size
column2: rainfall frequency is a key factor in determining the duration of moisture availability at the soil surface ()
column3: which affects the activity of biocrust constituents (). As an example
column4: a laboratory study with the common biocrust moss Syntrichia caninervis showed that a higher frequency of sufficiently large rainfall events is beneficial in terms of photosynthesis (). These authors found that a shift in the interval between two wetting events from 1 day to 5 days led to a decrease in the mean carbon balance by nearly 90%
column5: and became negative at an interval of 10 days. Furthermore
column6: the effect of watering patterns on the carbon flux response depends on the season considered
column7: as it plays an important role in determining the event size that is necessary to reach the compensation point for net photosynthesis (; ). Precipitation patterns can be beneficial in winter and lead to carbon losses in summer due to higher temperatures () and different physiological responses to rainfall events of the same size ().

column1: A spring to fall rainfall manipulation experiment on the Colorado Plateau (southwestern United States) showed that a 50% above-average precipitation frequency negatively impacts quantum yield

column6: soil cyanobacterial biomass and abundance also declined significantly following the second summer of altered precipitation patterns although the treatment increased overall moisture availability compared to control conditions (). In the long term

column1: Precipitation and water availability are the major driver of biocrust activity () and hence
column2: changes in precipitation patterns
column3: such as those projected for climate change in drylands worldwide ()
column4: will directly and indirectly influence the ability of biocrusts to fix and store carbon (). However
column5: we still know little about the impact of changes in precipitation on the carbon uptake across biocrust communities in different dryland regions and across seasons. In order to contribute to filling this knowledge gap
column6: we have conducted a short-term manipulative experiment in early spring to assess the effect of rainfall patterns on photosynthesis and respiration of Diploschistes diacapsis
column7: a lichen that generally is dominant in the biocrust communities in central Spain (). We compared the carbon exchange fluxes in response to two rainfall patterns that provided the same overall water amount; in one of the treatments
column8: single rainfall events were smaller
column9: but more frequent; while in the other one
column10: single rainfall events were larger
column11: but less frequent. By doing so
column12: we seek to understand which rainfall pattern could be more beneficial for carbon fixation by biocrusts

column1: The carbon balance of biocrust communities depends on the patterns of moisture availability
column2: which are likely to be altered by climate change. Therefore
column3: it is important to assess how different biocrust communities respond to an alteration in the rainfall event size and frequency and how regional and seasonal differences can influence this response. In this study
column4: we assessed how two different precipitation frequency patterns providing the same overall amount of water (i.e. 5 mm/day vs. 15 mm/3 days) affected photosynthesis and respiration of biocrusts dominated by D. diacapsis in central Spain. We observed that radiation and soil temperature
column5: together with the watering treatment
column6: affected the rates of net and gross photosynthesis
column7: as well as dark respiration.

column1: During most of the experimental period
column2: temperature was below the optimum of 22–24 °C for NP reported from D. diacapsis in Spain (). We observed the highest carbon flux rates on the days when temperatures exceeded 20 °C. However
column3: the rates of 4–5 μmol m−2 s−1 that are reported at optimum temperature () were not reached in our experiment
column4: where the maximum rates of net photosynthesis and dark respiration were ca. 3 μmol m−2 s−1. A major contribution to the difference of our measurements to the maximum net photosynthesis at optimum conditions reported in the literature is possibly the hydration status of the lichens
column5: because PAR values exceed NP compensation point of D. diacapsis at this temperature ().

column1: In addition to the different water availability in the treatments
column2: the lowered soil temperature in the high frequency samples (0.3–1 °C difference) possibly contributed to the observed differences between the treatments due to the exponential sensitivity of respiration to temperature. A difference of 1 °C in temperature can lead to a difference of around 5–8% in additive respiration of bare soil and D. diacapsis at the temperatures relevant to our study (; ;
column3: see ). However
column4: these temperature differences can only explain a small part of the observed total changes in dark respiration and net photosynthesis. Thus
column5: we conclude that the main driver of the differences is water availability but that soil temperature differences also contributed to the observed patterns.

column1: We did not find differences in the mean photosynthetic and respiratory response between the two pulse sizes evaluated (5 and 15 mm) on days when they were both applied. However
column2: on the first day of the experiment
column3: when soils and lichens were dry prior to watering
column4: respiration
column5: and net photosynthesis were higher in the samples receiving the larger pulse. The lichen D. diacapsis can achieve its maximum net photosynthesis rate at thallus water contents of around 0.5–0.75 mm precipitation equivalent (; ). There are studies showing an effect of suprasaturation at a relative water content of around 50% of the maximum water holding capacity () and others that do not show a depression of photosynthesis at suprasaturation (). Following these studies
column6: we can assume that in our experiment both pulses were large enough for moisture within the lichen to exceed this threshold. However
column7: it is unclear whether suprasaturation limited photosynthesis. An indication for a suprasaturation effect could be the fact that the larger watering pulse did not lead to a higher NP response
column8: as we would have expected. Generally
column9: larger rainfall events lead to longer periods of moist conditions and therefore
column10: more carbon can be fixed (; ).  reported higher net carbon fluxes and respiration rates from cyanobacterial-lichen crusted soils in the Gurbantunggut Desert after applying a single rainfall pulse of 15 mm compared to a 5 mm pulse applied on dry soil. On the first days of our study
column11: the 15 mm pulse led to higher net photosynthesis despite the higher respiration compared to the 5 mm pulse. The rewetting of dry biocrust communities and the underlying soil can lead to large immediate carbon losses caused by physical processes
column12: and increased respiration due to cell reparation processes (; ; ). This explains the high respiration rates on the first day despite soil moisture being lower than in the following days. The beneficial effect of the 15 mm watering pulse was not sustained throughout the experiment
column13: despite the soil moisture level after the water application being always higher for this pulse size. This indicates that not only the moisture content itself
column14: but also the condition of a sample prior to moistening plays a role in the carbon exchange response of biocrusts. Applying the larger pulse on previously wetted soil was not beneficial in comparison to a smaller pulse that was already large enough to sufficiently wet the sample to trigger photosynthetic activity.

column1: When looking at the simulated rainfall patterns
column2: we found that smaller
column3: more frequent watering pulses were beneficial in terms of net photosynthesis. The observed differences between treatments increased with differences in soil moisture
column4: and were higher during the last days of the experiment (when temperature and radiation increased). Our findings suggest a synergetic effect between the individual watering pulse size
column5: the frequency of its occurrence
column6: and other environmental factors such as antecedent moisture
column7: temperature
column8: and radiation. Apart from the size and frequency of rainfall events
column9: temperature is an important control for moisture availability in drylands () and evaporative losses increase with it. Consequently
column10: the response of biocrusts to altered precipitation frequencies is seasonally different because the water amount necessary to exceed the photosynthetic net compensation point differs between summer and winter (; ). There is evidence that
column11: apart from the indirect effect of temperature on water availability
column12: desiccation tolerance is also a seasonally dynamic physiological property in lichens and mosses (). For example
column13: the carbon balance of the biocrust moss S. caninervis was higher for the same watering event when collected in winter compared to summer despite identical laboratory conditions ().

column1: The interacting effect of season and temperature with alterations in rainfall patterns on the performance of biocrust constituents is a common observation in many studies. The correlation between rainfall frequency and biocrust growth was found to be positive for winter rain and negative for summer rain areas in a study across southern African sites (). In the southwestern United States
column2: the carbon balance of S. caninervis in response to rainfall frequency and amount was modulated by season and events leading to a positive carbon balance in winter could result in carbon losses in summer (). Other studies from the same area reported a negative effect of an increased summer precipitation frequency on different variables related to the photosynthetic performance of cyanobacterial- and lichen-dominated soil crusts (; ; ).

column1: To our knowledge
column2: there are no rainfall manipulation studies assessing the response of D. diacapsis dominated biocrusts to different rainfall frequencies. Most of the studies evaluating how alterations in the precipitation frequency affect physiology and functioning of biocrust constituents have been conducted in summer
column3: and therefore reported a negative impact of a higher frequency of small pulses. We conducted this study in early spring
column4: with rather cold nights and maximum temperatures around 30 °C. These moderate temperatures enabled a constant activation of lichen photosynthesis during daily measurements. The rainfall patterns applied in this study provided a total water amount of 60 mm
column5: which is well above the monthly saturation rainfall of 40 mm that is reported for maximum biocrust activity across Europe (). In combination
column6: this led to a positive net photosynthesis and crust activity during the experiment. Field measurements with lichen-dominated biocrusts in Spain show that periods of net carbon fixation occur during the winter months
column7: and are commonly restricted to a few hours in the morning or the late afternoon; in summer and during midday
column8: net photosynthesis is mostly negative (; ; ). In summer
column9: potential evaporation rates are high and both biocrusts and the underlying soil dry out relatively quickly upon rewetting. For an identical experiment conducted in the summer time
column10: we would therefore expect results that are more similar to those reported from the summer studies mentioned above. However
column11: since most rainfall events in central Spain occur during the spring and autumn/winter period ()
column12: the impact of different rainfall patterns should also be studied during these phases of high biocrust activity.

column1: In accordance with other studies
column2: we showed that precipitation frequency plays an important role for the carbon balance of lichen-dominated biocrusts in central Spain. Previous studies revealed a large variability in the carbon exchange response of different biocrust constituents and communities to rainfall. To our knowledge
column3: no studies so far have assessed the responses of D. diacapsis dominated biocrusts to altered early spring precipitation frequency. In contrast to what has been found in previous studies at high summer temperatures and conducted with other biocrust-forming lichens and mosses
column4: our results indicate that at moderate temperatures
column5: a higher rainfall frequency is beneficial given the same overall water amount over a short period. This clearly shows that the gas exchange response to different rainfall frequencies is modulated by radiation and temperature conditions leading to seasonal differences. We therefore call for detailed cross-site and cross-season comparisons of the impacts of altered rainfall patterns on the carbon fluxes of biocrust-dominated soils.

column1: The emission of large amounts of greenhouse gases seriously threatens the global climate and environment. Studying the current space-time characteristics of carbon storage and predicting future variations in land use and carbon storage can help realize the ‘double carbon’ goal. column1: The Integrated Valuation of Ecosystem Services and Trade-offs (InVEST) model is an ecological model for evaluating and quantifying ecosystem services. In this study
column2: the carbon storage and sequestration module of the InVEST model was used to calculate the carbon storage in Kunming. The basic assumption of this module is that the value of the carbon density of a specific land category is fixed
column3: and the carbon storage of that land type can be acquired by multiplying the value of carbon density with that of the land area (). The carbon storage module in the InVEST model divides the ecosystem carbon storage into four basic carbon pools ()
column4: which include terrestrial biogenic carbon
column5: subsurface biogenic carbon
column6: soil carbon
column7: and dead organic carbon. The sum of the carbon stock of the four carbon pools provides the value for the total carbon storage of the ecosystem in the area
column8: which can be achieved using  and  ().
