column12: which involves digesting the soil sample with sulfuric acid. The resulting ammonia was then distilled and titrated using a semi-automatic ammonia distillation unit (UDK-132
column13: NIB-B (3)-DSU-003). The soil phosphate content (PO43−) was measured following the protocol described by . This method typically involves extracting the available phosphorus from the soil using a suitable extractant
column14: followed by colorimetric analysis. The chloride content in the soil was assessed using the Mohrs’ titration method (). To determine the soil saturation percentage (SP)
column15: the soil samples were dried in an oven at 70 °C
column16: and 200 g of the dried soil was used to prepare a composite saturation paste
column17: which was then analyzed. Saturation percentage assayed by following formula:

column1: $SP\; \left( \% \right) = \displaystyle{{Amount\; of\; water\; added\
column2: \left( g \right)} \over {Oven\; dried\; soil\; \left( g \right)}}\; \times \; 100$SP(%)=Amountofwateradded(g)Ovendriedsoil(g)×100

column1: where SP % is saturation percentage.
To collect the necessary measurements
column2: a meter rod was utilized to measure the plant
column3: as well as the length of shoot and root directly. A digital loading balance was employed to determine the fresh weights of the shoot and root. Immediately after harvesting
column4: the plant parts were weighed to obtain their fresh weights. For dry weight analysis
column5: the plant samples were subjected to oven-drying at a temperature of 65 °C until a constant weight was achieved. This ensured the complete removal of moisture from the samples. The dry weights of the shoot and root were then measured using a digital loading balance. In the evaluation of leaf characteristics
column6: the focus was on the uppermost mature leaves. A manual count was conducted to ascertain the quantity of leaves on each plant
column7: and the leaf area was quantitatively measured using cm-graph paper. The leaf area was calculated using a formula provided by .

column1: ${\rm Leaf\; area} = {\rm Maximum\; leaf\; length\; } \times {\rm Maximum\; leaf\; width\; } \times {\rm Correction\; factor\; }\left( {0.74} \right)$Leafarea=Maximumleaflength×Maximumleafwidth×Correctionfactor(0.74)
Fresh samples were taken in falcon tubes and stored (−80 °C) for chlorophyll pigments
column2: osmoprotectants
column3: and antioxidants activity. For the analysis of proline
column4: fresh leaf samples were thoroughly homogenized in sulfo-salicylic acid. Then was transferred into cuvette containing ninhydrin solution. After subjected to water bath (100 °C) toluene was added for extraction of proline. Lastly
column5: readings were taken on a spectrophotometer (Model 220; Hitachi
column6: Tokyo
column7: Japan) at 520 nm wavelength ().

column1: $Proline\; \left( {\mu mol\; {g^{ - 1}}fresh\; weight} \right) = \displaystyle{{\mu g\; proline\; m{l^{ - 1}} \times \; ml\; of\; toluene/115.5} \over {\; sample\; weight\; \left( g \right)}}$Proline(μmolg−1freshweight)=μgprolineml−1×mloftoluene/115.5sampleweight(g)
To measure the glycine betaine content in the leaf samples
column2: fresh leaf samples weighing 0.5 g were soaked in 20 ml of deionized water (H2O) at a temperature of 25 °C for a duration of 24 h. Following the soaking period
column3: an extract was prepared from the soaked samples and assayed using the established protocols outlined by . For the analysis of total soluble proteins
column4: fresh leaf samples weighing 0.2 g were sliced and thoroughly crushed in 5 ml of phosphate buffer at a pH of 7.0. The buffer facilitated the extraction of proteins from the crushed leaf samples. The mixture of crushed leaf samples and buffer was then subjected to centrifugation at 5
column5: 000 rpm for 5 min. This centrifugation step effectively separated the solid components of the mixture from the liquid supernatant. The supernatant
column6: containing the soluble proteins
column7: was collected for further analysis. To quantify the protein content in the supernatant
column8: the method developed by  was employed. This method relies on a colorimetric assay to measure the protein concentration present in the sample.

column1: To estimate the photosynthetic pigments
column2: including chlorophylls (Chl a
column3: Chl b
column4: and TChl.) and carotenoids
column5: the methods described by  and  were followed. A spectrophotometer (Hitachi-220; Hitachi
column6: Tokyo
column7: Japan) was used for the measurements. The formulas used for calculations were:

column1: $Chl.\; a\; \left( {mg\; {g^{ - 1}}\; f.wt.} \right) = \left[ {12.7\left( {{\rm OD}663} \right) - {\rm \; }2.69\left( {{\rm OD}645} \right)} \right]{\rm \times}\displaystyle{{\rm V} \over {1000}}{\rm \times\; W}$Chl.a(mgg−1f.wt.)=[12.7(OD663)−2.69(OD645)]xV1000xW

$Chl.\; b\; \left( {mg\; {g^{ - 1}}\; f.wt.} \right) = \left[ {22.9\left( {{\rm OD}645} \right) - {\rm \; }4.68\left( {{\rm OD}663} \right)} \right]{\rm \times}\displaystyle{{\rm V} \over {1000}}{\rm \times\; W}$Chl.b(mgg−1f.wt.)=[22.9(OD645)−4.68(OD663)]xV1000xW
$$Total\;chl.\;\left( {mg\;{g^{ - 1}}\;f.wt.} \right) = \left[ {20.2\left( {{\rm{OD}}645} \right) - \;8.02\;\left( {{\rm{OD}}663} \right)} \right] \times {{\rm{V}} \over {1000}} \times \;{\rm{W}}$$
Totalchl.(mgg−1f.wt.)=[20.2(OD645)−8.02(OD663)]xV1000xW

$Carotenoids\; \left( {mg\; {g^{ - 1}}\; f.wt.} \right) = \left[ {12.7\left( {OD480} \right) - \; 0.114\; \left( {OD663} \right)} \right] - 0.638\; \left( {OD645} \right)/{2500^{}}$Carotenoids(mgg−1f.wt.)=[12.7(OD480)−0.114(OD663)]−0.638(OD645)/2500
For the measurement of total antioxidant activity
column2: a dried leaf sample weighing 0.5 g was placed in a test tube. To facilitate the extraction of antioxidants from the leaf tissue
column3: 20 mL of a 0.45% salt solution was added to the test tube. The sample was then subjected to heating in a water bath at 40 °C for a duration of 20 min. After the heating process
column4: the test tube was centrifuged at 3
column5: 000 rpm for 30 min
column6: enabling the separation of the supernatant from the solid residue. The supernatant
column7: which contained the extracted antioxidants
column8: was carefully separated and stored at −20 °C until further analysis. To measure the total antioxidant activity
column9: the FTC (Ferric Thiocyanate) method described by  was employed. This method involves assessing the ability of the antioxidants to inhibit lipid peroxidation by reacting with ferric ions.

column1: To examine the anatomy of the root
column2: stem
column3: and leaf
column4: the largest plant from each replicate was selected. For leaf anatomy
column5: a 2 cm section was obtained from the leaf base of fully mature and sun-exposed leaves. For stem anatomy
column6: a section was taken from the base of the internode of the main stem. Similarly
column7: for root anatomy
column8: a section was obtained from tap root near the junction of the root and shoot. The collected plant material was fixed using a formaldehyde acetic alcohol solution consisting of 10% formaldehyde
column9: 5% acetic acid
column10: 50% ethanol
column11: and 35% distilled water. The plant material was immersed in the fixative solution for 48 h
column12: followed by transfer to an acetic alcohol solution containing 25% acetic acid and 75% ethanol for long-term storage. To prepare the sections for microscopic analysis
column13: free-hand sections were made from the fixed plant material. These sections underwent a series of dehydration steps using ethanol. For staining
column14: the sections were subjected to the standard safranin and fast green double-staining technique
column15: as outlined by . Measurements of the sections were taken using a light microscope (Nikon SE Anti-Mould
column16: Nikon
column17: Tokyo
column18: Japan) equipped with an ocular micrometer that was calibrated using a stage micrometer. Micrographs of the stained sections were captured using a digital camera (Nikon FDX-35) mounted on a stereomicroscope (Nikon 104
column19: Japan).

column1: The morphological
column2: physiological
column3: and anatomical trait data were subjected to statistical analysis using a One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) in a complete randomized design with ten replicates. Mean values were compared using the least significant difference (LSD) test at a significance level of 5%. The statistical analysis was conducted using the Minitab software package (version 17.1.0; Pennsylvania State University
column4: State College
column5: PA
column6: USA). To examine the relationships between the different morphological
column7: physiological
column8: and anatomical traits and the soil physicochemical parameters of the collection sites
column9: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) was conducted. The analysis was carried out using the R-studio software
column10: and the data were plotted to visualize the patterns and associations. Furthermore
column11: heatmaps were constructed using the pheatmap package in R-studio. These heatmaps were used to cluster the selected groups based on (i) soil physicochemical attributes and morphophysiological parameters
column12: (ii) soil physicochemical attributes and root anatomy
column13: (iii) soil physicochemical attributes and stem anatomy
column14: and (iv) soil physicochemical attributes and leaf anatomy. The heatmaps provide a visual representation of the relationships and similarities among the different variables.

column1: The soil in most of the habitats was sandy (). The loamy soil was observed in five habitats RYK (near the wasteland)
column2: KHP (near waste deposit)
column3: VEH (near the roadside)
column4: JHG (along rice field) and LYH (wheat field) whereas loamy sand was observed in two habitats such as MUL (along river Chenab) and RJP (near M5 motorway). Clayey loam was seen in DGK habitat (along railway track). The soil electrical conductivity ranged from 0.76 to 6.73 dSm−1
column5: the maximum value of soil ECe was recorded at RYK (near the wasteland) and KHP (near waste deposit) sites and the minimum was observed at SDK (along barren land) and RJP (near M5 motorway). Habitats like water channel (LAP)
column6: along Chenab river (MUL) and near GT road (JHG) showed exceptionally highly level of soil ECe than rest of the populations. Most of the habitat comprised of alkaline pH
column7: ranging from 6.2 to 8.9. The acidic pH was observed only in one habitat RYK (near the wasteland). The soil organic matter (OM) varied from 0.21 to 0.56%. The maximum organic matter was noted in soil of Chenab river (MUL) and the minimum was measured in soil of roadside population (VEH). The soil saturation percentage (SP) ranged from 15 to 42%. The maximum saturation percentage was observed in soil of wheat filed (LYH) population. It was the minimum in soil of water canal (LAP) and GT road (JHG) populations. The soil Phosphate concentration varied from 1.6 mg Kg−1 in the SDK habitat to 3.6 mg Kg−1 in the LYH habitat. The nitrate content in the LYH habitat exhibited the highest value
column8: while the MUZ habitat recorded the lowest value. The soil chloride ion (Cl¯) reached its maximum (567.8 mg Kg−1) in the RYK habitat
column9: while the minimum (72.1 mg Kg−1) was observed in both the DGK and MUL habitats. The soil calcium ion (Ca2+) concentration ranged from 54.2 to 156.1 mg Kg−1. The RYK habitat showed the highest soil calcium concentration
column10: while the SDK habitat exhibited the lowest concentration. The soil sodium ion (Na+) ranged between 54.2 and 398.9 mg Kg−1
column11: with the RYK population having the highest value and the SDK habitat recording the lowest. The maximum soil potassium ion (K+) concentration was observed in the MUL and LYH habitats
column12: while the minimum was found in the SDK habitat.

column1: A summary of specific adaptive strategies of differently adapted populations of Parthenium hysterophorus collected from different regions of Punjab province are highlighted in . The evaluation of morpho-anatomical and physio-biochemical adaptive markers is crucial for understanding the underlying mechanisms of adaptation in differently adapted populations to multiple stresses (; ). In the face of severe drought conditions or physiological drought induced by other environmental stresses
column2: water conservation becomes a primary strategy (). In water-scarce conditions
column3: water conservation in plants is achieved through mechanisms such as water storage in parenchymatous tissues like pith and cortex (; )
column4: efficient water translocation facilitated by widening of vessels
column5: and reduction of water loss through the presence of mechanical tissues and a thick cuticle on the surface of plant organs (). Herein
column6: we tested the strength of adaptation and the extent of these adaptations in plant survival
column7: different populations of P. hysterophorus were sampled from a wide range of habitats. It was hypothesized that the invasive success of P. hysterophorus in diverse habitats is influenced by its phenotypic plasticity
column8: allowing it to adapt to a wide range of environmental conditions.

column1: The anatomical characteristics of plants have been recognized as highly responsive to climatic conditions (; ). This adaptability enables plants to thrive and survive in challenging environment (). The size of the root cross-sectional area is predominantly determined by the relative proportions of the cortical region and the vascular bundle area (). An expansion in root area not only enhances the capacity for water storage but also strengthens the mechanical integrity of the plant’s soft tissues. This
column2: in turn

column4: aided by physiological adjustments. The observed increase in root cross-sectional area indicates better growth in the population inhabiting waste land (RYK). Roots
column5: being underground plant parts

column7: and under harsh condition its strong friction of rhizospheric soil (). In resulting

Thicker epidermal layers play vital role in resisting the friction of soil compaction as well as impede the excessive water and solute translocation inside root tissues (). The water storage parenchyma (cortex) and vascular region (metaxylem vessels and phloem) in the roots play a crucial role

column2: when the incipient soil was severely deficient in nutrients and organic matter. Early colonizers were bryophytes such as mosses
column3: liverworts and hornworts
column4: and were composed of a few cells with no true roots

column1: Mycorrhizal and other (non-pathogenic) root-inhabiting fungi (such as endophytes) continue to play a vital role in soil nutrient cycling

column1: Fungal partners can be obligate biotrophs i.e.
column2: entirely dependent on living root cells as in AM symbiosis
column3: in which cell architecture and physiology is intensively reorganized to arrange the biotrophic interface (). Some fungal symbionts are facultative biotrophs or partly dependent on their living host such as some ECM fungi or dark septate endophytes (DSE) (; ). Sebacinalean endophytes (SE) have both biotrophic and necrotrophic (preceded by pre-programmed death of cortical cells) habits during their interaction with plant roots (; ). In return for the assorted benefits conferred to the host plant
column4: the root-associated fungi may depend on their host as the carbon (C) source. Simple sugars (hexoses) were presumed to be the sole C type received by fungal partners in mycorrhizal associations ()
column5: perhaps due to a primary research focus on carbohydrates as a possible food source rather than other organic molecules such as fatty acids (FAs) that were not technically feasible to detect at the time. However
column6: fed plants with [U-13C6] glucose and monitored the 13C patterns of fungal FAs in Lotus japonicus wild-types and mutants defective in AM-specific paralogs of lipid biosynthesis genes (KASI and GPAT6)
column7: and demonstrated that plants transfer 16:0 FAs to AM fungi. Accordingly
column8: sugars are now known not to be the only C form provided by host plants in mycorrhizal symbiosis.

column3: but not in root endophytes such as Colletotrichum tofieldiae and Serendipita indica (; ).

There is limited experimental evidence about the potential benefits for host plants. The location of the plant-fungal interaction on the mutualism–parasitism spectrum ultimately depends on the nutritional balance achieved by the partnership rather than the type of interface structure that is formed (). column1: Three waves of mycorrhizal evolution have been proposed by  including (i) the origin of AM associations in early terrestrial plants over 450 Mya
column2: (ii) evolution of ECM (except Pinaceae)
column3: nonmycorrhizal (NM) roots
column4: ERM
column5: and OM associations along the period of major root diversification in the Late Cretaceous
column6: and iii) development of specialized nutrition strategies and multifunctional (e.g.
column7: AM and ECM) roots in a few plant families. The third wave is an ongoing trend that is related to substantial climate change since the Palaeocene. Recent studies have revealed that the actual niches of different root symbionts could extend beyond those our respective domains of research predict (). Evolutionary tendency towards shifting ecological niches (evolutionary trajectories) seems to be a common event in fungi that occurs convergently in numerous independent taxa. In fungal root symbionts
column8: the key evolutionary trajectories include (i) additional food source plus protection against soil adversities and (ii) regressive evolution
column9: such as that of enzymes involved in saprotrophic nutrition (). Transitions between nutritional strategies in root-fungal relationships
column10: especially gains or losses of ECM and NM strategies
column11: are more common than previously thought (). In this review
column12: we have briefly mentioned the ecological niches of certain root symbionts
column13: which seem to have been overlooked.

column1: The majority of plants may be simultaneously associated with a multitude of symbiotic fungal clades in their natural ecosystems. Mycobiome studies using the state-of-the-art “omics” technologies have unearthed a range of root endophytic and root-colonizing saprotrophic relationships involving diverse fungal lineages in both mycorrhizal and NM plants (; ; ; ; ; ; ). For example
column2: two independent studies using 454-pyrosequencing have revealed diverse mycorrhizal and endophytic taxa associated with roots of 36 co-occurring plant species in an oak-dominated forest in northern Japan ()
column3: and three endangered orchid species in the Atlantic Forest
column4: in Brazil (). These studies support the proposition that beneficial root-fungal interactions could extend far beyond the well-established associations mentioned above (). It is increasingly apparent that study of plant performance in the absence of the co-evolved rhizosphere biota will give a limited and even skewed understanding of plant functioning in their natural environments () e.g.
column5: for applications such as increasing sustainability of food production in low input agroecosystems
column6: and habitat restoration for recovery of endangered species (). Efforts such as those to ameliorate degraded lands may be further hampered by insufficient understanding of the complex variety of plant-fungal interactions that may also need to be restored ().

column1: Overemphasizing the visibility of symbioses (i.e.
column2: root colonization and interface specialization) could have hindered our understanding of the actual functional contribution of root-associated fungi towards health and performance of terrestrial ecosystems. This review examines the morphological (interfacial matrix) and functional (primary focus on plant fitness) aspects of diverse symbioses with the aim of engendering discussion about a broader view of plant-fungal root symbioses that emphasizes both form and function. Furthermore
column3: our modest knowledge of fungal taxonomy and ecology also contributes to our limited understanding of the functioning of root fungal symbionts
column4: hence we will explore the taxonomically/ecologically diverse interactions observed recently by aid of high-throughput “omics” technologies.

column1: It is necessary to mention the current definition of symbiosis
column2: which is arguably the most controversial term in the history of biological terminology (). The term “symbiotismus” was coined by Albert Bernhard Frank in 1877 to describe the mutualistic relationship in lichens
column3: whereas the word “symbiosis” is credited to Anton de Bary who
column4: one year later
column5: defined symbiosis as “the living together of unlike named organisms” (; ). While some scientists persisted in the opinion that “symbiosis” should only be used for mutualistic relationships
column6: others extended the definition to encompass commensalistic and parasitic interactions (). The 130-year-old debate is over now and the current general biology and ecology textbooks use the broader definition of symbiosis that includes all possible interaction types
column7: and the restrictive definition (equalizing symbiosis with mutualism) has essentially disappeared (). Symbiosis (which literally means “living together”
column8: derived from the Greek word “sym”: together
column9: and “biosis”: living) is currently defined as any type of close and long-term ecological relationship between two living organisms of different species. Here
column10: we explore the root-fungal symbioses that are predominantly beneficial to host plants i.e.
column11: mutualistic and commensalistic symbioses
column12: where the fungal partner may or may not benefit from the association
column13: respectively.

column1: Approximately 80% of all vascular plant species establish AM symbiosis (72% are AM consistently
column2: while 7% form inconsistent NM-AM associations) including bryophytes
column3: pteridophytes
column4: gymnosperms and angiosperms () with the fungi that were
column5: until very recently
column6: classified within the phylum Glomeromycota (; ).  conducted extensive phylogenetic analyses of a broad range of fungal taxa and consequently split zygomycetes into two phyla Mucoromycota (mainly mycorrhizas
column7: root endophytes and saprotrophs) and Zoopagomycota (mainly pathogens of small animals and fungi)
column8: and placed AM fungi (AMF) within Mucoromycota as a subphylum i.e.
column9: Glomeromycotina. Although AMF are considered to be nonspecific or have very low species-level specificity ()

column12: habitat generalist plants vs habitat specialist plants) or the ecosystem type (; ; ). For example

column15: while generalist AMF prefer habitat generalist plants in both forest and grassland ecosystems (; ).

column1: The main benefits plants receive from AM symbiosis include access to the soil nutrients that are otherwise unavailable and resistance against pathogens and environmental stresses such as drought
column2: salinity and heavy metal toxicity (). AM fungi are not able to complete their lifecycle in the absence of a host and this effect has not been able to be overcome by tissue culture techniques. The close evolutionary relationship extending over the entire period of plant evolution on land
column3: and the remarkable intracellular development of these fungi that involves very close interaction between the plant and fungal nuclei suggests this symbiosis has evolved towards the same host nucleus dependence that have eukaryote cell organelle symbioses ()
column4: and other specialized microbial symbioses such as legume-rhizobium associations ().

column1: Although AM fungi generally colonize roots
column2: colonization of root hairs has also been documented (). Hyphae grow inter- and intracellularly through living epidermal and cortical cells
column3: in which they produce specialized shrub-shaped structures called arbuscules. Prior to hyphal penetration
column4: host epidermal cells located immediately below the fungal appressorium undergo intensive re-programming
column5: such as remodeling of actin filaments and microtubules
column6: and form a pre-penetration apparatus (PPA
column7: ). Therefore
column8: PPA formation is an appropriate cellular marker indicating which epidermal cells are about to be colonized. The PPA development includes differential expression of genes involved in cell wall modification such as expansin-like gene and cellulose synthase (). Following penetration into the root cortex via extracellular or intracellular hyphal growth
column9: and depending on the plant-fungus combination
column10: AM associations may produce arbuscules (Arum-type)
column11: thick hyphal coils (Paris-type)
column12: or intermediate structures (such as arbusculate coils) that cannot be easily defined (; ; ). Arbuscules are separated from the cytoplasm by a pre-arbuscular membrane
column13: originated by invagination of plant plasma membrane
column14: and are specialized interface structures where the fungal partner trades nutrients such as P
column15: taken up from soil using its extensive extraradical hyphae
column16: for simple carbohydrates from the host plant (). Arbuscules are preferentially formed inside inner cortical cells. Similar to arbuscules
column17: hyphal coils are surrounded by plant plasma membranes. The morphological variability of AM structures may be influenced by plant cultivars
column18: fungal isolates and soil conditions (). Arum-type associations may be superior in supplying nutrients to their host due to growing intercellularly in a longitudinal pattern (a feature missing in the Paris-type)
column19: leading to higher colonized root length. The Paris-type structures
column20: however
column21: live longer than arbuscules. Although the total percentage of root colonization (counting all intraradical structures) is generally considered to be an indicator of the AM presence and functionality
column22: the extent of colonization does not necessarily reflect the physiological responses of the plant host (; ; ). This could be related to relative abundance and activity of arbuscules as well as the extent of development of extraradical hyphae and their effectiveness in nutrient uptake.

column1: Recent work highlights the fungal influence on nutrient transporters in roots
column2: which differs for different species interactions (; ; ). Nutrient-related signals seem to control the development of AM symbiosis. Two conserved cis-elements (MYCS and P1BS) in the promoter regions of AM-inducible phosphate transporter genes are required simultaneously for symbiotic gene expression and root colonization (; ). Coordination of inorganic P (Pi) starvation and AM signalling pathways appears to be the prerequisite for establishment of the symbiosis
column3: but the precise regulatory mechanisms underlying this phenomenon are not known. Although generally considered a beneficial association for host plants
column4: the net effects of AM associations on the host plant can be positive
column5: neutral
column6: or even negative depending on the plant-fungus combination and genetics

column1: Fine endophytes are root endophytic fungi associated with angiosperms that have been traditionally classified as AM fungi (Glomus tenue) (; )
column2: but their taxonomic position has been quite ambiguous () until recently.  clarified the phylogenetic position of FRE and demonstrated that these fungi belong to Mucoromycotina
column3: not Glomeromycotina. Typical features of FRE include fine hyphae of less than 1.5 µm in diameter
column4: intracellular colonization of epidermal and cortical cells in a fan-like branching pattern
column5: and production of arbuscule- and vesicle-like structures (; ; ). Capability of Mucoromycotina to establish symbiosis with early terrestrial plants has raised the hypothesis that Mucoromycotina may be predecessors of Glomeromycotina (; ). The hypothesis is further supported by the close phylogenetic relationship between Mucoromycotina and AM fungi (; )
column6: recent demonstration of a mutually beneficial symbiosis between Mucoromycotina and Haplomitriopsida liverworts ()
column7: and their arbuscule-forming feature (). Improved growth of ryegrass (Lolium perenne)
column8: cocksfoot (Dactylis glomerata)
column9: and sweet vernal (Anthoxanthum odoratum) associated with FRE has been observed under P-deficient conditions (). FRE may have important ecological functions in plants exposed to abiotic stresses due to their ubiquitous presence under extreme environmental conditions such as high altitude

column11: cold temperatures
column12: and water-logging (; ; ; ). The available evidence suggests that the role of FRE fungi in supporting healthy plant communities should be further examined ().

column1: A sheath of organized hyphae called a mantle is formed around fine lateral roots. The colonized lateral roots have restricted apical growth (referred to as “short roots”)
column2: are thicker than non-colonized roots and have no root hairs. Some short roots have bifurcating branching patterns
column3: which is an ontogenetic plant trait (). Mantle functions as a transitory storage compartment for metabolites received from the host and also the nutrients absorbed from soil by extraradical hyphae (; ). Mantle might be less developed (sparse and loose) in some ECM associations (). The presence of fifteen genes encoding putative hexose transporters and the absence of genes involved in sucrose hydrolysis (invertases) in the genome of the model ECM fungus Laccaria bicolor suggest that this fungus receives its C source as simple six-C sugars such as glucose and fructose (). However
column4: this does not seem to be a general trend. An invertase gene is present in the genome of the ascomycetous ECM fungus Tuber melanosporum
column5: making it capable of hydrolyzing sucrose and potentially less dependent on the host plant (). Furthermore

column13: many strains cultured from host tissue do not represent the dominant Symbiodiniaceae in a host species (). We encourage efforts toward testing new media and bringing new species into culture ()

column17: cell division rates (growth)

column1: Studies evaluating the distribution of genetic variation within species
column2: often across spatiotemporal gradients or among host taxa
column3: seek to understand how populations are influenced by evolutionary processes such as gene flow
column4: genetic drift
column5: and selection (; ; ; ; ; ; ). Here
column6: we define a population as a group of individuals belonging to the same species that live and interbreed with each other in a given space and time. The study of Symbiodiniaceae populations is fundamental to improving the resolution at which phenotypes of interest are differentiated. Thus
column7: here we focus on allele-based identification and quantification of genetic variation.

column1: Because a single host can contain a mixture of multiple species and/or genera
column2: a first step in experimental design should include assessing sample sets for the presence of multiple distinct Symbiodiniaceae that may confound the interpretation of population-level genetic variation (see “Guidance for Community-Level Assessment of Symbiodiniaceae”). Such assessment can be done pre- and post-population-level analysis with established genetic markers (e.g.
column3: ITS2
column4: cp23S) and may be guided by published literature for some regions or host species. Pre-screening is especially advantageous where information on the community composition of Symbiodiniaceae is also sought and especially for hosts which tend to associate with multiple genera or species. Quantitative PCR (qPCR) is one potential technique to pre-screen Symbiodiniaceae samples for the presence of particular lineages (; ; ). After pre-screening
column5: population-level studies typically target genetic variation from the numerically dominant symbiont associating with a particular host or set of hosts ()
column6: while excluding any confounding genetic variation from additional species that may be present within host samples (; ). Post-screening of samples is also possible using tests of assignment to genetic clusters () or identifying and excluding samples with outlier allelic profiles. Post-screening may be more time- and cost-effective as verification can be performed on a subset of the total sample set.

column1: Microsatellite loci (or simple sequence repeats; SSRs) are segments of DNA where 1–6 base pairs are repeated in a tandem array; these loci are distributed abundantly across genomes of nearly all eukaryotic organisms (). Variations in the length of repeats are generated by polymerase slippage during DNA replication
column2: resulting in homologous regions (i.e.
column3: loci) of differing lengths (i.e.
column4: alleles) among individuals. Microsatellites are generally thought to represent neutral loci with high mutation rates. Their single-locus
column5: multiallelic
column6: and codominant properties can yield valuable information regarding ploidy and reveal genetic structure among populations within and between species. Furthermore
column7: microsatellite analyses are generally a PCR-based technique
column8: making them cost-effective relative to other methods (). With the advent of high-throughput sequencing and transcriptomics
column9: the generation of hundreds of potential microsatellite loci is now comparatively straightforward (e.g.
column10: ). For species or lineages where numerous loci are available
column11: costs and effort can remain low by multiplexing primer sets (). Taken together
column12: these features make microsatellites attractive for studying Symbiodiniaceae populations. These markers have been used to address questions related to overall diversity
column13: population structure within and between reefs
column14: gene flow
column15: dispersal
column16: and relatedness between symbionts (see  in ).

column1: Once the target Symbiodiniaceae species or lineage has been identified within a dataset
column2: these samples can be tested for variability using previously developed microsatellite loci via PCR amplification (). Primers for such loci have been developed for Symbiodiniaceae species across at least five genera: Symbiodinium ()
column3: Breviolum (; ; ; ; ; )
column4: Cladocopium (; ; ; ; )
column5: Durusdinium (; )
column6: and Philozoon (). Importantly
column7: these loci tend to have narrow phylogenetic ranges
column8: with primers developed for a given species typically working only on other closely-related species within the same genus. Therefore
column9: it is necessary to screen existing primers for utility with a given target species
column10: to ensure that allelic variability among the chosen suite of microsatellite loci is sufficient
column11: and to develop novel primer sets if existing primers fail or prove insufficiently specific. Ideally
column12: new Symbiodiniaceae primers should be tested against monoclonal cultures of species within the same genus (positive controls) as well as against symbiont-free sperm or apo-symbiotic larvae (negative controls) to rule out off-target PCR amplification of host DNA. Although more loci will generally increase discriminatory power in population-level studies
column13: as few as 2–3 loci have provided sufficient discriminatory power for some questions (; ).

column1: Given that Symbiodiniaceae are haploid in their vegetative life stage ()
column2: a single allele per microsatellite locus is expected when a host harbors a single clonal strain of Symbiodiniaceae (represented by a single multi-locus genotype; MLG). When a single allele is recovered from nearly all loci
column3: establishing MLGs is straightforward. However
column4: recovery of multiple alleles at a given locus from a single sample is not uncommon (). Instances of multiple alleles per locus can be interpreted as detection of cells from multiple genetic strains (multiple MLGs) within host tissues (; ; ; ;
column5: ). Examples of multiple MLGs tend to be more common within Indo-Pacific corals hosting Cladocopium species (; ; )
column6: whereas they are less common in Caribbean corals hosting Cladocopium and other genera (; ; ; ; ;
column8: ). Consistent patterns of multiple alleles for certain loci among a subset of monoclonal cultures has led to the proposal of whole or segmental genome duplication within certain Symbiodiniaceae. This scenario would make overestimation of symbiont genotype diversity within samples likely ()
column9: and make the assignment of MLGs difficult
column10: raising challenges for data analyses and interpretations.

column1: Several approaches have been developed to accommodate instances of multiple MLGs within a sample (; ; ; ; ; ; )
column2: including the exclusion of some samples and/or genotypes in certain cases. When multiple alleles for a given locus occur infrequently among samples
column3: two data sets can be created: (1) a set where all microsatellite alleles within each sample are used and scored for presence or absence (i.e.
column4: binary) within each sample
column5: and (2) a curtailed data set omitting samples with multiple alleles at one or more loci
column6: allowing MLGs to be assigned. Notably
column7: studies using this approach have come to similar conclusions across the two data sets (e.g.
column8: ; ; ; ; ; ). In general
column9: given that reported scales of genetic divergence are similar across studies using binary and MLG-based approaches
column10: and excluding many samples can lead to underestimating genetic diversity ()
column11: we suggest that the binary approach should be used when possible (e.g.
column12: a high proportion of samples exhibit multiple alleles per locus).

column1: While microsatellite analyses have proven informative and valuable in population genetic studies of Symbiodiniaceae
column2: they present challenges in data acquisition and interpretation. For example
column3: the long repetitive regions of microsatellites are often difficult to reliably amplify
column4: making it arduous to verify repeat length via fragment analysis. Microsatellites can suffer from allele dropout
column5: and low specificity of PCR primers
column6: which can potentially lead to diversity underestimates within a sample. Microsatellites themselves are subject to more general criticisms including unclear mutation models and the potential for homoplasy (). Additionally
column7: many analytical pipelines used to assess population genetic patterns make basic assumptions that Symbiodiniaceae do not follow (e.g.
column8: that organisms are diploid and exhibit predominantly sexual reproduction). In light of this
column9: researchers should be cautious about interpreting results from pipelines developed for organisms that exhibit more traditional population biology.

column1: The ITS2 region of rDNA is repeated in tandem arrays within all known Symbiodiniaceae genomes. For population-level assessments
column2: this universality presents an advantage over microsatellites
column3: but the multi-copy nature of this marker poses unique challenges. As long as appropriate analytical frameworks are applied (see “Guidance for Community-Level Assessment of Symbiodiniaceae”)
column4: ITS2 data can be used to resolve strains within species. Such assessments require consideration of similarities in the assemblages of ITS2 sequences and their relative abundances within each genome. For example
column5: genetic structure among Cladocopium thermophilum strains in the Persian/Arabian Gulf has been characterized (; ) and patterns of IGV obtained from amplicon sequencing data show fine-scale spatial structure among C. thermophilum populations separated by tens to hundreds of kilometers (). However
column6: recombination (i.e.
column7: whether two populations are interbreeding) is often considered sufficient for operational recognition that those entities are members of the same species (; ; ; ;
column8: ). Therefore
column9: it is difficult to determine whether ITS2-based genotypes correspond to distinct populations of the same species or different species. Other markers are also able to resolve at the population level
column10: but their application to Symbiodiniaceae population biology is limited. Examples include the chloroplast psbA minicircle noncoding region (psbAncr; ) and the chloroplast 23S ribosomal region (cp23S; ).

column1: Advancing our understanding of Symbiodiniaceae population biology will be greatly informed by leveraging samples that have single Symbiodiniaceae MLGs (). For example
column2: available monoclonal cultures of Symbiodiniaceae from several species could be used to develop and test new technologies and markers (including validation of copy number
column3: see “Accounting for Copy Number Variation”) and these technologies could then be extended to more complex associations in hospite (within a host organism). To overcome the challenges of widespread gene duplication in Symbiodiniaceae genomes (; ; )
column4: efforts should be directed toward identifying new low copy markers (or preferably single copy markers). Discovery of single copy loci may be informed by screening for universal single copy markers collated in the Benchmarking Universal Single-Copy Orthologs (BUSCO) database (; )
column5: although many BUSCOs are undetected in Symbiodiniaceae genomes (). Restriction-associated DNA sequencing may serve as a low-cost method for generating single copy markers for population-level assessments in Symbiodiniaceae (; ); however
column6: these methods require further development.

These types of analyses would allow for simultaneous quantification of gene flow and divergence among Symbiodiniaceae populations of co-occurring species and improve estimates of effective population sizes and clonality within and among species
column8: hosts
column9: and reefs. Another major advantage of genome-wide data is the potential to evaluate adaptive (non-neutral) genetic variation and signatures of selection across the genome (; ; ). For example
column10: identifying associations between traditional markers
column11: genomic regions
column12: Symbiodiniaceae functional traits
column13: and/or environmental variables–including those that are important for the survivorship of corals under warmer
column14: more acidic
column15: and more eutrophic oceans–remains a research priority (see “Beyond Genotype: Phenotyping Symbiodiniaceae” and “Integrating Multiomic Technologies to Study Symbiodiniaceae”).

column1: Generally defined

column3: what is meant by terms such as “together” and “interact” can vary ()
column4: particularly when considering free-living vs. symbiotic Symbiodiniaceae. Typically one to two (but up to 10) Symbiodiniaceae cells reside in a coral gastrodermal cell (; )
column5: potentially restricting direct interactions between the endosymbiont cells within a coral host. Here

column8: conspecific hosts or multiple host species). Environments that include multiple free-living Symbiodiniaceae species also constitute macroscale communities; e.g.
column9: benthic sediments (; ; )
column10: the water column (; )
column11: and macro-algal surfaces (; ).

column1: Macroscale Symbiodiniaceae communities contain more species and encompass higher genetic diversity than local Symbiodiniaceae communities because symbiotic diversity accumulates with increased host colony and habitat sampling (). Environmental samples include cells of symbiotic Symbiodiniaceae expelled from hosts as well as non-symbiotic
column2: free-living species. In contrast
column3: a given adult host typically harbors only one or two dominant Symbiodiniaceae species ()
column4: often from distinct genera
column5: as well as other species at low relative abundances (; ). In hospite Symbiodiniaceae communities can be transmitted vertically (promoting higher fidelity)
column6: reassembled horizontally (allowing for greater flexibility)
column7: or some combination of both (mixed-mode transmission) with each host generation (). The diversity of the free-living component of macroscale Symbiodiniaceae communities and the symbiotic component of local Symbiodiniaceae communities are each likely to be underestimated (e.g.
column9: but for different reasons. Free-living communities are relatively diffuse and are therefore more difficult to exhaustively sample. In contrast
column10: local Symbiodiniaceae community assessments are prone to sampling bias (but see
column11: e.g.
column12: ). Characterizations of local communities are often based on a single sample from a well-lit
column13: “top” surface of a colony. Sampling across a host’s surface has revealed heterogeneous distributions of dominant Symbiodiniaceae within colonies of Caribbean stony corals such as Colpophyllia
column14: Montastraea
column15: Orbicella
column16: Porites
column17: and Siderastrea (e.g.
column19: as well as some Pacific stony corals (e.g.
column20: ; ; ; ; ) and zoantharians such as Zoanthus () and Palythoa (). Whether local Symbiodiniaceae communities exhibit structure over smaller spatial scales in hospite (e.g.
column21: oral vs. aboral host surfaces) is unknown
column22: but could be resolved with single-cell techniques (see “Integrating Multiomic Technologies to Study Symbiodiniaceae”).

column1: Studying macroscale communities can provide insights into cnidarian-Symbiodiniaceae dynamics along environmental gradients (; ; ; ). Regional macroscale Symbiodiniaceae community structure (i.e.
column2: beta diversity) may also reflect chronic disturbance from anthropogenic activity () and help identify more resilient or resistant reefs (). Additionally
column3: macroscale communities in reef seawater
column4: sediments
column5: feces
column6: and on macro-algal surfaces may be important sources of symbiotic Symbiodiniaceae that can be acquired horizontally by prospective hosts (; ; ; ; ; ; ;
column7: ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ). Symbiodiniaceae in a free-living mode may influence important processes

column10: and gene flow within Symbiodiniaceae ().

column1: Positive and negative species interactions can occur within local Symbiodiniaceae communities resulting in resource and niche partitioning (; ; ). Quantifying these interactions may help disentangle the factors and processes governing Symbiodiniaceae community assembly in early host life history stages (; )
column2: as well as successional dynamics (or stability) in adult hosts. Studying local Symbiodiniaceae communities can also identify conditions that trigger symbiotic breakdown (i.e.
column3: dysbiosis). Dysbiosis has frequently been documented in the bacterial communities of stressed hosts (e.g.
column5: and may also be evident in local Symbiodiniaceae communities. Generally speaking
column6: dysbiosis can manifest itself in the host as: (1) an increase in symbiont richness (invasion or proliferation of low abundance symbionts)
column7: (2) a decrease in symbiont richness (loss of symbionts)
column8: or (3) more complex changes in community structure or beta diversity (). For example
column9: Symbiodinium necroappetens (; ) and some symbionts in the genera Durusdinium (; )
column10: Breviolum ()
column11: and Cladocopium () can opportunistically increase or decrease their abundance in bleached or stressed hosts. Stony coral juveniles in the field () and adults in tank-based experiments () have exhibited decreased survival in conjunction with more diverse local Symbiodiniaceae communities. Additional experiments to assess how frequently different types of dysbiosis occur in local Symbiodiniaceae communities are needed
column12: including in non-scleractinian hosts
column13: some of which can harbor up to 60 symbionts per host cell (). Testing the extent to which different types of dysbiosis are associated with specific cnidarian hosts
column14: as well as specific environmental contexts
column15: should also be prioritized.

column1: Current challenges in understanding local Symbiodiniaceae community diversity and dynamics include: (1) determining actual and relative abundances of Symbiodiniaceae species given IGV and copy number issues (see “Accounting for Copy Number Variation”); and (2) understanding the roles (if any) that low abundance Symbiodiniaceae play in holobiont survival and fitness (see ; ; ). This knowledge is key to connecting Symbiodiniaceae genotypes to phenotypes (see “Beyond Genotype: Phenotyping Symbiodiniaceae”). Low abundance Symbiodiniaceae may serve as a reservoir of in hospite algal genotypes that may increase to dominance (at least ephemerally) during or following a change in environmental conditions (; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ). The mechanisms controlling this turnover in hospite remain poorly understood
column2: but involve host rewards and sanctions (
column3: ) and competitive interactions among symbionts (). Competition among Symbiodiniaceae affects the initial uptake of symbionts in early coral ontogeny () and influences longer-term persistence in experimentally-generated symbioses ()
column4: but the relative importance of competition in shaping in hospite communities once they are established remains poorly understood. Beyond their potential to shift in hospite following bleaching events (; )
column5: low abundance Symbiodiniaceae could also contribute to emergent holobiont properties (; ). Quantification of holobiont traits with and without the addition of low abundance homologous Symbiodiniaceae (i.e.
column6: lineages that typically enter into a symbiotic relationship with a given host taxon) from a range of inoculation sources constitutes a critical next step to understanding the functional role these symbionts play in the host.

column1: Improving our understanding of the processes shaping Symbiodiniaceae communities is critical to predicting their distributions and potentially mitigating coral reef decline driven by global change. The methods below constitute suggested approaches for analyzing the diversity of macroscale and local Symbiodiniaceae communities. In some circumstances
column2: identifying numerically dominant Symbiodiniaceae lineages (as opposed to the total diversity of a Symbiodiniaceae community) may be sufficient for the question at hand because hosts are generally selective in the symbionts they harbor
column3: and some are highly specific to particular symbiont lineages (e.g.
column4: ; ). Whether quantifying numerically dominant lineages or total Symbiodiniaceae community
column5: the selection of molecular marker(s) and the approach(es) to data generation and analysis have implications for the interpretation of diversity. Molecular markers available for assessing Symbiodiniaceae community diversity are multicopy
column6: and thus
column7: present the challenge of distinguishing intragenomic from intergenomic variation. Inclusion of symbiont taxa above or below the species level in the calculation of alpha and beta community diversity is problematic as these metrics are designed for species-level input. Including anything but species-level data in the calculation of these metrics can obscure patterns and lead to under- or over-estimation of diversity.

column1: The Symbiodiniaceae SSU (i.e.
column2: ) and LSU rDNA markers as well as the cob mitochondrial marker are multicopy but are considered to behave like single copy loci because the vast majority of copies present are a single sequence. The few intragenomic sequence differences that do occur tend to be relatively straightforward to resolve in the context of identifying the dominant Symbiodiniaceae lineage within each genus. Many analysis algorithms produce amplicon sequence variants (ASVs)
column3: which are statistically inferred based on sequence variation within and among samples; the degree to which ASVs represent distinct genotypes may vary by marker and Symbiodiniaceae genus. For example
column4: LSU consistently resolves species within Symbiodinium ()
column5: but not for all of Breviolum (; ). Thus
column6: it is important to keep in mind that when assessing total community diversity (across multiple Symbiodiniaceae genera) with LSU
column7: the number of species within certain genera may be under-represented. Despite this
column8: markers that behave as if single copy are arguably the best option currently available for assessing total community diversity in Symbiodiniaceae as they avoid the many complications associated with interpreting variation from multi-copy markers ().

column1: Among the commonly used markers
column2: the hypervariable chloroplast psbA non-coding region (psbAncr) can resolve below the species level in Symbiodiniaceae (; ; ; ; )
column3: while the ITS2 region can resolve at
column4: below
column5: or above the species level depending on the lineage. Higher resolution comes at a considerable cost in terms of complexity of analyses.

column1: psbAncr: The psbAncr region can assess relatedness only among closely related Symbiodiniaceae lineages within the genus (; ). It is helpful to have a priori knowledge of the genera being amplified when using this marker (see “When should Researchers use Multiple Symbiodiniaceae Genetic Markers for Community-level Analyses?”) as available primers have known biases for specific genera. For example
column2: the Symbiodiniaceae psbAncr primers 7.4-Forw and 7.8-Rev () preferentially amplify Cladocopium in samples of mixed communities
column3: whereas the more recent psbAFor_1 and psbARev_1 do not (). Although psbAncr is multi-copy and can exhibit IGV in some species
column4: drawing inferences from these sequence datasets is still relatively straightforward because large genetic distances exist even between sequences from closely related species ()
column5: similar to markers that do not present IGV. However
column6: because the psbAncr region cannot be amplified across Symbiodiniaceae using a single set of primers
column7: this marker is suboptimal for some types of community-level analyses
column8: such as assessing total community diversity or beta diversity metrics. Nevertheless
column9: it would be appropriate to pair psbAncr with other markers; i.e.
column10: to resolve additional diversity within established ITS2 lineages (; ); and also to use this marker to verify ITS2 sequence variants generated via amplicon sequencing (; ).

column1: ITS2: The ITS2 region of Symbiodiniaceae rDNA resolves many species and some subspecies (). ITS2 has a broader application for defining lineages because one set of primers amplifies all known Symbiodiniaceae ITS2 sequences (note
column2: however
column3: that sequence variants only align well within-genus). These two favorable characteristics
column4: in concert with its history of use within the field
column5: make ITS2 a popular choice among researchers
column6: even in situations when greater resolution might be achieved with alternative marker(s). Intragenomic sequence diversity is relatively high within Symbiodiniaceae ITS2 (; ; ) and along with copy number
column7: varies considerably across genera () and likely species (though no data are currently available at this resolution). This IGV severely restricts the inferences that can be made regarding the relative abundance of community members in cases of multiple Symbiodiniaceae lineages per host (see “Accounting for Copy Number Variation”). The central issue in using ITS2 to characterize symbiont diversity in hospite is differentiating intragenomic sequence variants (those that reflect differences within one genetic entity) from intergenomic sequence variants (those that reflect differences between two or more genetic entities). This is of particular importance because
column8: unlike with psbAncr
column9: Symbiodiniaceae ITS2 intragenomic distances can be larger than intergenomic distances. Practically
column10: it can be challenging to determine if sequence variation comes from one species or multiple species. Varied awareness and treatment of this issue among Symbiodiniaceae researchers has generated significant debate
column11: which has often played out in peer review
column12: rather than being articulated
column13: addressed
column14: and resolved as a research community (see “Ensuring an Inclusive Symbiodiniaceae Research Community”).

column1: One technique to differentiate between intra- and inter-genomic sequence variants involves analyzing co-occurrence patterns. Sets of different sequences that co-occur across multiple biological replicates are more likely to be from the same genotype than to derive from multiple co-occurring lineages
column2: with each lineage contributing a subset of the sequences. This is particularly true in cases where the relative abundances of each of the sequences of the set are similar across biological replicates. There are gel-based (Denaturing Gradient Gel Electrophoresis
column3: DGGE; ) and high-throughput sequencing methods that require downstream bioinformatic analysis (e.g.
column4: ; ; ) to detect these co-occurring sequences in both dominant and low abundance taxa. Gel-based and in silico approaches each have their advantages and disadvantages
column5: which have been discussed elsewhere (). Because these techniques rely on identifying banding profiles that correspond to references (gel-based) or other biological replicates (gel- and bioinformatic-based)
column6: their power to resolve diversity generally increases with access to references or further biological replicates. For this purpose
column7: reference sets of DGGE profiles as published in the literature (e.g.
column9: or online reference databases of in silico profiles (e.g.
column10: at symportal.org) are available to researchers. However
column11: strong inferences can still often be made from relatively small datasets for Symbiodiniaceae taxa that are sampled multiple times in the dataset. Both techniques rely on the same biological assumption: that coral hosts commonly associate with one numerically dominant Symbiodiniaceae taxon per genus. In cases where this assumption does not hold–when congeneric Symbiodiniaceae co-occur in multiple biological replicates–diversity may be underestimated with multiple taxa being considered one. Identifying intergenomic and intragenomic variation is necessary for making conclusions about diversity when using multi-copy markers like ITS2. Differentiating between this variation can be challenging
column12: particularly when dealing with less common genotypes
column13: smaller numbers of biological replicates
column14: lower sequencing depths
column15: and complex communities; in these situations
column16: sequencing of the samples in question with an additional marker may be necessary. Critically
column17: such an additional marker must be able to resolve between the putative taxa. For example
column18: if attempting to ascertain whether two closely related Cladocopium taxa (e.g.
column19: within the C3-radiation) are present in a sample
column20: psbAncr would be more appropriate than cp23S as the former is highly likely to resolve between such taxa ()
column21: whereas the latter may or may not ().

Gel- or Sanger sequencing-based methods can provide qualitative information on diversity

column7: <2–11% for restriction fragment length polymorphism-based (RFLP-based) methods
column8: (); <5–30% for denaturing gradient gel electrophoresis-based (DGGE-based) methods
column9: (; ; )). Third

column11: only diversity that can be resolved can be detected

column1: When assessing the total diversity of macroscale Symbiodiniaceae communities
column2: it is important to consider how molecular techniques and approaches apply to ‘free-living Symbiodiniaceae’. In the broadest sense
column3: this term refers to all cells external to metazoan (e.g.
column4: coral
column5: mollusc) or protistan (e.g.
column6: ciliate
column7: foraminifera) hosts. These cells may be found in the water column or associated with benthic substrates. ‘Transiently free-living’ refers to Symbiodiniaceae cells that are recently released from nearby hosts but that are not adapted to proliferate outside of hosts (). In contrast
column8: ‘exclusively free-living’ refers to Symbiodiniaceae species with lifestyles entirely external to hosts (). Although exclusively free-living Symbiodiniaceae may be detected occasionally “within” host samples
column9: such detections can be interpreted as contamination resulting from host ingestion (rather than symbiosis establishment) or adherence to mucus (; ; ). Because ‘free-living’ (sensu lato) Symbiodiniaceae communities on reefs are complex mixtures of these two categories
column10: resolving this diversity presents specific challenges ().

column1: Beta diversity can be useful for measuring changes to Symbiodiniaceae community structure over space and time (; ). Although beta diversity encompasses a range of metrics including dissimilarity
column2: turnover
column3: nestedness
column4: and dispersion
column5: it is dispersion that is most commonly used to assess Symbiodiniaceae communities (; ; ; ; ; ; ). An important consideration when analyzing Symbiodiniaceae beta diversity data is establishing whether the analysis focuses on sequence beta diversity (e.g.
column6: amplicon sequence variant data
column7: which typically encompass copy number and intragenomic variability below the species level)
column8: or whether the analysis focuses on ecological beta diversity (e.g.
column9: species data). Either approach may be viable
column10: but it is important to explicitly state which is being used
column11: and to frame interpretations based on the potential pitfalls relevant to that approach.

column1: Copy number variation (CNV) is any genetic trait involving the number of copies of a gene in the genome of an individual. Efforts to quantify the absolute and relative abundances of different species in a local Symbiodiniaceae community are complicated by the presence of high CNV across taxa for key markers such as ITS2 (; ; ; ; ). Thus
column2: relative rDNA-PCR amplicon abundance does not necessarily equate to actual abundance of Symbiodiniaceae cells in a sample (especially when inter-genus comparisons are being made; ; ; ; ). For instance
column3: some symbiont taxa that have been reported to possess a considerably higher rDNA copy number than others (e.g.
column4: Cladocopium spp.; ); these high copy number taxa can appear to be abundant in mixed communities even though they might represent a low fraction of cells in hospite
column5: leading to inaccurate estimation of actual symbiont abundances (). The incorrect classification of low abundance vs. dominant taxa can impact interpretations related to biogeography and ecology. Such errors could be avoided if a correction factor is applied (e.g.
column6: dividing the abundance value by the number of copies present in the genome of the relevant species
column8: but such corrections rely on accurate copy number reference values
column9: which are not currently available for the majority of Symbiodiniaceae taxa. Most studies that have quantified CNV have been limited to comparisons between genera
column10: and there is considerable variation in the values reported across studies (e.g.
column11: ; ; ; ; ; ). Inconsistencies in reported CNV values may be attributed to variation among strains or species (within-genus differences can be as large as between-genus differences) or to methodological differences between studies. Therefore
column12: the extent of CNV within Symbiodiniaceae genera or across populations largely remains to be established.

column1: Lineage-specific qPCR assays have helped to quantitatively characterize mixed communities at the genus level (; ; ) and species level (). These targeted qPCR assays are more quantitative than sequencing approaches
column2: but still require correction for CNV. When applied to systems with known symbiont diversity
column3: qPCR can accurately and cost-effectively quantify local symbiont community structure and dynamics; however
column4: these primer sets must be developed on a per-taxon basis. Another approach is the use of flow cytometry to quantify and/or physically separate cells of interest. While the natural variability in cell characteristics (e.g.
column5: size
column6: shape
column7: fluorescence) cannot distinguish taxa ()
column8: the use of fluorescent probes to tag taxa of interest has successfully quantified the absolute and relative abundance of co-occurring taxa (; ). Importantly
column9: these methods are also conducive to subsequent genetic and physiological analyses of sorted cells. The development of further resources to account for CNV is an important priority within the field (see “Integrating Multiomic Technologies to Study Symbiodiniaceae”).

column1: As each marker has its own evolutionary history and methodological bias (e.g.
column2: primer bias
column3: CNV
column4: etc.)
column5: congruence among multiple independent markers should enable more robust characterization of Symbiodiniaceae community ecology (; ; ; ; ; ; ; ). Where possible
column6: when multiple markers are used
column7: the markers should complement each other’s taxonomic scope and power to resolve. For example
column8: it may be productive to pair the cp23S (larger taxonomic scope
column9: lower resolving power) with the psbAncr (smaller taxonomic scope
column10: higher resolving power). Instances where multiple markers produce conflicting results may help identify a Symbiodiniaceae lineage that cannot be accurately characterized with a single broad taxonomic marker. Additionally
column11: combining multiple markers can provide greater resolution and improved interpretability compared to single-marker approaches. For example
column12: the use of psbAncr can help overcome issues associated with interpretation of IGV and high copy number in ITS2 and provide support for ITS2-type profiles () by confirming which ITS2 IGVs are most likely part of a single lineage (; ). Budget and logistics permitting
column13: it is recommended to use multiple markers in some situations. For some ITS2 lineages (e.g.
column14: Breviolum B1 or Cladocopium C15; ; )

column16: but universal primers have yet to be designed for gene regions that capture this variation (e.g.
column17: psbAncr). Therefore

We recognize that each additional marker can greatly increase high-throughput sequencing project costs
column19: so such designs are only recommended when resources are available. Single marker studies can still provide great insight into symbiont community diversity as long as they are interpreted carefully.

Diversity metrics merit careful attention and gene sequence diversity should not be conflated with species diversity. Authors (and reviewers and editors) can weigh what results

column1: Not all genetically distinct Symbiodiniaceae taxa exhibit physiological differences (e.g.
column2: through functional convergence; ; )
column3: whereas unique isolates of the same taxon may be functionally divergent (e.g.
column4: ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ). This is not surprising given the effects of strong local selection within reef habitats (; ; ; ; ) and the role of acclimatization (). Stringent functional interrogation is therefore critical to determining how healthy cnidarian-symbiont associations will survive the climate crisis. This goal rests on advancing physiological descriptions

column6: and extending the methodological toolbox to characterize Symbiodiniaceae differences. Thus

column8: helping to build greater community consensus on methods and standards for describing phenotypes of interest ().

column1: While it is valuable to confirm symbiont traits when in symbiosis

column4: other algal-centric physiological metrics

column6: will be difficult to interpret. Single-cell sorting techniques may help assess unique phenotypic distinctions across different symbiont species from the same host ()
column7: but these techniques constitute additional effort and cost. Moreover

column9: prokaryotic
column10: and viral symbionts (; ; )
column11: some of which are associated with colony health and resilience to environmental stress (; ; ). Some viruses even infect Symbiodiniaceae cells themselves (; )
column12: with diverse potential impacts on Symbiodiniaceae phenotypes (; ). The degree to which these “secondary” symbionts impact the observed phenotype of Symbiodiniaceae cells in hospite is an active area of research (; ). Finally
column13: coral tissue thickness
column14: pigmentation
column15: skeletal reflectance
column16: or other coral-associated microorganisms can affect irradiance levels reaching Symbiodiniaceae in hospite (; ; ; ; ; ;
column17: ). Variation in these physiological metrics can therefore affect symbiont phenotype

column1: Traits where variability across species exceeds that within populations are ideally suited for phenotypic analysis
column2: but are presently unknown to the field or are challenging to measure in consistent and ecologically meaningful ways. Consequently
column3: high-throughput approaches for assessing Symbiodiniaceae phenotypes need to consider tradeoffs that are constrained by end goals. For example
column4: recent high-throughput approaches for assessing thermal tolerance at the whole coral level—such as coral bleaching automated stress systems (CBASS; )—and the single cell level () have incorporated short thermal challenges followed by stress characterization through the measurement of 1–2 physiological variables such as maximum PSII photochemical efficiency (Fv/Fm) and cell density. While single-phenotype assays can be informative within the context of ecosystem service values (e.g.
column5: identifying thermally tolerant corals for nursery propagation; )
column6: identification of functionally distinct Symbiodiniaceae phenotypes will benefit from measuring a broader spectrum of physiological metrics (). Phenotypic characterization using multiple photosynthetic metrics can provide some species-specific resolution ()
column7: and the non-invasive nature of chlorophyll a fluorometry lends itself to high-throughput approaches. However
column8: poor contextualization of photosynthetic parameters with respect to cnidarian resilience currently limits the use of these techniques alone for large-scale phenomic studies
column9: and may ultimately require integration of fitness metrics influenced by resource availability such as elemental composition via nutrient acquisition. While specific consensus on measurement protocols is beyond the scope of this perspective
column10: taking a multidisciplinary approach and transparently documenting important methodological choices will help move the field forward.

column1: Axenic or bacteria-depleted cultures are promising tools for connecting Symbiodiniaceae genotypes to phenotypes because their genetic identity is readily determined (see “Guidance for Species-Level Assessment of Symbiodiniaceae Diversity”) and morphological
column2: physiological
column3: and behavioral diversity are readily discernible among such algal isolates (; ; ). In terms of photo-physiology
column4: fluorometry has become a convenient and accessible tool to gauge “culture health” (; ; ). Fluorometry is also used in studies examining phenotypic variation focused on photosynthetic traits and how they are affected by resource availability (light
column5: nutrients) and temperature (; )
column6: and has been inferred to reflect holobiont health (). While photosynthetic traits are informative of cellular functioning
column7: they are insufficient in isolation of other measurements to explain phenotypic variation in growth (e.g.
column8: ; ; ). Recent data point to variable photo-physiological tolerance and thermal plasticity of genetically divergent Symbiodiniaceae grown in monoculture
column9: which has contributed to a deeper understanding of the algal symbiont response to increasing sea surface temperatures (; ; ; ). However
column10: a large number of genetically distinct algal symbionts identified in hospite have resisted sustained growth in culture (e.g. ; ). Furthermore
column11: physiological and functional ’omics data indicate that when in culture or freshly isolated
column12: Symbiodiniaceae exhibit responses to thermal stress that differ from those of the same population in hospite (; ; ). These data suggest that some physiological traits measured from culture-based studies may not be easily extrapolated to the symbiotic state. Such issues are particularly pronounced when measuring nutrient-associated phenotypes
column13: as most culture media are nutrient-replete while Symbiodiniaceae in hospite appear to be nutrient-limited ().

column2: that can also be easily assessed in nature (in hospite)

column6: or prior to

column8: as well as the actual environments in the cultures (light quality/quantity
column9: nutrients) as opposed to those measured in the incubators or assumed from the recipe of the medium used (e.g.

Ensuring inter-comparability among studies in the future will similarly depend on operating under a more consistent set of measurement protocols for phenotypic traits.

column1: With continued cost reductions and increases in computational power and accessibility
column2: advanced “-omic” technologies
column3: including genomics
column4: transcriptomics
column5: proteomics
column6: and metabolomics
