column1: Whole-genome sequencing (WGS) remains the gold standard for capturing genetic diversity (; ; ; ; ;
column2: ). While individual and concatenated genes can help resolve phylogenetic relationships and define taxonomic lineages (; )
column3: WGS data provide more comprehensive phylogenomic signals
column4: which can be used to investigate divergent selection. For example
column5: while a Symbiodiniaceae phylogeny reconstructed using k-mers (short
column6: sub-sequences of defined length k) derived from whole-genome sequences is largely consistent with the phylogeny reconstructed with LSU rDNA data ()
column7: different genomic regions exhibit distinct phylogenetic signals (). Further comparison of WGS data indicate that the similarity shared between different species within the genus Symbiodinium is comparable to that between different Symbiodiniaceae genera
column8: revealing more extensive divergence than anticipated and suggesting a need for future revision ().

column1: WGS efforts employ short- or long-read technologies
column2: or a combination of both strategies. Short-read sequencing technologies (e.g.
column3: Illumina) have typically offered a cost-effective approach for deep sequencing with low error rates (<1%) and have provided valuable insights into Symbiodiniaceae diversity
column4: including gene family expansions across different Symbiodiniaceae lineages (; ; ). Though short-read sequencing can identify lineage-specific divergence
column5: short reads are difficult to assemble
column6: especially with highly repetitive genomic regions. Long-read sequencing technologies such as Pacific Biosciences (PacBio) or Oxford Nanopore Technologies offer a viable alternative to improve the contiguity of highly fragmented short-read genomes. However
column7: long-read sequencing technologies are more expensive and error-prone relative to short-read platforms
column8: though these technologies are rapidly advancing (). Long-read data allow us to observe chromosome structure
column9: a greater number of genomic elements
column10: such as DNA transposons
column11: long terminal repeats
column12: or chromosomal enrichment for genes with similar biological functions (; ; ). Chromosome-level assemblies represent a major milestone in dinoflagellate genomics as they confirm that many genes are encoded in unidirectional clusters which correspond to large topological domains (; ). Not only does this discovery provide insights into the structure of genes in Symbiodiniaceae
column13: but the structure can be correlated with the encoded genes and their expression patterns to observe their interactions
column14: elucidating novel insights into the evolution of diverse Symbiodiniaceae lineages at the chromosome level ().

column1: Efforts are underway to expand the number of high-quality short- and long-read assemblies for cnidarian-associated and free-living Symbiodiniaceae and incorporate these data into taxonomic descriptions (; ; ). Additionally
column2: the two read types can be coupled (e.g.
column3: Illumina with PacBio HiFi) to incorporate both the contiguous sequences (>20 kb) of long reads with the low error rate of short reads
column4: allowing robust comparisons of sequence divergence within and across genomes (). Once more data become available
column5: it may be feasible to incorporate whole-genome information into future taxonomic and systematic revisions to the family (). However
column6: it will be crucial to achieve consensus on how to use these data to study Symbiodiniaceae diversity and taxonomy. A lack of consistency in methodology and quality standards persists
column7: making cross-study analyses difficult (e.g.
column8: ). First steps in such standardization have been taken (; )
column9: but will need to be expanded as more genome data become available and the community using these data grows. Additionally
column10: the current costs of completely sequencing the genomes of hundreds of potential Symbiodiniaceae species remains prohibitive. For the near future
column11: feasible alternatives for WGS include using reduced representation phylogenomic approaches (such as those targeting ultraconserved elements; ; )
column12: full-length rDNA gene amplicons ()
column13: or entire organellar genome sequences (). These alternatives may represent a compromise between WGS and phylogenetic marker studies by providing an intermediate amount of sequence information for taxonomic accuracy.

column1: Researchers are keen to make functional inferences about Symbiodiniaceae
column2: which requires focusing on coding regions. To do so requires sequencing the collection of RNAs within the cells (i.e.
column3: transcriptomics). Transcriptome sequencing characterizes molecular phenotypes
column4: such as transient responses to the environment
column5: and can reveal differential gene expression among taxa that could reflect selective pressures driving Symbiodiniaceae diversification at the functional level (; ; ). However
column6: the extent of gene expression changes among Symbiodiniaceae is often surprisingly subtle (e.g.
column8: although this is a matter of current debate (e.g.
column9: ; ). Furthermore
column10: transcription can be influenced through alternatively spliced transcripts (; )
column11: RNA editing (; ; )
column12: microRNA interactions ()
column13: and methylation of mRNAs (; ; ). These post-transcriptional modifications create variation in the transcriptome
column14: which can complicate transcriptomic interpretation
column15: but tracking the conservation and divergence of these variations across the Symbiodiniaceae phylogeny may elucidate novel insights into the evolution of diverse lineages.

column1: The primary concern with bulk transcriptomic analysis is that methods often pool transcriptomes from all Symbiodiniaceae cells within a host sample
column2: so only “average” expression profiles can be generated (). This approach may obscure nuances in the interactions between specific symbiont and host cells
column3: especially for less abundant symbionts. Single-cell transcriptomics (i.e.
column4: isolating individual cells and sequencing their transcriptomes) could solve this issue as gene expression could be explored within and among each symbiont cell in hospite. The generation of a cell atlas for the coral Stylophora pistillata has enabled the characterization of fine-scale metabolic interactions between symbionts and host gastrodermal cells (). Single-cell sequencing can also enable high-resolution interrogations of how Symbiodiniaceae and host cells interact during symbiosis establishment
column5: maintenance
column6: and breakdown
column7: particularly when Symbiodiniaceae cells can be isolated from different parts of the host coral that exhibit contrasting physiologies. By comparing expression from symbiont cells derived from different positions in the coral colony
column8: the location and ecological role of Symbiodiniaceae can be characterized
column9: which is a major priority for improving our understanding of symbiont communities within cnidarians.

column1: Proteomic analyses provide an alternative mechanism to explore Symbiodiniaceae physiology and the functional impacts of different symbionts on cnidarian-algal associations
column2: such as metabolic mismatches that occur when hosts associate with atypical (heterologous) symbionts (). Proteomic analyses use liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry to identify and quantify proteins
column3: which are more directly linked to phenotype than transcript abundance (). “Bottom-up” or “shotgun” approaches are commonly employed to identify and quantify as many proteins as possible in a sample in an untargeted manner
column4: and with modern instrumentation
column7: 000 proteins are commonly quantified in established model systems as well as Symbiodiniaceae (; ). Two features of proteomic analyses are particularly powerful. One is the characterization of post-translational modifications
column8: such as protein phosphorylation
column9: oxidation
column10: acetylation
column11: or ubiquitination (). Post-translational modifications help regulate protein activity and are crucial in many biological processes
column12: yet they cannot be detected by pre-translational analyses. The second is the ability to localize proteins to particular cellular compartments by either selectively enriching such compartments () or fractionating the global cell content. Spatial proteomics aims to resolve the compositional architecture of cells by mapping the subcellular location of thousands of proteins simultaneously (). Spatial resolution is achieved by separation and differential enrichment of cell content via density gradients or step-wise centrifugation and the subsequent quantitation of relative protein abundance across these fractions (; ). Protein populations from differentially enriched organelles
column13: membranes
column14: and molecular complexes will have consistent but distinct distribution profiles and can thus be classified and mapped accordingly. Global proteome maps are powerful blueprints of the cell and can provide functional context for many uncharacterized proteins. This may be of particular use with Symbiodiniaceae
column15: where sequence homology-based gene annotation is less effective due to their phylogenetic distance from well-studied organisms
column16: and due to the highly derived genomes of dinoflagellates. However
column17: these methods still require high-quality protein model search databases derived from genomic or transcriptomic sequences as they cannot identify proteins from complex samples de novo. Protein expression studies in combination with spatial proteomes will be powerful tools that can
column18: for example
column19: provide insights into the known architectural and physiological changes that accompany the symbiotic engagement of Symbiodiniaceae with their hosts. In the broader context
column20: proteomics as a means to study the functional phenotype of Symbiodiniaceae under various conditions will likely overcome many of the current limitations of gene expression studies in dinoflagellates.

column1: Epigenomics
column2: genome editing
column3: metabolomics
column4: and volatilomics are emerging areas within Symbiodiniaceae research. Epigenomic mechanisms
column5: such as DNA methylation or chromatin modification
column6: can modulate gene expression via gene suppression
column7: gene enhancement
column8: alternative mRNA splicing
column9: or the regulation of spurious transcription without requiring any changes to genomic sequences (; ; ). DNA methylation is one epigenetic modification that occurs when methyl groups are added to DNA nucleotides
column10: altering how transcriptional proteins bind to promoter regions thereby altering gene expression (). Symbiodiniaceae have unusually high levels of genome methylation (). Originally
column11: the high level of methylation raised uncertainty about whether methylation actually played a role in gene regulation
column12: but methylation has been linked to differential gene expression with varying irradiance (). Thus far
column13: epigenomic analyses have largely been focused on the host animal
column14: and questions are often centered around how methylation contributes to environmental tolerance (; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ; ). Therefore
column15: determining how methylation contributes to Symbiodiniaceae functional diversity requires further exploration.

column1: Overall
column2: Symbiodiniaceae genomes are very difficult to annotate. At present
column3: dinoflagellate genome and transcriptome projects rarely manage to annotate >50% of putative coding sequences via homology searches against genes that have been functionally characterized in other organisms (; ). In the future
column4: genome editing could be better developed to knock out Symbiodiniaceae genes with unknown functions
column5: making it easier to determine their biological roles. UV mutagenesis is a classic method for introducing mutations; it was used recently to create photosynthesis mutants via screening of colored mutants ()
column6: but its random nature is less than ideal for reverse genetics. RNA silencing is a more targeted approach that could potentially be exploited in Symbiodiniaceae studies ()
column7: but the rapidly advancing CRISPR/Cas9 technology is most desirable for its ability to knock out specific genes. Although genome editing efforts for protists have made encouraging progress ()
column8: success with Symbiodiniaceae remains elusive (; but see ).

column1: Biochemical analyses of Symbiodiniaceae are also in the early stages. Characterization of metabolic products (metabolomics) and volatile organic compounds (volatilomics) can provide insights into molecular cross-talk between partners. Among Symbiodiniaceae
column2: both metabolomic and volatilomic profiles are species-specific
column3: but they also fluctuate with environmental conditions (; ; ). Distinct biochemical profiles reflect the interactions and coadaptation (or lack thereof) between the host and symbiont ()
column4: so biochemical assays can lead to a greater understanding of the drivers of Symbiodiniaceae evolution. As with all the omics methods mentioned so far
column5: if metabolomics and volatilomics are to be used to understand Symbiodiniaceae divergence
column6: more data spanning the phylogeny will be required.

column1: Integrative approaches that use more than one type of technology may be required to answer intricate research questions about Symbiodiniaceae biology. For example

column9: can improve our ability to elucidate molecular mechanisms associated with phenotypes of interest. Thus

column12: yet the financial costs associated with using multiple technologies in parallel remains a limiting factor. Additionally

column17: which are desperately needed to meet the formidable challenges of the coral reef crisis.

column1: Equity and diversity issues exist in the scholarly publication process at multiple levels and across different areas of research. Men are first authors more often than women ()
column2: notably even when both authors are identified as having contributed equally to the work (). Such systematic and implicit gender biases are also evident in the peer-review processes (). Manuscript authors
column3: irrespective of gender
column4: are also less likely to suggest women reviewers (). Unprofessional reviews disproportionately impact members of underrepresented groups
column5: who report greater self-doubt after receiving such reviews
column6: ultimately reducing scientific productivity overall (). Beyond peer evaluation
column7: more men serve in editorial roles than women (; ; ; ; )
column8: and editors tend to invite men more often than women to write invited reviews or perspectives. For example
column9: the journal Molecular Ecology
column10: which often publishes research from the Symbiodiniaceae community
column11: found significant gender bias in authorship of invited ‘perspective’ articles
column12: with women only authoring between 17.2–28.6% of these pieces ().

column1: Language biases are also pervasive. English is currently the default language of science
column2: which disadvantages scientists who do not consider English as their primary language (). Non-native English speakers spend on average 97 more writing hours than native English speakers on preparation for each manuscript (). In addition
column3: ideas may be lost in translation or are often challenging to explain in a secondary language (). There are also costs associated with publishing in a non-native language: for example
column4: paying for translation and editorial services. Conversely
column5: fluent English speakers publish more research articles at higher rates than non-English speakers (). To address English-centric journals
column6: regional journals publish in their native languages ()
column7: but these publications are read by a smaller readership and are cited less ()
column8: and thereby viewed as less impactful
column9: and are less likely to be shared widely in the Symbiodiniaceae community.

column1: General actions that can be taken within our community to ensure a more equitable publication process include: (1) increasing diversity on editorial boards
column2: (2) increasing the diversity of invited reviewers
column3: as well as the authors we review for; (3) promoting cost reduction strategies (as implemented in journals like Frontiers
column4: PeerJ
column5: and PLoS One) whereby publication fees are prorated by country or institution type
column6: as well as other strategies that reduce editorial costs for non-native speakers (); (4) promoting double-blind review processes (); (5) intentionally citing articles led by diverse colleagues (e.g.
column7: from diverse gender identities and geographical areas)
column8: thereby increasing the diversity of perspectives in the field that contribute to discussion; and (6) gathering data about where and how these inequities exist and working together as a community to take actionable steps for equity in science.

column1: Parachute science
column2: sometimes referred to as “helicopter” or “colonial” science
column3: is a common practice whereby members of the scientific community from higher-income countries fail to involve local/indigenous/native people in an equitable fashion when performing research in lower-income countries (; ). These practices tend to be more common in ecology and conservation research ()
column4: including coral reef studies. As a community
column5: we need to understand the largely exploitative history of our discipline and avoid perpetuating it. We should stay informed regarding the history of the lands and peoples who live in the areas in which we conduct our studies. Our specific recommendations include: (1) developing laboratory manuals that include sections outlining values and best practices (including ethics approval and necessary permits); (2) adequately training students to conduct transparent research and develop equitable relationships with members of the host region; (3) supporting the establishment of long-term collaborations and exchange programs to involve local students in research; and (4) including the development of these relationships as important components of the tenure and promotion process in departments and institutions.

column1: Importantly
column2: researchers from institutions in high-income regions (e.g.
column3: North America
column4: Australia
column5: Western Europe) should: (1) be sensitive to the many challenges their colleagues in lower-income regions experience
column6: such as a lack of funding
column7: infrastructure
column8: and institutional support; (2) be respectful of these collaborators by treating them as peers and not as assistants
column9: involving them in all steps of the science
column10: and acknowledging their intellectual contributions during discussions
column11: and; (3) be fair to these researchers and their contributions through continued involvement in planning
column12: manuscripts
column13: projects
column14: and grants generated through these collaborations. Together
column15: these strategies can facilitate a more inclusive and collaborative Symbiodiniaceae community (; ). Importantly
column16: integration of members of the local community provides a long-term context to the collected scientific data
column17: such as anecdotal observations (e.g.
column18: episodes of bleaching) that may be critically informative to the research.

column1: There is an urgent need to increase the accessibility of Symbiodiniaceae science and foster collaboration
column2: as innovation is necessary to address the coral reef crisis. Toward this goal
column3: we have developed a living
column4: global database of Symbiodiniaceae researchers and their key research expertise (http://symcollab.reefgenomics.org/; ). The database can be queried based on topic and methodology to aid scientists in diversifying their networks. Researchers joining the database are then also invited to participate in an open Slack channel (‘Sym Slack’
column5: https://symslack.slack.com). These resources connect and promote diverse researchers and facilitate discussions of science from different perspectives. The COVID-19 pandemic also showcased the effectiveness of virtual conferences (case in point: this perspective is the product of a virtual workshop). Maintaining hybrid conferences with reduced costs for virtual attendance along with staggered schedules and access to presentation recordings to accommodate different time zones would ensure that the sharing of scientific information is more inclusive
column6: alongside continued efforts to drop conference charges for lower-income countries (e.g.
column7: the 15th International Coral Reef Symposium in 2022). Therefore
column8: we encourage conference organizers to facilitate virtual attendance and funding sources for those who have difficulties traveling to foster equitable networking opportunities across people from diverse backgrounds and academic stages. Additionally
column9: incentivizing consortia of Symbiodiniaceae researchers across diverse career stages and locations and explicitly engaging researchers from marginalized backgrounds would lead to stronger capacity building and greater transfer of knowledge. Lastly
column10: we encourage sponsors to continue expanding their funding schemes to support international collaborations. Several examples of these efforts exist
column11: including a new grant solicitation from the United States National Science Foundation
column12: which calls for collaborations with Brazilian scientists through the São Paulo Research Foundation. Similar schemes (e.g.
column13: Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft) also exist to foster collaboration between researchers in developing countries. The United States Fulbright Program and the European Union Marie Sklodowska-Curie Program are other examples that support collaborations across countries. The Japanese Society for the Promotion of Science provides funding for international exchange and research for graduate students
column14: postdoctoral scholars
column15: and early career scientists. These types of funding mechanisms are important because they promote wealth sharing across countries
column16: encourage collaboration while thwarting parachute science
column17: help with international challenges including research permitting
column18: and ultimately lead to more open sharing of data and ideas within our Symbiodiniaceae research community and beyond.

column1: Addressing (and ultimately solving) the challenges associated with the coral reef crisis is increasingly urgent as climate change accelerates. Microalgae in the family Symbiodiniaceae play a critical role in determining coral bleaching outcomes. Advancing our knowledge of the genetic diversity of these organisms
column2: how their diversity functionally impacts coral bleaching
column3: and how we can apply such knowledge to mitigate climate change consequences is vital. We have identified consensus approaches for studies of Symbiodiniaceae genetic diversity at the species and population levels
column4: while recognizing several outstanding issues regarding the characterization of community diversity. We highlight key paths forward for research including exploration of the phenotypic landscape and leveraging new technologies that are broadly applied in model systems. We also emphasize the need for increased collaboration and inclusivity among Symbiodiniaceae researchers. Overall
column5: we acknowledge the dire need for advancing our understanding of Symbiodiniaceae ecology
column6: physiology
column7: and evolution
column8: which will have the potential to expedite restoration practices and facilitate management decisions as we continue to push for political action on climate change.

83|10.7717/peerj.16586|Characterization of trade-offs between immunity and reproduction in the coral species Astrangia poculata| Villafranca  Natalie |2023-12-4|The ability to successfully defend against a variety of pathogenic threats is an essential component of organismal fitness (). However
column2: most immune defenses which prevent or mitigate damage from pathogens are energetically costly (). This is particularly important as organisms operate using a fixed budget of energetic resources that must be allocated to competing demands (i.e.
column3: growth
column4: reproduction
column5: defense
column6: etc; ). Consequently
column7: allocation of resources to one demand often comes at the cost of others
column8: creating trade-offs (). Optimal organismal fitness therefore requires careful allocation of resources across these demands to minimize negative effects of trade-offs and maximize reproductive output. Notably
column9: the high costs of maintaining immune systems and mounting immune responses often result in trade-offs with other traits (; ; ; ; ). Among the most well documented immune-related trade-offs are those between reproduction and immunity
column10: with organisms most often able to exhibit high immune capacity or high reproductive ability
column11: but not both (; ; ; ; ). However
column12: despite frequent empirical observation of immune-reproductive trade-offs and robust proposed theory
column13: many questions still remain regarding the generalizability of resource allocation theory and immune-related trade-offs. In some systems
column14: consistent immune-reproductive trade-offs have not been observed (; ; ; ; ). Furthermore
column15: it remains unclear how dynamic changes in resources/energetic budget might impact these trade-offs. It is theorized that since total energy and relative allocation are co-dependent
column16: variation in energy budget may have an impact on trade-offs such as those between immunity and reproduction
column17: however limited empirical evidence exists to support or refute this theory (; ; ; ). Combined
column18: these gaps in existing theory make it difficult to predict how resources are allocated among traits
column19: requiring further study to improve understanding of resource allocation and immune trade-offs in complex contexts. This is particularly important in light of the rising prevalence of epizootic outbreaks affecting vulnerable species across the globe (; ; ).

column1: Scleractinian or reef building corals are arguably one of the taxa most impacted by increases in epizootic outbreaks (; ; ). Disease outbreaks have been one of the largest drivers of coral declines in recent decades
column2: affecting almost all major reef building species (; ; ; ; ; ). Still
column3: despite the severity of these outbreaks
column4: many gaps remain in our understanding of associated disease processes. For example
column5: significant inter- and intra-specific variation has been observed in coral disease resilience (; ; ). While some studies have pointed to the importance of divergence in certain cellular processes in driving this variation (; ; ; ; )
column6: little is known regarding how broader ecological processes
column7: including resource allocation
column8: might contribute to variation in disease resistance. To date
column9: most studies of cnidarian resource allocation have focused on stress associated trade-offs. The negative effects of thermal anomalies on coral reproductive output have been well documented (; ; ). In contrast
column10: only a handful of studies have documented reproductive trade-offs involving immunological processes
column11: all of which had focused on the negative impacts of disease outbreaks on coral reproduction (; ). No studies have directly linked reproductive investment and constitutive immunity in cnidarians
column12: in part due to the historic lack of tractable model cnidarian systems.

column1: Recent advances in the development of cnidarian model and study systems have greatly expanded the scope of cnidarian research. One such emergent model species is Astrangia poculata (common name: northern star coral)
column2: a non-reef building stony coral that can be found along the Atlantic Coast of the United States ranging from Cape Cod
column3: MA to the Texas Gulf Coast (). Importantly
column4: unlike tropical corals which are dependent on symbiotic relationships for survival
column5: A. poculata associates facultatively with a single species of symbiont
column6: Breviolum psygmophilum () with colonies ranging from high symbiont density (“brown”) to low symbiont density (“white”; ). Classification of symbiotic states is based on color
column7: approximate chlorophyll concentration
column8: and symbiont density (“brown” >106 cells cm−2; “white” 104–106 cells cm−2; ). A key benefit of the symbiotic relationship between corals and their symbionts is the exchange of organic nutrients to the host (). Consequently
column9: facultative symbiosis may serve as a natural system for exploring the effects of variable resource budget on immune-associated trade-offs in corals
column10: though the exact effects of variation in symbiont density on host energetic budget are poorly studied (). In contrast
column11: recent studies have highlighted significant impacts of variation in A. poculata symbiont density on host immunity (; )
column12: suggesting that variation in host-symbiont dynamics have significant consequences for broader organismal physiology.

column1: Here we used the tractable A. poculata study system to investigate cnidarian resource allocation and potential trade-offs between reproduction and immunity (i.e.
column2: negative associations between reproduction and immunity resulting from resource limitation). We first assessed general effects of variability in symbiont density on host physiology (reproductive output
column3: energetic budget
column4: and immune activity). Next
column5: we assessed the effects of energetic budget on both reproductive output and immune activity generally
column6: independent of symbiont density. Finally
column7: we tested for reproductive-immune trade-offs. Our study provides a preliminary framework for future studies considering the inter-connected roles of symbiosis
column8: resource allocation
column9: and immunity in cnidarians.

column1: Portions of this manuscript were previously published as a preprint ().

Entire colonies of “white” (low symbiont density; n = 13) and “brown” (high symbiont densities; n = 7) A. poculata ranging in size from ~5–61 cm (17–86 polyps) were collected from Fort Wetherill in Jamestown
column2: Rhode Island (41°28′40″N
column4: at a depth of 10–15 m. Corals were collected under RI Department of Environmental Management Permit #825 issued to Koty Sharp/Roger Williams University. Previous studies have indicated A. poculata reaches peak gametogenesis between August and September each year (). Samples were returned to Roger Williams University (Bristol
column5: Rhode Island)
column6: and held overnight prior to experimental spawning. To trigger spawning
column7: individuals were moved from the recirculating tanks at ambient temperatures (~19 °C) to individual containers with 100 mL of filtered seawater heated to 27 °C. Colonies were then closely monitored for signs of spawning. Colony sex was confirmed based on gametes released at time of spawning (A. poculata is gonochoristic; ). Twenty male colonies were identified and used for the study
column8: 13 white and seven brown. Individuals were allowed to spawn for 30 min
column9: at which point gamete release had nearly or completely ceased. Following this period adult colonies were removed and flash frozen in liquid nitrogen. The water in the container was then thoroughly mixed and a 1 mL sample was taken from each colony for sperm density estimation. Sperm density was estimated by counting in triplicate on a haemocytometer; counts were then normalized to number of polyps per colony prior to statistical analyses. Flash frozen adult corals were later shipped to Texas State University for downstream sample processing and analysis.

column1: Flash frozen adult colonies were airbrushed using a Paasche air brusher (VL0221) with 100 mM Tris + 0.5 mM Dithiothreitol (DTT; pH 7.8) to remove tissue from the skeleton and extract symbionts and host-enriched proteins (). First
column2: in order to estimate symbiont density
column3: an area of 2.14 cm2 (1-2 polyps) was marked on a flat surface of the colony. Tissue was airbrushed from the area until no tissue remained. The resulting tissue slurry was placed in a 2.0 mL microcentrifuge tube
column4: and homogenized for 10 seconds using a handheld homogenizer (Fisherbrand 150). Following homogenization
column5: samples were centrifuged (2000 RPM for 3 min) and then washed with 500 µl of DI H2O. This process was repeated twice; final samples were preserved in 500 µl of Deionized H2O + 0.01% Sodium Dodecyl Sulfate (SDS) and stored at 4 °C. Symbiont density was later determined via microscopy (Nikon Eclipse E600) by counting in triplicate on a hemocytometer (; ).

column1: A cell free tissue extract was generated from the remaining portion of the A. poculata colony following established protocols (). Host immunity was then characterized using a suite of established biochemical immune assays designed to measure multiple components of host coral immunity. Specifically we measured antioxidant activity (catalase and peroxidase)
column2: components of the melanin synthesis cascade (total phenoloxidase activity and melanin concentration)
column3: and antibacterial activity following established protocols optimized for A. poculata (). The assays capture three main components of the coral immune system. Antioxidant activity is essential in both stress and immune response
column4: mitigating the impacts of toxic reactive oxygen species produced by pathogens
column5: as a byproduct of other immune pathways
column6: or as a result of stress (; ). The melanin synthesis cascade is a multi-functional pathway with important roles in immune defense. Melanin can be used to encapsulate pathogens
column7: and is directly cytotoxic to pathogens (). The production of melanin is dependent on phenoloxidase enzymes which are secreted in an inactive form (; ); total phenoloxidase assays captures activity of both active and inactive phenoloxidases. Finally
column8: we measured the ability of cell free protein extracts to inhibit growth of the known coral pathogen
column9: Vibrio coralliilyticus (Strain RE22Sm provided by D. Nelson at University of Rhode Island;
column10: ) as a metric of antibacterial activity. All immune assays were run in triplicate on 96 well plates using a Cytation 1 cell imaging multi-mode reader and Gen 5 software (BioTek Instruments
column11: Winooski
column12: VT
column13: USA). A Red660 (G Biosciences
column14: St. Louis
column15: Missouri) assay was used to determine protein concentration to standardize immune activity metrics (). Full processing and assay details can be found in .

column1: Energetic budget
column2: specifically lipid and carbohydrate concentration
column3: were estimated using a portion of the generated cell free tissue extract. Measurement of lipid and carbohydrate concentration is a standard approach for estimating coral energetic budget () A standard coral protocol for quantification of lipids within coral tissue slurries was used to estimate lipid content (). Similarly
column4: we estimated total carbohydrate concentration following previously established protocols (; ). Both lipid and carbohydrate concentration were standardized by dry tissue weight. Full methodological details can be found in .

column1: All statistical analyses were conducted in R (version 4.2.1; )
column2: with the RStudio integrated development environment (). Prior to statistical analyses
column3: outliers were removed based on results of a Rosner Test (EnvStats
column4: v 2.7.0; ) One outlier was identified and removed each for peroxidase
column5: melanin
column6: and lipid concentration. Data was checked for normality using a Shapiro Test (base R)
column7: and total phenoloxidase activity and sperm density were normalized using a log transformation. We then conducted statistical analyses taking the following approach: first we tested specific hypotheses regarding variation in symbiont density across colony types
column8: effects of variation in symbiont density
column9: and drivers of variation in sperm density. Then we considered the factors contributing to variation in immune function using both multivariate and univariate approaches. Full details of our statistical approaches are below.

column1: First
column2: differences in symbiont density between white and brown colonies were assessed using a non-parametric t-test (Wilcoxon test; base R) as symbiont density data distribution could not be normalized. Next
column3: the impact of both symbiotic state (categorical) and symbiont density (continuous) on lipid and carbohydrate concentration was evaluated using linear models (base R). Finally
column4: the impact of symbiont density and energetic budget (lipid and carbohydrate concentration) on sperm density was assessed. Using the R package MuMIn (v. 1.47.5; ) we dredged all possible linear models explaining this relationship

column6: and then applied model averaging for the best fit models (Δ AIC < 2).

column4: and sperm density on immune parameters. First
column5: a MANOVA was run to test for the impacts of symbiont state on immune parameters (base R). Then a principal component analysis was used to further compare immune profiles between white and brown colonies (base R). Prior to PCA analysis all numeric variables were centered and scaled. Next
column6: to determine the effects of our continuous predictors (symbiont density

column8: and sperm density) on immune profiles (response variables) centered and scaled values were used to conduct an RDA analysis with the R package vegan (v. 2.6-4; ). Forward model selection was used to determine significant predictors in our analysis. Finally
column9: a correlational analysis was conducted to test for associations between all continuous variables (predictor and response) using base R (Pearson correlation).

column1: Coral reefs globally are undergoing rapid
column2: unprecedented declines due to anthropogenic climate change and associated disease outbreaks (; ; ). Corals exhibit both inter- and intra-specific variation in susceptibility to disease and infection
column3: but the ecological factors that drive susceptibility are poorly understood (; ). Few studies have investigated trade-offs between key life-history traits and immunity in cnidarians (; ; ; ). Here
column4: we used the facultatively symbiotic coral Astrangia poculata to investigate potential reproductive-immune trade-offs in scleractinian corals
column5: and the impacts of variation in symbiont density and energy budgets on these trade-offs.

column1: Our study found limited links between variation in symbiont density and our measured physiological traits (lipid/carbohydrate concentration
column2: immune activity
column3: reproductive output). Most notably
column4: we observed no significant difference in total lipid and carbohydrate content as a result of variable symbiont density in adult coral colonies. While this is in agreement with a previous study of A. poculata ()
column5: it is in opposition to conventional thinking regarding symbiosis
column6: which would suggest that increased symbiont density results in increased energetic budget (; ; ; ). We propose two alternative hypotheses to explain these patterns: first
column7: it is possible corals with lower symbiont density compensate via increased heterotrophic feeding. Unlike tropical corals
column8: Astrangia is commonly found in low light
column9: nutritionally rich waters. Consequently
column10: these corals may obtain a significant portion of their nutrition from heterotrophy
column11: especially colonies with low symbiont densities (; ). Heterotrophic feeding has previously been demonstrated to have a significant role in the efficiency of physiological traits in Astrangia and other facultatively symbiotic corals (; ; ; ). Thus
column12: it is possible that our colonies with lower symbiont densities were compensating via heterotrophic feeding and actually not nutrient limited. The absence of nutrient limitation due to heterotrophic feeding may also explain the lack of trade-offs observed (see below). Alternatively
column13: total lipid and carbohydrate assays may not fully captured host energetic budget
column14: as coral energetic budgets are exceptionally complex (). Other approaches which account for carbon flux
column15: utilize stoichiometric approaches
column16: or assess diverse metabolites in both the presence and absence of heterotrophy
column17: may provide further insight regarding the relationship between symbiont state and energetic budget.

column1: Similar to energetic budget
column2: associations between symbiont density and immunity were limited. Our multivariate analyses failed to detect any differences in immunity between white and brown colonies
column3: nor as a result of symbiont density. However
column4: correlational analysis and univariate linear modeling detected a positive association between melanin concentration and symbiont density (but not symbiont state). Previous A. poculata studies have noted a significant positive association between melanin synthesis and symbiotic state/symbiont density (; ). This broad consensus regarding the positive association between melanin and symbiont density is most likely reflective of the dual roles of melanin synthesis in immunity and symbiont regulation. Melanin production can be used as a mechanism of symbiont shading in response to UV
column5: reducing organismal stress (). Further investigation of response of A. poculata to pathogens/immune threats
column6: as opposed to measurement of constitutive immunity
column7: will provide insight regarding the roles of melanin in immune responses
column8: and clarify the mechanisms of symbiont-melanin associations.

column1: Next
column2: we considered associations between symbiont density
column3: carbohydrate and lipid concentration and sperm density. Only carbohydrate concentration was positively associated with sperm density
column4: suggesting that sperm release is at least in part resource limited. As gametogenesis in A. poculata begins earlier in the year ()
column5: this association may be more indicative of the energetic costs of sperm release (spawning)
column6: rather than sperm production. Nutrient limitation has significant negative effects on multiple ejaculate traits ()
column7: reflective of the costs of sperm release. Further analysis of the association between energetic budget and sperm density throughout gametogenesis will provide improved understanding of temporal shifts in energetic allocation to reproduction.

column1: In addition to sperm density
column2: carbohydrate concentration was also positively associated with two metrics of immunity: melanin concentration and antibacterial activity. These associations are likely indicative of the high costs of both of these immune metrics. Melanin production is the result of a complex pathway; beginning with pathogen recognition and continuing through a series of protein cascades leading to the production of melanin (; ). The complexity of the pathway is likely associated with high metabolic costs. To such end
column3: previous studies have documented negative associations between lipid reserves and melanin concentration following immune stimulation (). Similarly
column4: antibacterial activity as measured here is the result of the action of many unique compounds. Corals and other cnidarians secrete a wide diversity of often complex antibacterial compounds (; ; )
column5: which may require significant energy to produce. In contrast
column6: other metrics of immunity measured here
column7: specifically antioxidants
column8: are single molecules/compounds
column9: and likely require significantly less metabolic input (). The low cost of investment in these components could explain the lack of strong correlation between our metrics of energetic budget and these immune parameters.

column1: Finally
column2: when considering the factors driving variation in immunity using both a multivariate and univariate approach
column3: we found limited evidence for any association between sperm density and immunity. A moderate positive association between total phenoloxidase and sperm density was observed using correlational analyses. There is conflicting evidence regarding the link between reproduction and phenoloxidase in other invertebrates
column4: with studies indicating both positive and negative associations (; ). Further study of immune-reproduction associations in cnidarians will help clarify this relationship.

column1: In total
column2: our results were in contrast with our hypothesis; we observed no significant trade-offs between constitutive immunity and reproduction. We can posit several hypotheses to explain these observations. First
column3: the timing of our experiment may not have properly reflected peak reproductive investment. Gametogenesis in A. poculata begins in early March-April and continues through June and July
column4: with colonies spawning in early August/September (). While we observed some association between sperm density and carbohydrate concentration indicative of resource allocation
column5: is possible that this allocation may have been higher at earlier points of gametogenesis
column6: as opposed to at the time of sampling in August (). Consequently
column7: the most pronounced trade-offs would have occurred earlier in the season
column8: and tapered off by the point of sampling as resources availability increased and more resources could be allocated to immunity. Second
column9: we chose here to measure sperm density due to sampling logistics. Sperm production is typically considered to be less energetically costly than production of eggs (; ; ). While we did observe some association between energetic resources and sperm
column10: it is likely that these associations are more pronounced in females and consequently reproductive trade-offs would be more evident when considering females. Finally
column11: our study does not account for multiple spawning events
column12: specifically spawning events that may have occurred naturally prior to sample collection. Some coral species spawn multiple times throughout a season
column13: and exact timing of Astrangia spawning in natural environments is unknown (). Thus
column14: it is possible that the collected Astrangia colonies had previously spawned in situ
column15: and the induced events were a secondary release with reduced sperm density not reflective of true reproductive investment. Congruent with this possibility
column16: several of our colonies had negligible measured sperm density. Future studies combining histology with immunological assays at different points of gametogenesis in both male and female colonies may clarify presence and timing of potential trade-offs.

column1: It is also possible that nuances in our study design did not allow us to observe trade-offs. It must be noted that trade-offs occur in a multi-dimensional trait space filled with competed demands (; )
column2: and our experimental analyses only captured two of these demands (i.e.
column3: reproduction and immunity). It is highly likely that more complicated trade-offs
column4: that involve other organismal demands (growth
column5: maintenance
column6: etc.
column7: ) may occur and were not captured by our study. Approaches that properly reflect the multi-dimensional trait space associated with resource allocation will be necessary to fully disentangle the relationship between immunity and other costly processes. Additionally
column8: our study measured a limited number of immune metrics; it is possible that trade-offs exist between reproduction and immunity but involve other components of the immune system. The coral immune system is complex
column9: involving many different components (receptors
column10: signaling cascades
column11: cellular and humoral effector responses
column12: etc.)
column13: all of which have different relative costs (; ; ; ). It is therefore reasonable to assume that trade-offs are not equivalent across components
column14: as has previously been observed in other systems (; ; ; ). Future studies should incorporate more comprehensive metrics of immunity using methods such as gene expression or proteomics.

column1: In sum
column2: our results fail to document notable associations between variation in symbiont density or energetic budget and metrics of host physiology (reproductive output and immunity). Furthermore
column3: we find no evidence of trade-offs between reproductive output and immune activity. Still
column4: our results are an important first step in broadening general understanding of resource allocation theory in cnidarians
column5: which can be applied to other organisms facing rapid environmental changes. The information provided here provides an important preliminary framework for future studies of immunological trade-offs in marine invertebrates and the potential effects of variation in energetic budget on these patterns.

84|10.7717/peerj.5934|Reproduction and metamorphosis in the Myristica Swamp tree frog, Mercurana myristicapalustris (Anura: Rhacophoridae)| Abraham  Robin Kurian |2018-11-21|Anuran reproduction has conventionally been classified into two broad suites of breeding systems: explosive and prolonged
column2: even though they are opposite extremes of a broad spectrum (). Explosive breeding is usually associated with habitats in seasonal climates
column3: in species that breed only once each year
column4: in aggregations around suitable oviposition sites (). In many species that breed explosively
column5: males congregate for only a few days of the year
column6: form ‘choirs’ and vocalize in choruses
column7: advertising locations of breeding sites to females that arrive simultaneously from surrounding areas (; ); in such species
column8: with the arrival of females
column9: the males stop singing and start competing energetically and pursue females to ensure mating opportunity (). In prolonged breeding
column10: on the other hand
column11: breeding can last many months and males establish individual territories and use different calls both to warn male rivals not to approach their territory and to stand out from the rest of the males and therefore be chosen by females (; ). Between these two general classes
column12: around 60 reproductive modes have been identified in anurans
column13: ranging from aquatic eggs deposited in water to vivparity (; ; ).

column1: The Asian tree frogs (family Rhacophoridae) comprise 421 recognized species ()
column2: which exhibit four general reproductive modes: aquatic breeding
column3: terrestrial gel nesting
column4: terrestrial foam nesting
column5: and direct development (). In 2013
column6: we described two new monotypic rhacophorid genera from the southern Western Ghats mountains of India (). Of these
column7: Mercurana is a lineage that is sister to the clade containing direct-developing Raorchestes + Pseudophilautus. Other
column8: early
column9: successively-branching taxa include Nasutixalus (three species
column10: from Northeast India
column11: Myanmar
column12: and Southern Tibet and Yunnan in China ()
column13: Kurixalus (15 species distributed from the Eastern Himalayas to insular Southeast Asia ()
column14: and Beddomixalus (one species
column15: from the southern Western Ghats (). All six genera are part of a larger clade including direct-developing taxa such as Philautus (55 species
column16: primarily insular Southeast Asia)
column17: Raorchestes (62 species
column18: primarily in the Indian Subcontinent)
column19: and Pseudophilautus (79 species
column20: primarily in Sri Lanka) (). The monophyly of this large clade traditionally has been interpreted as supported by a single unambiguous synapomorphy: the presence of a simple
column21: tubular Wolffian duct in males (; ). However
column22: within this clade
column23: the phylogenetic position of Mercurana is not entirely clear with respect to the clades of Raorchestes and Pseudophilautus (; ).

column1: Previously
column2: we documented the reproductive behaviour of Beddomixalus bijui in detail: the species breeds explosively
column3: ovipositing terrestrial
column4: non-pigmented eggs openly on the moist bed of highland swamps immediately following the first pre-monsoon rains (). Embryos develop into free-swimming tadpoles that complete larval growth in swamps that inundate with standing water after the rains intensify (). This strategy was classified as ‘terrestrial gel nesting with exotrophic tadpoles’ by .

column1: The Western Ghats mountain range is a globally important biodiversity hotspot that supports many unique ecosystems and numerous endemic families
column2: genera and species of plants
column3: animals and fungi (). This region also represents a major area of endemic amphibian radiations
column4: with hundreds of described species (; ). Mercurana myristicapalustris is a rare
column5: highly range-restricted species that has so far been located only at two sites in the Western Ghats
column6: both in Myristica swamp forests in the southern part of the state of Kerala. Myristica swamp forests are dominated by species of Myristicaceae (nutmeg family)
column7: and are found as pockets in low-lying
column8: poorly-drained depressions in evergreen forest formations in the Western Ghats (; ). Once widespread across the west coast of India
column9: these relics of primeval forests occur today as small fragments from Goa in the north to southern Kerala in the south (). We previously noted reproductive behaviour in M. myristicapalustris
column10: which appeared similar to that of Beddomixalus ()
column11: as likely indicative of explosive breeders. As in Beddomixalus
column12: we observed large aggregations of male Mercurana congregating at breeding sites during the pre-monsoon period
column13: vocalizing males calling from perches
column14: and females depositing eggs on the swamp floor.

column1: Males of some species in Pseudophilautus
column2: Polypedatus
column3: and Rhacophorus are known to defend limited calling and mating areas against conspecifics with combat
column4: suggesting territoriality (; ; ). We witnessed one instance of physical combat between males of M. myristicapalustris
column5: in what was perceived to be competition for optimal breeding sites ().

column1: Oviposition by female M. myristicapalustris occurs in shallow burrows
column2: excavated by the female in the loamy swamp floor ()
column3: suggesting that a rainfall-mediated stimulus may be vital for further larval development. The related Kurixalus idiootocus
column4: from Taiwan
column5: lays pigmented eggs in shallow depressions or holes at the edges of temporary ponds (); embryonic development apparently is stimulated by heavy rain
column6: and tadpoles complete development in water
column7: much like in Mercurana and Beddomixalus.

column1: In this study

column3: (c) is territorial
column4: and (d) is an explosive breeder
column5: typical of many other pre-monsoon breeding frogs in India.

column1: We monitored a population of M. myristicapalustris from 5 May–31 July 2016. The study area is a Myristica swamp forest patch

column4: one male approached an adjacent male at its habitual leaf perch. The resident male intensified calling rate upon detecting the intruder’s approach

column6: wrestling and kicking while calling at the same time
column7: till one of the two individuals either withdrew or was pushed off the perch. The combating males made the Type 2 call ()
column8: which comprised of screechy notes (pulses)
column9: and were louder than the other call types. After 240 s of physical combat
column10: the intruding male retreated. A second instance of male-male combat was observed (20:08 h
column12: involving two males violently kicking one another using their hind limbs
column13: till one fell out of the perch.

column1: Although the objective of this study was to perform focal-animal samples and instantaneous behavioural scans of Mercurana myristicapalustris
column2: we were prevented from making detailed observations on a larger sample of individuals. A primary reason for this difficulty is that the species’ Myristica swamp habitat has been reduced from historical extents to just a few fragments (). The presence of wild elephants rendered most fragments inaccessible for us in the evenings for reasons of security.

column1: During repeated visits to two sites over different years
column2: we noticed yearly variation in periodicity of Mercurana’s annual breeding period. One site was our primary study area from 2012 to 2017
column3: and the second was at Sasthanada
column4: where we recorded this species in 2017 and 2018. During this period at these sites
column5: we noted male reproductive activity to range from two-weeks in some years
column6: to barely one-month
column7: between mid-May and early June.

column1: Our observations in this study indicate that M. myristicapalustris exhibits a combination of characteristics of both explosive and prolonged breeding strategies
column2: which are two extremes of a spectrum of amphibian breeding systems. Males secured optimal breeding sites by actively defending territories
column3: despite the species’ apparently explosive breeding strategy. Males also engaged in active combat with intruding conspecific males
column4: and sites were occupied by defending males on multiple nights. Male site fidelity to exposed perches has been explained by two hypotheses that state (a) that it provides better visual field that improves visual and acoustic communication and
column5: (b) that it facilitates the defense of territories and search for mates (; ; ). Although sexual dimorphism too has been associated with male–male territorial combat behaviour ()
column6: sexual dimorphism in M. myristicapalustris may not be an evolutionary response to male territoriality.

column1: Male M. myristicapalustris have a repertoire of different call types
column2: of which we recorded an advertisement call
column3: an encounter call
column4: and three calls for which the function is as yet unknown. Several species of rhacophorids are known to possess diverse vocal repertoires
column5: producing a large number of distinct calls that are variable in structure
column6: duration
column7: amplitude and frequency
column8: in no predictable order (). In spite of noting territorial
column9: encounter
column10: fighting and displacement behaviours in M. myristicapalustris
column11: it is difficult with our low encounter rates and poor sample size
column12: to categorize our recordings to any of these particular aggressive behaviour categories (). A study by  implied that the related male Kurixalus odontotarsus added specific notes in compound calls (that contain two kinds of notes) to suppress effects of an opponent male’s calls in male-male competition
column13: while also providing females with information about the male’s ability to attract mates. We are yet unaware of any such suppressor notes in M. myristicapalustris
column14: although our study was not detailed enough to discern such a possibility. Many other species within the Rhacophoridae
column15: including those occurring in the Western Ghats
column16: such as species of Beddomixalus ()
column17: Raorchestes () and Pseudophilautus () also produce vocal repertoires of varying complexity.

column1: The two females observed in this study chose to mate with males that staked out territories closer to breeding sites in the lowest parts of the swamp that had the potential to be flooded for the longest period of time. Eggs were loosely deposited in shallow burrows made by the female on the swamp floor during the pre-monsoon season
column2: and this can be confirmed as a variant of a terrestrial nest. Utilization of burrows of varying depths as egg deposition and development sites is known primarily in terrestrial and fossorial frogs (; ; ). Terrestrial eggs have been known to have an advantage over aquatic eggs
column3: especially in their improved embryonic respiratory environment
column4: and thus freeing animals to colonize habitats without permanent water bodies (). However
column5: terrestrial eggs experience new risks from desiccation and terrestrial predators (). So
column6: many terrestrial egg laying species oviposit in terrestrial nests in which the necessary humidity is maintained
column7: such as the moss nests of the Australian moss frog (Bryobatrachus nimbus) (). The Australian brown toadlet (Pseudophryne bibroni) however
column8: oviposit in a terrestrial nest under moist leaf litter
column9: with tadpoles emerging upon being flooded ()
column10: just as observed in M. myristicapalustris. It has been noted in terrestrial nests of the red-backed toadlet (Pseudophryne coriacea) that hypoxia triggers egg hatching (). Our observations on M. myristicapalustris showed that in clutches oviposited at the same time
column11: rain-induced inundation of nest sites triggered egg hatching while clutches in sites that were not flooded took longer to hatch. We need more detailed observations to test the role of hypoxia in M. myristicapalustiris larval emergence.

column1: Time of oviposition in both instances of breeding that we observed was just before the intensive southwest monsoons
column2: which makes landfall on the southwest coast of India annually around the beginning of June. Our observations in this study provide insight into an error we made in our previous report of amplectic behaviour in this species. column1: This study reports on how Typhoon 17 affected Ryugu Reef with before and after transect data. Objectives of this study include examination of coral communities at different depths and the identification of species and functional groups most affected by typhoons. One hypothesis tested was that shallow mesophotic reefs with large monospecific stands are more resistant to storm damage than diverse reefs.

Five stations were designated at the Ryugu site (). Station 1 was the deepest (42 m) and Station 5 the shallowest (17 m). Stations are shown in  and summarized in . Temperature was recorded every 30 min using temperature loggers (HOBO U22 Temp Pro v2 logger; Onset Corp.
column2: Massachusetts

column1: Water motion was estimated at each station (except Station 1) by the dissolution of plaster balls. Plaster balls (10.5 cm diameter) were made following . Water speeds for each station were calculated following the equations provided in .
Ten meter line transects were surveyed both before (17 April 2012 and 11–12 September 2012) and after (14 December 2012) Typhoon 17 at locations near Stations 2 (7 transects before

column1: Community data were analyzed using PRIMER 6 statistical software in order to find differences in coral communities before and after the typhoon (). All percent cover data were square-root transformed prior to analysis to moderately down-weight the importance of large space occupying operational taxonomic units (OTU). A one-way analysis of similarities test (ANOSIM) was performed to determine the difference and magnitude of difference in the assemblages before and after Typhoon 17. Non-Metric Multidimensional scaling (nMDS) was used to visualize multivariate patterns on the basis of the Bray-Curtis matrix. Bubble plot (square-root transformed cover data) was added to the plots to visualize variation in relevant OTUs. Each circle in  displays relative abundance of live coral species (based on square root transformed data of the species’ occurrence along each transect). Finally

column6: there were many small differences in diverse OTUs at Station 3 compared to Station 2
column7: where only 4 OTUs contributed to 70% of the typhoon effects. Station 2
column8: primarily composed of P. foliosa
column9: was heavily impacted by this storm
column10: despite the fact that it was deeper than Station 3
column11: suggesting that the foliose structure of P. foliosa is vulnerable to physical disturbances. The large monospecific stand found here is also in a more stable environment
column12: likely making it more sensitive to disturbance (; ; ; ). Therefore
column13: our hypothesis that shallow mesophotic reefs with large monospecific stands are more resistant to storm damage is rejected.

column3: several Fungiidae corals were completely buried by newly generated Acropora rubble and other branching coral rubble. Accordingly
column4: despite minor changes in the functionality of the coral community observed before and after Typhoon 17
column5: the groups the most affected at Station 3 were the bushy
column6: columnar and plate-like corals. At Station 2
column7: the dominance of P. foliosa may have masked any differences. Most damaged colonies of P. foliosa were still alive after Typhoon 17
column8: suggesting that species composition of this area may not change. This opposes the common idea that only massive corals would remain after disturbance ()
column9: suggesting a strong potential for the recovery and resilience of Ryugu Reef. Pachyseris species are typically gonochoric spawners that are most likely unable to fuse and create clones (). If Pachyseris species reproduce only by spawning
column10: Pachyseris-dominated reefs such as Ryugu should have a much slower recovery rate than Acropora-dominated reefs such as the one found near Kume-jima. However
column11: recruitment of coral larvae may allow this reef to recover relatively quickly. During this study
column12: new P. foliosa polyps were observed growing two to three months after Typhoon 17
column13: suggesting that the Pachyseris portion of the reef had already started to recover from the damage it incurred.

Global climate change is expected to bring larger and stronger typhoons to Okinawa

column1: One biomarker that shows increasing promise is fluctuating asymmetry. column10: and longevity (). Additionally
column11: fluctuating asymmetry occurs across several taxonomic boundaries including plants
column12: vertebrates
column13: and invertebrates (). Following larval exposure to certain environmental conditions under standardized conditions (i.e. laboratory)

column2: among adult western honey bees (A. mellifera) collected from colonies located along an urbanization gradient in the Greater Sydney Region. The western honey bee is an excellent model organism to study the effects of anthropogenic stressors and urbanization more generally due to its ubiquity across different landscape types including cities. Furthermore
column3: wing asymmetry is common in western honey bees and frequently used to investigate the consequences of ploidy and hybridization (). We predicted wing shape and size fluctuating asymmetry would increase as anthropogenic stressors including anthropogenic land cover

column5: and housing density increased. We also sampled anthropogenic stressors at multiple spatial scales

We predicted the effects of anthropogenic stressors on fluctuating shape and size asymmetry would be consistent at both fine and coarse spatial scales. Multi-scale approaches like this should be a key consideration in urban ecology given they can substantially affect the interpretation of biological patterns. For example

column1: We sampled 22 permanent disease-free colonies managed by amateur bee keepers across several local government areas within the Greater Sydney Region (). All colonies were located within the Sydney Basin Bioregion

column5: from the 8th to the 14th of February 2016. The disposal of dead nest mates from within the colony by honey bee ‘undertakers
column6: ’ is a common behavioural response that facilitates colony hygiene (). These dead bees are typically older and have likely transitioned through all stages of the honey bee temporal caste system including cell cleaner
column7: nurse
column8: and forager (). By using these bees
column9: we attempted to reduce temporal dependent differences in exposure to environmental stressors during development. Asymmetry and

Asymmetry has been verified in small sample sizes including <10 individuals (). Each slide was photographed at 12.5 × magnification using a compound light microscope and microscope Camera Leica MC170 HD attachment. Using tPSDig software ()
column4: we digitized photographs and collected a set of 19 landmarks positioned at wing vein junctions (). To reduce measurement error
column5: this process was repeated

column1: We assessed wing size and shape variation between colonies using Procrustes analysis
column2: an approach that characterizes shape mathematically using a series of transformations to remove the effects of location
column3: scale
column4: and rotation (
column5: ). We conducted the analysis on landmarks collected from both the left and right wing of individuals in MorphoJ software (). The output from this analysis includes a set of landmark coordinates containing shape information for each wing that has been corrected for size and position. To characterise wing size
column6: we derived centroid sizes from landmark coordinates. Centroid size describes the spread of landmarks around their centre of gravity and is calculated as the square root of the sum of squared distances of each landmark from the centroid of each wing (). The centroid size for each wing was used to calculate wing size asymmetry by dividing the absolute difference between the left and right centroid sizes by the mean centroid size and multiplying by 100. Higher values indicate greater wing size asymmetry between the left and right wings. To calculate wing shape variation

column4: the difference between the first and second sets of measurements are recorded
column5: and the square root of the sum of squares of these differences is divided by the total number of measurements times two (). column8: respectively ().

column1: Colony location significantly affected both wing size and wing shape asymmetry. Furthermore
column2: the effect of specific landscape features on wing shape asymmetry differed depending on the spatial scale considered. This finding is novel and highlights the important spatial scale considerations needed to assess the applicability of biomarkers including fluctuating asymmetry in the future. In our study
column3: within the three km buffer
column4: the only factor adding significantly to the model was colony-to-road proximity whilst
column5: within the 500 m buffer
column6: colony-to-road proximity and two additional factors (anthropogenic land area and vegetation area) added significantly to the model. Ultimately
column7: our findings indicate colonies located in areas surrounded by more urban features (e.g. proximal roads
column8: less vegetation) contain bees with higher wing shape asymmetries. Whilst these findings differ from those conducted on non-Apis bees (E. nigrita) () our results are consistent with those of previous work demonstrating increased wing asymmetry in western honey bees inhabiting urban compared to less urban forested areas ().

column1: Wing shape differences have previously been reported between western honey bees collected from several forest and contaminated landscape populations
column2: including urban areas (). These studies grouped sites categorically as either urban or rural (; ). To appreciate the more nuanced effects site specific differences may have on wing asymmetry
column3: we collected continuous measures of several landscape traits typically favoured or lost through urbanization. We suspect using continuous rather than categorical predictor variables may be more appropriate especially when the focal species is likely to respond to fine scale changes in habitat including vegetation and impervious surface. Future studies would benefit from including variables collected over fine spatial scales such as the diversity of vegetation patches directly surrounding colonies
column4: and quality of food resources.

column1: We found associations between road proximity and wing shape asymmetry. Similar results have been reported in the western fence lizard
column2: Sceloporus occidentalis (Baird & Girard
column4: Squamata
column5: Phrynosomatidae)
column6: with individuals inhabiting sites containing off-highway vehicular roads reporting higher bilateral scale pattern asymmetry (). The specific mechanism(s) associated with associations between how proximal colonies were to roads and wing shape asymmetry in honey bees remains unclear. From a physiological perspective
column7: we suspect the air pollutants occurring near roads and associated with road use (e.g. oxides of nitrogen and carbon) may be important. Carbon dioxide
column8: a common traffic-related air pollutant
column9: for example
column10: significantly affects development in the moth Manduca sexta (Linnaeus
column12: Lepidoptera
column13: Sphingidae)
column14: increasing egg development time parabolically (). Furthermore
column15: CO2 exposure increases wing shape asymmetry in the bumblebee B. impatiens ().

column1: Why wing asymmetry sizes and wing shape asymmetries differed between colony locations remains enigmatic
column2: however
column3: we suspect features of the hive and/or management practices used by bee keepers may be important. Practices increasing pesticide exposure risk including frequent spraying and/or multi-pesticide use are associated with increases in asymmetry in several bee species including the western honey bee () and Scaptotrigona aff. Depilis (Moure
column5: Hymenoptera
column6: Apidae) ().

column1: Given associations between wing shape asymmetry and vegetation and anthropogenic land area were detected at 500 m but not three km we suspect wing shape asymmetry may be resistant to changes in landscape at coarser spatial scales. This could also reflect decreased food quality and/or lower diversity and abundance of flowering plants directly surrounding each colony (i.e. within a 500 m radius). The fact wing size asymmetry was unaffected by landscape traits at either spatial scale
column2: is unsurprising. This measure is generally constant between populations exposed to several environmental stressors including CO2 and cold temperatures ().

column1: Overall the present results indicate wing shape asymmetry in western honey bees may be related to how proximal colonies are to roads and depending on the spatial scale considered (e.g. 500 m vs. three km)
column2: other habitat variables including vegetation area and anthropogenic land area. While additional environmental conditions may also have contributed to the fluctuating asymmetry observed (e.g. temperature
column3: pesticides
column4: or parasitism)
column5: our findings provide support for an effect of roads. Given that roads generate light
column6: noise
column7: and air pollution and increase edge effects by dividing habitat
column8: combinations of these factors should be incorporated into future observational studies. It is difficult to avoid confounding factors in such studies
column9: however
column10: combinations of field and manipulative laboratory studies (e.g. greenhouse reared colonies are exposed to known concentrations of traffic related air pollutants) increase the confidence with which we can establish a cause-effect relationship. One additional avenue for future work is the potential fitness consequence of the asymmetry observed. To our knowledge
column11: this is the first study to assess the effects of spatial scale on associations between measures of wing asymmetry and landscape traits associated with urbanization. Understanding how spatial scale and the factors favoured or lost through urbanisation affect biomarkers like wing asymmetry is an important step required to quantify the utility of morphometrics for assessing urbanization impact.

column2: the intra-urban or urban core areas) are separated from less drastically transformed suburban
column3: rural
column4: agricultural and wildland zones (extra-urban areas) by an ecotone or peri-urban zone that can sometimes act as continuum

column1: Some studies have shown that high levels of urbanization change insect community structure and provoke a severe decline in their abundance
column2: species richness and diversity (; ). Insect communities associated with urban core zones also tend to be more homogeneous and less diverse in their ecological functions
column3: which increases their susceptibility to extinction (; ). Therefore in peri-urban areas
column4: insect diversity can be higher than within the urban core. This is probably due to the presence of both native and introduced entomofauna and of generalist insects that are able to persist within the more diverse/less modified remnant vegetation patches of peri-urban areas (; ).

column1: According to anecdotal evidence

column5: its surrounding areas

column7: in addition to characterizing their current habitats and identifying threats to their persistence in urban zones. We increased the firefly species list from only two previously documented species to 26 (a 92.3% increase) and found females of some species for the first time (which will be described in posterior taxonomical works). We also detected sites of local extinction
column8: which differed from sites with current firefly populations mainly in that they both lack vegetated spaces
column9: have high levels of urban development (i.e.

column11: it seems likely that these factors are strongly related to the local extinction of fireflies in sites where they had been present only a couple of decades ago.

column1: Based on previous studies
column2: a total of six genera and 16 firefly species had been reported in Michoacán state (); later six new Photinus species were described by . According to our findings
column3: the species list for Michoacán now contains at least nine genera (Cratomorphus and Pyractomena as new records) and 41 species (at least 14 new records for the state; ). This nearly doubles the previous list for Michoacán
column4: which now contains 14.6% of the total Mexican lampyrid fauna (284 species). Here
column5: we highlight the relevance of citizen science platforms such as Naturalista and targeted entomological sampling as highly important data sources for inventorying current biodiversity. Without those sources
