Citizen participation through interviews was not only relevant to increase the temporal scale of presence-absence data on fireflies but also to inform people on the conservation of insects and their habitats. However
column7: our interviews implied a bias towards adult bioluminescent species
column8: therefore future studies on urban fireflies based on citizen science could involve trained volunteers to perform diurnal surveys.

column1: Fireflies were mainly found in the Central and Southern regions of Morelia
column2: perhaps because those areas have been the most recently developed and still show moderate to high levels of vegetation as well as high levels of humidity throughout the year (). These areas were previously crop fields or conservation areas and were only recently changed to residential use. As we expected
column3: fireflies in Morelia are associated with a variety of vegetated spaces in three different land covers and are more frequent in extra-urban and peri-urban green spaces and conservation zones where the environmental conditions seem to be better for them. These favorable habitat conditions can be summarized as larger
column4: moderately to highly vegetated habitats near water bodies with low levels of urban development and low light pollution. In contrast
column5: in Morelia fireflies are less frequent in residential areas and were never detected in industrial or commercial lands. Residential areas are usually sparsely vegetated
column6: highly developed
column7: and show high levels of light pollution
column8: which are very similar to conditions in sites where fireflies are already extinct. Previous studies have also reported a negative impact of urbanization on lampyrids and other bioluminescent coleopterans of Elateroidea from Brazil and suggested that their disappearance or decrease in urbanized sites could be associated with a reduction of green spaces and increase in artificial illumination (; ). Meanwhile
column9: found that habitat loss
column10: light pollution and climate change are the main threats for at least 66.6% of North American firefly species
column11: and  also found a negative relationship between the abundance of two firefly species and the level of urbanization of their habitats in Turin
column12: Italy.

column1: Habitat loss due to urbanization is one of the main threats to fireflies on a global and regional scale and has especially strong negative impacts on habitat specialist fireflies (). Based on our results
column2: the proportion of artificial surfaces and light pollution (measured here as radiance) are the main stressors for fireflies from Morelia
column3: similar to other studies (e.g.
column4: ; ; ). Artificial surfaces are replacing vegetated areas at a high rate in the city
column5: and their proportions are so high that only 1% of the total urban area is currently covered by green spaces. This means that only 1.6 m2 of green space is available per inhabitant in Morelia
column6: despite the World Organization of Health recommendation of 9 m2 per inhabitant (; ). Thus
column7: there are not enough vegetated spaces for fireflies and other insects to survive. On the other hand
column8: light pollution is strongly associated with urbanization and is one of the main threats to nocturnal
column9: crepuscular
column10: and diurnal fireflies (e.g.
column11: by affecting sexual communication in bioluminescent adults
column12: and prolonging daily and seasonal activity
column13: and delaying dormancy in diurnal species; ;
column14: ). Excess artificial light influences life story traits of fireflies
column15: their assemblage structures
column16: and the population dynamics and dispersal of other insect species (; ). In Morelia
column17: we found high levels of light pollution derived from the increase in artificial light at night (ALAN)
column18: which likely has similar impacts on local firefly populations.

column1: In contrast
column2: sites with conservation and restoration programs have shown the persistence of firefly assemblages or even an increase in their richness and abundances in the last two decades. That is the case for the Universidad Latina de América
column3: an ecological conservation area within the urban core

Monitoring well-preserved firefly populations as well as those threatened by urbanization will be essential for the understanding of firefly population dynamics in the short and long terms in the cities in development.

column1: Due to their sensitivity to environmental degradation
column2: fireflies can be used as bioindicators of ecosystems health (; ). Based on our findings
column3: sites from the urban core and extinction sites in Morelia likely have the highest levels of environmental degradation
column4: while peri-urban sites could face the same scenarios in the near future if environmental planning and conservation actions are not taken soon. On other hand
column5: firefly fauna from Morelia is still quite rich in species
column6: representing an important proportion of Mexican fireflies (9.28%)
column7: and fireflies are highly appreciated by some people in the city. Sharing their experiences with fireflies and our academic findings through science communication strategies would support potential conservation programs and restoration planning at sites where fireflies are now living on the edge of extinction. Cities are rapidly expanding
column8: and peri-urbanization is becoming more frequent; in those scenarios
column9: fireflies could be used as good bioindicators of environmental health that could help to detect and monitor zones in need of protection for nocturnal biodiversity and human health.

column2: there has been a notable uptick in the incidence of extreme meteorological and climatic phenomena
column3: particularly droughts (; ; ). Droughts cause soil degradation
column4: desertification
column5: water shortages
column6: plant death
column7: dust storms
column8: fires
column9: and other disasters
column10: with serious impacts on agriculture
column11: ecology

column1: Traditional drought monitoring mainly relies on soil moisture content data at site scale to characterize the extent and severity of drought in the study area (; ; ). From these observational datasets
column2: an array of meteorological drought metrics have been established
column3: notably the Standardized Precipitation Index (SPI) (; )
column4: the Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI) ()
column5: and the Palmer Drought Severity Index (PDSI) (). Nevertheless
column6: the sporadic and imbalanced distribution of meteorological stations impedes the accurate determination of drought’s spatiotemporal dynamics (). In recent years
column7: spatial remote sensing technology has made great progress in monitoring drought
column8: with its advantages of rapidly obtaining large-scale spectral information of land cover in time and space
column9: supplementing the shortcomings of traditional monitoring methods (; ). It extends the measurement of traditional points to information of surfaces
column10: providing multiple parameters related to the development of drought processes such as precipitation
column11: soil moisture
column12: evapotranspiration
column13: physiological and ecological conditions of vegetation
column14: and surface thermal conditions. Satellite remote sensing monitoring drought technology has made great progress with the rapid development of global earth observation technology. Various remote sensing drought monitoring models and multiple remote sensing drought indices have emerged
column15: which have significant effects in drought monitoring applications in various countries
column16: and are an indispensable means in global drought resistance and disaster reduction (; ; ; ). The most widely used one is the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI). However
column17: its efficacy diminishes in heterogeneous landscapes due to geographical
column18: ecological
column19: and pedological influences (; ).  introduced the Vegetation Condition Index (VCI) by normalizing the NDVI value to a specific locale’s maximum range. The NDVI component related to weather is smaller than the component related to the ecosystem
column20: so normalization successfully maximizes the reduction of the ecosystem component. VCI can monitor the impact of drought on vegetation health alone
column21: but it is not enough to monitor because it only indicates one humidity condition ().

column1: In light of temperature’s potential to partially depict drought scenarios
column2: the Temperature-Vegetation Drought Index (TVDI) has been proposed. This index is formulated on the spectral reflectance properties near-infrared (NIR) and red channels
column3: leveraging the spatial relationship between the land surface temperature (LST) and NDVI as proxies for drought and soil hydration statuses (). TVDI
column4: which emerges from the juxtaposition of LST and vegetation index (VI) scatter plots
column5: is exclusively derived from remote sensing datasets
column6: eschewing supplementary data
column7: and exhibits proficiency in regions of moderate or intermittent vegetation. This foundational correlation has proven instrumental in ascertaining soil hydration levels and augmenting drought surveillance endeavors (). TVDI underscores the dynamic interdependence between NDVI and LST. Introducing temperature—a temporally responsive metric—enhances the prediction of hydric stress
column8: potentially modulating NDVI’s representation of vegetation coverage (). TVDI can evaluate soil moisture by synthesizing information from the visible
column9: near-infrared

column1: The Temperature Vegetation Dryness Index (TVDI) serves as a pivotal tool in remote sensing drought monitoring. It appraises the conditions of soil surface moisture conditions based upon the spatial features of a two-dimensional scatter plot similar to a triangle
column2: created from the surface temperature (LST) and vegetation index (VI) data. This LST-VI feature space is a scatter plot of LST and VI values for a given pixel in a two-dimensional coordinate system ().

column1: illustrates the triangle’s vertices represent three extreme situations in the LST-VI feature space: the upper left corresponds to to arid
column2: barren soil (low VI values and high LST values)
column3: the lower left represents wet soil (minimum VI and LST values in that region)
column4: and the bottom right vertex symbolizes the most saturated soil with highest vegetation cover (high VI values and low LST values). For bare soil areas
column5: changes in surface temperature are highly correlated with soil moisture changes. From the top left corner to the bottom left corner of the feature triangle
column6: the amount of surface soil water evaporation gradually increases from zero to the maximum value. The trajectory from the upper left to the lower left of this triangle indicates an incremental ascent in surface soil water evaporation. The bottom right vertex represents the surface soil being completely covered by vegetation and having sufficient water content
column7: with a water stress index of 0 in this region (). The triangle’s oblique side signifies a compromised soil moisture efficiency
column8: and minuscule surface water evaporation
column9: thereby being characterized as a “dry edge” state during drought periods. Conversely
column10: the base suggests ample soil moisture content
column11: unrestrictive to plant growth
column12: with the surface evapotranspiration being equated to potential evapotranspiration
column13: thus falling within a “wet edge” state ().

column1: The TVDI serves as a prominent tool for assessing diverse moisture conditions
column2: encompassing regions from unvegetated landscapes to those exhibiting dense vegetative growth (). Its reliability
column3: however
column4: may be affected by dynamic changes in vegetation cover and the intricacies of the terrain
column5: complicating the clear demarcation between arid and humid regions (). On a more expansive scale
column6: the variability introduced by distinct topographical elements translates to differing influences
column7: including precipitation
column8: ambient temperature
column9: and evapotranspiration rates. Such factors play pivotal roles in determining vegetation distribution and heterogeneity in a given area
column10: subsequently affecting its climatic and ecological equilibrium. The innate variability of soil attributes amplifies the uncertainty associated with TVDI’s soil moisture predictions (). Previous studies posited that the TVDI was best suited for areas exhibiting limited topographical diversity (). However
column11: modern enhancements in the methodology have expanded its applicability to larger expanses
column12: including the Eurasian landmass. Yet
column13: the precise adaptability of TVDI in certain locales warrants further exploration
column14: emphasizing the value of regional customization to bolster prediction precision and diminish potential inaccuracies.

column1: This study aims to evaluate the effectiveness of two TVDI indices for drought monitoring in Guangdong Province and to examined drought spatiotemporal variations in Guangdong Province during the 20 years from 2000 to 2019. Within age-structured populations

column3: fecundity
column4: and survival) of younger age classes which occur in response to elevated levels of extrinsic adult mortality (death of adult animals attributable to external factors such as disease or predation; ; ). To maximise individual fitness within an environment of high extrinsic adult mortality
column5: selection for increased developmental rates
column6: decreased size
column7: and age at sexual maturity
column8: and an increased adult life span (decreased intrinsic adult mortality) will occur (). This theory has been empirically borne out in a number of systems in response to several different sources of mortality

column11: and experimentally induced adult mortality ().

column1: Changes to population demography can have profound impacts on the growth and stability of infected populations (). This phenomenon has recently been demonstrated by
column2: who documented high adult mortality and associated truncation of population age structure in alpine tree frog (Litoria verreauxii alpina) populations infected with the lethal fungal pathogen Batrachochytrium dendrobatidis. Populations with truncated age structure were shown to be more vulnerable to decline due to stochastic recruitment failure than B. dendrobatidis-free populations (). These results highlight an important
column3: yet relatively unexplored mechanism by which infectious diseases can negatively affect their hosts.

column1: Infectious diseases are emerging at a faster rate and threatening a larger range of species than at any prior point in history (). Therefore
column2: it is imperative to better understand demographic shifts associated with disease
column3: and their consequence for population viability
column4: particularly in species of conservation concern.

column1: Worldwide
column2: amphibians are the most imperilled class of vertebrates (). Aside from threats such as habitat loss ()
column3: over-harvesting ()
column4: and climate change ()
column5: one major driver of amphibian declines is the emergence of a suite of infectious diseases (). One of the most widespread and deadly of these diseases
column6: ranavirosis
column7: is caused by viral pathogens belonging to the genus Ranavirus (; ). Ranaviruses are globally distributed and are capable of infecting and killing a wide range of species from three classes of ectothermic vertebrates (Amphibians
column8: ; Fish
column9: ; Reptiles
column10: ). Clinical ranavirosis is often characterised by severe dermal ulcerations ()
column11: as well as haemorrhaging and lesions affecting the internal organs (; ). At the population level
column12: acute incidences of ranavirosis often manifest in mass mortality events
column13: with extreme or complete population mortality reported in some instances (; ). Evidence from the UK shows that following an episode of mass mortality due to ranavirosis European common frog (Rana temporaria) populations can decline in size by more than 80% (). Though population recovery has been observed in some instances
column14: often population size remains supressed
column15: and some populations decline to local extinction ().  demonstrated that ranaviruses are potentially more widespread than previously thought
column16: and present within the environment even when resident frog populations show no overt signs of disease. These findings suggests that whether or not an outbreak of ranavirosis occurs may depend on currently unknown biotic or abiotic factors of an environment
column17: although there is evidence for a role of the cutaneous or environmental microbiome ()
column18: secondary host species
column19: and chemical usage (). Susceptibility to ranavirosis outbreaks varies among species
column20: but a number of ecological risk factors
column21: including life history strategy (the species specific cycle of growth
column22: maturation
column23: reproduction and death)
column24: have been identified (). To date
column25: no work has evaluated the impact of ranavirosis on the demographic structure of host populations using individual age data.

column1: In this study
column2: we used a unique comparative field system born out of the Frog Mortality Project (FMP; see ;  for details) to study the impacts of ranaviral disease history on the demographic structure of wild R. temporaria populations in the UK. Unlike in most other species of susceptible amphibian
column3: ranavirosis disproportionately kills adult R. temporaria
column4: rather than tadpoles (). We determined population age structure using skeletochronology (age determination by counting skeletal growth rings) to test the hypothesis that a history of ranavirosis truncates the age structure of R. temporaria populations. Additionally
column5: we investigated potential mechanisms for such age truncation by using morphometric data to explore if frogs originating from populations with a history of ranavirosis display reduced body size or evidence of more rapid growth compared to their counterparts from ostensibly disease-free populations. Finally
column6: to examine the potential effect of the demographic impacts of ranavirosis
column7: we used population projection modelling to simulate the dynamics of hypothetical R. temporaria populations under a range of stochastic environmental scenarios that impact recruitment
column8: adult survival
column9: or both. Based on previous evidence (; ; )
column10: we hypothesised that age structure truncation would heighten the vulnerability of R. temporaria populations to environmental stochasticity.

column1: This project was approved by the ethics boards of both the University of Exeter and Zoological Society of London and conducted under UK Home Office project license 80/2466. All field sampling was conducted under the personal Home Office license 30/10730 issued to Lewis Campbell.
Evidence from the same populations studied herein suggests that ranaviruses may be more ubiquitous within amphibian populations in the UK than previously thought
column2: being detectable even in populations with no history of ranavirosis (). We therefore used a history of ranavirosis related mortality (or lack of)

column4: to differentiate between populations that are impacted by ranaviruses and those which are not. Candidate R. temporaria populations were identified using the FMP database of privately owned fieldsites that have experienced at least one R. temporaria mass mortality event due to ranavirosis and a complimentary database of fieldssites thought to have not experienced a ranavirosis outbreak

column6: distributed throughout Southern England (). Although sizes varied

Briefly
column2: ranavirosis-positive fieldsites were classified as such if they had experienced a confirmed ranavirosis mass mortality event between 1997 and 1998 and ongoing discovery of frog carcasses with symptoms consistent with ranavirosis was reported by property owners to . Ranaviruses were confirmed as the causative agents of mortality within these populations using a combination of post-mortem pathological examination of frog carcasses and molecular diagnostic techniques (). In order to be classified as disease-free
column3: fieldsites must have been owned or occupied by the same people at all times since 1997 and have contained a well-monitored R. temporaria population for at least as long. Owners/occupiers of disease-free fieldsites must not have observed any signs or symptoms of ranavirosis at any time. See  for more detailed fieldsite selection criteria. Fieldsite proprietors were contacted to establish their willingness to be involved in this study. Five ranavirosis populations were successfully recruited and matched with five populations that have remained disease-free (). None of our ranavirosis-positive populations had experienced a mass mortality event within the last decade.

column1: To investigate how changes in population age structure

column3: can impact the dynamics and stability of R. temporaria populations
column4: we constructed population matrix models. Comprehensive methodologies of our matrix modelling can be found in our supplementary methods section (Methods S1). However
column5: in brief
column6: we created hypothetical R. temporaria populations of 150 sexually mature female animals and projected these populations 20 years into the future based on two matrices which represented potential vital rates at ranavirosis-positive R. temporaria population (increasing annual mortality in each adult age class) and a disease-free R. temporaria population (uniform adult mortality). We populated these matrices using the vital rates for R. temporaria published by  except that we increased the fecundity of each post sexual maturity age class of R. temporaria by 50 eggs compared to the previous age class. Theoretical clutch sizes ranged from 250 eggs per year per 2-year-old adults to 650 eggs per year per 10-year-old adults. This increase was done according to age by size data collected for this study and previously published work showing that the clutch sizes of R. temporaria are tightly positively correlated with female body size with a >3 fold increase between individuals of 50 mm versus individuals of 80 mm (). To analyse the impact of these adjustments we compared the sensitives of our adjusted matrices to one constructed with the unadjusted vital rates of . As previous evidence has shown that disease induced demographic shifts or the changes in vital rates which may bring them about can increase susceptibility to environmental change (; ) we then modelled our populations in environmentally stochastic scenarios which incorporated processes which may result in annual reproductive failure

column6: whereas sensitivity to changes in fecundity of older adult age classes was higher in our disease-free population matrix (). In concordance with the matrix constructed using the unadjusted vital rates of Biek et al. (2002)
column7: both of our altered matrices were most sensitive to fluctuations in the summed vital rates of post metamorphic R. temporaria life stages
column8: though larval survival demonstrated the highest or second highest individual elasticity in all matrices (). The most noticeable dissimilarity between our adjusted matrices and the unadjusted matrix was that matrix sensitivity was distributed more evenly between the fecundity of all adult age classes in the former

column1: Our stochastic projection models showed that disease-free populations subject to a 10% probability of recruitment failure per year were still able to consistently attain population sizes near carrying capacity much more often than ranavirosis-positive populations subjected to the same scenario
column2: which attained a more variable array of population sizes (; ). The inclusion of potential ranavirosis mass mortality events further destabilised ranavirosis-positive populations and under a scenario when both adult mass mortality and reproductive failure could occur within the same year populations were driven locally extinct in 58% of model iterations (; ).

column1: Our results illustrate that a history of ranavirosis can have a significant effect on the age structure of a population. We found that older R. temporaria (aged 6–10 years) are significantly less likely to be found within populations with a positive history of ranavirosis than they are in disease-free populations. Since none of our sampled populations have experienced a significant mass mortality event within the lifetime of any of the sampled frogs (∼10 years)
column2: this suggests a pattern of attritional mortality in ranavirosis-positive populations
column3: rather than the sudden and catastrophic loss of any particular age classes. Attritional mortality of adults is consistent with our knowledge of ranavirosis in R. temporaria. First
column4: R. temporaria are highly philopatric ()
column5: and our study populations occupy permanent
column6: urban
column7: or semi-urban garden ponds (; ). Ranaviruses have been shown to persist in such water bodies ()
column8: particularly in the presence of secondary host species such as fish or other
column9: less susceptible
column10: amphibian species (; ). None of our fieldsites supported natural or stocked populations of fish
column11: however
column12: we did encounter the common toad (Bufo bufo) at one population of each disease status. The presence or absence of secondary hosts therefore does not represent a consistent confounding variable between our disease history groups. Second
column13: mortality due to ranavirosis is annually recurrent (; )
column14: and unlike all other host-ranavirus systems
column15: infection in the UK primarily affects adult life stages of R. temporaria (; ). Third
column16: adaptive immune response to ranaviruses are apparently limited in R. temporaria (; ). Consequently
column17: it is possible that adult mortality within populations with a history of ranavirosis is maintained in such a way that an individual is more likely to become infected and succumb to ranavirosis the more often it returns to spawn. Such a scenario would explain the decreased likelihood of observing individuals older than 5 years of age in populations with a history of ranavirosis.

column1: Concurrently
column2: we found that a significantly higher number of 2–5 year old frogs were captured at disease-free populations than at populations with a history of ranavirosis. Life history theory predicts that the first compensatory response to high adult mortality should come in the survival rates of lower age classes (). We lack any data on the immature age classes present at our study populations and the snapshot nature of our study means we cannot draw inference on whether the survival rates of younger adult age classes are increased in ranavirosis-positive populations. However
column3: it is plausible that such an increase could result in the observed increased abundance of younger animals.

column1: Empirical studies conducted on populations of other vertebrates subjected to persistent infectious diseases have detected a reduction in the age or size at sexual maturity within diseased populations (; ; ; ). As such
column2: we hypothesised that R. temporaria originating from populations where ranavirosis causes increased adult mortality would reach sexual maturity at an earlier age than frogs from disease-free populations. We also hypothesised that a trade-off in the allocation of resources to early reproduction
column3: away from growth
column4: would cause frogs from ranavirosis-positive populations to attain a lower body size per age than those from disease-free populations. However
column5: we found that a positive history of ranavirosis is not associated with a significant impact on either age at sexual maturity or body size throughout life. These findings are in contrast with the findings of () who found that an infectious disease (B. dendrobatidis) reduced the size and age at sexual maturity of L. v. alpina.

column1: The age at which R. temporaria mature is intrinsically linked to attaining a minimum body length needed to successfully reproduce (; ). The age at which this length is attained has been shown to be heavily influenced by several environmental factors such as photo-period and altitude (). All frogs in our study were found to mature at either 2
column3: or 4 years of age and this is consistent with the findings of other similar studies on R. temporaria (; ; ). In fact
column4: no previous study has found male or female R. temporaria to reach maturity younger than 2 years of age and post-sexual maturity there is little detectable trade-off between growth and fecundity in response to sub-prime environments (). This evidence suggests that the life history strategy of R. temporaria may already be optimised to generate maximum reproductive fitness in light of other environmental factors and that scope for further plasticity in traits such as age at sexual maturity and subsequent growth rate in response to diseases may be minimal.

column1: Given the apparent lack of compensatory change in the onset of sexual maturity an alternative explanation for the fact that we encountered 2- and 3-year-old breeding frogs at ranavirosis-positive populations but not disease-free populations may be behavioural changes that enhance the life time reproductive success of individuals in the face of high adult mortality. Participation in spawning events in exposed aquatic environments is associated with a significant mortality risk to an individual
column2: caused either by exposure to predation or the act of mating itself (). Additionally
column3: smaller R. temporaria present in breeding populations are easily outcompeted by larger individuals
column4: who are better able to secure a mate and achieve amplexus (). The loss of larger
column5: more competitive individuals at ranavirosis-positive fieldsites may release smaller R. temporaria from this intraspecific competitive pressure. Increased opportunity for less competitive individuals to participate successfully in reproductive events may result in animals that would normally defer breeding until subsequent years or larger body sizes attempting to reproduce earlier. Additionally
column6: in environments of high adult mortality the number of lifetime reproductive events is potentially limited. In such environments lifetime reproductive success is likely higher when an individual exploits all possible chances to produce offspring. Thus smaller
column7: less competitive individuals may attempt to breed earlier than they would do in the absence of disease induced mortality
column8: irrespective of the odds of being out competed by older/larger frogs.

column1: Any or all of the potential processes outlined above could lead to the observed truncation of age structures in R. temporaria populations with a history of ranavirosis. While our results provide strong evidence of such truncation
column2: they are based on data collected from adult R. temporaria sampled during one breeding season. As such we are unable to draw conclusions as to the mechanisms which drive observed changes to population age structure. Additionally
column3: due to the correlational nature of our study we are unable to emphatically exclude the possibility that the age structure truncation we observed is attributable to some other unmeasured variable between our studied populations. Although the lack of systematic differences between our fieldsites other than disease history suggests that this is unlikely. Nevertheless
column4: our results highlight a potentially important and as yet unexplored facet of the relationship between ranaviruses and their amphibian hosts. The impact of demographic shifts has been demonstrated in a number of study systems (; ; ; ; ) and as such the relationship between ranaviruses and population demographics undoubtedly warrants further investigation. A long-term
column5: mark-recapture study within the same populations used here would likely prove a critical first step in elucidating the mechanisms which bring about shifts in R. temporaria population age structure.

column1: Previous investigations have shown that age structure truncation
column2: associated with an emerging pathogen
column3: severely reduces the viability of host populations
column4: particularly under variable environmental conditions (). We used population matrix models to probe the potential impacts of a truncated age structure
column5: and the demographic processes which may bring about such changes
column6: on the viability of R. temporaria populations in the UK. Additionally
column7: we introduced environmental stochasticity into our models in order to explore the combined impact of these two challenges simultaneously.

column1: When modelled in the absence of increased adult mortality due to ranavirosis
column2: starting population vectors representing both a disease-free population and a ranavirosis-positive population (truncated age structure) exhibited minimal difference in their dynamics. This finding suggests that the level of age structure truncation we document is unlikely to reduce the viability of R. temporaria populations. The same was true when both population age structures were modelled in the presence of increasing adult mortality due to ranavirosis
column3: suggesting that even under such conditions the relatively high survival of pre-metamorphic
column4: juvenile and young adult age classes is enough to maintain population viability. This suggestion is supported by the elasticity of our population matrices which demonstrated that the survival of tadpoles
column5: juveniles and early adult life stages have higher predicted impact on the population growth dynamics of our populations than the survival of older adult age classes. This result is supportive of the findings of other investigations of amphibian population dynamics (; ).

column1: However
column2: under environmentally stochastic scenarios
column3: where recruitment was reduced
column4: populations that were subjected to increasing adult mortality due to ranavirosis faired much worse than ranavirosis-free populations. These results are consistent with previous observations of age structure truncation increasing the vulnerability of populations to environmental stochasticity (; ; ). Body size
column5: age and fecundity are positively correlated in R. temporaria ()
column6: as well as in many other species (; ; ; ; ). The relative absence of the oldest and largest breeding animals from disease positive populations means that per capita fecundity will be reduced and annual recruitment rates lowered. Such changes likely heighten the impacts of any events that result in failed recruitment or further adult mortality.

column1: It is important to note that our matrix models are approximations of the populations that they represent
column2: and the results of our models are therefore demonstrative of potential additional impacts of ranavirosis
column3: rather than definitively demonstrating the existence of such impacts. Although our simulations are based on published literature
column4: they necessarily incorporate several assumptions. A key assumption in our models is the number of eggs produced per each individual
column5: per each adult year of age. Although we have strong grounds to suggest that fecundity of female R. temporaria increases with age and size
column6: the number of eggs produced by each frog of a given age or size is unknown in our study populations. If a larger number of eggs are produced than represented by our matrices
column7: it may be that the impact of stochastic recruitment failure upon our populations is reduced and vice versa. Similarly
column8: we reduce the survival of each adult age class into the next by 5% for each year of development post-sexual maturity. Whilst we found no impact of the amount by which we reduced this annual survival parameter on the dynamics of our modelled populations
column9: in reality survival may not decrease annually in such a uniform manner. Finally
column10: we set an upper limit for our theoretical populations of 150 female animals. However
column11: due to the nature of our fieldsites we lack the ability to make accurate estimations of actual population sizes at our studied populations. Larger actual population sizes may buffer populations against the potential impacts that we have demonstrated but smaller actual population sizes may mean that the potential demographic impacts of ranavirosis may be even more consequential for R. temporaria populations. Further investigation of the life history traits and an accurate estimation of sizes of our study populations would allow for more robust parameterisation of our models and the ability to more accurately predict the impact of ongoing ranavirosis within them.

column1: Despite these limitations
column2: our population models corroborate previous empirical data collected from within our study system. A long-term study of population sizes has shown that following an outbreak of ranavirosis
column3: characterised by a mass mortality event
column4: UK R. temporaria populations follow three possible trajectories. These are: (1) complete recovery to post outbreak population levels
column5: (2) persistence at a largely reduced population size
column6: or (3) local extinction (). Our simulated R. temporaria populations were projected to population sizes that incorporate all of these possible outcomes
column7: dependant on the levels of environmental stochasticity to which a population was subjected. Importantly
column8: our simulations suggest that the fate of a population subjected to ranavirosis may depend heavily upon the stability of its environment
column9: providing a potential explanation as to why some populations appear to persist with endemic infectious disease
column10: while others are driven to local extirpation.

column1: Variations in adult fecundity due to body size are often not included in population projection modelling (; but see ). However
column2: our results highlight the importance of considering age or body size specific changes in fecundity in population modelling
column3: particularly when considered threats disproportionately impact certain age classes. Ensuring matrix models of populations represent the life history of the study species as closely as possible is essential
column4: especially when seeking to inform conservation or policy decision making.

column1: Our results highlight an increasing need to better understand the impact of disease on the demography of host populations and the processes which can bring about such demographic shifts. Further investigation of this relationship
column2: possibly via a long-term mark-recapture study on the same populations used here could help elucidate the exact mechanisms responsible for age structure truncation in R. temporaria populations. This work also further suggests that the emergence of an infectious disease within a population can heighten its vulnerability to external stressors. Although the theoretical stressor incorporated into our models was environmental stochasticity
column3: the same is likely to be true for all types of stressor including anthropogenic. This result is timely given that we live in a time of unprecedented disease emergence and anthropogenic change ().

column1: Forests in Southeast Asia provide a wide range of important ecosystem services to many people and communities. Unfortunately
column2: these forests have been disrupted by widespread deforestation and land use change (; )
column3: resulting in various stages of forests in the same area (). In particular
column4: the areas that were previously used for agricultural purposes have been abandoned for several years
column5: and naturally or artificially transformed into secondary forests. Consequently
column6: many of the degraded forests may not contribute much to improving biodiversity and mitigating climate change through carbon dioxide removal from the atmosphere. Therefore
column7: reforestation projects have emerged in many countries in the tropics
column8: highlighting the use of native species to avoid competition with other native trees
column9: which can help restore biodiversity and sequester carbon (). However
column10: planting more trees in existing secondary forests may raise some concerns because trees are potentially heavy water users and might deplete water resources (). Also
column11: reforestation may not be desirable in certain areas because it may reduce water availability for the existing trees and increase the evapotranspiration rate () and thus leading to reductions in runoff (). With these regards
column12: an appropriate selection of tree species for planting is among the priority tasks for forest restoration since species-specific water-use characteristics play an important role in changing the components of the forest hydrologic cycle (). However
column13: the availability of such information is still limited in tropical forests
column14: especially in secondary ones. Hence
column15: it is imperative to evaluate the response patterns of T to environmental factors in secondary tropical forests that would offer necessary information on species-specific water-use characteristics.

column1: Khao Yai National Park (KYNP) is a UNESCO world heritage site. Most of the areas of KYNP consist of a mosaic of different stages of vegetation succession with more than 60% of the forests undergoing different stages of regeneration while the remainder are old-growth forests. Thus
column2: secondary forests in KYNP are important to biodiversity conservation and climate change mitigation through the regulation of atmospheric carbon. With these regards
column3: this study was performed in a secondary tropical forest at KYNP
column4: representing a young forest aged ∼10 years. In this study site
column5: the dominant tree species include Syzygium antisepticum and Adinandra integerrima. Syzygium antisepticum can be found as the dominant species in other tropical forests such as tropical evergreen swamp forests in Cambodia ()
column6: dry evergreen forests in northeastern Thailand () and tropical coastal sand dune in southern Thailand (). Adinandra integerrima can be found in other parts of Thailand
column7: such as Doi Inthanon National Park in the northern region () and other countries in the tropics
column8: such as Cambodia
column9: China

column13: this study aims to (1) estimate T of Syzygium antisepticum and Adinandra integerrima in a secondary tropical forest in KYNP

column16: findings from this study may provide a recommendation for selecting appropriate tree species for forest restoration in the tropical region.

column1: The results from this study imply that Sa may provide ecosystem disservice in dry areas due to its high water consumption which results in low water supply for the downstream community
column2: but it may slow down runoff in the region that experiences heavy precipitation. In contrast
column3: Ai may provide ecosystem benefits by conservatively using water
column4: even under drought conditions
column5: but may increase runoff when storms come with high rainfall. Another implication is that Ai may be suitable for reforestation in the area where droughts frequently occur in downstream ecosystems through its conservative water-use behavior
column6: thus maintaining runoff from the forests during drought. Moreover
column7: because Ai showed relatively constant water use regardless of tree size
column8: the species would still provide such benefits to the ecosystems even when it grows larger in the future. In contrast
column9: Sa may be appropriate for reforestation in the area with frequent floods because it has high water consumption during high water availability which may decelerate runoff from forests into downstream ecosystems. This would benefit the downstream ecosystems when storms occur. Regardless
column10: mixed planting species seem to be suitable for reforestation in the areas where extreme events do not frequently occur because both species can maintain their water use at moderate soil moisture regardless of tree size
column11: therefore preventing the depletion of soil water availability. In addition
column12: mixed planting species could reduce the competition for limited water resources because the differences in root structures of different tree species lead to less competition for water (). Nevertheless
column13: reforestation projects should emphasize the use of native species to avoid competition with other native trees on the site ().

column1: We estimated tree water use (T) of dominant tree species including Syzygium antisepticum (Sa) and Adinandra integerrima (Ai) in a secondary tropical forest in Khao Yai National Park from sap flux density ( Js) which was continuously monitored with custom-made thermal dissipation probes and compared T of both species in different tree size classes. In addition
column2: we evaluated the responses of T to environmental factors of both species in different tree size classes. The results showed that T of Sa was significantly higher than Ai and that large trees had higher T than small ones which was related to relatively lower sapwood area in the small trees. Further analysis of the response patterns of T showed that Sa was more sensitive to increasing VPD than Ai while Ai can maintain their water use regardless of tree size under low soil moisture. This implies that Ai may be able to cope with the negative effects of droughts and retain such capacity when they grow. With ample soil moisture
column3: both species can maintain their tree water use regardless of tree size. When soil moisture becomes high
column4: the T of both species in both sizes increases with rising VPD and then saturated at high VPD. Nevertheless
column5: T of both species in large size was less sensitive to rising VPD than in small size which may be explained by the deeper access to groundwater in large trees. For the implications for management
column6: our results suggest that Ai may be suitable for reforestation in the area where droughts frequently occur in the downstream ecosystem through its conservative water-use behavior and may benefit downstream ecosystems with continuous runoff from the forest despite droughts. Moreover
column7: Ai has conservative water-use behavior regardless of tree size. Thus
column8: Ai would still provide these benefits to ecosystems when they grow larger in the future. In contrast
column9: Sa seems suitable for reforestation in the area with frequent floods because it has high water consumption during high water availability which may slow down runoff from forest into downstream ecosystems when storms come. However
column10: mixed planting species may be suitable for reforestation in areas where extreme events do not frequently occur because both species can maintain their water use at moderate soil moisture regardless of tree size which prevents the depletion of soil water availability. In this case
column11: depending on the purposes of reforestation
column12: Sa and Ai may provide either benefits or negative effects to the ecosystems. In conclusion
column13: this study highlights the dependency of responses of T to environmental conditions on tree species and size. Such information would benefit the selection of tree species for reforestation that could adapt well to certain environments and support policy design on the management of tropical forests and water resources. Nevertheless
column14: a further study involving additional field measurements of the physiological parameters of trees
column15: such as root depth
column16: is needed to support the proposed findings.

column1: 91|10.7717/peerj.16399|Potential of psychrotolerant rhizobacteria for the growth promotion of wheat ( Triticum aestivum L.)| Abdullah  Muhammad |2023-11-30|"Wheat is the second most important crop after rice
column2: contributing 35% of total global grain production. Global wheat demand is estimated to increase up to 70% by 2050 to feed the ever-increasing human population (). Pakistan annually produces 26.4 million tons of wheat
column3: which contributes 1.8% to the GDP (). Improving crop productivity has become a very important goal in satisfying food demand (). Climate change is the major limiting factor in wheat productivity and poses a constant threat to food security (). Climate change intensifies abiotic stresses such as heat and cold
column4: which reduces crop production (; ). Cold stress is a major abiotic stress
column5: in which plant experiences lower temperatures ranging from 0 °C to 15 °C (; ). During cold stress
column6: the plant produces reactive oxygen species (ROS) that damage the macromolecules and ultimately disrupt cellular homeostasis (; ; ). Wheat is cultivated during the winter season in temperate regions and 85% of its area endures cold stress ().

column1: Different methods have been used to deal with cold stress
column2: such as sprinkler irrigation
column3: wind machines
column4: and heaters (). Chemical fertilizers are usually used to enhance crop production and mitigate plant stresses
column5: but these fertilizers jeopardize environmental sustainability (). Biofertilizers are based upon plant-beneficial bacteria that just like the chemical fertilizers
column6: enhance soil fertility and nutrient availability for plants but do not harm the environment (; ). These microbes in the rhizosphere facilitate plants to cope with biotic and abiotic stresses () by upregulating the expression of stress-responsive genes (; ). Rhizospheric microorganisms such as Pseudomonas
column7: Bradyrhizobium japonicum
column8: and Bacillus are known to display abiotic stress tolerance and rescue stressed plants (; ; ). The wheat rhizosphere is a niche for many bacteria
column9: including Paenibacillus
column10: Bacillus subtilis
column11: Bacillus thuringiensis
column12: Arthrobacter nicotianae
column13: Micrococcus luteus
column14: Bacillus amyloliquefaciens
column15: Pantoea
column16: Pseudomonas
column17: Methylobacterium
column18: Agrobacterium (; ; ; ; ). Microbial diversity and plant growth-promoting traits of microbes are severely affected by adverse environmental conditions such as cold stress (; ). Low temperature cause ice crystallization in the microbial membrane
column19: affecting the activity of essential enzymes reducing their activities to 2–4 folds for every 10 °C decrease in the temperature (). Moreover
column20: plant growth-promoting characteristics such as phosphate solubilization
column21: and IAA production also get influenced by low temperatures
column22: affecting the plant–microbe symbiosis which further decreases the plant growth rate ().

column1: Psychrotolerant bacteria
column2: on the other hand
column3: are the bacterial communities that thrive in low-temperature conditions (). These bacteria prevent ice crystal formation in their membranes by synthesizing anti-freezing proteins
column4: thereby regulating downstream processes (). In addition
column5: psychrotolerant bacteria can improve plant growth under cold stress conditions through nitrogen fixation
column6: phosphate solubilization
column7: and phytohormone production even at 4 °C (). Hence
column8: the use of plant-beneficial psychrotolerant bacteria is promiscuous for optimizing agricultural production while mitigating the negative effects of cold stress (). Psychrotolerant bacteria belonging to the genera Pseudomonas
column9: Burkholderia
column10: Bradyrhizobium
column11: Azospirrilum
column12: Bacillus
column13: and Raoultella demonstrate the potential to increase plant growth under cold stress (; ; ; ).

column6: 500 bp product of 16S rRNA gene from bacteria WR22 and WR24 were sequenced commercially and sequence contigs were compared with database sequences using the NCBI BLAST tool. 16S rRNA gene from WR22 showed maximum similarity (99.34%) with Cupriavidus campinensis while WR24 with Enterobacter ludwigii (99.53%). column7: where Methanoregula boonei was used as an outgroup. The tree was constructed based on the similarity of the 16S rRNA gene sequence of the species. Bacterial isolate WR22 was clustered in the neighborhood of Cupriavidus campinensis WS2
column8: and bacterial isolate WR24 was clustered in the neighborhood of Enterobacter ludwigii EN-119T in the phylogenetic tree. WR24 was clustered in clade 1 which includes all the species of Enterobacter. WR22 was clustered in clade 2 which includes all the species of Cupriavidus. Each clade includes the species of the same genera because the species of the same genera have a maximum similarity of 16S rRNA gene sequence (). A pairwise identity chart was constructed using the SDT tool and the color-coded chart indicates the percentage identity between the species ranging from 80 to 100% ().

column1: Climate change is one of the most crucial problems worldwide and a major threat to food security (). Cold stress exerts negative effects on the vegetative growth of wheat and ultimately results in yield losses affecting 85% of wheat cultivation annually (). Though cold tolerance can be induced in plants using genetic engineering tools
column2: the process is quite time-consuming
column3: tricky
column4: and laborious. On the other hand
column5: the use of plant growth-promoting rhizobacteria (PGPR) to rescue cold-affected plants is a quite simple
column6: effective
column7: and eco-friendly technology ().

column1: Numerous studies have demonstrated that psychrotolerant PGPR isolated from plants growing in cold regions has the potential to upregulate cold-tolerance mechanisms in plants such as wheat
column2: grapevine
column3: canola
column4: tomato etc. (; ; ; ; ). In this study
column5: ten cold-tolerant bacteria were isolated from wheat rhizosphere. Isolation was carried out at 4 °C considering that bacteria present in the sample are adapted to grow at low temperatures. Our results are in agreement with previous studies showing that bacteria can tolerate different temperatures at 4 °C
column10: and 28 °C (; ; ). Later
column11: the bacteria were tested for their ability to grow at extremely low temperatures (−4 °C). Four bacteria were efficient enough to show growth even at −4 °C. Such extreme cold temperature conditions develop in many wheat cultivation areas of the world (; ; ).  isolated PGPR from peas surviving at 5 °C.

After the cold-tolerance assay
column2: the bacteria were characterized for biochemical assays and plant growth promotion ability. PGPR have evolved direct and indirect mechanisms to increase plant growth (). Direct mechanisms are involved in regulating the growth hormones and macronutrients that affect plant growth directly. This include phosphate solubilization
column3: IAA production
column4: nitrogen fixation
column5: etc. Indirect mechanisms are generally happening outside the plant and bacteria increase plant growth indirectly by killing plant pathogens and increasing resistance against abiotic stress (; ; ).

column1: Phosphate is the second most important nutrient after nitrogen for plant growth. It helps in root hair development and improves plant growth (). In this study
column2: WR24 showed maximum phosphate solubilization at 4 °C
column3: while WR15 showed maximum phosphate solubilization at 28 °C. Previously
column4: isolated cold-tolerant phosphate solubilizing bacteria from agricultural soil and described their potential to increase tomato plant growth. Indole-3-acetic acid plays a very important role in root growth and development
column5: which helps in the uptake of nutrients (). In this study
column6: it was found that the production of IAA was reduced at low temperatures as compared to mesophilic conditions. WR24 showed the highest production of IAA at 4 °C and 28 °C whereas WR24 showed a higher production of IAA at 28 °C. Similar results are demonstrated by  and . Nitrogen is the most important nutrient required for plant growth and a major constitute of genetic material. In this study
column7: WR22 showed maximum nitrogen fixation ability at 4 °C
column8: while WR18 exhibited the highest ability at 28 °C. The amount of nitrogen fixation was higher at 4 °C as compared to 28 °C. Our results are in agreement with
column9: who reported efficient nitrogen fixation of the Chenopodium quinoa-associated bacteria at 6 °C and 25 °C. Cellulase and pectinase are involved in the decomposition of dead organic matter present in the soil and enrich the soil nutrient profile (). In this study
column10: maximum cellulase production at 4 °C was shown by WR22 while at 28 °C by WR20. Maximum pectinase production was observed by WR22 at both 4 °C and 28 °C
column11: but the amount of pectinase was higher at 4 °C.  also explained that cold-tolerant Bacillus isolated from glacial lakes has the potential to produce cellulase and pectinase at 4 °C
column12: which help to break cell wall of phytopathogens. Zinc is a micronutrient that helps in the synthesis of proteins
column13: and nucleic acid metabolism and acts as a cofactor for auxin production (). In this study
column14: WR24 and WR23 showed the highest zinc solubilization efficiency at 4 °C while at 28 °C the maximum efficiency was shown by WR19 and WR20 with ZnO and ZnCO3
column15: respectively.  also isolated bacteria from wheat rhizosphere and reported zinc solubilization efficiency at 28 °C by using ZnO as a zinc source.

column1: Biofilm formation is a major characteristic of bacteria to survive under stress conditions. In this study
column2: WR24 showed the highest biofilm formation ability at both 4 °C and 28 °C. The amount of biofilm was higher at 4 °C as compared to 28 °C. Our results are in agreement with  who reported the biofilm formation potential of psychrotolerant Bacillus strains isolated from Tibetan prefectures at 4 °C and 28 °C.

column1: Over all
column2: in vitro testing revealed that each bacterium has an optimum temperature to perform its inherent character. Some bacteria perform a specific character at low temperatures while other bacteria do at high temperature (). WR22 and WR24 showed better plant growth-promoting characters at 4 °C because these bacteria are adapted to cold conditions and work efficiently at low temperatures. Overall production of plant growth-promoting characters was high at 28 °C as compared to 4 °C
column3: which might be due to the reduction in bacterial metabolic activities at 4 °C.  and  also explained that plant growth-promoting efficiency is reduced at a lower temperature.

column1: Phylogenetic analysis of potential bacteria revealed the maximum similarity of WR22 with Cupriavidus campinesis and WR24 with Enterobacter ludwigii. This is the first report on the novel association of C. campinesis and E. ludwigii in the wheat rhizosphere. Previously
column2: reported Cupriavidus campinesis as a novel species isolated from root nodules of the Mimosa plant. Isolation of E. ludwigii from the rhizosphere of Lolium perenne and rice was reported in many studies (; ).  also isolated E. ludwigii from the roots of the medicinal plant Leucas aspera.

column1: Inoculation of Cupriavidus campinesis WR22 and Enterobacter ludwigii WR24 exhibited the maximum potential to enhance plant growth parameters under controlled conditions. Previously
column2: revealed that inoculation of E. ludwigii can increase wheat growth parameters under controlled conditions experiment.  inoculated rice with E. ludwigii
column3: which increased the plant growth parameter under cadmium stress.  reported that E. ludwigii improved the growth of barley up to 56%.  also demonstrated that the inoculation of E. ludwigii increased the growth of alfalfa under cold-stress conditions. Recently
column4: reported that inoculation of Cupriavidus sp. increases plant growth parameters in Brassica napus plants. Taking all the findings together
column5: it is suggested that C. campinesis WR22 and E. ludwigii WR24 have maximum PGP potential and could be used as cold-tolerant biofertilizers after field trials.

column1: Psychrotolerant PGPR have the potential to increase the growth of wheat in cold areas. In this study
column2: ten bacteria were isolated and five efficient bacteria were subjected to plant assay based on the cold-tolerance and plant growth-promoting properties. Inoculations of WR22 and WR24 were found promising for wheat growth promotion. Phylogenetic analysis revealed the highest similarity of WR22 with Cupriavidus campinesis and WR24 with Enterobacter ludwigii. This is the first report on the novel association of C. campinesis WR22 and E. ludwigii WR24 in the wheat rhizosphere. Conclusively
column3: C. campinesis WR22 and E. ludwigii WR24 could be used as cold-tolerant biofertilizers after field trials.
